subject: Physics
question_html:
1. With the help of a labelled diagram, explain the principle of working of a moving coil galvanometer. Write the purpose of using (I) radial magnetic field, and (II) soft iron core, in it.
  2. Define current sensitivity of a galvanometer. "Increasing the current sensitivity may not necessarily increase the voltage sensitivity." Give reason.
question_text: With the help of a labelled diagram, explain the principle of working of a moving coil galvanometer. Write the purpose of using (I) radial magnetic field, and (II) soft iron core, in it. Define current sensitivity of a galvanometer. "Increasing the current sensitivity may not necessarily increase the voltage sensitivity." Give reason.
explanation: Principle: A current carrying coil placed in uniform magnetic field, experiences a torque. Radial magnetic field makes the scale linear Use of soft iron core is to increase the strength of magnetic field/ increase sensitivity of the galvanometer. Current sensitivity is defined as deflection per unit current. Voltage sensitivity {tex} V_s=\frac{\Phi}{V}=\left(\frac{N A B}{k}\right) \frac{{I}}{V}=\left(\frac{N A B}{k}\right) \frac{1}{R}{/tex} Increase in number of turns, increases the current sensitivity and resistance of the galvanometer in the same proportion of current sensitivity therefore Voltage sensitivity remains unchanged.
solution: Principle: A current carrying coil placed in uniform magnetic field, experiences a torque. Radial magnetic field makes the scale linear Use of soft iron core is to increase the strength of magnetic field/ increase sensitivity of the galvanometer. Current sensitivity is defined as deflection per unit current. Voltage sensitivity {tex} V_s=\frac{\Phi}{V}=\left(\frac{N A B}{k}\right) \frac{{I}}{V}=\left(\frac{N A B}{k}\right) \frac{1}{R}{/tex} Increase in number of turns, increases the current sensitivity and resistance of the galvanometer in the same proportion of current sensitivity therefore Voltage sensitivity remains unchanged.
solution_html:
1. Principle:A current carrying coil placed in uniform magnetic field, experiences a torque.
    1. Radial magnetic field makes the scale linear
    2. Use of soft iron core is to increase the strength of magnetic field/ increase sensitivity of the galvanometer.
  2. Current sensitivity is defined as deflection per unit current.
    Voltage sensitivity {tex} V_s=\frac{\Phi}{V}=\left(\frac{N A B}{k}\right) \frac{{I}}{V}=\left(\frac{N A B}{k}\right) \frac{1}{R}{/tex}
    Increase in number of turns, increases the current sensitivity and resistance of the galvanometer in the same proportion of current sensitivity therefore Voltage sensitivity remains unchanged.

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. In which cases does a charged particle not experience a force in a magnetic field?
  2. A square loop MNPK of side '{tex}l{/tex}' carrying a current '{tex}{I}_{2}{/tex}' is kept close to a long straight wire in the same plane and the wire carries a steady current {tex}I_{1}{/tex} as shown in the figure. Obtain the magnitude of magnetic force exerted by the wire on the loop.
question_text: In which cases does a charged particle not experience a force in a magnetic field? A square loop MNPK of side ' {tex}l{/tex} ' carrying a current ' {tex}{I}_{2}{/tex} ' is kept close to a long straight wire in the same plane and the wire carries a steady current {tex}I_{1}{/tex} as shown in the figure. Obtain the magnitude of magnetic force exerted by the wire on the loop.
explanation: If charged particle is at rest {tex}{v}=0{/tex} If B is parallel or antiparallel to v Magnitude of force acting between two current carrying conductor {tex} {F}=\frac{\mu_0}{4 \pi} \frac{2 {I}_1 {I}_2}{{r}} {~L} {/tex} Force on segment MN of loop {tex} {F}_{{MN}}=\frac{\mu_0}{4 \pi} \frac{2 {I}_1 {I}_2}{l} \times l \text { (Towards the wire) } {/tex} Force on segment KP of loop {tex} {F}_{K P}=\frac{\mu_0}{4 \pi} \frac{2 {I}_1 {I}_2}{2 l} \times l \text { (Away from the wire) } {/tex} No force due to segment MK and NP Net force on the loop {tex} {F}={F}_{M N}-{F}_{K P} {/tex} {tex} {~F}=\frac{\mu_0}{4 \pi} {I}_1 {I}_2 {/tex}
solution: If charged particle is at rest {tex}{v}=0{/tex} If B is parallel or antiparallel to v Magnitude of force acting between two current carrying conductor {tex} {F}=\frac{\mu_0}{4 \pi} \frac{2 {I}_1 {I}_2}{{r}} {~L} {/tex} Force on segment MN of loop {tex} {F}_{{MN}}=\frac{\mu_0}{4 \pi} \frac{2 {I}_1 {I}_2}{l} \times l \text { (Towards the wire) } {/tex} Force on segment KP of loop {tex} {F}_{K P}=\frac{\mu_0}{4 \pi} \frac{2 {I}_1 {I}_2}{2 l} \times l \text { (Away from the wire) } {/tex} No force due to segment MK and NP Net force on the loop {tex} {F}={F}_{M N}-{F}_{K P} {/tex} {tex} {~F}=\frac{\mu_0}{4 \pi} {I}_1 {I}_2 {/tex}
solution_html:
• If charged particle is at rest {tex}{v}=0{/tex}
    • If B is parallel or antiparallel to v
  2. Magnitude of force acting between two current carrying conductor
    {tex} {F}=\frac{\mu_0}{4 \pi} \frac{2 {I}_1 {I}_2}{{r}} {~L} {/tex}
    Force on segment MN of loop
    {tex} {F}_{{MN}}=\frac{\mu_0}{4 \pi} \frac{2 {I}_1 {I}_2}{l} \times l \text { (Towards the wire) } {/tex}
    Force on segment KP of loop
    {tex} {F}_{K P}=\frac{\mu_0}{4 \pi} \frac{2 {I}_1 {I}_2}{2 l} \times l \text { (Away from the wire) } {/tex}
    No force due to segment MK and NP
    Net force on the loop
    {tex} {F}={F}_{M N}-{F}_{K P} {/tex}
    {tex} {~F}=\frac{\mu_0}{4 \pi} {I}_1 {I}_2 {/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. Write Ampere's circuital law in mathematical form and explain the terms used.
    2. As the current carrying solenoid is made longer, the magnetic field produced outside it approaches zero. Why?
    3. A flexible loop of irregular shape carrying current when located in an external magnetic field, changes to a circular shape. Give reason.
  2. A galvanometer of resistance G is converted into a voltmeter to measure up to V volts, by connecting a resistance {tex}{R}_{1}{/tex} in series with the coil. If {tex}R_{1}{/tex} is replaced by {tex}R_{2}{/tex}, then it can only measure up to {tex}\frac{{V}}{2}{/tex} volt. Find the value of the resistance {tex}{R}_{3}{/tex} (in terms of {tex}{R}_{1}{/tex} and {tex}{R}_{2}{/tex}) needed to convert it into a voltmeter that can read up to 2 V.
question_text: Write Ampere's circuital law in mathematical form and explain the terms used. As the current carrying solenoid is made longer, the magnetic field produced outside it approaches zero. Why? A flexible loop of irregular shape carrying current when located in an external magnetic field, changes to a circular shape. Give reason. A galvanometer of resistance G is converted into a voltmeter to measure up to V volts, by connecting a resistance {tex}{R}_{1}{/tex} in series with the coil. If {tex}R_{1}{/tex} is replaced by {tex}R_{2}{/tex} , then it can only measure up to {tex}\frac{{V}}{2}{/tex} volt. Find the value of the resistance {tex}{R}_{3}{/tex} (in terms of {tex}{R}_{1}{/tex} and {tex}{R}_{2}{/tex} ) needed to convert it into a voltmeter that can read up to 2 V.
explanation: {tex}\oint \vec{B} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}=\mu_0 {I}_e{/tex} {tex}{I}_{{e}}={/tex} Total current through the surface {tex}{B}={/tex} Magnetic field {tex}{dl}={/tex} length of small element As length of solenoid increases, it appears like a long cylindrical metal sheet, so field outside approaches zero. For a given perimeter, a circle encloses greater area than any other shape, which maximizes the flux. {tex} \text { } {R}_1=\frac{{V}}{{I}_{{g}}}-{G} \Rightarrow \frac{{~V}}{{I}_{{g}}}={R}_1+{G} {/tex} ...(1) {tex} {R}_2=\frac{{V}}{2 {I}_{{g}}}-{G} \Rightarrow \frac{{~V}}{2 {I}_{{g}}}={R}_2+{G} {/tex} ...(2) Solving (1) & (2) {tex} {G} ={R}_1-2 {R}_2 {/tex} {tex} {R}_3 =\frac{2 {~V}}{{I}_{{g}}}-{G} {/tex} ...(3) Solving using eq (1) & (3) {tex} R_3=3 R_1-2 R_2 {/tex}
solution: {tex}\oint \vec{B} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}=\mu_0 {I}_e{/tex} {tex}{I}_{{e}}={/tex} Total current through the surface {tex}{B}={/tex} Magnetic field {tex}{dl}={/tex} length of small element As length of solenoid increases, it appears like a long cylindrical metal sheet, so field outside approaches zero. For a given perimeter, a circle encloses greater area than any other shape, which maximizes the flux. {tex} \text { } {R}_1=\frac{{V}}{{I}_{{g}}}-{G} \Rightarrow \frac{{~V}}{{I}_{{g}}}={R}_1+{G} {/tex} ...(1) {tex} {R}_2=\frac{{V}}{2 {I}_{{g}}}-{G} \Rightarrow \frac{{~V}}{2 {I}_{{g}}}={R}_2+{G} {/tex} ...(2) Solving (1) & (2) {tex} {G} ={R}_1-2 {R}_2 {/tex} {tex} {R}_3 =\frac{2 {~V}}{{I}_{{g}}}-{G} {/tex} ...(3) Solving using eq (1) & (3) {tex} R_3=3 R_1-2 R_2 {/tex}
solution_html:
1. {tex}\oint \vec{B} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}=\mu_0 {I}_e{/tex}
      {tex}{I}_{{e}}={/tex} Total current through the surface
      {tex}{B}={/tex} Magnetic field
      {tex}{dl}={/tex} length of small element
    2. As length of solenoid increases, it appears like a long cylindrical metal sheet, so field outside approaches zero.
    3. For a given perimeter, a circle encloses greater area than any other shape, which maximizes the flux.
  2. {tex} \text { } {R}_1=\frac{{V}}{{I}_{{g}}}-{G} \Rightarrow \frac{{~V}}{{I}_{{g}}}={R}_1+{G} {/tex} ...(1)
    {tex} {R}_2=\frac{{V}}{2 {I}_{{g}}}-{G} \Rightarrow \frac{{~V}}{2 {I}_{{g}}}={R}_2+{G} {/tex} ...(2)
    Solving (1) & (2)
    {tex} {G} ={R}_1-2 {R}_2 {/tex}
    {tex} {R}_3 =\frac{2 {~V}}{{I}_{{g}}}-{G} {/tex} ...(3)
    Solving using eq (1) & (3)
    {tex} R_3=3 R_1-2 R_2 {/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. Current I (= 1 A) is passing through a copper rod ({tex}{n}=8.5 \times 10^{28} {~m}^{-3}{/tex}) of varying cross-sections as shown in the figure. The areas of cross-section at points A and B along its length are {tex}1.0 \times 10^{-7} {~m}^{2}{/tex} and {tex}2.0 \times 10^{-7} {~m}^{2}{/tex} respectively. Calculate:
    1. the ratio of electric fields at points A and B .
    2. the drift velocity of free electrons at point {tex}B{/tex}.
  2. Two point charges {tex}q_1(=16 \mu {C}){/tex} and {tex}q_2(=1 \mu {C}){/tex} are placed at points {tex}\vec{r}_1=(3 {~m}) \hat{i}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{r}_2=(4 {~m}) \hat{j}{/tex}. Find the net electric field {tex}\vec{E}{/tex} at point {tex}\vec{r}=(3 {~m}) \hat{i}+(4 {~m}) \hat{j}{/tex}.
question_text: Current I (= 1 A) is passing through a copper rod ( {tex}{n}=8.5 \times 10^{28} {~m}^{-3}{/tex} ) of varying cross-sections as shown in the figure. The areas of cross-section at points A and B along its length are {tex}1.0 \times 10^{-7} {~m}^{2}{/tex} and {tex}2.0 \times 10^{-7} {~m}^{2}{/tex} respectively. Calculate: the ratio of electric fields at points A and B . the drift velocity of free electrons at point {tex}B{/tex} . Two point charges {tex}q_1(=16 \mu {C}){/tex} and {tex}q_2(=1 \mu {C}){/tex} are placed at points {tex}\vec{r}_1=(3 {~m}) \hat{i}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{r}_2=(4 {~m}) \hat{j}{/tex} . Find the net electric field {tex}\vec{E}{/tex} at point {tex}\vec{r}=(3 {~m}) \hat{i}+(4 {~m}) \hat{j}{/tex} .
explanation: {tex} \vec{j}= \sigma \vec{E} {/tex} {tex} \frac{j_A}{j_B} =\frac{E_A}{E_B} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{I / A_A}{I / A_B} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{A_B}{A_A} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{2}{1} {/tex} {tex} {v}_d =\frac{{I}}{{neA}} {/tex} {tex} = \frac{1}{8.5 \times 10^{28} \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 2 \times 10^{-7}} {/tex} {tex} =3.6 \times 10^{-4} {~m} / {s} {/tex} {tex} \vec{E}= \frac{K q}{r^2} \hat{r} {/tex} {tex} \vec{E}_1= \frac{9 \times 10^9 \times 16 \times 10^{-6}}{(4)^2} \hat{j} {/tex} {tex} =9 \times 10^3 \hat{j} {/tex} {tex} \vec{E}_2= \frac{9 \times 10^9 \times 1 \times 10^{-6}}{(3)^2} \hat{i} {/tex} {tex} =10^3 \hat{i} {/tex} {tex} \vec{E}_{\text {net }} =(\hat{i}+9 \hat{j}) 10^3 {~N} / {C} {/tex}
solution: {tex} \vec{j}= \sigma \vec{E} {/tex} {tex} \frac{j_A}{j_B} =\frac{E_A}{E_B} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{I / A_A}{I / A_B} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{A_B}{A_A} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{2}{1} {/tex} {tex} {v}_d =\frac{{I}}{{neA}} {/tex} {tex} = \frac{1}{8.5 \times 10^{28} \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 2 \times 10^{-7}} {/tex} {tex} =3.6 \times 10^{-4} {~m} / {s} {/tex} {tex} \vec{E}= \frac{K q}{r^2} \hat{r} {/tex} {tex} \vec{E}_1= \frac{9 \times 10^9 \times 16 \times 10^{-6}}{(4)^2} \hat{j} {/tex} {tex} =9 \times 10^3 \hat{j} {/tex} {tex} \vec{E}_2= \frac{9 \times 10^9 \times 1 \times 10^{-6}}{(3)^2} \hat{i} {/tex} {tex} =10^3 \hat{i} {/tex} {tex} \vec{E}_{\text {net }} =(\hat{i}+9 \hat{j}) 10^3 {~N} / {C} {/tex}
solution_html:
1. {tex} \vec{j}= \sigma \vec{E} {/tex}
      {tex} \frac{j_A}{j_B} =\frac{E_A}{E_B} {/tex}
      {tex} =\frac{I / A_A}{I / A_B} {/tex}
      {tex} =\frac{A_B}{A_A} {/tex}
      {tex} =\frac{2}{1} {/tex}
    2. {tex} {v}_d =\frac{{I}}{{neA}} {/tex}
      {tex} = \frac{1}{8.5 \times 10^{28} \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 2 \times 10^{-7}} {/tex}
      {tex} =3.6 \times 10^{-4} {~m} / {s} {/tex}


  2. {tex} \vec{E}= \frac{K q}{r^2} \hat{r} {/tex}
    {tex} \vec{E}_1= \frac{9 \times 10^9 \times 16 \times 10^{-6}}{(4)^2} \hat{j} {/tex}
    {tex} =9 \times 10^3 \hat{j} {/tex}
    {tex} \vec{E}_2= \frac{9 \times 10^9 \times 1 \times 10^{-6}}{(3)^2} \hat{i} {/tex}
    {tex} =10^3 \hat{i} {/tex}
    {tex} \vec{E}_{\text {net }} =(\hat{i}+9 \hat{j}) 10^3 {~N} / {C} {/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. A rectangular loop of sides {tex}l{/tex} and b carries a current I clockwise. Write the magnetic moment {tex}\vec{m}{/tex} of the loop and show its direction in a diagram.
  2. The loop is placed in a uniform magnetic field {tex}\overrightarrow{{B}}{/tex} and is free to rotate about an axis which is perpendicular to {tex}\overrightarrow{{B}}{/tex}. Prove that the loop experiences no net force, but a torque {tex}\vec{\tau}=\overrightarrow{{m}} \times \overrightarrow{{B}}{/tex}.
question_text: A rectangular loop of sides {tex}l{/tex} and b carries a current I clockwise. Write the magnetic moment {tex}\vec{m}{/tex} of the loop and show its direction in a diagram. The loop is placed in a uniform magnetic field {tex}\overrightarrow{{B}}{/tex} and is free to rotate about an axis which is perpendicular to {tex}\overrightarrow{{B}}{/tex} . Prove that the loop experiences no net force, but a torque {tex}\vec{\tau}=\overrightarrow{{m}} \times \overrightarrow{{B}}{/tex} .
explanation: {tex}\overrightarrow{{m}}={I} \overrightarrow{{A}}{/tex} {tex} {F}_1={F}_2={IbB} {/tex} {tex}{F}_1=\text { Force on } {AB} \text { into the plane } {/tex} {tex} {F}_2=\text { Force on } {CD} \text { out of the plane } {/tex} Since forces are equal & opposite so net force {tex}=0{/tex} Both forces form a couple, magnitude of torque acting on the coil is {tex} \therefore \tau ={F}_1 \frac{l}{2} \sin \theta+{F}_2 \frac{l}{2} \sin \theta {/tex} {tex} ={I} {b} {B} l \sin \theta {/tex} {tex} ={I} {A} {B} \sin \theta {/tex} {tex} ={m} {B} \sin \theta {/tex} {tex} \vec{\tau} =\overrightarrow{{m}} \times \overrightarrow{{B}} {/tex}
solution: {tex}\overrightarrow{{m}}={I} \overrightarrow{{A}}{/tex} {tex} {F}_1={F}_2={IbB} {/tex} {tex}{F}_1=\text { Force on } {AB} \text { into the plane } {/tex} {tex} {F}_2=\text { Force on } {CD} \text { out of the plane } {/tex} Since forces are equal & opposite so net force {tex}=0{/tex} Both forces form a couple, magnitude of torque acting on the coil is {tex} \therefore \tau ={F}_1 \frac{l}{2} \sin \theta+{F}_2 \frac{l}{2} \sin \theta {/tex} {tex} ={I} {b} {B} l \sin \theta {/tex} {tex} ={I} {A} {B} \sin \theta {/tex} {tex} ={m} {B} \sin \theta {/tex} {tex} \vec{\tau} =\overrightarrow{{m}} \times \overrightarrow{{B}} {/tex}
solution_html:
1. {tex}\overrightarrow{{m}}={I} \overrightarrow{{A}}{/tex}
  2. {tex} {F}_1={F}_2={IbB} {/tex}
    {tex}{F}_1=\text { Force on } {AB} \text { into the plane } {/tex}
    {tex} {F}_2=\text { Force on } {CD} \text { out of the plane } {/tex}

    Since forces are equal & opposite so net force {tex}=0{/tex}
    Both forces form a couple, magnitude of torque acting on the coil is
    {tex} \therefore \tau ={F}_1 \frac{l}{2} \sin \theta+{F}_2 \frac{l}{2} \sin \theta {/tex}
    {tex} ={I} {b} {B} l \sin \theta {/tex}
    {tex} ={I} {A} {B} \sin \theta {/tex}
    {tex} ={m} {B} \sin \theta {/tex}
    {tex} \vec{\tau} =\overrightarrow{{m}} \times \overrightarrow{{B}} {/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. Write Biot-Savart's law in vector form.
  2. Two identical circular coils A and B, each of radius R , carrying currents {tex}I{/tex} and {tex}\sqrt{3} I{/tex} respectively, are placed concentrically in XY and YZ planes respectively. Find the magnitude and direction of the net magnetic field at their common centre.
question_text: Write Biot-Savart's law in vector form. Two identical circular coils A and B, each of radius R , carrying currents {tex}I{/tex} and {tex}\sqrt{3} I{/tex} respectively, are placed concentrically in XY and YZ planes respectively. Find the magnitude and direction of the net magnetic field at their common centre.
explanation: {tex}\overrightarrow{{dB}}=\frac{\mu_0}{4 \pi} \frac{{I}(\overrightarrow{{d}} \times \overrightarrow{{r}})}{{r}^3}{/tex} {tex} {B}_1 =\frac{\mu_0 {I}}{2 {R}} {/tex} {tex} {~B}_2 =\frac{\mu_0 \sqrt{3} {I}}{2 {R}} {/tex} {tex} {~B} =\sqrt{{B}_1^2+{B}_2^2} {/tex} {tex} \therefore {~B} =\frac{\mu_0 {I}}{2 {R}} \sqrt{1+3} {/tex} {tex} B =\frac{\mu_0 I}{R} {/tex} {tex} \tan \theta =\frac{B_1}{B_2}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} {/tex} Direction of net magnetic field is {tex}30^{\circ}{/tex} with direction of {tex}{B}_2 / 60^{\circ}{/tex} with the direction of {tex}B_1{/tex} .
solution: {tex}\overrightarrow{{dB}}=\frac{\mu_0}{4 \pi} \frac{{I}(\overrightarrow{{d}} \times \overrightarrow{{r}})}{{r}^3}{/tex} {tex} {B}_1 =\frac{\mu_0 {I}}{2 {R}} {/tex} {tex} {~B}_2 =\frac{\mu_0 \sqrt{3} {I}}{2 {R}} {/tex} {tex} {~B} =\sqrt{{B}_1^2+{B}_2^2} {/tex} {tex} \therefore {~B} =\frac{\mu_0 {I}}{2 {R}} \sqrt{1+3} {/tex} {tex} B =\frac{\mu_0 I}{R} {/tex} {tex} \tan \theta =\frac{B_1}{B_2}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} {/tex} Direction of net magnetic field is {tex}30^{\circ}{/tex} with direction of {tex}{B}_2 / 60^{\circ}{/tex} with the direction of {tex}B_1{/tex} .
solution_html:
1. {tex}\overrightarrow{{dB}}=\frac{\mu_0}{4 \pi} \frac{{I}(\overrightarrow{{d}} \times \overrightarrow{{r}})}{{r}^3}{/tex}
  2. {tex} {B}_1 =\frac{\mu_0 {I}}{2 {R}} {/tex}
    {tex} {~B}_2 =\frac{\mu_0 \sqrt{3} {I}}{2 {R}} {/tex}
    {tex} {~B} =\sqrt{{B}_1^2+{B}_2^2} {/tex}
    {tex} \therefore {~B} =\frac{\mu_0 {I}}{2 {R}} \sqrt{1+3} {/tex}
    {tex} B =\frac{\mu_0 I}{R} {/tex}
    {tex} \tan \theta =\frac{B_1}{B_2}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} {/tex}
    Direction of net magnetic field is {tex}30^{\circ}{/tex} with direction of {tex}{B}_2 / 60^{\circ}{/tex} with the direction of {tex}B_1{/tex}.

question_html: A particle of mass {tex}m{/tex} and charge {tex}q{/tex} moves along {tex}y{/tex}-axis in a region in which a uniform magnetic field {tex}\overrightarrow{{B}}{/tex} is pointing along x-axis. The Lorentz force acting on the charge will point along:
question_text: A particle of mass {tex}m{/tex} and charge {tex}q{/tex} moves along {tex}y{/tex} -axis in a region in which a uniform magnetic field {tex}\overrightarrow{{B}}{/tex} is pointing along x-axis. The Lorentz force acting on the charge will point along:

subject: Physics
question_html:
An electron of mass {tex}m{/tex} and charge -{tex}e{/tex} is revolving anticlockwise around the nucleus of an atom.

  1. Obtain the expression for the magnetic dipole moment ({tex}\mu{/tex}) of the atom.
  2. If {tex}\overrightarrow{{L}}{/tex} is the angular momentum of electron, show that {tex}\vec{\mu}=-\left(\frac{{e}}{2 {~m}}\right) \overrightarrow{{L}} .{/tex}
question_text: An electron of mass {tex}m{/tex} and charge - {tex}e{/tex} is revolving anticlockwise around the nucleus of an atom. Obtain the expression for the magnetic dipole moment ( {tex}\mu{/tex} ) of the atom. If {tex}\overrightarrow{{L}}{/tex} is the angular momentum of electron, show that {tex}\vec{\mu}=-\left(\frac{{e}}{2 {~m}}\right) \overrightarrow{{L}} .{/tex}
explanation: {tex} \mu =I A {/tex} {tex} =\frac{e}{T} \times A {/tex} {tex} =\frac{e}{\frac{2 \pi r}{v}} \times \pi r^2 {/tex} {tex} =\frac{1}{2} {evr} {/tex} {tex} {L} ={mvr} {/tex} {tex} \mu =\frac{{evr} \times {m}}{2 \times {m}} {/tex} {tex} =\left(\frac{e}{2 m}\right) L {/tex} Direction of {tex}\vec{\mu}{/tex} is opposite to that of {tex}\overrightarrow{{L}}{/tex} {tex} \vec{\mu}=-\left(\frac{{e}}{2 {~m}}\right) \overrightarrow{{L}} {/tex}
solution: {tex} \mu =I A {/tex} {tex} =\frac{e}{T} \times A {/tex} {tex} =\frac{e}{\frac{2 \pi r}{v}} \times \pi r^2 {/tex} {tex} =\frac{1}{2} {evr} {/tex} {tex} {L} ={mvr} {/tex} {tex} \mu =\frac{{evr} \times {m}}{2 \times {m}} {/tex} {tex} =\left(\frac{e}{2 m}\right) L {/tex} Direction of {tex}\vec{\mu}{/tex} is opposite to that of {tex}\overrightarrow{{L}}{/tex} {tex} \vec{\mu}=-\left(\frac{{e}}{2 {~m}}\right) \overrightarrow{{L}} {/tex}
solution_html:
1. {tex} \mu =I A {/tex}
    {tex} =\frac{e}{T} \times A {/tex}
    {tex} =\frac{e}{\frac{2 \pi r}{v}} \times \pi r^2 {/tex}
    {tex} =\frac{1}{2} {evr} {/tex}
  2. {tex} {L} ={mvr} {/tex}
    {tex} \mu =\frac{{evr} \times {m}}{2 \times {m}} {/tex}
    {tex} =\left(\frac{e}{2 m}\right) L {/tex}
    Direction of {tex}\vec{\mu}{/tex} is opposite to that of {tex}\overrightarrow{{L}}{/tex}
    {tex} \vec{\mu}=-\left(\frac{{e}}{2 {~m}}\right) \overrightarrow{{L}} {/tex}

question_html: A voltmeter of resistance {tex}1000 \Omega{/tex} can measure up to 25 V. How will you convert it so that it can read up to 250 V?
question_text: A voltmeter of resistance {tex}1000 \Omega{/tex} can measure up to 25 V. How will you convert it so that it can read up to 250 V?
explanation: {tex} {I} =\frac{V}{R} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{25}{1000} {/tex} {tex} =25 \times 10^{-3} {~A} {/tex} Resistance to be connected to voltmeter {tex} {R}^{\prime} =\frac{{V}^{\prime}}{{I}}-R {/tex} {tex} =\frac{250}{25 \times 10^{-3}}-1000 {/tex} {tex} =9000 \Omega {/tex} This {tex}9000 \Omega{/tex} is in series with voltmeter.
solution: {tex} {I} =\frac{V}{R} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{25}{1000} {/tex} {tex} =25 \times 10^{-3} {~A} {/tex} Resistance to be connected to voltmeter {tex} {R}^{\prime} =\frac{{V}^{\prime}}{{I}}-R {/tex} {tex} =\frac{250}{25 \times 10^{-3}}-1000 {/tex} {tex} =9000 \Omega {/tex} This {tex}9000 \Omega{/tex} is in series with voltmeter.
solution_html:
{tex} {I} =\frac{V}{R} {/tex}
{tex} =\frac{25}{1000} {/tex}
{tex} =25 \times 10^{-3} {~A} {/tex}
Resistance to be connected to voltmeter
{tex} {R}^{\prime} =\frac{{V}^{\prime}}{{I}}-R {/tex}
{tex} =\frac{250}{25 \times 10^{-3}}-1000 {/tex}
{tex} =9000 \Omega {/tex}
This {tex}9000 \Omega{/tex} is in series with voltmeter.

question_html:
A rectangular loop carries a current of 1 A. A straight long wire carrying 2 A current is kept near the loop in the same plane as shown in the figure.

Find:

  1. the torque acting on the loop, and
  2. the magnitude and direction of the net force on the loop.
question_text: A rectangular loop carries a current of 1 A. A straight long wire carrying 2 A current is kept near the loop in the same plane as shown in the figure. Find: the torque acting on the loop, and the magnitude and direction of the net force on the loop.
explanation: {tex}\tau=m B \sin \theta{/tex} As {tex}\vec{m}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{B}{/tex} are in same direction, {tex}\theta=0^{0}{/tex} {tex}\tau=0{/tex} {tex}F=\frac{\mu_{0} I_{1} I_{2} l}{2 \pi r}{/tex} {tex}F_{n e t} =\frac{\mu_{0} I_{1} I_{2} l}{2 \pi}\left(\frac{1}{r_{1}}-\frac{1}{r_{2}}\right){/tex} {tex} =\frac{4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times 2 \times 1 \times 5 \times 10^{-2}}{2 \pi \times 10^{-2}}\left(1-\frac{1}{2}\right) {/tex} {tex}F_{n e t} =1 \times 10^{-6} {~N}{/tex} Net force on the loop is towards the long straight wire
solution: {tex}\tau=m B \sin \theta{/tex} As {tex}\vec{m}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{B}{/tex} are in same direction, {tex}\theta=0^{0}{/tex} {tex}\tau=0{/tex} {tex}F=\frac{\mu_{0} I_{1} I_{2} l}{2 \pi r}{/tex} {tex}F_{n e t} =\frac{\mu_{0} I_{1} I_{2} l}{2 \pi}\left(\frac{1}{r_{1}}-\frac{1}{r_{2}}\right){/tex} {tex} =\frac{4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times 2 \times 1 \times 5 \times 10^{-2}}{2 \pi \times 10^{-2}}\left(1-\frac{1}{2}\right) {/tex} {tex}F_{n e t} =1 \times 10^{-6} {~N}{/tex} Net force on the loop is towards the long straight wire
solution_html:
1. {tex}\tau=m B \sin \theta{/tex}
    As {tex}\vec{m}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{B}{/tex} are in same direction, {tex}\theta=0^{0}{/tex}
    {tex}\tau=0{/tex}
  2. {tex}F=\frac{\mu_{0} I_{1} I_{2} l}{2 \pi r}{/tex}
    {tex}F_{n e t} =\frac{\mu_{0} I_{1} I_{2} l}{2 \pi}\left(\frac{1}{r_{1}}-\frac{1}{r_{2}}\right){/tex}
    {tex} =\frac{4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times 2 \times 1 \times 5 \times 10^{-2}}{2 \pi \times 10^{-2}}\left(1-\frac{1}{2}\right) {/tex}
    {tex}F_{n e t} =1 \times 10^{-6} {~N}{/tex}
    Net force on the loop is towards the long straight wire

question_html:
1. A charged particle {tex}q{/tex} moving with a velocity {tex}\vec{v}{/tex} is subjected to a uniform magnetic field {tex}\overrightarrow{{B}}{/tex} acting perpendicular to {tex}\overrightarrow{{v}}{/tex}. If a uniform electric field {tex}\vec{E}{/tex} is also set up in the region along the direction of {tex}\overrightarrow{{B}}{/tex}, describe the path followed by the particle and draw its shape.
  2. How will the magnetic field inside a long solenoid be affected when:
    1. the radius of the turns of the solenoid is increased,
    2. the length of solenoid as well as the total number of its turns are doubled?
question_text: A charged particle {tex}q{/tex} moving with a velocity {tex}\vec{v}{/tex} is subjected to a uniform magnetic field {tex}\overrightarrow{{B}}{/tex} acting perpendicular to {tex}\overrightarrow{{v}}{/tex} . If a uniform electric field {tex}\vec{E}{/tex} is also set up in the region along the direction of {tex}\overrightarrow{{B}}{/tex} , describe the path followed by the particle and draw its shape. How will the magnetic field inside a long solenoid be affected when: the radius of the turns of the solenoid is increased, the length of solenoid as well as the total number of its turns are doubled?
explanation: Due to magnetic field particle will follow circular path and due to electric field, particle will accelerate along the electric field. As a result particle will follow a helical path with constant radius but increasing pitch. {tex}{B}=\frac{\mu_{{o}} {NI}}{l}{/tex} No change {tex}\frac{{N}}{l}=\frac{2 {~N}}{2 l}{/tex} No Change
solution: Due to magnetic field particle will follow circular path and due to electric field, particle will accelerate along the electric field. As a result particle will follow a helical path with constant radius but increasing pitch. {tex}{B}=\frac{\mu_{{o}} {NI}}{l}{/tex} No change {tex}\frac{{N}}{l}=\frac{2 {~N}}{2 l}{/tex} No Change
solution_html:
1. Due to magnetic field particle will follow circular path and due to electric field, particle will accelerate along the electric field.
    As a result particle will follow a helical path with constant radius but increasing pitch.
    1. {tex}{B}=\frac{\mu_{{o}} {NI}}{l}{/tex} No change
    2. {tex}\frac{{N}}{l}=\frac{2 {~N}}{2 l}{/tex} No Change

question_html: Two infinitely long conductors kept along {tex}{XX}^{\prime}{/tex} and {tex}{YY}^{\prime}{/tex} axes are carrying current {tex}I_1{/tex} and {tex}I_2{/tex} along {tex}-X{/tex} axis and {tex}-Y{/tex} axis respectively. Find the magnitude and direction of the net magnetic field produced at point {tex}{P}({X}, {Y}){/tex}.
question_text: Two infinitely long conductors kept along {tex}{XX}^{\prime}{/tex} and {tex}{YY}^{\prime}{/tex} axes are carrying current {tex}I_1{/tex} and {tex}I_2{/tex} along {tex}-X{/tex} axis and {tex}-Y{/tex} axis respectively. Find the magnitude and direction of the net magnetic field produced at point {tex}{P}({X}, {Y}){/tex} .
explanation: Magnetic field due to conductor carrying current {tex}{I}_1\left(\overrightarrow{{~B}}_1\right)=\frac{\mu_0 {I}_1}{2 \pi {Y}}(-\hat{k}){/tex} Magnetic field due to conductor Carrying current {tex}{I}_2\left(\overrightarrow{{~B}}_2\right)=\frac{\mu_0 {I}_2}{2 \pi {X}}(\hat{k}){/tex} {tex} \overrightarrow{{B}}_{{P}}=\overrightarrow{{B}}_1+\overrightarrow{{B}}_2 {/tex} {tex} \overrightarrow{{~B}}_{{P}}=\frac{\mu_0}{2 \pi}\left[\frac{{I}_2}{{X}}-\frac{{I}_1}{{Y}}\right] \hat{k} {/tex} Direction will be along the Z-axis.
solution: Magnetic field due to conductor carrying current {tex}{I}_1\left(\overrightarrow{{~B}}_1\right)=\frac{\mu_0 {I}_1}{2 \pi {Y}}(-\hat{k}){/tex} Magnetic field due to conductor Carrying current {tex}{I}_2\left(\overrightarrow{{~B}}_2\right)=\frac{\mu_0 {I}_2}{2 \pi {X}}(\hat{k}){/tex} {tex} \overrightarrow{{B}}_{{P}}=\overrightarrow{{B}}_1+\overrightarrow{{B}}_2 {/tex} {tex} \overrightarrow{{~B}}_{{P}}=\frac{\mu_0}{2 \pi}\left[\frac{{I}_2}{{X}}-\frac{{I}_1}{{Y}}\right] \hat{k} {/tex} Direction will be along the Z-axis.
solution_html:
Magnetic field due to conductor carrying current {tex}{I}_1\left(\overrightarrow{{~B}}_1\right)=\frac{\mu_0 {I}_1}{2 \pi {Y}}(-\hat{k}){/tex}
Magnetic field due to conductor Carrying current {tex}{I}_2\left(\overrightarrow{{~B}}_2\right)=\frac{\mu_0 {I}_2}{2 \pi {X}}(\hat{k}){/tex}
{tex} \overrightarrow{{B}}_{{P}}=\overrightarrow{{B}}_1+\overrightarrow{{B}}_2 {/tex}
{tex} \overrightarrow{{~B}}_{{P}}=\frac{\mu_0}{2 \pi}\left[\frac{{I}_2}{{X}}-\frac{{I}_1}{{Y}}\right] \hat{k} {/tex}
Direction will be along the Z-axis.

The particle remains at a distance d from the conductor.

question_text: In a region of a uniform electric field {tex}\vec{E}{/tex} , a negatively charged particle is moving with a constant velocity {tex}\vec{v}=-v_0 \hat{i}{/tex} near a long straight conductor coinciding with {tex}{XX}^{\prime}{/tex} axis and carrying current I towards -X axis. The particle remains at a distance d from the conductor. Draw diagram showing direction of electric and magnetic fields. What are the various forces acting on the charged particle? Find the value of {tex}v_{{o}}{/tex} in terms of {tex}{E}, {d}{/tex} and I.
explanation: Electric force Magnetic force {tex} {e} v_o {~B}={eE} {/tex} {tex} v_o {\times}\left[\frac{\mu_o {I}}{2 \pi {~d}}\right]={E} {/tex} {tex} v_o=\frac{(2 \pi {~d}) {E}}{\mu_o {I}} {/tex}
solution: Electric force Magnetic force {tex} {e} v_o {~B}={eE} {/tex} {tex} v_o {\times}\left[\frac{\mu_o {I}}{2 \pi {~d}}\right]={E} {/tex} {tex} v_o=\frac{(2 \pi {~d}) {E}}{\mu_o {I}} {/tex}
solution_html:
2. Electric force
    Magnetic force
  3. {tex} {e} v_o {~B}={eE} {/tex}
    {tex} v_o {\times}\left[\frac{\mu_o {I}}{2 \pi {~d}}\right]={E} {/tex}
    {tex} v_o=\frac{(2 \pi {~d}) {E}}{\mu_o {I}} {/tex}

question_html: A current of 5 A is passing along +X direction through a wire lying along X -axis. Find the magnetic field {tex}\overrightarrow{{B}}{/tex} at a point {tex}\vec{r}=(3 \hat{i}+4 \hat{j}) {m}{/tex} due to 1 cm element of the wire, centered at origin.
question_text: A current of 5 A is passing along +X direction through a wire lying along X -axis. Find the magnetic field {tex}\overrightarrow{{B}}{/tex} at a point {tex}\vec{r}=(3 \hat{i}+4 \hat{j}) {m}{/tex} due to 1 cm element of the wire, centered at origin.
explanation: Using Biot - Savart's law {tex} \vec{B} =\frac{\mu_0}{4 \pi} \frac{I \overrightarrow{d l} \times \vec{r}}{r^3} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{\left(10^{-7}\right)\left[5\left(10^{-2}\right) \hat{{i}} \times(3 \hat{{i}}+4 \hat{{j}})\right]}{5^3} {/tex} {tex} =1.6 \times 10^{-10} \hat{k} T {/tex}
solution: Using Biot - Savart's law {tex} \vec{B} =\frac{\mu_0}{4 \pi} \frac{I \overrightarrow{d l} \times \vec{r}}{r^3} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{\left(10^{-7}\right)\left[5\left(10^{-2}\right) \hat{{i}} \times(3 \hat{{i}}+4 \hat{{j}})\right]}{5^3} {/tex} {tex} =1.6 \times 10^{-10} \hat{k} T {/tex}
solution_html:
Using Biot - Savart's law
{tex} \vec{B} =\frac{\mu_0}{4 \pi} \frac{I \overrightarrow{d l} \times \vec{r}}{r^3} {/tex}
{tex} =\frac{\left(10^{-7}\right)\left[5\left(10^{-2}\right) \hat{{i}} \times(3 \hat{{i}}+4 \hat{{j}})\right]}{5^3} {/tex}
{tex} =1.6 \times 10^{-10} \hat{k} T {/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html:
A particle of charge {tex}q{/tex} is moving with a velocity {tex}\vec{v}{/tex} at a distance '{tex}d{/tex}' from a long straight wire carrying a current 'I' as shown in figure. At this instant, it is subjected to a uniform electric field {tex}\vec{E}{/tex} such that the particle keeps moving undeviated. In terms of unit vectors {tex}\hat{i}, \hat{j}{/tex} and {tex}\hat{k}{/tex}, find-

  1. the magnetic field {tex}\overrightarrow{{B}}{/tex},
  2. the magnetic force {tex}\overrightarrow{{F}}_{{m}}{/tex}, and
  3. the electric field {tex}\overrightarrow{{E}}{/tex}, acting on the charge.
question_text: A particle of charge {tex}q{/tex} is moving with a velocity {tex}\vec{v}{/tex} at a distance ' {tex}d{/tex} ' from a long straight wire carrying a current 'I' as shown in figure. At this instant, it is subjected to a uniform electric field {tex}\vec{E}{/tex} such that the particle keeps moving undeviated. In terms of unit vectors {tex}\hat{i}, \hat{j}{/tex} and {tex}\hat{k}{/tex} , find- the magnetic field {tex}\overrightarrow{{B}}{/tex} , the magnetic force {tex}\overrightarrow{{F}}_{{m}}{/tex} , and the electric field {tex}\overrightarrow{{E}}{/tex} , acting on the charge.
explanation: {tex}\vec{B}=\frac{\mu_{o} I}{2 \pi d}(-\hat{K}){/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow{{F}}_{{B}}={q}(\overrightarrow{{v}} \times \overrightarrow{{B}})=\frac{{qv} \mu_{{o}} {I}}{2 \pi {~d}}(-\hat{{j}}){/tex} {tex}{q} \overrightarrow{{F}}_{{e}}=-\overrightarrow{{F}}_{{B}}{/tex} (For undeviation of charge particle) {tex}\therefore \overrightarrow{{F}}_{{e}}=\frac{{qv} \mu_{{o}} {I}}{2 \pi {~d}}(\hat{{j}}){/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow{{F}}_{{e}}={q} \overrightarrow{{E}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \overrightarrow{{E}}=\frac{\mu_{{o}} {vI}}{2 \pi {~d}} \hat{{j}}{/tex}
solution: {tex}\vec{B}=\frac{\mu_{o} I}{2 \pi d}(-\hat{K}){/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow{{F}}_{{B}}={q}(\overrightarrow{{v}} \times \overrightarrow{{B}})=\frac{{qv} \mu_{{o}} {I}}{2 \pi {~d}}(-\hat{{j}}){/tex} {tex}{q} \overrightarrow{{F}}_{{e}}=-\overrightarrow{{F}}_{{B}}{/tex} (For undeviation of charge particle) {tex}\therefore \overrightarrow{{F}}_{{e}}=\frac{{qv} \mu_{{o}} {I}}{2 \pi {~d}}(\hat{{j}}){/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow{{F}}_{{e}}={q} \overrightarrow{{E}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \overrightarrow{{E}}=\frac{\mu_{{o}} {vI}}{2 \pi {~d}} \hat{{j}}{/tex}
solution_html:
1. {tex}\vec{B}=\frac{\mu_{o} I}{2 \pi d}(-\hat{K}){/tex}
  2. {tex}\overrightarrow{{F}}_{{B}}={q}(\overrightarrow{{v}} \times \overrightarrow{{B}})=\frac{{qv} \mu_{{o}} {I}}{2 \pi {~d}}(-\hat{{j}}){/tex}
  3. {tex}{q} \overrightarrow{{F}}_{{e}}=-\overrightarrow{{F}}_{{B}}{/tex} (For undeviation of charge particle)
    {tex}\therefore \overrightarrow{{F}}_{{e}}=\frac{{qv} \mu_{{o}} {I}}{2 \pi {~d}}(\hat{{j}}){/tex}
    {tex}\overrightarrow{{F}}_{{e}}={q} \overrightarrow{{E}}{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore \overrightarrow{{E}}=\frac{\mu_{{o}} {vI}}{2 \pi {~d}} \hat{{j}}{/tex}

question_html:
1. Use Ampere's law to derive the expression for the magnetic field due to a long straight current carrying wire of infinite length.
  2. Why is Ampere's law used for the derivation in (a) above and not Biot-Savart's law? Explain.
question_text: Use Ampere's law to derive the expression for the magnetic field due to a long straight current carrying wire of infinite length. Why is Ampere's law used for the derivation in (a) above and not Biot-Savart's law? Explain.
explanation: Consider an amperian loop of radius R around the wire carrying current I. {tex} \phi \vec{B} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l} =\mu_o i {/tex} {tex} B \times 2 \pi r =\mu_o i {/tex} {tex} B =\frac{\mu_o i}{2 \pi r} {/tex} The expression for magnetic field due to a long straight current carrying infinite wire can be obtained easily using Ampere's circuital law whereas Biot- Savart's law requires lengthy calculation.
solution: Consider an amperian loop of radius R around the wire carrying current I. {tex} \phi \vec{B} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l} =\mu_o i {/tex} {tex} B \times 2 \pi r =\mu_o i {/tex} {tex} B =\frac{\mu_o i}{2 \pi r} {/tex} The expression for magnetic field due to a long straight current carrying infinite wire can be obtained easily using Ampere's circuital law whereas Biot- Savart's law requires lengthy calculation.
solution_html:
1. Consider an amperian loop of radius R around the wire carrying current I.
    {tex} \phi \vec{B} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l} =\mu_o i {/tex}
    {tex} B \times 2 \pi r =\mu_o i {/tex}
    {tex} B =\frac{\mu_o i}{2 \pi r} {/tex}
  2. The expression for magnetic field due to a long straight current carrying infinite wire can be obtained easily using Ampere's circuital law whereas Biot- Savart's law requires lengthy calculation.

question_html: A circular coil of wire having 200 turns, each of radius 4.0 cm is placed in a horizontal plane. It carries a current of 0.40 A in clockwise direction. Find the magnitude and direction of the magnetic field at the centre of the coil.
question_text: A circular coil of wire having 200 turns, each of radius 4.0 cm is placed in a horizontal plane. It carries a current of 0.40 A in clockwise direction. Find the magnitude and direction of the magnetic field at the centre of the coil.
explanation: {tex} B= \frac{\mu_0 N I}{2 r} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times 200 \times 0.40}{2 \times 4 \times 10^{-2}} {/tex} {tex} =1.256 \times 10^{-3} {~T} {/tex} Direction is perpendicularly inward into the horizontal plane.
solution: {tex} B= \frac{\mu_0 N I}{2 r} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times 200 \times 0.40}{2 \times 4 \times 10^{-2}} {/tex} {tex} =1.256 \times 10^{-3} {~T} {/tex} Direction is perpendicularly inward into the horizontal plane.

{tex} B= \frac{\mu_0 N I}{2 r} {/tex}
{tex} =\frac{4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times 200 \times 0.40}{2 \times 4 \times 10^{-2}} {/tex}
{tex} =1.256 \times 10^{-3} {~T} {/tex}

question_html:
1. Define magnetic moment of a current-carrying coil. Write its SI unit.
  2. A coil of 60 turns and area {tex}1.5 \times 10^{-3} {~m}^{3}{/tex} carrying 2A current lies in a vertical plane. It experiences a torque of 0.12 Nm when placed in a uniform horizontal magnetic field. The torque acting on the coil changes to 0.05 Nm after the coil is rotated about its diameter by {tex}90^{\circ}{/tex}, in the magnetic field. Find the magnitude of the magnetic field.
question_text: Define magnetic moment of a current-carrying coil. Write its SI unit. A coil of 60 turns and area {tex}1.5 \times 10^{-3} {~m}^{3}{/tex} carrying 2A current lies in a vertical plane. It experiences a torque of 0.12 Nm when placed in a uniform horizontal magnetic field. The torque acting on the coil changes to 0.05 Nm after the coil is rotated about its diameter by {tex}90^{\circ}{/tex} , in the magnetic field. Find the magnitude of the magnetic field.
explanation: Magnetic moment of a current carrying coil is defined as the product of current flowing through the coil and Area of the coil. {tex}\tau={/tex} NIAB {tex}\sin \theta{/tex} {tex}\tau_{1}=0.12=60 \times 2 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-3} \times \operatorname{Bsin} \theta{/tex} {tex}{B} \sin \theta=\frac{2}{3}{/tex} {tex}\tau_{2}=0.05=60 \times 2 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-3} \times {B} \cos \theta{/tex} {tex}{B} \cos \theta=\frac{5}{18}{/tex} {tex}{B}=\sqrt{{B}^{2} \sin ^{2} \theta+{B}^{2} \cos ^{2} \theta}{/tex} {tex}=\sqrt{\left(\frac{2}{3}\right)^{2}+\left(\frac{5}{18}\right)^{2}}=\frac{13}{18} {~T}{/tex}
solution: Magnetic moment of a current carrying coil is defined as the product of current flowing through the coil and Area of the coil. {tex}\tau={/tex} NIAB {tex}\sin \theta{/tex} {tex}\tau_{1}=0.12=60 \times 2 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-3} \times \operatorname{Bsin} \theta{/tex} {tex}{B} \sin \theta=\frac{2}{3}{/tex} {tex}\tau_{2}=0.05=60 \times 2 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-3} \times {B} \cos \theta{/tex} {tex}{B} \cos \theta=\frac{5}{18}{/tex} {tex}{B}=\sqrt{{B}^{2} \sin ^{2} \theta+{B}^{2} \cos ^{2} \theta}{/tex} {tex}=\sqrt{\left(\frac{2}{3}\right)^{2}+\left(\frac{5}{18}\right)^{2}}=\frac{13}{18} {~T}{/tex}
solution_html:
1. Magnetic moment of a current carrying coil is defined as the product of current flowing through the coil and Area of the coil.
  2. {tex}\tau={/tex} NIAB {tex}\sin \theta{/tex}
    {tex}\tau_{1}=0.12=60 \times 2 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-3} \times \operatorname{Bsin} \theta{/tex}
    {tex}{B} \sin \theta=\frac{2}{3}{/tex}
    {tex}\tau_{2}=0.05=60 \times 2 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-3} \times {B} \cos \theta{/tex}
    {tex}{B} \cos \theta=\frac{5}{18}{/tex}
    {tex}{B}=\sqrt{{B}^{2} \sin ^{2} \theta+{B}^{2} \cos ^{2} \theta}{/tex}
    {tex}=\sqrt{\left(\frac{2}{3}\right)^{2}+\left(\frac{5}{18}\right)^{2}}=\frac{13}{18} {~T}{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. A current carrying loop can be considered as a magnetic dipole placed along its axis. Explain.
  2. Obtain the relation for magnetic dipole moment {tex}\overrightarrow{{M}}{/tex} of current carrying coil. Give the direction of {tex}\overrightarrow{{M}}{/tex}.
  3. A current carrying coil is placed in an external uniform magnetic field. The coil is free to turn in the magnetic field. What is the net force acting on the coil? Obtain the orientation of the coil in stable equilibrium. Show that in this orientation the flux of the total field (field produced by the loop + external field) through the coil is maximum.
question_text: A current carrying loop can be considered as a magnetic dipole placed along its axis. Explain. Obtain the relation for magnetic dipole moment {tex}\overrightarrow{{M}}{/tex} of current carrying coil. Give the direction of {tex}\overrightarrow{{M}}{/tex} . A current carrying coil is placed in an external uniform magnetic field. The coil is free to turn in the magnetic field. What is the net force acting on the coil? Obtain the orientation of the coil in stable equilibrium. Show that in this orientation the flux of the total field (field produced by the loop + external field) through the coil is maximum.
explanation: The two faces of a current carrying loop behave like two poles of a magnet therefore can be considered as a magnetic dipole placed along its axis. Magnetic moment (M) {tex}\alpha{/tex} Current (I) {tex}\alpha{/tex} Area (A) {tex} \therefore \overrightarrow{{M}}={I} \overrightarrow{{~A}} {/tex} Direction is same as the area vector. Net force acting on the coil is zero. The potential energy ( {tex}U_B{/tex} ) of a current carrying loop in an external magnetic field {tex}=-\overrightarrow{{M}} \cdot \overrightarrow{{B}}{/tex} For the coil to be in stable equilibrium {tex}U_B{/tex} should be minimum so {tex}\theta=0^{\circ}{/tex} . Therefore, magnetic flux {tex}(\phi){/tex} due to the total field {tex}=\left({B}_{\text {coil }}+{B}_{\text {ext }}\right) {A}{/tex} , which is its maximum value.
solution: The two faces of a current carrying loop behave like two poles of a magnet therefore can be considered as a magnetic dipole placed along its axis. Magnetic moment (M) {tex}\alpha{/tex} Current (I) {tex}\alpha{/tex} Area (A) {tex} \therefore \overrightarrow{{M}}={I} \overrightarrow{{~A}} {/tex} Direction is same as the area vector. Net force acting on the coil is zero. The potential energy ( {tex}U_B{/tex} ) of a current carrying loop in an external magnetic field {tex}=-\overrightarrow{{M}} \cdot \overrightarrow{{B}}{/tex} For the coil to be in stable equilibrium {tex}U_B{/tex} should be minimum so {tex}\theta=0^{\circ}{/tex} . Therefore, magnetic flux {tex}(\phi){/tex} due to the total field {tex}=\left({B}_{\text {coil }}+{B}_{\text {ext }}\right) {A}{/tex} , which is its maximum value.
solution_html:
1. The two faces of a current carrying loop behave like two poles of a magnet therefore can be considered as a magnetic dipole placed along its axis.
  2. Magnetic moment (M) {tex}\alpha{/tex} Current (I)
    {tex}\alpha{/tex} Area (A)
    {tex} \therefore \overrightarrow{{M}}={I} \overrightarrow{{~A}} {/tex}
    Direction is same as the area vector.
  3. Net force acting on the coil is zero.
    The potential energy ( {tex}U_B{/tex} ) of a current carrying loop in an external magnetic field {tex}=-\overrightarrow{{M}} \cdot \overrightarrow{{B}}{/tex}
    For the coil to be in stable equilibrium {tex}U_B{/tex} should be minimum so {tex}\theta=0^{\circ}{/tex}.
    Therefore, magnetic flux {tex}(\phi){/tex} due to the total field {tex}=\left({B}_{\text {coil }}+{B}_{\text {ext }}\right) {A}{/tex}, which is its maximum value.

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. What is the source of force acting on a current-carrying conductor placed in a magnetic field? Obtain the expression for force acting between two long straight parallel conductors carrying steady currents and hence define 'ampere'.
  2. A point charge {tex}q{/tex} is moving with velocity {tex}\vec{v}{/tex} in a uniform magnetic field {tex}\overrightarrow{{B}}{/tex}. Find the work done by the magnetic force on the charge.
  3. Explain the necessary conditions in which the trajectory of a charged particle is helical in a uniform magnetic field.
question_text: What is the source of force acting on a current-carrying conductor placed in a magnetic field? Obtain the expression for force acting between two long straight parallel conductors carrying steady currents and hence define 'ampere'. A point charge {tex}q{/tex} is moving with velocity {tex}\vec{v}{/tex} in a uniform magnetic field {tex}\overrightarrow{{B}}{/tex} . Find the work done by the magnetic force on the charge. Explain the necessary conditions in which the trajectory of a charged particle is helical in a uniform magnetic field.
explanation: Reason- The source of force is the interaction between the field produced by the current carrying conductor and the external field in which it is placed. Two long parallel conductors a & b, separated by a distance d, carrying currents {tex}{I}_{{a}}{/tex} and {tex}{I}_{{b}}{/tex} , respectively. The magnetic field due to a, {tex}{B}_{{a}}=\frac{\mu_{0} {I}_{{a}}}{2 \pi {~d}}{/tex} The force {tex}{F}_{{ba}}{/tex} , is the force on a segment L of 'b' due to 'a'. {tex}{F}_{{ba}}={I}_{{b}} {LB}_{{a}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\mu_{0} {I}_{{a}} {I}_{{b}}}{2 \pi {~d}} {~L}{/tex} Definition- The 'ampere' is that value of steady current which, when maintained in each of the two very long, straight, parallel conductors of negligible cross-section, and placed one metre apart in vacuum, would produce on each of these conductors a force equal to {tex}2 \times 10^{-7}{/tex} newton per metre of length. Work done by the magnetic force on the charge is zero as force is perpendicular to {tex}\overrightarrow{{v}}{/tex} . The velocity ( {tex}\vec{v}{/tex} ) is at an arbitrary angle {tex}\theta{/tex} w.r.t the magnetic field ( {tex}\vec{B}{/tex} ).
solution: Reason- The source of force is the interaction between the field produced by the current carrying conductor and the external field in which it is placed. Two long parallel conductors a & b, separated by a distance d, carrying currents {tex}{I}_{{a}}{/tex} and {tex}{I}_{{b}}{/tex} , respectively. The magnetic field due to a, {tex}{B}_{{a}}=\frac{\mu_{0} {I}_{{a}}}{2 \pi {~d}}{/tex} The force {tex}{F}_{{ba}}{/tex} , is the force on a segment L of 'b' due to 'a'. {tex}{F}_{{ba}}={I}_{{b}} {LB}_{{a}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\mu_{0} {I}_{{a}} {I}_{{b}}}{2 \pi {~d}} {~L}{/tex} Definition- The 'ampere' is that value of steady current which, when maintained in each of the two very long, straight, parallel conductors of negligible cross-section, and placed one metre apart in vacuum, would produce on each of these conductors a force equal to {tex}2 \times 10^{-7}{/tex} newton per metre of length. Work done by the magnetic force on the charge is zero as force is perpendicular to {tex}\overrightarrow{{v}}{/tex} . The velocity ( {tex}\vec{v}{/tex} ) is at an arbitrary angle {tex}\theta{/tex} w.r.t the magnetic field ( {tex}\vec{B}{/tex} ).
solution_html:
Reason-

  1. The source of force is the interaction between the field produced by the current carrying conductor and the external field in which it is placed.

    Two long parallel conductors a & b, separated by a distance d, carrying currents {tex}{I}_{{a}}{/tex} and {tex}{I}_{{b}}{/tex}, respectively.
    The magnetic field due to a,
    {tex}{B}_{{a}}=\frac{\mu_{0} {I}_{{a}}}{2 \pi {~d}}{/tex}
    The force {tex}{F}_{{ba}}{/tex}, is the force on a segment L of 'b' due to 'a'.
    {tex}{F}_{{ba}}={I}_{{b}} {LB}_{{a}}{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{\mu_{0} {I}_{{a}} {I}_{{b}}}{2 \pi {~d}} {~L}{/tex}
    Definition-
    The 'ampere' is that value of steady current which, when maintained in each of the two very long, straight, parallel conductors of negligible cross-section, and placed one metre apart in vacuum, would produce on each of these conductors a force equal to {tex}2 \times 10^{-7}{/tex} newton per metre of length.
  2. Work done by the magnetic force on the charge is zero as force is perpendicular to {tex}\overrightarrow{{v}}{/tex}.
  3. The velocity ({tex}\vec{v}{/tex}) is at an arbitrary angle {tex}\theta{/tex} w.r.t the magnetic field ({tex}\vec{B}{/tex}).

question_html:
1. Write vector form of Biot-Savart law.
  2. Two insulated long straight wires, each carrying {tex}2 \cdot 0{/tex} A current are kept along {tex}{xx}^{\prime}{/tex} and {tex}{yy}^{\prime}{/tex} axis as shown in the figure. Find the magnitude and direction of resultant magnetic field at point {tex}{P}(4 {~m}, 5 {~m}){/tex}.
question_text: Write vector form of Biot-Savart law. Two insulated long straight wires, each carrying {tex}2 \cdot 0{/tex} A current are kept along {tex}{xx}^{\prime}{/tex} and {tex}{yy}^{\prime}{/tex} axis as shown in the figure. Find the magnitude and direction of resultant magnetic field at point {tex}{P}(4 {~m}, 5 {~m}){/tex} .
explanation: {tex}{d} \vec{B}=\frac{\mu_{{o}}}{4 \pi} \frac{{I}(\overrightarrow{{d}} \times \overrightarrow{{r}})}{{r}^{3}}{/tex} As {tex}B=\frac{\mu_{{o}}}{4 \pi} \frac{2 {I}}{{a}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore {B}_{1}={/tex} Magnetic field due to current carrying wire along {tex}{X} X^{\prime}{/tex} {tex}\vec{B}_{1}=\frac{\mu_{{o}}}{4 \pi} \frac{2 {I}}{{a}}=\frac{2 \times 2 \times 10^{-7}}{5}=\left(8 \times 10^{-8} {~T}\right)(\hat{{k}}){/tex} {tex}{B}_{2}={/tex} Magnetic field due to current carrying wire along {tex}Y Y^{\prime}{/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow{{B}}_{2}=\frac{\mu_{{o}}}{4 \pi} \frac{2 {I}}{{a}}=\frac{2 \times 2 \times 10^{-7}}{4}=10 \times 10^{-8} {~T}(-\hat{{k}}){/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow{{B}}_{\text {net }}=\overrightarrow{{B}}_{2}+\overrightarrow{{B}}_{1}{/tex} {tex}=2 \times 10^{-8} T(-\hat{k}){/tex} Direction of resultant magnetic field along-Z axis.
solution: {tex}{d} \vec{B}=\frac{\mu_{{o}}}{4 \pi} \frac{{I}(\overrightarrow{{d}} \times \overrightarrow{{r}})}{{r}^{3}}{/tex} As {tex}B=\frac{\mu_{{o}}}{4 \pi} \frac{2 {I}}{{a}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore {B}_{1}={/tex} Magnetic field due to current carrying wire along {tex}{X} X^{\prime}{/tex} {tex}\vec{B}_{1}=\frac{\mu_{{o}}}{4 \pi} \frac{2 {I}}{{a}}=\frac{2 \times 2 \times 10^{-7}}{5}=\left(8 \times 10^{-8} {~T}\right)(\hat{{k}}){/tex} {tex}{B}_{2}={/tex} Magnetic field due to current carrying wire along {tex}Y Y^{\prime}{/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow{{B}}_{2}=\frac{\mu_{{o}}}{4 \pi} \frac{2 {I}}{{a}}=\frac{2 \times 2 \times 10^{-7}}{4}=10 \times 10^{-8} {~T}(-\hat{{k}}){/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow{{B}}_{\text {net }}=\overrightarrow{{B}}_{2}+\overrightarrow{{B}}_{1}{/tex} {tex}=2 \times 10^{-8} T(-\hat{k}){/tex} Direction of resultant magnetic field along-Z axis.
solution_html:
1. {tex}{d} \vec{B}=\frac{\mu_{{o}}}{4 \pi} \frac{{I}(\overrightarrow{{d}} \times \overrightarrow{{r}})}{{r}^{3}}{/tex}
  2. As {tex}B=\frac{\mu_{{o}}}{4 \pi} \frac{2 {I}}{{a}}{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore {B}_{1}={/tex} Magnetic field due to current carrying wire along {tex}{X} X^{\prime}{/tex}
    {tex}\vec{B}_{1}=\frac{\mu_{{o}}}{4 \pi} \frac{2 {I}}{{a}}=\frac{2 \times 2 \times 10^{-7}}{5}=\left(8 \times 10^{-8} {~T}\right)(\hat{{k}}){/tex}
    {tex}{B}_{2}={/tex} Magnetic field due to current carrying wire along {tex}Y Y^{\prime}{/tex}
    {tex}\overrightarrow{{B}}_{2}=\frac{\mu_{{o}}}{4 \pi} \frac{2 {I}}{{a}}=\frac{2 \times 2 \times 10^{-7}}{4}=10 \times 10^{-8} {~T}(-\hat{{k}}){/tex}
    {tex}\overrightarrow{{B}}_{\text {net }}=\overrightarrow{{B}}_{2}+\overrightarrow{{B}}_{1}{/tex} {tex}=2 \times 10^{-8} T(-\hat{k}){/tex}
    Direction of resultant magnetic field along-Z axis.

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. Derive an expression for the torque acting on a rectangular current loop suspended in a uniform magnetic field.
  2. A charged particle is moving in a circular path with velocity {tex}\vec{V}{/tex} in a uniform magnetic field {tex}\overrightarrow{{B}}{/tex}. It is made to pass through a sheet of lead and as a consequence, it looses one half of its kinetic energy without change in its direction. How will (1) the radius of its path (2) its time period of revolution change?
question_text: Derive an expression for the torque acting on a rectangular current loop suspended in a uniform magnetic field. A charged particle is moving in a circular path with velocity {tex}\vec{V}{/tex} in a uniform magnetic field {tex}\overrightarrow{{B}}{/tex} . It is made to pass through a sheet of lead and as a consequence, it looses one half of its kinetic energy without change in its direction. How will (1) the radius of its path (2) its time period of revolution change?
explanation: {tex}\vec{F}_{1}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{F}_{2}{/tex} are the forces acting on two arms of the rectangular coil having sides a and b. {tex}\left|\vec{F}_{1}\right|=\left|\vec{F}_{2}\right|=I b B {/tex} (b = length of the arm {tex}){/tex} Forces constitute a couple. The magnitude of Torque on the loop is- {tex}\tau =F_{1} \frac{a}{2} \sin \theta+F_{2} \frac{a}{2} \sin \theta{/tex} {tex}=I a b B \sin \theta{/tex} {tex}=I A B \sin \theta{/tex} {tex}\vec{\tau} =I \vec{A} \times \vec{B}{/tex} {tex}r=\frac{m v}{q B}=\frac{\sqrt{2 m K}}{q B}{/tex} {tex}r \quad \alpha \sqrt{\mathrm{~K}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{\mathrm{r}^{\prime}}{\mathrm{r}}=\frac{\sqrt{\mathrm{K} / 2}}{\sqrt{\mathrm{~K}}}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex} {tex}{r}^{\prime}=\frac{{r}}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex} {tex}{T}=\frac{2 \pi {~m}}{{qB}}{/tex} Time period does not depend on Kinetic Energy {tex}\therefore{/tex} Time period will not change.
solution: {tex}\vec{F}_{1}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{F}_{2}{/tex} are the forces acting on two arms of the rectangular coil having sides a and b. {tex}\left|\vec{F}_{1}\right|=\left|\vec{F}_{2}\right|=I b B {/tex} (b = length of the arm {tex}){/tex} Forces constitute a couple. The magnitude of Torque on the loop is- {tex}\tau =F_{1} \frac{a}{2} \sin \theta+F_{2} \frac{a}{2} \sin \theta{/tex} {tex}=I a b B \sin \theta{/tex} {tex}=I A B \sin \theta{/tex} {tex}\vec{\tau} =I \vec{A} \times \vec{B}{/tex} {tex}r=\frac{m v}{q B}=\frac{\sqrt{2 m K}}{q B}{/tex} {tex}r \quad \alpha \sqrt{\mathrm{~K}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{\mathrm{r}^{\prime}}{\mathrm{r}}=\frac{\sqrt{\mathrm{K} / 2}}{\sqrt{\mathrm{~K}}}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex} {tex}{r}^{\prime}=\frac{{r}}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex} {tex}{T}=\frac{2 \pi {~m}}{{qB}}{/tex} Time period does not depend on Kinetic Energy {tex}\therefore{/tex} Time period will not change.
solution_html:
1. {tex}\vec{F}_{1}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{F}_{2}{/tex} are the forces acting on two arms of the rectangular coil having sides a and b.
    {tex}\left|\vec{F}_{1}\right|=\left|\vec{F}_{2}\right|=I b B {/tex} (b = length of the arm{tex}){/tex}
    Forces constitute a couple. The magnitude of Torque on the loop is-
    {tex}\tau =F_{1} \frac{a}{2} \sin \theta+F_{2} \frac{a}{2} \sin \theta{/tex}
    {tex}=I a b B \sin \theta{/tex}
    {tex}=I A B \sin \theta{/tex}
    {tex}\vec{\tau} =I \vec{A} \times \vec{B}{/tex}
    1. {tex}r=\frac{m v}{q B}=\frac{\sqrt{2 m K}}{q B}{/tex}
      {tex}r \quad \alpha \sqrt{\mathrm{~K}}{/tex}
      {tex}\frac{\mathrm{r}^{\prime}}{\mathrm{r}}=\frac{\sqrt{\mathrm{K} / 2}}{\sqrt{\mathrm{~K}}}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex}
      {tex}{r}^{\prime}=\frac{{r}}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex}
    2. {tex}{T}=\frac{2 \pi {~m}}{{qB}}{/tex}
      Time period does not depend on Kinetic Energy
      {tex}\therefore{/tex} Time period will not change.

subject: Physics
question_html: Using Biot-Savart law, derive expression for the magnetic field {tex}(\overrightarrow{{B}}){/tex} due to a circular current carrying loop at a point on its axis and hence at its centre.
question_text: Using Biot-Savart law, derive expression for the magnetic field {tex}(\overrightarrow{{B}}){/tex} due to a circular current carrying loop at a point on its axis and hence at its centre.
explanation: From Biot Savart's Law {tex}|d \vec{B}|=\frac{\mu_{\circ}}{4 \pi} \frac{I|d \vec{l} \times \vec{r}|}{r^{3}}{/tex} Now {tex}{r}^{2}={x}^{2}+{R}^{2}{/tex} Because {tex}|d \vec{l} \times \vec{r}|=r d l{/tex} {tex}\therefore d B=\frac{\mu_{\circ}}{4 \pi} \frac{i d l}{\left(x^{2}+R^{2}\right)}{/tex} {tex}d \vec{B}{/tex} has two components. All the components perpendicular to x-axis are summed over and we obtain a null result. Only x - components contribute. The net contribution along x-direction {tex}d B_{x}=d B \cos \theta{/tex} {tex}\cos \theta=\frac{R}{\left(R^{2}+x^{2}\right)^{\frac{1}{2}}}{/tex} Thus: {tex}d B_{x}=\frac{\mu_{0} i}{4 \pi} d l \frac{R}{\left(R^{2}+x^{2}\right)^{\frac{3}{2}}}{/tex} Summing {tex}d B_{x}{/tex} over the entire loop {tex}\oint d l=2 \pi R{/tex} {tex}\vec{B}=B_{x} \hat{i}=\frac{\mu_{0} i R^{2}}{2\left(x^{2}+R^{2}\right)^{\frac{3}{2}}} \hat{i}{/tex} Magnetic field at the centre of the loop- Here {tex}{x}=0{/tex} {tex}\therefore \vec{B}=\frac{\mu_{{o}} i}{2 R} \hat{i}{/tex}
solution: From Biot Savart's Law {tex}|d \vec{B}|=\frac{\mu_{\circ}}{4 \pi} \frac{I|d \vec{l} \times \vec{r}|}{r^{3}}{/tex} Now {tex}{r}^{2}={x}^{2}+{R}^{2}{/tex} Because {tex}|d \vec{l} \times \vec{r}|=r d l{/tex} {tex}\therefore d B=\frac{\mu_{\circ}}{4 \pi} \frac{i d l}{\left(x^{2}+R^{2}\right)}{/tex} {tex}d \vec{B}{/tex} has two components. All the components perpendicular to x-axis are summed over and we obtain a null result. Only x - components contribute. The net contribution along x-direction {tex}d B_{x}=d B \cos \theta{/tex} {tex}\cos \theta=\frac{R}{\left(R^{2}+x^{2}\right)^{\frac{1}{2}}}{/tex} Thus: {tex}d B_{x}=\frac{\mu_{0} i}{4 \pi} d l \frac{R}{\left(R^{2}+x^{2}\right)^{\frac{3}{2}}}{/tex} Summing {tex}d B_{x}{/tex} over the entire loop {tex}\oint d l=2 \pi R{/tex} {tex}\vec{B}=B_{x} \hat{i}=\frac{\mu_{0} i R^{2}}{2\left(x^{2}+R^{2}\right)^{\frac{3}{2}}} \hat{i}{/tex} Magnetic field at the centre of the loop- Here {tex}{x}=0{/tex} {tex}\therefore \vec{B}=\frac{\mu_{{o}} i}{2 R} \hat{i}{/tex}
solution_html:
From Biot Savart's Law
{tex}|d \vec{B}|=\frac{\mu_{\circ}}{4 \pi} \frac{I|d \vec{l} \times \vec{r}|}{r^{3}}{/tex}
Now {tex}{r}^{2}={x}^{2}+{R}^{2}{/tex}
Because {tex}|d \vec{l} \times \vec{r}|=r d l{/tex}
{tex}\therefore d B=\frac{\mu_{\circ}}{4 \pi} \frac{i d l}{\left(x^{2}+R^{2}\right)}{/tex}
{tex}d \vec{B}{/tex} has two components.
All the components perpendicular to x-axis are summed over and we obtain a null result.
Only x - components contribute. The net contribution along x-direction
{tex}d B_{x}=d B \cos \theta{/tex}
{tex}\cos \theta=\frac{R}{\left(R^{2}+x^{2}\right)^{\frac{1}{2}}}{/tex}
Thus:
{tex}d B_{x}=\frac{\mu_{0} i}{4 \pi} d l \frac{R}{\left(R^{2}+x^{2}\right)^{\frac{3}{2}}}{/tex}
Summing {tex}d B_{x}{/tex} over the entire loop
{tex}\oint d l=2 \pi R{/tex}
{tex}\vec{B}=B_{x} \hat{i}=\frac{\mu_{0} i R^{2}}{2\left(x^{2}+R^{2}\right)^{\frac{3}{2}}} \hat{i}{/tex}
Magnetic field at the centre of the loop-
Here {tex}{x}=0{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \vec{B}=\frac{\mu_{{o}} i}{2 R} \hat{i}{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html:
A rectangular loop ({tex}1 {~cm} \times 5 {~cm}{/tex}) of wire carrying current of 0.4 A is placed in a magnetic field {tex}\vec{B}=(0.03 \hat{i}+0.04 \hat{k}) {T}{/tex} such that the outward unit vector normal to the plane of the loop is {tex}(0.60 \hat{i}-0.80 \hat{j}){/tex}. Calculate:

  1. the magnetic moment {tex}\vec{m}{/tex} of the loop, and
  2. the torque {tex}\vec{\tau}{/tex} acting on the loop.
question_text: A rectangular loop ( {tex}1 {~cm} \times 5 {~cm}{/tex} ) of wire carrying current of 0.4 A is placed in a magnetic field {tex}\vec{B}=(0.03 \hat{i}+0.04 \hat{k}) {T}{/tex} such that the outward unit vector normal to the plane of the loop is {tex}(0.60 \hat{i}-0.80 \hat{j}){/tex} . Calculate: the magnetic moment {tex}\vec{m}{/tex} of the loop, and the torque {tex}\vec{\tau}{/tex} acting on the loop.
explanation: {tex} \overrightarrow{{m}}={I} \overrightarrow{{~A}}={I}|\overrightarrow{{~A}}| \hat{{n}} {/tex} {tex} = 0.25 {~A} \times\left(4 \times 5 \times 10^{-4} {~m}^2\right)[0.8 \hat{{i}}-0.6 \hat{{j}}] {/tex} {tex} = 5 \times 10^{-4}(0.8 \hat{{i}}-0.6 \hat{{j}}) {A} {~m}^2 {/tex} {tex} \overrightarrow{{t}}=\overrightarrow{{m}} \times \overrightarrow{{B}} {/tex} {tex} =5 \times 10^{-4}(0.8 \hat{{i}}-0.6 \hat{{j}}) \times(0.2 \hat{{j}}+0.5 \hat{{k}}) {N} {~m} {/tex} {tex} =5 \times 10^{-4}(0.16 \hat{{k}}-0.4 \hat{{j}}-0.3 \hat{{i}}) {N} {~m} {/tex} {tex} =5 \times 10^{-4}(-0.3 \hat{{i}}-0.4 \hat{{j}}+0.16 \hat{{k}}) {N} {~m} {/tex}
solution: {tex} \overrightarrow{{m}}={I} \overrightarrow{{~A}}={I}|\overrightarrow{{~A}}| \hat{{n}} {/tex} {tex} = 0.25 {~A} \times\left(4 \times 5 \times 10^{-4} {~m}^2\right)[0.8 \hat{{i}}-0.6 \hat{{j}}] {/tex} {tex} = 5 \times 10^{-4}(0.8 \hat{{i}}-0.6 \hat{{j}}) {A} {~m}^2 {/tex} {tex} \overrightarrow{{t}}=\overrightarrow{{m}} \times \overrightarrow{{B}} {/tex} {tex} =5 \times 10^{-4}(0.8 \hat{{i}}-0.6 \hat{{j}}) \times(0.2 \hat{{j}}+0.5 \hat{{k}}) {N} {~m} {/tex} {tex} =5 \times 10^{-4}(0.16 \hat{{k}}-0.4 \hat{{j}}-0.3 \hat{{i}}) {N} {~m} {/tex} {tex} =5 \times 10^{-4}(-0.3 \hat{{i}}-0.4 \hat{{j}}+0.16 \hat{{k}}) {N} {~m} {/tex}
solution_html:
1. {tex} \overrightarrow{{m}}={I} \overrightarrow{{~A}}={I}|\overrightarrow{{~A}}| \hat{{n}} {/tex}
    {tex} = 0.25 {~A} \times\left(4 \times 5 \times 10^{-4} {~m}^2\right)[0.8 \hat{{i}}-0.6 \hat{{j}}] {/tex}
    {tex} = 5 \times 10^{-4}(0.8 \hat{{i}}-0.6 \hat{{j}}) {A} {~m}^2 {/tex}
  2. {tex} \overrightarrow{{t}}=\overrightarrow{{m}} \times \overrightarrow{{B}} {/tex}
    {tex} =5 \times 10^{-4}(0.8 \hat{{i}}-0.6 \hat{{j}}) \times(0.2 \hat{{j}}+0.5 \hat{{k}}) {N} {~m} {/tex}
    {tex} =5 \times 10^{-4}(0.16 \hat{{k}}-0.4 \hat{{j}}-0.3 \hat{{i}}) {N} {~m} {/tex}
    {tex} =5 \times 10^{-4}(-0.3 \hat{{i}}-0.4 \hat{{j}}+0.16 \hat{{k}}) {N} {~m} {/tex}

question_html:
A circular loop of wire of radius 7.0 cm carrying a current of 1.0 A is placed in a magnetic field {tex}\overrightarrow{{B}}=(0 \cdot 5 \hat{{i}}+0 \cdot 4 \hat{{k}}) {T}{/tex} such that the outward unit vector normal to the plane of the loop is {tex}[0 \cdot 60 \hat{i}+0 \cdot 80 \hat{j}]{/tex}. Calculate:

  1. the magnetic moment {tex}\vec{m}{/tex} of the loop, and
  2. the torque {tex}\vec{\tau}{/tex} acting on the loop. (Take {tex}\pi=\frac{22}{7}{/tex})
question_text: A circular loop of wire of radius 7.0 cm carrying a current of 1.0 A is placed in a magnetic field {tex}\overrightarrow{{B}}=(0 \cdot 5 \hat{{i}}+0 \cdot 4 \hat{{k}}) {T}{/tex} such that the outward unit vector normal to the plane of the loop is {tex}[0 \cdot 60 \hat{i}+0 \cdot 80 \hat{j}]{/tex} . Calculate: the magnetic moment {tex}\vec{m}{/tex} of the loop, and the torque {tex}\vec{\tau}{/tex} acting on the loop. (Take {tex}\pi=\frac{22}{7}{/tex} )
explanation: {tex} \overrightarrow{{m}} ={IA} \hat{{n}}={I} \pi {r}^2 \hat{{n}} {/tex} {tex} =1.0 \times \frac{22}{7} \times 7 \times 7 \times 10^{-4}[0.6 \hat{{i}}+0.8 \hat{{j}}] {/tex} {tex} =154 \times 10^{-4}[0.6 \hat{{i}}+0.8 \hat{{j}}] {/tex} {tex} =(92.4 \hat{{i}}+123.2 \hat{{j}}) \times 10^{-4} {~A} {~m}^2 {/tex} {tex} \vec{\tau} =\overrightarrow{{m}} \times \overrightarrow{{B}} {/tex} {tex} =\left[(92.4 \hat{{i}}+123.2 \hat{{j}}) \times 10^{-4}\right] \times[0.5 \hat{{i}}+0.4 \hat{{k}}] {/tex} {tex} =\left[(49.28 \hat{{i}}-36.96 \hat{{j}}-61.6 \hat{{k}}] \times 10^{-4} {~N} {~m}\right. {/tex}
solution: {tex} \overrightarrow{{m}} ={IA} \hat{{n}}={I} \pi {r}^2 \hat{{n}} {/tex} {tex} =1.0 \times \frac{22}{7} \times 7 \times 7 \times 10^{-4}[0.6 \hat{{i}}+0.8 \hat{{j}}] {/tex} {tex} =154 \times 10^{-4}[0.6 \hat{{i}}+0.8 \hat{{j}}] {/tex} {tex} =(92.4 \hat{{i}}+123.2 \hat{{j}}) \times 10^{-4} {~A} {~m}^2 {/tex} {tex} \vec{\tau} =\overrightarrow{{m}} \times \overrightarrow{{B}} {/tex} {tex} =\left[(92.4 \hat{{i}}+123.2 \hat{{j}}) \times 10^{-4}\right] \times[0.5 \hat{{i}}+0.4 \hat{{k}}] {/tex} {tex} =\left[(49.28 \hat{{i}}-36.96 \hat{{j}}-61.6 \hat{{k}}] \times 10^{-4} {~N} {~m}\right. {/tex}

1. {tex} \overrightarrow{{m}} ={IA} \hat{{n}}={I} \pi {r}^2 \hat{{n}} {/tex}
    {tex} =1.0 \times \frac{22}{7} \times 7 \times 7 \times 10^{-4}[0.6 \hat{{i}}+0.8 \hat{{j}}] {/tex}
    {tex} =154 \times 10^{-4}[0.6 \hat{{i}}+0.8 \hat{{j}}] {/tex}
    {tex} =(92.4 \hat{{i}}+123.2 \hat{{j}}) \times 10^{-4} {~A} {~m}^2 {/tex}
  2. {tex} \vec{\tau} =\overrightarrow{{m}} \times \overrightarrow{{B}} {/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. A proton moving with velocity {tex}\overrightarrow{{V}}{/tex} in a non-uniform magnetic field traces a path as shown in the figure.

    The path followed by the proton is always in the plane of the paper. What is the direction of the magnetic field in the region near points {tex}{P}, {Q}{/tex} and R? What can you say about relative magnitude of magnetic fields at these points?
  2. A current carrying circular loop of area A produces a magnetic field B at its centre. Show that the magnetic moment of the loop is {tex}\frac{2 {BA}}{\mu_{0}} \sqrt{\frac{{~A}}{\pi}}{/tex}.
question_text: A proton moving with velocity {tex}\overrightarrow{{V}}{/tex} in a non-uniform magnetic field traces a path as shown in the figure. The path followed by the proton is always in the plane of the paper. What is the direction of the magnetic field in the region near points {tex}{P}, {Q}{/tex} and R? What can you say about relative magnitude of magnetic fields at these points? A current carrying circular loop of area A produces a magnetic field B at its centre. Show that the magnetic moment of the loop is {tex}\frac{2 {BA}}{\mu_{0}} \sqrt{\frac{{~A}}{\pi}}{/tex} .
explanation: Near point P Magnetic field is acting into the plane of the paper as Force is acting upwards. Near point Q Magnetic field is into the plane of paper as force is acting upwards. Near point R Magnetic field is acting out of the plane of the paper as {tex}\vec{F}{/tex} is acting downwards. Relative Magnitude of the Magnetic field. As B {tex}\alpha \frac{1}{r}{/tex} Therefore, Near point P, magnitude of B is small. Near point {tex}{Q}, {B}{/tex} is relatively smaller than point P. Near point {tex}{R}, {B}{/tex} is relatively larger than point P. ( {tex}{B}_{{Q}} \lt {B}_{{P}} \lt {B}_{{R}}{/tex} ) Let r be the radius of the circular coil and I is the current in the coil then {tex}B =\frac{\mu_{0} I}{2 r} \text { or } I=\frac{2 B r}{\mu_{0}}{/tex} {tex}{A} =\pi {r}^{2}{/tex} {tex} {r}=\sqrt{\frac{A}{\pi}}{/tex} {tex}{M} ={IA}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{2 B r}{\mu_{0}} A{/tex} {tex}=\frac{2 B A}{\mu_{0}} \sqrt{\frac{A}{\pi}}{/tex}
solution: Near point P Magnetic field is acting into the plane of the paper as Force is acting upwards. Near point Q Magnetic field is into the plane of paper as force is acting upwards. Near point R Magnetic field is acting out of the plane of the paper as {tex}\vec{F}{/tex} is acting downwards. Relative Magnitude of the Magnetic field. As B {tex}\alpha \frac{1}{r}{/tex} Therefore, Near point P, magnitude of B is small. Near point {tex}{Q}, {B}{/tex} is relatively smaller than point P. Near point {tex}{R}, {B}{/tex} is relatively larger than point P. ( {tex}{B}_{{Q}} \lt {B}_{{P}} \lt {B}_{{R}}{/tex} ) Let r be the radius of the circular coil and I is the current in the coil then {tex}B =\frac{\mu_{0} I}{2 r} \text { or } I=\frac{2 B r}{\mu_{0}}{/tex} {tex}{A} =\pi {r}^{2}{/tex} {tex} {r}=\sqrt{\frac{A}{\pi}}{/tex} {tex}{M} ={IA}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{2 B r}{\mu_{0}} A{/tex} {tex}=\frac{2 B A}{\mu_{0}} \sqrt{\frac{A}{\pi}}{/tex}
solution_html:
1. Near point P
    Magnetic field is acting into the plane of the paper as Force is acting upwards.
    Near point Q
    Magnetic field is into the plane of paper as force is acting upwards.
    Near point R
    Magnetic field is acting out of the plane of the paper as {tex}\vec{F}{/tex} is acting downwards.
    Relative Magnitude of the Magnetic field.
    As B {tex}\alpha \frac{1}{r}{/tex}
    Therefore,
    Near point P, magnitude of B is small.
    Near point {tex}{Q}, {B}{/tex} is relatively smaller than point P.
    Near point {tex}{R}, {B}{/tex} is relatively larger than point P.
    ({tex}{B}_{{Q}} \lt {B}_{{P}} \lt {B}_{{R}}{/tex})
  2. Let r be the radius of the circular coil and I is the current in the coil then
    {tex}B =\frac{\mu_{0} I}{2 r} \text { or } I=\frac{2 B r}{\mu_{0}}{/tex}
    {tex}{A} =\pi {r}^{2}{/tex} {tex} {r}=\sqrt{\frac{A}{\pi}}{/tex}
    {tex}{M} ={IA}{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{2 B r}{\mu_{0}} A{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{2 B A}{\mu_{0}} \sqrt{\frac{A}{\pi}}{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html:
Read the following paragraph and answer the questions that follow. (4 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 = 4)
A galvanometer is an instrument used to show the direction and strength of the current passing through it. In a galvanometer, a coil placed in a magnetic field experiences a torque and hence gets deflected when a current passes through it. The name is derived from the surname of Italian scientist L. Galvani, who in 1791 discovered that electric current makes a dead frog's leg jerk. A spring attached with the coil provides a counter torque.
In equilibrium, the deflecting torque is balanced by the restoring torque of the spring and we have:
{tex} {NBAI}={k} \phi {/tex}
where N is the total number of turns in the coil
A is the area of cross-section of each turn
{tex}B{/tex} is the radial magnetic field
k is the torsional constant of the spring
{tex}\phi{/tex} is the angular deflection of the coil
As the current ({tex}I_g{/tex}) which produces full scale deflection in the galvanometer is very small, the galvanometer cannot as such be used to measure current in electric circuits. A small resistance, called shunt, of a suitable value is connected with the galvanometer to convert it into an ammeter of desired range. By using a higher resistance, a galvanometer can also be converted into a voltmeter.

  1. The value of the current sensitivity of a galvanometer is given by:
    1. {tex}\frac{{k}}{\text { NBA }}{/tex}
    2. {tex}\frac{\text { NBA }}{{k}}{/tex}
    3. {tex}\frac{{kBA}}{{N}}{/tex}
    4. {tex}\frac{{kNB}}{{A}}{/tex}
  2. A galvanometer of resistance {tex}6 \Omega{/tex} shows full scale deflection for a current of 0.2 A. The value of shunt to be used with this galvanometer to convert it into an ammeter of range ({tex}0-5 {~A}{/tex}) is:
    1. {tex}0.25 \Omega{/tex}
    2. {tex}0.30 \Omega{/tex}
    3. {tex}0.50 \Omega{/tex}
    4. {tex}6.0 \Omega{/tex}
  3. The value of resistance of the ammeter in case (ii) will be:
    1. {tex}0.20 \Omega{/tex}
    2. {tex}0.24 \Omega{/tex}
    3. {tex}6.0 \Omega{/tex}
    4. {tex}6.25 \Omega{/tex}
    1. A galvanometer is converted into a voltmeter of range {tex}(0-{V}){/tex} by connecting with it, a resistance {tex}R_1{/tex}. If {tex}R_1{/tex} is replaced by {tex}R_2{/tex}, the range becomes {tex}(0-2 {~V}){/tex}. The resistance of the galvanometer is:
      1. {tex}\left(R_2-2 R_1\right){/tex}
      2. {tex}\left(R_2-R_1\right){/tex}
      3. {tex}\left({R}_1+{R}_2\right){/tex}
      4. {tex}\left(R_1-2 R_2\right){/tex}

        OR

    2. A current of 5 mA flows through a galvanometer. Its coil has 100 turns, each of area of cross-section {tex}18 {~cm}^2{/tex} and is suspended in a magnetic field 0.20 T. The deflecting torque acting on the coil will be:
      1. {tex}3.6 \times 10^{-3} {Nm}{/tex}
      2. {tex}1.8 \times 10^{-4} {Nm}{/tex}
      3. {tex}2.4 \times 10^{-3} {Nm}{/tex}
      4. {tex}1.2 \times 10^{-4} {Nm}{/tex}

The value of shunt to be used with this galvanometer to convert it into an ammeter of range ( {tex}0-5 {~A}{/tex} ) is: {tex}0.25 \Omega{/tex} {tex}0.30 \Omega{/tex} {tex}0.50 \Omega{/tex} {tex}6.0 \Omega{/tex} The value of resistance of the ammeter in case (ii) will be: {tex}0.20 \Omega{/tex} {tex}0.24 \Omega{/tex} {tex}6.0 \Omega{/tex} {tex}6.25 \Omega{/tex} A galvanometer is converted into a voltmeter of range {tex}(0-{V}){/tex} by connecting with it, a resistance {tex}R_1{/tex} . The deflecting torque acting on the coil will be: {tex}3.6 \times 10^{-3} {Nm}{/tex} {tex}1.8 \times 10^{-4} {Nm}{/tex} {tex}2.4 \times 10^{-3} {Nm}{/tex} {tex}1.2 \times 10^{-4} {Nm}{/tex}
explanation: (B) {tex}\frac{N B A}{K}{/tex} (A) {tex}0.25 \Omega{/tex} (B) {tex}0.24 \Omega{/tex} (A) {tex}\left({R}_2-2 {R}_1\right){/tex} OR (B) {tex}1.8 \times 10^{-4} {Nm}{/tex}
solution: (B) {tex}\frac{N B A}{K}{/tex} (A) {tex}0.25 \Omega{/tex} (B) {tex}0.24 \Omega{/tex} (A) {tex}\left({R}_2-2 {R}_1\right){/tex} OR (B) {tex}1.8 \times 10^{-4} {Nm}{/tex}
solution_html:
1. (B) {tex}\frac{N B A}{K}{/tex}
  2. (A) {tex}0.25 \Omega{/tex}
  3. (B) {tex}0.24 \Omega{/tex}
    1. (A) {tex}\left({R}_2-2 {R}_1\right){/tex}

      OR

    2. (B) {tex}1.8 \times 10^{-4} {Nm}{/tex}

question_html:
An electron (mass {tex}9 \times 10^{-31} {~kg}{/tex} and charge {tex}1.6 \times 10^{-19} {C}{/tex}) is moving in a circle with a speed of {tex}3.2 \times 10^{7} {~m} / {s}{/tex} in a magnetic field of {tex}4.5 \times 10^{-4} {~T}{/tex} perpendicular to it.
Calculate:

  1. the radius of the circular path,
  2. its frequency of rotation, and
  3. its energy.
question_text: An electron (mass {tex}9 \times 10^{-31} {~kg}{/tex} and charge {tex}1.6 \times 10^{-19} {C}{/tex} ) is moving in a circle with a speed of {tex}3.2 \times 10^{7} {~m} / {s}{/tex} in a magnetic field of {tex}4.5 \times 10^{-4} {~T}{/tex} perpendicular to it. Calculate: the radius of the circular path, its frequency of rotation, and its energy.
explanation: {tex} r=\frac{m v}{q B} {/tex} {tex} = \frac{9 \times 10^{-31} \times 3.2 \times 10^7}{1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 4.5 \times 10^{-4}} {/tex} {tex} = 0.4 {~m} {/tex} {tex} v=\frac{v}{2 \pi r}{/tex} {tex} =\frac{3.2 \times 10^7}{2 \pi \times 0.4} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{4}{\pi} \times 10^7 {~Hz} {/tex} {tex} {E} =\frac{1}{2} {~m} v^2 {/tex} {tex} =\frac{1}{2} \times 9 \times 10^{-31} \times 3.2 \times 3.2 \times 10^{14} {/tex} {tex} =4.608 \times 10^{-16} {~J} {/tex}
solution: {tex} r=\frac{m v}{q B} {/tex} {tex} = \frac{9 \times 10^{-31} \times 3.2 \times 10^7}{1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 4.5 \times 10^{-4}} {/tex} {tex} = 0.4 {~m} {/tex} {tex} v=\frac{v}{2 \pi r}{/tex} {tex} =\frac{3.2 \times 10^7}{2 \pi \times 0.4} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{4}{\pi} \times 10^7 {~Hz} {/tex} {tex} {E} =\frac{1}{2} {~m} v^2 {/tex} {tex} =\frac{1}{2} \times 9 \times 10^{-31} \times 3.2 \times 3.2 \times 10^{14} {/tex} {tex} =4.608 \times 10^{-16} {~J} {/tex}
solution_html:
1. {tex} r=\frac{m v}{q B} {/tex}
    {tex} = \frac{9 \times 10^{-31} \times 3.2 \times 10^7}{1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 4.5 \times 10^{-4}} {/tex}
    {tex} = 0.4 {~m} {/tex}
  2. {tex} v=\frac{v}{2 \pi r}{/tex}
    {tex} =\frac{3.2 \times 10^7}{2 \pi \times 0.4} {/tex}
    {tex} =\frac{4}{\pi} \times 10^7 {~Hz} {/tex}
  3. {tex} {E} =\frac{1}{2} {~m} v^2 {/tex}
    {tex} =\frac{1}{2} \times 9 \times 10^{-31} \times 3.2 \times 3.2 \times 10^{14} {/tex}
    {tex} =4.608 \times 10^{-16} {~J} {/tex}
