subject: Physics
question_html:
A proton with kinetic energy {tex}1.3384 \times 10^{-14} {~J}{/tex} moving horizontally from north to south, enters a uniform magnetic field B of 2.0 mT directed eastward. Calculate:

  1. the speed of the proton
  2. the magnitude of acceleration of the proton
  3. the radius of the path traced by the proton

[Take {tex}({q} / {m}){/tex} for proton {tex}\left.=1.0 \times 10^8 {C} / {kg}\right]{/tex}
question_text: A proton with kinetic energy {tex}1.3384 \times 10^{-14} {~J}{/tex} moving horizontally from north to south, enters a uniform magnetic field B of 2.0 mT directed eastward. Calculate: the speed of the proton the magnitude of acceleration of the proton the radius of the path traced by the proton [Take {tex}({q} / {m}){/tex} for proton {tex}\left.=1.0 \times 10^8 {C} / {kg}\right]{/tex}
explanation: Speed of the Proton The kinetic energy K of the proton is related to its speed v by: K = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} mv 2 We need to find v. Using the charge-to-mass ratio {tex}\frac{q}{m}{/tex} {tex}m=\frac{q}{\frac{q}{m}}{/tex} where {tex}\frac{q}{m}{/tex} = 1.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 8 C/kg. Rearranging: {tex}m=\frac{q}{\frac{q}{m}}{/tex} {tex}m=\frac{1.6 \times 10^{-19}}{1.0 \times 10^8}=1.6 \times 10^{-27} \mathrm{~kg}{/tex} Substituting this into the kinetic energy formula: {tex}v^2=\frac{2 K}{m}{/tex} {tex} v^2=\frac{2 \times 1.3384 \times 10^{-14}}{1.6 \times 10^{-27}} {/tex} {tex} v^2=\frac{2.6768 \times 10^{-14}}{1.6 \times 10^{-27}} {/tex} {tex} v^2=1.673 \times 10^{13} {/tex} {tex} v=\sqrt{1.673 \times 10^{13}}{/tex} {tex}v \approx 4.1 \times 10^6 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}{/tex} Magnitude of the Acceleration of the Proton The force F on the proton due to the magnetic field is given by: F = qvB This force provides the centripetal acceleration a: F = ma So, {tex}a=\frac{F}{m}=\frac{q v B}{m}{/tex} Using {tex} \frac{q}{m}=1.0 \times 10^8 {C} / {kg} {/tex} {tex} {l}a=\frac{q v B}{m}=\frac{q}{m} \cdot v \cdot B {/tex} {tex}a=\left(1.0 \times 10^8\right) \cdot\left(4.1 \times 10^6\right) \cdot\left(2.0 \times 10^{-3}\right) {/tex} {tex}a=1.0 \times 10^8 \cdot 8.2 \times 10^3 \times 10^{-3} {/tex} {tex}a=8.2 \times 10^5 {~m} / {s}^2{/tex} Radius of the Path Traced by the Proton The radius r of the circular path in the magnetic field is given by: {tex} r=\frac{m v}{q B} {/tex} Using {tex} \frac{q}{m}=1.0 \times 10^8 {/tex} {tex} r=\frac{v}{\frac{q}{m} \cdot B} {/tex} {tex} r=\frac{4.1 \times 10^6}{1.0 \times 10^8 .2 .0 \times 10^{-3}} {/tex} {tex} r=\frac{4.1 \times 10^6}{2.0 \times 10^5} {/tex} {tex} r=20.5 {~m}{/tex}
solution: Speed of the Proton The kinetic energy K of the proton is related to its speed v by: K = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} mv 2 We need to find v. Using the charge-to-mass ratio {tex}\frac{q}{m}{/tex} {tex}m=\frac{q}{\frac{q}{m}}{/tex} where {tex}\frac{q}{m}{/tex} = 1.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 8 C/kg. Rearranging: {tex}m=\frac{q}{\frac{q}{m}}{/tex} {tex}m=\frac{1.6 \times 10^{-19}}{1.0 \times 10^8}=1.6 \times 10^{-27} \mathrm{~kg}{/tex} Substituting this into the kinetic energy formula: {tex}v^2=\frac{2 K}{m}{/tex} {tex} v^2=\frac{2 \times 1.3384 \times 10^{-14}}{1.6 \times 10^{-27}} {/tex} {tex} v^2=\frac{2.6768 \times 10^{-14}}{1.6 \times 10^{-27}} {/tex} {tex} v^2=1.673 \times 10^{13} {/tex} {tex} v=\sqrt{1.673 \times 10^{13}}{/tex} {tex}v \approx 4.1 \times 10^6 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}{/tex} Magnitude of the Acceleration of the Proton The force F on the proton due to the magnetic field is given by: F = qvB This force provides the centripetal acceleration a: F = ma So, {tex}a=\frac{F}{m}=\frac{q v B}{m}{/tex} Using {tex} \frac{q}{m}=1.0 \times 10^8 {C} / {kg} {/tex} {tex} {l}a=\frac{q v B}{m}=\frac{q}{m} \cdot v \cdot B {/tex} {tex}a=\left(1.0 \times 10^8\right) \cdot\left(4.1 \times 10^6\right) \cdot\left(2.0 \times 10^{-3}\right) {/tex} {tex}a=1.0 \times 10^8 \cdot 8.2 \times 10^3 \times 10^{-3} {/tex} {tex}a=8.2 \times 10^5 {~m} / {s}^2{/tex} Radius of the Path Traced by the Proton The radius r of the circular path in the magnetic field is given by: {tex} r=\frac{m v}{q B} {/tex} Using {tex} \frac{q}{m}=1.0 \times 10^8 {/tex} {tex} r=\frac{v}{\frac{q}{m} \cdot B} {/tex} {tex} r=\frac{4.1 \times 10^6}{1.0 \times 10^8 .2 .0 \times 10^{-3}} {/tex} {tex} r=\frac{4.1 \times 10^6}{2.0 \times 10^5} {/tex} {tex} r=20.5 {~m}{/tex}
solution_html:
1. Speed of the Proton
    The kinetic energy K of the proton is related to its speed v by:
    K = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}mv2
    We need to find v. Using the charge-to-mass ratio {tex}\frac{q}{m}{/tex}
    {tex}m=\frac{q}{\frac{q}{m}}{/tex}
    where {tex}\frac{q}{m}{/tex} = 1.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 108C/kg.
    Rearranging:
    {tex}m=\frac{q}{\frac{q}{m}}{/tex}
    {tex}m=\frac{1.6 \times 10^{-19}}{1.0 \times 10^8}=1.6 \times 10^{-27} \mathrm{~kg}{/tex}
    Substituting this into the kinetic energy formula:
    {tex}v^2=\frac{2 K}{m}{/tex}
    {tex} v^2=\frac{2 \times 1.3384 \times 10^{-14}}{1.6 \times 10^{-27}} {/tex}
    {tex} v^2=\frac{2.6768 \times 10^{-14}}{1.6 \times 10^{-27}} {/tex}
    {tex} v^2=1.673 \times 10^{13} {/tex}
    {tex} v=\sqrt{1.673 \times 10^{13}}{/tex}
    {tex}v \approx 4.1 \times 10^6 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}{/tex}
  2. Magnitude of the Acceleration of the Proton
    The force F on the proton due to the magnetic field is given by:
    F = qvB
    This force provides the centripetal acceleration a:
    F = ma
    So,
    {tex}a=\frac{F}{m}=\frac{q v B}{m}{/tex}
    Using {tex} \frac{q}{m}=1.0 \times 10^8 {C} / {kg} {/tex}
    {tex} {l}a=\frac{q v B}{m}=\frac{q}{m} \cdot v \cdot B {/tex}
    {tex}a=\left(1.0 \times 10^8\right) \cdot\left(4.1 \times 10^6\right) \cdot\left(2.0 \times 10^{-3}\right) {/tex}
    {tex}a=1.0 \times 10^8 \cdot 8.2 \times 10^3 \times 10^{-3} {/tex}
    {tex}a=8.2 \times 10^5 {~m} / {s}^2{/tex}
  3. Radius of the Path Traced by the Proton
    The radius r of the circular path in the magnetic field is given by:
    {tex} r=\frac{m v}{q B} {/tex}
    Using {tex} \frac{q}{m}=1.0 \times 10^8 {/tex}
    {tex} r=\frac{v}{\frac{q}{m} \cdot B} {/tex}
    {tex} r=\frac{4.1 \times 10^6}{1.0 \times 10^8 .2 .0 \times 10^{-3}} {/tex}
    {tex} r=\frac{4.1 \times 10^6}{2.0 \times 10^5} {/tex}
    {tex} r=20.5 {~m}{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html: Two long, straight, parallel conductors carry steady currents in opposite directions. Explain the nature of the force of interaction between them. Obtain an expression for the magnitude of the force between the two conductors. Hence define one ampere.
question_text: Two long, straight, parallel conductors carry steady currents in opposite directions. Explain the nature of the force of interaction between them. Obtain an expression for the magnitude of the force between the two conductors. Hence define one ampere.
explanation: Nature of force is repulsive. Magnetic field due to current {tex}I_a{/tex} at all points of conductor {tex}b{/tex} {tex}B_{a b}=\frac{\mu_0 I_a}{2 \pi d} {/tex} directed downwards Force experienced by conductor b on its segment of length {tex}l{/tex} {tex}F_{a b}=I_b l B_{a b}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\mu_0 I_a I_b}{2 \pi d} l {/tex} directed towards left Similarly Force experienced by conductor a on its segment of length {tex}l{/tex} {tex}F_{b a}=\frac{\mu_0 I_a I_b}{2 \pi d} l {/tex} directed towards right One ampere is that steady current which when maintained in each of two very long straight parallel conductors of negligible cross- section, placed one metre apart in vacuum produces a force of {tex}2 \times 10^{-7} {~N} / {m}{/tex} on each conductor.
solution: Nature of force is repulsive. Magnetic field due to current {tex}I_a{/tex} at all points of conductor {tex}b{/tex} {tex}B_{a b}=\frac{\mu_0 I_a}{2 \pi d} {/tex} directed downwards Force experienced by conductor b on its segment of length {tex}l{/tex} {tex}F_{a b}=I_b l B_{a b}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\mu_0 I_a I_b}{2 \pi d} l {/tex} directed towards left Similarly Force experienced by conductor a on its segment of length {tex}l{/tex} {tex}F_{b a}=\frac{\mu_0 I_a I_b}{2 \pi d} l {/tex} directed towards right One ampere is that steady current which when maintained in each of two very long straight parallel conductors of negligible cross- section, placed one metre apart in vacuum produces a force of {tex}2 \times 10^{-7} {~N} / {m}{/tex} on each conductor.
solution_html:
Nature of force is repulsive.

Magnetic field due to current {tex}I_a{/tex} at all points of conductor {tex}b{/tex}
{tex}B_{a b}=\frac{\mu_0 I_a}{2 \pi d} {/tex} directed downwards
Force experienced by conductor b on its segment of length {tex}l{/tex}
{tex}F_{a b}=I_b l B_{a b}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{\mu_0 I_a I_b}{2 \pi d} l {/tex} directed towards left
Similarly
Force experienced by conductor a on its segment of length {tex}l{/tex}
{tex}F_{b a}=\frac{\mu_0 I_a I_b}{2 \pi d} l {/tex} directed towards right
One ampere is that steady current which when maintained in each of two very long straight parallel conductors of negligible cross- section, placed one metre apart in vacuum produces a force of {tex}2 \times 10^{-7} {~N} / {m}{/tex} on each conductor.

subject: Physics
question_html: Obtain an expression for the torque {tex}\vec{\tau}{/tex} acting on a current carrying loop in a uniform magnetic field {tex}\overrightarrow{{B}}{/tex}. Draw the necessary diagram.
question_text: Obtain an expression for the torque {tex}\vec{\tau}{/tex} acting on a current carrying loop in a uniform magnetic field {tex}\overrightarrow{{B}}{/tex} . Draw the necessary diagram.
explanation: Forces on arm BC and DA are equal and opposite and act along the axis of the coil. Being collinear they cancel each other. Forces on arms AB and CD are equal and opposite but not collinear. They form a couple. {tex} F_1=F_2=I b B {/tex} {tex} \tau=F_1 \frac{a}{2} \sin \theta+F_2 \frac{a}{2} \sin \theta {/tex} {tex} \tau=I a b B \sin \theta {/tex} (where A = ab & m = IA) {tex} \vec{\tau}=\vec{m} \times \vec{B} {/tex}
solution: Forces on arm BC and DA are equal and opposite and act along the axis of the coil. Being collinear they cancel each other. Forces on arms AB and CD are equal and opposite but not collinear. They form a couple. {tex} F_1=F_2=I b B {/tex} {tex} \tau=F_1 \frac{a}{2} \sin \theta+F_2 \frac{a}{2} \sin \theta {/tex} {tex} \tau=I a b B \sin \theta {/tex} (where A = ab & m = IA) {tex} \vec{\tau}=\vec{m} \times \vec{B} {/tex}
solution_html:
Forces on arm BC and DA are equal and opposite and act along the axis of the coil. Being collinear they cancel each other.
Forces on arms AB and CD are equal and opposite but not collinear. They form a couple.
{tex} F_1=F_2=I b B {/tex}
{tex} \tau=F_1 \frac{a}{2} \sin \theta+F_2 \frac{a}{2} \sin \theta {/tex}
{tex} \tau=I a b B \sin \theta {/tex}
(where A = ab & m = IA)
{tex} \vec{\tau}=\vec{m} \times \vec{B} {/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html:
A circular coil with cross-sectional area 0.2 cm2 carries a current of 4 A. It is kept in a uniform magnetic field of magnitude 0.5 T normal to the plane of the coil. Calculate:

  1. the net force on the coil.
  2. the torque on the coil.
  3. the average force on each electron in the coil due to the magnetic field. The free electron density in the material of the coil is 1028 m-3.
question_text: A circular coil with cross-sectional area 0.2 cm 2 carries a current of 4 A. It is kept in a uniform magnetic field of magnitude 0.5 T normal to the plane of the coil. Calculate: the net force on the coil. the torque on the coil. the average force on each electron in the coil due to the magnetic field. The free electron density in the material of the coil is 10 28 m -3 .
explanation: Net force = zero By symmetry, force on each element of the coil is equal and opposite to the force on the diametrically opposite element of the coil. Hence, the net force is zero. Torque on the coil. {tex} \vec{\tau}=\vec{m} \times \vec{B} {/tex} {tex} \tau=m B \sin \theta [\vec{m} \| \vec{B}] {/tex} {tex} \tau=m B \sin 0^{\circ} \theta=0^{\circ} {/tex} {tex} \tau=0 {/tex} {tex} f_{a v g} =e\left(\overrightarrow{{v}}_d \times \vec{B}\right) {/tex} {tex} =e {v}_d B \sin 90^{\circ} {/tex} {tex} =e {v}_d B {/tex} {tex} \therefore I =n e A {v}_d {/tex} {tex} {v}_d =\frac{I}{n e A} {/tex} {tex} f_{\text {avg }} =e \times \frac{I}{n e A} B {/tex} {tex} =\frac{I B}{n A} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{4 \times 0.5}{10^{28} \times 0.2 \times 10^{-4}} {/tex} {tex} =10^{-23} {~N} {/tex}
solution: Net force = zero By symmetry, force on each element of the coil is equal and opposite to the force on the diametrically opposite element of the coil. Hence, the net force is zero. Torque on the coil. {tex} \vec{\tau}=\vec{m} \times \vec{B} {/tex} {tex} \tau=m B \sin \theta [\vec{m} \| \vec{B}] {/tex} {tex} \tau=m B \sin 0^{\circ} \theta=0^{\circ} {/tex} {tex} \tau=0 {/tex} {tex} f_{a v g} =e\left(\overrightarrow{{v}}_d \times \vec{B}\right) {/tex} {tex} =e {v}_d B \sin 90^{\circ} {/tex} {tex} =e {v}_d B {/tex} {tex} \therefore I =n e A {v}_d {/tex} {tex} {v}_d =\frac{I}{n e A} {/tex} {tex} f_{\text {avg }} =e \times \frac{I}{n e A} B {/tex} {tex} =\frac{I B}{n A} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{4 \times 0.5}{10^{28} \times 0.2 \times 10^{-4}} {/tex} {tex} =10^{-23} {~N} {/tex}
solution_html:
1. Net force = zero
    By symmetry, force on each element of the coil is equal and opposite to the force on the diametrically opposite element of the coil. Hence, the net force is zero.
  2. Torque on the coil.
    {tex} \vec{\tau}=\vec{m} \times \vec{B} {/tex}
    {tex} \tau=m B \sin \theta [\vec{m} \| \vec{B}] {/tex}
    {tex} \tau=m B \sin 0^{\circ} \theta=0^{\circ} {/tex}
    {tex} \tau=0 {/tex}
  3. {tex} f_{a v g} =e\left(\overrightarrow{{v}}_d \times \vec{B}\right) {/tex}
    {tex} =e {v}_d B \sin 90^{\circ} {/tex}
    {tex} =e {v}_d B {/tex}
    {tex} \therefore I =n e A {v}_d {/tex}
    {tex} {v}_d =\frac{I}{n e A} {/tex}
    {tex} f_{\text {avg }} =e \times \frac{I}{n e A} B {/tex}
    {tex} =\frac{I B}{n A} {/tex}
    {tex} =\frac{4 \times 0.5}{10^{28} \times 0.2 \times 10^{-4}} {/tex}
    {tex} =10^{-23} {~N} {/tex}

question_html:
1. A particle of mass {tex}m{/tex} and charge {tex}q{/tex} is moving with a velocity {tex}\vec{v}{/tex} in a magnetic field {tex}\vec{B}{/tex} as shown in the figure. Show that it follows a helical path. Hence, obtain its frequency of revolution.
  2. In a hydrogen atom, the electron moves in an orbit of radius {tex}2 \overset{o}A{/tex} making {tex}8 \times 10^{14}{/tex} revolutions per second. Find the magnetic moment associated with the orbital motion of the electron.
question_text: A particle of mass {tex}m{/tex} and charge {tex}q{/tex} is moving with a velocity {tex}\vec{v}{/tex} in a magnetic field {tex}\vec{B}{/tex} as shown in the figure. Show that it follows a helical path. Hence, obtain its frequency of revolution. In a hydrogen atom, the electron moves in an orbit of radius {tex}2 \overset{o}A{/tex} making {tex}8 \times 10^{14}{/tex} revolutions per second. Find the magnetic moment associated with the orbital motion of the electron.
explanation: {tex}{v}_{\perp}={v} \sin \theta{/tex} is perpendicular to {tex}\vec{B}{/tex} and {tex}{v}_{\|}={vcos} \theta{/tex} is parallel to {tex}\overrightarrow{{B}}{/tex} Due to {tex}{v}_{\perp}{/tex} the charge describes circular path and {tex}{v}_{\|}{/tex} pushes it in the direction of {tex}\vec{B}{/tex} . Therefore under the combined effect of two components the charged particle describes helical path, as shown in the figure. The centripetal force {tex} \frac{m v_{\perp}^2}{r}=B q v_{\perp} {/tex} {tex} v_{\perp}=\frac{B q r}{m} \left(v_{\perp}=v \sin \theta\right) {/tex} Time period = {tex}\mathrm{T}=\frac{2 \pi r}{v_{\perp}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{2 \pi m}{B q} {/tex} frequency v {tex}=\frac{1}{T}=\frac{B q}{2 \pi m} {/tex} Magnetic moment {tex}m=I {~A}{/tex} {tex} I=\frac{e}{T}=e v {/tex} {tex} =1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 8 \times 10^{14} {/tex} {tex} =1.28 \times 10^{-4} {~A} {/tex} {tex} M=1.28 \times 10^{-4} \times 3.14 \times\left(2 \times 10^{-10}\right)^2 {/tex} {tex} =5.12 \pi \times 10^{-24} {Am}^2=1.6 \times 10^{-23} {Am}^2 {/tex}
solution: {tex}{v}_{\perp}={v} \sin \theta{/tex} is perpendicular to {tex}\vec{B}{/tex} and {tex}{v}_{\|}={vcos} \theta{/tex} is parallel to {tex}\overrightarrow{{B}}{/tex} Due to {tex}{v}_{\perp}{/tex} the charge describes circular path and {tex}{v}_{\|}{/tex} pushes it in the direction of {tex}\vec{B}{/tex} . Therefore under the combined effect of two components the charged particle describes helical path, as shown in the figure. The centripetal force {tex} \frac{m v_{\perp}^2}{r}=B q v_{\perp} {/tex} {tex} v_{\perp}=\frac{B q r}{m} \left(v_{\perp}=v \sin \theta\right) {/tex} Time period = {tex}\mathrm{T}=\frac{2 \pi r}{v_{\perp}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{2 \pi m}{B q} {/tex} frequency v {tex}=\frac{1}{T}=\frac{B q}{2 \pi m} {/tex} Magnetic moment {tex}m=I {~A}{/tex} {tex} I=\frac{e}{T}=e v {/tex} {tex} =1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 8 \times 10^{14} {/tex} {tex} =1.28 \times 10^{-4} {~A} {/tex} {tex} M=1.28 \times 10^{-4} \times 3.14 \times\left(2 \times 10^{-10}\right)^2 {/tex} {tex} =5.12 \pi \times 10^{-24} {Am}^2=1.6 \times 10^{-23} {Am}^2 {/tex}
solution_html:
1. {tex}{v}_{\perp}={v} \sin \theta{/tex} is perpendicular to {tex}\vec{B}{/tex} and
    {tex}{v}_{\|}={vcos} \theta{/tex} is parallel to {tex}\overrightarrow{{B}}{/tex}
    Due to {tex}{v}_{\perp}{/tex} the charge describes circular path and {tex}{v}_{\|}{/tex}pushes it in the direction of {tex}\vec{B}{/tex}. Therefore under the combined effect of two components the charged particle describes helical path, as shown in the figure.
    The centripetal force
    {tex} \frac{m v_{\perp}^2}{r}=B q v_{\perp} {/tex}
    {tex} v_{\perp}=\frac{B q r}{m} \left(v_{\perp}=v \sin \theta\right) {/tex}
    Time period = {tex}\mathrm{T}=\frac{2 \pi r}{v_{\perp}}{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{2 \pi m}{B q} {/tex}
    frequency v {tex}=\frac{1}{T}=\frac{B q}{2 \pi m} {/tex}
  2. Magnetic moment {tex}m=I {~A}{/tex}
    {tex} I=\frac{e}{T}=e v {/tex}
    {tex} =1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 8 \times 10^{14} {/tex}
    {tex} =1.28 \times 10^{-4} {~A} {/tex}
    {tex} M=1.28 \times 10^{-4} \times 3.14 \times\left(2 \times 10^{-10}\right)^2 {/tex}
    {tex} =5.12 \pi \times 10^{-24} {Am}^2=1.6 \times 10^{-23} {Am}^2 {/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. What is current sensitivity of a galvanometer? Show how the current sensitivity of a galvanometer may be increased. "Increasing the current sensitivity of a galvanometer may not necessarily increase its voltage sensitivity." Explain.
  2. A moving coil galvanometer has a resistance {tex}15 \Omega{/tex} and takes 20 mA to produce full scale deflection. How can this galvanometer be converted into a voltmeter of range 0 to {tex}100 {~V} ?{/tex}
question_text: What is current sensitivity of a galvanometer? Show how the current sensitivity of a galvanometer may be increased. "Increasing the current sensitivity of a galvanometer may not necessarily increase its voltage sensitivity." Explain. A moving coil galvanometer has a resistance {tex}15 \Omega{/tex} and takes 20 mA to produce full scale deflection. How can this galvanometer be converted into a voltmeter of range 0 to {tex}100 {~V} ?{/tex}
explanation: Deflection produced per unit current is called its current sensitivity. Current sensitivity can be increased by (a) increasing number of turns in coil (b) increasing area of coil in magnetic field (c) decreasing K (Torsional Constant) (any one) {tex}V_s=\frac{\theta}{V}=\frac{N B A}{K R}{/tex} If current sensitivity is increased by increasing number of turns of the coil, the resistance of the galvanometer will also increase. Thus voltage sensitivity may not increase. {tex} V =I_G(R+G) {/tex} {tex} R =\frac{V}{I_G}-G {/tex} {tex} =\frac{100}{20 \times 10^{-3}}-15 {/tex} {tex} =5000-15 {/tex} {tex} =4985 \Omega {/tex} By connecting {tex}4985 \Omega{/tex} in series with galvanometer it is converted to voltmeter of range {tex}(0-100 {~V}){/tex}
solution: Deflection produced per unit current is called its current sensitivity. Current sensitivity can be increased by (a) increasing number of turns in coil (b) increasing area of coil in magnetic field (c) decreasing K (Torsional Constant) (any one) {tex}V_s=\frac{\theta}{V}=\frac{N B A}{K R}{/tex} If current sensitivity is increased by increasing number of turns of the coil, the resistance of the galvanometer will also increase. Thus voltage sensitivity may not increase. {tex} V =I_G(R+G) {/tex} {tex} R =\frac{V}{I_G}-G {/tex} {tex} =\frac{100}{20 \times 10^{-3}}-15 {/tex} {tex} =5000-15 {/tex} {tex} =4985 \Omega {/tex} By connecting {tex}4985 \Omega{/tex} in series with galvanometer it is converted to voltmeter of range {tex}(0-100 {~V}){/tex}
solution_html:
1. Deflection produced per unit current is called its current sensitivity.
    Current sensitivity can be increased by
    (a) increasing number of turns in coil
    (b) increasing area of coil in magnetic field
    (c) decreasing K (Torsional Constant)
    (any one)
    {tex}V_s=\frac{\theta}{V}=\frac{N B A}{K R}{/tex}
    If current sensitivity is increased by increasing number of turns of the coil, the resistance of the galvanometer will also increase. Thus voltage sensitivity may not increase.
  2. {tex} V =I_G(R+G) {/tex}
    {tex} R =\frac{V}{I_G}-G {/tex}
    {tex} =\frac{100}{20 \times 10^{-3}}-15 {/tex}
    {tex} =5000-15 {/tex}
    {tex} =4985 \Omega {/tex}
    By connecting {tex}4985 \Omega{/tex} in series with galvanometer it is converted to voltmeter of range {tex}(0-100 {~V}){/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html: A circular loop of wire, carrying a current 'I' is lying in xy-plane with its centre coinciding with the origin. It is subjected to a uniform magnetic field pointing along + z-axis. The loop will:
question_text: A circular loop of wire, carrying a current 'I' is lying in xy-plane with its centre coinciding with the origin. It is subjected to a uniform magnetic field pointing along + z-axis. The loop will:

options.explanation: A circular loop of wire carrying a current and placed in a uniform magnetic field experiences a force due to the interaction between the magnetic field and the current. According to the right-hand rule, the direction of the force on the loop depends on the orientation of the magnetic field and the direction of the current. In this scenario, since the magnetic field is pointing along the +z-axis and the current flows in the loop (in the xy-plane), the forces acting on opposite sides of the loop will cancel each other out. This means that there will be no net force acting on the loop.

explanation: A circular loop of wire carrying a current and placed in a uniform magnetic field experiences a force due to the interaction between the magnetic field and the current. According to the right-hand rule, the direction of the force on the loop depends on the orientation of the magnetic field and the direction of the current. In this scenario, since the magnetic field is pointing along the +z-axis and the current flows in the loop (in the xy-plane), the forces acting on opposite sides of the loop will cancel each other out. This means that there will be no net force acting on the loop.

subject: Physics
question_html: State Bohr's second postulate for his theory of hydrogen atom. Prove that the radius of Bohr's orbit of a hydrogen atom is directly proportional to {tex}{n}^2{/tex}, where n is the principal quantum number.
question_text: State Bohr's second postulate for his theory of hydrogen atom. Prove that the radius of Bohr's orbit of a hydrogen atom is directly proportional to {tex}{n}^2{/tex} , where n is the principal quantum number.
explanation: Bohr’s first postulate An electron in an atom revolves in certain stable orbits without the emission of radiant energy. Bohr’s second postulate Electron revolves around the nucleus only in those orbits for which the angular momentum is integral multiple of {tex}\frac{h}{2 \pi}{/tex} . Electrostatic force between revolving electron and nucleus provides requisite centripetal force {tex} \frac{m v_n^2}{r_n}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{e^2}{r_n^2} {/tex} {tex} v_n=\frac{e}{\sqrt{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 m r_n}} {/tex} ...(i) {tex} m v_n r_n=\frac{n h}{2 \pi} {/tex} ...(ii) using equations (i) and (ii) {tex} r_n=\left(\frac{n^2}{m}\right)\left(\frac{h}{2 \pi}\right)^2 \frac{4 \pi \varepsilon_0}{e^2} {/tex}
solution: Bohr’s first postulate An electron in an atom revolves in certain stable orbits without the emission of radiant energy. Bohr’s second postulate Electron revolves around the nucleus only in those orbits for which the angular momentum is integral multiple of {tex}\frac{h}{2 \pi}{/tex} . Electrostatic force between revolving electron and nucleus provides requisite centripetal force {tex} \frac{m v_n^2}{r_n}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{e^2}{r_n^2} {/tex} {tex} v_n=\frac{e}{\sqrt{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 m r_n}} {/tex} ...(i) {tex} m v_n r_n=\frac{n h}{2 \pi} {/tex} ...(ii) using equations (i) and (ii) {tex} r_n=\left(\frac{n^2}{m}\right)\left(\frac{h}{2 \pi}\right)^2 \frac{4 \pi \varepsilon_0}{e^2} {/tex}
solution_html:
• Bohr’s first postulate
    An electron in an atom revolves in certain stable orbits without the emission of radiant energy.
  • Bohr’s second postulate
    Electron revolves around the nucleus only in those orbits for which the angular momentum is integral multiple of {tex}\frac{h}{2 \pi}{/tex}.

Electrostatic force between revolving electron and nucleus provides requisite centripetal force
{tex} \frac{m v_n^2}{r_n}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{e^2}{r_n^2} {/tex}
{tex} v_n=\frac{e}{\sqrt{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 m r_n}} {/tex} ...(i)
{tex} m v_n r_n=\frac{n h}{2 \pi} {/tex} ...(ii)
using equations (i) and (ii)
{tex} r_n=\left(\frac{n^2}{m}\right)\left(\frac{h}{2 \pi}\right)^2 \frac{4 \pi \varepsilon_0}{e^2} {/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html: An electron (charge -e, mass m ) is revolving around a nucleus, in a hydrogen atom, in a circle of radius {tex}r{/tex}. Derive an expression, in vector form, for the magnetic dipole moment, {tex}\vec{\mu}_{{e}}{/tex} in terms of its orbital angular momentum {tex}\overrightarrow{{L}}{/tex}. What is gyromagnetic ratio?
question_text: An electron (charge -e, mass m ) is revolving around a nucleus, in a hydrogen atom, in a circle of radius {tex}r{/tex} . Derive an expression, in vector form, for the magnetic dipole moment, {tex}\vec{\mu}_{{e}}{/tex} in terms of its orbital angular momentum {tex}\overrightarrow{{L}}{/tex} . What is gyromagnetic ratio?
explanation: One conductor experiences a force due to magnetic field of the other conductor Magnetic field produced by conductor 'a' at all points along the length of conductor 'b' {tex} B_a=\frac{\mu_0 I_a}{2 \pi d} {/tex} Force on conductor 'b' due to this magnetic field {tex} F_{b a} =I_b L B_a {/tex} {tex} F_{b a} =\frac{\mu_0 I_a I_b L}{2 \pi d} {/tex} {tex} f_{b a} =\frac{F_{b a}}{L}=\frac{\mu_0 I_a I_b}{2 \pi d} \text { directed away from a } {/tex} {tex} f_{a b} =\frac{F_{a b}}{L}=\frac{\mu_0 I_a I_b}{2 \pi d} \text { directed away from b } {/tex} Repulsive, the forces acting on them are away from each other.
solution: One conductor experiences a force due to magnetic field of the other conductor Magnetic field produced by conductor 'a' at all points along the length of conductor 'b' {tex} B_a=\frac{\mu_0 I_a}{2 \pi d} {/tex} Force on conductor 'b' due to this magnetic field {tex} F_{b a} =I_b L B_a {/tex} {tex} F_{b a} =\frac{\mu_0 I_a I_b L}{2 \pi d} {/tex} {tex} f_{b a} =\frac{F_{b a}}{L}=\frac{\mu_0 I_a I_b}{2 \pi d} \text { directed away from a } {/tex} {tex} f_{a b} =\frac{F_{a b}}{L}=\frac{\mu_0 I_a I_b}{2 \pi d} \text { directed away from b } {/tex} Repulsive, the forces acting on them are away from each other.
solution_html:
One conductor experiences a force due to magnetic field of the other conductor

Magnetic field produced by conductor 'a' at all points along the length of conductor 'b'
{tex} B_a=\frac{\mu_0 I_a}{2 \pi d} {/tex}
Force on conductor 'b' due to this magnetic field
{tex} F_{b a} =I_b L B_a {/tex}
{tex} F_{b a} =\frac{\mu_0 I_a I_b L}{2 \pi d} {/tex}
{tex} f_{b a} =\frac{F_{b a}}{L}=\frac{\mu_0 I_a I_b}{2 \pi d} \text { directed away from a } {/tex}
{tex} f_{a b} =\frac{F_{a b}}{L}=\frac{\mu_0 I_a I_b}{2 \pi d} \text { directed away from b } {/tex}
Repulsive, the forces acting on them are away from each other.

subject: Physics
question_html:
Assertion (A): The energy of charged particle moving in a uniform magnetic field does not change.
Reason (R): Work done by the magnetic field on the charge is zero.
question_text: Assertion (A): The energy of charged particle moving in a uniform magnetic field does not change. Reason (R): Work done by the magnetic field on the charge is zero.

options.text: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A.
options.html: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A.
options.explanation: The force on a charged particle moving in a uniform magnetic field always acts in direction perpendicular to the direction of motion of the charge. As work done by magnetic field on the charge is zero, [W = FS cos {tex}\theta{/tex} ], so the energy of the charged particle does not change.

options.text: Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A.
options.html: Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A.

options.text: A is true but R is false.
options.html: A is true but R is false.

options.text: A is false but R is true.
options.html: A is false but R is true.

correct_option_text: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A.
explanation: The force on a charged particle moving in a uniform magnetic field always acts in direction perpendicular to the direction of motion of the charge. As work done by magnetic field on the charge is zero, [W = FS cos {tex}\theta{/tex} ], so the energy of the charged particle does not change.

subject: Physics
question_html: Two long insulated straight wires carrying currents of 3 A and 5 A are arranged in XY plane as shown in figure. Find the magnitude and direction of the net magnetic fields at points {tex}{P}_1(2 {~m}, 2 {~m}){/tex} and {tex}{P}_2(-1 {~m}, 1 {~m}){/tex}.
question_text: Two long insulated straight wires carrying currents of 3 A and 5 A are arranged in XY plane as shown in figure. Find the magnitude and direction of the net magnetic fields at points {tex}{P}_1(2 {~m}, 2 {~m}){/tex} and {tex}{P}_2(-1 {~m}, 1 {~m}){/tex} .
explanation: Net magnetic field at point {tex}{P}_{1}{/tex} {tex}{B} ={B}_{{y}(\text { wire })}-{B}_{{x}(\text { wire })}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\mu_{0} {I}_{1}}{2 \pi {r}}-\frac{\mu_{0} {I}_{2}}{2 \pi {r}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\mu_{0}}{2 \pi \times 2}(5-3){/tex} {tex}{B} =2 \times 10^{-7} {~T}{/tex} Net magnetic field at point {tex}{P}_{2}{/tex} {tex}{B} ={B}_{{y}(\text { wire })}+{B}_{{x}(\text { wire })}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\mu_{0} {I}_{1}}{2 \pi {r}}+\frac{\mu_{0} {I}_{2}}{2 \pi {r}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{4 \mu_{0}}{2 \pi \times 1}(5+3){/tex} {tex}=\frac{4 \mu_{0}}{\pi}{/tex} {tex}=16 \times 10^{-7} {~T}{/tex} The direction of net magnetic field is along +ve z-axis.
solution: Net magnetic field at point {tex}{P}_{1}{/tex} {tex}{B} ={B}_{{y}(\text { wire })}-{B}_{{x}(\text { wire })}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\mu_{0} {I}_{1}}{2 \pi {r}}-\frac{\mu_{0} {I}_{2}}{2 \pi {r}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\mu_{0}}{2 \pi \times 2}(5-3){/tex} {tex}{B} =2 \times 10^{-7} {~T}{/tex} Net magnetic field at point {tex}{P}_{2}{/tex} {tex}{B} ={B}_{{y}(\text { wire })}+{B}_{{x}(\text { wire })}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\mu_{0} {I}_{1}}{2 \pi {r}}+\frac{\mu_{0} {I}_{2}}{2 \pi {r}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{4 \mu_{0}}{2 \pi \times 1}(5+3){/tex} {tex}=\frac{4 \mu_{0}}{\pi}{/tex} {tex}=16 \times 10^{-7} {~T}{/tex} The direction of net magnetic field is along +ve z-axis.
solution_html:
Net magnetic field at point {tex}{P}_{1}{/tex}
{tex}{B} ={B}_{{y}(\text { wire })}-{B}_{{x}(\text { wire })}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{\mu_{0} {I}_{1}}{2 \pi {r}}-\frac{\mu_{0} {I}_{2}}{2 \pi {r}}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{\mu_{0}}{2 \pi \times 2}(5-3){/tex}
{tex}{B} =2 \times 10^{-7} {~T}{/tex}
Net magnetic field at point {tex}{P}_{2}{/tex}
{tex}{B} ={B}_{{y}(\text { wire })}+{B}_{{x}(\text { wire })}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{\mu_{0} {I}_{1}}{2 \pi {r}}+\frac{\mu_{0} {I}_{2}}{2 \pi {r}}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{4 \mu_{0}}{2 \pi \times 1}(5+3){/tex}
{tex}=\frac{4 \mu_{0}}{\pi}{/tex}
{tex}=16 \times 10^{-7} {~T}{/tex}
The direction of net magnetic field is along +ve z-axis.

question_html: An electron moving with a velocity {tex}\vec{v}=\left(1.0 \times 10^{7} {~m} / {s}\right) \hat{{i}}+\left(0.5 \times 10^{7} {~m} / {s}\right) \hat{{j}}{/tex} enters a region of uniform magnetic field {tex}\vec{{B}}=(0.5 {mT}) \hat{{j}}{/tex}. question_text: An electron moving with a velocity {tex}\vec{v}=\left(1.0 \times 10^{7} {~m} / {s}\right) \hat{{i}}+\left(0.5 \times 10^{7} {~m} / {s}\right) \hat{{j}}{/tex} enters a region of uniform magnetic field {tex}\vec{{B}}=(0.5 {mT}) \hat{{j}}{/tex} . Find the radius of the circular path described by it. While rotating; does the electron trace a linear path too? If so, calculate the linear distance covered by it during the period of one revolution.
explanation: Radius of circular path {tex}r =\frac{m v_{x}}{e B}{/tex} {tex}r =\frac{9.1 \times 10^{-31} \times 1 \times 10^{7}}{1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 0.5 \times 10^{-3}}{/tex} {tex}=11.38 \times 10^{-2} {~m}{/tex} Yes, it traces a linear path too. Linear distance during period of one revolution {tex}{y} =\frac{2 \pi {~m}}{{eB}} \times {v}_{{y}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{2 \times \pi \times 9.1 \times 10^{-31} \times 0.5 \times 10^{7}}{1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 0.5 \times 10^{-3}}{/tex} {tex}=0.357 {~m}{/tex} {tex}= 0.36 {~m}{/tex}
solution: Radius of circular path {tex}r =\frac{m v_{x}}{e B}{/tex} {tex}r =\frac{9.1 \times 10^{-31} \times 1 \times 10^{7}}{1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 0.5 \times 10^{-3}}{/tex} {tex}=11.38 \times 10^{-2} {~m}{/tex} Yes, it traces a linear path too. Linear distance during period of one revolution {tex}{y} =\frac{2 \pi {~m}}{{eB}} \times {v}_{{y}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{2 \times \pi \times 9.1 \times 10^{-31} \times 0.5 \times 10^{7}}{1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 0.5 \times 10^{-3}}{/tex} {tex}=0.357 {~m}{/tex} {tex}= 0.36 {~m}{/tex}
solution_html:
Radius of circular path
{tex}r =\frac{m v_{x}}{e B}{/tex}
{tex}r =\frac{9.1 \times 10^{-31} \times 1 \times 10^{7}}{1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 0.5 \times 10^{-3}}{/tex}
{tex}=11.38 \times 10^{-2} {~m}{/tex}
Yes, it traces a linear path too.
Linear distance during period of one revolution
{tex}{y} =\frac{2 \pi {~m}}{{eB}} \times {v}_{{y}}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{2 \times \pi \times 9.1 \times 10^{-31} \times 0.5 \times 10^{7}}{1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 0.5 \times 10^{-3}}{/tex}
{tex}=0.357 {~m}{/tex}
{tex}= 0.36 {~m}{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. State and explain Ampere's circuital law.
  2. Two long straight parallel wires separated by 20 cm. carry 5 A and 10 A current respectively, in the same direction. Find the magnitude and direction of the net magnetic field at a point midway between them.
question_text: State and explain Ampere's circuital law. Two long straight parallel wires separated by 20 cm. carry 5 A and 10 A current respectively, in the same direction. Find the magnitude and direction of the net magnetic field at a point midway between them.
explanation: Line integral of magnetic field over a closed loop in vacuum is equal to {tex}\mu_{0}{/tex} times the total current passing through the loop. {tex} B=\frac{\mu_0 I}{2 \pi r} {/tex} Net magnetic field {tex} {B} ={B}_2-{B}_1 {/tex} {tex} B =\frac{\mu_0 \times 10^2}{20 \pi}[10-5] {/tex} {tex} B =\frac{4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times 10^2 \times 5}{20 \pi} {/tex} {tex} B =10^{-5} {~T} {/tex} Along the direction of magnetic field produced by the conductor carrying current 10 A.
solution: Line integral of magnetic field over a closed loop in vacuum is equal to {tex}\mu_{0}{/tex} times the total current passing through the loop. {tex} B=\frac{\mu_0 I}{2 \pi r} {/tex} Net magnetic field {tex} {B} ={B}_2-{B}_1 {/tex} {tex} B =\frac{\mu_0 \times 10^2}{20 \pi}[10-5] {/tex} {tex} B =\frac{4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times 10^2 \times 5}{20 \pi} {/tex} {tex} B =10^{-5} {~T} {/tex} Along the direction of magnetic field produced by the conductor carrying current 10 A.
solution_html:
1. Line integral of magnetic field over a closed loop in vacuum is equal to {tex}\mu_{0}{/tex} times the total current passing through the loop.

  2. {tex} B=\frac{\mu_0 I}{2 \pi r} {/tex}
    Net magnetic field
    {tex} {B} ={B}_2-{B}_1 {/tex}
    {tex} B =\frac{\mu_0 \times 10^2}{20 \pi}[10-5] {/tex}
    {tex} B =\frac{4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times 10^2 \times 5}{20 \pi} {/tex}
    {tex} B =10^{-5} {~T} {/tex}
    Along the direction of magnetic field produced by the conductor carrying current 10 A.

subject: Physics
question_html: Derive an expression for magnetic force {tex}\vec{F}{/tex} acting on a straight conductor of length L carrying current I in an external magnetic field {tex}\vec{{B}}{/tex}. Is it valid when the conductor is in zig-zag form? Justify.
question_text: Derive an expression for magnetic force {tex}\vec{F}{/tex} acting on a straight conductor of length L carrying current I in an external magnetic field {tex}\vec{{B}}{/tex} . Is it valid when the conductor is in zig-zag form? Justify.
explanation: Total number of mobile charge carriers in a conductor of length L, crosssectional area {tex}A{/tex} and number density of charge carriers {tex}n{/tex} : = nLA Force acting on the charge carriers in external magnetic field {tex}\vec{{B}}{/tex} {tex}\vec{{F}}=({nAL}) {q} \vec{{v}}_{{d}} \times \vec{{B}}{/tex} ...(1) Where {tex}\vec{v}_{d}{/tex} is the drift velocity of the charge carriers Current flowing {tex}{I}={v}_{{d}} {qnA}{/tex} {tex}{I} \vec{{L}}=\vec{{v}}_{{d}} q n A {~L}{/tex} ...(2) On solving equation (1) and (2) {tex}\vec{{F}}={I}(\vec{{L}} \times \vec{{B}}){/tex} Yes, because this force can be calculated by considering zig-zag conductor as a collection of linear strips ( {tex}{d} \vec{{l}}{/tex} ) and summing them vectorically.
solution: Total number of mobile charge carriers in a conductor of length L, crosssectional area {tex}A{/tex} and number density of charge carriers {tex}n{/tex} : = nLA Force acting on the charge carriers in external magnetic field {tex}\vec{{B}}{/tex} {tex}\vec{{F}}=({nAL}) {q} \vec{{v}}_{{d}} \times \vec{{B}}{/tex} ...(1) Where {tex}\vec{v}_{d}{/tex} is the drift velocity of the charge carriers Current flowing {tex}{I}={v}_{{d}} {qnA}{/tex} {tex}{I} \vec{{L}}=\vec{{v}}_{{d}} q n A {~L}{/tex} ...(2) On solving equation (1) and (2) {tex}\vec{{F}}={I}(\vec{{L}} \times \vec{{B}}){/tex} Yes, because this force can be calculated by considering zig-zag conductor as a collection of linear strips ( {tex}{d} \vec{{l}}{/tex} ) and summing them vectorically.
solution_html:
Total number of mobile charge carriers in a conductor of length L, crosssectional area {tex}A{/tex} and number density of charge carriers {tex}n{/tex}:
= nLA
Force acting on the charge carriers in external magnetic field {tex}\vec{{B}}{/tex}
{tex}\vec{{F}}=({nAL}) {q} \vec{{v}}_{{d}} \times \vec{{B}}{/tex} ...(1)
Where {tex}\vec{v}_{d}{/tex} is the drift velocity of the charge carriers
Current flowing
{tex}{I}={v}_{{d}} {qnA}{/tex}
{tex}{I} \vec{{L}}=\vec{{v}}_{{d}} q n A {~L}{/tex} ...(2)
On solving equation (1) and (2)
{tex}\vec{{F}}={I}(\vec{{L}} \times \vec{{B}}){/tex}
Yes, because this force can be calculated by considering zig-zag conductor as a collection of linear strips ({tex}{d} \vec{{l}}{/tex}) and summing them vectorically.

subject: Physics
question_html: Two circular coils of radius R each and having equal number of turns N, carry equal currents I in the same direction. They are placed coaxially at a distance {tex}2 \sqrt{3}{/tex} R. Find the magnitude and direction of the net magnetic field produced at the midpoint of the line joining their centres.
question_text: Two circular coils of radius R each and having equal number of turns N, carry equal currents I in the same direction. They are placed coaxially at a distance {tex}2 \sqrt{3}{/tex} R. Find the magnitude and direction of the net magnetic field produced at the midpoint of the line joining their centres.
explanation: Magnetic field due to a circular coil having N number of turns at point on the axis of the coil {tex}{B}=\frac{{N} \mu_{0} {IR}^{2}}{2\left({a}^{2}+{R}^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}}{/tex} {tex}\text { Net field at mid point }={B}_{1}+{B}_{2}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{{N} \mu_{0} {IR}^{2}}{\left({a}^{2}+{R}^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}}{/tex} {tex}\text { (As } a=\sqrt{3} R \text { ) }{/tex} {tex}\text { Net magnetic field } {B}=\frac{{N} \mu_{0} {IR}^{2}}{\left(3 {R}^{2}+{R}^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}}{/tex} {tex}{~B}=\frac{{N} \mu_{0} {I}}{8 {R}}{/tex} {tex}\text { depending on direction of current in the two loops. }{/tex}
solution: Magnetic field due to a circular coil having N number of turns at point on the axis of the coil {tex}{B}=\frac{{N} \mu_{0} {IR}^{2}}{2\left({a}^{2}+{R}^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}}{/tex} {tex}\text { Net field at mid point }={B}_{1}+{B}_{2}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{{N} \mu_{0} {IR}^{2}}{\left({a}^{2}+{R}^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}}{/tex} {tex}\text { (As } a=\sqrt{3} R \text { ) }{/tex} {tex}\text { Net magnetic field } {B}=\frac{{N} \mu_{0} {IR}^{2}}{\left(3 {R}^{2}+{R}^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}}{/tex} {tex}{~B}=\frac{{N} \mu_{0} {I}}{8 {R}}{/tex} {tex}\text { depending on direction of current in the two loops. }{/tex}
solution_html:
Magnetic field due to a circular coil having N number of turns at point on the axis of the coil
{tex}{B}=\frac{{N} \mu_{0} {IR}^{2}}{2\left({a}^{2}+{R}^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}}{/tex}
{tex}\text { Net field at mid point }={B}_{1}+{B}_{2}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{{N} \mu_{0} {IR}^{2}}{\left({a}^{2}+{R}^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}}{/tex}
{tex}\text { (As } a=\sqrt{3} R \text { ) }{/tex}
{tex}\text { Net magnetic field } {B}=\frac{{N} \mu_{0} {IR}^{2}}{\left(3 {R}^{2}+{R}^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}}{/tex}
{tex}{~B}=\frac{{N} \mu_{0} {I}}{8 {R}}{/tex}
{tex}\text { depending on direction of current in the two loops. }{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html:
A particle of mass {tex}m{/tex} and charge {tex}q{/tex} is moving in a magnetic field {tex}\vec{B}{/tex} with a velocity {tex}\vec{{v}}{/tex}. Discuss, giving reasons, the shape of its trajectory when the angle between {tex}\vec{{V}}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{{B}}{/tex} is:

  1. {tex}0^{\circ}{/tex}
  2. {tex}90^{\circ}{/tex}
  3. {tex}120^{\circ}{/tex}
question_text: A particle of mass {tex}m{/tex} and charge {tex}q{/tex} is moving in a magnetic field {tex}\vec{B}{/tex} with a velocity {tex}\vec{{v}}{/tex} . Discuss, giving reasons, the shape of its trajectory when the angle between {tex}\vec{{V}}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{{B}}{/tex} is: {tex}0^{\circ}{/tex} {tex}90^{\circ}{/tex} {tex}120^{\circ}{/tex}
explanation: {tex}{\text { We know } \vec{F}=q(\vec{v} \times \vec{B})}{/tex} Alternatively:- The particle moves in a straight line. As the angle between {tex}\vec{{v}}\ \& \ \vec{{B}}{/tex} is {tex}0^{0}{/tex} hence {tex}\vec{{F}}=0{/tex} Alternatively- The particle moves in a circular path. F is perpendicular to both {tex}\vec{{V}}\ \& \ \vec{{B}}{/tex} Alternatively The particle follows a helical path. {tex}{F}={q} v {~B} \operatorname{Sin} \theta{/tex} Component of velocity parallel to magnetic field tends to move the particle along linear path while the component perpendicular to magnetic field tends to move the particle in circular path. As a consequence the particle moves in a helical path.
solution: {tex}{\text { We know } \vec{F}=q(\vec{v} \times \vec{B})}{/tex} Alternatively:- The particle moves in a straight line. As the angle between {tex}\vec{{v}}\ \& \ \vec{{B}}{/tex} is {tex}0^{0}{/tex} hence {tex}\vec{{F}}=0{/tex} Alternatively- The particle moves in a circular path. F is perpendicular to both {tex}\vec{{V}}\ \& \ \vec{{B}}{/tex} Alternatively The particle follows a helical path. {tex}{F}={q} v {~B} \operatorname{Sin} \theta{/tex} Component of velocity parallel to magnetic field tends to move the particle along linear path while the component perpendicular to magnetic field tends to move the particle in circular path. As a consequence the particle moves in a helical path.
solution_html:
1. {tex}{\text { We know } \vec{F}=q(\vec{v} \times \vec{B})}{/tex}

    Alternatively:- The particle moves in a straight line.
    As the angle between {tex}\vec{{v}}\ \& \ \vec{{B}}{/tex} is {tex}0^{0}{/tex} hence {tex}\vec{{F}}=0{/tex}

  2. Alternatively- The particle moves in a circular path.
    F is perpendicular to both {tex}\vec{{V}}\ \& \ \vec{{B}}{/tex}

  3. Alternatively The particle follows a helical path.
    {tex}{F}={q} v {~B} \operatorname{Sin} \theta{/tex}
    Component of velocity parallel to magnetic field tends to move the particle along linear path while the component perpendicular to magnetic field tends to move the particle in circular path. As a consequence the particle moves in a helical path.

question_html:
A galvanometer is converted into a voltmeter of range {tex}(0-{V}){/tex} volt using a resistor of {tex}9900 \Omega{/tex}. If a resistor of {tex}4900 \Omega{/tex} is used, the range becomes half, i.e. {tex}\left(0-\frac{V}{2}\right){/tex} volt. Calculate:

  1. resistance of the galvanometer
  2. resistance required to convert it into a voltmeter of range {tex}(0-2 {~V}){/tex} volt.
question_text: A galvanometer is converted into a voltmeter of range {tex}(0-{V}){/tex} volt using a resistor of {tex}9900 \Omega{/tex} . If a resistor of {tex}4900 \Omega{/tex} is used, the range becomes half, i.e. {tex}\left(0-\frac{V}{2}\right){/tex} volt. Calculate: resistance of the galvanometer resistance required to convert it into a voltmeter of range {tex}(0-2 {~V}){/tex} volt.
solution_html:
1. {tex}{V}={I}_{{g}}({R}+{G}){/tex}
    {tex}{V}={I}_{{g}}(9900+{G}){/tex} ...(1)
    {tex}\frac{{V}}{2}={I}_{{g}}(4900+{G}){/tex} ...(2)
    On Solving above eq (1) and eq (2)
    {tex}{G}=100 \Omega{/tex}
  2. {tex}2 {~V}={I}_{{g}}\left({R}_{1}+{G}\right){/tex} ...(3)
    From eq (1) and (3)
    {tex}{R}_{1}=19900 \Omega{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html:
A circular loop of radius 10 cm carrying current of 1.0 A lies in {tex}x-{y}{/tex} plane. A long straight wire lies in the same plane parallel to {tex}x{/tex} - axis at a distance of 20 cm as shown in figure.

Find the direction and value of current that has to be maintained in the wire so that the net magnetic field at O is zero.
question_text: A circular loop of radius 10 cm carrying current of 1.0 A lies in {tex}x-{y}{/tex} plane. A long straight wire lies in the same plane parallel to {tex}x{/tex} - axis at a distance of 20 cm as shown in figure. Find the direction and value of current that has to be maintained in the wire so that the net magnetic field at O is zero.
explanation: Magnetic field due to circular current loop at its centre O. {tex}B_{1} =\frac{\mu_{0} I_{1}}{2 r}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\mu_{0} \times 1}{2 \times 0.1}{/tex} {tex}=5 \mu_{0} T{/tex} The magnetic field {tex}B_1{/tex} is perpendicular to plane of loop and directed inwards. Magnetic field due to long current carrying straight wire at O. {tex} {B}_2=\frac{\mu_0 {I}_2}{2 \pi {r}} {/tex} {tex} {~B}_2=\frac{\mu_0 {I}_2}{2 \pi \times 0.2} {~T} {/tex} For net magnetic field at O to be zero, {tex}{B}_{1}{/tex} should be equal and opposite to {tex}{B}_{2}{/tex} . {tex}5 \mu_{0} =\frac{\mu_{0} {I}_{2}}{0.4 \pi}{/tex} {tex}{I}_{2} =2 \pi {~A}{/tex} {tex}=6.28 {~A}{/tex} Direction of current in the straight wire is along +ve x axis.
solution: Magnetic field due to circular current loop at its centre O. {tex}B_{1} =\frac{\mu_{0} I_{1}}{2 r}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\mu_{0} \times 1}{2 \times 0.1}{/tex} {tex}=5 \mu_{0} T{/tex} The magnetic field {tex}B_1{/tex} is perpendicular to plane of loop and directed inwards. Magnetic field due to long current carrying straight wire at O. {tex} {B}_2=\frac{\mu_0 {I}_2}{2 \pi {r}} {/tex} {tex} {~B}_2=\frac{\mu_0 {I}_2}{2 \pi \times 0.2} {~T} {/tex} For net magnetic field at O to be zero, {tex}{B}_{1}{/tex} should be equal and opposite to {tex}{B}_{2}{/tex} . {tex}5 \mu_{0} =\frac{\mu_{0} {I}_{2}}{0.4 \pi}{/tex} {tex}{I}_{2} =2 \pi {~A}{/tex} {tex}=6.28 {~A}{/tex} Direction of current in the straight wire is along +ve x axis.
solution_html:
Magnetic field due to circular current loop at its centre O.
{tex}B_{1} =\frac{\mu_{0} I_{1}}{2 r}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{\mu_{0} \times 1}{2 \times 0.1}{/tex}
{tex}=5 \mu_{0} T{/tex}
The magnetic field {tex}B_1{/tex} is perpendicular to plane of loop and directed inwards.
Magnetic field due to long current carrying straight wire at O.
{tex} {B}_2=\frac{\mu_0 {I}_2}{2 \pi {r}} {/tex}
{tex} {~B}_2=\frac{\mu_0 {I}_2}{2 \pi \times 0.2} {~T} {/tex}
For net magnetic field at O to be zero, {tex}{B}_{1}{/tex} should be equal and opposite to {tex}{B}_{2}{/tex}.
{tex}5 \mu_{0} =\frac{\mu_{0} {I}_{2}}{0.4 \pi}{/tex}
{tex}{I}_{2} =2 \pi {~A}{/tex}
{tex}=6.28 {~A}{/tex}
Direction of current in the straight wire is along +ve x axis.

subject: Physics
question_html:
An electron is moving with a velocity {tex}\vec{v}=\left(3 \times 10^{6} \frac{{~m}}{{~s}}\right) \hat{{i}}{/tex}. It enters a region of magnetic field {tex}\vec{{B}}=(91 {mT}) \hat{{k}}{/tex}.

  1. Calculate the magnetic force {tex}\overrightarrow{{F}}_{{B}}{/tex} acting on electron and the radius of its path.
  2. Trace the path described by it.
question_text: An electron is moving with a velocity {tex}\vec{v}=\left(3 \times 10^{6} \frac{{~m}}{{~s}}\right) \hat{{i}}{/tex} . It enters a region of magnetic field {tex}\vec{{B}}=(91 {mT}) \hat{{k}}{/tex} . Calculate the magnetic force {tex}\overrightarrow{{F}}_{{B}}{/tex} acting on electron and the radius of its path. Trace the path described by it.
explanation: {tex}\overrightarrow{{F}}_{{B}}={q}(\overrightarrow{{v}} \times \overrightarrow{{B}}){/tex} {tex}=-1.6 \times 10^{-19}\left[\left(3 \times 10^{6} \hat{{i}}\right) \times\left(91 \times 10^{-3} \hat{{k}}\right)\right]{/tex} {tex}=1.6 \times 10^{-19}\left[3 \times 10^{6} \times 91 \times 10^{-3}\right] \hat{{j}}{/tex} {tex}=4.368 \times 10^{-14} \hat{{j}} {~N}{/tex} {tex}{r}=\frac{{mv}}{{qB}}{/tex} {tex}{r}=\frac{9.1 \times 10^{-31} \times 3 \times 10^{6}}{1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 91 \times 10^{-3}} {~m}{/tex} {tex}{r}=1.875 \times 10^{-4} {~m}{/tex} Anticlockwise circular path
solution: {tex}\overrightarrow{{F}}_{{B}}={q}(\overrightarrow{{v}} \times \overrightarrow{{B}}){/tex} {tex}=-1.6 \times 10^{-19}\left[\left(3 \times 10^{6} \hat{{i}}\right) \times\left(91 \times 10^{-3} \hat{{k}}\right)\right]{/tex} {tex}=1.6 \times 10^{-19}\left[3 \times 10^{6} \times 91 \times 10^{-3}\right] \hat{{j}}{/tex} {tex}=4.368 \times 10^{-14} \hat{{j}} {~N}{/tex} {tex}{r}=\frac{{mv}}{{qB}}{/tex} {tex}{r}=\frac{9.1 \times 10^{-31} \times 3 \times 10^{6}}{1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 91 \times 10^{-3}} {~m}{/tex} {tex}{r}=1.875 \times 10^{-4} {~m}{/tex} Anticlockwise circular path
solution_html:
1. {tex}\overrightarrow{{F}}_{{B}}={q}(\overrightarrow{{v}} \times \overrightarrow{{B}}){/tex}
    {tex}=-1.6 \times 10^{-19}\left[\left(3 \times 10^{6} \hat{{i}}\right) \times\left(91 \times 10^{-3} \hat{{k}}\right)\right]{/tex}
    {tex}=1.6 \times 10^{-19}\left[3 \times 10^{6} \times 91 \times 10^{-3}\right] \hat{{j}}{/tex}
    {tex}=4.368 \times 10^{-14} \hat{{j}} {~N}{/tex}
    {tex}{r}=\frac{{mv}}{{qB}}{/tex}
    {tex}{r}=\frac{9.1 \times 10^{-31} \times 3 \times 10^{6}}{1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 91 \times 10^{-3}} {~m}{/tex}
    {tex}{r}=1.875 \times 10^{-4} {~m}{/tex}
  2. Anticlockwise circular path

subject: Physics
question_html: A long straight horizontal wire is carrying a current I. At an instant, an alpha particle at a distance {tex}r{/tex} from it, is travelling with speed {tex}v{/tex} parallel to the wire in a direction opposite to the current. Find the magnitude and direction of the force experienced by the particle at this instant.
question_text: A long straight horizontal wire is carrying a current I. At an instant, an alpha particle at a distance {tex}r{/tex} from it, is travelling with speed {tex}v{/tex} parallel to the wire in a direction opposite to the current. Find the magnitude and direction of the force experienced by the particle at this instant.
explanation: Magnetic field due to current carrying wire at distance r {tex}{B}=\frac{\mu_{{o}} {I}}{2 \pi {r}}{/tex} Charge on alpha particle, {tex}{q}=2 {e}{/tex} Force acting on a charged particle in motion in a uniform magnetic field. {tex}\vec{{F}} ={q}(\vec{{v}} \times \vec{{B}}){/tex} {tex}\therefore {F} =2 {ev}\left(\frac{\mu_{{o}} {I}}{2 \pi {r}}\right) \left(\because \theta=90^{\circ}\right){/tex} {tex}{F} =\frac{\mu_{{o}} {evI}}{\pi {r}}{/tex} Direction of the force on alpha particle is away from the wire.
solution: Magnetic field due to current carrying wire at distance r {tex}{B}=\frac{\mu_{{o}} {I}}{2 \pi {r}}{/tex} Charge on alpha particle, {tex}{q}=2 {e}{/tex} Force acting on a charged particle in motion in a uniform magnetic field. {tex}\vec{{F}} ={q}(\vec{{v}} \times \vec{{B}}){/tex} {tex}\therefore {F} =2 {ev}\left(\frac{\mu_{{o}} {I}}{2 \pi {r}}\right) \left(\because \theta=90^{\circ}\right){/tex} {tex}{F} =\frac{\mu_{{o}} {evI}}{\pi {r}}{/tex} Direction of the force on alpha particle is away from the wire.
solution_html:
Magnetic field due to current carrying wire at distance r
{tex}{B}=\frac{\mu_{{o}} {I}}{2 \pi {r}}{/tex}
Charge on alpha particle, {tex}{q}=2 {e}{/tex}
Force acting on a charged particle in motion in a uniform magnetic field.
{tex}\vec{{F}} ={q}(\vec{{v}} \times \vec{{B}}){/tex}
{tex}\therefore {F} =2 {ev}\left(\frac{\mu_{{o}} {I}}{2 \pi {r}}\right) \left(\because \theta=90^{\circ}\right){/tex}
{tex}{F} =\frac{\mu_{{o}} {evI}}{\pi {r}}{/tex}
Direction of the force on alpha particle is away from the wire.

subject: Physics
question_html: An infinite straight conductor is kept along {tex}{X}^{\prime} {X}{/tex} axis and carries a current I. A charge {tex}q{/tex} at point {tex}P(0, r){/tex} starts moving with velocity {tex}\vec{v}=v_{0} \hat{j}{/tex} as shown in figure. Find the direction and magnitude of force initially experienced by the charge.
question_text: An infinite straight conductor is kept along {tex}{X}^{\prime} {X}{/tex} axis and carries a current I. A charge {tex}q{/tex} at point {tex}P(0, r){/tex} starts moving with velocity {tex}\vec{v}=v_{0} \hat{j}{/tex} as shown in figure. Find the direction and magnitude of force initially experienced by the charge.
explanation: Magnetic field at P due to infinite straight conductor carrying current {tex}\vec{{B}}=\frac{\mu_{0} {I}}{2 \pi {r}} \hat{{k}}{/tex} Force on charge q in this magnetic field {tex}\vec{F}=q(\vec{v} \times \vec{B}){/tex} {tex}\vec{F}=q\left[\left(v_{0} \hat{j}\right) \times\left(\frac{\mu_{0} I}{2 \pi r}\right) \hat{k}\right]{/tex} {tex}\vec{F}=\frac{\mu_{0} q v_{0} I}{2 \pi r} \hat{i}{/tex} The magnitude of force {tex}{F}=\frac{\mu_{0} {qv}_{0} {I}}{2 \pi {r}}{/tex} The direction of force on charge is along +ve X-axis.
solution: Magnetic field at P due to infinite straight conductor carrying current {tex}\vec{{B}}=\frac{\mu_{0} {I}}{2 \pi {r}} \hat{{k}}{/tex} Force on charge q in this magnetic field {tex}\vec{F}=q(\vec{v} \times \vec{B}){/tex} {tex}\vec{F}=q\left[\left(v_{0} \hat{j}\right) \times\left(\frac{\mu_{0} I}{2 \pi r}\right) \hat{k}\right]{/tex} {tex}\vec{F}=\frac{\mu_{0} q v_{0} I}{2 \pi r} \hat{i}{/tex} The magnitude of force {tex}{F}=\frac{\mu_{0} {qv}_{0} {I}}{2 \pi {r}}{/tex} The direction of force on charge is along +ve X-axis.
solution_html:
Magnetic field at P due to infinite straight conductor carrying current
{tex}\vec{{B}}=\frac{\mu_{0} {I}}{2 \pi {r}} \hat{{k}}{/tex}
Force on charge q in this magnetic field
{tex}\vec{F}=q(\vec{v} \times \vec{B}){/tex}
{tex}\vec{F}=q\left[\left(v_{0} \hat{j}\right) \times\left(\frac{\mu_{0} I}{2 \pi r}\right) \hat{k}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\vec{F}=\frac{\mu_{0} q v_{0} I}{2 \pi r} \hat{i}{/tex}
The magnitude of force {tex}{F}=\frac{\mu_{0} {qv}_{0} {I}}{2 \pi {r}}{/tex}
The direction of force on charge is along +ve X-axis.

subject: Physics
question_html: A long straight wire AB carries a current I. A particle (mass m and charge q) moves with a velocity {tex}\vec{v}{/tex}, parallel to the wire, at a distance d from it as shown in the figure. Obtain the expression for the force experienced by the particle and mention its directions.
question_text: A long straight wire AB carries a current I. A particle (mass m and charge q) moves with a velocity {tex}\vec{v}{/tex} , parallel to the wire, at a distance d from it as shown in the figure. Obtain the expression for the force experienced by the particle and mention its directions.
explanation: The magnetic field produced by current carrying conductor AB {tex}B=\frac{\mu_0 I}{2 \pi d}{/tex} , directed into the plane of paper. Force experienced by charged particle {tex}\vec{F_m}=q(\vec{\mathrm{v}} \times \vec{B}){/tex} {tex}\therefore \vec{\mathrm{v}} \perp \vec{B}{/tex} F m = q vB sin 90 o {tex}F_m=q \mathrm{v} \times \frac{\mu_0 I}{2 \pi d}{/tex} Force is repulsive/acts towards right / away from the conductor.
solution: The magnetic field produced by current carrying conductor AB {tex}B=\frac{\mu_0 I}{2 \pi d}{/tex} , directed into the plane of paper. Force experienced by charged particle {tex}\vec{F_m}=q(\vec{\mathrm{v}} \times \vec{B}){/tex} {tex}\therefore \vec{\mathrm{v}} \perp \vec{B}{/tex} F m = q vB sin 90 o {tex}F_m=q \mathrm{v} \times \frac{\mu_0 I}{2 \pi d}{/tex} Force is repulsive/acts towards right / away from the conductor.
solution_html:
The magnetic field produced by current carrying conductor AB
{tex}B=\frac{\mu_0 I}{2 \pi d}{/tex}, directed into the plane of paper.
Force experienced by charged particle
{tex}\vec{F_m}=q(\vec{\mathrm{v}} \times \vec{B}){/tex}
{tex}\therefore \vec{\mathrm{v}} \perp \vec{B}{/tex}
Fm = q vB sin 90o
{tex}F_m=q \mathrm{v} \times \frac{\mu_0 I}{2 \pi d}{/tex}
Force is repulsive/acts towards right / away from the conductor.

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. Write the principle and explain the working of a moving coil galvanometer. A galvanometer as such cannot be used to measure the current in a circuit. Why?
  2. Why is the magnetic field made radial in a moving coil galvanometer? How is it achieved?
question_text: Write the principle and explain the working of a moving coil galvanometer. A galvanometer as such cannot be used to measure the current in a circuit. Why? Why is the magnetic field made radial in a moving coil galvanometer? How is it achieved?
explanation: Principle- When a current carrying coil is placed in a uniform magnetic field, it experiences a torque. Working:- When a current flows through the coil of a galvanometer, a torque acts on it. It is given as {tex}\tau=N i A B \sin \theta{/tex} For radial magnetic field; sin {tex}\theta{/tex} = 1 The spring provides a counter or restoring toqrue {tex}k \phi{/tex} . {tex}k \phi{/tex} = NiAB In equilibrium; {tex}\phi=\left(\frac{N A B}{k}\right) i{/tex} Galvanometer cannot be used as such to measure current because: It has large resistance and hence will decrease the value of current in the circuit. The magnetic field is made radial in a moving coil galvanometer so that the area vector of the coil is always normal to the magnetic field {tex}(\vec{B}){/tex} Hence,sin {tex}\theta{/tex} = 1 always The magnetic field is made radial in a moving coil galvanometer to make the scale linear. It is achieved by using curved magnetic poles.
solution: Principle- When a current carrying coil is placed in a uniform magnetic field, it experiences a torque. Working:- When a current flows through the coil of a galvanometer, a torque acts on it. It is given as {tex}\tau=N i A B \sin \theta{/tex} For radial magnetic field; sin {tex}\theta{/tex} = 1 The spring provides a counter or restoring toqrue {tex}k \phi{/tex} . {tex}k \phi{/tex} = NiAB In equilibrium; {tex}\phi=\left(\frac{N A B}{k}\right) i{/tex} Galvanometer cannot be used as such to measure current because: It has large resistance and hence will decrease the value of current in the circuit. The magnetic field is made radial in a moving coil galvanometer so that the area vector of the coil is always normal to the magnetic field {tex}(\vec{B}){/tex} Hence,sin {tex}\theta{/tex} = 1 always The magnetic field is made radial in a moving coil galvanometer to make the scale linear. It is achieved by using curved magnetic poles.
solution_html:
1. Principle- When a current carrying coil is placed in a uniform magnetic field, it experiences a torque.
    Working:- When a current flows through the coil of a galvanometer, a torque acts on it. It is given as 
    {tex}\tau=N i A B \sin \theta{/tex}
    For radial magnetic field; sin {tex}\theta{/tex} = 1
    The spring provides a counter or restoring toqrue {tex}k \phi{/tex}.
    {tex}k \phi{/tex} = NiAB
    In equilibrium; {tex}\phi=\left(\frac{N A B}{k}\right) i{/tex}
    Galvanometer cannot be used as such to measure current because:
    It has large resistance and hence will decrease the value of current in the circuit.
  2. The magnetic field is made radial in a moving coil galvanometer so that the area vector of the coil  is always normal to the magnetic field {tex}(\vec{B}){/tex} Hence,sin {tex}\theta{/tex} = 1 always
    The magnetic field is made radial in a moving coil galvanometer to make the scale linear. It is achieved by using curved magnetic poles.

subject: Physics
question_html: Two wires of equal lengths are shaped in the form of a square loop and a circular loop. Both loops are suspended in a uniform magnetic field. Prove that for the same current, the circular loop will experience larger torque.
question_text: Two wires of equal lengths are shaped in the form of a square loop and a circular loop. Both loops are suspended in a uniform magnetic field. Prove that for the same current, the circular loop will experience larger torque.
explanation: Let the length of the wire be l. For square loop; l = 4a {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a = {tex}\frac{l}{4}{/tex} Area of square loop = a 2 = {tex}\frac{l^2}{16}{/tex} ...(i) For Circular loop; {tex}l=2 \pi r \Rightarrow r=\frac{l}{2 \pi}{/tex} Area of circular loop (A c ) = {tex}\pi r^2=\frac{l^2}{4 \pi}{/tex} ...(ii) Torque acting on the loop {tex}(\tau) \alpha \mathrm{A}{/tex} {tex}\because A_c>A_s \therefore \tau_c>\tau_s{/tex}
solution: Let the length of the wire be l. For square loop; l = 4a {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a = {tex}\frac{l}{4}{/tex} Area of square loop = a 2 = {tex}\frac{l^2}{16}{/tex} ...(i) For Circular loop; {tex}l=2 \pi r \Rightarrow r=\frac{l}{2 \pi}{/tex} Area of circular loop (A c ) = {tex}\pi r^2=\frac{l^2}{4 \pi}{/tex} ...(ii) Torque acting on the loop {tex}(\tau) \alpha \mathrm{A}{/tex} {tex}\because A_c>A_s \therefore \tau_c>\tau_s{/tex}
solution_html:
Let the length of the wire be l.
For square loop; l = 4a {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a = {tex}\frac{l}{4}{/tex}
Area of square loop = a2 = {tex}\frac{l^2}{16}{/tex} ...(i)
For Circular loop; {tex}l=2 \pi r \Rightarrow r=\frac{l}{2 \pi}{/tex}
Area of circular loop (Ac) = {tex}\pi r^2=\frac{l^2}{4 \pi}{/tex} ...(ii)
Torque acting on the loop {tex}(\tau) \alpha \mathrm{A}{/tex}
{tex}\because A_c>A_s \therefore \tau_c>\tau_s{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html: Two circular loops A and B, each of radius 3 m, are placed coaxially at a distance of 4 m. They carry currents of 3 A and 2 A in opposite directions respectively. Find the net magnetic field at the centre of loop A.
question_text: Two circular loops A and B, each of radius 3 m, are placed coaxially at a distance of 4 m. They carry currents of 3 A and 2 A in opposite directions respectively. Find the net magnetic field at the centre of loop A.
explanation: Magnetic field on the axis of loop A is given as {tex}B_1=\frac{\mu_0 I}{2 r}{/tex} {tex}B_1=\frac{4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times 3}{2 \times 3}{/tex} = 2 {tex}\pi{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -7 T = 6.28 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -7 T Magnetic field on axis of loop B {tex}B_2=\frac{\mu_0 I R^2}{2\left(R^2+x^2\right)^{3 / 2}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{2 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times 2 \times 9}{\left(3^2+4^2\right)^{3 / 2}}{/tex} , opposite to B 1 {tex}B_2=\frac{36 \pi \times 10^{-7}}{125}{/tex} T = 0.9 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -7 T B net = B 1 - B 2 = 6.28 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -7 - 0.9 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -7 B net = 5.38 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -7 T
solution: Magnetic field on the axis of loop A is given as {tex}B_1=\frac{\mu_0 I}{2 r}{/tex} {tex}B_1=\frac{4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times 3}{2 \times 3}{/tex} = 2 {tex}\pi{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -7 T = 6.28 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -7 T Magnetic field on axis of loop B {tex}B_2=\frac{\mu_0 I R^2}{2\left(R^2+x^2\right)^{3 / 2}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{2 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times 2 \times 9}{\left(3^2+4^2\right)^{3 / 2}}{/tex} , opposite to B 1 {tex}B_2=\frac{36 \pi \times 10^{-7}}{125}{/tex} T = 0.9 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -7 T B net = B 1 - B 2 = 6.28 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -7 - 0.9 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -7 B net = 5.38 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -7 T
solution_html:
Magnetic field on the axis of loop A is given as 

{tex}B_1=\frac{\mu_0 I}{2 r}{/tex}
{tex}B_1=\frac{4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times 3}{2 \times 3}{/tex} = 2{tex}\pi{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 10-7T = 6.28 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-7T

Magnetic field on axis of loop B 
{tex}B_2=\frac{\mu_0 I R^2}{2\left(R^2+x^2\right)^{3 / 2}}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{2 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times 2 \times 9}{\left(3^2+4^2\right)^{3 / 2}}{/tex}, opposite to B1
{tex}B_2=\frac{36 \pi \times 10^{-7}}{125}{/tex}T = 0.9 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-7T
Bnet = B1 - B2
= 6.28 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-7 - 0.9 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-7
Bnet = 5.38 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-7T

subject: Physics
question_html:
Two infinitely long parallel conductors A and B, separated by 2 cm, carry 6 A and 2 A currents in opposite directions respectively. Find:

  1. the net magnetic field at a point midway between A and B.
  2. the force acting per unit length on B.
question_text: Two infinitely long parallel conductors A and B, separated by 2 cm, carry 6 A and 2 A currents in opposite directions respectively. Find: the net magnetic field at a point midway between A and B. the force acting per unit length on B.
explanation: Magnetic field due to straight current carrying conductor {tex}B_1=\frac{\mu_0 I_1}{2 \pi r}=\frac{2 \times 10^{-7} \times 6}{1 \times 10^{-2}}{/tex} = 1.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -4 T into the page {tex}B_2=\frac{\mu_0 I_2}{2 \pi r}=\frac{2 \times 10^{-7} \times 2}{1 \times 10^{-2}}{/tex} = 0.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -4 T into the page {tex}\because{/tex} B 1 and B 2 are in same direction, so net magnetic field is given as B net = B 1 + B 2 = 1.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -4 T + 0.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -4 T = 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -4 T into the page Magnitude of magnetic force per unit length {tex}\frac{F}{l}=\frac{\mu_0}{2 \pi} \frac{I_1 I_2}{r}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{2 \times 10^{-7} \times 6 \times 2}{2 \times 10^{-2}}{/tex} = 1.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -4 N/m
solution: Magnetic field due to straight current carrying conductor {tex}B_1=\frac{\mu_0 I_1}{2 \pi r}=\frac{2 \times 10^{-7} \times 6}{1 \times 10^{-2}}{/tex} = 1.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -4 T into the page {tex}B_2=\frac{\mu_0 I_2}{2 \pi r}=\frac{2 \times 10^{-7} \times 2}{1 \times 10^{-2}}{/tex} = 0.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -4 T into the page {tex}\because{/tex} B 1 and B 2 are in same direction, so net magnetic field is given as B net = B 1 + B 2 = 1.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -4 T + 0.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -4 T = 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -4 T into the page Magnitude of magnetic force per unit length {tex}\frac{F}{l}=\frac{\mu_0}{2 \pi} \frac{I_1 I_2}{r}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{2 \times 10^{-7} \times 6 \times 2}{2 \times 10^{-2}}{/tex} = 1.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -4 N/m
solution_html:
1. Magnetic field due to straight current carrying conductor {tex}B_1=\frac{\mu_0 I_1}{2 \pi r}=\frac{2 \times 10^{-7} \times 6}{1 \times 10^{-2}}{/tex}
    = 1.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4T into the page
    {tex}B_2=\frac{\mu_0 I_2}{2 \pi r}=\frac{2 \times 10^{-7} \times 2}{1 \times 10^{-2}}{/tex}
    = 0.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4T into the page
    {tex}\because{/tex} B1 and B2 are in same direction, so net magnetic field is given as 
    Bnet = B1 + B2
    = 1.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4T + 0.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4T
    = 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4T into the page
  2. Magnitude of magnetic force per unit length
    {tex}\frac{F}{l}=\frac{\mu_0}{2 \pi} \frac{I_1 I_2}{r}{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{2 \times 10^{-7} \times 6 \times 2}{2 \times 10^{-2}}{/tex} = 1.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 N/m

subject: Physics
question_html: A particle of charge q and mass m starts moving with uniform velocity {tex}\mathrm{v}_0 \hat{\mathrm{i}}{/tex}. Specify the direction of magnetic field which should be set up in the region so that the particle moves (a) straight undeviated, and (b) in a circle. Justify your answers.
question_text: A particle of charge q and mass m starts moving with uniform velocity {tex}\mathrm{v}_0 \hat{\mathrm{i}}{/tex} . Specify the direction of magnetic field which should be set up in the region so that the particle moves (a) straight undeviated, and (b) in a circle. Justify your answers.
explanation: For straight undeviated path, magnetic field should be set up parallel or antiparallel to velocity vector {tex}\vec{F}=q(\vec{v} \times \vec{B}){/tex} If charge particle moves in circle magnetic field should be setup perpendicular to the velocity vector. {tex}\vec{F}=q(\vec{v} \times \vec{B}){/tex} {tex}\vec{v} \perp \vec{B}{/tex}
solution: For straight undeviated path, magnetic field should be set up parallel or antiparallel to velocity vector {tex}\vec{F}=q(\vec{v} \times \vec{B}){/tex} If charge particle moves in circle magnetic field should be setup perpendicular to the velocity vector. {tex}\vec{F}=q(\vec{v} \times \vec{B}){/tex} {tex}\vec{v} \perp \vec{B}{/tex}
solution_html:
1. For straight undeviated path, magnetic field should be set up parallel or antiparallel to velocity vector
    {tex}\vec{F}=q(\vec{v} \times \vec{B}){/tex}
  2. If charge particle moves in circle magnetic field should be setup perpendicular to the velocity vector.
    {tex}\vec{F}=q(\vec{v} \times \vec{B}){/tex}
    {tex}\vec{v} \perp \vec{B}{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html: A current carrying circular loop and a straight wire bent partly in the form of a semicircle are placed as shown in the figure. Find the magnitude and direction of net magnetic field at point O.
question_text: A current carrying circular loop and a straight wire bent partly in the form of a semicircle are placed as shown in the figure. Find the magnitude and direction of net magnetic field at point O.
explanation: Magnetic field due to inner circle at O, {tex}B_1=\frac{\mu_0 I}{2 r}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{2 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times 1}{4 \times 10^{-2}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\pi}{2} \times{/tex} 10 -5 T into the page Magnetic field due to semi-circle at O, {tex}B_2=\frac{\mu_0 I}{2 r} \cdot \frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{2 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times 2}{12 \times 10^{-2}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\pi}{6} \times{/tex} 10 -5 T out of the page as magnetic fields are in opposite directions so B net = B 1 - B 2 {tex}=\left(\frac{\pi}{2} \times 10^{-5}-\frac{\pi}{6} \times 10^{-5}\right) T \approx 1.0 \times 10^{-5} T{/tex} Direction of the net magnetic field is into the page, since B 1 > B 2 .
solution: Magnetic field due to inner circle at O, {tex}B_1=\frac{\mu_0 I}{2 r}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{2 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times 1}{4 \times 10^{-2}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\pi}{2} \times{/tex} 10 -5 T into the page Magnetic field due to semi-circle at O, {tex}B_2=\frac{\mu_0 I}{2 r} \cdot \frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{2 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times 2}{12 \times 10^{-2}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\pi}{6} \times{/tex} 10 -5 T out of the page as magnetic fields are in opposite directions so B net = B 1 - B 2 {tex}=\left(\frac{\pi}{2} \times 10^{-5}-\frac{\pi}{6} \times 10^{-5}\right) T \approx 1.0 \times 10^{-5} T{/tex} Direction of the net magnetic field is into the page, since B 1 > B 2 .
solution_html:
Magnetic field due to inner circle at O,
{tex}B_1=\frac{\mu_0 I}{2 r}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{2 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times 1}{4 \times 10^{-2}}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{\pi}{2} \times{/tex} 10-5 T into the page
Magnetic field due to semi-circle at O,
{tex}B_2=\frac{\mu_0 I}{2 r} \cdot \frac{1}{2}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{2 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times 2}{12 \times 10^{-2}}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{\pi}{6} \times{/tex} 10-5 T out of the page

as magnetic fields are in opposite directions so 
Bnet = B1 - B2
{tex}=\left(\frac{\pi}{2} \times 10^{-5}-\frac{\pi}{6} \times 10^{-5}\right) T \approx 1.0 \times 10^{-5} T{/tex}
Direction of the net magnetic field is into the page, since B1 > B2.

question_html: Write the expression for the Lorentz force on a particle of charge q moving with a velocity {tex}\vec{v}{/tex} in a magnetic field {tex}\vec{B}{/tex}. When is the magnitude of this force maximum? Show that no work is done by this force on the particle during its motion from a point {tex}\vec{r}_1{/tex} to point {tex}\vec{r}_2{/tex}.
question_text: Write the expression for the Lorentz force on a particle of charge q moving with a velocity {tex}\vec{v}{/tex} in a magnetic field {tex}\vec{B}{/tex} . When is the magnitude of this force maximum? Show that no work is done by this force on the particle during its motion from a point {tex}\vec{r}_1{/tex} to point {tex}\vec{r}_2{/tex} .
explanation: {tex}\vec{F}_m=q(\vec{\mathrm{v}} \times \vec{B}){/tex} F m = qvB sin {tex}\theta{/tex} {tex}(\because \vec{\mathrm{v}} \perp \vec{B}){/tex} Force is maximum for {tex}\theta{/tex} = 90 o As magnetic force always acts in a direction perpendicular to the velocity vector, hence no work is done by this force on the particle during motion.
solution: {tex}\vec{F}_m=q(\vec{\mathrm{v}} \times \vec{B}){/tex} F m = qvB sin {tex}\theta{/tex} {tex}(\because \vec{\mathrm{v}} \perp \vec{B}){/tex} Force is maximum for {tex}\theta{/tex} = 90 o As magnetic force always acts in a direction perpendicular to the velocity vector, hence no work is done by this force on the particle during motion.
solution_html:
{tex}\vec{F}_m=q(\vec{\mathrm{v}} \times \vec{B}){/tex}
Fm = qvB sin {tex}\theta{/tex} {tex}(\because \vec{\mathrm{v}} \perp \vec{B}){/tex}
Force is maximum for {tex}\theta{/tex} = 90o
As magnetic force always acts in a direction perpendicular to the velocity vector, hence no work is done by this force on the particle during motion.

question_html:
1. Briefly describe how the current sensitivity of a moving coil galvanometer can be increased.
  2. A galvanometer shows full scale deflection for current Ig. A resistance R1 is required to convert it into a voltmeter of range (0 - V) and a resistance R2 to convert it into a voltmeter of range (0 - 2V). Find the resistance of the galvanometer.
question_text: Briefly describe how the current sensitivity of a moving coil galvanometer can be increased. A galvanometer shows full scale deflection for current I g . A resistance R 1 is required to convert it into a voltmeter of range (0 - V) and a resistance R 2 to convert it into a voltmeter of range (0 - 2V). Find the resistance of the galvanometer.
explanation: Current sensitivity of moving coil galvanometer can be increased by increasing number of turns in the coil (I s {tex}\alpha{/tex} N) increasing magnetic field strength (I s {tex}\alpha{/tex} B) using a material having lesser value of torsional constant {tex}\left(I_s \alpha \frac{1}{k}\right){/tex} increasing area of cross-section of the coil resistance required to convert galvanometer into voltmeter {tex}R=\frac{V}{I_g}-G{/tex} For range (0 - V) {tex}R_1=\frac{V}{I_g}-G{/tex} ...(i) For range (0 - 2V) {tex}R_2=\frac{2 V}{I_g}-G{/tex} ...(ii) using (i) and (ii) G = R 2 - 2R 1
solution: Current sensitivity of moving coil galvanometer can be increased by increasing number of turns in the coil (I s {tex}\alpha{/tex} N) increasing magnetic field strength (I s {tex}\alpha{/tex} B) using a material having lesser value of torsional constant {tex}\left(I_s \alpha \frac{1}{k}\right){/tex} increasing area of cross-section of the coil resistance required to convert galvanometer into voltmeter {tex}R=\frac{V}{I_g}-G{/tex} For range (0 - V) {tex}R_1=\frac{V}{I_g}-G{/tex} ...(i) For range (0 - 2V) {tex}R_2=\frac{2 V}{I_g}-G{/tex} ...(ii) using (i) and (ii) G = R 2 - 2R 1
solution_html:
1. Current sensitivity of moving coil galvanometer can be increased by
    1. increasing number of turns in the coil (Is {tex}\alpha{/tex} N)
    2. increasing magnetic field strength (Is {tex}\alpha{/tex} B)
    3. using a material having lesser value of torsional constant {tex}\left(I_s \alpha \frac{1}{k}\right){/tex}
    4. increasing area of cross-section of the coil
  2. resistance required to convert galvanometer into voltmeter {tex}R=\frac{V}{I_g}-G{/tex}

For range (0 - V)
{tex}R_1=\frac{V}{I_g}-G{/tex} ...(i)
For range (0 - 2V)
{tex}R_2=\frac{2 V}{I_g}-G{/tex} ...(ii)
using (i) and (ii)
G = R2 - 2R1

question_html:
Two identical circular loops P and Q, each of radius R carrying current I are kept in perpendicular planes such that they have a common centre O as shown in the figure.

Find the magnitude and direction of the net magnetic field at point O.
question_text: Two identical circular loops P and Q, each of radius R carrying current I are kept in perpendicular planes such that they have a common centre O as shown in the figure. Find the magnitude and direction of the net magnetic field at point O.
explanation: Magnetic fields are at right angles to each other and are given as {tex}B_1=\frac{\mu_0 I}{2 R}{/tex} {tex}B_2=\frac{\mu_0 I}{2 R}{/tex} {tex}B=\sqrt{B_1^2+B_2^2}{/tex} {tex}B=\sqrt{2\left(\frac{\mu_0 I}{2 R}\right)^2}{/tex} {tex}B=\sqrt{2} \frac{\mu_0 I}{2 R}{/tex}
solution: Magnetic fields are at right angles to each other and are given as {tex}B_1=\frac{\mu_0 I}{2 R}{/tex} {tex}B_2=\frac{\mu_0 I}{2 R}{/tex} {tex}B=\sqrt{B_1^2+B_2^2}{/tex} {tex}B=\sqrt{2\left(\frac{\mu_0 I}{2 R}\right)^2}{/tex} {tex}B=\sqrt{2} \frac{\mu_0 I}{2 R}{/tex}
solution_html:
Magnetic fields are at right angles to each other and are given as

{tex}B_1=\frac{\mu_0 I}{2 R}{/tex}
{tex}B_2=\frac{\mu_0 I}{2 R}{/tex}
{tex}B=\sqrt{B_1^2+B_2^2}{/tex}
{tex}B=\sqrt{2\left(\frac{\mu_0 I}{2 R}\right)^2}{/tex}
{tex}B=\sqrt{2} \frac{\mu_0 I}{2 R}{/tex}

{tex}\vec{F}=q(\vec{V} \times \vec{B}){/tex}
{tex}B=\frac{\mu_o I}{2 \pi y}{/tex} and {tex}\theta{/tex} = 90o

question_html: A wire of length l is in the form of a circular loop A of one turn. This loop is reshaped into loop B of three turns. Find the ratio of the magnetic fields at the centres of loop A and loop B for the same current through them.
question_text: A wire of length l is in the form of a circular loop A of one turn. This loop is reshaped into loop B of three turns. Find the ratio of the magnetic fields at the centres of loop A and loop B for the same current through them.
explanation: {tex}B=\frac{\mu_o N I}{2 r} {/tex} {tex}l=2 \pi r ; \quad 2 \pi r_A=3\left(2 \pi r_B\right){/tex} {tex}r_B=\frac{r_A}{3}{/tex} {tex}\frac{B_A}{B_B}=\frac{\mu_a N_A I}{2 r_A} \times \frac{2 r_B}{\mu_0 N_B I}{/tex} {tex}\frac{B_A}{B_B}=\frac{1}{9}{/tex}
solution: {tex}B=\frac{\mu_o N I}{2 r} {/tex} {tex}l=2 \pi r ; \quad 2 \pi r_A=3\left(2 \pi r_B\right){/tex} {tex}r_B=\frac{r_A}{3}{/tex} {tex}\frac{B_A}{B_B}=\frac{\mu_a N_A I}{2 r_A} \times \frac{2 r_B}{\mu_0 N_B I}{/tex} {tex}\frac{B_A}{B_B}=\frac{1}{9}{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}B=\frac{\mu_o N I}{2 r} {/tex}
{tex}l=2 \pi r ; \quad 2 \pi r_A=3\left(2 \pi r_B\right){/tex}
{tex}r_B=\frac{r_A}{3}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{B_A}{B_B}=\frac{\mu_a N_A I}{2 r_A} \times \frac{2 r_B}{\mu_0 N_B I}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{B_A}{B_B}=\frac{1}{9}{/tex}
