subject: Physics
question_html:
1. Derive an expression for the force acting on a current carrying straight conductor kept in a magnetic field. State the rule which is used to find the direction of this force. Give the condition under which this force is (1) maximum, and (2) minimum.
  2. Two long parallel straight wires A and B are 2.5 cm apart in air. They carry 5.0 A and 2.5 A currents respectively in opposite directions. Calculate the magnitude of the force exerted by wire A on a 10 cm length of wire B.
question_text: Derive an expression for the force acting on a current carrying straight conductor kept in a magnetic field. State the rule which is used to find the direction of this force. Give the condition under which this force is (1) maximum, and (2) minimum. Two long parallel straight wires A and B are 2.5 cm apart in air. They carry 5.0 A and 2.5 A currents respectively in opposite directions. Calculate the magnitude of the force exerted by wire A on a 10 cm length of wire B.
explanation: Consider a rod of uniform cross sectional area A and length l. Let the number density of mobile charge carriers in it be n. Thus the total number of mobile charge carriers in it is n l A. For steady current I, drift velocity of electrons {tex}\vec{v_d}{/tex} , in the presence of external magnetic field {tex}\vec {B}{/tex} , the force on these carriers is {tex}\vec{\mathrm{F}}=\mathrm{n} l \mathrm{Aq}\left(\vec{\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{d}}} \times \vec{\mathrm{B}}\right){/tex} {tex}=[\vec{j} A l] \times \vec{B}{/tex} {tex}=I(\vec{l} \times \vec{B}){/tex} Where {tex}n q \vec{v_d}{/tex} is current density {tex}(\vec{j}){/tex} and {tex}|\vec{j} \mathrm{~A}|{/tex} is current (I) Fleming’s left hand Rule: If forefinger, middle finger and thumb are stretched in mutually perpendicular directions, such that forefinger indicates the direction of magnetic field, middle finger indicates the direction of current in the conductor, then thumb indicates the direction of force on the conductor. {tex}\mathrm{F}=\frac{\mu_o}{4 \pi} \frac{2 I_1 I_2}{d} l{/tex} {tex}=\frac{10^{-7} \times 2 \times 5 \times 2.5}{2.5 \times 10^{-2}} \times 10 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~N}{/tex} = 10 -5 N
solution: Consider a rod of uniform cross sectional area A and length l. Let the number density of mobile charge carriers in it be n. Thus the total number of mobile charge carriers in it is n l A. For steady current I, drift velocity of electrons {tex}\vec{v_d}{/tex} , in the presence of external magnetic field {tex}\vec {B}{/tex} , the force on these carriers is {tex}\vec{\mathrm{F}}=\mathrm{n} l \mathrm{Aq}\left(\vec{\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{d}}} \times \vec{\mathrm{B}}\right){/tex} {tex}=[\vec{j} A l] \times \vec{B}{/tex} {tex}=I(\vec{l} \times \vec{B}){/tex} Where {tex}n q \vec{v_d}{/tex} is current density {tex}(\vec{j}){/tex} and {tex}|\vec{j} \mathrm{~A}|{/tex} is current (I) Fleming’s left hand Rule: If forefinger, middle finger and thumb are stretched in mutually perpendicular directions, such that forefinger indicates the direction of magnetic field, middle finger indicates the direction of current in the conductor, then thumb indicates the direction of force on the conductor. {tex}\mathrm{F}=\frac{\mu_o}{4 \pi} \frac{2 I_1 I_2}{d} l{/tex} {tex}=\frac{10^{-7} \times 2 \times 5 \times 2.5}{2.5 \times 10^{-2}} \times 10 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~N}{/tex} = 10 -5 N
solution_html:
1. Consider a rod of uniform cross sectional area A and length l. Let the number density of mobile charge carriers in it be n.
    Thus the total number of mobile charge carriers in it is n l A.
    For steady current I, drift velocity of electrons {tex}\vec{v_d}{/tex}, in the presence of external magnetic field {tex}\vec {B}{/tex}, the force on these carriers is
    {tex}\vec{\mathrm{F}}=\mathrm{n} l \mathrm{Aq}\left(\vec{\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{d}}} \times \vec{\mathrm{B}}\right){/tex}
    {tex}=[\vec{j} A l] \times \vec{B}{/tex}
    {tex}=I(\vec{l} \times \vec{B}){/tex}
    Where {tex}n q \vec{v_d}{/tex} is current density {tex}(\vec{j}){/tex} and {tex}|\vec{j} \mathrm{~A}|{/tex} is current (I)
    Fleming’s left hand Rule: If forefinger, middle finger and thumb are stretched in mutually perpendicular directions, such that forefinger indicates the direction of magnetic field, middle finger indicates the direction of current in the conductor, then thumb indicates the direction of force on the conductor.

  2. {tex}\mathrm{F}=\frac{\mu_o}{4 \pi} \frac{2 I_1 I_2}{d} l{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{10^{-7} \times 2 \times 5 \times 2.5}{2.5 \times 10^{-2}} \times 10 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~N}{/tex}
    = 10-5 N

question_html:
1. Derive an expression for magnetic field on the axis of a current carrying circular loop.
  2. Write any two points of difference between a diamagnetic and a paramagnetic substance.
question_text: Derive an expression for magnetic field on the axis of a current carrying circular loop. Write any two points of difference between a diamagnetic and a paramagnetic substance.
explanation: {tex}d B=\frac{\mu_0}{4 \pi} \frac{I|d {l} \times {r}|}{r^3}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\mu_o \text { idl } \sin 90^{\circ}}{4 \pi\left(x^2+R^2\right)}{/tex} {tex}d B_{\perp}{/tex} cancels out. Net B = {tex}\int d B_x=\int d B \cos \theta{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\mu_o}{4 \pi} \int \frac{i d l}{\left(x^2+R^2\right)} \times \frac{R}{\left(x^2+R^2\right)^{1 / 2}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\mu_o i R}{4 \pi\left(x^2+R^2\right)^{3 / 2}} \int d l{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\mu_o i R}{4 \pi\left(x^2+R^2\right)^{3 / 2}}(2 \pi R){/tex} {tex}{B}=B_x \hat{{i}}=\frac{\mu_0 I R^2}{2\left(x^2+R^2\right)^{3 / 2}} \hat{{i}}{/tex} Differences Diamagnetic Materials Paramagnetic Materials (i) Susceptibility is between -1 and 0. (i) Susceptibility is a small positive number.(slightly greater than zero.) (ii) Relative permeability is between 0 and 1. (ii) Relative permeability is slightly greater than 1. (iii) Tendency to move from stronger to weaker part of external magnetism. (iii) Tendency to move from region of weak to strong magnetic field. (iv) Field inside the material is reduced. (iv) Field inside is slightly enhanced.
solution: {tex}d B=\frac{\mu_0}{4 \pi} \frac{I|d {l} \times {r}|}{r^3}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\mu_o \text { idl } \sin 90^{\circ}}{4 \pi\left(x^2+R^2\right)}{/tex} {tex}d B_{\perp}{/tex} cancels out. Net B = {tex}\int d B_x=\int d B \cos \theta{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\mu_o}{4 \pi} \int \frac{i d l}{\left(x^2+R^2\right)} \times \frac{R}{\left(x^2+R^2\right)^{1 / 2}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\mu_o i R}{4 \pi\left(x^2+R^2\right)^{3 / 2}} \int d l{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\mu_o i R}{4 \pi\left(x^2+R^2\right)^{3 / 2}}(2 \pi R){/tex} {tex}{B}=B_x \hat{{i}}=\frac{\mu_0 I R^2}{2\left(x^2+R^2\right)^{3 / 2}} \hat{{i}}{/tex} Differences Diamagnetic Materials Paramagnetic Materials (i) Susceptibility is between -1 and 0. (i) Susceptibility is a small positive number.(slightly greater than zero.) (ii) Relative permeability is between 0 and 1. (ii) Relative permeability is slightly greater than 1. (iii) Tendency to move from stronger to weaker part of external magnetism. (iii) Tendency to move from region of weak to strong magnetic field. (iv) Field inside the material is reduced. (iv) Field inside is slightly enhanced.
solution_html:
1. {tex}d B=\frac{\mu_0}{4 \pi} \frac{I|d {l} \times {r}|}{r^3}{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{\mu_o \text { idl } \sin 90^{\circ}}{4 \pi\left(x^2+R^2\right)}{/tex}
    {tex}d B_{\perp}{/tex} cancels out.
    Net B = {tex}\int d B_x=\int d B \cos \theta{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{\mu_o}{4 \pi} \int \frac{i d l}{\left(x^2+R^2\right)} \times \frac{R}{\left(x^2+R^2\right)^{1 / 2}}{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{\mu_o i R}{4 \pi\left(x^2+R^2\right)^{3 / 2}} \int d l{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{\mu_o i R}{4 \pi\left(x^2+R^2\right)^{3 / 2}}(2 \pi R){/tex}
    {tex}{B}=B_x \hat{{i}}=\frac{\mu_0 I R^2}{2\left(x^2+R^2\right)^{3 / 2}} \hat{{i}}{/tex}

  2. Differences

    Diamagnetic Materials Paramagnetic Materials
    (i) Susceptibility is between -1 and 0. (i) Susceptibility is a small positive number.(slightly greater than zero.)
    (ii) Relative permeability is between 0 and 1. (ii) Relative permeability is slightly greater than 1.
    (iii) Tendency to move from stronger to weaker part of external magnetism. (iii) Tendency to move from region of weak to strong magnetic field.
    (iv) Field inside the material is reduced. (iv) Field inside is slightly enhanced.

question_text: A long straight wire of radius a carries a steady current I . The current is uniformly distributed across its area of cross-section. The ratio of magnitude of magnetic field {tex}\vec{B}_1{/tex} at {tex}\frac{a}{2}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{B}_2{/tex} at distance 2a is

options.explanation: Consider two amperian loops of radius {tex}\frac a2{/tex} and 2a as shown in the diagram. Applying ampere's circuital law for these loops, we get {tex}\oint{/tex} B. {tex}d L=\mu_0 I_{\text {enclosed }}{/tex} For the smaller loop, {tex} \Rightarrow B \times 2 \pi \frac{a}{2}=\mu_0 \times \frac{1}{\pi a^2} \times\left(\frac{a}{2}\right)^2 {/tex} {tex} =\mu_0 I \times \frac{1}{4}=\frac{\mu_0 I}{4} {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow B_1=\frac{\mu_0 I}{4 \pi a} {/tex} {tex} B^{\prime} \times 2 \pi(2 a)=\mu_0 I {/tex} {tex} \frac{B}{B^{\prime}}=\frac{\mu_0 I}{4 \pi a} \times \frac{4 \pi a}{\mu_0 I}=1{/tex}

explanation: Consider two amperian loops of radius {tex}\frac a2{/tex} and 2a as shown in the diagram. Applying ampere's circuital law for these loops, we get {tex}\oint{/tex} B. {tex}d L=\mu_0 I_{\text {enclosed }}{/tex} For the smaller loop, {tex} \Rightarrow B \times 2 \pi \frac{a}{2}=\mu_0 \times \frac{1}{\pi a^2} \times\left(\frac{a}{2}\right)^2 {/tex} {tex} =\mu_0 I \times \frac{1}{4}=\frac{\mu_0 I}{4} {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow B_1=\frac{\mu_0 I}{4 \pi a} {/tex} {tex} B^{\prime} \times 2 \pi(2 a)=\mu_0 I {/tex} {tex} \frac{B}{B^{\prime}}=\frac{\mu_0 I}{4 \pi a} \times \frac{4 \pi a}{\mu_0 I}=1{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. State Biot-Savart's law for the magnetic field due to a current carrying element. Use this law to obtain an expression for the magnetic field at the centre of a circular loop of radius 'a' and carrying a current I. Draw the magnetic field lines for a current loop indicating the direction of magnetic field.
  2. An electron is revolving around the nucleus in a circular orbit with a speed of 107 ms-1. If the radius of the orbit is 10-10 m, find the current constituted by the revolving electron in the orbit.
question_text: State Biot-Savart's law for the magnetic field due to a current carrying element. Use this law to obtain an expression for the magnetic field at the centre of a circular loop of radius 'a' and carrying a current I . Draw the magnetic field lines for a current loop indicating the direction of magnetic field. An electron is revolving around the nucleus in a circular orbit with a speed of 10 7 ms -1 . If the radius of the orbit is 10 -10 m, find the current constituted by the revolving electron in the orbit.
explanation: The magnetic field at a point due to a current carrying element is proportional to magnitude of current, element length and inversely proportional to the square of the distance from the element. {tex}\vec{d B}=\frac{\mu_o}{4 \pi} I \frac{\vec{d l} \times \vec{r}}{r^3}{/tex} {tex}|d B|=\frac{\mu_o}{4 \pi} \frac{I d l \sin \theta}{r^2}{/tex} Consider a circular coil of radius a carrying current I. According to Biot-Savart’s law {tex}|\vec{d B}|=\frac{\mu_o}{4 \pi} \frac{I d l \sin \theta}{r^2}{/tex} At point A {tex}\mathrm{I} \vec{d l} \perp \vec{a}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\theta{/tex} = 90 o , sin 90 o = 1 Hence dB = {tex}\frac{\mu_o}{4 \pi} \frac{I d l}{a^2}{/tex} Magnetic field at centre {tex}\mathrm{B}=\int_\limits o^{2 \pi a} d B=\int_\limits0^{2 \pi a} \frac{\mu_o}{4 \pi} \frac{I d l}{a^2}{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{B}=\frac{\mu_o}{4 \pi} \times \frac{I}{a^2} \times 2 \pi a{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{B}=\frac{\mu_0 I}{2 a}{/tex} q = e, v = 10 7 ms -1 , r = 10 -10 m {tex}\mathrm{i}=\frac{q}{T}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\mathrm{qv}}{2 \pi \mathrm{r}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\mathrm{eV}}{2 \pi \mathrm{r}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 10^7}{2 \times \pi \times 10^{-10}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{0.8}{\pi} \times{/tex} 10 -2 A = 0.255 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -2 A = 2.55 mA
solution: The magnetic field at a point due to a current carrying element is proportional to magnitude of current, element length and inversely proportional to the square of the distance from the element. {tex}\vec{d B}=\frac{\mu_o}{4 \pi} I \frac{\vec{d l} \times \vec{r}}{r^3}{/tex} {tex}|d B|=\frac{\mu_o}{4 \pi} \frac{I d l \sin \theta}{r^2}{/tex} Consider a circular coil of radius a carrying current I. According to Biot-Savart’s law {tex}|\vec{d B}|=\frac{\mu_o}{4 \pi} \frac{I d l \sin \theta}{r^2}{/tex} At point A {tex}\mathrm{I} \vec{d l} \perp \vec{a}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\theta{/tex} = 90 o , sin 90 o = 1 Hence dB = {tex}\frac{\mu_o}{4 \pi} \frac{I d l}{a^2}{/tex} Magnetic field at centre {tex}\mathrm{B}=\int_\limits o^{2 \pi a} d B=\int_\limits0^{2 \pi a} \frac{\mu_o}{4 \pi} \frac{I d l}{a^2}{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{B}=\frac{\mu_o}{4 \pi} \times \frac{I}{a^2} \times 2 \pi a{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{B}=\frac{\mu_0 I}{2 a}{/tex} q = e, v = 10 7 ms -1 , r = 10 -10 m {tex}\mathrm{i}=\frac{q}{T}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\mathrm{qv}}{2 \pi \mathrm{r}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\mathrm{eV}}{2 \pi \mathrm{r}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 10^7}{2 \times \pi \times 10^{-10}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{0.8}{\pi} \times{/tex} 10 -2 A = 0.255 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -2 A = 2.55 mA
solution_html:
1. The magnetic field at a point due to a current carrying element is proportional to magnitude of current, element length and inversely proportional to the square of the distance from the element.
    {tex}\vec{d B}=\frac{\mu_o}{4 \pi} I \frac{\vec{d l} \times \vec{r}}{r^3}{/tex}
    {tex}|d B|=\frac{\mu_o}{4 \pi} \frac{I d l \sin \theta}{r^2}{/tex}

    Consider a circular coil of radius a carrying current I.

    According to Biot-Savart’s law
    {tex}|\vec{d B}|=\frac{\mu_o}{4 \pi} \frac{I d l \sin \theta}{r^2}{/tex}
    At point A
    {tex}\mathrm{I} \vec{d l} \perp \vec{a}{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\theta{/tex} = 90o, sin 90o = 1
    Hence dB = {tex}\frac{\mu_o}{4 \pi} \frac{I d l}{a^2}{/tex}
    Magnetic field at centre
    {tex}\mathrm{B}=\int_\limits o^{2 \pi a} d B=\int_\limits0^{2 \pi a} \frac{\mu_o}{4 \pi} \frac{I d l}{a^2}{/tex}
    {tex}\mathrm{B}=\frac{\mu_o}{4 \pi} \times \frac{I}{a^2} \times 2 \pi a{/tex}
    {tex}\mathrm{B}=\frac{\mu_0 I}{2 a}{/tex}
  2. q = e, v = 107 ms-1, r = 10-10 m
    {tex}\mathrm{i}=\frac{q}{T}{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{\mathrm{qv}}{2 \pi \mathrm{r}}{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{\mathrm{eV}}{2 \pi \mathrm{r}}{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 10^7}{2 \times \pi \times 10^{-10}}{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{0.8}{\pi} \times{/tex} 10-2 A
    = 0.255 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-2 A = 2.55 mA

subject: Physics
question_html: A wire of length L is bent round in the form of (i) a square, and then (ii) an equilateral triangle. If current I is passed through each of them, find the ratio of magnetic moment of the square loop to that of the triangle.
question_text: A wire of length L is bent round in the form of (i) a square, and then (ii) an equilateral triangle. If current I is passed through each of them, find the ratio of magnetic moment of the square loop to that of the triangle.
explanation: For square loop; L = 4a {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a = {tex}\frac{L}{4}{/tex} Area of square loop = {tex}\frac{L^2}{16}{/tex} For equilateral triangle; L = 3a {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a = {tex}\frac{L}{3}{/tex} Area of equilateral triangle = {tex}\frac{\sqrt{3}}{4}{/tex} a 2 {tex}=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{4}\left(\frac{L^2}{9}\right)=\frac{\sqrt{3} L^2}{36}{/tex} Magnetic moment {tex}\alpha{/tex} Area of the loop {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{m_s}{m_t}=\frac{L^2}{16} \times \frac{36}{\sqrt{3} L^2}=\frac{3 \sqrt{3}}{4}{/tex}
solution: For square loop; L = 4a {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a = {tex}\frac{L}{4}{/tex} Area of square loop = {tex}\frac{L^2}{16}{/tex} For equilateral triangle; L = 3a {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a = {tex}\frac{L}{3}{/tex} Area of equilateral triangle = {tex}\frac{\sqrt{3}}{4}{/tex} a 2 {tex}=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{4}\left(\frac{L^2}{9}\right)=\frac{\sqrt{3} L^2}{36}{/tex} Magnetic moment {tex}\alpha{/tex} Area of the loop {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{m_s}{m_t}=\frac{L^2}{16} \times \frac{36}{\sqrt{3} L^2}=\frac{3 \sqrt{3}}{4}{/tex}
solution_html:
For square loop; L = 4a {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a = {tex}\frac{L}{4}{/tex}
Area of square loop = {tex}\frac{L^2}{16}{/tex}
For equilateral triangle; L = 3a {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a = {tex}\frac{L}{3}{/tex}
Area of equilateral triangle = {tex}\frac{\sqrt{3}}{4}{/tex} a2
{tex}=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{4}\left(\frac{L^2}{9}\right)=\frac{\sqrt{3} L^2}{36}{/tex}
Magnetic moment {tex}\alpha{/tex} Area of the loop
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{m_s}{m_t}=\frac{L^2}{16} \times \frac{36}{\sqrt{3} L^2}=\frac{3 \sqrt{3}}{4}{/tex}

The assertion is true. Since, M = IA, {tex}\therefore{/tex} M {tex}\propto{/tex} A. The reason is true. Also reason is the proper explanation of the assertion.

question_html: The magnetic moment of a circular coil carrying current I, having N turns, each of radius r, is M. Find the magnetic moment of the same coil if it is unwound and rewound into a coil having 2N turns for the same current.
question_text: The magnetic moment of a circular coil carrying current I, having N turns, each of radius r, is M. Find the magnetic moment of the same coil if it is unwound and rewound into a coil having 2N turns for the same current.
explanation: N( {tex}2 \pi \mathrm{r}{/tex} ) = 2 N {tex}\left(2 \pi r^{\prime}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} r' = {tex}\frac{r}{2}{/tex} {tex}\because{/tex} M = NIA = NI {tex}\left(\pi r^2\right){/tex} M' = (2N)(I) {tex}\left(\frac{\pi r^2}{4}\right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{M}{2}{/tex}
solution: N( {tex}2 \pi \mathrm{r}{/tex} ) = 2 N {tex}\left(2 \pi r^{\prime}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} r' = {tex}\frac{r}{2}{/tex} {tex}\because{/tex} M = NIA = NI {tex}\left(\pi r^2\right){/tex} M' = (2N)(I) {tex}\left(\frac{\pi r^2}{4}\right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{M}{2}{/tex}
solution_html:
N({tex}2 \pi \mathrm{r}{/tex}) = 2 N{tex}\left(2 \pi r^{\prime}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} r' = {tex}\frac{r}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\because{/tex} M = NIA = NI{tex}\left(\pi r^2\right){/tex}
M' = (2N)(I){tex}\left(\frac{\pi r^2}{4}\right){/tex}= {tex}\frac{M}{2}{/tex}

question_html:
1. An {tex}\alpha{/tex}-particle, a deuteron and a proton enter into a uniform magnetic field normally with the same kinetic energy and describe circular paths. Find the ratio of radii of their paths.
  2. Give the direction of magnetic field acting on the current carrying coil ACDE shown in the figure so that the coil is in unstable equilibrium.
  3. Why do we use a low resistance ammeter in a circuit to measure current?
question_text: An {tex}\alpha{/tex} -particle, a deuteron and a proton enter into a uniform magnetic field normally with the same kinetic energy and describe circular paths. Find the ratio of radii of their paths. Give the direction of magnetic field acting on the current carrying coil ACDE shown in the figure so that the coil is in unstable equilibrium. Why do we use a low resistance ammeter in a circuit to measure current?
explanation: {tex}\frac{1}{2} m_\alpha v_\alpha^2{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2} m_p v_p^2 {/tex} {tex}\frac{V_\alpha}{V_p}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{\frac{m_p}{m_\alpha}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} Now, {tex}\frac{1}{2} m_d v_d^2{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2} m_p v_p^2{/tex} {tex}\frac{v_d}{v_p}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex} Radius of the circular path r = {tex}\frac{m v}{B q} {/tex} {tex}\frac{r_\alpha}{r_p}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{1}{/tex} & {tex}\frac{r_d}{r_p}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\sqrt{2}}{1}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} r a : r d : r p = 1 : {tex}\sqrt{2}{/tex} : 1 Magnetic field is along Z axis Low resistance ammeter does not affect the net current drawn from the battery.
solution: {tex}\frac{1}{2} m_\alpha v_\alpha^2{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2} m_p v_p^2 {/tex} {tex}\frac{V_\alpha}{V_p}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{\frac{m_p}{m_\alpha}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} Now, {tex}\frac{1}{2} m_d v_d^2{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2} m_p v_p^2{/tex} {tex}\frac{v_d}{v_p}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex} Radius of the circular path r = {tex}\frac{m v}{B q} {/tex} {tex}\frac{r_\alpha}{r_p}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{1}{/tex} & {tex}\frac{r_d}{r_p}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\sqrt{2}}{1}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} r a : r d : r p = 1 : {tex}\sqrt{2}{/tex} : 1 Magnetic field is along Z axis Low resistance ammeter does not affect the net current drawn from the battery.
solution_html:
1. {tex}\frac{1}{2} m_\alpha v_\alpha^2{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2} m_p v_p^2 {/tex}
    {tex}\frac{V_\alpha}{V_p}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{\frac{m_p}{m_\alpha}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
    Now,
    {tex}\frac{1}{2} m_d v_d^2{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2} m_p v_p^2{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{v_d}{v_p}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex}
    Radius of the circular path
    r = {tex}\frac{m v}{B q} {/tex}
    {tex}\frac{r_\alpha}{r_p}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{1}{/tex} & {tex}\frac{r_d}{r_p}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\sqrt{2}}{1}{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} ra : rd : rp = 1 : {tex}\sqrt{2}{/tex} : 1
  2. Magnetic field is along Z axis
  3. Low resistance ammeter does not affect the net current drawn from the battery.

question_html:
1. Draw a diagram to show the magnetic field lines produced by two parallel straight wires carrying currents in the same direction. Obtain an expression for the force per unit length between these wires and hence define SI unit of current.
  2. The figure shows a circular loop connected to a battery. The arc ACB of length l1 carries a current I1 and arc ADB of length l2 carries a current I2. Show that the net magnetic field at the centre of the loop is zero.
question_text: Draw a diagram to show the magnetic field lines produced by two parallel straight wires carrying currents in the same direction. Obtain an expression for the force per unit length between these wires and hence define SI unit of current. The figure shows a circular loop connected to a battery. The arc ACB of length l 1 carries a current I 1 and arc ADB of length l 2 carries a current I 2 . Show that the net magnetic field at the centre of the loop is zero.
explanation: Magnetic field induction at a point P on conductor C 2 D 2 due to current I 1 passing through C 1 D 1 is B 1 = {tex}\frac{\mu_o I_1}{2 \pi r}{/tex} where r is the separation between two conductors. According to right hand thumb rule, the direction of magnetic field B 1 is perpendicular to the plane of paper, directed inwards. As the current carrying conductor {tex}\mathrm{C}_2 \mathrm{D}_2 {/tex} lies in the magnetic field B 1 (produced by the current through C 1 D 1 therefore , the length of C 2 D 2 will experience a force given by F 2 = B 1 I 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 = B 1 I 2 1 Putting the value of B1, we have {tex}\frac{F_2}{l}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mu_o}{2 \Pi} \cdot \frac{I_1 I_2}{r}{/tex} The ampere is the value of that steady current which, when maintained in each of the two very long straight, parallel conductors of negligible cross section and placed in one meter apart in vacuum would produce of each of these conductors a force equal to 2 {tex} \times{/tex} 10 -7 Newton per meter of length. B 1 = {tex}\frac{\mu_o I_1 \alpha}{2 r}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mu_o I_1 l_1}{2 r^2}{/tex} vertically outward B 2 = {tex}\frac{\mu_o I_2 \beta}{2 r}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mu_o I_2 l_2}{2 r^2}{/tex} vertically inward From calculation, using potential difference is same in parallel, {tex} I_1 l_1=I_2 l_2{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{B}_1=\mathrm{B}_2{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Net magnetic field {tex} \mathbf{B}=\mathbf{B}_1+\mathbf{B}_2{/tex} = 0 {tex}B_{A C B}-B_{A D B}{/tex} = 0
solution: Magnetic field induction at a point P on conductor C 2 D 2 due to current I 1 passing through C 1 D 1 is B 1 = {tex}\frac{\mu_o I_1}{2 \pi r}{/tex} where r is the separation between two conductors. According to right hand thumb rule, the direction of magnetic field B 1 is perpendicular to the plane of paper, directed inwards. As the current carrying conductor {tex}\mathrm{C}_2 \mathrm{D}_2 {/tex} lies in the magnetic field B 1 (produced by the current through C 1 D 1 therefore , the length of C 2 D 2 will experience a force given by F 2 = B 1 I 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 = B 1 I 2 1 Putting the value of B1, we have {tex}\frac{F_2}{l}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mu_o}{2 \Pi} \cdot \frac{I_1 I_2}{r}{/tex} The ampere is the value of that steady current which, when maintained in each of the two very long straight, parallel conductors of negligible cross section and placed in one meter apart in vacuum would produce of each of these conductors a force equal to 2 {tex} \times{/tex} 10 -7 Newton per meter of length. B 1 = {tex}\frac{\mu_o I_1 \alpha}{2 r}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mu_o I_1 l_1}{2 r^2}{/tex} vertically outward B 2 = {tex}\frac{\mu_o I_2 \beta}{2 r}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mu_o I_2 l_2}{2 r^2}{/tex} vertically inward From calculation, using potential difference is same in parallel, {tex} I_1 l_1=I_2 l_2{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{B}_1=\mathrm{B}_2{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Net magnetic field {tex} \mathbf{B}=\mathbf{B}_1+\mathbf{B}_2{/tex} = 0 {tex}B_{A C B}-B_{A D B}{/tex} = 0
solution_html:
1. Magnetic field induction at a point P on conductor C2 D2 due to current I1 passing through C1 D1 is

    B1 = {tex}\frac{\mu_o I_1}{2 \pi r}{/tex} where r is the separation between two conductors.
    According to right hand thumb rule, the direction of magnetic field B1 is perpendicular to the plane of paper, directed inwards.
    As the current carrying conductor {tex}\mathrm{C}_2 \mathrm{D}_2 {/tex} lies in the magnetic field B1 (produced by the current through C1D1 therefore , the length of C2 D2 will experience a force given by
    F2 = B1 I2 {tex}\times{/tex}1 = B1 I2 1
    Putting the value of B1, we have {tex}\frac{F_2}{l}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mu_o}{2 \Pi} \cdot \frac{I_1 I_2}{r}{/tex}
    The ampere is the value of that steady current which, when maintained in each of the two very long straight, parallel conductors of negligible cross section and placed in one meter apart in vacuum would produce of each of these conductors a force equal to 2 {tex} \times{/tex} 10-7 Newton per meter of length.

  2. B1 = {tex}\frac{\mu_o I_1 \alpha}{2 r}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mu_o I_1 l_1}{2 r^2}{/tex} vertically outward
    B2 = {tex}\frac{\mu_o I_2 \beta}{2 r}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mu_o I_2 l_2}{2 r^2}{/tex} vertically inward

From calculation, using potential difference is same in parallel,{tex} I_1 l_1=I_2 l_2{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{B}_1=\mathrm{B}_2{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}Net magnetic field {tex} \mathbf{B}=\mathbf{B}_1+\mathbf{B}_2{/tex} = 0
{tex}B_{A C B}-B_{A D B}{/tex} = 0

J/E= {tex}\sigma{/tex}

question_html:
Two long straight parallel wires A and B separated by a distance d, carry equal current I flowing in same direction as shown in the figure.

  1. Find the magnetic field at a point P situated between them at a distance x from one wire.
  2. Show graphically the variation of the magnetic field with distance x for 0 < x < d.
question_text: Two long straight parallel wires A and B separated by a distance d, carry equal current I flowing in same direction as shown in the figure. Find the magnetic field at a point P situated between them at a distance x from one wire. Show graphically the variation of the magnetic field with distance x for 0 < x < d.
explanation: Field at P due to wire at A {tex}\vec B_A{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I}{2 \pi x}{/tex} (upward) Field at P due to wire at B {tex}\vec B_B{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I}{2 \pi(d-x)}{/tex} (downward) Therefore, total field at P {tex}\vec{B}=\vec{B}_{A}+\vec{B}_{B}{/tex} {tex}\vec B{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I}{2 \pi x}-\frac{\mu_{0} I}{2 \pi(d-x)}{/tex} {tex}\vec B{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I}{2 \pi}\left[\frac{1}{x}-\frac{1}{d-x}\right]{/tex} {tex}\vec B{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I}{2 \pi}\left[\frac{d-x-x}{x(d-x)}\right]{/tex} {tex}\vec B{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I(d-2 x)}{2 \pi x(d-x)}{/tex} upward variation of magneic field with distance x is shown in figure:-
solution: Field at P due to wire at A {tex}\vec B_A{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I}{2 \pi x}{/tex} (upward) Field at P due to wire at B {tex}\vec B_B{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I}{2 \pi(d-x)}{/tex} (downward) Therefore, total field at P {tex}\vec{B}=\vec{B}_{A}+\vec{B}_{B}{/tex} {tex}\vec B{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I}{2 \pi x}-\frac{\mu_{0} I}{2 \pi(d-x)}{/tex} {tex}\vec B{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I}{2 \pi}\left[\frac{1}{x}-\frac{1}{d-x}\right]{/tex} {tex}\vec B{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I}{2 \pi}\left[\frac{d-x-x}{x(d-x)}\right]{/tex} {tex}\vec B{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I(d-2 x)}{2 \pi x(d-x)}{/tex} upward variation of magneic field with distance x is shown in figure:-
solution_html:
1. Field at P due to wire at A
    {tex}\vec B_A{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I}{2 \pi x}{/tex} (upward)
    Field at P due to wire at B
    {tex}\vec B_B{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I}{2 \pi(d-x)}{/tex} (downward)
    Therefore, total field at P
    {tex}\vec{B}=\vec{B}_{A}+\vec{B}_{B}{/tex} 
    {tex}\vec B{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I}{2 \pi x}-\frac{\mu_{0} I}{2 \pi(d-x)}{/tex} 
    {tex}\vec B{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I}{2 \pi}\left[\frac{1}{x}-\frac{1}{d-x}\right]{/tex} 
    {tex}\vec B{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I}{2 \pi}\left[\frac{d-x-x}{x(d-x)}\right]{/tex} 
    {tex}\vec B{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I(d-2 x)}{2 \pi x(d-x)}{/tex} upward

  2. variation of magneic field with distance x is shown in figure:-

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. Write an expression of magnetic moment associated with a current (I) carrying circular coil of radius r having N turns.
  2. Consider the above mentioned coil placed in YZ plane with its centre at the origin. Derive expression for the value of magnetic field due to it at point (x, 0, 0).
question_text: Write an expression of magnetic moment associated with a current (I) carrying circular coil of radius r having N turns. Consider the above mentioned coil placed in YZ plane with its centre at the origin. Derive expression for the value of magnetic field due to it at point (x, 0, 0).
explanation: Magnetic moment associated with a current carrying circular coil of radius r having N turns, {tex}\vec M{/tex} = {tex}N I\left(\pi r^{2}\right) \hat{n}{/tex} From Biot-savart law, the magnetic field at point P(x, 0,0) due to current element {tex}\vec{d l}{/tex} , a 2 =r 2 +x 2 {tex}\vec{dB}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I d l \sin 90^{\circ}}{4 \pi \ a^2} {/tex} Now, the vertical component {tex}\vec{dB}{/tex} cos {tex}\theta{/tex} will cancel out for entire coil. So, {tex}\vec B{/tex} at P {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} thus the magnetic field is given by:- B = {tex}\int d B \sin \theta{/tex} B = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I}{4 \pi a^{2}} \sin \theta \int d l{/tex} Now {tex}\int d l=2 \pi r{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} B = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I}{4 \pi a^{2}} \times \frac{r}{a} \times 2 \pi r\left[\because \sin \theta=\frac{r}{a}\right]{/tex} B = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I r^{2}}{2 a^{3}}{/tex} {tex}\vec B{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I r^{2}}{2\left(r^{2}+x^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}} \hat{i}{/tex} For coil having N turns, {tex}\vec B{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I N r^{2}}{2\left(r^{2}+x^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}} \hat{i}{/tex}
solution: Magnetic moment associated with a current carrying circular coil of radius r having N turns, {tex}\vec M{/tex} = {tex}N I\left(\pi r^{2}\right) \hat{n}{/tex} From Biot-savart law, the magnetic field at point P(x, 0,0) due to current element {tex}\vec{d l}{/tex} , a 2 =r 2 +x 2 {tex}\vec{dB}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I d l \sin 90^{\circ}}{4 \pi \ a^2} {/tex} Now, the vertical component {tex}\vec{dB}{/tex} cos {tex}\theta{/tex} will cancel out for entire coil. So, {tex}\vec B{/tex} at P {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} thus the magnetic field is given by:- B = {tex}\int d B \sin \theta{/tex} B = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I}{4 \pi a^{2}} \sin \theta \int d l{/tex} Now {tex}\int d l=2 \pi r{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} B = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I}{4 \pi a^{2}} \times \frac{r}{a} \times 2 \pi r\left[\because \sin \theta=\frac{r}{a}\right]{/tex} B = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I r^{2}}{2 a^{3}}{/tex} {tex}\vec B{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I r^{2}}{2\left(r^{2}+x^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}} \hat{i}{/tex} For coil having N turns, {tex}\vec B{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I N r^{2}}{2\left(r^{2}+x^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}} \hat{i}{/tex}
solution_html:
1. Magnetic moment associated with a current carrying circular coil of radius r having N turns,
    {tex}\vec M{/tex} = {tex}N I\left(\pi r^{2}\right) \hat{n}{/tex} 

  2. From Biot-savart law, the magnetic field at point P(x, 0,0) due to current element {tex}\vec{d l}{/tex}, a2 =r2 +x2
    {tex}\vec{dB}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I d l \sin 90^{\circ}}{4 \pi \ a^2} {/tex} 
    Now, the vertical component {tex}\vec{dB}{/tex} cos {tex}\theta{/tex} will cancel out for entire coil. So,
    {tex}\vec B{/tex} at P {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}thus the magnetic field is given by:- B = {tex}\int d B \sin \theta{/tex} 
    B = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I}{4 \pi a^{2}} \sin \theta \int d l{/tex} 
    Now {tex}\int d l=2 \pi r{/tex} 
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} B = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I}{4 \pi a^{2}} \times \frac{r}{a} \times 2 \pi r\left[\because \sin \theta=\frac{r}{a}\right]{/tex} 
    B = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I r^{2}}{2 a^{3}}{/tex} 
    {tex}\vec B{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I r^{2}}{2\left(r^{2}+x^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}} \hat{i}{/tex} 
    For coil having N turns,
    {tex}\vec B{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I N r^{2}}{2\left(r^{2}+x^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}} \hat{i}{/tex}

question_html:
1. Define current sensitivity of a galvanometer. Write its expression.
  2. A galvanometer has resistance G and shows full scale deflection for current Ig.
    1. How can it be converted into an ammeter to measure current up to I0 (I0 > Ig)?
    2. What is the effective resistance of this ammeter?
question_text: Define current sensitivity of a galvanometer. Write its expression. A galvanometer has resistance G and shows full scale deflection for current I g . How can it be converted into an ammeter to measure current up to I 0 (I 0 > I g )? What is the effective resistance of this ammeter?
explanation: Current sensitivity: It is defined as the deflection produced in the galvanometer when a unit current flows through it. I = k n A B θ, Where N = no. of turns in the coil, B = magnetic field, A = area of the coil of galvanometer Or The sensitivity (i.e. current sensitivity) of a galvanometer is defined as the angle of deflection per unit current flowing through it. Galvanometer can be converted into an ammeter by connecting a shunt (small resistance) S with parallel to the galvanometer. As galvanometer and shunt are connected in parallel, so, Potential across G = Potential across S I g G = (I - I g )S {tex}\therefore{/tex} S = {tex}\frac{I_{g}}{I-I_{g}} G{/tex} Effective resistance of this ammeter will be {tex}\frac{1}{R_{s}}=\frac{1}{G}+\frac{1}{S}{/tex} R A = {tex}\frac{G S}{G+S}{/tex}
solution: Current sensitivity: It is defined as the deflection produced in the galvanometer when a unit current flows through it. I = k n A B θ, Where N = no. of turns in the coil, B = magnetic field, A = area of the coil of galvanometer Or The sensitivity (i.e. current sensitivity) of a galvanometer is defined as the angle of deflection per unit current flowing through it. Galvanometer can be converted into an ammeter by connecting a shunt (small resistance) S with parallel to the galvanometer. As galvanometer and shunt are connected in parallel, so, Potential across G = Potential across S I g G = (I - I g )S {tex}\therefore{/tex} S = {tex}\frac{I_{g}}{I-I_{g}} G{/tex} Effective resistance of this ammeter will be {tex}\frac{1}{R_{s}}=\frac{1}{G}+\frac{1}{S}{/tex} R A = {tex}\frac{G S}{G+S}{/tex}
solution_html:
1. Current sensitivity: It is defined as the deflection produced in the galvanometer when a unit current flows through it.
     I = k n A B θ, Where N = no. of turns in the coil, B = magnetic field, A = area of the coil of galvanometer Or The sensitivity (i.e. current sensitivity) of a galvanometer is defined as the angle of deflection per unit current flowing through it.

    1. Galvanometer can be converted into an ammeter by connecting a shunt (small resistance) S with parallel to the galvanometer.
      As galvanometer and shunt are connected in parallel, so,
      Potential across G = Potential across S
      Ig G = (I - Ig)S
      {tex}\therefore{/tex} S = {tex}\frac{I_{g}}{I-I_{g}} G{/tex} 
    2. Effective resistance of this ammeter will be
      {tex}\frac{1}{R_{s}}=\frac{1}{G}+\frac{1}{S}{/tex} 
      RA = {tex}\frac{G S}{G+S}{/tex}

question_html: An isosceles right angled current carrying loop PQR is placed in a uniform magnetic field {tex}\vec B{/tex} pointing along PR. If the magnetic force acting on the arm PQ is F, then the magnetic force which acts on the arm QR will be
question_text: An isosceles right angled current carrying loop PQR is placed in a uniform magnetic field {tex}\vec B{/tex} pointing along PR. If the magnetic force acting on the arm PQ is F, then the magnetic force which acts on the arm QR will be

options.explanation: Force along PR is zero as magnetic field is along PR. The net force on a current-carrying loop is always zero. Hence on AC, f net = f PQ +f PR + f QR {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 0 = F+ 0+ (F on QR) Hence, F on QR is -F.

explanation: Force along PR is zero as magnetic field is along PR. The net force on a current-carrying loop is always zero. Hence on AC, f net = f PQ +f PR + f QR {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 0 = F+ 0+ (F on QR) Hence, F on QR is -F.

question_html:
1. A circular loop of radius R carries a current I. Obtain an expression for the magnetic field at a point on its axis at a distance x from its centre.
  2. A conducting rod of length 2 m is placed on a horizontal table in north-south direction. It carries a current of 5 A from south to north. Find the direction and magnitude of the magnetic force acting on the rod. Given that the Earth's magnetic field at the place is 0.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 T and angle of dip is {tex}\frac{\pi}{6}{/tex}.
question_text: A circular loop of radius R carries a current I. Obtain an expression for the magnetic field at a point on its axis at a distance x from its centre. A conducting rod of length 2 m is placed on a horizontal table in north-south direction. It carries a current of 5 A from south to north. Find the direction and magnitude of the magnetic force acting on the rod. Given that the Earth's magnetic field at the place is 0.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -4 T and angle of dip is {tex}\frac{\pi}{6}{/tex} .
explanation: Consider two small current elements on the circular loop which are opposite to each other. then the magnetic field at a point p which is at a distance X from the centre of the coil is due to the x component of the fields of A and B and y components cancel out. {tex}\because{/tex} dB X = {tex}\frac{\mu_0}{4 \pi} \frac{I d l \times \sin (90)}{\left(\sqrt{R^2+X^2}\right)^2}{/tex} cos {tex}\theta{/tex} dB X = {tex}\frac{\mu_0 I d l}{4 \pi} \times \frac{1}{\left(\sqrt{R^2+X^2}\right)^2} {/tex} {tex}\times \frac{R}{\sqrt{R^2+X^2}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mu_0 I d l}{4 \pi} \frac{R}{\left(X^2+R^2\right)^{\frac{3}{2}}}{/tex} B = {tex}\int{/tex} dBX = {tex}\frac{\mu_0 I R}{4 \pi\left(x^2+R^2\right)^{\frac{3}{2}}}{/tex} {tex}\int{/tex} dl = {tex}\frac{\mu_0 I \times 2 \pi R^2}{4 \pi\left(x^2+R^2\right)^{\frac{3}{2}}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mu_0 I R^2}{2\left(x^2+R^2\right)^{\frac{3}{2}}}{/tex} F = ilB sin {tex}\theta{/tex} = 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -4 {tex}\frac{sin \pi}{6}{/tex} = 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -4 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.5 = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -4 N Towards east
solution: Consider two small current elements on the circular loop which are opposite to each other. then the magnetic field at a point p which is at a distance X from the centre of the coil is due to the x component of the fields of A and B and y components cancel out. {tex}\because{/tex} dB X = {tex}\frac{\mu_0}{4 \pi} \frac{I d l \times \sin (90)}{\left(\sqrt{R^2+X^2}\right)^2}{/tex} cos {tex}\theta{/tex} dB X = {tex}\frac{\mu_0 I d l}{4 \pi} \times \frac{1}{\left(\sqrt{R^2+X^2}\right)^2} {/tex} {tex}\times \frac{R}{\sqrt{R^2+X^2}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mu_0 I d l}{4 \pi} \frac{R}{\left(X^2+R^2\right)^{\frac{3}{2}}}{/tex} B = {tex}\int{/tex} dBX = {tex}\frac{\mu_0 I R}{4 \pi\left(x^2+R^2\right)^{\frac{3}{2}}}{/tex} {tex}\int{/tex} dl = {tex}\frac{\mu_0 I \times 2 \pi R^2}{4 \pi\left(x^2+R^2\right)^{\frac{3}{2}}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mu_0 I R^2}{2\left(x^2+R^2\right)^{\frac{3}{2}}}{/tex} F = ilB sin {tex}\theta{/tex} = 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -4 {tex}\frac{sin \pi}{6}{/tex} = 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -4 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.5 = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -4 N Towards east
solution_html:
1. Consider two small current elements on the circular loop which are opposite to each other.
    then the magnetic field at a point p which is at a distance X from the centre of the coil is due to the x component of the fields of A and B and y components cancel out.
    {tex}\because{/tex} dBX = {tex}\frac{\mu_0}{4 \pi} \frac{I d l \times \sin (90)}{\left(\sqrt{R^2+X^2}\right)^2}{/tex} cos{tex}\theta{/tex}
    dBX = {tex}\frac{\mu_0 I d l}{4 \pi} \times \frac{1}{\left(\sqrt{R^2+X^2}\right)^2} {/tex}{tex}\times \frac{R}{\sqrt{R^2+X^2}}{/tex}
    = {tex}\frac{\mu_0 I d l}{4 \pi} \frac{R}{\left(X^2+R^2\right)^{\frac{3}{2}}}{/tex}
    B = {tex}\int{/tex}dBX = {tex}\frac{\mu_0 I R}{4 \pi\left(x^2+R^2\right)^{\frac{3}{2}}}{/tex}{tex}\int{/tex}dl
    = {tex}\frac{\mu_0 I \times 2 \pi R^2}{4 \pi\left(x^2+R^2\right)^{\frac{3}{2}}}{/tex}
    = {tex}\frac{\mu_0 I R^2}{2\left(x^2+R^2\right)^{\frac{3}{2}}}{/tex}
  2. F = ilB sin{tex}\theta{/tex} = 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.6 {tex}\times{/tex}10-4{tex}\frac{sin \pi}{6}{/tex}
    = 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.5
    = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 N
    Towards east

question_html: A galvanometer of resistance 16 {tex}\Omega{/tex} shows full scale deflection for a current of 4 mA. How will you convert it into a voltmeter to measure a voltage up to 3 V?
question_text: A galvanometer of resistance 16 {tex}\Omega{/tex} shows full scale deflection for a current of 4 mA. How will you convert it into a voltmeter to measure a voltage up to 3 V?
explanation: R = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{v}}{\mathrm{Ig}}-\mathrm{G}{/tex} Here V = 3 V I g = 4 mA = 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 A G = 16 {tex}\Omega{/tex} R = {tex}\frac{3}{4 \times 10^{-3}}-16{/tex} = {tex}\frac{3 \times 1000}{4}-16{/tex} = 734 {tex}\Omega{/tex} Hence, in order to measure a voltage upto 3 V, a resistance of 734 {tex}\Omega{/tex} in series is added with the galvanometer.
solution: R = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{v}}{\mathrm{Ig}}-\mathrm{G}{/tex} Here V = 3 V I g = 4 mA = 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 A G = 16 {tex}\Omega{/tex} R = {tex}\frac{3}{4 \times 10^{-3}}-16{/tex} = {tex}\frac{3 \times 1000}{4}-16{/tex} = 734 {tex}\Omega{/tex} Hence, in order to measure a voltage upto 3 V, a resistance of 734 {tex}\Omega{/tex} in series is added with the galvanometer.
solution_html:
R = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{v}}{\mathrm{Ig}}-\mathrm{G}{/tex}
Here V = 3 V
Ig = 4 mA = 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 A
G = 16 {tex}\Omega{/tex}
R = {tex}\frac{3}{4 \times 10^{-3}}-16{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{3 \times 1000}{4}-16{/tex}
= 734 {tex}\Omega{/tex}
Hence, in order to measure a voltage upto 3 V, a resistance of  734 {tex}\Omega{/tex} in series is added with the galvanometer.

question_html: A circular loop carrying a current 5 A, produces a magnetic field of {tex}\pi{/tex} mT, at its centre. Find the value of the magnetic moment of the loop.
question_text: A circular loop carrying a current 5 A, produces a magnetic field of {tex}\pi{/tex} mT, at its centre. Find the value of the magnetic moment of the loop.
explanation: B = {tex}\frac{\mu o i}{2 r}{/tex} or r = {tex}\frac{\mu o i}{2 B}{/tex} r = {tex}\frac{4 \pi \times 5 \times 10^{-7}}{2 \pi \times 10^{-3}}{/tex} = 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 m m = iA = i {tex}\pi{/tex} r 2 = 5 {tex}\pi{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -6 A-m 2 = 1.57 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -5 A-m 2 Hence, the magnetic moment of the loop is 1.57 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -5 A-m 2
solution: B = {tex}\frac{\mu o i}{2 r}{/tex} or r = {tex}\frac{\mu o i}{2 B}{/tex} r = {tex}\frac{4 \pi \times 5 \times 10^{-7}}{2 \pi \times 10^{-3}}{/tex} = 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 m m = iA = i {tex}\pi{/tex} r 2 = 5 {tex}\pi{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -6 A-m 2 = 1.57 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -5 A-m 2 Hence, the magnetic moment of the loop is 1.57 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -5 A-m 2
solution_html:
B = {tex}\frac{\mu o i}{2 r}{/tex} or r = {tex}\frac{\mu o i}{2 B}{/tex}
r = {tex}\frac{4 \pi \times 5 \times 10^{-7}}{2 \pi \times 10^{-3}}{/tex} = 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 m
m = iA = i {tex}\pi{/tex}r2
= 5 {tex}\pi{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 10-6 A-m2
= 1.57 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 A-m2
Hence, the magnetic moment of the loop is 1.57 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 A-m2

question_html:
1. Derive the expression for the force acting per unit length between two long straight parallel current carrying conductors. Hence define one ampere.
  2. Two long parallel straight conductors are placed 12 cm apart in air. They carry equal currents of 3 A each. Find the magnitude and direction of the magnetic field at a point midway between them (drawing a figure) when the currents in them flow in opposite directions.
question_text: Derive the expression for the force acting per unit length between two long straight parallel current carrying conductors. Hence define one ampere. Two long parallel straight conductors are placed 12 cm apart in air. They carry equal currents of 3 A each. Find the magnitude and direction of the magnetic field at a point midway between them (drawing a figure) when the currents in them flow in opposite directions.
explanation: B 1 = {tex}\frac{\mu_o I_1}{2 \pi d}{/tex} {tex}\vec {F}{/tex} = I( {tex}\vec{l} \times \vec{B}{/tex} ) F 21 = I 2 l 2 B 1 sin90 o = {tex}I_2 l_2 \frac{\mu_o I_1}{2 \pi d}{/tex} Force per unit length f 21 = {tex}\frac{F_{21}}{l_2}=\frac{\mu_o I_1 I_2}{2 \pi d}{/tex} Definition of 1 ampere - One ampere is defied as that steady current which, when maintained in each of the two very long, straight parallel conductors of negligible cross section, and placed at a distance 1 meter apart in vacuum, will produce on each of the conductors a force equal to 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 −7 N per metre of length. {tex}\vec {B}{/tex} = {tex}\vec{B_1}+\vec{B_2}{/tex} B = {tex}\frac{\mu_o I_1}{2 \pi r_1}+\frac{\mu_o I_2}{2 \pi r_2}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mu_o}{2 \pi}\left(\frac{3}{6 \times 10^{-2}}+\frac{3}{6 \times 10^{-2}}\right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times 3}{\pi \times 6 \times 10^{-2}}{/tex} = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -5 tesla Direction of {tex}\vec {B}{/tex} at midpoint is perpendicular to the plane containing the two conductors and pointing towards downwards.
solution: B 1 = {tex}\frac{\mu_o I_1}{2 \pi d}{/tex} {tex}\vec {F}{/tex} = I( {tex}\vec{l} \times \vec{B}{/tex} ) F 21 = I 2 l 2 B 1 sin90 o = {tex}I_2 l_2 \frac{\mu_o I_1}{2 \pi d}{/tex} Force per unit length f 21 = {tex}\frac{F_{21}}{l_2}=\frac{\mu_o I_1 I_2}{2 \pi d}{/tex} Definition of 1 ampere - One ampere is defied as that steady current which, when maintained in each of the two very long, straight parallel conductors of negligible cross section, and placed at a distance 1 meter apart in vacuum, will produce on each of the conductors a force equal to 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 −7 N per metre of length. {tex}\vec {B}{/tex} = {tex}\vec{B_1}+\vec{B_2}{/tex} B = {tex}\frac{\mu_o I_1}{2 \pi r_1}+\frac{\mu_o I_2}{2 \pi r_2}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mu_o}{2 \pi}\left(\frac{3}{6 \times 10^{-2}}+\frac{3}{6 \times 10^{-2}}\right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times 3}{\pi \times 6 \times 10^{-2}}{/tex} = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -5 tesla Direction of {tex}\vec {B}{/tex} at midpoint is perpendicular to the plane containing the two conductors and pointing towards downwards.
solution_html:
1. B1 = {tex}\frac{\mu_o I_1}{2 \pi d}{/tex}
    {tex}\vec {F}{/tex} = I({tex}\vec{l} \times \vec{B}{/tex})
    F21 = I2l2B1sin90o
    = {tex}I_2 l_2 \frac{\mu_o I_1}{2 \pi d}{/tex}
    Force per unit length
    f21 = {tex}\frac{F_{21}}{l_2}=\frac{\mu_o I_1 I_2}{2 \pi d}{/tex}
    Definition of 1 ampere - One ampere is defied as that steady current which, when maintained in each of the two very long, straight parallel conductors of negligible cross section, and placed at a distance 1 meter apart in vacuum, will produce on each of the conductors a force equal to 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10−7N per metre of length.

  2. {tex}\vec {B}{/tex} = {tex}\vec{B_1}+\vec{B_2}{/tex}
    B = {tex}\frac{\mu_o I_1}{2 \pi r_1}+\frac{\mu_o I_2}{2 \pi r_2}{/tex}
    = {tex}\frac{\mu_o}{2 \pi}\left(\frac{3}{6 \times 10^{-2}}+\frac{3}{6 \times 10^{-2}}\right){/tex}
    = {tex}\frac{4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times 3}{\pi \times 6 \times 10^{-2}}{/tex}
    = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 tesla
    Direction of {tex}\vec {B}{/tex} at midpoint is perpendicular to the plane containing the two conductors and pointing towards downwards.

question_html: Two identical coils P and Q each of radius R are lying in perpendicular planes such that they have a common centre. Find the magnitude and direction of the magnetic field at the common centre when they carry currents equal to I and {tex}\sqrt{3}{/tex} I respectively.
question_text: Two identical coils P and Q each of radius R are lying in perpendicular planes such that they have a common centre. Find the magnitude and direction of the magnetic field at the common centre when they carry currents equal to I and {tex}\sqrt{3}{/tex} I respectively.
explanation: {tex}\vec{B_{p}}=\frac{\mu_{0} I}{2 R}{/tex} , vertically upwards {tex}\vec{{B}_{Q}}=\frac{\mu_{0} \sqrt{3} I}{2 R}{/tex} , along with horizontal The resultant field at the centre is B = {tex}\sqrt{B_{P}^{2}+B_{Q}^{2}}=\left[\left(\frac{\mu_{0} I}{2 R}\right)^{2}+\left(\frac{\mu_{0} \sqrt{3} I}{2 R}\right)^{2}\right]^{1 / 2}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I}{2 R}(1+3)^{1 / 2}=\left(\frac{\mu_{0} I}{R}\right){/tex} {tex}\tan \theta=\frac{B P}{B Q}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\theta{/tex} = 30 o
solution: {tex}\vec{B_{p}}=\frac{\mu_{0} I}{2 R}{/tex} , vertically upwards {tex}\vec{{B}_{Q}}=\frac{\mu_{0} \sqrt{3} I}{2 R}{/tex} , along with horizontal The resultant field at the centre is B = {tex}\sqrt{B_{P}^{2}+B_{Q}^{2}}=\left[\left(\frac{\mu_{0} I}{2 R}\right)^{2}+\left(\frac{\mu_{0} \sqrt{3} I}{2 R}\right)^{2}\right]^{1 / 2}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I}{2 R}(1+3)^{1 / 2}=\left(\frac{\mu_{0} I}{R}\right){/tex} {tex}\tan \theta=\frac{B P}{B Q}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\theta{/tex} = 30 o
solution_html:
{tex}\vec{B_{p}}=\frac{\mu_{0} I}{2 R}{/tex}, vertically upwards
{tex}\vec{{B}_{Q}}=\frac{\mu_{0} \sqrt{3} I}{2 R}{/tex}, along with horizontal
The resultant field at the centre is
B = {tex}\sqrt{B_{P}^{2}+B_{Q}^{2}}=\left[\left(\frac{\mu_{0} I}{2 R}\right)^{2}+\left(\frac{\mu_{0} \sqrt{3} I}{2 R}\right)^{2}\right]^{1 / 2}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I}{2 R}(1+3)^{1 / 2}=\left(\frac{\mu_{0} I}{R}\right){/tex}
{tex}\tan \theta=\frac{B P}{B Q}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\theta{/tex} = 30o

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. Derive with the help of a diagram the expression for the magnetic field inside a very long solenoid having n turns per unit length carrying a current I.
  2. How is a toroid different from a solenoid?
question_text: Derive with the help of a diagram the expression for the magnetic field inside a very long solenoid having n turns per unit length carrying a current I. How is a toroid different from a solenoid?
explanation: Consider the loop abcd. Along cd, the magnetic field is zero. Along bc and ad, the field component is zero, hence makes no contribution. Let field along ab be B. Let n be the number of turns per unit length. Thus, the total number of turns is nh. The enclosed current is I e = I(nh) From Ampere’s circuital law, BL = {tex}\mu_o{/tex} I e Bh = {tex}\mu_o{/tex} I (nh) [ here L = h ] Therefore, magnetic field B = {tex}\mu_o{/tex} n I Its direction is given by right hand thumb rule. The toroid is a hollow circular ring having large number of turns closely wound. It is viewed as solenoid bent into circular shape. The magnetic field, B of toroid having N turns is, {tex}B=\frac{\mu_o\;N\;I}{2\pi\;r}{/tex} where N = {tex}2\pi r\;n{/tex} Also, Solenoid behaves like a bar magnet whereas Toroid does not.
solution: Consider the loop abcd. Along cd, the magnetic field is zero. Along bc and ad, the field component is zero, hence makes no contribution. Let field along ab be B. Let n be the number of turns per unit length. Thus, the total number of turns is nh. The enclosed current is I e = I(nh) From Ampere’s circuital law, BL = {tex}\mu_o{/tex} I e Bh = {tex}\mu_o{/tex} I (nh) [ here L = h ] Therefore, magnetic field B = {tex}\mu_o{/tex} n I Its direction is given by right hand thumb rule. The toroid is a hollow circular ring having large number of turns closely wound. It is viewed as solenoid bent into circular shape. The magnetic field, B of toroid having N turns is, {tex}B=\frac{\mu_o\;N\;I}{2\pi\;r}{/tex} where N = {tex}2\pi r\;n{/tex} Also, Solenoid behaves like a bar magnet whereas Toroid does not.
solution_html:
1.  
    Consider the loop abcd. Along cd, the magnetic field is zero. Along bc and ad, the field component is zero, hence makes no contribution. Let field along ab be B. Let n be the number of turns per unit length.
    Thus, the total number of turns is nh.
    The enclosed current is Ie = I(nh)
    From Ampere’s circuital law,
    BL = {tex}\mu_o{/tex} Ie
    Bh = {tex}\mu_o{/tex} I (nh) [ here L = h ]
    Therefore, magnetic field B = {tex}\mu_o{/tex} n I
    Its direction is given by right hand thumb rule.
  2. The toroid is a hollow circular ring having large number of turns closely wound. It is viewed as solenoid bent into circular shape.
    The magnetic field, B of toroid having N turns is,
    {tex}B=\frac{\mu_o\;N\;I}{2\pi\;r}{/tex}
    where N = {tex}2\pi r\;n{/tex}
    Also, Solenoid behaves like a bar magnet whereas Toroid does not.

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. Derive the expression for the torque acting on a current carrying loop placed in a magnetic field.
  2. Explain the significance of a radial magnetic field when a current carrying coil is kept in it.
question_text: Derive the expression for the torque acting on a current carrying loop placed in a magnetic field. Explain the significance of a radial magnetic field when a current carrying coil is kept in it.
explanation: Consider the figure. A rectangular loop is placed such that a uniform magnetic field B is in the plane of the loop. Forces on the arms AD and BC are zero. Force on arm AB = F 1 = l b B (directed into the plane of the loop) Force on arm CD = F 2 = I b B (directed out of the plane of the paper) Thus net force on the loop is zero. The magnitude of the torque on the loop due to forces F 1 and F 2 is, {tex}\tau{/tex} = F 1 {tex}\frac{a}{2}{/tex} + F 2 {tex}\frac{a}{2}{/tex} = l (ab) B = l A B (where A = ab = area of the loop) If there are n such turns, the torque will be nlab sin {tex}\theta{/tex} . The magnetic moment of the current, m=IA {tex}\therefore \vec{\tau}=\vec{m} \times \vec{B}{/tex} The uniform radial magnetic field keeps the plane of the coil always parallel to the direction of the magnetic field. Thus produces a constant torque on the coil irrespective of the rotation of the coil. In application, when a current carrying coil is kept in a radial magnetic field the corresponding moving coil galvanometer would have a linear scale.
solution: Consider the figure. A rectangular loop is placed such that a uniform magnetic field B is in the plane of the loop. Forces on the arms AD and BC are zero. Force on arm AB = F 1 = l b B (directed into the plane of the loop) Force on arm CD = F 2 = I b B (directed out of the plane of the paper) Thus net force on the loop is zero. The magnitude of the torque on the loop due to forces F 1 and F 2 is, {tex}\tau{/tex} = F 1 {tex}\frac{a}{2}{/tex} + F 2 {tex}\frac{a}{2}{/tex} = l (ab) B = l A B (where A = ab = area of the loop) If there are n such turns, the torque will be nlab sin {tex}\theta{/tex} . The magnetic moment of the current, m=IA {tex}\therefore \vec{\tau}=\vec{m} \times \vec{B}{/tex} The uniform radial magnetic field keeps the plane of the coil always parallel to the direction of the magnetic field. Thus produces a constant torque on the coil irrespective of the rotation of the coil. In application, when a current carrying coil is kept in a radial magnetic field the corresponding moving coil galvanometer would have a linear scale.
solution_html:
1. Consider the figure. A rectangular loop is placed such that a uniform magnetic field B is in the plane of the loop.



    Forces on the arms AD and BC are zero.
    Force on arm AB = F1 = l b B (directed into the plane of the loop)
    Force on arm CD = F2 = I b B (directed out of the plane of the paper)
    Thus net force on the loop is zero. 
    The magnitude of the torque on the loop due to forces F1 and F2 is,
    {tex}\tau{/tex} = F1{tex}\frac{a}{2}{/tex} + F2{tex}\frac{a}{2}{/tex} 
    = l (ab) B
    = l A B (where A = ab = area of the loop)
     If there are n such turns, the torque will be nlab sin{tex}\theta{/tex}.
    The magnetic moment of the current, m=IA
    {tex}\therefore \vec{\tau}=\vec{m} \times \vec{B}{/tex}

  2. The uniform radial magnetic field keeps the plane of the coil always parallel to the direction of the magnetic field. Thus produces a constant torque on the coil irrespective of the rotation of the coil. In application, when a current carrying coil is kept in a radial magnetic field the corresponding moving coil galvanometer would have a linear scale.

question_html: How is the equation for Ampere's circuital law modified in the presence of displacement current? Explain.
question_text: How is the equation for Ampere's circuital law modified in the presence of displacement current? Explain.
explanation: {tex}\oint \vec{B} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}=\mu_0 i{/tex} There is an inconsistency in the Ampere's Circuital law. Maxwell modified it as {tex}\oint \vec{B} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}=\mu_0\left(i_c+i_d\right){/tex} where i d {tex}=\varepsilon_0 \frac{d \phi_E}{d t}{/tex}
solution: {tex}\oint \vec{B} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}=\mu_0 i{/tex} There is an inconsistency in the Ampere's Circuital law. Maxwell modified it as {tex}\oint \vec{B} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}=\mu_0\left(i_c+i_d\right){/tex} where i d {tex}=\varepsilon_0 \frac{d \phi_E}{d t}{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}\oint \vec{B} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}=\mu_0 i{/tex}
There is an inconsistency in the Ampere's Circuital law.
Maxwell modified it as
{tex}\oint \vec{B} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}=\mu_0\left(i_c+i_d\right){/tex}
where id {tex}=\varepsilon_0 \frac{d \phi_E}{d t}{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html: Derive, with the help of a diagram, the expression for the magnetic field inside a very long solenoid having n turns per unit length carrying a current I.
question_text: Derive, with the help of a diagram, the expression for the magnetic field inside a very long solenoid having n turns per unit length carrying a current I.
explanation: Let us consider a very long straight solenoid having 'n' turns per unit length and carrying electric current 'I' as shown. Let us consider a point well inside the solenoid at which the magnetic induction is to be found. Consider a rectangular path ABCD of the line of induction such that AB = L = length of the rectangular path. The number of turns enclosed by the rectangles is nL. Hence the total electric current flowing through the rectangular path is nLI. According to Ampere's law, {tex}\oint \vec{B} \cdot \vec{dl}=\mu_0 n L I{/tex} ...(i) Now for closed loop {tex}\oint \vec{B} \cdot \vec{d l}=\int_\limits a^b \vec{B} \cdot \vec{d l}+\int_\limits b^c \vec{B} \cdot \vec{d l}{/tex} {tex}+\int_\limits c^d \vec{B} \cdot \vec{d l}+\int_\limits d^a \vec{B} \cdot \vec{dl}{/tex} As the direction of {tex}\overrightarrow{d l}{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow{B}{/tex} in path bc and ad are perpendicular {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\int_\limits b^c \vec{B} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}=\int_\limits d^a \vec{B} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}=0{/tex} Near the ends of the solenoid, the lines of the field are crowded. While for rest of the space, the lines are so widely spaced that the magnetic field is negligible. {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\int_\limits c^d \vec{B} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}=0{/tex} Thus {tex}\oint \vec{B} \cdot \vec{d}=\int_\limits a^b \vec{B} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}+0+0+0{/tex} {tex}=\int_\limits a^b \vec{B} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}{/tex} But {tex}\vec {B}{/tex} and {tex} \vec {dl}{/tex} are in the same direction {tex}\vec{B} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l} =B \cdot d l \cdot \cos 0^{\circ}=B \cdot d l {/tex} {tex}\oint \vec{B} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l} {/tex} {tex}=\int_\limits a^b B \cdot d l{/tex} {tex} =B \int_\limits a^b d l=B L {/tex} ...(ii) From equation (i) and (ii) we get BL = {tex}\mu_0 n L I{/tex} B = {tex}\mu_0 n I{/tex}
solution: Let us consider a very long straight solenoid having 'n' turns per unit length and carrying electric current 'I' as shown. Let us consider a point well inside the solenoid at which the magnetic induction is to be found. Consider a rectangular path ABCD of the line of induction such that AB = L = length of the rectangular path. The number of turns enclosed by the rectangles is nL. Hence the total electric current flowing through the rectangular path is nLI. According to Ampere's law, {tex}\oint \vec{B} \cdot \vec{dl}=\mu_0 n L I{/tex} ...(i) Now for closed loop {tex}\oint \vec{B} \cdot \vec{d l}=\int_\limits a^b \vec{B} \cdot \vec{d l}+\int_\limits b^c \vec{B} \cdot \vec{d l}{/tex} {tex}+\int_\limits c^d \vec{B} \cdot \vec{d l}+\int_\limits d^a \vec{B} \cdot \vec{dl}{/tex} As the direction of {tex}\overrightarrow{d l}{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow{B}{/tex} in path bc and ad are perpendicular {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\int_\limits b^c \vec{B} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}=\int_\limits d^a \vec{B} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}=0{/tex} Near the ends of the solenoid, the lines of the field are crowded. While for rest of the space, the lines are so widely spaced that the magnetic field is negligible. {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\int_\limits c^d \vec{B} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}=0{/tex} Thus {tex}\oint \vec{B} \cdot \vec{d}=\int_\limits a^b \vec{B} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}+0+0+0{/tex} {tex}=\int_\limits a^b \vec{B} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}{/tex} But {tex}\vec {B}{/tex} and {tex} \vec {dl}{/tex} are in the same direction {tex}\vec{B} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l} =B \cdot d l \cdot \cos 0^{\circ}=B \cdot d l {/tex} {tex}\oint \vec{B} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l} {/tex} {tex}=\int_\limits a^b B \cdot d l{/tex} {tex} =B \int_\limits a^b d l=B L {/tex} ...(ii) From equation (i) and (ii) we get BL = {tex}\mu_0 n L I{/tex} B = {tex}\mu_0 n I{/tex}
solution_html:
Let us consider a very long straight solenoid having 'n' turns per unit length and carrying electric current 'I' as shown. Let us consider a point well inside the solenoid at which the magnetic induction is to be found.
Consider a rectangular path ABCD of the line of induction such that AB = L = length of the rectangular path. The number of turns enclosed by the rectangles is nL. Hence the total electric current flowing through the rectangular path is nLI. According to Ampere's law,

{tex}\oint \vec{B} \cdot \vec{dl}=\mu_0 n L I{/tex} ...(i)
Now for closed loop
{tex}\oint \vec{B} \cdot \vec{d l}=\int_\limits a^b \vec{B} \cdot \vec{d l}+\int_\limits b^c \vec{B} \cdot \vec{d l}{/tex} {tex}+\int_\limits c^d \vec{B} \cdot \vec{d l}+\int_\limits d^a \vec{B} \cdot \vec{dl}{/tex}
As the direction of {tex}\overrightarrow{d l}{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow{B}{/tex} in path bc and ad are perpendicular
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\int_\limits b^c \vec{B} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}=\int_\limits d^a \vec{B} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}=0{/tex}
Near the ends of the solenoid, the lines of the field are crowded. While for rest of the space, the lines are so widely spaced that the magnetic field is negligible.
{tex}\therefore{/tex}  {tex}\int_\limits c^d \vec{B} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}=0{/tex}
Thus 
{tex}\oint \vec{B} \cdot \vec{d}=\int_\limits a^b \vec{B} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}+0+0+0{/tex} {tex}=\int_\limits a^b \vec{B} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}{/tex}
But {tex}\vec {B}{/tex} and {tex} \vec {dl}{/tex} are in the same direction
{tex}\vec{B} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l} =B \cdot d l \cdot \cos 0^{\circ}=B \cdot d l {/tex}
{tex}\oint \vec{B} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l} {/tex} {tex}=\int_\limits a^b B \cdot d l{/tex}
{tex} =B \int_\limits a^b d l=B L {/tex} ...(ii)
From equation (i) and (ii) we get
BL = {tex}\mu_0 n L I{/tex}
B = {tex}\mu_0 n I{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. Derive the expression for the magnetic field due to a current carrying coil of radius R at a distance x from the centre along the X-axis.
  2. A straight wire carrying a current of 5 A is bent into a semicircular arc of radius 2 cm as shown in the figure. Find the magnitude and direction of the magnetic field at the centre of the arc.
question_text: Derive the expression for the magnetic field due to a current carrying coil of radius R at a distance x from the centre along the X-axis. A straight wire carrying a current of 5 A is bent into a semicircular arc of radius 2 cm as shown in the figure. Find the magnitude and direction of the magnetic field at the centre of the arc.
explanation: Consider a circular loop having current I of radius R in y-z plane. Let O be the origin. Let P be the point on x-axis at the distance x from the origin O where the magnetic field is to be calculated. Consider {tex}dl{/tex} element of the loop. According to Biot Savart Law, the magnitude dB of the magnetic field due to {tex}dl{/tex} is given by, {tex}|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{dB}}|{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mu_0}{4\pi}\frac{I\;dl\;\sin90^\circ}{r^2}{/tex} Where r = {tex}\sqrt{x^{2}+R^{2}}{/tex} {tex}|\overrightarrow{d B}|{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mu_0}{4\pi}\frac{I\;dl}{\left(x^2+R^2\right)}{/tex} The direction of {tex}\overrightarrow{d B}{/tex} is perpendicular to {tex}\overrightarrow{d l}{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow{r}{/tex} . It has components {tex}\mathrm{d} \mathrm{B}_{x}{/tex} and {tex}\mathrm{dB}_{\perp}{/tex} . The components {tex}\mathrm{dB}_{\perp}{/tex} due to the whole coil cancel out in pairs. Net field B = {tex}\int{/tex} dB x = {tex}\int{/tex} dB cos {tex}\theta{/tex} Now, {tex}\cos\;\theta=\frac R{(x^2+R^2)^{1/2}}{/tex} Therefore, dB x = {tex}\frac{\mu_0\;I\;dl}{4\pi}\frac R{\left(x^2+R^2\right)^{3/2}}{/tex} The summation of elements {tex}dl{/tex} over the circular loop yields the circumference, {tex}2\pi R{/tex} Thus, net magnetic field (in vector form), {tex}\vec{B}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mu_0IR^2}{2\left(x^2+R^2\right)^\frac32}\widehat i{/tex} At, x = 0, {tex}B=\frac{\mu_o\;I}{2R}\;\widehat i{/tex} From above, for circular loop, the magnitude of magnetic field B is {tex}B=\frac{\mu_o\;I}{2R}\;{/tex} For the semi-circular loop, the magnitude of the net magnetic field, B = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} \mathrm{I}}{4 \mathrm{R}}{/tex} i.e. half of the circular loop = {tex}\frac{4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times 5}{4 \times 2 \times 10^{-2}}{/tex} = 7.85 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -5 T And the field is directed inwards perpendicular to the plane of the page.
solution: Consider a circular loop having current I of radius R in y-z plane. Let O be the origin. Let P be the point on x-axis at the distance x from the origin O where the magnetic field is to be calculated. Consider {tex}dl{/tex} element of the loop. According to Biot Savart Law, the magnitude dB of the magnetic field due to {tex}dl{/tex} is given by, {tex}|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{dB}}|{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mu_0}{4\pi}\frac{I\;dl\;\sin90^\circ}{r^2}{/tex} Where r = {tex}\sqrt{x^{2}+R^{2}}{/tex} {tex}|\overrightarrow{d B}|{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mu_0}{4\pi}\frac{I\;dl}{\left(x^2+R^2\right)}{/tex} The direction of {tex}\overrightarrow{d B}{/tex} is perpendicular to {tex}\overrightarrow{d l}{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow{r}{/tex} . It has components {tex}\mathrm{d} \mathrm{B}_{x}{/tex} and {tex}\mathrm{dB}_{\perp}{/tex} . The components {tex}\mathrm{dB}_{\perp}{/tex} due to the whole coil cancel out in pairs. Net field B = {tex}\int{/tex} dB x = {tex}\int{/tex} dB cos {tex}\theta{/tex} Now, {tex}\cos\;\theta=\frac R{(x^2+R^2)^{1/2}}{/tex} Therefore, dB x = {tex}\frac{\mu_0\;I\;dl}{4\pi}\frac R{\left(x^2+R^2\right)^{3/2}}{/tex} The summation of elements {tex}dl{/tex} over the circular loop yields the circumference, {tex}2\pi R{/tex} Thus, net magnetic field (in vector form), {tex}\vec{B}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mu_0IR^2}{2\left(x^2+R^2\right)^\frac32}\widehat i{/tex} At, x = 0, {tex}B=\frac{\mu_o\;I}{2R}\;\widehat i{/tex} From above, for circular loop, the magnitude of magnetic field B is {tex}B=\frac{\mu_o\;I}{2R}\;{/tex} For the semi-circular loop, the magnitude of the net magnetic field, B = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} \mathrm{I}}{4 \mathrm{R}}{/tex} i.e. half of the circular loop = {tex}\frac{4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times 5}{4 \times 2 \times 10^{-2}}{/tex} = 7.85 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -5 T And the field is directed inwards perpendicular to the plane of the page.
solution_html:
1. Consider a circular loop having current I of radius R in y-z plane. Let O be the origin. Let P be the point on x-axis at the distance x from the origin O where the magnetic field is to be calculated. Consider {tex}dl{/tex} element of the loop. 

    According to Biot Savart Law, the magnitude dB of the magnetic field due to {tex}dl{/tex} is given by,
    {tex}|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{dB}}|{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mu_0}{4\pi}\frac{I\;dl\;\sin90^\circ}{r^2}{/tex}
    Where r = {tex}\sqrt{x^{2}+R^{2}}{/tex}
    {tex}|\overrightarrow{d B}|{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mu_0}{4\pi}\frac{I\;dl}{\left(x^2+R^2\right)}{/tex}
    The direction of {tex}\overrightarrow{d B}{/tex} is perpendicular to {tex}\overrightarrow{d l}{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow{r}{/tex}.
    It has components {tex}\mathrm{d} \mathrm{B}_{x}{/tex} and {tex}\mathrm{dB}_{\perp}{/tex}. The components {tex}\mathrm{dB}_{\perp}{/tex} due to the whole coil cancel out in pairs.
    Net field B = {tex}\int{/tex} dBx = {tex}\int{/tex} dB cos {tex}\theta{/tex}
    Now, {tex}\cos\;\theta=\frac R{(x^2+R^2)^{1/2}}{/tex}
    Therefore, dBx = {tex}\frac{\mu_0\;I\;dl}{4\pi}\frac R{\left(x^2+R^2\right)^{3/2}}{/tex}
    The summation of elements {tex}dl{/tex} over the circular loop yields the circumference, {tex}2\pi R{/tex}
    Thus, net magnetic field (in vector form),
    {tex}\vec{B}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mu_0IR^2}{2\left(x^2+R^2\right)^\frac32}\widehat i{/tex}
    At, x = 0,
    {tex}B=\frac{\mu_o\;I}{2R}\;\widehat i{/tex}
     
  2. From above, for circular loop, the magnitude of magnetic field B is
    {tex}B=\frac{\mu_o\;I}{2R}\;{/tex}
    For the semi-circular loop, the magnitude of the net magnetic field, B = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} \mathrm{I}}{4 \mathrm{R}}{/tex} i.e. half of the circular loop
    = {tex}\frac{4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times 5}{4 \times 2 \times 10^{-2}}{/tex}
    = 7.85 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 T
    And the field is directed inwards perpendicular to the plane of the page.

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. State and explain the law used to determine magnetic field at a point due to a current element. Derive the expression for the magnetic field due to a circular current carrying loop of radius r at its centre.
  2. A long wire with a small current element of length 1 cm is placed at the origin and carries a current of 10 A along the X-axis. Find out the magnitude and direction of the magnetic field due to the element on the Y-axis at a distance 0.5 m from it.
question_text: State and explain the law used to determine magnetic field at a point due to a current element. Derive the expression for the magnetic field due to a circular current carrying loop of radius r at its centre. A long wire with a small current element of length 1 cm is placed at the origin and carries a current of 10 A along the X-axis. Find out the magnitude and direction of the magnetic field due to the element on the Y-axis at a distance 0.5 m from it.
explanation: Biot Savart law is the law that is used to determine the magnetic field at a point due to a current element. Let finite conductor XY carrying current I as shown in Figure. Let dl be the infinitesimal element of the conductor. The magnetic field, dB due to a current element, dl at a point P which is at a distance r, is given by {tex}\vec {dB}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mu_{\mathrm{o}}}{4 \pi} I \frac{\overrightarrow{d l} \times \vec{r}}{{r}^{3}}{/tex} The magnitude of this field is, {tex}\left|d\overrightarrow B\right|=\frac{\mu_o}{4\pi}\frac{Idl\;\sin\theta}{r^2}{/tex} Now, to calculate the magnetic field on the axis of a circular current loop of radius R, consider the figure below: Now here, {tex}r=\sqrt{R^2+x^2}{/tex} . Also {tex}\left|dl\times r\right|=r\;dl{/tex} So, the magnitude of dB due to dl is, {tex}\left|d\overrightarrow B\right|=\frac{\mu_o}{4\pi}\frac{Idl}{x^2+R^2}{/tex} Now dB has two components. The perpendicular component gets cancelled and only x-component survives. So, dB x = dB cos {tex}\theta{/tex} cos {tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}\frac R{(x^2+R^2)^{1/2}}{/tex} Thus, dB x = {tex}\frac{\mu_oIdl}{4\pi}\frac R{(x^2+R^2)^{3/2}}{/tex} Therefore, field due to the whole circular loop is, {tex}| \vec B |{/tex} = {tex}\int{/tex} dB x = {tex}\frac{\mu_oI}{4\pi}\frac R{(x^2+R^2)^{3/2}}{/tex} {tex}\int{/tex} dl = {tex}\frac{\mu_oI}{4\pi}\frac R{(x^2+R^2)^{3/2}}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 2 {tex}\pi{/tex} R = {tex}\frac{\mu_oIR^2}{2(x^2+R^2)^{3/2}}{/tex} At the centre of loop, x = 0, thus {tex}| \vec B |{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I}{2 R}{/tex} {tex}| \vec {dB} |{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mu_{o}}{4 \pi} \frac{Id l \sin \theta}{{r}^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{4 \pi \times 10^{-7}}{4 \pi}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\frac{10 \times\left(1 \times 10^{-2}\right) \times \sin 90^{\circ}}{(0.5)^{2}}{/tex} = 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -8 T And the field is directed perpendicular to the plane and directed into it.

Let dl be the infinitesimal element of the conductor. The magnetic field, dB due to a current element, dl at a point P which is at a distance r, is given by {tex}\vec {dB}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mu_{\mathrm{o}}}{4 \pi} I \frac{\overrightarrow{d l} \times \vec{r}}{{r}^{3}}{/tex} The magnitude of this field is, {tex}\left|d\overrightarrow B\right|=\frac{\mu_o}{4\pi}\frac{Idl\;\sin\theta}{r^2}{/tex} Now, to calculate the magnetic field on the axis of a circular current loop of radius R, consider the figure below: Now here, {tex}r=\sqrt{R^2+x^2}{/tex} . Also {tex}\left|dl\times r\right|=r\;dl{/tex} So, the magnitude of dB due to dl is, {tex}\left|d\overrightarrow B\right|=\frac{\mu_o}{4\pi}\frac{Idl}{x^2+R^2}{/tex} Now dB has two components. The perpendicular component gets cancelled and only x-component survives. So, dB x = dB cos {tex}\theta{/tex} cos {tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}\frac R{(x^2+R^2)^{1/2}}{/tex} Thus, dB x = {tex}\frac{\mu_oIdl}{4\pi}\frac R{(x^2+R^2)^{3/2}}{/tex} Therefore, field due to the whole circular loop is, {tex}| \vec B |{/tex} = {tex}\int{/tex} dB x = {tex}\frac{\mu_oI}{4\pi}\frac R{(x^2+R^2)^{3/2}}{/tex} {tex}\int{/tex} dl = {tex}\frac{\mu_oI}{4\pi}\frac R{(x^2+R^2)^{3/2}}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 2 {tex}\pi{/tex} R = {tex}\frac{\mu_oIR^2}{2(x^2+R^2)^{3/2}}{/tex} At the centre of loop, x = 0, thus {tex}| \vec B |{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I}{2 R}{/tex} {tex}| \vec {dB} |{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mu_{o}}{4 \pi} \frac{Id l \sin \theta}{{r}^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{4 \pi \times 10^{-7}}{4 \pi}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\frac{10 \times\left(1 \times 10^{-2}\right) \times \sin 90^{\circ}}{(0.5)^{2}}{/tex} = 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -8 T And the field is directed perpendicular to the plane and directed into it.
solution_html:
1. Biot Savart law is the law that is used to determine the magnetic field at a point due to a current element.
    Let finite conductor XY carrying current I as shown in Figure. Let dl be the infinitesimal element of the conductor. The magnetic field, dB due to a current element, dl at a point P which is at a distance r, is given by
    {tex}\vec {dB}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mu_{\mathrm{o}}}{4 \pi} I \frac{\overrightarrow{d l} \times \vec{r}}{{r}^{3}}{/tex}


    The magnitude of this field is,
    {tex}\left|d\overrightarrow B\right|=\frac{\mu_o}{4\pi}\frac{Idl\;\sin\theta}{r^2}{/tex}
    Now, to calculate the magnetic field on the axis of a circular current loop of radius R, consider the figure below:

    Now here, {tex}r=\sqrt{R^2+x^2}{/tex}. Also {tex}\left|dl\times r\right|=r\;dl{/tex}
    So, the magnitude of dB due to dl is,
    {tex}\left|d\overrightarrow B\right|=\frac{\mu_o}{4\pi}\frac{Idl}{x^2+R^2}{/tex}
    Now dB has two components. The perpendicular component gets cancelled and only x-component survives. So,
    dBx = dB cos{tex}\theta{/tex}
    cos{tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}\frac R{(x^2+R^2)^{1/2}}{/tex}
    Thus, dBx = {tex}\frac{\mu_oIdl}{4\pi}\frac R{(x^2+R^2)^{3/2}}{/tex}
    Therefore, field due to the whole circular loop is,
    {tex}| \vec B |{/tex} = {tex}\int{/tex}dBx = {tex}\frac{\mu_oI}{4\pi}\frac R{(x^2+R^2)^{3/2}}{/tex}{tex}\int{/tex}dl = {tex}\frac{\mu_oI}{4\pi}\frac R{(x^2+R^2)^{3/2}}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 2{tex}\pi{/tex}R = {tex}\frac{\mu_oIR^2}{2(x^2+R^2)^{3/2}}{/tex}
    At the centre of loop, x = 0, thus
    {tex}| \vec B |{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I}{2 R}{/tex}
     

  2. {tex}| \vec {dB} |{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mu_{o}}{4 \pi} \frac{Id l \sin \theta}{{r}^{2}}{/tex}
    = {tex}\frac{4 \pi \times 10^{-7}}{4 \pi}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\frac{10 \times\left(1 \times 10^{-2}\right) \times \sin 90^{\circ}}{(0.5)^{2}}{/tex} = 4{tex}\times{/tex}10-8 T
    And the field is directed perpendicular to the plane and directed into it.
