question_html:
1. An iron ring of relative permeability {tex}\mu{/tex}r has windings of insulated copper wire of n turns per metre. When the current in the windings is I, find the expression for the magnetic field in the ring.
  2. The susceptibility of a magnetic material is 0·9853. Identify the type of magnetic material. Draw the modification of the field pattern on keeping a piece of this material in a uniform magnetic field.
question_text: An iron ring of relative permeability {tex}\mu{/tex} r has windings of insulated copper wire of n turns per metre. When the current in the windings is I, find the expression for the magnetic field in the ring. The susceptibility of a magnetic material is 0·9853. Identify the type of magnetic material. Draw the modification of the field pattern on keeping a piece of this material in a uniform magnetic field.
explanation: From Ampere's circuital law, we have, {tex}\oint \vec{B} \cdot d \vec{l}=\mu_{0} \mu_{r} I_{e n c l o s e d}{/tex} .... (i) For the field inside the ring, we can write {tex}\oint \vec{B} \cdot d \vec{l}=\oint B d l=B \cdot 2 \pi r{/tex} (r = radius of the ring) Also, {tex}I_{\text {enclosed}}=(2 \pi r n) I{/tex} using equation (i) {tex}\therefore B .2 \pi r=\mu_{0} \mu_{r} \cdot(n .2 \pi r) I{/tex} {tex}\therefore B=\mu_{0} \mu_{r} n I{/tex} The material is paramagnetic. The field pattern gets modified as shown in the figure below.
solution: From Ampere's circuital law, we have, {tex}\oint \vec{B} \cdot d \vec{l}=\mu_{0} \mu_{r} I_{e n c l o s e d}{/tex} .... (i) For the field inside the ring, we can write {tex}\oint \vec{B} \cdot d \vec{l}=\oint B d l=B \cdot 2 \pi r{/tex} (r = radius of the ring) Also, {tex}I_{\text {enclosed}}=(2 \pi r n) I{/tex} using equation (i) {tex}\therefore B .2 \pi r=\mu_{0} \mu_{r} \cdot(n .2 \pi r) I{/tex} {tex}\therefore B=\mu_{0} \mu_{r} n I{/tex} The material is paramagnetic. The field pattern gets modified as shown in the figure below.
solution_html:
1. From Ampere's circuital law, we have,
    {tex}\oint \vec{B} \cdot d \vec{l}=\mu_{0} \mu_{r} I_{e n c l o s e d}{/tex} .... (i)
    For the field inside the ring, we can write
    {tex}\oint \vec{B} \cdot d \vec{l}=\oint B d l=B \cdot 2 \pi r{/tex}
    (r = radius of the ring)
    Also, {tex}I_{\text {enclosed}}=(2 \pi r n) I{/tex} using equation (i)
    {tex}\therefore B .2 \pi r=\mu_{0} \mu_{r} \cdot(n .2 \pi r) I{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore B=\mu_{0} \mu_{r} n I{/tex}
  2. The material is paramagnetic.
    The field pattern gets modified as shown in the figure below.

question_html: Two infinitely long straight wires A1 and A2 carrying currents I and 2I flowing in the same direction are kept d distance apart. Where should a third straight wire A3 carrying current 1.5 I be placed between A1 and A2 so that it experiences no net force due to A1 and A2? Does the net force acting on A3 depend on the current flowing through it?
question_text: Two infinitely long straight wires A 1 and A 2 carrying currents I and 2I flowing in the same direction are kept d distance apart. Where should a third straight wire A 3 carrying current 1.5 I be placed between A 1 and A 2 so that it experiences no net force due to A 1 and A 2 ? Does the net force acting on A 3 depend on the current flowing through it?
explanation: Finding the position of third wire Reason {tex}F_1=\frac{\mu_0}{4 \pi} \frac{I \times 1.5 I}{x}{/tex} {tex}F_2=\frac{\mu_0}{4 \pi} \frac{2 I \times 1.5 I}{(d-x)}{/tex} For no force on the conductor, A 3 {tex}\frac{\mu_0}{4 \pi} \frac{I \times 1.5 I}{x}=\frac{\mu_0}{4 \pi} \frac{2 I \times 1.5 I}{(d-x)}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{x}=\frac{2}{(d-x)}{/tex} 3x = d {tex}x=\frac{d}{3}{/tex} The net force acting on A 3 is zero only when current through is 1.5I. So, it depends on the current flowing through it. [ Note: Also accept in general, it will depend on the value of I 3 .]
solution: Finding the position of third wire Reason {tex}F_1=\frac{\mu_0}{4 \pi} \frac{I \times 1.5 I}{x}{/tex} {tex}F_2=\frac{\mu_0}{4 \pi} \frac{2 I \times 1.5 I}{(d-x)}{/tex} For no force on the conductor, A 3 {tex}\frac{\mu_0}{4 \pi} \frac{I \times 1.5 I}{x}=\frac{\mu_0}{4 \pi} \frac{2 I \times 1.5 I}{(d-x)}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{x}=\frac{2}{(d-x)}{/tex} 3x = d {tex}x=\frac{d}{3}{/tex} The net force acting on A 3 is zero only when current through is 1.5I. So, it depends on the current flowing through it. [ Note: Also accept in general, it will depend on the value of I 3 .]
solution_html:
Finding the position of third wire Reason
{tex}F_1=\frac{\mu_0}{4 \pi} \frac{I \times 1.5 I}{x}{/tex}
{tex}F_2=\frac{\mu_0}{4 \pi} \frac{2 I \times 1.5 I}{(d-x)}{/tex}

For no force on the conductor, A3
{tex}\frac{\mu_0}{4 \pi} \frac{I \times 1.5 I}{x}=\frac{\mu_0}{4 \pi} \frac{2 I \times 1.5 I}{(d-x)}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{1}{x}=\frac{2}{(d-x)}{/tex}
3x = d
{tex}x=\frac{d}{3}{/tex}
The net force acting on A3 is zero only when current through is 1.5I. So, it depends on the current flowing through it.
[Note: Also accept in general, it will depend on the value of I3.]

subject: Physics
question_html: A square loop of side a carrying a current 12 is kept at distance x from an infinitely long straight wire carrying a current I1 as shown in the figure. Obtain the expression for the resultant force acting on the loop.
question_text: A square loop of side a carrying a current 12 is kept at distance x from an infinitely long straight wire carrying a current I 1 as shown in the figure. Obtain the expression for the resultant force acting on the loop.
explanation: Force per unit length between two parallel straight conductors {tex}F=\frac{\mu_0}{4 \pi} \frac{2 I_1 I_2}{d}{/tex} Force on the part of the loop which is parallel to infinite straight wire and at a distance x from it. {tex}F_1=\frac{\mu_0}{2 \pi} \frac{I_1 I_2 a}{x}{/tex} (away from the infinite straight wire) Force on the part of the loop which is at a distance (x + a) from it, {tex}F_2=\frac{\mu_0}{2 \pi} \frac{l_1 l_2 a}{(x+a)}{/tex} (towards the infinite straight wire) Net force, F = F 1 - F 2 {tex}F=\frac{\mu_0}{2 \pi} l_1 l_2a\left[\frac{1}{x}-\frac{1}{x+a}\right]{/tex} {tex}F=\frac{\mu_0 I_1 l_2 a^2}{2 \pi \;x(x+a)}{/tex} (away from the infinite straight wire)
solution: Force per unit length between two parallel straight conductors {tex}F=\frac{\mu_0}{4 \pi} \frac{2 I_1 I_2}{d}{/tex} Force on the part of the loop which is parallel to infinite straight wire and at a distance x from it. {tex}F_1=\frac{\mu_0}{2 \pi} \frac{I_1 I_2 a}{x}{/tex} (away from the infinite straight wire) Force on the part of the loop which is at a distance (x + a) from it, {tex}F_2=\frac{\mu_0}{2 \pi} \frac{l_1 l_2 a}{(x+a)}{/tex} (towards the infinite straight wire) Net force, F = F 1 - F 2 {tex}F=\frac{\mu_0}{2 \pi} l_1 l_2a\left[\frac{1}{x}-\frac{1}{x+a}\right]{/tex} {tex}F=\frac{\mu_0 I_1 l_2 a^2}{2 \pi \;x(x+a)}{/tex} (away from the infinite straight wire)
solution_html:
Force per unit length between two parallel straight conductors 
{tex}F=\frac{\mu_0}{4 \pi} \frac{2 I_1 I_2}{d}{/tex}
Force on the part of the loop which is parallel to infinite straight wire and at a distance x from it.
{tex}F_1=\frac{\mu_0}{2 \pi} \frac{I_1 I_2 a}{x}{/tex}
(away from the infinite straight wire) Force on the part of the loop which is at a distance (x + a) from it,
{tex}F_2=\frac{\mu_0}{2 \pi} \frac{l_1 l_2 a}{(x+a)}{/tex}
(towards the infinite straight wire)
Net force, F = F1 - F2
{tex}F=\frac{\mu_0}{2 \pi} l_1 l_2a\left[\frac{1}{x}-\frac{1}{x+a}\right]{/tex}
{tex}F=\frac{\mu_0 I_1 l_2 a^2}{2 \pi \;x(x+a)}{/tex}
(away from the infinite straight wire)

subject: Physics
question_html: A square loop of sides 5 cm carrying a current of 0.2 A in the clockwise direction is placed at a distance of 10 cm from an infinitely long wire carrying a current of 1 A as shown. Calculate (i) the resultant magnetic force, and (ii) the torque, if any, acting on the loop.
question_text: A square loop of sides 5 cm carrying a current of 0.2 A in the clockwise direction is placed at a distance of 10 cm from an infinitely long wire carrying a current of 1 A as shown. Calculate (i) the resultant magnetic force, and (ii) the torque, if any, acting on the loop.
explanation: Force experienced by the length SP of the loop per unit length {tex}F_1=\frac{2 u_0I_1I_2}{4\pi r_1}{/tex} {tex}F_1=\frac{2 \times 10^{-7} \times 1 \times 0.2}{10 \times 10^{-2}}{/tex} = {tex}4 \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{Nm}^{-1}{/tex} Force is attractive. Force experienced by the length RQ of the loop per unit length {tex}F_2=\frac{2 \mu_0 l_1 l_2}{4 \pi r_2}{/tex} {tex}F_2=\frac{2 \times 10^{-7} \times 1 \times 0.2}{15 \times 10^{-2}}{/tex} {tex}=2.6 \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{Nm}^{-1}{/tex} Force is repulsive. So, the net force experienced by the loop is (per unit length) F = (F 1 - F 2 ) Total force experienced by the loop is: F NET = (F 1 - F 2 ) l = (1.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -7 ) {tex}\times{/tex} 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -2 = 7 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -9 Net force is attractive in nature. As the lines of action of forces coincide torque is zero.
solution: Force experienced by the length SP of the loop per unit length {tex}F_1=\frac{2 u_0I_1I_2}{4\pi r_1}{/tex} {tex}F_1=\frac{2 \times 10^{-7} \times 1 \times 0.2}{10 \times 10^{-2}}{/tex} = {tex}4 \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{Nm}^{-1}{/tex} Force is attractive. Force experienced by the length RQ of the loop per unit length {tex}F_2=\frac{2 \mu_0 l_1 l_2}{4 \pi r_2}{/tex} {tex}F_2=\frac{2 \times 10^{-7} \times 1 \times 0.2}{15 \times 10^{-2}}{/tex} {tex}=2.6 \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{Nm}^{-1}{/tex} Force is repulsive. So, the net force experienced by the loop is (per unit length) F = (F 1 - F 2 ) Total force experienced by the loop is: F NET = (F 1 - F 2 ) l = (1.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -7 ) {tex}\times{/tex} 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -2 = 7 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -9 Net force is attractive in nature. As the lines of action of forces coincide torque is zero.
solution_html:
1. Force experienced by the length SP of the loop per unit length {tex}F_1=\frac{2 u_0I_1I_2}{4\pi r_1}{/tex}
    {tex}F_1=\frac{2 \times 10^{-7} \times 1 \times 0.2}{10 \times 10^{-2}}{/tex} = {tex}4 \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{Nm}^{-1}{/tex}
    Force is attractive.
    Force experienced by the length RQ of the loop per unit length
    {tex}F_2=\frac{2 \mu_0 l_1 l_2}{4 \pi r_2}{/tex}
    {tex}F_2=\frac{2 \times 10^{-7} \times 1 \times 0.2}{15 \times 10^{-2}}{/tex} {tex}=2.6 \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{Nm}^{-1}{/tex}
    Force is repulsive.
    So, the net force experienced by the loop is (per unit length)
    F = (F1 - F2)
    Total force experienced by the loop is:
    FNET = (F1 - F2) l = (1.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-7) {tex}\times{/tex} 5 {tex}\times{/tex}10-2 = 7 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-9
    Net force is attractive in nature.
  2. As the lines of action of forces coincide torque is zero.

subject: Physics
question_html:
Answer the following:

  1. Magnetic field lines can be entirely confined within the core of a toroid, but not within a straight solenoid. Why?
  2. Does a bar magnet exert a torque on itself due to its own field? Justify your answer.
  3. When an electron revolves around a nucleus, obtain the expression for the magnetic moment associated with it.
question_text: Answer the following: Magnetic field lines can be entirely confined within the core of a toroid, but not within a straight solenoid. Why? Does a bar magnet exert a torque on itself due to its own field? Justify your answer. When an electron revolves around a nucleus, obtain the expression for the magnetic moment associated with it.
explanation: If field lines were extremely confined between two ends of a straight solenoid, the flux through the cross section at each end would be non zero. But the flux of field B through any closed surface must always be zero, For a toroid this difficulty is absent. No, there is no force on torque on an element due to the field produced by that element itself. {tex}I=\frac{e}{T}, T=\frac{2 \pi r}{v}{/tex} {tex}I=\frac{e v}{2 \pi r}, \mu=I \pi r^2=\frac{e v r}{2}{/tex}
solution: If field lines were extremely confined between two ends of a straight solenoid, the flux through the cross section at each end would be non zero. But the flux of field B through any closed surface must always be zero, For a toroid this difficulty is absent. No, there is no force on torque on an element due to the field produced by that element itself. {tex}I=\frac{e}{T}, T=\frac{2 \pi r}{v}{/tex} {tex}I=\frac{e v}{2 \pi r}, \mu=I \pi r^2=\frac{e v r}{2}{/tex}
solution_html:
1. If field lines were extremely confined between two ends of a straight solenoid, the flux through the cross section at each end would be non zero. But the flux of field B through any closed surface must always be zero, For a toroid this difficulty is absent.
  2. No, there is no force on torque on an element due to the field produced by that element itself.
  3. {tex}I=\frac{e}{T}, T=\frac{2 \pi r}{v}{/tex}
    {tex}I=\frac{e v}{2 \pi r}, \mu=I \pi r^2=\frac{e v r}{2}{/tex}

question_html:
1. Briefly explain how a galvanometer is converted into an ammeter.
  2. A galvanometer coil has a resistance of 15 {tex}\Omega{/tex} and it shows full scale deflection for a current of 4 mA. Convert it into an ammeter of range 0 to 6 A.
question_text: Briefly explain how a galvanometer is converted into an ammeter. A galvanometer coil has a resistance of 15 {tex}\Omega{/tex} and it shows full scale deflection for a current of 4 mA. Convert it into an ammeter of range 0 to 6 A.
explanation: By connecting a small resistance called shunt (S) in parallel to coil of the galvanometer. The value of S is related to the maximum current (I) to be measured as S = I g G / I-I g . G = 15 {tex}\Omega{/tex} I g = 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 A I = 6A I g G = (I - I g )S {tex}S=\frac{\mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{g}} \mathrm{G}}{\mathrm{I}-{\mathrm{I}}_{\mathrm{g}}}=\frac{4 \times 10^{-3} \times 15}{6-4 \times 10^{-3}}{/tex} = 0.01 {tex}\Omega{/tex}
solution: By connecting a small resistance called shunt (S) in parallel to coil of the galvanometer. The value of S is related to the maximum current (I) to be measured as S = I g G / I-I g . G = 15 {tex}\Omega{/tex} I g = 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 A I = 6A I g G = (I - I g )S {tex}S=\frac{\mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{g}} \mathrm{G}}{\mathrm{I}-{\mathrm{I}}_{\mathrm{g}}}=\frac{4 \times 10^{-3} \times 15}{6-4 \times 10^{-3}}{/tex} = 0.01 {tex}\Omega{/tex}
solution_html:
1. By connecting a small resistance called shunt (S) in parallel to coil of the galvanometer. The value of S is related to the maximum current (I) to be measured as S = Ig G / I-Ig.
  2. G = 15 {tex}\Omega{/tex}
    Ig = 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 A
    I = 6A
    IgG = (I - Ig)S
    {tex}S=\frac{\mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{g}} \mathrm{G}}{\mathrm{I}-{\mathrm{I}}_{\mathrm{g}}}=\frac{4 \times 10^{-3} \times 15}{6-4 \times 10^{-3}}{/tex}
    = 0.01 {tex}\Omega{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. Briefly explain how a galvanometer is converted into a voltmeter.
  2. A voltmeter of a certain range is constructed by connecting a resistance of 980 {tex}\Omega{/tex} in series with a galvanometer. When the resistance of 470 {tex}\Omega{/tex} is connected in series, the range gets halved. Find the resistance of the galvanometer.
question_text: Briefly explain how a galvanometer is converted into a voltmeter. A voltmeter of a certain range is constructed by connecting a resistance of 980 {tex}\Omega{/tex} in series with a galvanometer. When the resistance of 470 {tex}\Omega{/tex} is connected in series, the range gets halved. Find the resistance of the galvanometer.
explanation: A galvanometer may be converted into voltmeter by connecting a high value resistance R in series with coil of the galvanometer. The value of (R) is related to the maximum voltage (V) to be measured as {tex}\mathrm{R}=\frac{\mathrm{V}}{\mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{g}}}-\mathrm{G}{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{I}_g \quad=\frac{\mathrm{V}}{\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{g}}+\mathrm{R}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{\mathrm{V}}{\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{g}}+980}=\frac{\mathrm{V}}{2\left(\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{g}}+470\right)}{/tex} 2R g + 940 = R g + 980 R g = 40 {tex}\Omega{/tex}
solution: A galvanometer may be converted into voltmeter by connecting a high value resistance R in series with coil of the galvanometer. The value of (R) is related to the maximum voltage (V) to be measured as {tex}\mathrm{R}=\frac{\mathrm{V}}{\mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{g}}}-\mathrm{G}{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{I}_g \quad=\frac{\mathrm{V}}{\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{g}}+\mathrm{R}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{\mathrm{V}}{\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{g}}+980}=\frac{\mathrm{V}}{2\left(\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{g}}+470\right)}{/tex} 2R g + 940 = R g + 980 R g = 40 {tex}\Omega{/tex}
solution_html:
1. A galvanometer may be converted into voltmeter by connecting a high value resistance R in series with coil of the galvanometer. The value of (R) is related to the maximum voltage (V) to be measured as {tex}\mathrm{R}=\frac{\mathrm{V}}{\mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{g}}}-\mathrm{G}{/tex}
  2. {tex}\mathrm{I}_g \quad=\frac{\mathrm{V}}{\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{g}}+\mathrm{R}}{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{\mathrm{V}}{\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{g}}+980}=\frac{\mathrm{V}}{2\left(\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{g}}+470\right)}{/tex}
    2Rg + 940 = Rg + 980
    Rg = 40{tex}\Omega{/tex}

question_html: A long straight wire AB carries a current of 3 A. A proton P travels at 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 106 m/s parallel to the wire 0.3 m from it and in a direction opposite to the current as shown in the figure. Calculate the force which the magnetic field due to the current carrying wire exerts on the proton. Also give its direction.
question_text: A long straight wire AB carries a current of 3 A. A proton P travels at 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 6 m/s parallel to the wire 0.3 m from it and in a direction opposite to the current as shown in the figure. Calculate the force which the magnetic field due to the current carrying wire exerts on the proton. Also give its direction.
explanation: Force on Proton F = Bqv {tex}\mathrm{B}=\frac{\mu_0 I}{2 \pi a} \left(\theta=90^{\circ}\right){/tex} {tex}\mathrm{F}=\frac{\mu_0 I q v}{2 \pi a}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times 3 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 5 \times 10^6}{2 \pi \times 0.3}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{30 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-20}}{0.3}=1.6 \times 10^{18} \mathrm{~N}{/tex} Direction- right at point P/away from AB
solution: Force on Proton F = Bqv {tex}\mathrm{B}=\frac{\mu_0 I}{2 \pi a} \left(\theta=90^{\circ}\right){/tex} {tex}\mathrm{F}=\frac{\mu_0 I q v}{2 \pi a}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times 3 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 5 \times 10^6}{2 \pi \times 0.3}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{30 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-20}}{0.3}=1.6 \times 10^{18} \mathrm{~N}{/tex} Direction- right at point P/away from AB
solution_html:
Force on Proton
F = Bqv
{tex}\mathrm{B}=\frac{\mu_0 I}{2 \pi a} \left(\theta=90^{\circ}\right){/tex}
{tex}\mathrm{F}=\frac{\mu_0 I q v}{2 \pi a}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times 3 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 5 \times 10^6}{2 \pi \times 0.3}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{30 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-20}}{0.3}=1.6 \times 10^{18} \mathrm{~N}{/tex}
Direction- right at point P/away from AB

question_html: Two long straight wires carrying currents of 2 A and 5 A in the same direction are kept parallel, 10 cm apart from each other. Calculate the force acting between them and write its nature.
question_text: Two long straight wires carrying currents of 2 A and 5 A in the same direction are kept parallel, 10 cm apart from each other. Calculate the force acting between them and write its nature.
explanation: Force per unit length between straight current carrying conductors, {tex}\mathrm{F}=\frac{\mu_0 I_1 I_2}{2 \pi \mathrm{r}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times 2 \times 5}{2 \pi \times 10 \times 10^{-2}}{/tex} = 20 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -6 = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -5 N/m Since the current is in the same direction the force will be attractive.
solution: Force per unit length between straight current carrying conductors, {tex}\mathrm{F}=\frac{\mu_0 I_1 I_2}{2 \pi \mathrm{r}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times 2 \times 5}{2 \pi \times 10 \times 10^{-2}}{/tex} = 20 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -6 = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -5 N/m Since the current is in the same direction the force will be attractive.
solution_html:
Force per unit length between straight current carrying conductors,
{tex}\mathrm{F}=\frac{\mu_0 I_1 I_2}{2 \pi \mathrm{r}}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times 2 \times 5}{2 \pi \times 10 \times 10^{-2}}{/tex}
= 20 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-6
= 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 N/m

Since the current is in the same direction the force will be attractive.

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. Depict the magnetic field lines due to a circular current carrying loop showing the direction of field lines.
  2. A current I is flowing in a conductor placed along the x-axis as shown in the figure. Find the magnitude and direction of the magnetic field due to a small current element {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{d} l}{/tex} dl lying at the origin at points (i) (0, d, 0) and (ii) (0, 0, d).
question_text: Depict the magnetic field lines due to a circular current carrying loop showing the direction of field lines. A current I is flowing in a conductor placed along the x-axis as shown in the figure. Find the magnitude and direction of the magnetic field due to a small current element {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{d} l}{/tex} dl lying at the origin at points (i) (0, d, 0) and (ii) (0, 0, d).
explanation: {tex}\mathrm{d} \vec{\mathrm{B}}=\frac{\mu_0 I(\mathrm{~d} \vec{\mathrm{l}} \times \vec{\mathrm{r}})}{4 \pi \mathrm{r}^3}{/tex} Where, {tex}\vec{r}=(0-0) \hat{i}+(d-0) \hat{j}+(0-0) \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}=(\mathrm{d}) \hat{\mathrm{j}}{/tex} {tex}|\vec{\mathrm{r}}|=\mathrm{d}{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{d} \vec{B}=\frac{\mu_0 I}{4 \pi} \frac{(\mathrm{dl}(\hat{\mathrm{i}}) \times(\mathrm{d}) \hat{\mathrm{j}})}{4 \pi \mathrm{d}^3}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\mu_0 \mathrm{I}(\mathrm{dl}) \hat{k}}{\mathrm{~d}^2}{/tex} {tex}\vec{\mathrm{r}}=(\mathrm{d}) \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}|\vec{\mathrm{r}}|=\mathrm{d}{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{d} \vec{B}=\frac{\mu_0 \mathrm{I}(\mathrm{dl})(\mathrm{d}) \hat{k}}{\mathrm{~d}^3}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\mu_0 \mathrm{I}(\mathrm{dl})(\hat{-j})}{\mathrm{d}^2}{/tex}
solution: {tex}\mathrm{d} \vec{\mathrm{B}}=\frac{\mu_0 I(\mathrm{~d} \vec{\mathrm{l}} \times \vec{\mathrm{r}})}{4 \pi \mathrm{r}^3}{/tex} Where, {tex}\vec{r}=(0-0) \hat{i}+(d-0) \hat{j}+(0-0) \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}=(\mathrm{d}) \hat{\mathrm{j}}{/tex} {tex}|\vec{\mathrm{r}}|=\mathrm{d}{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{d} \vec{B}=\frac{\mu_0 I}{4 \pi} \frac{(\mathrm{dl}(\hat{\mathrm{i}}) \times(\mathrm{d}) \hat{\mathrm{j}})}{4 \pi \mathrm{d}^3}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\mu_0 \mathrm{I}(\mathrm{dl}) \hat{k}}{\mathrm{~d}^2}{/tex} {tex}\vec{\mathrm{r}}=(\mathrm{d}) \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}|\vec{\mathrm{r}}|=\mathrm{d}{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{d} \vec{B}=\frac{\mu_0 \mathrm{I}(\mathrm{dl})(\mathrm{d}) \hat{k}}{\mathrm{~d}^3}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\mu_0 \mathrm{I}(\mathrm{dl})(\hat{-j})}{\mathrm{d}^2}{/tex}
solution_html:
1. {tex}\mathrm{d} \vec{\mathrm{B}}=\frac{\mu_0 I(\mathrm{~d} \vec{\mathrm{l}} \times \vec{\mathrm{r}})}{4 \pi \mathrm{r}^3}{/tex}
      Where, {tex}\vec{r}=(0-0) \hat{i}+(d-0) \hat{j}+(0-0) \hat{k}{/tex}
      {tex}=(\mathrm{d}) \hat{\mathrm{j}}{/tex}
      {tex}|\vec{\mathrm{r}}|=\mathrm{d}{/tex}
      {tex}\mathrm{d} \vec{B}=\frac{\mu_0 I}{4 \pi} \frac{(\mathrm{dl}(\hat{\mathrm{i}}) \times(\mathrm{d}) \hat{\mathrm{j}})}{4 \pi \mathrm{d}^3}{/tex}
      {tex}=\frac{\mu_0 \mathrm{I}(\mathrm{dl}) \hat{k}}{\mathrm{~d}^2}{/tex}
    2. {tex}\vec{\mathrm{r}}=(\mathrm{d}) \hat{k}{/tex}
      {tex}|\vec{\mathrm{r}}|=\mathrm{d}{/tex}
      {tex}\mathrm{d} \vec{B}=\frac{\mu_0 \mathrm{I}(\mathrm{dl})(\mathrm{d}) \hat{k}}{\mathrm{~d}^3}{/tex}
      {tex}=\frac{\mu_0 \mathrm{I}(\mathrm{dl})(\hat{-j})}{\mathrm{d}^2}{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html: A long straight wire AB carries a current of 4 A. A proton P travels at 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 106 ms-1 parallel to the wire 0.2 m from it and in a direction opposite to the current as shown in the figure. Calculate the force which the magnetic field due to the current carrying wire exerts on the proton. Also specify its direction.
question_text: A long straight wire AB carries a current of 4 A. A proton P travels at 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 6 ms -1 parallel to the wire 0.2 m from it and in a direction opposite to the current as shown in the figure. Calculate the force which the magnetic field due to the current carrying wire exerts on the proton. Also specify its direction.
explanation: The magnetic field at point P due to the current in wire AB, B = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I}{2 \pi r}=\frac{4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times 4}{2 \pi \times 0.2}{/tex} = 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -6 T This field acts on the proton normally into the plane of the paper. According to Fleming's left-hand rule, a magnetic force acts on the proton towards right in the plane of the paper. The magnitude of this force is F = qvB sin 90 o = 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -19 {tex}\times{/tex} 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -6 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 = 2.56 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -18 N
solution: The magnetic field at point P due to the current in wire AB, B = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I}{2 \pi r}=\frac{4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times 4}{2 \pi \times 0.2}{/tex} = 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -6 T This field acts on the proton normally into the plane of the paper. According to Fleming's left-hand rule, a magnetic force acts on the proton towards right in the plane of the paper. The magnitude of this force is F = qvB sin 90 o = 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -19 {tex}\times{/tex} 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -6 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 = 2.56 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -18 N
solution_html:
The magnetic field at point P due to the current in wire AB,
B = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I}{2 \pi r}=\frac{4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times 4}{2 \pi \times 0.2}{/tex} = 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-6 T
This field acts on the proton normally into the plane of the paper. According to Fleming's left-hand rule, a magnetic force acts on the proton towards right in the plane of the paper. The magnitude of this force is
F = qvB sin 90o
= 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-19 {tex}\times{/tex} 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 106 {tex}\times{/tex} 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-6 {tex}\times{/tex} 1
= 2.56 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-18 N

subject: Physics
question_html: A proton, a deuteron and an alpha particle, are accelerated through the same potential difference and then subjected to a uniform magnetic field {tex}\vec{B}{/tex}, perpendicular to the direction of their motions. Compare (i) their kinetic energies, and (ii) if the radius of the circular path described by proton is 5 cm, determine the radii of the paths described by deuteron and alpha particle.
question_text: A proton, a deuteron and an alpha particle, are accelerated through the same potential difference and then subjected to a uniform magnetic field {tex}\vec{B}{/tex} , perpendicular to the direction of their motions. Compare (i) their kinetic energies, and (ii) if the radius of the circular path described by proton is 5 cm, determine the radii of the paths described by deuteron and alpha particle.
explanation: For a given p.d., the kinetic energy of a charged particle is proportional to its charge. {tex}\therefore{/tex} K p : K d : {tex}K_{\alpha}{/tex} = e : e : 2e = 1 : 1 : 2 Radius of the circular path of any particle of kinetic energy K {tex}r=\frac{m v}{q B}=\frac{m}{q B} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2 K}{m}}=\frac{\sqrt{2 m K}}{q B}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} For proton, {tex}r_{p}=\frac{\sqrt{2 m_{p} K_{p}}}{e B}{/tex} = 5cm For deutron, {tex}r_{d}=\frac{\sqrt{2 m_{d} K_{d}}}{e B}=\frac{\sqrt{2 \times 2 m_{p} \times K_{p}}}{e B}{/tex} {tex}=\sqrt{2} r_{p}{/tex} = 1.414 {tex}\times{/tex} 5 cm = 7.07 cm For {tex}\alpha{/tex} -particle, {tex}r_{a}=\frac{\sqrt{2 m_{\alpha} K_{d}}}{2 e B}=\frac{\sqrt{2 \times 4 \times m_{p} \times 2 K_{p}}}{2 e B}{/tex} = 2r p = 10 cm
solution: For a given p.d., the kinetic energy of a charged particle is proportional to its charge. {tex}\therefore{/tex} K p : K d : {tex}K_{\alpha}{/tex} = e : e : 2e = 1 : 1 : 2 Radius of the circular path of any particle of kinetic energy K {tex}r=\frac{m v}{q B}=\frac{m}{q B} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2 K}{m}}=\frac{\sqrt{2 m K}}{q B}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} For proton, {tex}r_{p}=\frac{\sqrt{2 m_{p} K_{p}}}{e B}{/tex} = 5cm For deutron, {tex}r_{d}=\frac{\sqrt{2 m_{d} K_{d}}}{e B}=\frac{\sqrt{2 \times 2 m_{p} \times K_{p}}}{e B}{/tex} {tex}=\sqrt{2} r_{p}{/tex} = 1.414 {tex}\times{/tex} 5 cm = 7.07 cm For {tex}\alpha{/tex} -particle, {tex}r_{a}=\frac{\sqrt{2 m_{\alpha} K_{d}}}{2 e B}=\frac{\sqrt{2 \times 4 \times m_{p} \times 2 K_{p}}}{2 e B}{/tex} = 2r p = 10 cm
solution_html:
For a given p.d., the kinetic energy of a charged particle is proportional to its charge.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Kp : Kd : {tex}K_{\alpha}{/tex} = e : e : 2e = 1 : 1 : 2
Radius of the circular path of any particle of kinetic energy K
{tex}r=\frac{m v}{q B}=\frac{m}{q B} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2 K}{m}}=\frac{\sqrt{2 m K}}{q B}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} For proton, {tex}r_{p}=\frac{\sqrt{2 m_{p} K_{p}}}{e B}{/tex} = 5cm
For deutron, {tex}r_{d}=\frac{\sqrt{2 m_{d} K_{d}}}{e B}=\frac{\sqrt{2 \times 2 m_{p} \times K_{p}}}{e B}{/tex}
{tex}=\sqrt{2} r_{p}{/tex} = 1.414 {tex}\times{/tex} 5 cm = 7.07 cm
For {tex}\alpha{/tex}-particle, {tex}r_{a}=\frac{\sqrt{2 m_{\alpha} K_{d}}}{2 e B}=\frac{\sqrt{2 \times 4 \times m_{p} \times 2 K_{p}}}{2 e B}{/tex}
= 2rp = 10 cm

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. Obtain the conditions under which an electron does not suffer any deflection while passing through a magnetic field.
  2. Two protons P and Q moving with the same speed pass through the magnetic fields {tex}\vec{\mathrm{B}_1}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{\mathrm{B}_2}{/tex} respectively, at right angles to the field directions. If {tex}\left|{\overrightarrow{B}_2}\right|>\left|\overrightarrow{B}_1\right|{/tex}, which of the two protons will describe the circular path of smaller radius? Explain.
question_text: Obtain the conditions under which an electron does not suffer any deflection while passing through a magnetic field. Two protons P and Q moving with the same speed pass through the magnetic fields {tex}\vec{\mathrm{B}_1}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{\mathrm{B}_2}{/tex} respectively, at right angles to the field directions. If {tex}\left|{\overrightarrow{B}_2}\right|>\left|\overrightarrow{B}_1\right|{/tex} , which of the two protons will describe the circular path of smaller radius? Explain.
explanation: No deflection if electron moves parallel or anti parallel to the magnetic field The radius of circular path {tex}r=\frac{m v}{B q}{/tex} {tex}\frac{r_1}{r_2}=\frac{B_2}{B_1}{/tex} As B 1 < B 2 r 2 < r 1
solution: No deflection if electron moves parallel or anti parallel to the magnetic field The radius of circular path {tex}r=\frac{m v}{B q}{/tex} {tex}\frac{r_1}{r_2}=\frac{B_2}{B_1}{/tex} As B 1 < B 2 r 2 < r 1
solution_html:
1. No deflection if electron moves parallel or anti parallel to the magnetic field
  2. The radius of circular path {tex}r=\frac{m v}{B q}{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{r_1}{r_2}=\frac{B_2}{B_1}{/tex}
    As B1 < B2 r2 < r1

subject: Physics
question_html: Explain how a galvanometer can be converted into an ammeter of a given range. Derive an expression for shunt resistance and current for full scale deflection. Find the effective resistance of the ammeter.
question_text: Explain how a galvanometer can be converted into an ammeter of a given range. Derive an expression for shunt resistance and current for full scale deflection. Find the effective resistance of the ammeter.
explanation: A galvanometer is converted into an ammeter when a suitable shunt resistance is connected in parallel with the galvanometer. Glg = (I - Ig)S {tex}\mathrm{S}=\left(\frac{1 g}{l-1 g}\right) \mathrm{G}{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{R}_{eff}=\frac{G S}{G+S}{/tex}
solution: A galvanometer is converted into an ammeter when a suitable shunt resistance is connected in parallel with the galvanometer. Glg = (I - Ig)S {tex}\mathrm{S}=\left(\frac{1 g}{l-1 g}\right) \mathrm{G}{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{R}_{eff}=\frac{G S}{G+S}{/tex}
solution_html:
A galvanometer is converted into an ammeter when a suitable shunt resistance is connected in parallel with the galvanometer.

Glg = (I - Ig)S
{tex}\mathrm{S}=\left(\frac{1 g}{l-1 g}\right) \mathrm{G}{/tex}
{tex}\mathrm{R}_{eff}=\frac{G S}{G+S}{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html: Two coils P and Q of radius R and 2R are lying in the same plane with their centres coinciding. Find the magnitude and direction of the resultant magnetic field at the common centre if they respectively carry currents 3I and 2I in opposite directions.
question_text: Two coils P and Q of radius R and 2R are lying in the same plane with their centres coinciding. Find the magnitude and direction of the resultant magnetic field at the common centre if they respectively carry currents 3I and 2I in opposite directions.
explanation: Given the radius of coil P = R the radius of coil Q = 2R both coils lie in the same plane and their centres coincide {tex}B_P=\frac{3 \mu_0 I}{2 R}{/tex} {tex}B_Q=\frac{\mu_0 2 I}{2(2 R)}{/tex} Net {tex}B=\frac{3 \mu_0 I}{2 R}-\frac{\mu_0 I}{2 R}=\frac{2 \mu_0 I}{2 R}=\frac{\mu_0 I}{R}{/tex} The resultant direction of the magnetic field is in the direction of the magnetic field produced by coil P.
solution: Given the radius of coil P = R the radius of coil Q = 2R both coils lie in the same plane and their centres coincide {tex}B_P=\frac{3 \mu_0 I}{2 R}{/tex} {tex}B_Q=\frac{\mu_0 2 I}{2(2 R)}{/tex} Net {tex}B=\frac{3 \mu_0 I}{2 R}-\frac{\mu_0 I}{2 R}=\frac{2 \mu_0 I}{2 R}=\frac{\mu_0 I}{R}{/tex} The resultant direction of the magnetic field is in the direction of the magnetic field produced by coil P.
solution_html:
Given
the radius of coil P = R
the radius of coil Q = 2R
both coils lie in the same plane and their centres coincide
{tex}B_P=\frac{3 \mu_0 I}{2 R}{/tex}
{tex}B_Q=\frac{\mu_0 2 I}{2(2 R)}{/tex}
Net {tex}B=\frac{3 \mu_0 I}{2 R}-\frac{\mu_0 I}{2 R}=\frac{2 \mu_0 I}{2 R}=\frac{\mu_0 I}{R}{/tex}
The resultant direction of the magnetic field is in the direction of the magnetic field produced by coil P.

subject: Physics
question_html:
A particle of charge q and mass m is moving with velocity {tex}\vec{v}{/tex} in the positive x-direction.

  1. It is subjected to a uniform magnetic field B directed along negative z-direction. Explain briefly the trajectory it would describe.
  2. When the particle is subjected simultaneously to both the magnetic and electric fields directed along the z-axis and y-axis respectively, obtain the condition when the particle will go undeflected.
question_text: A particle of charge q and mass m is moving with velocity {tex}\vec{v}{/tex} in the positive x-direction. It is subjected to a uniform magnetic field B directed along negative z-direction. Explain briefly the trajectory it would describe. When the particle is subjected simultaneously to both the magnetic and electric fields directed along the z-axis and y-axis respectively, obtain the condition when the particle will go undeflected.
explanation: Here the velocity of particle {tex}\vec{v}{/tex} is along the positive x - direction. The magnetic field is along the negative z-direction. The force experienced by the particle is {tex}F=q[v \times B]{/tex} So the direction of F is found by applying the screw rule or the right-hand thumb rule. It is along the positive y-direction. So the particle makes a circular path, where the radius of the path is {tex}\frac{m v^2}{r}=q v B{/tex} or {tex}r=\frac{m v}{q B}{/tex} For undeflected motion magnetic force on the charge = electric force on the charge i.e. qvB = qE Which means when the particle have velocity {tex}v=\frac{E}{B}{/tex} , the particle will go undeflected
solution: Here the velocity of particle {tex}\vec{v}{/tex} is along the positive x - direction. The magnetic field is along the negative z-direction. The force experienced by the particle is {tex}F=q[v \times B]{/tex} So the direction of F is found by applying the screw rule or the right-hand thumb rule. It is along the positive y-direction. So the particle makes a circular path, where the radius of the path is {tex}\frac{m v^2}{r}=q v B{/tex} or {tex}r=\frac{m v}{q B}{/tex} For undeflected motion magnetic force on the charge = electric force on the charge i.e. qvB = qE Which means when the particle have velocity {tex}v=\frac{E}{B}{/tex} , the particle will go undeflected
solution_html:
1. Here the velocity of particle {tex}\vec{v}{/tex} is along the positive x - direction. The magnetic field is along the negative z-direction.
    The force experienced by the particle is
    {tex}F=q[v \times B]{/tex}
    So the direction of F is found by applying the screw rule or the right-hand thumb rule. It is along the positive y-direction. So the particle makes a circular path, where the radius of the path is
    {tex}\frac{m v^2}{r}=q v B{/tex} or {tex}r=\frac{m v}{q B}{/tex}
  2. For undeflected motion magnetic force on the charge = electric force on the charge
    i.e. qvB = qE
    Which means when the particle have velocity {tex}v=\frac{E}{B}{/tex}, the particle will go undeflected

subject: Physics
question_html: Two coils P and Q of radius R and 2R respectively are lying in perpendicular planes having a common centre. Find the magnitude and direction of the resultant magnetic field at the common centre, if they carry the currents I and {tex}2 \sqrt{3}{/tex} I respectively.
question_text: Two coils P and Q of radius R and 2R respectively are lying in perpendicular planes having a common centre. Find the magnitude and direction of the resultant magnetic field at the common centre, if they carry the currents I and {tex}2 \sqrt{3}{/tex} I respectively.
explanation: The magnetic field at the centre of the circular current is {tex}B=\frac{\mu_0 I}{2 R}{/tex} For coil P {tex}B_P=\frac{\mu_0 I}{2 R}{/tex} For coil Q of radius 2R carrying a current of {tex}2 \sqrt{3} I{/tex} {tex}B_Q=\frac{\mu_0 2 \sqrt{3} I}{2 \times 2 R}=\frac{\sqrt{3} \mu_0 I}{2 R}{/tex} P and Q are perpendicular to each other therefore the resultant magnetic field is given by. {tex}B_{\text {resultant }}=\sqrt{B_Q^2+B_P^2}{/tex} {tex}B_{\text {resultant }}=\sqrt{\left(\frac{\sqrt{3} \mu_0 I}{2 R}\right)^2+\left(\frac{\mu_0 I}{2 R}\right)^2}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\mu_0 I}{2 R} \sqrt{3+1}=\frac{\mu_0 I}{R}{/tex} The direction of the magnetic field is {tex}\tan \theta=\frac{B_Q}{B_P}=\sqrt{3}{/tex} {tex}\theta{/tex} = 60 o
solution: The magnetic field at the centre of the circular current is {tex}B=\frac{\mu_0 I}{2 R}{/tex} For coil P {tex}B_P=\frac{\mu_0 I}{2 R}{/tex} For coil Q of radius 2R carrying a current of {tex}2 \sqrt{3} I{/tex} {tex}B_Q=\frac{\mu_0 2 \sqrt{3} I}{2 \times 2 R}=\frac{\sqrt{3} \mu_0 I}{2 R}{/tex} P and Q are perpendicular to each other therefore the resultant magnetic field is given by. {tex}B_{\text {resultant }}=\sqrt{B_Q^2+B_P^2}{/tex} {tex}B_{\text {resultant }}=\sqrt{\left(\frac{\sqrt{3} \mu_0 I}{2 R}\right)^2+\left(\frac{\mu_0 I}{2 R}\right)^2}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\mu_0 I}{2 R} \sqrt{3+1}=\frac{\mu_0 I}{R}{/tex} The direction of the magnetic field is {tex}\tan \theta=\frac{B_Q}{B_P}=\sqrt{3}{/tex} {tex}\theta{/tex} = 60 o
solution_html:
The magnetic field at the centre of the circular current is
{tex}B=\frac{\mu_0 I}{2 R}{/tex}
For coil P
{tex}B_P=\frac{\mu_0 I}{2 R}{/tex}
For coil Q of radius 2R carrying a current of {tex}2 \sqrt{3} I{/tex}
{tex}B_Q=\frac{\mu_0 2 \sqrt{3} I}{2 \times 2 R}=\frac{\sqrt{3} \mu_0 I}{2 R}{/tex}
P and Q are perpendicular to each other therefore the resultant magnetic field is given by.
{tex}B_{\text {resultant }}=\sqrt{B_Q^2+B_P^2}{/tex}
{tex}B_{\text {resultant }}=\sqrt{\left(\frac{\sqrt{3} \mu_0 I}{2 R}\right)^2+\left(\frac{\mu_0 I}{2 R}\right)^2}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{\mu_0 I}{2 R} \sqrt{3+1}=\frac{\mu_0 I}{R}{/tex}
The direction of the magnetic field is
{tex}\tan \theta=\frac{B_Q}{B_P}=\sqrt{3}{/tex}
{tex}\theta{/tex} = 60o

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. In a moving coil galvanometer, why is the magnetic field required to be radial?
  2. A 100 turn closely wound circular coil of radius 10 cm carries a current of 3.2 A. Calculate (i) the magnetic field at the centre of the coil, and (b) its magnetic moment.
question_text: In a moving coil galvanometer, why is the magnetic field required to be radial? A 100 turn closely wound circular coil of radius 10 cm carries a current of 3.2 A. Calculate (i) the magnetic field at the centre of the coil, and (b) its magnetic moment.
explanation: We need a radial magnetic field to ensure that the deflection of the galvanometer needle is directly proportional to the current flowing through its coil. Magnetic field at the centre {tex}B_o=\frac{\mu_0}{2 r} N I{/tex} {tex}=\frac{4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times 100 \times 3.2}{2 \times 10 \times 10^{-2}} \mathrm{~T}{/tex} {tex}\cong 2 \times 10^{-3} T=2 \mathrm{~m} T{/tex} Magnetic Moment = NIA {tex}100 \times 3.2 \times \pi \times\left(10^{-1}\right)^2{/tex} ampere (meter) 2 {tex}\cong 10 \mathrm{Am}^2{/tex}
solution: We need a radial magnetic field to ensure that the deflection of the galvanometer needle is directly proportional to the current flowing through its coil. Magnetic field at the centre {tex}B_o=\frac{\mu_0}{2 r} N I{/tex} {tex}=\frac{4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times 100 \times 3.2}{2 \times 10 \times 10^{-2}} \mathrm{~T}{/tex} {tex}\cong 2 \times 10^{-3} T=2 \mathrm{~m} T{/tex} Magnetic Moment = NIA {tex}100 \times 3.2 \times \pi \times\left(10^{-1}\right)^2{/tex} ampere (meter) 2 {tex}\cong 10 \mathrm{Am}^2{/tex}
solution_html:
1. We need a radial magnetic field to ensure that the deflection of the galvanometer needle is directly proportional to the current flowing through its coil.
    1. Magnetic field at the centre
      {tex}B_o=\frac{\mu_0}{2 r} N I{/tex}
      {tex}=\frac{4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times 100 \times 3.2}{2 \times 10 \times 10^{-2}} \mathrm{~T}{/tex}
      {tex}\cong 2 \times 10^{-3} T=2 \mathrm{~m} T{/tex}
    2. Magnetic Moment = NIA
      {tex}100 \times 3.2 \times \pi \times\left(10^{-1}\right)^2{/tex} ampere (meter)2
      {tex}\cong 10 \mathrm{Am}^2{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html: A charged particle q is moving in the presence of a magnetic field B which is inclined to an angle 30o with the direction of the motion of the particle. Draw the trajectory followed by the particle in the presence of the field and explain how the particle describes this path.
question_text: A charged particle q is moving in the presence of a magnetic field B which is inclined to an angle 30 o with the direction of the motion of the particle. Draw the trajectory followed by the particle in the presence of the field and explain how the particle describes this path.
explanation: The force acting on a charged particle in a magnetic field is given by; {tex}\vec{F}=q(\vec{V} \times \vec{B}){/tex} Where q = charge of the particle v = velocity of particle and B = magnetic field in that region Let velocity has a component along with B, this component remains unchanged as the motion along the magnetic field will not be affected by the magnetic field. The motion in a plane perpendicular to B is circular one, thereby producing a helical motion. Thus the described path will be helical as shown in the figure.
solution: The force acting on a charged particle in a magnetic field is given by; {tex}\vec{F}=q(\vec{V} \times \vec{B}){/tex} Where q = charge of the particle v = velocity of particle and B = magnetic field in that region Let velocity has a component along with B, this component remains unchanged as the motion along the magnetic field will not be affected by the magnetic field. The motion in a plane perpendicular to B is circular one, thereby producing a helical motion. Thus the described path will be helical as shown in the figure.
solution_html:
The force acting on a charged particle in a magnetic field is given by; {tex}\vec{F}=q(\vec{V} \times \vec{B}){/tex}
Where q = charge of the particle
v = velocity of particle and
B = magnetic field in that region
Let velocity has a component along with B, this component remains unchanged as the motion along the magnetic field will not be affected by the magnetic field.
The motion in a plane perpendicular to B is circular one, thereby producing a helical motion. Thus the described path will be helical as shown in the figure.

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. Why do we use a shunt to convert a galvanometer into an ammeter ?
  2. A galvanometer of resistance 15 {tex}\Omega{/tex} shows a full scale deflection on the meter scale for a current of 6 mA. Calculate the value of the shunt resistance required to convert the galvanometer into an ammeter of range 0 - 6 A.
question_text: Why do we use a shunt to convert a galvanometer into an ammeter ? A galvanometer of resistance 15 {tex}\Omega{/tex} shows a full scale deflection on the meter scale for a current of 6 mA. Calculate the value of the shunt resistance required to convert the galvanometer into an ammeter of range 0 - 6 A.
explanation: The ammeter is connected in series, in the relevant circuit branch. Hence its resistance must be (very) low so that the circuit current is not affected. A (very) low shunt resistance makes the effective resistance of galvanometer (very) low. question_html:
1. Why is a high resistance required to convert a galvanometer into a voltmeter?
  2. A galvanometer coil has a resistance of 12 {tex}\Omega{/tex} and the meter shows full scale deflection for a current of 3 mA. How will you convert it into a voltmeter of range 0 to 18 V?
question_text: Why is a high resistance required to convert a galvanometer into a voltmeter? A galvanometer coil has a resistance of 12 {tex}\Omega{/tex} and the meter shows full scale deflection for a current of 3 mA. How will you convert it into a voltmeter of range 0 to 18 V?
explanation: We need high (series) resistance because ideal voltmeter should have an infinite resistance. {tex} R=\left\lceil\frac{V}{i g}-G\right]{/tex} Here {tex}V=18 \mathrm{~V}, \mathrm{G}=12 \Omega, \mathrm{i}_{\mathrm{g}}=3 \mathrm{~mA}{/tex} {tex} R=\left[\frac{18}{3 \times 10^{-3}}-12\right] \Omega {/tex} {tex} =[6000-12] \Omega=5988 \Omega {/tex}
solution: We need high (series) resistance because ideal voltmeter should have an infinite resistance. {tex} R=\left\lceil\frac{V}{i g}-G\right]{/tex} Here {tex}V=18 \mathrm{~V}, \mathrm{G}=12 \Omega, \mathrm{i}_{\mathrm{g}}=3 \mathrm{~mA}{/tex} {tex} R=\left[\frac{18}{3 \times 10^{-3}}-12\right] \Omega {/tex} {tex} =[6000-12] \Omega=5988 \Omega {/tex}
solution_html:
1. We need high (series) resistance because ideal voltmeter should have an infinite resistance.
  2. {tex} R=\left\lceil\frac{V}{i g}-G\right]{/tex} Here {tex}V=18 \mathrm{~V}, \mathrm{G}=12 \Omega, \mathrm{i}_{\mathrm{g}}=3 \mathrm{~mA}{/tex}
    {tex} R=\left[\frac{18}{3 \times 10^{-3}}-12\right] \Omega {/tex}
    {tex} =[6000-12] \Omega=5988 \Omega {/tex}

question_html: In the circuit given below, find the voltmeter reading across a 4 {tex}\Omega{/tex} resistor.
question_text: In the circuit given below, find the voltmeter reading across a 4 {tex}\Omega{/tex} resistor.
explanation: Equivalent resistance of the loop R 1 = 2 + 4 + 2 = 8 {tex}\Omega{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{R_2}=\frac{1}{8}+\frac{1}{8} \Rightarrow R_2=4 \Omega{/tex} R 2 in series with 2 {tex}\Omega{/tex} and 2 {tex}\Omega{/tex} R equivalent = 2 + 4 + 2 = 8 {tex}\Omega{/tex} Current from battery {tex}=\frac{V}{R_1}=\frac{16}{8}=2 \mathrm{~A}{/tex} Let I 1 = Current flowing through 4 {tex}\Omega{/tex} Let I 2 = Current flowing through 8 {tex}\Omega{/tex} I 1 + I 2 = 2A, 8 {tex}\times{/tex} I 2 = 8 {tex}\times{/tex} I 1 I 1 = I 2 = 1A Potential across 4 {tex}\Omega{/tex} = I 1 R = 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 4 = 4V
solution: Equivalent resistance of the loop R 1 = 2 + 4 + 2 = 8 {tex}\Omega{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{R_2}=\frac{1}{8}+\frac{1}{8} \Rightarrow R_2=4 \Omega{/tex} R 2 in series with 2 {tex}\Omega{/tex} and 2 {tex}\Omega{/tex} R equivalent = 2 + 4 + 2 = 8 {tex}\Omega{/tex} Current from battery {tex}=\frac{V}{R_1}=\frac{16}{8}=2 \mathrm{~A}{/tex} Let I 1 = Current flowing through 4 {tex}\Omega{/tex} Let I 2 = Current flowing through 8 {tex}\Omega{/tex} I 1 + I 2 = 2A, 8 {tex}\times{/tex} I 2 = 8 {tex}\times{/tex} I 1 I 1 = I 2 = 1A Potential across 4 {tex}\Omega{/tex} = I 1 R = 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 4 = 4V
solution_html:
Equivalent resistance of the loop
R1 = 2 + 4 + 2 = 8{tex}\Omega{/tex}
{tex}\frac{1}{R_2}=\frac{1}{8}+\frac{1}{8} \Rightarrow R_2=4 \Omega{/tex}
R2 in series with 2{tex}\Omega{/tex} and 2{tex}\Omega{/tex}
Requivalent = 2 + 4 + 2 = 8{tex}\Omega{/tex}
Current from battery {tex}=\frac{V}{R_1}=\frac{16}{8}=2 \mathrm{~A}{/tex}
Let I1 = Current flowing through 4{tex}\Omega{/tex}
Let I2 = Current flowing through 8{tex}\Omega{/tex}
I1 + I2 = 2A, 8 {tex}\times{/tex} I2 = 8 {tex}\times{/tex} I1
I1 = I2 = 1A
Potential across 4{tex}\Omega{/tex} = I1 R = 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 4 = 4V

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. A particle of charge 'q' and mass 'm', moving with velocity ' {tex}\vec{v}{/tex} ' is subjected to a uniform magnetic field {tex}\vec{B}{/tex} perpendicular to its velocity. Show that the particle describes a circular path. Obtain expression for the radius of the circular path of the particle.
  2. Explain, how its path will be affected if the velocity {tex}\vec{v}{/tex} makes an angle {tex}\theta\left(\neq 90^{\circ}\right){/tex} with the direction of the magnetic field.
question_text: A particle of charge 'q' and mass 'm', moving with velocity ' {tex}\vec{v}{/tex} ' is subjected to a uniform magnetic field {tex}\vec{B}{/tex} perpendicular to its velocity. Show that the particle describes a circular path. Obtain expression for the radius of the circular path of the particle. Explain, how its path will be affected if the velocity {tex}\vec{v}{/tex} makes an angle {tex}\theta\left(\neq 90^{\circ}\right){/tex} with the direction of the magnetic field.
explanation: {tex}\vec{F}=q(\vec{v} \times \vec{B}){/tex} Force {tex}\vec{F}{/tex} on a moving charged particle in a magnetic field acts perpendicular to the velocity vector at all instants. It therefore, changes only the direction of velocity without changing its magnitude. This results in a circular motion of the particle for which the force {tex}\vec{F}{/tex} provides the needed centripetal force. {tex}\left(=\frac{m v^2}{r}\right){/tex} Here {tex}\mathrm{F}=\mathrm{qvB} \sin \Theta{/tex} {tex}=\mathrm{qvB} \quad(\text { as } \Theta=\frac{\pi}{2}){/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{m v^2}{r}=q v B{/tex} {tex}\therefore r=\frac{m v}{q B} {/tex} If {tex}\Theta \neq 90^{\circ}{/tex} , then velocity will have a component along {tex}\vec{B}{/tex} also and the charged particle will move along {tex}\vec{B}{/tex} with this component of velocity while describing circular motion in a plane perpendicular to {tex}\vec{B}{/tex} . Its motion is, therefore, helical.
solution: {tex}\vec{F}=q(\vec{v} \times \vec{B}){/tex} Force {tex}\vec{F}{/tex} on a moving charged particle in a magnetic field acts perpendicular to the velocity vector at all instants. It therefore, changes only the direction of velocity without changing its magnitude. This results in a circular motion of the particle for which the force {tex}\vec{F}{/tex} provides the needed centripetal force. {tex}\left(=\frac{m v^2}{r}\right){/tex} Here {tex}\mathrm{F}=\mathrm{qvB} \sin \Theta{/tex} {tex}=\mathrm{qvB} \quad(\text { as } \Theta=\frac{\pi}{2}){/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{m v^2}{r}=q v B{/tex} {tex}\therefore r=\frac{m v}{q B} {/tex} If {tex}\Theta \neq 90^{\circ}{/tex} , then velocity will have a component along {tex}\vec{B}{/tex} also and the charged particle will move along {tex}\vec{B}{/tex} with this component of velocity while describing circular motion in a plane perpendicular to {tex}\vec{B}{/tex} . Its motion is, therefore, helical.
solution_html:
1. {tex}\vec{F}=q(\vec{v} \times \vec{B}){/tex}
    Force {tex}\vec{F}{/tex} on a moving charged particle in a magnetic field acts perpendicular to the velocity vector at all instants. It therefore, changes only the direction of velocity without changing its magnitude. This results in a circular motion of the particle for which the force {tex}\vec{F}{/tex} provides the needed centripetal force. {tex}\left(=\frac{m v^2}{r}\right){/tex}
    Here {tex}\mathrm{F}=\mathrm{qvB} \sin \Theta{/tex}
    {tex}=\mathrm{qvB} \quad(\text { as } \Theta=\frac{\pi}{2}){/tex}
    {tex}\therefore \frac{m v^2}{r}=q v B{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore r=\frac{m v}{q B} {/tex}
  2. If {tex}\Theta \neq 90^{\circ}{/tex}, then velocity will have a component along {tex}\vec{B}{/tex} also and the charged particle will move along {tex}\vec{B}{/tex} with this component of velocity while describing circular motion in a plane perpendicular to {tex}\vec{B}{/tex}. Its motion is, therefore, helical.

subject: Physics
question_html: A multirange voltmeter can be constructed by using a galvanometer circuit as shown in Fig. We want to construct a voltmeter that can measure 2V, 20V and 200V using a galvanometer of resistance 10Ω and that produces maximum deflection for current of 1 mA. Find R1, R2 and R3 that have to be used.
question_text: A multirange voltmeter can be constructed by using a galvanometer circuit as shown in Fig. We want to construct a voltmeter that can measure 2V, 20V and 200V using a galvanometer of resistance 10Ω and that produces maximum deflection for current of 1 mA. Find R 1 , R 2 and R 3 that have to be used.
explanation: Key concept: The galvanometer can also be used as a voltmeter to measure the voltage across a given section of the circuit. For this a very high resistance wire is to be connected in series with galvanometer. The relationship is given by l G (G + R) =V where lg is the range of galvanometer, G is the resistance of galvanometer and R is the resistance of wire connected in series with galvanometer. Applying expression in different situations For i G (R 1 + G) = 2 for 2 V range For i G (G + R 1 + R 2 ) = 20 for 20 V range And For i G (G + R 1 + R 2 + R 3 ) = 200 for 200 v range By solving, we get R 1 = 1990 {tex}\Omega{/tex} , R 2 = 18 k {tex}\Omega{/tex} and R 3 = 180 K {tex}\Omega{/tex} . these are the required values.
solution: Key concept: The galvanometer can also be used as a voltmeter to measure the voltage across a given section of the circuit. For this a very high resistance wire is to be connected in series with galvanometer. The relationship is given by l G (G + R) =V where lg is the range of galvanometer, G is the resistance of galvanometer and R is the resistance of wire connected in series with galvanometer. Applying expression in different situations For i G (R 1 + G) = 2 for 2 V range For i G (G + R 1 + R 2 ) = 20 for 20 V range And For i G (G + R 1 + R 2 + R 3 ) = 200 for 200 v range By solving, we get R 1 = 1990 {tex}\Omega{/tex} , R 2 = 18 k {tex}\Omega{/tex} and R 3 = 180 K {tex}\Omega{/tex} . these are the required values.
solution_html:
Key concept: The galvanometer can also be used as a voltmeter to measure the voltage across a given section of the circuit. For this a very high resistance wire is to be connected in series with galvanometer. The relationship is given by lG (G + R) =V where lg is the range of galvanometer, G is the resistance of galvanometer and R is the resistance of wire connected in series with galvanometer.

Applying expression in different situations
For iG (R1 + G) = 2 for 2 V range 
For iG (G + R1 + R2) = 20 for 20 V range 
And For  iG (G + R1 + R2 + R3) = 200 for 200 v range
By solving, we get
R1 = 1990 {tex}\Omega{/tex}, R2 = 18 k{tex}\Omega{/tex} and R3 = 180 K{tex}\Omega{/tex}. these are the required values.

question_html: A proton and an electron travelling along parallel paths enter a region of the uniform magnetic field, acting perpendicular to their paths. Which of them will move in a circular path with a higher frequency?
question_text: A proton and an electron travelling along parallel paths enter a region of the uniform magnetic field, acting perpendicular to their paths. Which of them will move in a circular path with a higher frequency?
explanation: Electron moves in a circular path with higher frequency.m e =mass of electron , m p =mass of proton. {tex}\frac{m v^{2}}{r}=q v B, r=\frac{m v}{q B}{/tex} {tex}\omega=\frac{v}{r}=\frac{q B}{m}{/tex} {tex}\omega=2 \pi f \Rightarrow \frac{q B}{m}=2 \pi f \Rightarrow f \propto \frac{1}{m}{/tex} Since m e < m p , therefore f e > f p Thus, the electron moves in circular path with higher frequency.
solution: Electron moves in a circular path with higher frequency.m e =mass of electron , m p =mass of proton. {tex}\frac{m v^{2}}{r}=q v B, r=\frac{m v}{q B}{/tex} {tex}\omega=\frac{v}{r}=\frac{q B}{m}{/tex} {tex}\omega=2 \pi f \Rightarrow \frac{q B}{m}=2 \pi f \Rightarrow f \propto \frac{1}{m}{/tex} Since m e < m p , therefore f e > f p Thus, the electron moves in circular path with higher frequency.
solution_html:
Electron moves in a circular path with higher frequency.me=mass of electron , mp=mass of proton.
{tex}\frac{m v^{2}}{r}=q v B, r=\frac{m v}{q B}{/tex}
{tex}\omega=\frac{v}{r}=\frac{q B}{m}{/tex}
{tex}\omega=2 \pi f \Rightarrow \frac{q B}{m}=2 \pi f \Rightarrow f \propto \frac{1}{m}{/tex}
Since me < mp, therefore fe > fp
Thus, the electron moves in circular path with higher frequency.

subject: Physics
question_html:
A bar magnet of the magnetic moment 6 J/T is aligned at 60° with a uniform external magnetic field of 0.44 T. Calculate

  1. the work is done in turning the magnet to align its magnetic moment
    1. normal to the magnetic field,
    2. opposite to the magnetic field, and
  2. the torque on the magnet in the final orientation in case (ii).
question_text: A bar magnet of the magnetic moment 6 J/T is aligned at 60° with a uniform external magnetic field of 0.44 T. Calculate the work is done in turning the magnet to align its magnetic moment normal to the magnetic field, opposite to the magnetic field, and the torque on the magnet in the final orientation in case (ii).
explanation: Formula and Calculation of work done in the two cases Work done = m B (cos {tex}\theta_1{/tex} - cos {tex}\theta_2{/tex} ) {tex}\theta_1{/tex} = 60°, {tex}\theta_2{/tex} = 90° and magnetic field B = 0.44 T {tex}\therefore{/tex} Work done = m B (cos 60° - cos 90°) = mB {tex}\left(\frac{1}{2}-0\right)=\frac{1}{2} m B{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \times 6 \times 0 \cdot 44 \mathrm{J}=1 \cdot 32 \mathrm{J}{/tex} {tex}\theta_1{/tex} = 60°, {tex}\theta_2{/tex} = 180° {tex}\therefore{/tex} Work done = m B (cos 60° - cos 180°) = mB {tex}\left(\frac{1}{2}-(-1)\right)=\frac{3}{2} m B{/tex} = {tex}\frac{3}{2} \times 6 \times 0.44 \mathrm{J}=3.96 \mathrm{J}{/tex} Calculation of torque in case Torque = {tex}|\vec{m} \times \vec{B}|{/tex} =mB sin {tex}\theta{/tex} For {tex}\theta{/tex} = 180°, we have Torque = 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.44 sin 180° = 0
solution: Formula and Calculation of work done in the two cases Work done = m B (cos {tex}\theta_1{/tex} - cos {tex}\theta_2{/tex} ) {tex}\theta_1{/tex} = 60°, {tex}\theta_2{/tex} = 90° and magnetic field B = 0.44 T {tex}\therefore{/tex} Work done = m B (cos 60° - cos 90°) = mB {tex}\left(\frac{1}{2}-0\right)=\frac{1}{2} m B{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \times 6 \times 0 \cdot 44 \mathrm{J}=1 \cdot 32 \mathrm{J}{/tex} {tex}\theta_1{/tex} = 60°, {tex}\theta_2{/tex} = 180° {tex}\therefore{/tex} Work done = m B (cos 60° - cos 180°) = mB {tex}\left(\frac{1}{2}-(-1)\right)=\frac{3}{2} m B{/tex} = {tex}\frac{3}{2} \times 6 \times 0.44 \mathrm{J}=3.96 \mathrm{J}{/tex} Calculation of torque in case Torque = {tex}|\vec{m} \times \vec{B}|{/tex} =mB sin {tex}\theta{/tex} For {tex}\theta{/tex} = 180°, we have Torque = 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.44 sin 180° = 0
solution_html:
1. Formula and
    Calculation of work done in the two cases
    1. Work done = mB(cos {tex}\theta_1{/tex} - cos {tex}\theta_2{/tex})
      {tex}\theta_1{/tex} = 60°,  {tex}\theta_2{/tex} = 90° and magnetic field B = 0.44 T
      {tex}\therefore{/tex} Work done = mB(cos 60° - cos 90°)
      = mB{tex}\left(\frac{1}{2}-0\right)=\frac{1}{2} m B{/tex}
      = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \times 6 \times 0 \cdot 44 \mathrm{J}=1 \cdot 32 \mathrm{J}{/tex}
    2. {tex}\theta_1{/tex} = 60°, {tex}\theta_2{/tex} = 180°
      {tex}\therefore{/tex} Work done = mB(cos 60° - cos 180°)
      = mB{tex}\left(\frac{1}{2}-(-1)\right)=\frac{3}{2} m B{/tex}
      = {tex}\frac{3}{2} \times 6 \times 0.44 \mathrm{J}=3.96 \mathrm{J}{/tex} 
  2. Calculation of torque in case
    Torque = {tex}|\vec{m} \times \vec{B}|{/tex} =mB sin {tex}\theta{/tex}
    For {tex}\theta{/tex} = 180°, we have
    Torque = 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.44 sin 180° = 0

subject: Physics
question_html:
A particle of mass m and charge q is in motion at speed v parallel to a long straight conductor carrying current I as shown below:

Find the magnitude and direction of the electric field required so that the particle goes undeflected.
question_text: A particle of mass m and charge q is in motion at speed v parallel to a long straight conductor carrying current I as shown below: Find the magnitude and direction of the electric field required so that the particle goes undeflected.
explanation: For the charged particle to move undeflected a particle of mass m and charge q moves with a velocity v. Net force, {tex}\vec{F}=\vec{F}_{E}+\vec{F}_{m}=\overrightarrow{0}{/tex} {tex}\vec{F}_{E}=-\vec{F}_{m}{/tex} ...(i) {tex}\vec{F_{E}} \rightarrow{/tex} electric force, {tex}\vec{F}_{m} \rightarrow{/tex} magnetic force {tex}|\overrightarrow{F_{E}}|=|\overrightarrow{F_{m}}|{/tex} ....(ii) qE = Bqv sin 90° = Bqv E =vB ...(iii) B = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I}{2 \pi r}{/tex} ...(iv) Using (iv) and (iii) E = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0}}{2 \pi} \frac{v I}{r}{/tex} Magnetic force F m is towards the wire. {tex}\therefore{/tex} Electric force and electric field should be away from the line.
solution: For the charged particle to move undeflected a particle of mass m and charge q moves with a velocity v. Net force, {tex}\vec{F}=\vec{F}_{E}+\vec{F}_{m}=\overrightarrow{0}{/tex} {tex}\vec{F}_{E}=-\vec{F}_{m}{/tex} ...(i) {tex}\vec{F_{E}} \rightarrow{/tex} electric force, {tex}\vec{F}_{m} \rightarrow{/tex} magnetic force {tex}|\overrightarrow{F_{E}}|=|\overrightarrow{F_{m}}|{/tex} ....(ii) qE = Bqv sin 90° = Bqv E =vB ...(iii) B = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I}{2 \pi r}{/tex} ...(iv) Using (iv) and (iii) E = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0}}{2 \pi} \frac{v I}{r}{/tex} Magnetic force F m is towards the wire. {tex}\therefore{/tex} Electric force and electric field should be away from the line.
solution_html:
For the charged particle to move undeflected a particle of mass m and charge q moves with a velocity v.
Net force, {tex}\vec{F}=\vec{F}_{E}+\vec{F}_{m}=\overrightarrow{0}{/tex}
{tex}\vec{F}_{E}=-\vec{F}_{m}{/tex} ...(i)
{tex}\vec{F_{E}} \rightarrow{/tex} electric force, {tex}\vec{F}_{m} \rightarrow{/tex} magnetic force
{tex}|\overrightarrow{F_{E}}|=|\overrightarrow{F_{m}}|{/tex} ....(ii)
qE = Bqv sin 90° = Bqv
E =vB ...(iii)
B = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I}{2 \pi r}{/tex} ...(iv)
Using (iv) and (iii)
E = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0}}{2 \pi} \frac{v I}{r}{/tex}
Magnetic force Fm is towards the wire.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Electric force and electric field should be away from the line.

question_html: Two protons of equal kinetic energies enter a region of the uniform magnetic field. The first proton enters normal to the field direction while the second enters at 30° to the field direction. Name the trajectories followed by them.
question_text: Two protons of equal kinetic energies enter a region of the uniform magnetic field. The first proton enters normal to the field direction while the second enters at 30° to the field direction. Name the trajectories followed by them.
explanation: Normal: Circular force = qvBsin {tex}\phi {/tex} (when the particle enters perpendicular, {tex}\phi {/tex} = 90 o In this case, since sin {tex}\phi {/tex} = 1 is the maximum value and the direction of the force is perpendicular to the direction of motion of the particle, hence the particle starts moving in a CIRCULAR path with radius,) At an angle of 30°: It will follow the helical path. (In this case the force acting on it has two components, one parallel to the motion which will cause a linear motion, the other component will act perpendicular to motion which will cause circular motion. Hence, due to the resultant of these two-component the particle will move along a HELICAL path.)
solution: Normal: Circular force = qvBsin {tex}\phi {/tex} (when the particle enters perpendicular, {tex}\phi {/tex} = 90 o In this case, since sin {tex}\phi {/tex} = 1 is the maximum value and the direction of the force is perpendicular to the direction of motion of the particle, hence the particle starts moving in a CIRCULAR path with radius,) At an angle of 30°: It will follow the helical path. (In this case the force acting on it has two components, one parallel to the motion which will cause a linear motion, the other component will act perpendicular to motion which will cause circular motion. Hence, due to the resultant of these two-component the particle will move along a HELICAL path.)
solution_html:
1. Normal: Circular 
    force = qvBsin{tex}\phi {/tex} 
    (when the particle enters perpendicular, {tex}\phi {/tex} = 90o
    In this case, since sin{tex}\phi {/tex} = 1 is the maximum value and the direction of the force is perpendicular to the direction of motion of the particle, hence the particle starts moving in a CIRCULAR path with radius,)
  2. At an angle of 30°: It will follow the helical path.
    (In this case the force acting on it has two components, one parallel to the motion which will cause a linear motion, the other component will act perpendicular to motion which will cause circular motion.
    Hence, due to the resultant of these two-component the particle will move along a HELICAL path.)

question_text: Current sensitivity of a moving coil galvanometer is 5 div/mA and its voltage sensitivity (angular deflection per unit voltage applied) is 20 div/V. The resistance of the galvanometer is:

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. Define SI unit of current in terms of the force between two parallel current-carrying conductors.
  2. Two long straight parallel conductors carrying steady currents Ia and Ib along the same direction are separated by a distance d. How does one explain the force of attraction between them? If a third conductor carrying a current Ic in the opposite direction is placed just in the middle of these conductors, find the resultant force acting on the third conductor.
question_text: Define SI unit of current in terms of the force between two parallel current-carrying conductors. Two long straight parallel conductors carrying steady currents I a and I b along the same direction are separated by a distance d. How does one explain the force of attraction between them? If a third conductor carrying a current I c in the opposite direction is placed just in the middle of these conductors, find the resultant force acting on the third conductor.
explanation: The S.I unit of current is ampere. The magnitude of force per unit length of two parallel current-carrying conductors. {tex}F=\frac{\mu_0 I_a I_b}{2 \pi d}{/tex} Ampere is that value of steady-state current, which when maintained in each of the very two long, straight, parallel conductors of negligible cross-section and placed 1m apart in a vacuum produces 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -7 N/m force on each conductor. One ampere is the value of that steady current which, when maintained in each of the two very long, straight, parallel conductors of negligible cross-section, and placed one metre apart in vacuum, would produce on each of these conductors a force equal to 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -7 newton per metre of length. The wire (b) experiences a force due to the magnetic field caused by the current flowing in wire (a). The magnetic field at any point on the wire (b) due to the current in wire (a) is perpendicular to the plane of two wires and pointing inwards and hence force on it will be towards wire (a). Similarly force on wire (a) will be towards wire (b). Hence two wires carrying currents in same direction attract each other. XA Force on wire (3) due to wire (1) = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I_{a} I_{c}}{2 \pi\left(\frac{d}{2}\right)}{/tex} towards right Force on wire 3 due to wire 2 {tex}\left(\frac{\mu_{0} I_{b} I_{c}}{2 \pi\left(\frac{d}{2}\right)}\right){/tex} towards left Net force on wire 3 = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I_{c}}{\pi d}{/tex} [I a - I b ] towards right Also accept = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I_{c}}{\pi d}{/tex} [I b - I a ] towards left Note: please do not deduct last {tex}\frac12{/tex} mark if the student does not write the direction of force.
solution: The S.I unit of current is ampere. The magnitude of force per unit length of two parallel current-carrying conductors. {tex}F=\frac{\mu_0 I_a I_b}{2 \pi d}{/tex} Ampere is that value of steady-state current, which when maintained in each of the very two long, straight, parallel conductors of negligible cross-section and placed 1m apart in a vacuum produces 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -7 N/m force on each conductor. One ampere is the value of that steady current which, when maintained in each of the two very long, straight, parallel conductors of negligible cross-section, and placed one metre apart in vacuum, would produce on each of these conductors a force equal to 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -7 newton per metre of length. The wire (b) experiences a force due to the magnetic field caused by the current flowing in wire (a). The magnetic field at any point on the wire (b) due to the current in wire (a) is perpendicular to the plane of two wires and pointing inwards and hence force on it will be towards wire (a). Similarly force on wire (a) will be towards wire (b). Hence two wires carrying currents in same direction attract each other. XA Force on wire (3) due to wire (1) = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I_{a} I_{c}}{2 \pi\left(\frac{d}{2}\right)}{/tex} towards right Force on wire 3 due to wire 2 {tex}\left(\frac{\mu_{0} I_{b} I_{c}}{2 \pi\left(\frac{d}{2}\right)}\right){/tex} towards left Net force on wire 3 = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I_{c}}{\pi d}{/tex} [I a - I b ] towards right Also accept = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I_{c}}{\pi d}{/tex} [I b - I a ] towards left Note: please do not deduct last {tex}\frac12{/tex} mark if the student does not write the direction of force.
solution_html:
1. The S.I unit of current is ampere.
    The magnitude of force per unit length of two parallel current-carrying conductors.
    {tex}F=\frac{\mu_0 I_a I_b}{2 \pi d}{/tex}
    Ampere is that value of steady-state current, which when maintained in each of the very two long, straight, parallel conductors of negligible cross-section and placed 1m apart in a vacuum produces 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-7 N/m force on each conductor.
    1. One ampere is the value of that steady current which, when maintained in each of the two very long, straight, parallel conductors of negligible cross-section, and placed one metre apart in vacuum, would produce on each of these conductors a force equal to 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-7 newton per metre of length.
    2. The wire (b) experiences a force due to the magnetic field caused by the current flowing in wire (a).

      The magnetic field at any point on the wire (b) due to the current in wire (a) is perpendicular to the plane of two wires and pointing inwards and hence force on it will be towards wire (a). Similarly force on wire (a) will be towards wire (b). Hence two wires carrying currents in same direction attract each other. XA
      Force on wire (3) due to wire (1)
      = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I_{a} I_{c}}{2 \pi\left(\frac{d}{2}\right)}{/tex} towards right Force on wire 3 due to wire 2 {tex}\left(\frac{\mu_{0} I_{b} I_{c}}{2 \pi\left(\frac{d}{2}\right)}\right){/tex} towards left

      Net force on wire 3
      = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I_{c}}{\pi d}{/tex}[Ia - Ib] towards right
      Also accept
      = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I_{c}}{\pi d}{/tex}[Ib - Ia] towards left
      Note: please do not deduct last {tex}\frac12{/tex} mark if the student does not write the direction of force.

question_html: Derive an expression for the torque on a rectangular coil of area A, carrying a current I and placed in a magnetic field B. The angle between the direction of B and vector perpendicular to the plane of the coil is 0. Indicate the direction of the torque acting on the loop.
question_text: Derive an expression for the torque on a rectangular coil of area A, carrying a current I and placed in a magnetic field B. The angle between the direction of B and vector perpendicular to the plane of the coil is 0. Indicate the direction of the torque acting on the loop.
explanation: A rectangular loop ABCD of dimensions l and b, carrying a steady current is placed in uniform magnetic field as shown in fig; such that normal of the plane is at angle {tex}\theta{/tex} with the magnetic field lines. The force F BC and F AD on arms BC and AD are equal, opposite and along the axis of the coil, so they cancel each other. The forces F AB and F CD are also equal and opposite, but are not collinear, so they constitute a couple, and the magnitude of the torque can be given as {tex}\tau{/tex} = F AB {tex}\cdot{/tex} {tex}\frac{b}{2}{/tex} sin {tex}\theta{/tex} + F CD {tex}\cdot{/tex} {tex}\frac{b}{2}{/tex} sin {tex}\theta{/tex} Since, {tex}\left|\mathrm{F}_{\mathrm{AB}}\right|{/tex} = |F CD | = BI {tex}\ell{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} = BI {tex}\ell{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} b sin {tex}\theta{/tex} = BI ( {tex}\ell{/tex} b) sin {tex}\theta{/tex} = BI Asin {tex}\theta{/tex} [A = {tex}\ell{/tex} b = area of the rectangle] Since magnetic moment m = I|A| {tex}\tau{/tex} = mB sin {tex}\theta{/tex} In vector from {tex}\vec{\tau}{/tex} = {tex}\vec{m} \times \vec{B}{/tex}
solution: A rectangular loop ABCD of dimensions l and b, carrying a steady current is placed in uniform magnetic field as shown in fig; such that normal of the plane is at angle {tex}\theta{/tex} with the magnetic field lines. The force F BC and F AD on arms BC and AD are equal, opposite and along the axis of the coil, so they cancel each other. The forces F AB and F CD are also equal and opposite, but are not collinear, so they constitute a couple, and the magnitude of the torque can be given as {tex}\tau{/tex} = F AB {tex}\cdot{/tex} {tex}\frac{b}{2}{/tex} sin {tex}\theta{/tex} + F CD {tex}\cdot{/tex} {tex}\frac{b}{2}{/tex} sin {tex}\theta{/tex} Since, {tex}\left|\mathrm{F}_{\mathrm{AB}}\right|{/tex} = |F CD | = BI {tex}\ell{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} = BI {tex}\ell{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} b sin {tex}\theta{/tex} = BI ( {tex}\ell{/tex} b) sin {tex}\theta{/tex} = BI Asin {tex}\theta{/tex} [A = {tex}\ell{/tex} b = area of the rectangle] Since magnetic moment m = I|A| {tex}\tau{/tex} = mB sin {tex}\theta{/tex} In vector from {tex}\vec{\tau}{/tex} = {tex}\vec{m} \times \vec{B}{/tex}
solution_html:
A rectangular loop ABCD of dimensions l and b, carrying a steady current is placed in uniform magnetic field as shown in fig; such that normal of the plane is at angle {tex}\theta{/tex} with the magnetic field lines.
The force FBC and FAD on arms BC and AD are equal, opposite and along the axis of the coil, so they cancel each other.
The forces FAB and FCD are also equal and opposite, but are not collinear, so they constitute a couple, and the magnitude of the torque can be given as
{tex}\tau{/tex} = FAB {tex}\cdot{/tex} {tex}\frac{b}{2}{/tex} sin{tex}\theta{/tex} + FCD {tex}\cdot{/tex} {tex}\frac{b}{2}{/tex} sin{tex}\theta{/tex}
Since,
{tex}\left|\mathrm{F}_{\mathrm{AB}}\right|{/tex} = |FCD| = BI{tex}\ell{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} = BI{tex}\ell{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} b sin{tex}\theta{/tex}
= BI ({tex}\ell{/tex}b) sin{tex}\theta{/tex}
= BI Asin{tex}\theta{/tex} [A = {tex}\ell{/tex}b = area of the rectangle]
Since magnetic moment m = I|A|
{tex}\tau{/tex} = mB sin {tex}\theta{/tex}
In vector from {tex}\vec{\tau}{/tex} = {tex}\vec{m} \times \vec{B}{/tex}

question_html: Define the current sensitivity of a moving coil galvanometer and state its SI unit.
question_text: Define the current sensitivity of a moving coil galvanometer and state its SI unit.
explanation: The current sensitivity of a galvanometer is defined as the deflection produced in the galvanometer on passing unit current through it. Current sensitivity {tex}=\frac{\alpha}{I}=\frac{N B A}{k}{/tex} The SI unit of current sensitivity is radian ampere -1 .
solution: The current sensitivity of a galvanometer is defined as the deflection produced in the galvanometer on passing unit current through it. Current sensitivity {tex}=\frac{\alpha}{I}=\frac{N B A}{k}{/tex} The SI unit of current sensitivity is radian ampere -1 .
solution_html:
The current sensitivity of a galvanometer is defined as the deflection produced in the galvanometer on passing unit current through it.
Current sensitivity {tex}=\frac{\alpha}{I}=\frac{N B A}{k}{/tex}
The SI unit of current sensitivity is radian ampere-1.

  2. A neutron, an electron and an alpha particle moving with equal velocities, enter a uniform magnetic field going into the plane of the paper as shown in the figure. Trace their paths in the field and justify your answer.
question_text: Write the expression for the magnetic force acting on a charged particle moving with velocity v in the presence of magnetic field B. A neutron, an electron and an alpha particle moving with equal velocities, enter a uniform magnetic field going into the plane of the paper as shown in the figure. Trace their paths in the field and justify your answer.
explanation: When a charged particle,q moves with velocity,v in a uniform magnetic field, B, then magnetic lorentz force,F acting on it is given by, {tex}\ { F } = q ( { v } \times { B } ){/tex} The direction of force on the charged particle is given by {tex}(v \times B ){/tex} . Here, direction of v is + {tex}\hat i{/tex} and direction of B is {tex}- \hat { k }{/tex} . For {tex}\alpha {/tex} -particle, charge is positive So, direction of force is {tex}+(+\widehat i\times-\widehat k)\;i.e.+\widehat j{/tex} Thus for {tex}\alpha {/tex} -particle, it describes a circle with anti-clockwise motion. For neutron: It is a neutral particle, so it goes undeviated as {tex}\ { F } = q ( { v } \times { B } ) = 0{/tex} For electron: Magnetic Force is given by {tex}\ { F } = - e ( \ { v } \times \ { B } ){/tex} So, direction is {tex}-(+\widehat i\times-\widehat k)\Rightarrow-\widehat{j}{/tex} Thus, e - describes a circle with clockwise motion. The path of the particles in the presence of magnetic field is shown below:

For neutron: It is a neutral particle, so it goes undeviated as {tex}\ { F } = q ( { v } \times { B } ) = 0{/tex} For electron: Magnetic Force is given by {tex}\ { F } = - e ( \ { v } \times \ { B } ){/tex} So, direction is {tex}-(+\widehat i\times-\widehat k)\Rightarrow-\widehat{j}{/tex} Thus, e - describes a circle with clockwise motion. 1. When a charged particle,q moves with velocity,v in a uniform magnetic field, B, then magnetic lorentz force,F acting on it is given by,{tex}\ { F } = q ( { v } \times { B } ){/tex}
     
  2. The direction of force on the charged particle is given by {tex}(v \times B ){/tex}. Here, direction of v is +{tex}\hat i{/tex} and direction of B is {tex}- \hat { k }{/tex}.
