question_html: Two identical loops P and Q each of radius 5 cm are lying in perpendicular planes such that they have a common centre as shown in the figure. Find the magnitude and direction of the net magnetic field at the common centre of the two coils, if they carry currents equal to 3A and 4A, respectively.
question_text: Two identical loops P and Q each of radius 5 cm are lying in perpendicular planes such that they have a common centre as shown in the figure. Find the magnitude and direction of the net magnetic field at the common centre of the two coils, if they carry currents equal to 3A and 4A, respectively.
explanation: Magnetic field due to circular loop P is directed vertically upwards and is given by, {tex}B _ { P } = \frac { \mu _ { 0 } I _ { P } } { 2 r }{/tex} Magnetic field due to circular loop Q is directed horizontally and is given by, {tex}B_Q=\frac{\mu_0I_Q}{2r}{/tex} So, net magnetic field at the centre of the loop is given by, {tex}B _ { \mathrm { net } } = \sqrt { B _ { P } ^ { 2 } + B _ { Q } ^ { 2 } } = \sqrt { \left( \mu _ { 0 } I _ { P } / 2 r \right) ^ { 2 } + \left( \mu _ { 0 } I _ { Q } / 2 r \right) ^ { 2 } }{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\mu_0}{2r}\sqrt{I_P^2+I_Q^2}=\frac{4\pi\times10^{-7}}{2\times5\times10^{-2}}\times5=2\pi\times10^{-5}\;\mathrm T{/tex} , The direction of current I is not given, hence, direction of B→B→ cannot be predicted. The net magnetic field makes an angle {tex}\theta {/tex} with B Q which is given by, {tex}\tan \theta = \frac { B _ { P } } { B _ { Q } } = \frac { I _ { P } } { I _ { Q } } = \frac { 3 } { 4 }{/tex} {tex}\theta=36.87^o{/tex}
solution: Magnetic field due to circular loop P is directed vertically upwards and is given by, {tex}B _ { P } = \frac { \mu _ { 0 } I _ { P } } { 2 r }{/tex} Magnetic field due to circular loop Q is directed horizontally and is given by, {tex}B_Q=\frac{\mu_0I_Q}{2r}{/tex} So, net magnetic field at the centre of the loop is given by, {tex}B _ { \mathrm { net } } = \sqrt { B _ { P } ^ { 2 } + B _ { Q } ^ { 2 } } = \sqrt { \left( \mu _ { 0 } I _ { P } / 2 r \right) ^ { 2 } + \left( \mu _ { 0 } I _ { Q } / 2 r \right) ^ { 2 } }{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\mu_0}{2r}\sqrt{I_P^2+I_Q^2}=\frac{4\pi\times10^{-7}}{2\times5\times10^{-2}}\times5=2\pi\times10^{-5}\;\mathrm T{/tex} , The direction of current I is not given, hence, direction of B→B→ cannot be predicted. The net magnetic field makes an angle {tex}\theta {/tex} with B Q which is given by, {tex}\tan \theta = \frac { B _ { P } } { B _ { Q } } = \frac { I _ { P } } { I _ { Q } } = \frac { 3 } { 4 }{/tex} {tex}\theta=36.87^o{/tex}
solution_html:
Magnetic field due to circular loop P is directed vertically upwards and is given by,

{tex}B _ { P } = \frac { \mu _ { 0 } I _ { P } } { 2 r }{/tex}

Magnetic field due to circular loop Q is directed horizontally and is given by,

{tex}B_Q=\frac{\mu_0I_Q}{2r}{/tex}

So, net magnetic field at the centre of the loop is given by,

{tex}B _ { \mathrm { net } } = \sqrt { B _ { P } ^ { 2 } + B _ { Q } ^ { 2 } } = \sqrt { \left( \mu _ { 0 } I _ { P } / 2 r \right) ^ { 2 } + \left( \mu _ { 0 } I _ { Q } / 2 r \right) ^ { 2 } }{/tex}

{tex}=\frac{\mu_0}{2r}\sqrt{I_P^2+I_Q^2}=\frac{4\pi\times10^{-7}}{2\times5\times10^{-2}}\times5=2\pi\times10^{-5}\;\mathrm T{/tex}, The direction of current I is not given, hence, direction of B→B→ cannot be predicted.
The net magnetic field makes an angle {tex}\theta {/tex} with BQ which is given by,

{tex}\tan \theta = \frac { B _ { P } } { B _ { Q } } = \frac { I _ { P } } { I _ { Q } } = \frac { 3 } { 4 }{/tex}

{tex}\theta=36.87^o{/tex}

question_html: An electron of mass me revolves around a nucleus of charge +Ze. Show that it behaves like a tiny magnetic dipole. Hence, prove that the magnetic moment associated with it is expressed as {tex}\vec \mu = - \frac{e}{{2{m_e}}}\vec L{/tex}, where {tex}\vec L{/tex} is the orbital angular momentum of the electron. Give the significance of negative sign.
question_text: An electron of mass m e revolves around a nucleus of charge +Ze. Show that it behaves like a tiny magnetic dipole. Hence, prove that the magnetic moment associated with it is expressed as {tex}\vec \mu = - \frac{e}{{2{m_e}}}\vec L{/tex} , where {tex}\vec L{/tex} is the orbital angular momentum of the electron. Give the significance of negative sign.
explanation: When electron revolves around a positively charged nucleus, the circular loop constitutes a current, which have a definite dipole moment. So, it behaves as a tiny magnetic dipole. The equivalent current is given by , {tex}I\;=\;\frac eT{/tex} where, e is charge of electron, T is the time period of revolution, {tex}T\;=\;\frac{2\mathrm{ {tex}\pi{/tex} r}}v{/tex} where r is electron orbital radius and v is the orbital speed. Thus, {tex}I=\frac{ev}{2\mathrm{ {tex}\pi{/tex} r}}{/tex} Magnetic moment is given by, {tex}\mu=I\;A\;=I\;\mathrm\pi r^2\;=\;\frac{evr}2{/tex} and its direction is into the plane of paper. Now, magnetic moment is {tex}\mu=\frac e{2m_e}(m_evr)\;=\;\frac e{2m_e}L{/tex} where L is orbital angular momentum. In vector form, {tex}\vec \mu = \frac { - e \vec L } { 2 M _ { e } }{/tex} Here, negative sign indicates that angular momentum of electron is opposite in direction to magnetic moment.
solution: When electron revolves around a positively charged nucleus, the circular loop constitutes a current, which have a definite dipole moment. So, it behaves as a tiny magnetic dipole. The equivalent current is given by , {tex}I\;=\;\frac eT{/tex} where, e is charge of electron, T is the time period of revolution, {tex}T\;=\;\frac{2\mathrm{ {tex}\pi{/tex} r}}v{/tex} where r is electron orbital radius and v is the orbital speed. Thus, {tex}I=\frac{ev}{2\mathrm{ {tex}\pi{/tex} r}}{/tex} Magnetic moment is given by, {tex}\mu=I\;A\;=I\;\mathrm\pi r^2\;=\;\frac{evr}2{/tex} and its direction is into the plane of paper. Now, magnetic moment is {tex}\mu=\frac e{2m_e}(m_evr)\;=\;\frac e{2m_e}L{/tex} where L is orbital angular momentum. In vector form, {tex}\vec \mu = \frac { - e \vec L } { 2 M _ { e } }{/tex} Here, negative sign indicates that angular momentum of electron is opposite in direction to magnetic moment.
solution_html:
When electron revolves around a positively charged nucleus, the circular loop constitutes a current, which have a definite dipole moment. So, it behaves as a tiny magnetic dipole.
The equivalent  current is given by , {tex}I\;=\;\frac eT{/tex}
where, e is charge of electron, T is the time period of revolution, 
{tex}T\;=\;\frac{2\mathrm{{tex}\pi{/tex}r}}v{/tex}
where r is electron orbital radius and v is the orbital speed.
Thus, {tex}I=\frac{ev}{2\mathrm{{tex}\pi{/tex}r}}{/tex}
Magnetic moment is given by,
{tex}\mu=I\;A\;=I\;\mathrm\pi r^2\;=\;\frac{evr}2{/tex}
and its direction is into the plane of paper.
Now, magnetic moment is {tex}\mu=\frac e{2m_e}(m_evr)\;=\;\frac e{2m_e}L{/tex}
where L is orbital angular momentum.
In vector form, {tex}\vec \mu = \frac { - e \vec L } { 2 M _ { e } }{/tex}
Here, negative sign indicates that angular momentum of electron is opposite in direction to magnetic moment.

question_html:
1. Write the expression for the force {tex}\vec F{/tex} acting on a charged particle of mass m and charge q moving with velocity {tex}\vec v{/tex} in a magnetic field {tex}\vec B{/tex}. Under what conditions, will it move in
    1. a circular path and
    2. a helical path?
  2. Show that the kinetic energy of the particle moving in the magnetic field remains constant.
question_text: Write the expression for the force {tex}\vec F{/tex} acting on a charged particle of mass m and charge q moving with velocity {tex}\vec v{/tex} in a magnetic field {tex}\vec B{/tex} . Under what conditions, will it move in a circular path and a helical path? Show that the kinetic energy of the particle moving in the magnetic field remains constant.
explanation: The expression for force acting on the charged particle q in vector form is given by, {tex}\vec F = q ( \ {\vec v } \times \ { \vec B } ){/tex} where B is uniform magnetic field and v is velocity with which particle is moving. From this equation, it is clear that direction of force is perpendicular to the plane containing both v and B. Hence, (a) When velocity v is perpendicular to B, the perpendicular force acts as centripetal force and a circular motion is produced perpendicular to the magnetic field. (b) When velocity has component parallel to the field B, the resultant motion is a helical motion along the magnetic field. In case, B is not perpendicular to v, a component of v remains perpendicular to B. It creates circular path. The component of v parallel to B will create linear path. Here, the particle will have circular path due to v cos {tex}\theta {/tex} and linear path due to {tex}v\;\sin\theta{/tex} . Both, when combined, gives a helical path. remains unchanged, A charged particle moving in a perpendicular magnetic field describes a circular path, on which its speed v does not change. So its kinetic energy {tex}K(=1/2mv^2){/tex} remains unchanged.
solution: The expression for force acting on the charged particle q in vector form is given by, {tex}\vec F = q ( \ {\vec v } \times \ { \vec B } ){/tex} where B is uniform magnetic field and v is velocity with which particle is moving. From this equation, it is clear that direction of force is perpendicular to the plane containing both v and B. Hence, (a) When velocity v is perpendicular to B, the perpendicular force acts as centripetal force and a circular motion is produced perpendicular to the magnetic field. (b) When velocity has component parallel to the field B, the resultant motion is a helical motion along the magnetic field. In case, B is not perpendicular to v, a component of v remains perpendicular to B. It creates circular path. The component of v parallel to B will create linear path. Here, the particle will have circular path due to v cos {tex}\theta {/tex} and linear path due to {tex}v\;\sin\theta{/tex} . Both, when combined, gives a helical path. remains unchanged, A charged particle moving in a perpendicular magnetic field describes a circular path, on which its speed v does not change. So its kinetic energy {tex}K(=1/2mv^2){/tex} remains unchanged.
solution_html:
1. The expression for force acting on the charged particle q in vector form is given by,
    {tex}\vec F = q ( \ {\vec v } \times \ { \vec B } ){/tex}
    where B is uniform magnetic field and v is velocity with which particle is moving.

    From this equation, it is clear that direction of force is perpendicular to the plane containing both v and B. Hence,
    (a) When velocity v is perpendicular to B, the perpendicular force acts as centripetal force and a circular motion is produced perpendicular to the magnetic field.
    (b) When velocity has component parallel to the field B, the resultant motion is a helical motion along the magnetic field. In case, B is not perpendicular to v, a component of v remains perpendicular to B. It creates circular path. The component of v parallel to B will create linear path. Here, the particle will have circular path due to v cos{tex}\theta {/tex} and linear path due to {tex}v\;\sin\theta{/tex}. Both, when combined, gives a helical path.
     
  2. remains unchanged, A charged particle moving in a perpendicular magnetic field describes a circular path, on which its speed v does not change. So its kinetic energy {tex}K(=1/2mv^2){/tex} remains unchanged.

question_html: A charge q of mass m is moving with a velocity of v, at right angles to a uniform magnetic field B. Deduce the expression for the radius of the circular path it describes.
question_text: A charge q of mass m is moving with a velocity of v, at right angles to a uniform magnetic field B. Deduce the expression for the radius of the circular path it describes.
explanation: Derivation of the expression for the radius Force experienced by a charged particle in the magnetic field {tex}\vec B{/tex} . {tex}\vec{F}=q(\vec{v} \times \vec{B}){/tex} As v and B are perpendiculars, F = qvB (sin90 0 = 1) This force is perpendicular to the direction of velocity and hence acts as centripetal force. Both of the forces are equal so that {tex}\frac{m v^{2}}{r} {/tex} = qvB r = {tex}\frac{m v}{q B}{/tex}
solution: Derivation of the expression for the radius Force experienced by a charged particle in the magnetic field {tex}\vec B{/tex} . {tex}\vec{F}=q(\vec{v} \times \vec{B}){/tex} As v and B are perpendiculars, F = qvB (sin90 0 = 1) This force is perpendicular to the direction of velocity and hence acts as centripetal force. Both of the forces are equal so that {tex}\frac{m v^{2}}{r} {/tex} = qvB r = {tex}\frac{m v}{q B}{/tex}
solution_html:
Derivation of the expression for the radius 
Force experienced by a charged particle in the magnetic field {tex}\vec B{/tex}.
{tex}\vec{F}=q(\vec{v} \times \vec{B}){/tex}
As v and B are perpendiculars, F = qvB (sin900 = 1)
This force is perpendicular to the direction of velocity and hence acts as centripetal force. Both of the forces are equal so that
{tex}\frac{m v^{2}}{r} {/tex} = qvB
r = {tex}\frac{m v}{q B}{/tex}

question_html: A proton and an alpha particle having the same kinetic energy are, in turn, passed through a region of the uniform magnetic field, acting normal to the plane of the paper and travel in circular paths. Deduce the ratio of the radii of the circular paths described by them.
question_text: A proton and an alpha particle having the same kinetic energy are, in turn, passed through a region of the uniform magnetic field, acting normal to the plane of the paper and travel in circular paths. Deduce the ratio of the radii of the circular paths described by them.
explanation: Derivation of ratio of the radii of the circular paths r = {tex}\frac{m v}{q B}{/tex} But {tex}\frac{p^{2}}{2 m}=k \Rightarrow p=\sqrt{2 m k}{/tex} = mv {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{r_{\alpha}}{r_{p}}=\frac{\sqrt{2 m_{\alpha} k_{\alpha}} / q_{a} B}{\sqrt{2 m_{p} k_{p}} / q_{p} B}{/tex} But k {tex}\alpha{/tex} = k p {tex}\frac{r_{\alpha}}{r_{p}}=\frac{q_{p}}{q_{\alpha}} \sqrt{\frac{m_{\alpha}}{m_{p}}}{/tex} Since, charge of alpha particle q {tex}\alpha{/tex} = 2q p mass of alpha particle m {tex}\alpha{/tex} = 4m p {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{r_{\alpha}}{r_{p}}=\frac{ q_{p}}{2q_{p}} \sqrt{\frac{4m_{p}}{m_{p}}}{/tex} = 1:1
solution: Derivation of ratio of the radii of the circular paths r = {tex}\frac{m v}{q B}{/tex} But {tex}\frac{p^{2}}{2 m}=k \Rightarrow p=\sqrt{2 m k}{/tex} = mv {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{r_{\alpha}}{r_{p}}=\frac{\sqrt{2 m_{\alpha} k_{\alpha}} / q_{a} B}{\sqrt{2 m_{p} k_{p}} / q_{p} B}{/tex} But k {tex}\alpha{/tex} = k p {tex}\frac{r_{\alpha}}{r_{p}}=\frac{q_{p}}{q_{\alpha}} \sqrt{\frac{m_{\alpha}}{m_{p}}}{/tex} Since, charge of alpha particle q {tex}\alpha{/tex} = 2q p mass of alpha particle m {tex}\alpha{/tex} = 4m p {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{r_{\alpha}}{r_{p}}=\frac{ q_{p}}{2q_{p}} \sqrt{\frac{4m_{p}}{m_{p}}}{/tex} = 1:1
solution_html:
Derivation of ratio of the radii of the circular paths r = {tex}\frac{m v}{q B}{/tex}
But {tex}\frac{p^{2}}{2 m}=k \Rightarrow p=\sqrt{2 m k}{/tex} = mv
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{r_{\alpha}}{r_{p}}=\frac{\sqrt{2 m_{\alpha} k_{\alpha}} / q_{a} B}{\sqrt{2 m_{p} k_{p}} / q_{p} B}{/tex}
But k{tex}\alpha{/tex} = kp
{tex}\frac{r_{\alpha}}{r_{p}}=\frac{q_{p}}{q_{\alpha}} \sqrt{\frac{m_{\alpha}}{m_{p}}}{/tex}
Since,  charge of alpha particle q{tex}\alpha{/tex} = 2qp
mass of alpha particle m{tex}\alpha{/tex} = 4mp
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{r_{\alpha}}{r_{p}}=\frac{ q_{p}}{2q_{p}} \sqrt{\frac{4m_{p}}{m_{p}}}{/tex} = 1:1

subject: Physics
question_html: Using Biot-Savart's law, deduce the expression for the magnetic field at a point (x) on the axis of a circular current-carrying loop of radius R. How is the direction of the magnetic field determined at this point?
question_text: Using Biot-Savart's law, deduce the expression for the magnetic field at a point (x) on the axis of a circular current-carrying loop of radius R. How is the direction of the magnetic field determined at this point?
explanation: We have, {tex}\overrightarrow{d B} =\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} I \frac{|\overrightarrow{d I} \times \vec{r}|}{r^{3}} {/tex} r 2 = x 2 + R 2 {tex}\therefore{/tex} dB = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I}{4 \pi} \frac{d l}{\left(x^{2}+R^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}}{/tex} We need to add only the components of dB along the axis of the coil. Hence, B = {tex}\int \frac{\mu_{0} }{4 \pi} \cdot \frac{I d l}{\left(x^{2}+R^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}} \cos \theta{/tex} = {tex}\int \frac{\mu_{0} }{4 \pi} \frac{(I d l) R}{\left(x^{2}+R^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}}{/tex} summation over dl=2 {tex}\pi{/tex} R = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I R^{2}}{2\left(x^{2}+R^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \vec{B} =\frac{\mu_{0} I R^{2}}{2\left(x^{2}+R^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}} \hat{i}{/tex}
solution: We have, {tex}\overrightarrow{d B} =\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} I \frac{|\overrightarrow{d I} \times \vec{r}|}{r^{3}} {/tex} r 2 = x 2 + R 2 {tex}\therefore{/tex} dB = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I}{4 \pi} \frac{d l}{\left(x^{2}+R^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}}{/tex} We need to add only the components of dB along the axis of the coil. Hence, B = {tex}\int \frac{\mu_{0} }{4 \pi} \cdot \frac{I d l}{\left(x^{2}+R^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}} \cos \theta{/tex} = {tex}\int \frac{\mu_{0} }{4 \pi} \frac{(I d l) R}{\left(x^{2}+R^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}}{/tex} summation over dl=2 {tex}\pi{/tex} R = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I R^{2}}{2\left(x^{2}+R^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \vec{B} =\frac{\mu_{0} I R^{2}}{2\left(x^{2}+R^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}} \hat{i}{/tex}
solution_html:
We have, {tex}\overrightarrow{d B} =\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} I \frac{|\overrightarrow{d I} \times \vec{r}|}{r^{3}} {/tex}
r2 = x2 + R2
{tex}\therefore{/tex} dB = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I}{4 \pi} \frac{d l}{\left(x^{2}+R^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}}{/tex}
We need to add only the components of dB along the axis of the coil.
Hence, B = {tex}\int \frac{\mu_{0} }{4 \pi} \cdot \frac{I d l}{\left(x^{2}+R^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}} \cos \theta{/tex}
= {tex}\int \frac{\mu_{0} }{4 \pi} \frac{(I d l) R}{\left(x^{2}+R^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}}{/tex} summation over dl=2{tex}\pi{/tex}R
= {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I R^{2}}{2\left(x^{2}+R^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \vec{B} =\frac{\mu_{0} I R^{2}}{2\left(x^{2}+R^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}} \hat{i}{/tex}

question_html: Define the term current sensitivity of a galvanometer. In the circuits shown in the figures, the galvanometer shows no deflection in each case. Find the ratio of R1 and R2.
question_text: Define the term current sensitivity of a galvanometer. In the circuits shown in the figures, the galvanometer shows no deflection in each case. Find the ratio of R 1 and R 2 .
explanation: The current sensitivity of the galvanometer is the deflection produced when the unit current passes through the galvanometer. Or The sensitivity (i.e. current sensitivity) of a galvanometer is defined as the angle of deflection per unit current flowing through it. A galvanometer is said to be sensitive if it produces large deflection for a small current. I = {tex}\frac{C}{n B A} \theta{/tex} where C is torsional constant. Current sensitivity = {tex}\frac{\theta}{I}=\frac{n B A}{C}{/tex} Consider the circuit I: For a balanced Wheatstone bridge, there will be no deflection in the galvanometer, so {tex}\frac{4}{R_{1}} =\frac{6}{4}{/tex} Hence, R 1 = {tex}\frac{4 \times 4}{6}=\frac{8}{3} \Omega{/tex} Consider the circuit II: For equivalent circuit, when the Wheatstone bridge is balanced, there will be no deflection in the galvanometer, so {tex}\frac{6}{12}=\frac{R_{2}}{8} {/tex} Hence, R 2 = {tex}\frac{6 \times 8}{12}=4 \Omega{/tex} Now ratio, {tex}\begin{aligned} &\therefore \frac{R_{1}}{R_{2}}=\frac{\frac{8}{3}}{4}=\frac{2}{3} \end{aligned}{/tex}
solution: The current sensitivity of the galvanometer is the deflection produced when the unit current passes through the galvanometer. Or The sensitivity (i.e. current sensitivity) of a galvanometer is defined as the angle of deflection per unit current flowing through it. A galvanometer is said to be sensitive if it produces large deflection for a small current. I = {tex}\frac{C}{n B A} \theta{/tex} where C is torsional constant. Current sensitivity = {tex}\frac{\theta}{I}=\frac{n B A}{C}{/tex} Consider the circuit I: For a balanced Wheatstone bridge, there will be no deflection in the galvanometer, so {tex}\frac{4}{R_{1}} =\frac{6}{4}{/tex} Hence, R 1 = {tex}\frac{4 \times 4}{6}=\frac{8}{3} \Omega{/tex} Consider the circuit II: For equivalent circuit, when the Wheatstone bridge is balanced, there will be no deflection in the galvanometer, so {tex}\frac{6}{12}=\frac{R_{2}}{8} {/tex} Hence, R 2 = {tex}\frac{6 \times 8}{12}=4 \Omega{/tex} Now ratio, {tex}\begin{aligned} &\therefore \frac{R_{1}}{R_{2}}=\frac{\frac{8}{3}}{4}=\frac{2}{3} \end{aligned}{/tex}
solution_html:
1. The current sensitivity of the galvanometer is the deflection produced when the unit current passes through the galvanometer. Or The sensitivity (i.e. current sensitivity) of a galvanometer is defined as the angle of deflection per unit current flowing through it. A galvanometer is said to be sensitive if it produces large deflection for a small current.
    I = {tex}\frac{C}{n B A} \theta{/tex}
    where C is torsional constant.
    Current sensitivity = {tex}\frac{\theta}{I}=\frac{n B A}{C}{/tex}
  2. Consider the circuit I:
    For a balanced Wheatstone bridge, there will be no deflection in the galvanometer, so
    {tex}\frac{4}{R_{1}} =\frac{6}{4}{/tex}
    Hence, R1 = {tex}\frac{4 \times 4}{6}=\frac{8}{3} \Omega{/tex}
    Consider the circuit II:
    For equivalent circuit, when the Wheatstone bridge is balanced, there will be no deflection in the galvanometer, so
    {tex}\frac{6}{12}=\frac{R_{2}}{8} {/tex}
    Hence, R2 = {tex}\frac{6 \times 8}{12}=4 \Omega{/tex}
    Now ratio,
    {tex}\begin{aligned} &\therefore \frac{R_{1}}{R_{2}}=\frac{\frac{8}{3}}{4}=\frac{2}{3} \end{aligned}{/tex}

question_html:
1. State Biot - Savart's law and express this law in the vector form.
  2. Two identical circular coils, P and Q each of radius R, carrying currents 1A and {tex}\sqrt 3 {/tex}A respectively, are placed concentrically and perpendicular to each other lying in the XY and YZ planes. Find the magnitude and direction of the net magnetic field at the centre of the coils.
question_text: State Biot - Savart's law and express this law in the vector form. Two identical circular coils, P and Q each of radius R, carrying currents 1A and {tex}\sqrt 3 {/tex} A respectively, are placed concentrically and perpendicular to each other lying in the XY and YZ planes. Find the magnitude and direction of the net magnetic field at the centre of the coils.
explanation: Biot-Savarat's Law: It states that magnetic field due to current element Idl of a conducting wire at a distance r from it is given as {tex}\overrightarrow{dB}=\frac{\mu_0}{4\mathrm\pi}\frac{\overrightarrow{Idl}\times\overrightarrow r}{r^3}{/tex} The direction of magnetic field can be find out by using right hand thumb rule. As magnetic field at the centre of circular coil is given as {tex}B=\frac{\mu_0I}{2\mathrm{ {tex}\pi{/tex} r}}{/tex} Using this formula for P coil and Q coil, {tex}B_P=\frac{\mu_0}{2\mathrm{ {tex}\pi{/tex} r}} {/tex} {tex}B_Q=\frac{\mu_0\sqrt3}{2\mathrm{ {tex}\pi{/tex} r}}{/tex} As BP and BQ are perpendicular to each other as shown in diagram, so net magnetic field is given as So, net magnetic field, {tex}B_{net}=\sqrt{B_P^2+B_Q^2} {/tex} Putting the values we get, {tex}B_{net}=\frac{2\mu_0}{2\mathrm{ {tex}\pi{/tex} r}}=\frac{\mu_0}{\mathrm{ {tex}\pi{/tex} r}}{/tex} Also direction of magnetic field, tan θ {tex} = \frac{{{B_p}}}{{{B_Q}}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac 1{\sqrt 3}{/tex} = tan 30° So, {tex}\theta{/tex} = 30° Hence, net magnetic field directed in 30° with horizontal plane.
solution: Biot-Savarat's Law: It states that magnetic field due to current element Idl of a conducting wire at a distance r from it is given as {tex}\overrightarrow{dB}=\frac{\mu_0}{4\mathrm\pi}\frac{\overrightarrow{Idl}\times\overrightarrow r}{r^3}{/tex} The direction of magnetic field can be find out by using right hand thumb rule. As magnetic field at the centre of circular coil is given as {tex}B=\frac{\mu_0I}{2\mathrm{ {tex}\pi{/tex} r}}{/tex} Using this formula for P coil and Q coil, {tex}B_P=\frac{\mu_0}{2\mathrm{ {tex}\pi{/tex} r}} {/tex} {tex}B_Q=\frac{\mu_0\sqrt3}{2\mathrm{ {tex}\pi{/tex} r}}{/tex} As BP and BQ are perpendicular to each other as shown in diagram, so net magnetic field is given as So, net magnetic field, {tex}B_{net}=\sqrt{B_P^2+B_Q^2} {/tex} Putting the values we get, {tex}B_{net}=\frac{2\mu_0}{2\mathrm{ {tex}\pi{/tex} r}}=\frac{\mu_0}{\mathrm{ {tex}\pi{/tex} r}}{/tex} Also direction of magnetic field, tan θ {tex} = \frac{{{B_p}}}{{{B_Q}}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac 1{\sqrt 3}{/tex} = tan 30° So, {tex}\theta{/tex} = 30° Hence, net magnetic field directed in 30° with horizontal plane.
solution_html:
1. Biot-Savarat's Law: It states that magnetic field due to current element Idl of a conducting wire at a distance r from it is given as
    {tex}\overrightarrow{dB}=\frac{\mu_0}{4\mathrm\pi}\frac{\overrightarrow{Idl}\times\overrightarrow r}{r^3}{/tex}
    The direction of magnetic field can be find out by using right hand thumb rule.
  2. As magnetic field at the centre of circular coil is given as 
    {tex}B=\frac{\mu_0I}{2\mathrm{{tex}\pi{/tex}r}}{/tex}
    Using this formula for P coil and Q coil,
    {tex}B_P=\frac{\mu_0}{2\mathrm{{tex}\pi{/tex}r}} {/tex}
    {tex}B_Q=\frac{\mu_0\sqrt3}{2\mathrm{{tex}\pi{/tex}r}}{/tex}
    As BP and BQ are perpendicular to each other as shown in diagram, so net magnetic field is given as

    So, net magnetic field, {tex}B_{net}=\sqrt{B_P^2+B_Q^2} {/tex}
    Putting the values we get,
    {tex}B_{net}=\frac{2\mu_0}{2\mathrm{{tex}\pi{/tex}r}}=\frac{\mu_0}{\mathrm{{tex}\pi{/tex}r}}{/tex}
    Also direction of magnetic field,
    tan θ {tex} = \frac{{{B_p}}}{{{B_Q}}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac 1{\sqrt 3}{/tex} = tan 30°
    So, {tex}\theta{/tex} = 30°
    Hence, net magnetic field directed in 30° with horizontal plane.

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. State the condition under which a charged particle moving with velocity v goes undeflected in a magnetic field B.
  2. An electron, after being accelerated through a potential difference of 104 V enters a uniform magnetic field of 0-04 T, perpendicular to its direction of motion. Calculate the radius of curvature of its trajectory.
question_text: State the condition under which a charged particle moving with velocity v goes undeflected in a magnetic field B. An electron, after being accelerated through a potential difference of 10 4 V enters a uniform magnetic field of 0-04 T, perpendicular to its direction of motion. Calculate the radius of curvature of its trajectory.
explanation: The force experienced, {tex}\vec{F}=q(\vec{v} \times \vec{B}){/tex} The charge will go undeflected when {tex}\vec v{/tex} is parallel or antiparallel to {tex}\vec B{/tex} {tex}\because \vec F{/tex} = 0 [Alternatively, If v makes an angle of 0° or 180° with {tex}\vec B{/tex} ] The KE of electron KE = {tex}\frac{e^{2} r^{2} B^{2}}{2 m}{/tex} = eV ,V is equal to 10 4 V. {tex}\begin{aligned} \therefore r=\frac{1}{B} \sqrt{\frac{2 m V}{e}} \end{aligned}{/tex} = {tex}\left[\sqrt{\frac{2 \times 9.1 \times 10^{-31} \times 10^{4}}{1.6 \times 10^{-19}}} \times \frac{1}{0.04}\right]{/tex} = 8.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 m
solution: The force experienced, {tex}\vec{F}=q(\vec{v} \times \vec{B}){/tex} The charge will go undeflected when {tex}\vec v{/tex} is parallel or antiparallel to {tex}\vec B{/tex} {tex}\because \vec F{/tex} = 0 [Alternatively, If v makes an angle of 0° or 180° with {tex}\vec B{/tex} ] The KE of electron KE = {tex}\frac{e^{2} r^{2} B^{2}}{2 m}{/tex} = eV ,V is equal to 10 4 V. {tex}\begin{aligned} \therefore r=\frac{1}{B} \sqrt{\frac{2 m V}{e}} \end{aligned}{/tex} = {tex}\left[\sqrt{\frac{2 \times 9.1 \times 10^{-31} \times 10^{4}}{1.6 \times 10^{-19}}} \times \frac{1}{0.04}\right]{/tex} = 8.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 m
solution_html:
1. The force experienced, {tex}\vec{F}=q(\vec{v} \times \vec{B}){/tex}
    The charge will go undeflected when {tex}\vec v{/tex} is parallel or antiparallel to {tex}\vec B{/tex}
    {tex}\because \vec F{/tex} = 0
    [Alternatively, If v makes an angle of 0° or 180° with {tex}\vec B{/tex}]
  2. The KE of electron
    KE  = {tex}\frac{e^{2} r^{2} B^{2}}{2 m}{/tex} = eV  ,V is equal to 104 V.
    {tex}\begin{aligned} \therefore r=\frac{1}{B} \sqrt{\frac{2 m V}{e}} \end{aligned}{/tex} = {tex}\left[\sqrt{\frac{2 \times 9.1 \times 10^{-31} \times 10^{4}}{1.6 \times 10^{-19}}} \times \frac{1}{0.04}\right]{/tex}
    = 8.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 m

subject: Physics
question_html: A horizontal wire AB of length l and mass m carries a steady current I1 free to move in the vertical plane is in equilibrium at a height of h over another parallel long wire CD carrying a steady current Iy which is fixed in a horizontal plane. Derive an expression for force acting per unit length on the wire AB and write the conditions for which wire AB is in equilibrium?
question_text: A horizontal wire AB of length l and mass m carries a steady current I 1 free to move in the vertical plane is in equilibrium at a height of h over another parallel long wire CD carrying a steady current Iy which is fixed in a horizontal plane. Derive an expression for force acting per unit length on the wire AB and write the conditions for which wire AB is in equilibrium?
explanation: AB and CD are two parallel wires, so when the current flows in similar direction, both the wires will attract and when the current in one wire flows in the opposite direction, they repel. Now consider the above diagram, if wire AB is steady, then weight per unit length will be equal to force per unit length, so Weight per unit length = {tex}\frac{m g}{l}{/tex} Further, the restoring force/length = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \times \frac{2 I_{1} I_{2}}{h}{/tex} thus the condition for equilibrium is given by , magnetic force per unit length will be mass per unit length {tex}\times{/tex} g, so F = {tex}\frac{m}{l} g=\frac{\mu_{0} I_{1} I_{2}}{2 \pi h}{/tex}
solution: AB and CD are two parallel wires, so when the current flows in similar direction, both the wires will attract and when the current in one wire flows in the opposite direction, they repel. Now consider the above diagram, if wire AB is steady, then weight per unit length will be equal to force per unit length, so Weight per unit length = {tex}\frac{m g}{l}{/tex} Further, the restoring force/length = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \times \frac{2 I_{1} I_{2}}{h}{/tex} thus the condition for equilibrium is given by , magnetic force per unit length will be mass per unit length {tex}\times{/tex} g, so F = {tex}\frac{m}{l} g=\frac{\mu_{0} I_{1} I_{2}}{2 \pi h}{/tex}
solution_html:
AB and CD are two parallel wires, so when the current flows in similar direction, both the wires will attract and when the current in one wire flows in the opposite direction, they repel.

Now consider the above diagram, if wire AB is steady, then weight per unit length will be equal to force per unit length, so
Weight per unit length = {tex}\frac{m g}{l}{/tex}
Further, the restoring force/length = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \times \frac{2 I_{1} I_{2}}{h}{/tex}
thus the condition for  equilibrium is given by , magnetic force per unit length will be mass per unit length {tex}\times{/tex} g, so F = {tex}\frac{m}{l} g=\frac{\mu_{0} I_{1} I_{2}}{2 \pi h}{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html: An electron after being accelerated through a potential difference of 104 V enters a uniform magnetic field of 0.04 T perpendicular to its direction of motion. Calculate the radius of curvature of its trajectory.
question_text: An electron after being accelerated through a potential difference of 10 4 V enters a uniform magnetic field of 0.04 T perpendicular to its direction of motion. Calculate the radius of curvature of its trajectory.
explanation: Here V = 10 4 V, B = 0.04 T, e = 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -19 C, m = 9.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -31 kg An electron accelerated through a p.d. V acquires a velocity v given by {tex}\frac{1}{2} m v^{2}{/tex} = eV or v = {tex}\sqrt{\frac{2 e V}{m}}{/tex} As the electron describes a circular path of radius of r in the perpendicular magnetic field B, therefore, {tex}\frac{m v^{2}}{r}{/tex} = evB or r = {tex}\frac{m v}{e B}=\frac{m}{e B} \sqrt{\frac{2 e V}{m}}=\frac{\sqrt{2 m e V}}{e B}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\sqrt{2 \times 9.1 \times 10^{-31} \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 10^{4}}}{1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 0.04}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{5.4 \times 10^{-23}}{1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 0.04}{/tex} = 8.43 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 m = 8.43 mm
solution: Here V = 10 4 V, B = 0.04 T, e = 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -19 C, m = 9.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -31 kg An electron accelerated through a p.d. V acquires a velocity v given by {tex}\frac{1}{2} m v^{2}{/tex} = eV or v = {tex}\sqrt{\frac{2 e V}{m}}{/tex} As the electron describes a circular path of radius of r in the perpendicular magnetic field B, therefore, {tex}\frac{m v^{2}}{r}{/tex} = evB or r = {tex}\frac{m v}{e B}=\frac{m}{e B} \sqrt{\frac{2 e V}{m}}=\frac{\sqrt{2 m e V}}{e B}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\sqrt{2 \times 9.1 \times 10^{-31} \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 10^{4}}}{1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 0.04}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{5.4 \times 10^{-23}}{1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 0.04}{/tex} = 8.43 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 m = 8.43 mm
solution_html:
Here V = 104 V, B = 0.04 T,
e = 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-19 C, m = 9.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-31 kg
An electron accelerated through a p.d. V acquires a velocity v given by
{tex}\frac{1}{2} m v^{2}{/tex} = eV or v = {tex}\sqrt{\frac{2 e V}{m}}{/tex}
As the electron describes a circular path of radius of r in the perpendicular magnetic field B, therefore,
{tex}\frac{m v^{2}}{r}{/tex} = evB
or r = {tex}\frac{m v}{e B}=\frac{m}{e B} \sqrt{\frac{2 e V}{m}}=\frac{\sqrt{2 m e V}}{e B}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{\sqrt{2 \times 9.1 \times 10^{-31} \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 10^{4}}}{1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 0.04}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{5.4 \times 10^{-23}}{1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 0.04}{/tex}
= 8.43 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 m = 8.43 mm

subject: Physics
question_html:
The figure shows three infinitely long straight parallel current-carrying conductors. Find the

  1. magnitude and direction of the net magnetic field at point A lying on conductor 1,
  2. magnetic force on conductor 2.
question_text: The figure shows three infinitely long straight parallel current-carrying conductors. Find the magnitude and direction of the net magnetic field at point A lying on conductor 1, magnetic force on conductor 2.
explanation: Magnetic field at point A due to current 3I in conductor 2 {tex}B_{2}=\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi}, \frac{2(3 I)}{8}=\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi}, \frac{6 I}{r}{/tex} acting into the plane of the paper Magnetic field at point A due to current 4l in conductor 3, {tex}B_{2}=\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \cdot \frac{2(4 l)}{3 r}=\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \cdot \frac{8 I}{3 r}{/tex} acting out of the plane of the paper Net field at point A, {tex}B_{A}=B_{2}-B_{3}=\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi}\left(\frac{6 I}{r}-\frac{8 I}{3 r}\right)=\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \cdot \frac{10 I}{3 r}{/tex} acting into the plane of paper Force exerted by conductor 1 on conductor 2, {tex}F_{21}=\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi}, \frac{2(I)(3 I)}{r}{/tex} away from conductor 1 towards conductor 3, Force exerted by conductor 3 on conductor 2, {tex}F_{31}=\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi}, \frac{2(3 I)(4 I)}{2 r}{/tex} away from conductor 3/towards conductor 1 Net magnetic force on conductor 2, {tex}F_{2}=F_{23}-F_{21}=\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi}\left(\frac{12 I^{2}}{r}-\frac{6 I^{2}}{r}\right)=\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \cdot \frac{6 I^{2}}{r}{/tex} , towards conductor 1.
solution: Magnetic field at point A due to current 3I in conductor 2 {tex}B_{2}=\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi}, \frac{2(3 I)}{8}=\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi}, \frac{6 I}{r}{/tex} acting into the plane of the paper Magnetic field at point A due to current 4l in conductor 3, {tex}B_{2}=\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \cdot \frac{2(4 l)}{3 r}=\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \cdot \frac{8 I}{3 r}{/tex} acting out of the plane of the paper Net field at point A, {tex}B_{A}=B_{2}-B_{3}=\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi}\left(\frac{6 I}{r}-\frac{8 I}{3 r}\right)=\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \cdot \frac{10 I}{3 r}{/tex} acting into the plane of paper Force exerted by conductor 1 on conductor 2, {tex}F_{21}=\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi}, \frac{2(I)(3 I)}{r}{/tex} away from conductor 1 towards conductor 3, Force exerted by conductor 3 on conductor 2, {tex}F_{31}=\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi}, \frac{2(3 I)(4 I)}{2 r}{/tex} away from conductor 3/towards conductor 1 Net magnetic force on conductor 2, {tex}F_{2}=F_{23}-F_{21}=\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi}\left(\frac{12 I^{2}}{r}-\frac{6 I^{2}}{r}\right)=\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \cdot \frac{6 I^{2}}{r}{/tex} , towards conductor 1.
solution_html:
1. Magnetic field at point A due to current 3I in conductor 2
    {tex}B_{2}=\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi}, \frac{2(3 I)}{8}=\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi}, \frac{6 I}{r}{/tex} acting into the plane of the paper
    Magnetic field at point A due to current 4l in conductor 3,
    {tex}B_{2}=\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \cdot \frac{2(4 l)}{3 r}=\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \cdot \frac{8 I}{3 r}{/tex} acting out of the plane of the paper
    Net field at point A,
    {tex}B_{A}=B_{2}-B_{3}=\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi}\left(\frac{6 I}{r}-\frac{8 I}{3 r}\right)=\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \cdot \frac{10 I}{3 r}{/tex} acting into the plane of paper
  2. Force exerted by conductor 1 on conductor 2,
    {tex}F_{21}=\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi}, \frac{2(I)(3 I)}{r}{/tex}
    away from conductor 1 towards conductor 3,
    Force exerted by conductor 3 on conductor 2,
    {tex}F_{31}=\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi}, \frac{2(3 I)(4 I)}{2 r}{/tex}
    away from conductor 3/towards conductor 1
    Net magnetic force on conductor 2,
    {tex}F_{2}=F_{23}-F_{21}=\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi}\left(\frac{12 I^{2}}{r}-\frac{6 I^{2}}{r}\right)=\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \cdot \frac{6 I^{2}}{r}{/tex}, towards conductor 1.

question_html: A charged particle of mass'm', charge 'q' moving at uniform velocity 'v', enters a uniform magnetic field 'B' acting normal to the plane of the paper. Deduce expression for the (t) radius of the circular path in which it travels, (ii) kinetic energy of the particle (assuming v << c).
question_text: A charged particle of mass'm', charge 'q' moving at uniform velocity 'v', enters a uniform magnetic field 'B' acting normal to the plane of the paper. Deduce expression for the (t) radius of the circular path in which it travels, (ii) kinetic energy of the particle (assuming v << c).
explanation: Force exerted by the magnetic field = Centripetal force on the charge or {tex}q v B \sin 90^{\circ}=\frac{m v^{2}}{r}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Radius {tex}r=\frac{m v}{q B}{/tex} Velocity, {tex}v=\frac{q B r}{m}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \mathrm{K} . \mathrm{E} .=\frac{1}{2} m v^{2}=\frac{1}{2} m\left(\frac{q B r}{m}\right)^{2}=\frac{B^{2} q^{2} r^{2}}{2 m}{/tex}
solution: Force exerted by the magnetic field = Centripetal force on the charge or {tex}q v B \sin 90^{\circ}=\frac{m v^{2}}{r}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Radius {tex}r=\frac{m v}{q B}{/tex} Velocity, {tex}v=\frac{q B r}{m}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \mathrm{K} . \mathrm{E} .=\frac{1}{2} m v^{2}=\frac{1}{2} m\left(\frac{q B r}{m}\right)^{2}=\frac{B^{2} q^{2} r^{2}}{2 m}{/tex}
solution_html:
1. Force exerted by the magnetic field
    = Centripetal force on the charge
    or {tex}q v B \sin 90^{\circ}=\frac{m v^{2}}{r}{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} Radius {tex}r=\frac{m v}{q B}{/tex}
  2. Velocity,{tex}v=\frac{q B r}{m}{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore \mathrm{K} . \mathrm{E} .=\frac{1}{2} m v^{2}=\frac{1}{2} m\left(\frac{q B r}{m}\right)^{2}=\frac{B^{2} q^{2} r^{2}}{2 m}{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html: Describe the working principle of a moving coil galvanometer. Why is it necessary to use (i) a radial magnetic field and (ii) a cylindrical soft iron core in a galvanometer? Can a galvanometer as such be used for measuring the current? Explain.
question_text: Describe the working principle of a moving coil galvanometer. Why is it necessary to use (i) a radial magnetic field and (ii) a cylindrical soft iron core in a galvanometer? Can a galvanometer as such be used for measuring the current? Explain.
explanation: Principle of a moving coil galvanometer: A current-carrying coil placed in a magnetic field experiences a current dependent torque, which tends to rotate the coil and produces angular deflection. Radial magnetic field maximizes the torque on the coil and provides a linear current scale. A cylindrical soft iron core makes the field radial. This increases the strength of the magnetic field and hence increases the sensitivity of the galvanometer. A galvanometer as such cannot be used to measure current in a circuit due to two reasons: A galvanometer can detect only small currents and its coil is likely to be damaged by currents of (mA/A) range. For measuring currents, it has to be first calibrated.
solution: Principle of a moving coil galvanometer: A current-carrying coil placed in a magnetic field experiences a current dependent torque, which tends to rotate the coil and produces angular deflection. Radial magnetic field maximizes the torque on the coil and provides a linear current scale. A cylindrical soft iron core makes the field radial. This increases the strength of the magnetic field and hence increases the sensitivity of the galvanometer. A galvanometer as such cannot be used to measure current in a circuit due to two reasons: A galvanometer can detect only small currents and its coil is likely to be damaged by currents of (mA/A) range. For measuring currents, it has to be first calibrated.
solution_html:
Principle of a moving coil galvanometer: A current-carrying coil placed in a magnetic field experiences a current dependent torque, which tends to rotate the coil and produces angular deflection.

  1. Radial magnetic field maximizes the torque on the coil and provides a linear current scale.
  2. A cylindrical soft iron core makes the field radial. This increases the strength of the magnetic field and hence increases the sensitivity of the galvanometer.

A galvanometer as such cannot be used to measure current in a circuit due to two reasons:

  1. A galvanometer can detect only small currents and its coil is likely to be damaged by currents of (mA/A) range.
  2. For measuring currents, it has to be first calibrated.

question_html:
1. Derive an expression for the magnetic field at a point on the axis of a current-carrying circular loop.
  2. A coil of 100 turns (tightly bound) and radius 10 cm, carries a current of 1 A. What is the magnitude of the magnetic field at the entre of the coil.
question_text: Derive an expression for the magnetic field at a point on the axis of a current-carrying circular loop. A coil of 100 turns (tightly bound) and radius 10 cm, carries a current of 1 A. What is the magnitude of the magnetic field at the entre of the coil.
explanation: i. We have, {tex}\overrightarrow{d B} =\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} I \frac{|\overrightarrow{d I} \times \vec{r}|}{r^{3}} {/tex} r 2 = x 2 + R 2 {tex}\therefore{/tex} dB = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I}{4 \pi} \frac{d l}{\left(x^{2}+R^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}}{/tex} We need to add only the components of dB along the axis of the coil. Hence, B = {tex}\int \frac{\mu_{0} I}{4 \pi} \cdot \frac{I d l}{\left(x^{2}+R^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}} \cos \theta{/tex} = {tex}\int \frac{\mu_{0} I}{4 \pi} \frac{(I d l) R}{\left(x^{2}+R^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I R^{2}}{2\left(x^{2}+R^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \vec{B} =\frac{\mu_{0} I R^{2}}{2\left(x^{2}+R^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}} \hat{i}{/tex} ii. Since the coil is tightly bound, we may take each circular element to have the same radius R = 10 cm = 0.1 m, N = 100 Magnitude of magnetic field at the enter of the coil: B = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} N I}{2 R}{/tex} B = {tex}\frac{4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times 10^{2} \times 1}{2 \times 10^{-1}}{/tex} = 2 {tex}\pi\times{/tex} 10 -4 = 6.28 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -4 T
solution: i. We have, {tex}\overrightarrow{d B} =\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} I \frac{|\overrightarrow{d I} \times \vec{r}|}{r^{3}} {/tex} r 2 = x 2 + R 2 {tex}\therefore{/tex} dB = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I}{4 \pi} \frac{d l}{\left(x^{2}+R^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}}{/tex} We need to add only the components of dB along the axis of the coil. Hence, B = {tex}\int \frac{\mu_{0} I}{4 \pi} \cdot \frac{I d l}{\left(x^{2}+R^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}} \cos \theta{/tex} = {tex}\int \frac{\mu_{0} I}{4 \pi} \frac{(I d l) R}{\left(x^{2}+R^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I R^{2}}{2\left(x^{2}+R^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \vec{B} =\frac{\mu_{0} I R^{2}}{2\left(x^{2}+R^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}} \hat{i}{/tex} ii. Since the coil is tightly bound, we may take each circular element to have the same radius R = 10 cm = 0.1 m, N = 100 Magnitude of magnetic field at the enter of the coil: B = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} N I}{2 R}{/tex} B = {tex}\frac{4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times 10^{2} \times 1}{2 \times 10^{-1}}{/tex} = 2 {tex}\pi\times{/tex} 10 -4 = 6.28 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -4 T
solution_html:
i.


We have, {tex}\overrightarrow{d B} =\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} I \frac{|\overrightarrow{d I} \times \vec{r}|}{r^{3}} {/tex}
r2 = x2 + R2
{tex}\therefore{/tex} dB = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I}{4 \pi} \frac{d l}{\left(x^{2}+R^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}}{/tex}
We need to add only the components of dB along the axis of the coil.
Hence, B = {tex}\int \frac{\mu_{0} I}{4 \pi} \cdot \frac{I d l}{\left(x^{2}+R^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}} \cos \theta{/tex}
= {tex}\int \frac{\mu_{0} I}{4 \pi} \frac{(I d l) R}{\left(x^{2}+R^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I R^{2}}{2\left(x^{2}+R^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \vec{B} =\frac{\mu_{0} I R^{2}}{2\left(x^{2}+R^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}} \hat{i}{/tex}

ii. Since the coil is tightly bound, we may take each circular element to have the same radius R = 10 cm = 0.1 m, N = 100
Magnitude of magnetic field at the enter of the coil: B = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} N I}{2 R}{/tex}
B = {tex}\frac{4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times 10^{2} \times 1}{2 \times 10^{-1}}{/tex}
= 2{tex}\pi\times{/tex} 10-4
= 6.28 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4T

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. Write the expression for the force {tex}\vec{F}{/tex} acting on a particle of mass m and charge q moving with velocity {tex}\vec{v}{/tex} in a magnetic field {tex}\vec{B}{/tex}. Under what conditions will it move in (i) a circular path and (ii) a helical path?
  2. Show that the kinetic energy of the particle moving in a magnetic field remains constant.
question_text: Write the expression for the force {tex}\vec{F}{/tex} acting on a particle of mass m and charge q moving with velocity {tex}\vec{v}{/tex} in a magnetic field {tex}\vec{B}{/tex} . Under what conditions will it move in (i) a circular path and (ii) a helical path? Show that the kinetic energy of the particle moving in a magnetic field remains constant.
explanation: {tex}\vec{F}=q(\vec{v} \times \vec{B}){/tex} When the charged particle moves perpendicular to the magnetic field, it will move in a circular path. When charged particle moves obliquely (neither parallel nor perpendicular) to the magnetic field, it will move in a helical path. The magnetic force acts on the charged particle perpendicular to its instantaneous velocity {tex}\vec{v}{/tex} , at all instants. It cannot change the speed of the particle. As speed remains constant, the kinetic energy also remains constant.
solution: {tex}\vec{F}=q(\vec{v} \times \vec{B}){/tex} When the charged particle moves perpendicular to the magnetic field, it will move in a circular path. When charged particle moves obliquely (neither parallel nor perpendicular) to the magnetic field, it will move in a helical path. The magnetic force acts on the charged particle perpendicular to its instantaneous velocity {tex}\vec{v}{/tex} , at all instants. It cannot change the speed of the particle. As speed remains constant, the kinetic energy also remains constant.
solution_html:
1. {tex}\vec{F}=q(\vec{v} \times \vec{B}){/tex}
    1. When the charged particle moves perpendicular to the magnetic field, it will move in a circular path.
    2. When charged particle moves obliquely (neither parallel nor perpendicular) to the magnetic field, it will move in a helical path.
  2. The magnetic force acts on the charged particle perpendicular to its instantaneous velocity {tex}\vec{v}{/tex} , at all instants. It cannot change the speed of the particle. As speed remains constant, the kinetic energy also remains constant.

question_html:
Describe the working principle of a moving coil galvanometer. Why is it necessary to use

  1. a radial magnetic field and
  2. a cylindrical soft iron core in a galvanometer?

Write the expression for current sensitivity of the galvanometer. Can a galvanometer as such be used for measuring the current? Explain.
question_text: Describe the working principle of a moving coil galvanometer. Why is it necessary to use a radial magnetic field and a cylindrical soft iron core in a galvanometer? Write the expression for current sensitivity of the galvanometer. Can a galvanometer as such be used for measuring the current? Explain.
explanation: A current-carrying coil, when placed in an external magnetic field, experiences magnetic torque. The angle through which the coil is deflected due to the effect of the magnetic torque is proportional to the magnitude of current in the coil. The radial magnetic field is used in a suspended coil galvanometer to provide a uniform torque on the coil because it provides a uniform magnetic field. It ensures that the magnetic field is radial and uniform throughout the area of the coil. In order to produce magnetic field inside the galvanometer device, we can use any magnetic material but soft iron core is especially used in galvanometer because Soft iron is ferromagnetic in nature which in turn strengthen the magnetic field lines, and stronger the magnetic field lines passing through the coil larger Expression for current sensitivity {tex}I_{s}=\frac{\theta}{I} {/tex} The galvanometer is a very sensitive device. For a small amount of current, the galvanometer deflection will be maximum. Hence, it may get damaged when a strong current is passed through the galvanometer. So the galvanometer is not used to measure the current.
solution: A current-carrying coil, when placed in an external magnetic field, experiences magnetic torque. The angle through which the coil is deflected due to the effect of the magnetic torque is proportional to the magnitude of current in the coil. The radial magnetic field is used in a suspended coil galvanometer to provide a uniform torque on the coil because it provides a uniform magnetic field. It ensures that the magnetic field is radial and uniform throughout the area of the coil. In order to produce magnetic field inside the galvanometer device, we can use any magnetic material but soft iron core is especially used in galvanometer because Soft iron is ferromagnetic in nature which in turn strengthen the magnetic field lines, and stronger the magnetic field lines passing through the coil larger Expression for current sensitivity {tex}I_{s}=\frac{\theta}{I} {/tex} The galvanometer is a very sensitive device. For a small amount of current, the galvanometer deflection will be maximum. Hence, it may get damaged when a strong current is passed through the galvanometer. So the galvanometer is not used to measure the current.
solution_html:
A current-carrying coil, when placed in an external magnetic field, experiences magnetic torque. The angle through which the coil is deflected due to the effect of the magnetic torque is proportional to the magnitude of current in the coil.

  1. The radial magnetic field is used in a suspended coil galvanometer to provide a uniform torque on the coil because it provides a uniform magnetic field. It ensures that the magnetic field is radial and uniform throughout the area of the coil.
  2. In order to produce magnetic field inside the galvanometer device, we can use any magnetic material but soft iron core is especially used in galvanometer because Soft iron is ferromagnetic in nature which in turn strengthen the magnetic field lines, and stronger the magnetic field lines passing through the coil larger

Expression for current sensitivity

{tex}I_{s}=\frac{\theta}{I} {/tex}

The galvanometer is a very sensitive device. For a small amount of current, the galvanometer deflection will be maximum. Hence, it may get damaged when a strong current is passed through the galvanometer. So the galvanometer is not used to measure the current.

for (0o or 180o)

question_html:
1. With the help of a diagram, explain the principle and working of a moving coil galvanometer.
  2. What is the importance of a radial magnetic field and how is it produced?
  3. Why is it necessary to introduce a cylindrical soft iron core inside the coil of a galvanometer?
  4. Increasing the current sensitivity of a galvanometer may not necessarily increase its voltage sensitivity. Justify this statement.
question_text: With the help of a diagram, explain the principle and working of a moving coil galvanometer. What is the importance of a radial magnetic field and how is it produced? Why is it necessary to introduce a cylindrical soft iron core inside the coil of a galvanometer? Increasing the current sensitivity of a galvanometer may not necessarily increase its voltage sensitivity. Justify this statement.
explanation: Principle and working: When current (I) is passed in the coil, torque τ acts on the coil, given by {tex}\tau{/tex} = NIABsin {tex}\theta{/tex} When the magnetic field is radial, as in the case of cylindrical pole pieces and soft iron core, then in every position of coil the plane of the coil, is parallel to the magnetic field lines, so that {tex}\theta{/tex} = 90 o and sin 90 o = 1 Deflecting torque, {tex}\tau{/tex} = NIAB If C is the torsional rigidity of the wire and {tex}\theta{/tex} is the twist of suspension strip, then restoring torque = C {tex}\theta{/tex} For equilibrium, deflecting torque = restoring torque i.e. NIAB = C {tex}\theta{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}\frac{N A B}{C}{/tex} I ...(i) i.e. {tex}\theta{/tex} {tex}\propto{/tex} I Importance (or function) of uniform radial magnetic field: In radial magnetic field sin {tex}\theta{/tex} = 1, so torque is {tex}\tau{/tex} = NIAB. This makes the deflection ( {tex}\theta{/tex} ) proportional to current.To produce radial magnetic field pole pieces of permanent magnet are made cylindrical and a soft iron core is placed between them. The soft iron core helps in making the field radial and reduce energy losses produced due to eddy currents. lection of coil is directly proportional to current flowing in the coil and hence we can construct a linear scale. Importance of uniform radial magnetic field: Torque for current carrying coil in a magnetic field is {tex}\tau{/tex} = NIAB sin {tex}\theta{/tex} In radial magnetic field Sin {tex}\theta{/tex} = 1, so torque is {tex}\tau{/tex} = NIAB sin {tex}\theta{/tex} This makes the deflection proportional to current. In other words, the radial magnetic field makes the scale linear. The cylindrical soft iron core makes the filed radial and increases the strength of the magnetic field. i.e. the magnitude of the torque. A voltmeter is used to measure p.d. across a resistance in an electrical circuit. If a voltmeter has very high resistance, it will not affect the resistance of circuit, hence reading will be true. That is why while using a moving coil galvanometer on a voltmeter, a high resistance in series is required. An ammeter is used to measure current in circuit, hence it is connected in series with the circuit. If an ammeter has very low resistance it will not affect the circuit - resistance and so reading will be true. That is why while using a moving coil galvanometer as an ammeter, a shunt (small resistance in parallel) is used.

This makes the deflection ( {tex}\theta{/tex} ) proportional to current.To produce radial magnetic field pole pieces of permanent magnet are made cylindrical and a soft iron core is placed between them. The soft iron core helps in making the field radial and reduce energy losses produced due to eddy currents. lection of coil is directly proportional to current flowing in the coil and hence we can construct a linear scale. Importance of uniform radial magnetic field: Torque for current carrying coil in a magnetic field is {tex}\tau{/tex} = NIAB sin {tex}\theta{/tex} In radial magnetic field Sin {tex}\theta{/tex} = 1, so torque is {tex}\tau{/tex} = NIAB sin {tex}\theta{/tex} This makes the deflection proportional to current. In other words, the radial magnetic field makes the scale linear. The cylindrical soft iron core makes the filed radial and increases the strength of the magnetic field. i.e. the magnitude of the torque. A voltmeter is used to measure p.d. across a resistance in an electrical circuit. If a voltmeter has very high resistance, it will not affect the resistance of circuit, hence reading will be true. That is why while using a moving coil galvanometer on a voltmeter, a high resistance in series is required. An ammeter is used to measure current in circuit, hence it is connected in series with the circuit. If an ammeter has very low resistance it will not affect the circuit - resistance and so reading will be true. That is why while using a moving coil galvanometer as an ammeter, a shunt (small resistance in parallel) is used.
solution_html:
1. Principle and working: When current (I) is passed in the coil, torque τ acts on the coil, given by {tex}\tau{/tex} = NIABsin{tex}\theta{/tex}

    When the magnetic field is radial, as in the case of cylindrical pole pieces and soft iron core, then in every position of coil the plane of the coil, is parallel to the magnetic field lines, so that {tex}\theta{/tex} = 90o and sin 90o = 1 Deflecting torque, {tex}\tau{/tex} = NIAB
    If C is the torsional rigidity of the wire and {tex}\theta{/tex} is the twist of suspension strip, then restoring torque = C{tex}\theta{/tex} For equilibrium, deflecting torque = restoring torque
    i.e. NIAB = C{tex}\theta{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}\frac{N A B}{C}{/tex}I ...(i)
    i.e. {tex}\theta{/tex} {tex}\propto{/tex} I
  2. Importance (or function) of uniform radial magnetic field: In radial magnetic field sin {tex}\theta{/tex} = 1, so torque is {tex}\tau{/tex} = NIAB. This makes the deflection ({tex}\theta{/tex}) proportional to current.To produce radial magnetic field pole pieces of permanent magnet are made cylindrical and a soft iron core is placed between them. The soft iron core helps in making the field radial and reduce energy losses produced due to eddy currents.
  3. lection of coil is directly proportional to current flowing in the coil and hence we can construct a linear scale. Importance of uniform radial magnetic field: Torque for current carrying coil in a magnetic field is {tex}\tau{/tex} = NIAB sin{tex}\theta{/tex}
    In radial magnetic field Sin{tex}\theta{/tex} = 1, so torque is {tex}\tau{/tex} = NIAB sin{tex}\theta{/tex}
    This makes the deflection proportional to current. In other words, the radial magnetic field makes the scale linear.
    The cylindrical soft iron core makes the filed radial and increases the strength of the magnetic field. i.e. the magnitude of the torque.
  4. A voltmeter is used to measure p.d. across a resistance in an electrical circuit. If a voltmeter has very high resistance, it will not affect the resistance of circuit, hence reading will be true. That is why while using a moving coil galvanometer on a voltmeter, a high resistance in series is required. An ammeter is used to measure current in circuit, hence it is connected in series with the circuit. If an ammeter has very low resistance it will not affect the circuit - resistance and so reading will be true. That is why while using a moving coil galvanometer as an ammeter, a shunt (small resistance in parallel) is used.

question_html: Two long straight parallel conductors carry steady current I1 and I2 separated by a distance d. If the currents are flowing in the same direction, show how the magnetic field set up in one produces an attractive force on the other. Obtain the expression for this force. Hence define one ampere.
question_text: Two long straight parallel conductors carry steady current I 1 and I 2 separated by a distance d. If the currents are flowing in the same direction, show how the magnetic field set up in one produces an attractive force on the other. Obtain the expression for this force. Hence define one ampere.
explanation: F ab = Force experienced by wire 'a' of length 'l' due to the magnetic field of wire 'b'. F ba = Force experienced by wire 'b' of length 'l' due to magnetic field of wire 'a'. B a = Magnetic field due to wire 'a' B b = Magnetic field due to wire 'b' {tex}B_{a}=\frac{\mu_{0} I_{a}}{2 \pi d}{/tex} since {tex}\vec{F}=I(\vec{l} \times \vec{B}){/tex} {tex}F_{b a}=I_{2} l \frac{\mu_{0} I_{1}}{2 \pi d}{/tex} Similarly, {tex}F_{a b}=I_{1} \cdot l \frac{\mu_{0} I_{2}}{2 \pi d}{/tex} The direction of force experienced by the wire 'a' is toward the wire 'b'. (As shown in the diagram). Similarly, the direction of force experienced by the wire b is toward the wire 'a'. Thus, the force is attractive. If two long wires are placed in vacuum at a separation of 1 m, one Ampere would be defined as the current in each wire that would produce a force of 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -7 Nm - 1 per unit length of wire. or One Ampere is defined as the current that flows with electric charge of one Coulomb per second

Fab = Force experienced by wire 'a' of length 'l' due to the magnetic field of wire 'b'.
Fba = Force experienced by wire 'b' of length 'l' due to magnetic field of wire 'a'.
Ba = Magnetic field due to wire 'a'
Bb = Magnetic field due to wire 'b'
{tex}B_{a}=\frac{\mu_{0} I_{a}}{2 \pi d}{/tex}
since {tex}\vec{F}=I(\vec{l} \times \vec{B}){/tex}
{tex}F_{b a}=I_{2} l \frac{\mu_{0} I_{1}}{2 \pi d}{/tex}
Similarly, {tex}F_{a b}=I_{1} \cdot l \frac{\mu_{0} I_{2}}{2 \pi d}{/tex}

The direction of force experienced by the wire 'a' is toward the wire 'b'. (As shown in the diagram). Similarly, the direction of force experienced by the wire b is toward the wire 'a'. Thus, the force is attractive.
If two long wires are placed in vacuum at a separation of 1 m, one Ampere would be defined as the current in each wire that would produce a force of 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-7 Nm-1 per unit length of wire. or One Ampere is defined as the current that flows with electric charge of one Coulomb per second

question_html: A long straight wire of a circular cross-section·of radius a carries steady current I. The current is uniformly distributed across the cross-section. Apply Ampere's circuital law to calculate the magnetic field B(r) at a point in the region for (i) r < a and (ii) r > a. Draw a graph showing the dependence of B(r) on r.
question_text: A long straight wire of a circular cross-section·of radius a carries steady current I. The current is uniformly distributed across the cross-section. Apply Ampere's circuital law to calculate the magnetic field B(r) at a point in the region for (i) r < a and (ii) r > a. Draw a graph showing the dependence of B(r) on r.
explanation: Consider the case r < a. The Amperian loop is a circle labelled 1 (fig.) For this loop, taking the radius of the circle to be r Let this loop contains charge I e. This current enclosed I e is not I, but is less than this value. Since the current distribution is uniform, the current enclosed is {tex}I_e=I\left(\frac{\pi r^2}{\mathrm{ {tex}\pi{/tex} a}^2}\right)=I\left(\frac{r^2}{a^2}\right){/tex} Using Ampere's law, {tex}B.2\pi r=\mu_0I\left(\frac{r^2}{a^2}\right){/tex} {tex}B=\frac{\mu_0I}{2\pi}\left(\frac r{a^2}\right){/tex} So, {tex}B \propto r\;\;\;for\;r<a{/tex} Consider the case r > a. The Amperian loop, labelled 2, is a circle concentric with the cross-section. For this loop, {tex}L=2\mathrm{ {tex}\pi{/tex} r}{/tex} I e = Current enclosed by the loop = I The result is the familiar expression for a long straight wire {tex}B.2\pi r=\mu_0I{/tex} {tex}B=\frac{{\mathrm\mu}_0\mathrm I}{2\mathrm{ {tex}\pi{/tex} r}}{/tex} {tex}B\;\propto\;\frac1r {/tex} when {tex}r\;>\;a{/tex} Fig. shows a plot of the magnitude of B with distance r from the centre of the wire. The direction of the field is tangential tothe respective circular loop (1 or 2) and given by the right-hand rule.
solution: Consider the case r < a. The Amperian loop is a circle labelled 1 (fig.) For this loop, taking the radius of the circle to be r Let this loop contains charge I e. This current enclosed I e is not I, but is less than this value. Since the current distribution is uniform, the current enclosed is {tex}I_e=I\left(\frac{\pi r^2}{\mathrm{ {tex}\pi{/tex} a}^2}\right)=I\left(\frac{r^2}{a^2}\right){/tex} Using Ampere's law, {tex}B.2\pi r=\mu_0I\left(\frac{r^2}{a^2}\right){/tex} {tex}B=\frac{\mu_0I}{2\pi}\left(\frac r{a^2}\right){/tex} So, {tex}B \propto r\;\;\;for\;r<a{/tex} Consider the case r > a. The Amperian loop, labelled 2, is a circle concentric with the cross-section. For this loop, {tex}L=2\mathrm{ {tex}\pi{/tex} r}{/tex} I e = Current enclosed by the loop = I The result is the familiar expression for a long straight wire {tex}B.2\pi r=\mu_0I{/tex} {tex}B=\frac{{\mathrm\mu}_0\mathrm I}{2\mathrm{ {tex}\pi{/tex} r}}{/tex} {tex}B\;\propto\;\frac1r {/tex} when {tex}r\;>\;a{/tex} Fig. shows a plot of the magnitude of B with distance r from the centre of the wire. The direction of the field is tangential tothe respective circular loop (1 or 2) and given by the right-hand rule.
solution_html:
1. Consider the case r < a. The Amperian loop is a circle labelled 1 (fig.)

    For this loop, taking the radius of the circle to be r
    Let this loop contains charge Ie. This current enclosed Ie is not I, but is less than this value.
    Since the current distribution is uniform, the current enclosed is
    {tex}I_e=I\left(\frac{\pi r^2}{\mathrm{{tex}\pi{/tex}a}^2}\right)=I\left(\frac{r^2}{a^2}\right){/tex}
    Using Ampere's law,
    {tex}B.2\pi r=\mu_0I\left(\frac{r^2}{a^2}\right){/tex}
    {tex}B=\frac{\mu_0I}{2\pi}\left(\frac r{a^2}\right){/tex}
    So, {tex}B \propto r\;\;\;for\;r<a{/tex}

  2. Consider the case r > a. The Amperian loop, labelled 2, is a circle concentric with the cross-section. For this loop,
    {tex}L=2\mathrm{{tex}\pi{/tex}r}{/tex}
    Ie = Current enclosed by the loop = I
    The result is the familiar expression for a long straight wire
    {tex}B.2\pi r=\mu_0I{/tex}
    {tex}B=\frac{{\mathrm\mu}_0\mathrm I}{2\mathrm{{tex}\pi{/tex}r}}{/tex}
    {tex}B\;\propto\;\frac1r {/tex}  when {tex}r\;>\;a{/tex}

    Fig. shows a plot of the magnitude of B with distance r from the centre of the wire. The direction of the field is tangential tothe respective circular loop (1 or 2) and given by the right-hand rule.

question_html:
1. A straight thick long wire of uniform cross-section of radius a is carrying a steady current I. Use Ampere's circuital law to obtain a relation showing the variation of the magnetic field (Br) inside and outside the wire with distance r, (r {tex}\leq{/tex} a) and (r > a) of the field point from the centre of its cross-section. Plot a graph showing the variation of field B with distance r.
  2. Calculate the ratio of the magnetic field at a point {tex}\frac{a}{2}{/tex} above the surface of the wire to that at a point {tex}\frac{a}{2}{/tex} below its surface. What is the maximum value of the field of this wire?
question_text: A straight thick long wire of uniform cross-section of radius a is carrying a steady current I. Use Ampere's circuital law to obtain a relation showing the variation of the magnetic field (B r ) inside and outside the wire with distance r, (r {tex}\leq{/tex} a) and (r > a) of the field point from the centre of its cross-section. Plot a graph showing the variation of field B with distance r. Calculate the ratio of the magnetic field at a point {tex}\frac{a}{2}{/tex} above the surface of the wire to that at a point {tex}\frac{a}{2}{/tex} below its surface. What is the maximum value of the field of this wire?
explanation: Application of Ampere's law to a long straight cylindrical wire: By symmetry, the magnetic lines of force will be circles, with their centres on the axis of the cylinder and in planes perpendicular to the axis of the cylinder. So we consider Amperean loop as a circle of radius r. The field at outside points. The Amperean loop is a circle labelled 2 having radius r > a. Length of the loop, L = 2 {tex}\pi{/tex} r Net current enclosed by the loop = I By Ampere's circuital law, BL= {tex}\mu_{0} I{/tex} or {tex}B \times 2 \pi r=\mu_{0} I{/tex} or B = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I}{2 \pi r}{/tex} ...[for r > a] i.e., {tex}B \propto \frac{1}{r}{/tex} ...[For outside points] Field at inside points. The Amperean loop is a circle labelled 1 with r < a. Length of the loop, L = 2 {tex}\pi{/tex} r Clearly, the current enclosed by loop 1 is less than As the current distribution is uniform, the fraction of I enclosed is I' = {tex}\frac{I}{\pi a^{2}} \times \pi r^{2}=\frac{I r^{2}}{a^{2}}{/tex} Applying Ampere's law, BL = {tex}\mu_{0} I^{\prime}{/tex} or {tex}B \times 2 \pi r=\mu_{0} \frac{I r^{2}}{a^{2}}{/tex} or B = {tex}\left(\frac{\mu_{0} I}{2 \pi a^{2}}\right) r{/tex} ...[for r > a] i.e., B {tex}\propto{/tex} r ...[For inside points] Thus the field B is proportional to r as we move from the axis of the cylinder towards its surface and then it decreases as {tex}\frac{1}{r}{/tex} . The variation of B with distance r from the centre of the wire is shown in given figure Above diagram is the sketch of the magnitude of the magnetic field for the long conductor of radius a. Suppose the point P lies at distance {tex}\frac a2{/tex} above the surface of the wire and point Q lies at distance {tex}\frac a2{/tex} below the surface as shown in below figure. Magnetic field at point P at distance r = {tex}\frac{3a}{2}{/tex} from the axis of the wire is B P = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I}{2 \pi r}=\frac{\mu_{0} I}{2 \pi(\frac 3 2) a}=\frac{\mu_{0} I}{3 \pi a}{/tex} Magnetic field at point Q at distance r = {tex}\frac{a}{2}{/tex} from the axis of the wire is B Q = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I r}{2 \pi a^{2}}=\frac{\mu_{0} I}{2 \pi a^{2}}\left(\frac{a}{2}\right)=\frac{\mu_{0} I}{4 \pi a}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{B_{P}}{B_{Q}}=\frac{\mu_{0} I}{3 \pi a} \times \frac{4 \pi a}{\mu_{0} I}{/tex} = 4 : 3 Clearly, B is maximum on the surface of the wire i.e., at r = a. Hence, B max = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I}{2 \pi a}{/tex}

So we consider Amperean loop as a circle of radius r. The field at outside points. The Amperean loop is a circle labelled 2 having radius r > a. The Amperean loop is a circle labelled 1 with r < a. Length of the loop, L = 2 {tex}\pi{/tex} r Clearly, the current enclosed by loop 1 is less than As the current distribution is uniform, the fraction of I enclosed is I' = {tex}\frac{I}{\pi a^{2}} \times \pi r^{2}=\frac{I r^{2}}{a^{2}}{/tex} Applying Ampere's law, BL = {tex}\mu_{0} I^{\prime}{/tex} or {tex}B \times 2 \pi r=\mu_{0} \frac{I r^{2}}{a^{2}}{/tex} or B = {tex}\left(\frac{\mu_{0} I}{2 \pi a^{2}}\right) r{/tex} ...[for r > a] i.e., B {tex}\propto{/tex} r ...[For inside points] Thus the field B is proportional to r as we move from the axis of the cylinder towards its surface and then it decreases as {tex}\frac{1}{r}{/tex} . The variation of B with distance r from the centre of the wire is shown in given figure Above diagram is the sketch of the magnitude of the magnetic field for the long conductor of radius a. Suppose the point P lies at distance {tex}\frac a2{/tex} above the surface of the wire and point Q lies at distance {tex}\frac a2{/tex} below the surface as shown in below figure. Magnetic field at point P at distance r = {tex}\frac{3a}{2}{/tex} from the axis of the wire is B P = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I}{2 \pi r}=\frac{\mu_{0} I}{2 \pi(\frac 3 2) a}=\frac{\mu_{0} I}{3 \pi a}{/tex} Magnetic field at point Q at distance r = {tex}\frac{a}{2}{/tex} from the axis of the wire is B Q = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I r}{2 \pi a^{2}}=\frac{\mu_{0} I}{2 \pi a^{2}}\left(\frac{a}{2}\right)=\frac{\mu_{0} I}{4 \pi a}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{B_{P}}{B_{Q}}=\frac{\mu_{0} I}{3 \pi a} \times \frac{4 \pi a}{\mu_{0} I}{/tex} = 4 : 3 Clearly, B is maximum on the surface of the wire i.e., at r = a. Hence, B max = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I}{2 \pi a}{/tex}
solution_html:
1. Application of Ampere's law to a long straight cylindrical wire: By symmetry, the magnetic lines of force will be circles, with their centres on the axis of the cylinder and in planes perpendicular to the axis of the cylinder. So we consider Amperean loop as a circle of radius r.
    The field at outside points. The Amperean loop is a circle labelled 2 having radius r > a.
    Length of the loop, L = 2{tex}\pi{/tex}r
    Net current enclosed by the loop = I
    By Ampere's circuital law,
    BL= {tex}\mu_{0} I{/tex} 
    or {tex}B \times 2 \pi r=\mu_{0} I{/tex}
    or B = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I}{2 \pi r}{/tex} ...[for r > a]
    i.e., {tex}B \propto \frac{1}{r}{/tex} ...[For outside points]
    Field at inside points. The Amperean loop is a circle labelled 1 with r < a.
    Length of the loop, L = 2{tex}\pi{/tex}r
    Clearly, the current enclosed by loop 1 is less than As the current distribution is uniform, the fraction of I enclosed is
    I' = {tex}\frac{I}{\pi a^{2}} \times \pi r^{2}=\frac{I r^{2}}{a^{2}}{/tex}
    Applying Ampere's law,
    BL = {tex}\mu_{0} I^{\prime}{/tex}
    or {tex}B \times 2 \pi r=\mu_{0} \frac{I r^{2}}{a^{2}}{/tex}
    or B = {tex}\left(\frac{\mu_{0} I}{2 \pi a^{2}}\right) r{/tex} ...[for r > a]
    i.e., B {tex}\propto{/tex} r ...[For inside points]
    Thus the field B is proportional to r as we move from the axis of the cylinder towards its surface and then it decreases as {tex}\frac{1}{r}{/tex}. The variation of B with distance r from the centre of the wire is shown in given figure

    Above diagram is the sketch of the magnitude of the magnetic field for the long conductor of radius a.
  2. Suppose the point P lies at distance {tex}\frac a2{/tex} above the surface of the wire and point Q lies at distance {tex}\frac a2{/tex} below the surface as shown in below figure.

    Magnetic field at point P at distance r = {tex}\frac{3a}{2}{/tex} from the axis of the wire is
    BP = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I}{2 \pi r}=\frac{\mu_{0} I}{2 \pi(\frac 3 2) a}=\frac{\mu_{0} I}{3 \pi a}{/tex}
    Magnetic field at point Q at distance r = {tex}\frac{a}{2}{/tex} from the axis of the wire is
    BQ = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I r}{2 \pi a^{2}}=\frac{\mu_{0} I}{2 \pi a^{2}}\left(\frac{a}{2}\right)=\frac{\mu_{0} I}{4 \pi a}{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{B_{P}}{B_{Q}}=\frac{\mu_{0} I}{3 \pi a} \times \frac{4 \pi a}{\mu_{0} I}{/tex} = 4 : 3
    Clearly, B is maximum on the surface of the wire i.e., at r = a. Hence,
    Bmax = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I}{2 \pi a}{/tex}

question_html: A circular coil, having 100 turns of wire, of the radius (nearly) 20 cm each, lies in the XY plane with its centre at the origin of coordinates. Find the magnetic field at the point (0, 0, {tex}20\sqrt 3{/tex} cm) when this coil carries a current of {tex}\left(\frac{2}{\pi}\right){/tex} A.
question_text: A circular coil, having 100 turns of wire, of the radius (nearly) 20 cm each, lies in the XY plane with its centre at the origin of coordinates. Find the magnetic field at the point (0, 0, {tex}20\sqrt 3{/tex} cm) when this coil carries a current of {tex}\left(\frac{2}{\pi}\right){/tex} A.
explanation: N=100 turns and radius= 0.20 cm .The plane of the coil is XY plane and the field point is on the Z-axis. {tex}\therefore {/tex} The magnetic field on the axial point B = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I R^{2} N}{2\left(R^{2}+z^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times \frac{2}{\pi} \times(0.2)^{2} \times 100}{2\left[(0.2)^{2}+(0.2 \sqrt{3})^{2}\right]^{3 / 2}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{8 \times 0.04 \times 10^{-7} \times 100}{2 \times 0.04 \times 8 \times 0.2}{/tex} = 25 {tex}\mu {/tex} T

question_html: Write the expression for the magnetic force {tex}\vec F{/tex} acting on a charged particle q moving with velocity v in the presence of the magnetic field {tex}\vec B{/tex} in a vector form. Show that no work is done and no change in the magnitude of the velocity of the particle is produced by this force. Hence, define the unit of the magnetic field.
question_text: Write the expression for the magnetic force {tex}\vec F{/tex} acting on a charged particle q moving with velocity v in the presence of the magnetic field {tex}\vec B{/tex} in a vector form. Show that no work is done and no change in the magnitude of the velocity of the particle is produced by this force. Hence, define the unit of the magnetic field.
explanation: The required expression is {tex}\vec{F}=q(\vec{v} \times \vec{B}){/tex} The magnetic force, at all instants, is, therefore, perpendicular to the instantaneous direction of {tex}\vec v{/tex} , which is also the instantaneous direction of displacement ( {tex}\vec {ds}{/tex} ). Since F is perpendicular to ( {tex}\vec {ds}{/tex} ), at all instants, work done =( {tex}\vec F.\vec {ds}{/tex} ) as cos90 0 is zero. There being no work done, there can be no change in the magnitude of {tex}\vec v{/tex} . From {tex}\vec{F}=q(\vec{v} \times \vec{B}){/tex} we get {tex}|\vec{F}|{/tex} = qvB sin {tex}\theta{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} F = B if q = 1, v = 1 and {tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} Hence, the magnetic field, {tex}\vec B{/tex} at a point equals one tesla if a charge of one coulomb, moving with a velocity of 1 m/s, along a direction perpendicular to the direction of {tex}\vec B{/tex} , experiences a force of one newton.

Since F is perpendicular to ( {tex}\vec {ds}{/tex} ), at all instants, work done =( {tex}\vec F.\vec {ds}{/tex} ) as cos90 0 is zero. There being no work done, there can be no change in the magnitude of {tex}\vec v{/tex} . From {tex}\vec{F}=q(\vec{v} \times \vec{B}){/tex} we get {tex}|\vec{F}|{/tex} = qvB sin {tex}\theta{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} F = B if q = 1, v = 1 and {tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} Hence, the magnetic field, {tex}\vec B{/tex} at a point equals one tesla if a charge of one coulomb, moving with a velocity of 1 m/s, along a direction perpendicular to the direction of {tex}\vec B{/tex} , experiences a force of one newton.
