There being no work done, there can be no change in the magnitude of {tex}\vec v{/tex}.
From {tex}\vec{F}=q(\vec{v} \times \vec{B}){/tex} we get
{tex}|\vec{F}|{/tex}  = qvB sin {tex}\theta{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} F = B if q = 1, v = 1 and {tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex}
Hence, the magnetic field, {tex}\vec B{/tex} at a point equals one tesla if a charge of one coulomb, moving with a velocity of 1 m/s, along a direction perpendicular to the direction of {tex}\vec B{/tex}, experiences a force of one newton.

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. Express Biot-Savart law in the vector form.
  2. Use it to obtain the expression for the magnetic field at an axial point, distanced from the centre of a circular coil of radius R carrying current I.
  3.  Also, find the ratio of the magnitudes of the magnetic field of this coil at the centre and at an axial point for which x = R{tex}\sqrt 3{/tex}.
question_text: Express Biot-Savart law in the vector form. Use it to obtain the expression for the magnetic field at an axial point, distanced from the centre of a circular coil of radius R carrying current I. Also, find the ratio of the magnitudes of the magnetic field of this coil at the centre and at an axial point for which x = R {tex}\sqrt 3{/tex} .
explanation: According to Biot savart law magnetic field inversely proportional to the square of the distance r. Its direction is perpendicular to the plane containing {tex}\vec {dl}{/tex} and {tex}\vec r{/tex} . In vector notation, {tex}\overrightarrow{d B}=\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} I \frac{\overrightarrow{d I} \times \vec{r}}{r^{3}}{/tex} We have, {tex}\overrightarrow{d B} =\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} I \frac{|\overrightarrow{d I} \times \vec{r}|}{r^{3}} {/tex} r 2 = x 2 + R 2 {tex}\therefore{/tex} dB = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I}{4 \pi} \frac{d l}{\left(x^{2}+R^{2}\right)^{}}{/tex} We need to add only the components of dB along the axis of the coil. Hence, B = {tex}\int \frac{\mu_{0} }{4 \pi} \cdot \frac{I d l}{\left(x^{2}+R^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}} \cos \theta{/tex} and cos {tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}R\over(x^2+R^2)^{1/2}{/tex} = {tex}\int \frac{\mu_{0} }{4 \pi} \frac{(I d l) R}{\left(x^{2}+R^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}}{/tex} integrate over 0 to {tex}2\pi R{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I R^{2}}{2\left(x^{2}+R^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \vec{B} =\frac{\mu_{0} I R^{2}}{2\left(x^{2}+R^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}} \hat{i}{/tex} At centre of coil: x = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} B 1 = {tex}\frac{\mu_o i R^{2}}{2 R^{3}}=\frac{\mu_o i}{2 R}{/tex} At R {tex}\sqrt 3{/tex} = x {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} B 2 = {tex}\frac{\mu_o i R^{2}}{2\left(R^{2}+3 R^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mu_o i R^{2}}{2\left(4 R^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}}=\frac{\mu_oi R^{2}}{2(2 R)^{3}}=\frac{\mu_o i R^{2}}{16R^{3}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mu_o i}{16 R}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} B 1 : B 2 = 8 : 1
solution: According to Biot savart law magnetic field inversely proportional to the square of the distance r. Its direction is perpendicular to the plane containing {tex}\vec {dl}{/tex} and {tex}\vec r{/tex} . In vector notation, {tex}\overrightarrow{d B}=\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} I \frac{\overrightarrow{d I} \times \vec{r}}{r^{3}}{/tex} We have, {tex}\overrightarrow{d B} =\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} I \frac{|\overrightarrow{d I} \times \vec{r}|}{r^{3}} {/tex} r 2 = x 2 + R 2 {tex}\therefore{/tex} dB = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I}{4 \pi} \frac{d l}{\left(x^{2}+R^{2}\right)^{}}{/tex} We need to add only the components of dB along the axis of the coil. Hence, B = {tex}\int \frac{\mu_{0} }{4 \pi} \cdot \frac{I d l}{\left(x^{2}+R^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}} \cos \theta{/tex} and cos {tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}R\over(x^2+R^2)^{1/2}{/tex} = {tex}\int \frac{\mu_{0} }{4 \pi} \frac{(I d l) R}{\left(x^{2}+R^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}}{/tex} integrate over 0 to {tex}2\pi R{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I R^{2}}{2\left(x^{2}+R^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \vec{B} =\frac{\mu_{0} I R^{2}}{2\left(x^{2}+R^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}} \hat{i}{/tex} At centre of coil: x = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} B 1 = {tex}\frac{\mu_o i R^{2}}{2 R^{3}}=\frac{\mu_o i}{2 R}{/tex} At R {tex}\sqrt 3{/tex} = x {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} B 2 = {tex}\frac{\mu_o i R^{2}}{2\left(R^{2}+3 R^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mu_o i R^{2}}{2\left(4 R^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}}=\frac{\mu_oi R^{2}}{2(2 R)^{3}}=\frac{\mu_o i R^{2}}{16R^{3}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mu_o i}{16 R}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} B 1 : B 2 = 8 : 1
solution_html:
1.  According to Biot savart law magnetic field inversely proportional to the square of the distance r. Its direction is perpendicular to the plane containing {tex}\vec {dl}{/tex} and {tex}\vec r{/tex}.
    In vector notation,  {tex}\overrightarrow{d B}=\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} I \frac{\overrightarrow{d I} \times \vec{r}}{r^{3}}{/tex}

  2. We have, {tex}\overrightarrow{d B} =\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} I \frac{|\overrightarrow{d I} \times \vec{r}|}{r^{3}} {/tex}
    r2 = x2 + R2
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} dB = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I}{4 \pi} \frac{d l}{\left(x^{2}+R^{2}\right)^{}}{/tex}
    We need to add only the components of dB along the axis of the coil.
    Hence, B = {tex}\int \frac{\mu_{0} }{4 \pi} \cdot \frac{I d l}{\left(x^{2}+R^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}} \cos \theta{/tex} and cos{tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}R\over(x^2+R^2)^{1/2}{/tex}
    = {tex}\int \frac{\mu_{0} }{4 \pi} \frac{(I d l) R}{\left(x^{2}+R^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}}{/tex} integrate over 0 to {tex}2\pi R{/tex}
    = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I R^{2}}{2\left(x^{2}+R^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}}{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore \vec{B} =\frac{\mu_{0} I R^{2}}{2\left(x^{2}+R^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}} \hat{i}{/tex}
  3. At centre of coil: x = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} B1 = {tex}\frac{\mu_o i R^{2}}{2 R^{3}}=\frac{\mu_o i}{2 R}{/tex}
    At R{tex}\sqrt 3{/tex} = x {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} B2 = {tex}\frac{\mu_o i R^{2}}{2\left(R^{2}+3 R^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mu_o i R^{2}}{2\left(4 R^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}}=\frac{\mu_oi R^{2}}{2(2 R)^{3}}=\frac{\mu_o i R^{2}}{16R^{3}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mu_o i}{16 R}{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} B1 : B2 = 8 : 1

subject: Physics
question_html: Why is an ammeter connected in series in a circuit?
question_text: Why is an ammeter connected in series in a circuit?
explanation: An ammeter is connected in series in a circuit so that whole of the current, which it is required to measure, passes through it. Moreover, an ammeter has a low resistance, so its insertion in the series circuit does not practically change the main current.
solution: An ammeter is connected in series in a circuit so that whole of the current, which it is required to measure, passes through it. Moreover, an ammeter has a low resistance, so its insertion in the series circuit does not practically change the main current.
solution_html: An ammeter is connected in series in a circuit so that whole of the current, which it is required to measure, passes through it. Moreover, an ammeter has a low resistance, so its insertion in the series circuit does not practically change the main current.

subject: Physics
question_html: Why is a voltmeter always connected in parallel with a circuit element across which voltage is to be measured?
question_text: Why is a voltmeter always connected in parallel with a circuit element across which voltage is to be measured?
explanation: A voltmeter is a high resistance galvanometer. When it is connected in parallel across any element of a circuit, it draws a very small current from the main circuit. Most of the current pass through that element. Hence potential difference across that component is not affected materially.
solution: A voltmeter is a high resistance galvanometer. When it is connected in parallel across any element of a circuit, it draws a very small current from the main circuit. Most of the current pass through that element. Hence potential difference across that component is not affected materially.
solution_html: A voltmeter is a high resistance galvanometer. When it is connected in parallel across any element of a circuit, it draws a very small current from the main circuit. Most of the current pass through that element. Hence potential difference across that component is not affected materially.

question_text: A neutron, an electron and an alpha-particle moving with equal velocities enter a uniform magnetic field going into the plane of the paper as shown in Figure. Trace their paths in the field and justify your answer.
explanation: Neutrons, being neutral, are not deflected by the magnetic field. Alpha particles and electrons will tend to move along circular paths in opposite directions as shown in Figure, in accordance with Fleming's left-hand rule.
solution: Neutrons, being neutral, are not deflected by the magnetic field. Alpha particles and electrons will tend to move along circular paths in opposite directions as shown in Figure, in accordance with Fleming's left-hand rule.

question_text: A long straight wire of radius carries a steady current I. The current is uniformly distributed over its cross-section. The ratio of the magnetic fields B and B' at radial distances {tex}\frac{a}{2}{/tex} and 2a respectively, from the axis of the wire is:

options.explanation: We use Ampere's circuital law. For loop 1: {tex}B_{1} \times 2 \pi \frac{a}{2}=\mu_{0} \frac{I}{\pi a^{2}} \times \frac{\pi a^{2}}{4}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \ B_{1}=\frac{\mu_{0} I}{4 \pi a}{/tex} For loop 2: {tex}B_{2} \times 2 \pi(2 a)=\mu_{0} I{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \ B_{2}=\frac{\mu_{0} I}{4 \pi a}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{B_{1}}{B_{2}}=1{/tex}

explanation: We use Ampere's circuital law. For loop 1: {tex}B_{1} \times 2 \pi \frac{a}{2}=\mu_{0} \frac{I}{\pi a^{2}} \times \frac{\pi a^{2}}{4}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \ B_{1}=\frac{\mu_{0} I}{4 \pi a}{/tex} For loop 2: {tex}B_{2} \times 2 \pi(2 a)=\mu_{0} I{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \ B_{2}=\frac{\mu_{0} I}{4 \pi a}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{B_{1}}{B_{2}}=1{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html: A long wire is bent into a circular coil of one turn and then into a circular coil of a smaller radius having n turns. If the same current passes in both cases find the ratio of the magnetic fields produced at the centres in the two cases.
question_text: A long wire is bent into a circular coil of one turn and then into a circular coil of a smaller radius having n turns. If the same current passes in both cases find the ratio of the magnetic fields produced at the centres in the two cases.
explanation: Let l be the length of the wire. When the wire is first bent in the form of one turn circular coil, {tex}l=2 \pi r_{1}{/tex} or {tex}r_{1}=\frac{l}{2 \pi}, N=1{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad B_{1}=\frac{\mu_{0} N I}{2 r_{1}}=\frac{\mu_{0} \times 1 \times I}{2 \times(l / 2 \pi)}=\frac{\mu_{0} \pi l}{l}{/tex} When the wire is bent in the form of n-turn coil, {tex}l=n \times 2 \pi r_{2} \text { or } r_{2}=\frac{l}{2 n \pi}, N=n{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad B_{2}=\frac{\mu_{0} n I}{2(l / 2 n \pi)}=\frac{\mu_{0} \pi n^{2} I}{l}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad \frac{B_{1}}{B_{2}}=\frac{1}{n^{2}}=1: n^{2}{/tex}
solution: Let l be the length of the wire. When the wire is first bent in the form of one turn circular coil, {tex}l=2 \pi r_{1}{/tex} or {tex}r_{1}=\frac{l}{2 \pi}, N=1{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad B_{1}=\frac{\mu_{0} N I}{2 r_{1}}=\frac{\mu_{0} \times 1 \times I}{2 \times(l / 2 \pi)}=\frac{\mu_{0} \pi l}{l}{/tex} When the wire is bent in the form of n-turn coil, {tex}l=n \times 2 \pi r_{2} \text { or } r_{2}=\frac{l}{2 n \pi}, N=n{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad B_{2}=\frac{\mu_{0} n I}{2(l / 2 n \pi)}=\frac{\mu_{0} \pi n^{2} I}{l}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad \frac{B_{1}}{B_{2}}=\frac{1}{n^{2}}=1: n^{2}{/tex}
solution_html:
Let l be the length of the wire. When the wire is first bent in the form of one turn circular coil, {tex}l=2 \pi r_{1}{/tex} or {tex}r_{1}=\frac{l}{2 \pi}, N=1{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad B_{1}=\frac{\mu_{0} N I}{2 r_{1}}=\frac{\mu_{0} \times 1 \times I}{2 \times(l / 2 \pi)}=\frac{\mu_{0} \pi l}{l}{/tex}
When the wire is bent in the form of n-turn coil,
{tex}l=n \times 2 \pi r_{2} \text { or } r_{2}=\frac{l}{2 n \pi}, N=n{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad B_{2}=\frac{\mu_{0} n I}{2(l / 2 n \pi)}=\frac{\mu_{0} \pi n^{2} I}{l}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad \frac{B_{1}}{B_{2}}=\frac{1}{n^{2}}=1: n^{2}{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. State Ampere's circuital law connecting the line integral of B over a closed path to the net current crossing the area bounded by the path.
  2. Use Ampere's law to derive the formula for the magnetic field due to an infinitely long straight current carrying wire.
  3. Explain carefully why the derivation as in (b) is not valid for magnetic field in a plane normal to a current-carrying straight wire of finite length and passing through the midpoint of the axis.
question_text: State Ampere's circuital law connecting the line integral of B over a closed path to the net current crossing the area bounded by the path. Use Ampere's law to derive the formula for the magnetic field due to an infinitely long straight current carrying wire. Explain carefully why the derivation as in (b) is not valid for magnetic field in a plane normal to a current-carrying straight wire of finite length and passing through the midpoint of the axis.
explanation: Ampere's Circuital Law: It gives the relationship between the current and the magnetic field created by it. This law says that the integral of magnetic field density (B) along an imaginary closed path is equal to the product of current enclosed by the path and permeability of the medium. Now, from Ampere’s Circuital law, {tex}\int{/tex} B.dI = {tex}\mu_0l{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\int{/tex} B.dl = {tex}\mu_0l{/tex} [Since angle between B and dl is zero] {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} B {tex}\int{/tex} dl = {tex}\mu_0l{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} B.2 {tex}\pi{/tex} r = {tex}\mu_0l{/tex} [Since the total length of dl is equal to circumference of the circle considered] {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} B = {tex}\frac{\mu_0l}{2\pi r}{/tex} which is the required expression for magnetic field of induction due to a long straight current carrying wire. A straight conductor of finite length cannot by itself form a complete, steady current circuit. Additional conductors are necessary to close the circuit. These will spoil the symmetry of the problem. The difficulty disappears if the conductor is infinitely long.
solution: Ampere's Circuital Law: It gives the relationship between the current and the magnetic field created by it. This law says that the integral of magnetic field density (B) along an imaginary closed path is equal to the product of current enclosed by the path and permeability of the medium. Now, from Ampere’s Circuital law, {tex}\int{/tex} B.dI = {tex}\mu_0l{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\int{/tex} B.dl = {tex}\mu_0l{/tex} [Since angle between B and dl is zero] {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} B {tex}\int{/tex} dl = {tex}\mu_0l{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} B.2 {tex}\pi{/tex} r = {tex}\mu_0l{/tex} [Since the total length of dl is equal to circumference of the circle considered] {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} B = {tex}\frac{\mu_0l}{2\pi r}{/tex} which is the required expression for magnetic field of induction due to a long straight current carrying wire. A straight conductor of finite length cannot by itself form a complete, steady current circuit. Additional conductors are necessary to close the circuit. These will spoil the symmetry of the problem. The difficulty disappears if the conductor is infinitely long.
solution_html:
1. Ampere's Circuital Law: It gives the relationship between the current and the magnetic field created by it. This law says that the integral of magnetic field density (B) along an imaginary closed path is equal to the product of current enclosed by the path and permeability of the medium.
  2. Now, from Ampere’s Circuital law, {tex}\int{/tex}B.dI = {tex}\mu_0l{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\int{/tex}B.dl = {tex}\mu_0l{/tex} [Since angle between B and dl is zero]
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} B{tex}\int{/tex}dl = {tex}\mu_0l{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} B.2{tex}\pi{/tex}r = {tex}\mu_0l{/tex} [Since the total length of dl is equal to circumference of the circle considered]
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} B ={tex}\frac{\mu_0l}{2\pi r}{/tex} which is the required expression for magnetic field of induction due to a long straight current carrying wire.
  3. A straight conductor of finite length cannot by itself form a complete, steady current circuit. Additional conductors are necessary to close the circuit. These will spoil the symmetry of the problem. The difficulty disappears if the conductor is infinitely long.

question_html:
1. A point charge q moving with speed v v enters a uniform magnetic field B that is acting into the plane of the paper as shown. What is the path followed by the charge q and in which plane does it move?
  2. How does the path followed by the charge get affected if its velocity has a component parallel to B?
  3. If an electric {tex}\vec{E}{/tex} is also applied such that the particle continues moving along the original straight line path, what would be the magnitude and direction of the electric field {tex}\vec{E}{/tex} ?
question_text: A point charge q moving with speed v v enters a uniform magnetic field B that is acting into the plane of the paper as shown. What is the path followed by the charge q and in which plane does it move? How does the path followed by the charge get affected if its velocity has a component parallel to B? If an electric {tex}\vec{E}{/tex} is also applied such that the particle continues moving along the original straight line path, what would be the magnitude and direction of the electric field {tex}\vec{E}{/tex} ?
explanation: The charge q moves in the XY plane describing a circular path in the anticlockwise sense. The path of the charged particle will become helical. By Fleming's left hand rule, the magnetic Lorentz force acts on charge q in -Y direction {tex}\therefore{/tex} Applied electric field should be in the + Y direction to make the charged particle move along the original straight line path. F e = F m {tex}q E=q v B \sin 90^{\circ} \quad \text { or } \quad E=v B{/tex}
solution: The charge q moves in the XY plane describing a circular path in the anticlockwise sense. The path of the charged particle will become helical. By Fleming's left hand rule, the magnetic Lorentz force acts on charge q in -Y direction {tex}\therefore{/tex} Applied electric field should be in the + Y direction to make the charged particle move along the original straight line path. F e = F m {tex}q E=q v B \sin 90^{\circ} \quad \text { or } \quad E=v B{/tex}
solution_html:
1. The charge q moves in the XY plane describing a circular path in the anticlockwise sense.
  2. The path of the charged particle will become helical.
  3. By Fleming's left hand rule, the magnetic Lorentz force acts on charge q in -Y direction
    {tex}\therefore{/tex}Applied electric field should be in the + Y direction to make the charged particle move along the original straight line path.
    Fe = Fm
    {tex}q E=q v B \sin 90^{\circ} \quad \text { or } \quad E=v B{/tex}

question_html:
Three long straight parallel wires are kept as shown in Fig. The wire (3) carries a current I

  1. The direction of flow of current I in wire (3), is such that the net force, on wire (1), due to the other two wires, is zero.
  2. By reversing the direction of I, the net force, on wire (2), due to the other two wires, becomes zero. What will be the directions of current 7, in the two cases? Also obtain the relation between the magnitudes of currents I1, I2 and I
question_text: Three long straight parallel wires are kept as shown in Fig. The wire (3) carries a current I The direction of flow of current I in wire (3), is such that the net force, on wire (1), due to the other two wires, is zero. By reversing the direction of I, the net force, on wire (2), due to the other two wires, becomes zero. What will be the directions of current 7, in the two cases? Also obtain the relation between the magnitudes of currents I 1 , I 2 and I
explanation: The net force on wire (1) will be zero only when the forces exerted by wires (2) and (3) on wire (1) are equal and opposite. For this, the current in wire (3) must flow in a direction opposite to I 2 ie., downwards or along -Y direction. {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I_{1} I}{2 \pi(2 a)}=\frac{\mu_{0} I_{1} I_{2}}{2 \pi(a)} \quad \therefore \quad I_{1}=2 I_{2}{/tex} When the current in the wire (3) is reversed, I flow upwards or along +Y direction. The net force on wire (2) will become zero when the forces exerted by wires (1) and (3) are equal and opposite. {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} L_{1} I_{2}}{2 \pi a}=\frac{\mu_{0} I_{2} I}{2 \pi a} \quad \therefore \quad I=I_{1}=2 I_{2}{/tex}
solution: The net force on wire (1) will be zero only when the forces exerted by wires (2) and (3) on wire (1) are equal and opposite. For this, the current in wire (3) must flow in a direction opposite to I 2 ie., downwards or along -Y direction. {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I_{1} I}{2 \pi(2 a)}=\frac{\mu_{0} I_{1} I_{2}}{2 \pi(a)} \quad \therefore \quad I_{1}=2 I_{2}{/tex} When the current in the wire (3) is reversed, I flow upwards or along +Y direction. The net force on wire (2) will become zero when the forces exerted by wires (1) and (3) are equal and opposite. {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} L_{1} I_{2}}{2 \pi a}=\frac{\mu_{0} I_{2} I}{2 \pi a} \quad \therefore \quad I=I_{1}=2 I_{2}{/tex}
solution_html:
1. The net force on wire (1) will be zero only when the forces exerted by wires (2) and (3) on wire (1) are equal and opposite. For this, the current in wire (3) must flow in a direction opposite to I2 ie., downwards or along -Y direction.
    {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I_{1} I}{2 \pi(2 a)}=\frac{\mu_{0} I_{1} I_{2}}{2 \pi(a)} \quad \therefore \quad I_{1}=2 I_{2}{/tex}
  2. When the current in the wire (3) is reversed, I flow upwards or along +Y direction. The net force on wire (2) will become zero when the forces exerted by wires (1) and (3) are equal and opposite.
    {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} L_{1} I_{2}}{2 \pi a}=\frac{\mu_{0} I_{2} I}{2 \pi a} \quad \therefore \quad I=I_{1}=2 I_{2}{/tex}

question_html:
1. State Ampere's circuital law. Use this law to obtain the expression for the magnetic field inside an air cored toroid of average radius 'r', having 'n' turns per unit length and carrying a steady current I. Show that the magnetic field in the open space inside and exterior to the toroid is zero.
  2. An observer to the left of a solenoid of N turns each of cross-section area 'A' observes that a steady current I in it flows in the clockwise direction. Depict the magnetic field lines due to the solenoid specifying its polarity and show that it acts as a bar magnet of magnetic ll moment m = NIA.
question_text: State Ampere's circuital law. Use this law to obtain the expression for the magnetic field inside an air cored toroid of average radius 'r', having 'n' turns per unit length and carrying a steady current I. Show that the magnetic field in the open space inside and exterior to the toroid is zero. An observer to the left of a solenoid of N turns each of cross-section area 'A' observes that a steady current I in it flows in the clockwise direction. Depict the magnetic field lines due to the solenoid specifying its polarity and show that it acts as a bar magnet of magnetic ll moment m = NIA.
explanation: Ampere's circuital law states that “the line integral of the magnetic field surrounding closed-loop equals to the number of times the algebraic sum of currents passing through the loop. For points in the open space inside the toroid: No current flows through the Amperian loop, so I = 0 {tex}\oint \vec{B} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{dl}}{/tex} = {tex}\mu_0{/tex} I = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} |B| inside = 0 For points in the open space exterior to the toroid: The net current entering the plane of the toroid is exactly cancelled by the net current leaving the plane of the toroid. {tex}\oint \vec{B} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{dl}}{/tex} = {tex}\mu_0{/tex} I = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} |B| exterior = 0 For observer, current is flowing in clockwise direction hence we will see magnetic field lines going towards South Pole. The solenoid can be regarded as a combination of circular loops placed side by side, each behaving like a magnet of magnetic moment IA, where I is the current and A area of the loop. These magnets neutralise each other except at the ends where south and north poles appear. Magnetic moment of bar magnet = NIA
solution: Ampere's circuital law states that “the line integral of the magnetic field surrounding closed-loop equals to the number of times the algebraic sum of currents passing through the loop. For points in the open space inside the toroid: No current flows through the Amperian loop, so I = 0 {tex}\oint \vec{B} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{dl}}{/tex} = {tex}\mu_0{/tex} I = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} |B| inside = 0 For points in the open space exterior to the toroid: The net current entering the plane of the toroid is exactly cancelled by the net current leaving the plane of the toroid. {tex}\oint \vec{B} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{dl}}{/tex} = {tex}\mu_0{/tex} I = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} |B| exterior = 0 For observer, current is flowing in clockwise direction hence we will see magnetic field lines going towards South Pole. The solenoid can be regarded as a combination of circular loops placed side by side, each behaving like a magnet of magnetic moment IA, where I is the current and A area of the loop. These magnets neutralise each other except at the ends where south and north poles appear. Magnetic moment of bar magnet = NIA
solution_html:
1. Ampere's circuital law states that “the line integral of the magnetic field surrounding closed-loop equals to the number of times the algebraic sum of currents passing through the loop.
    For points in the open space inside the toroid: No current flows through the Amperian loop, so I = 0
    {tex}\oint \vec{B} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{dl}}{/tex} = {tex}\mu_0{/tex}I = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} |B|inside = 0
    For points in the open space exterior to the toroid: The net current entering the plane of the toroid is exactly cancelled by the net current leaving the plane of the toroid.

    {tex}\oint \vec{B} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{dl}}{/tex} = {tex}\mu_0{/tex}I = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} |B|exterior = 0
  2. For observer, current is flowing in clockwise direction hence we will see magnetic field lines going towards South Pole.

    The solenoid can be regarded as a combination of circular loops placed side by side, each behaving like a magnet of magnetic moment IA, where I is the current and A area of the loop.

    These magnets neutralise each other except at the ends where south and north poles appear. Magnetic moment of bar magnet = NIA

explanation: K.E. of the particle is not affected because no work is done by the perpendicular magnetid force acting on it.

question_html:
A square loop of side 20 cm carrying current of 1A kept near an infinite long straight wire carrying a current of 2A in the same plane as shown in the figure.

Calculate the magnitude and direction of the net force exerted on the loop due to the current carrying conductor.
question_text: A square loop of side 20 cm carrying current of 1A kept near an infinite long straight wire carrying a current of 2A in the same plane as shown in the figure. Calculate the magnitude and direction of the net force exerted on the loop due to the current carrying conductor.
explanation: Force exerted on the loop due to current carrying conductor is given by, {tex}F = \frac { \mu _ { 0 } I _ { 1 } I _ { 2 } l } { 2 \pi r }{/tex} Force on arm AB, {tex}F _ { A B } = \frac { \mu _ { 0 } \times 2 \times 1 \times 20 \times 10 ^ { - 2 } } { 2 \pi \times 10 \times 10 ^ { - 2 } }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 2 \mu _ { 0 } } { \pi } N{/tex} (Attractive, towards the wire) where N is unit of Force in Newtons. Force on arm CD, {tex}F _ { C D } = \frac { \mu _ { 0 } \times 2 \times 1 \times 20 \times 10 ^ { - 2 } } { 2 \pi \times 30 \times 10 ^ { - 2 } }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 2 \mu _ { 0 } } { 3 \pi } N{/tex} (Repulsive, away from the wire) Force on arms BC and DA are equal and opposite, so they cancel out each other. The net force on the loop ABCD is F = F AB - F CD {tex}= \frac { \mu _ { 0 } } { \pi } \left[ 2 - \frac { 2 } { 3 } \right]{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 4 \mu _ { 0 } } { 3 \pi } = \frac { 4 \times 4 \pi \times 10 ^ { - 7 } } { 3 \pi }{/tex} {tex}= 5.33 \times 10 ^ { - 7 } \mathrm { N }{/tex} (Attractive, towards the wire) This net force is directed towards the infinitely long straight wire.
solution: Force exerted on the loop due to current carrying conductor is given by, {tex}F = \frac { \mu _ { 0 } I _ { 1 } I _ { 2 } l } { 2 \pi r }{/tex} Force on arm AB, {tex}F _ { A B } = \frac { \mu _ { 0 } \times 2 \times 1 \times 20 \times 10 ^ { - 2 } } { 2 \pi \times 10 \times 10 ^ { - 2 } }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 2 \mu _ { 0 } } { \pi } N{/tex} (Attractive, towards the wire) where N is unit of Force in Newtons. Force on arm CD, {tex}F _ { C D } = \frac { \mu _ { 0 } \times 2 \times 1 \times 20 \times 10 ^ { - 2 } } { 2 \pi \times 30 \times 10 ^ { - 2 } }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 2 \mu _ { 0 } } { 3 \pi } N{/tex} (Repulsive, away from the wire) Force on arms BC and DA are equal and opposite, so they cancel out each other. The net force on the loop ABCD is F = F AB - F CD {tex}= \frac { \mu _ { 0 } } { \pi } \left[ 2 - \frac { 2 } { 3 } \right]{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 4 \mu _ { 0 } } { 3 \pi } = \frac { 4 \times 4 \pi \times 10 ^ { - 7 } } { 3 \pi }{/tex} {tex}= 5.33 \times 10 ^ { - 7 } \mathrm { N }{/tex} (Attractive, towards the wire) This net force is directed towards the infinitely long straight wire.
solution_html:
Force exerted on the loop due to current carrying conductor is given by,
{tex}F = \frac { \mu _ { 0 } I _ { 1 } I _ { 2 } l } { 2 \pi r }{/tex}
Force on arm AB, {tex}F _ { A B } = \frac { \mu _ { 0 } \times 2 \times 1 \times 20 \times 10 ^ { - 2 } } { 2 \pi \times 10 \times 10 ^ { - 2 } }{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { 2 \mu _ { 0 } } { \pi } N{/tex} (Attractive, towards the wire)

where N is unit of Force in Newtons.

Force on arm CD, {tex}F _ { C D } = \frac { \mu _ { 0 } \times 2 \times 1 \times 20 \times 10 ^ { - 2 } } { 2 \pi \times 30 \times 10 ^ { - 2 } }{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { 2 \mu _ { 0 } } { 3 \pi } N{/tex} (Repulsive, away from the wire)
Force on arms BC and DA are equal and opposite, so they cancel out each other.
The net force on the loop ABCD is
F = FAB - FCD
{tex}= \frac { \mu _ { 0 } } { \pi } \left[ 2 - \frac { 2 } { 3 } \right]{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { 4 \mu _ { 0 } } { 3 \pi } = \frac { 4 \times 4 \pi \times 10 ^ { - 7 } } { 3 \pi }{/tex}
{tex}= 5.33 \times 10 ^ { - 7 } \mathrm { N }{/tex} (Attractive, towards the wire)
This net force is directed towards the infinitely long straight wire.

subject: Physics
question_html: State the principle of working of a galvanometer. A galvanometer of resistance G is converted into a voltmeter to measure upto V volts by connecting a resistance R1 in series with the coil. If a resistance R2 is connected in series with it, then it can measure upto {tex}\frac{V}{2}{/tex} volts. Find the resistance, in terms of R1 and R2, required to be connected to convert it into a voltmeter that can read upto 2V. Also, find the resistance G of the galvanometer in terms of R1 and R2.
question_text: State the principle of working of a galvanometer. A galvanometer of resistance G is converted into a voltmeter to measure upto V volts by connecting a resistance R 1 in series with the coil. If a resistance R 2 is connected in series with it, then it can measure upto {tex}\frac{V}{2}{/tex} volts. Find the resistance, in terms of R 1 and R 2 , required to be connected to convert it into a voltmeter that can read upto 2V. Also, find the resistance G of the galvanometer in terms of R 1 and R 2 .
explanation: Principle of moving coil galvanometer: It states that when a current-carrying coil is placed in a magnetic field, it experiences torque due to the magnetic force. When resistance R 1 is connected, then the galvanometer equation can be written as: {tex}R_1{}=\frac VI-G {/tex} ..... (i) When resistance R 2 is connected, then the galvanometer equation can be written as: {tex}R_2=\frac V{2I}-G{/tex} ...... (ii) From both these equations, we get, {tex}R_1-R_2=\frac V{2I}{/tex} ..... (iii) and G = R 1 - 2R 2 ..... (iv) When resistance R ₃ is connected to make it read 2V, then the galvanometer equation can be written as: {tex}R_3=\frac{2V}I-G {/tex} Using equations (iii) and (iv) we get R 3 = 4(R 1 - R 2 ) - (R 1 - 2R 2 ) = 3R 1 - 2R 2
solution: Principle of moving coil galvanometer: It states that when a current-carrying coil is placed in a magnetic field, it experiences torque due to the magnetic force. When resistance R 1 is connected, then the galvanometer equation can be written as: {tex}R_1{}=\frac VI-G {/tex} ..... (i) When resistance R 2 is connected, then the galvanometer equation can be written as: {tex}R_2=\frac V{2I}-G{/tex} ...... (ii) From both these equations, we get, {tex}R_1-R_2=\frac V{2I}{/tex} ..... (iii) and G = R 1 - 2R 2 ..... (iv) When resistance R ₃ is connected to make it read 2V, then the galvanometer equation can be written as: {tex}R_3=\frac{2V}I-G {/tex} Using equations (iii) and (iv) we get R 3 = 4(R 1 - R 2 ) - (R 1 - 2R 2 ) = 3R 1 - 2R 2
solution_html:
Principle of moving coil galvanometer: It states that when a current-carrying coil is placed in a magnetic field, it experiences torque due to the magnetic force.
When resistance R1 is connected, then the galvanometer equation can be written as:
{tex}R_1{}=\frac VI-G {/tex} ..... (i)   
When resistance R2 is connected, then the galvanometer equation can be written as:
{tex}R_2=\frac V{2I}-G{/tex} ...... (ii)
From both these equations, we get,
{tex}R_1-R_2=\frac V{2I}{/tex} ..... (iii)
and
G = R1 - 2R2 ..... (iv)
When resistance R₃ is connected to make it read 2V, then the galvanometer equation can be written as:
{tex}R_3=\frac{2V}I-G {/tex}
Using equations (iii) and (iv) we get
R3 = 4(R1 - R2) - (R1 - 2R2) = 3R1 - 2R2

question_html: An electron moving with a velocity of 107 ms-1 enters a uniform magnetic field of 1 T, along a direction parallel to the field. What would be its trajectory in this field?
question_text: An electron moving with a velocity of 10 7 ms -1 enters a uniform magnetic field of 1 T, along a direction parallel to the field. What would be its trajectory in this field?
explanation: The electron will continue to follow its straight path because a parallel magnetic field does not exert any force on the electron.
solution: The electron will continue to follow its straight path because a parallel magnetic field does not exert any force on the electron.
solution_html: The electron will continue to follow its straight path because a parallel magnetic field does not exert any force on the electron.

question_html:
1. Use Biot-Savart's law to derive the expression for the magnetic field due to a circular coil of radius R having N turns at a point on the axis at a distance V from its centre.
    2. Draw the magnetic field lines due to this coil,
  2. A current I enters a uniform circular loop of radius 'R' at point M and flows out at N as shown in the figure. Obtain the net magnetic field at the centre of the loop.
question_text: Use Biot-Savart's law to derive the expression for the magnetic field due to a circular coil of radius R having N turns at a point on the axis at a distance V from its centre. Draw the magnetic field lines due to this coil, A current I enters a uniform circular loop of radius 'R' at point M and flows out at N as shown in the figure. Obtain the net magnetic field at the centre of the loop.
explanation: We have, {tex}\overrightarrow{d B} =\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} I \frac{|\overrightarrow{d I} \times \vec{r}|}{r^{3}} {/tex} r 2 = x 2 + R 2 {tex}\therefore{/tex} dB = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I}{4 \pi} \frac{d l}{\left(x^{2}+R^{2}\right)^{}}{/tex} and cos {tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}R \over{{({x^2+R^2)}}^{1/2}}{/tex} We need to add only the components of dB along the axis of the coil. Hence, B = {tex}\int \frac{\mu_{0} }{4 \pi} \cdot \frac{I d l}{\left(x^{2}+R^{2}\right)^{}} \cos \theta{/tex} = {tex}\int \frac{\mu_{0} }{4 \pi} \frac{(I d l) R}{\left(x^{2}+R^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I R^{2}}{2\left(x^{2}+R^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}}{/tex} integrate over 2 {tex}\pi{/tex} R {tex}\therefore \vec{B} =\frac{\mu_{0} I R^{2}}{2\left(x^{2}+R^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}} \hat{i}{/tex} B = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I}{2 r}{/tex} Field lines: A current I enters a uniform circular loop of radius 'R' at point M and flows out at N as shown in the figure. Let current I be divided at point M into two parts I 1 and I 2 ; in bigger and smaller parts of the loop respectively. The magnetic field of current at point O, {tex}\overrightarrow{B_{1}}=\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \frac{\theta I_{1}}{R}=\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \times \frac{\pi}{2} \times \frac{I_{1}}{R}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0}}{8} \frac{I_{1}}{R}{/tex} (in the direction of B 1 ) Magnetic field due to current I 2 at point O {tex}\begin{aligned} \overrightarrow{B_{2}} &=\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 } \frac{3 }{2} \frac{I_{2}}{R} =\frac{3 \mu_{0}}{8} \frac{I_{2}}{R} \end{aligned}{/tex} (in the direction of B 2 ) The net magnetic field, {tex}\vec{B}=\vec{B}_{1}+\vec{B}_{2}{/tex} {tex}|\vec{B}|=\frac{\mu_{0} I_{1}}{8 R}-\frac{\mu_{0} I_{2}}{8 R} \times 3{/tex} ...(i) But I 1 = 3I 2 (As the resistance of the bigger part is three times that of the smaller part of the loop.) Substituting I 1 = 3I 2 in equation (i), {tex}|\vec{B}|{/tex} = 0
solution: We have, {tex}\overrightarrow{d B} =\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} I \frac{|\overrightarrow{d I} \times \vec{r}|}{r^{3}} {/tex} r 2 = x 2 + R 2 {tex}\therefore{/tex} dB = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I}{4 \pi} \frac{d l}{\left(x^{2}+R^{2}\right)^{}}{/tex} and cos {tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}R \over{{({x^2+R^2)}}^{1/2}}{/tex} We need to add only the components of dB along the axis of the coil. Hence, B = {tex}\int \frac{\mu_{0} }{4 \pi} \cdot \frac{I d l}{\left(x^{2}+R^{2}\right)^{}} \cos \theta{/tex} = {tex}\int \frac{\mu_{0} }{4 \pi} \frac{(I d l) R}{\left(x^{2}+R^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I R^{2}}{2\left(x^{2}+R^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}}{/tex} integrate over 2 {tex}\pi{/tex} R {tex}\therefore \vec{B} =\frac{\mu_{0} I R^{2}}{2\left(x^{2}+R^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}} \hat{i}{/tex} B = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I}{2 r}{/tex} Field lines: A current I enters a uniform circular loop of radius 'R' at point M and flows out at N as shown in the figure. Let current I be divided at point M into two parts I 1 and I 2 ; in bigger and smaller parts of the loop respectively. The magnetic field of current at point O, {tex}\overrightarrow{B_{1}}=\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \frac{\theta I_{1}}{R}=\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \times \frac{\pi}{2} \times \frac{I_{1}}{R}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0}}{8} \frac{I_{1}}{R}{/tex} (in the direction of B 1 ) Magnetic field due to current I 2 at point O {tex}\begin{aligned} \overrightarrow{B_{2}} &=\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 } \frac{3 }{2} \frac{I_{2}}{R} =\frac{3 \mu_{0}}{8} \frac{I_{2}}{R} \end{aligned}{/tex} (in the direction of B 2 ) The net magnetic field, {tex}\vec{B}=\vec{B}_{1}+\vec{B}_{2}{/tex} {tex}|\vec{B}|=\frac{\mu_{0} I_{1}}{8 R}-\frac{\mu_{0} I_{2}}{8 R} \times 3{/tex} ...(i) But I 1 = 3I 2 (As the resistance of the bigger part is three times that of the smaller part of the loop.) Substituting I 1 = 3I 2 in equation (i), {tex}|\vec{B}|{/tex} = 0
solution_html:
1.  

    1.  
      We have, {tex}\overrightarrow{d B} =\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} I \frac{|\overrightarrow{d I} \times \vec{r}|}{r^{3}} {/tex}
      r2 = x2 + R2
      {tex}\therefore{/tex} dB = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I}{4 \pi} \frac{d l}{\left(x^{2}+R^{2}\right)^{}}{/tex} and cos{tex}\theta{/tex}= {tex}R \over{{({x^2+R^2)}}^{1/2}}{/tex}
      We need to add only the components of dB along the axis of the coil.
      Hence, B = {tex}\int \frac{\mu_{0} }{4 \pi} \cdot \frac{I d l}{\left(x^{2}+R^{2}\right)^{}} \cos \theta{/tex}
      = {tex}\int \frac{\mu_{0} }{4 \pi} \frac{(I d l) R}{\left(x^{2}+R^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}}{/tex}
      = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I R^{2}}{2\left(x^{2}+R^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}}{/tex} integrate over 2{tex}\pi{/tex}R
      {tex}\therefore \vec{B} =\frac{\mu_{0} I R^{2}}{2\left(x^{2}+R^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}} \hat{i}{/tex}
    2. B = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I}{2 r}{/tex}
      Field lines: 
  2. A current I enters a uniform circular loop of radius 'R' at point M and flows out at N as shown in the figure. Let current I be divided at point M into two parts I1 and I2; in bigger and smaller parts of the loop respectively.
    The magnetic field of current at point O, {tex}\overrightarrow{B_{1}}=\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \frac{\theta I_{1}}{R}=\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \times \frac{\pi}{2} \times \frac{I_{1}}{R}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0}}{8} \frac{I_{1}}{R}{/tex} (in the direction of B1)

    Magnetic field due to current I2 at point O
    {tex}\begin{aligned} \overrightarrow{B_{2}} &=\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 } \frac{3 }{2} \frac{I_{2}}{R} =\frac{3 \mu_{0}}{8} \frac{I_{2}}{R} \end{aligned}{/tex} (in the direction of B2)
    The net magnetic field, {tex}\vec{B}=\vec{B}_{1}+\vec{B}_{2}{/tex}
    {tex}|\vec{B}|=\frac{\mu_{0} I_{1}}{8 R}-\frac{\mu_{0} I_{2}}{8 R} \times 3{/tex} ...(i)
    But I1 = 3I2 (As the resistance of the bigger part is three times that of the smaller part of the loop.)
    Substituting I1 = 3I2 in equation (i),
    {tex}|\vec{B}|{/tex} = 0

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. State Ampere's circuital law. Use this law to obtain the expression for the magnetic field inside an air-cored toroid of average radius 'r', having 'n' turns per unit length and carrying a steady current I.
  2. An observer to the left of a solenoid of N turns each of cross-section area 'A' observes that a steady current I in it flows in the clockwise direction. Depict the magnetic field lines due to the solenoid specifying its polarity and show that it acts as a bar magnet of magnetic moment m = NIA.
question_text: State Ampere's circuital law. Use this law to obtain the expression for the magnetic field inside an air-cored toroid of average radius 'r', having 'n' turns per unit length and carrying a steady current I. An observer to the left of a solenoid of N turns each of cross-section area 'A' observes that a steady current I in it flows in the clockwise direction. Depict the magnetic field lines due to the solenoid specifying its polarity and show that it acts as a bar magnet of magnetic moment m = NIA.
explanation: Line integral of magnetic field over a closed loop is equal to the {tex}\mu_o{/tex} times the total current passing through the surface enclosed by the loop. Alternatively: {tex}\oint \vec{B} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}=\mu_{0} I{/tex} Let the current flowing through each turn of the toroid be I. The total number of turns equals n. (2 {tex}\pi{/tex} r) where, n is the number of turns per unit length. Applying Ampere's circuital law, for the Amperian loop, for interior points. {tex}\oint \overrightarrow{\mathbf{B}} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}=\mu_{0}(n \cdot 2 \pi r) I{/tex} {tex}\begin{aligned} &\Rightarrow &B \times 2 \pi r=\mu_{0} n 2 \pi r I\\ \end{aligned}{/tex} , By comparing the equations we get B = {tex}\mu_{0} {/tex} nI The solenoid contains N loops, each carrying a current I. Therefore, each loop acts as a magnetic dipole. The magnetic moment for a current I, flowing in a loop of area (vector) A is given by M = lA The magnetic moments of all loops are aligned in the same direction. Hence, net magnetic moment due to whole solenoid equals to NIA.
solution: Line integral of magnetic field over a closed loop is equal to the {tex}\mu_o{/tex} times the total current passing through the surface enclosed by the loop. Alternatively: {tex}\oint \vec{B} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}=\mu_{0} I{/tex} Let the current flowing through each turn of the toroid be I. The total number of turns equals n. (2 {tex}\pi{/tex} r) where, n is the number of turns per unit length. Applying Ampere's circuital law, for the Amperian loop, for interior points. {tex}\oint \overrightarrow{\mathbf{B}} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}=\mu_{0}(n \cdot 2 \pi r) I{/tex} {tex}\begin{aligned} &\Rightarrow &B \times 2 \pi r=\mu_{0} n 2 \pi r I\\ \end{aligned}{/tex} , By comparing the equations we get B = {tex}\mu_{0} {/tex} nI The solenoid contains N loops, each carrying a current I. Therefore, each loop acts as a magnetic dipole. The magnetic moment for a current I, flowing in a loop of area (vector) A is given by M = lA The magnetic moments of all loops are aligned in the same direction. Hence, net magnetic moment due to whole solenoid equals to NIA.
solution_html:
1. Line integral of magnetic field over a closed loop is equal to the {tex}\mu_o{/tex} times the total current passing through the surface enclosed by the loop.
    Alternatively: {tex}\oint \vec{B} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}=\mu_{0} I{/tex}

    Let the current flowing through each turn of the toroid be I. The total number of turns equals n. (2{tex}\pi{/tex}r) where, n is the number of turns per unit length.
    Applying Ampere's circuital law, for the Amperian loop, for interior points.
    {tex}\oint \overrightarrow{\mathbf{B}} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}=\mu_{0}(n \cdot 2 \pi r) I{/tex}  
    {tex}\begin{aligned} &\Rightarrow &B \times 2 \pi r=\mu_{0} n 2 \pi r I\\ \end{aligned}{/tex},
    By comparing the equations we get 
    B = {tex}\mu_{0} {/tex}nI


  2. The solenoid contains N loops, each carrying a current I. Therefore, each loop acts as a magnetic dipole. The magnetic moment for a current I, flowing in a loop of area (vector) A is given by M = lA
    The magnetic moments of all loops are aligned in the same direction. Hence, net magnetic moment due to whole solenoid equals to NIA.

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. Why is the need for magnetic field radial in a moving coil galvanometer? Explain how it is achieved?
  2. A galvanometer of resistance 'G' can be converted into a voltmeter of range (0 - V) volts by connecting a resistance 'R' in series with it. How much resistance will be required to change its range from 0 to {tex}\frac V2{/tex}?
question_text: Why is the need for magnetic field radial in a moving coil galvanometer? Explain how it is achieved? A galvanometer of resistance 'G' can be converted into a voltmeter of range (0 - V) volts by connecting a resistance 'R' in series with it. How much resistance will be required to change its range from 0 to {tex}\frac V2{/tex} ?
explanation: Need for a radial magnetic field: The relation between the current (I) flowing through the galvanometer coil, and the angular deflection ( {tex}\phi{/tex} ) of the coil (from its equilibrium position), is {tex}\phi=\left(\frac{N A B I \sin \theta}{k}\right){/tex} where {tex}\theta{/tex} is the angle between the magnetic field {tex}\vec B{/tex} and normal to the coil. Thus {tex}I{/tex} is not directly proportional to {tex}\phi{/tex} . We can ensure this proportionality by having {tex}\theta{/tex} = 90°. This is possible only when the magnetic field {tex}\vec B{/tex} , is of a radial magnetic field. In such a field, the plane of the rotating coil is always parallel to {tex}\vec B{/tex} . To get a radial magnetic field, the pole pieces of the magnet, are made concave in shape. Also, a soft iron cylinder is used as the core. We have R = {tex}\left(\frac{V}{I_{m}}-G\right){/tex} {tex}\begin{aligned} &\therefore &I_{m}=\frac{V}{R+G} \end{aligned}{/tex} We must also have {tex}I'_{m}=\frac{\left(\frac{V}{2}\right)}{R^{\prime}+G}{/tex} where, R' = Resistance required to change the range from 0 to {tex}\frac V2{/tex} , I m = I m ' {tex}\therefore \quad \frac{V}{R+G}=\frac{V}{2\left(R^{\prime}+G\right)}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad R^{\prime}=\frac{R-G}{2}{/tex}
solution: Need for a radial magnetic field: The relation between the current (I) flowing through the galvanometer coil, and the angular deflection ( {tex}\phi{/tex} ) of the coil (from its equilibrium position), is {tex}\phi=\left(\frac{N A B I \sin \theta}{k}\right){/tex} where {tex}\theta{/tex} is the angle between the magnetic field {tex}\vec B{/tex} and normal to the coil. Thus {tex}I{/tex} is not directly proportional to {tex}\phi{/tex} . We can ensure this proportionality by having {tex}\theta{/tex} = 90°. This is possible only when the magnetic field {tex}\vec B{/tex} , is of a radial magnetic field. In such a field, the plane of the rotating coil is always parallel to {tex}\vec B{/tex} . To get a radial magnetic field, the pole pieces of the magnet, are made concave in shape. Also, a soft iron cylinder is used as the core. We have R = {tex}\left(\frac{V}{I_{m}}-G\right){/tex} {tex}\begin{aligned} &\therefore &I_{m}=\frac{V}{R+G} \end{aligned}{/tex} We must also have {tex}I'_{m}=\frac{\left(\frac{V}{2}\right)}{R^{\prime}+G}{/tex} where, R' = Resistance required to change the range from 0 to {tex}\frac V2{/tex} , I m = I m ' {tex}\therefore \quad \frac{V}{R+G}=\frac{V}{2\left(R^{\prime}+G\right)}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad R^{\prime}=\frac{R-G}{2}{/tex}
solution_html:
1. Need for a radial magnetic field:
    The relation between the current (I) flowing through the galvanometer coil, and the angular deflection ({tex}\phi{/tex}) of the coil (from its equilibrium position), is {tex}\phi=\left(\frac{N A B I \sin \theta}{k}\right){/tex}
    where {tex}\theta{/tex} is the angle between the magnetic field {tex}\vec B{/tex}and normal to the coil.
    Thus {tex}I{/tex} is not directly proportional to {tex}\phi{/tex}. We can ensure this proportionality by having {tex}\theta{/tex} = 90°.
    This is possible only when the magnetic field {tex}\vec B{/tex}, is of a radial magnetic field. In such a field, the plane of the rotating coil is always parallel to {tex}\vec B{/tex}. To get a radial magnetic field, the pole pieces of the magnet, are made concave in shape. Also, a soft iron cylinder is used as the core. 

  2. We have R = {tex}\left(\frac{V}{I_{m}}-G\right){/tex}
    {tex}\begin{aligned} &\therefore &I_{m}=\frac{V}{R+G} \end{aligned}{/tex}
    We must also have
    {tex}I'_{m}=\frac{\left(\frac{V}{2}\right)}{R^{\prime}+G}{/tex}
    where, R' = Resistance required to change the range from 0 to {tex}\frac V2{/tex},
    Im = Im'
    {tex}\therefore \quad \frac{V}{R+G}=\frac{V}{2\left(R^{\prime}+G\right)}{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore \quad R^{\prime}=\frac{R-G}{2}{/tex}

question_html:
1. Show how Biot-Savart's law can be alternatively expressed in the form of Ampere's circuital law. Use this law to obtain the expression for the magnetic field inside a solenoid of length l, cross-sectional area A having 'n' closely wound turns and carrying a steady current 'l'. Draw the magnetic field lines of a finite solenoid carrying current I.
  2. A straight horizontal conducting rod of length 0.45 m and mass 60 g is suspended by two vertical wires at its ends. A current of 5.0 A is set up in the rod through the wires.
    Find the magnitude and direction of the magnetic field which should be set up in order that the tension in the wire is zero.
question_text: Show how Biot-Savart's law can be alternatively expressed in the form of Ampere's circuital law. Use this law to obtain the expression for the magnetic field inside a solenoid of length l, cross-sectional area A having 'n' closely wound turns and carrying a steady current 'l'. Draw the magnetic field lines of a finite solenoid carrying current I. A straight horizontal conducting rod of length 0.45 m and mass 60 g is suspended by two vertical wires at its ends. A current of 5.0 A is set up in the rod through the wires. Find the magnitude and direction of the magnetic field which should be set up in order that the tension in the wire is zero.
explanation: A current-carrying surface can be divided into small line elements of length dl. Considering tangential components of magnetic field and finding sum of all elements of B.dl tends to an integral ,which can be expressed as: {tex}\oint \vec{B} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}=\mu_{0} i{/tex} This form is known as Ampere's circuital law. Let n be the number of turns per unit length. Then total number of turns in the length 'h' is nh. Hence, total enclosed current = nhl Using Ampere's circuital law {tex}\oint \vec{B} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l} =\mu_{0} n h I{/tex} Bh = {tex}\mu_{0}{/tex} nhl B = {tex}\mu_{0}{/tex} nI mass m= 60g. and I= 5 A L= 0.45 meter As per the given figure, magnetic field must be vertically inwards, to make tension zero, (If a student shows currently in the opposite direction the magnetic field should be set up vertically upwards). ILB = mg For tension to be zero, B = {tex}\frac{m g}{\mathrm{IL}}=\frac{60 \times 10^{-3} \times 9.8}{5.0 \times 0.45} \mathrm{T}{/tex} = 0.26 T
solution: A current-carrying surface can be divided into small line elements of length dl. Considering tangential components of magnetic field and finding sum of all elements of B.dl tends to an integral ,which can be expressed as: {tex}\oint \vec{B} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}=\mu_{0} i{/tex} This form is known as Ampere's circuital law. Let n be the number of turns per unit length. Then total number of turns in the length 'h' is nh. Hence, total enclosed current = nhl Using Ampere's circuital law {tex}\oint \vec{B} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l} =\mu_{0} n h I{/tex} Bh = {tex}\mu_{0}{/tex} nhl B = {tex}\mu_{0}{/tex} nI mass m= 60g. and I= 5 A L= 0.45 meter As per the given figure, magnetic field must be vertically inwards, to make tension zero, (If a student shows currently in the opposite direction the magnetic field should be set up vertically upwards). ILB = mg For tension to be zero, B = {tex}\frac{m g}{\mathrm{IL}}=\frac{60 \times 10^{-3} \times 9.8}{5.0 \times 0.45} \mathrm{T}{/tex} = 0.26 T
solution_html:
1. A current-carrying surface can be divided into  small line elements of length dl. Considering  tangential components of magnetic field and  finding sum of all elements of  B.dl tends to an integral ,which can be expressed as: {tex}\oint \vec{B} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}=\mu_{0} i{/tex}
    This form is known as Ampere's circuital law.

    Let n be the number of turns per unit length. Then total number of turns in the length 'h' is nh. Hence, total enclosed current = nhl
    Using Ampere's circuital law
    {tex}\oint \vec{B} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l} =\mu_{0} n h I{/tex}
    Bh = {tex}\mu_{0}{/tex}nhl
    B = {tex}\mu_{0}{/tex}nI

  2.  mass m= 60g. and I= 5 A L= 0.45 meter As per the given figure, magnetic field must be vertically inwards, to make tension zero, (If a student shows currently in the opposite direction the magnetic field should be set up vertically upwards).
    ILB = mg
    For tension to be zero, B = {tex}\frac{m g}{\mathrm{IL}}=\frac{60 \times 10^{-3} \times 9.8}{5.0 \times 0.45} \mathrm{T}{/tex} = 0.26 T

question_text: A wire carrying current I has the shape as shown in the given figure. Linear parts if the wire are very long and parallel to X-axis while a semicircular portion of radius R is lying in the Y-Z plane. The magnetic field at point O is:

options.explanation: The field at O due to each linear part, {tex}\vec{B}_{1}=\vec{B}_{3}=\frac{\mu_{0} I}{4 \pi R}\left(\sin 90^{\circ}+\sin 0^{\circ}\right)(-\hat{k}){/tex} {tex}=\frac{\mu_{0} I}{4 \pi R}(-k){/tex} The field at O due to the semicircular part, {tex}\vec{B}_{2}=\frac{\mu_{0} I}{4 R}(-\hat{i}){/tex} {tex}\vec{B}_{0}=\vec{B}_{1}+\vec{B}_{2}=\frac{\mu_{0} I}{4 \pi R}(\pi \hat{i}+2 \hat{k}){/tex}

explanation: The field at O due to each linear part, {tex}\vec{B}_{1}=\vec{B}_{3}=\frac{\mu_{0} I}{4 \pi R}\left(\sin 90^{\circ}+\sin 0^{\circ}\right)(-\hat{k}){/tex} {tex}=\frac{\mu_{0} I}{4 \pi R}(-k){/tex} The field at O due to the semicircular part, {tex}\vec{B}_{2}=\frac{\mu_{0} I}{4 R}(-\hat{i}){/tex} {tex}\vec{B}_{0}=\vec{B}_{1}+\vec{B}_{2}=\frac{\mu_{0} I}{4 \pi R}(\pi \hat{i}+2 \hat{k}){/tex}

question_html: An electron moving horizontally with a velocity of 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 104 m /s enters a region of the uniform magnetic field of 10-5 T acting vertically downward as shown in the given figure. Draw its trajectory and find out the time it takes to come out of the region of the magnetic field.
question_text: An electron moving horizontally with a velocity of 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 4 m /s enters a region of the uniform magnetic field of 10 -5 T acting vertically downward as shown in the given figure. Draw its trajectory and find out the time it takes to come out of the region of the magnetic field.
explanation: The electron moves along a semicircular trajectory inside the magnetic field and comes out, as shown in given figure. Radius r of the path is given by: {tex}\frac{m v^{2}}{r}{/tex} = qvB or r = {tex}\frac{m v}{q B}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{9.1 \times 10^{-31} \times 4 \times 10^{4}}{1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 10^{-5}} \mathrm{~m}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{9.1 \times 4}{1.6} \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~m}{/tex} = 22.75 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 m Time taken to come out of the region of magnetic field, t = {tex}\frac{\pi r}{v}=\frac{22 \times 22.75 \times 10^{-3}}{7 \times 4 \times 10^{4}} \mathrm{~s}{/tex} = 17.875 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -7 s {tex}\simeq{/tex} 1.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -6 s
solution: The electron moves along a semicircular trajectory inside the magnetic field and comes out, as shown in given figure. Radius r of the path is given by: {tex}\frac{m v^{2}}{r}{/tex} = qvB or r = {tex}\frac{m v}{q B}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{9.1 \times 10^{-31} \times 4 \times 10^{4}}{1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 10^{-5}} \mathrm{~m}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{9.1 \times 4}{1.6} \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~m}{/tex} = 22.75 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 m Time taken to come out of the region of magnetic field, t = {tex}\frac{\pi r}{v}=\frac{22 \times 22.75 \times 10^{-3}}{7 \times 4 \times 10^{4}} \mathrm{~s}{/tex} = 17.875 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -7 s {tex}\simeq{/tex} 1.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -6 s
solution_html:
The electron moves along a semicircular trajectory inside the magnetic field and comes out, as shown in given figure. Radius r of the path is given by:
{tex}\frac{m v^{2}}{r}{/tex} = qvB
or r = {tex}\frac{m v}{q B}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{9.1 \times 10^{-31} \times 4 \times 10^{4}}{1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 10^{-5}} \mathrm{~m}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{9.1 \times 4}{1.6} \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~m}{/tex}
= 22.75 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 m

Time taken to come out of the region of magnetic field,
t = {tex}\frac{\pi r}{v}=\frac{22 \times 22.75 \times 10^{-3}}{7 \times 4 \times 10^{4}} \mathrm{~s}{/tex}
= 17.875 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-7 s {tex}\simeq{/tex} 1.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-6 s

The speed of the particle will become {tex}\frac{\sqrt{5}}{2} v_{0}{/tex} after a time
question_text: A particle having charge q and mass m starts moving from the origin under the action of an electric field {tex}\vec{E}=E_{0} \hat{i}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{B}=B_{0} \hat{i}{/tex} with a velocity {tex}v=v_{0} \hat{j}{/tex} . The speed of the particle will become {tex}\frac{\sqrt{5}}{2} v_{0}{/tex} after a time

options.explanation: {tex}v=\sqrt{v_{y}^{2}+v_{x}^{2}}=\sqrt{v_{0}^{2}+\left(\frac{q E}{m} t\right)^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\left(\frac{\sqrt{5}}{2} v_{0}\right)^{2}=v_{0}^{2}+\left(\frac{q E}{m} t\right)^{2}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}t=\frac{m v_{0}}{2 q E}{/tex}

options.html: {tex}\frac{\sqrt{3} m v_{0}}{2 q E}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{\sqrt{5} m v_{0}}{2 q E}{/tex}

explanation: {tex}v=\sqrt{v_{y}^{2}+v_{x}^{2}}=\sqrt{v_{0}^{2}+\left(\frac{q E}{m} t\right)^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\left(\frac{\sqrt{5}}{2} v_{0}\right)^{2}=v_{0}^{2}+\left(\frac{q E}{m} t\right)^{2}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}t=\frac{m v_{0}}{2 q E}{/tex}

question_html: A galvanometer of resistance 'G' can be converted into a voltmeter of range (0 - V) volts by connecting a resistance 'R' in series with it. How much resistance will be required to change its range from 0 to {tex}\frac{V}{2}{/tex} ?
question_text: A galvanometer of resistance 'G' can be converted into a voltmeter of range (0 - V) volts by connecting a resistance 'R' in series with it. How much resistance will be required to change its range from 0 to {tex}\frac{V}{2}{/tex} ?
explanation: In the first case {tex}R=\frac{V}{I_{g}}-G{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad I_{g}=\frac{V}{R+G}{/tex} Let R' be the required resistance to change the range from 0 to {tex}\frac{V}{2}{/tex} . So in the second case, {tex}I_{g}=\frac{V / 2}{R^{\prime}+G}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad \frac{V}{R+G}=\frac{V / 2}{R^{\prime}+G}{/tex} or 2 R '+ 2 G = R + G Hence, {tex}R^{\prime}=\frac{R-G}{2}{/tex}
solution: In the first case {tex}R=\frac{V}{I_{g}}-G{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad I_{g}=\frac{V}{R+G}{/tex} Let R' be the required resistance to change the range from 0 to {tex}\frac{V}{2}{/tex} . So in the second case, {tex}I_{g}=\frac{V / 2}{R^{\prime}+G}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad \frac{V}{R+G}=\frac{V / 2}{R^{\prime}+G}{/tex} or 2 R '+ 2 G = R + G Hence, {tex}R^{\prime}=\frac{R-G}{2}{/tex}
solution_html:
In the first case {tex}R=\frac{V}{I_{g}}-G{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad I_{g}=\frac{V}{R+G}{/tex}
Let R' be the required resistance to change the range from 0 to {tex}\frac{V}{2}{/tex}. So in the second case, {tex}I_{g}=\frac{V / 2}{R^{\prime}+G}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad \frac{V}{R+G}=\frac{V / 2}{R^{\prime}+G}{/tex}
or 2 R '+ 2 G = R + G
Hence, {tex}R^{\prime}=\frac{R-G}{2}{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html: The current flowing in the galvanometer G when the key K2 is kept open is I. On closing the key K2, the current in the galvanometer becomes {tex}\frac{1}{n}{/tex}, where n is an integer. Obtain an expression for resistance Rg of the galvanometer in terms of R, S and n. To what form does this expression reduce when the value of R is very large as compared to S?
question_text: The current flowing in the galvanometer G when the key K 2 is kept open is I. On closing the key K 2 , the current in the galvanometer becomes {tex}\frac{1}{n}{/tex} , where n is an integer. Obtain an expression for resistance R g of the galvanometer in terms of R, S and n. To what form does this expression reduce when the value of R is very large as compared to S?
explanation: With key K 2 open, the current I in the galvanometer will be {tex}I=\frac{\varepsilon}{R+R_{g}}{/tex} When K 2 is closed, the equivalent resistance of the parallel combination of S and R g is given by {tex}R^{\prime}=\frac{S R_{g}}{S+R_{g}}{/tex} The total current I' drawn from the battery would now be {tex}I^{\prime}=\frac{\varepsilon}{R+R^{\prime}}{/tex} This current gets divided through S and G in the inverse ratio of S and R g . Hence, the current I" through the galvanometer will be {tex}I^{\prime \prime}=\frac{S}{S+R_{g}} I^{\prime}=\frac{S}{S+R_{g}} \cdot \frac{\varepsilon}{\left(R+R^{\prime}\right)}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{S . E}{S+R_{g}} \cdot \frac{1}{R+\frac{S R_{g}}{S+R_{g}}}=\frac{S E}{R S+R R_{g}+S R_{g}}{/tex} But {tex}I^{\prime \prime}=\frac{I}{n}=\frac{1}{n} \cdot \frac{\varepsilon}{R+R_{g}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{\varepsilon}{n\left(R+R_{g}\right)}=\frac{s e}{R S+R R_{g}+S R_{g}}{/tex} or nRS+ nSR g = RS+ RR g + SR g or (n -l)R S = RR g -(n -l)SR g or (n -l)R S = R g (R -(n -l)S Hence, the resistance of the galvanometer is {tex}R_{g}=\frac{(n-1) R S}{R-(n-1) S}{/tex} When R » S, we have, {tex}R_{g}=\frac{(n-1) R S}{R}=(n-1) S{/tex}
solution: With key K 2 open, the current I in the galvanometer will be {tex}I=\frac{\varepsilon}{R+R_{g}}{/tex} When K 2 is closed, the equivalent resistance of the parallel combination of S and R g is given by {tex}R^{\prime}=\frac{S R_{g}}{S+R_{g}}{/tex} The total current I' drawn from the battery would now be {tex}I^{\prime}=\frac{\varepsilon}{R+R^{\prime}}{/tex} This current gets divided through S and G in the inverse ratio of S and R g . Hence, the current I" through the galvanometer will be {tex}I^{\prime \prime}=\frac{S}{S+R_{g}} I^{\prime}=\frac{S}{S+R_{g}} \cdot \frac{\varepsilon}{\left(R+R^{\prime}\right)}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{S . E}{S+R_{g}} \cdot \frac{1}{R+\frac{S R_{g}}{S+R_{g}}}=\frac{S E}{R S+R R_{g}+S R_{g}}{/tex} But {tex}I^{\prime \prime}=\frac{I}{n}=\frac{1}{n} \cdot \frac{\varepsilon}{R+R_{g}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{\varepsilon}{n\left(R+R_{g}\right)}=\frac{s e}{R S+R R_{g}+S R_{g}}{/tex} or nRS+ nSR g = RS+ RR g + SR g or (n -l)R S = RR g -(n -l)SR g or (n -l)R S = R g (R -(n -l)S Hence, the resistance of the galvanometer is {tex}R_{g}=\frac{(n-1) R S}{R-(n-1) S}{/tex} When R » S, we have, {tex}R_{g}=\frac{(n-1) R S}{R}=(n-1) S{/tex}
solution_html:
With key K2 open, the current I in the galvanometer will be {tex}I=\frac{\varepsilon}{R+R_{g}}{/tex}
When K2 is closed, the equivalent resistance of the parallel combination of S and Rg is given by {tex}R^{\prime}=\frac{S R_{g}}{S+R_{g}}{/tex}
The total current I' drawn from the battery would now be  {tex}I^{\prime}=\frac{\varepsilon}{R+R^{\prime}}{/tex}
This current gets divided through S and G in the inverse ratio of S and Rg.
Hence, the current I" through the galvanometer will be {tex}I^{\prime \prime}=\frac{S}{S+R_{g}} I^{\prime}=\frac{S}{S+R_{g}} \cdot \frac{\varepsilon}{\left(R+R^{\prime}\right)}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{S . E}{S+R_{g}} \cdot \frac{1}{R+\frac{S R_{g}}{S+R_{g}}}=\frac{S E}{R S+R R_{g}+S R_{g}}{/tex}
But {tex}I^{\prime \prime}=\frac{I}{n}=\frac{1}{n} \cdot \frac{\varepsilon}{R+R_{g}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \frac{\varepsilon}{n\left(R+R_{g}\right)}=\frac{s e}{R S+R R_{g}+S R_{g}}{/tex}
or nRS+ nSRg = RS+ RRg + SRg
or (n -l)R S = RRg -(n -l)SRg
or (n -l)R S = Rg(R -(n -l)S
Hence, the resistance of the galvanometer is {tex}R_{g}=\frac{(n-1) R S}{R-(n-1) S}{/tex}
When R » S, we have, {tex}R_{g}=\frac{(n-1) R S}{R}=(n-1) S{/tex}

question_html: A uniform magnetic field {tex}\vec{B}{/tex} is set up along the positive x-axis. A particle of charge ‘q ' and mass 'm ’ moving with a velocity {tex}\vec{v}{/tex} enters the field at the origin in X - Y plane such that it has velocity components both along and perpendicular to the magnetic field {tex}\vec{B}{/tex}. Trace, giving reason, the trajectory followed by the particle. Find out the expression for the distance moved by the particle along the magnetic field in one rotation.
question_text: A uniform magnetic field {tex}\vec{B}{/tex} is set up along the positive x-axis. A particle of charge ‘q ' and mass 'm ’ moving with a velocity {tex}\vec{v}{/tex} enters the field at the origin in X - Y plane such that it has velocity components both along and perpendicular to the magnetic field {tex}\vec{B}{/tex} . Trace, giving reason, the trajectory followed by the particle. Find out the expression for the distance moved by the particle along the magnetic field in one rotation.
explanation: If component v x of the velocity vector is along the magnetic field, and remain constant, the charge particle will follow a helical trajectory; as shown in fig If the velocity component v y is perpendicular to the magnetic field B, the magnetic force acts like a centripetal force qv y B. So, {tex}q v_y B=\frac{\mathrm{mv}_v^2}{r} \Rightarrow v_y=\frac{\mathrm{qBr}}{m}{/tex} Since tangent velocity {tex}v_y=r \omega{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow r \omega=\frac{\mathrm{qBr}}{m} \Rightarrow \omega=\frac{\mathrm{qB}}{m}{/tex} Time taken for one revolution, {tex}T=\frac{2 \pi}{\omega}=\frac{2 \pi m}{q B}{/tex} and the distance moved along the magnetic field in the helical path is x = v x . {tex}=v_x \cdot \frac{2 \pi m}{q B}{/tex}
solution: If component v x of the velocity vector is along the magnetic field, and remain constant, the charge particle will follow a helical trajectory; as shown in fig If the velocity component v y is perpendicular to the magnetic field B, the magnetic force acts like a centripetal force qv y B. So, {tex}q v_y B=\frac{\mathrm{mv}_v^2}{r} \Rightarrow v_y=\frac{\mathrm{qBr}}{m}{/tex} Since tangent velocity {tex}v_y=r \omega{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow r \omega=\frac{\mathrm{qBr}}{m} \Rightarrow \omega=\frac{\mathrm{qB}}{m}{/tex} Time taken for one revolution, {tex}T=\frac{2 \pi}{\omega}=\frac{2 \pi m}{q B}{/tex} and the distance moved along the magnetic field in the helical path is x = v x . {tex}=v_x \cdot \frac{2 \pi m}{q B}{/tex}
solution_html:
If component vx of the velocity vector is along the magnetic field, and remain constant, the charge particle will follow a helical trajectory; as shown in fig
If the velocity component vy is perpendicular to the magnetic field B, the magnetic force acts like a centripetal force qvy B.

So, {tex}q v_y B=\frac{\mathrm{mv}_v^2}{r} \Rightarrow v_y=\frac{\mathrm{qBr}}{m}{/tex}
Since tangent velocity {tex}v_y=r \omega{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow r \omega=\frac{\mathrm{qBr}}{m} \Rightarrow \omega=\frac{\mathrm{qB}}{m}{/tex}
Time taken for one revolution, {tex}T=\frac{2 \pi}{\omega}=\frac{2 \pi m}{q B}{/tex}
and the distance moved along the magnetic field in the helical path is x = vx.
{tex}=v_x \cdot \frac{2 \pi m}{q B}{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. Write any two important points of similarities and differences each between Coulomb's law for the electrostatic field and Biot-Savart's law for the magnetic field.
  2. Use Biot-Savart's law to find the expression for the magnetic field due to a circular loop of radius 'r' carrying current 'I', at its centre.
question_text: Write any two important points of similarities and differences each between Coulomb's law for the electrostatic field and Biot-Savart's law for the magnetic field. Use Biot-Savart's law to find the expression for the magnetic field due to a circular loop of radius 'r' carrying current 'I', at its centre.
explanation: Similarities: Both electrostatic field and magnetic field: Follows the principle of superposition. Depends inversely on the square of distance from source to the point of interest. Differences: Electrostatic field is produced by a scalar source (q) and the magnetic field is produced by a vector (I.dI). Electrostatic field is along the displacement vector between source and point of interest; while magnetic field is perpendicular to the plane, containing the displacement vector and vector source. According to Biot- Savart law, {tex}d\overrightarrow{\mathbf{B}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mu_0}{4 \pi} \frac{\mathrm{I} d l}{\left(x^2+\mathrm{R}^2\right)}{/tex} {tex}\left[\begin{array}{l} \because|\vec{d} l \times \vec{r}|=r d l ; \\ r=\left(x^2+\mathrm{R}^2\right)^{\frac{1}{2}} \end{array}\right]{/tex} From figure, cos {tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}\frac{R}{\left(x^2+R^2\right)^{\frac{1}{2}}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Net contribution along x-direction B = {tex}\sum{/tex} dB cos {tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}\int{/tex} dB cos {tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}\int_\limits0^{2 \pi \mathrm{R}} \frac{\mu_0 I d l}{4 \pi}{/tex} {tex}\frac{\mathrm{R}}{\left(x^2+\mathrm{R}^2\right)^{\frac{3}{2}}}{/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mu_0 \mathrm{IR}^2}{2\left(\mathrm{R}^2+x^2\right)^{\frac{3}{2}}} \hat{i}{/tex}
solution: Similarities: Both electrostatic field and magnetic field: Follows the principle of superposition. Depends inversely on the square of distance from source to the point of interest. Differences: Electrostatic field is produced by a scalar source (q) and the magnetic field is produced by a vector (I.dI). Electrostatic field is along the displacement vector between source and point of interest; while magnetic field is perpendicular to the plane, containing the displacement vector and vector source. According to Biot- Savart law, {tex}d\overrightarrow{\mathbf{B}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mu_0}{4 \pi} \frac{\mathrm{I} d l}{\left(x^2+\mathrm{R}^2\right)}{/tex} {tex}\left[\begin{array}{l} \because|\vec{d} l \times \vec{r}|=r d l ; \\ r=\left(x^2+\mathrm{R}^2\right)^{\frac{1}{2}} \end{array}\right]{/tex} From figure, cos {tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}\frac{R}{\left(x^2+R^2\right)^{\frac{1}{2}}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Net contribution along x-direction B = {tex}\sum{/tex} dB cos {tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}\int{/tex} dB cos {tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}\int_\limits0^{2 \pi \mathrm{R}} \frac{\mu_0 I d l}{4 \pi}{/tex} {tex}\frac{\mathrm{R}}{\left(x^2+\mathrm{R}^2\right)^{\frac{3}{2}}}{/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mu_0 \mathrm{IR}^2}{2\left(\mathrm{R}^2+x^2\right)^{\frac{3}{2}}} \hat{i}{/tex}
solution_html:
1. Similarities:
    Both electrostatic field and magnetic field: Follows the principle of superposition.
    Depends inversely on the square of distance from source to the point of interest.
    Differences:
    1. Electrostatic field is produced by a scalar source (q) and the magnetic field is produced by a vector (I.dI).
    2. Electrostatic field is along the displacement vector between source and point of interest; while magnetic field is perpendicular to the plane, containing the displacement vector and vector source.
  2. According to Biot- Savart law,
    {tex}d\overrightarrow{\mathbf{B}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mu_0}{4 \pi} \frac{\mathrm{I} d l}{\left(x^2+\mathrm{R}^2\right)}{/tex}{tex}\left[\begin{array}{l} \because|\vec{d} l \times \vec{r}|=r d l ; \\ r=\left(x^2+\mathrm{R}^2\right)^{\frac{1}{2}} \end{array}\right]{/tex} From figure, cos{tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}\frac{R}{\left(x^2+R^2\right)^{\frac{1}{2}}}{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} Net contribution along x-direction
    B = {tex}\sum{/tex}dB cos {tex}\theta{/tex}
    = {tex}\int{/tex}dB cos {tex}\theta{/tex}
    = {tex}\int_\limits0^{2 \pi \mathrm{R}} \frac{\mu_0 I d l}{4 \pi}{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{\mathrm{R}}{\left(x^2+\mathrm{R}^2\right)^{\frac{3}{2}}}{/tex}
    {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mu_0 \mathrm{IR}^2}{2\left(\mathrm{R}^2+x^2\right)^{\frac{3}{2}}} \hat{i}{/tex}

question_html: A square shaped plane coil of area 100 cm2 of 200 turns carries a steady current of 5 A. It is placed in a uniform magnetic field of 0.2 T acting perpendicular to the plane of the coil. Calculate the torque on the coil when its plane makes an angle of 60o with the direction of the field. In which orientation will the coil be in stable equilibrium?
question_text: A square shaped plane coil of area 100 cm 2 of 200 turns carries a steady current of 5 A. It is placed in a uniform magnetic field of 0.2 T acting perpendicular to the plane of the coil. Calculate the torque on the coil when its plane makes an angle of 60 o with the direction of the field. In which orientation will the coil be in stable equilibrium?
explanation: {tex}\tau=N I B A \sin \theta{/tex} {tex}=200 \times 5 \times 0.2 \times 100 \times 10^{-4} \sin \left(90^{\circ}-60^{\circ}\right){/tex} = 1 Nm.
solution: {tex}\tau=N I B A \sin \theta{/tex} {tex}=200 \times 5 \times 0.2 \times 100 \times 10^{-4} \sin \left(90^{\circ}-60^{\circ}\right){/tex} = 1 Nm.
solution_html:
{tex}\tau=N I B A \sin \theta{/tex} {tex}=200 \times 5 \times 0.2 \times 100 \times 10^{-4} \sin \left(90^{\circ}-60^{\circ}\right){/tex}
= 1 Nm.

question_html:
1. Show how Biot-Savart law can be alternatively expressed in the form of Ampere's circuital law. Use this law to obtain the expression for the magnetic field inside a solenoid of length T, cross-sectional area A having N closely wound turns and carrying a steady current I
  2. Sketch the magnetic field lines for a finite solenoid. Explain why the field at the exterior midpoint is weak while at the interior it is uniform and strong.
question_text: Show how Biot-Savart law can be alternatively expressed in the form of Ampere's circuital law. Use this law to obtain the expression for the magnetic field inside a solenoid of length T, cross-sectional area A having N closely wound turns and carrying a steady current I Sketch the magnetic field lines for a finite solenoid. Explain why the field at the exterior midpoint is weak while at the interior it is uniform and strong.
explanation: A current carrying surface with small line elements of length dl where tangential components of magnetic field and sum of elements B.dl in an integral form be expressed as {tex}\oint \vec{B}{/tex} {tex}\cdot \overrightarrow{d l}{/tex} = {tex}\mu_0 i{/tex} This form is known as Ampere's circuital law Let 'n' be the number of turns per unit length. Then total number of turns in the length 'h' is nh. Hence, total enclosed current = nhl Using Ampere's circuital law {tex}\oint \vec{B} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}{/tex} = {tex}\mu_0{/tex} nhI Bh = {tex}\mu_0{/tex} nhI B = {tex}\mu_0{/tex} nl
solution: A current carrying surface with small line elements of length dl where tangential components of magnetic field and sum of elements B.dl in an integral form be expressed as {tex}\oint \vec{B}{/tex} {tex}\cdot \overrightarrow{d l}{/tex} = {tex}\mu_0 i{/tex} This form is known as Ampere's circuital law Let 'n' be the number of turns per unit length. Then total number of turns in the length 'h' is nh. Hence, total enclosed current = nhl Using Ampere's circuital law {tex}\oint \vec{B} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}{/tex} = {tex}\mu_0{/tex} nhI Bh = {tex}\mu_0{/tex} nhI B = {tex}\mu_0{/tex} nl
solution_html:
1. A current carrying surface with small line elements of length dl where tangential components of magnetic field and sum of elements B.dl in an integral form be expressed as
    {tex}\oint \vec{B}{/tex} {tex}\cdot \overrightarrow{d l}{/tex} = {tex}\mu_0 i{/tex}
    This form is known as Ampere's circuital law

    Let 'n' be the number of turns per unit length. Then total number of turns in the length 'h' is nh. Hence, total enclosed current = nhl Using Ampere's circuital law
    {tex}\oint \vec{B} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}{/tex} = {tex}\mu_0{/tex}nhI
    Bh = {tex}\mu_0{/tex}nhI
    B = {tex}\mu_0{/tex}nl
