question_html:
1. Write, using Biot-Savart law, the expression for the magnetic field element {tex}\overrightarrow{d l}{/tex} carrying current 1 at a distance {tex}\vec{r}{/tex} from it in a vector form. Hence derive the expression for the magnetic field due to a current carrying loop of radius R at a point P distant x from its centre along the axis of the loop.
  2. Explain how Biot-Savart law enables one to express the Ampere's circuital law in the integral form, viz.,{tex}\oint \vec{B} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}=\mu_{0} I{/tex} where I is the total current passing through the surface.
question_text: Write, using Biot-Savart law, the expression for the magnetic field element {tex}\overrightarrow{d l}{/tex} carrying current 1 at a distance {tex}\vec{r}{/tex} from it in a vector form. Hence derive the expression for the magnetic field due to a current carrying loop of radius R at a point P distant x from its centre along the axis of the loop. Explain how Biot-Savart law enables one to express the Ampere's circuital law in the integral form, viz., {tex}\oint \vec{B} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}=\mu_{0} I{/tex} where I is the total current passing through the surface.
explanation: According to Biot-Savart Law, magnetic field due to a current carrying element dl is: {tex}d\overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mu_0 \mathrm{I}}{4 \pi \mathrm{r}^3}(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{l}} \times \mathrm{r}){/tex} The magnetic field due to a current carrying circular loop is shown in the figure. Vertical components of the magnetic field cancel out by the diametrically opposite end on the wire. Horizontal component due to an element dl is given by: dB = {tex}\frac{\mu_0 \mathrm{I}}{4 \pi \mathrm{r}^2} \sin \theta \mathrm{dl}{/tex} dB = {tex}\frac{\mu_0 \mathrm{IR}}{4 \pi \mathrm{r}^3} \mathrm{dl}{/tex} {tex}\int \mathrm{dB}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mu_0 \mathrm{IR}}{4 \pi \mathrm{r}^3} \int \mathrm{dl}{/tex} B = {tex}\frac{\mu_0 \mathrm{IR}}{4 \pi\left(\mathrm{R}^2+\mathrm{x}^2\right)^{\frac{3}{2}}} 2 \pi \mathrm{R}{/tex} B = {tex}\frac{\mu_0 \mathrm{IR}^2}{2\left(\mathrm{R}^2+\mathrm{x}^2\right)^{\frac{3}{2}}}{/tex} From Biot-Savart Law, it can be concluded that the magnetic field around an infinitely long current carrying conductor is B = {tex}\frac{\mu_0I}{2\pi r}{/tex} or B {tex}\times{/tex} 2 {tex}\pi{/tex} r = {tex}\mu _0l{/tex} {tex}\int{/tex} B.dl = {tex}\mu _0l{/tex}
solution: According to Biot-Savart Law, magnetic field due to a current carrying element dl is: {tex}d\overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mu_0 \mathrm{I}}{4 \pi \mathrm{r}^3}(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{l}} \times \mathrm{r}){/tex} The magnetic field due to a current carrying circular loop is shown in the figure. Vertical components of the magnetic field cancel out by the diametrically opposite end on the wire. Horizontal component due to an element dl is given by: dB = {tex}\frac{\mu_0 \mathrm{I}}{4 \pi \mathrm{r}^2} \sin \theta \mathrm{dl}{/tex} dB = {tex}\frac{\mu_0 \mathrm{IR}}{4 \pi \mathrm{r}^3} \mathrm{dl}{/tex} {tex}\int \mathrm{dB}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mu_0 \mathrm{IR}}{4 \pi \mathrm{r}^3} \int \mathrm{dl}{/tex} B = {tex}\frac{\mu_0 \mathrm{IR}}{4 \pi\left(\mathrm{R}^2+\mathrm{x}^2\right)^{\frac{3}{2}}} 2 \pi \mathrm{R}{/tex} B = {tex}\frac{\mu_0 \mathrm{IR}^2}{2\left(\mathrm{R}^2+\mathrm{x}^2\right)^{\frac{3}{2}}}{/tex} From Biot-Savart Law, it can be concluded that the magnetic field around an infinitely long current carrying conductor is B = {tex}\frac{\mu_0I}{2\pi r}{/tex} or B {tex}\times{/tex} 2 {tex}\pi{/tex} r = {tex}\mu _0l{/tex} {tex}\int{/tex} B.dl = {tex}\mu _0l{/tex}
solution_html:
1. According to Biot-Savart Law, magnetic field due to a current carrying element dl is: {tex}d\overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mu_0 \mathrm{I}}{4 \pi \mathrm{r}^3}(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{l}} \times \mathrm{r}){/tex}
    The magnetic field due to a current carrying circular loop is shown in the figure. Vertical components of the magnetic field cancel out by the diametrically opposite end on the wire.
    Horizontal component due to an element dl is given by:
    dB = {tex}\frac{\mu_0 \mathrm{I}}{4 \pi \mathrm{r}^2} \sin \theta \mathrm{dl}{/tex}
    dB = {tex}\frac{\mu_0 \mathrm{IR}}{4 \pi \mathrm{r}^3} \mathrm{dl}{/tex}
    {tex}\int \mathrm{dB}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mu_0 \mathrm{IR}}{4 \pi \mathrm{r}^3} \int \mathrm{dl}{/tex}
    B = {tex}\frac{\mu_0 \mathrm{IR}}{4 \pi\left(\mathrm{R}^2+\mathrm{x}^2\right)^{\frac{3}{2}}} 2 \pi \mathrm{R}{/tex}
    B = {tex}\frac{\mu_0 \mathrm{IR}^2}{2\left(\mathrm{R}^2+\mathrm{x}^2\right)^{\frac{3}{2}}}{/tex}
  2. From Biot-Savart Law, it can be concluded that the magnetic field around an infinitely long current carrying conductor is B = {tex}\frac{\mu_0I}{2\pi r}{/tex}
    or B {tex}\times{/tex} 2{tex}\pi{/tex}r = {tex}\mu _0l{/tex}
    {tex}\int{/tex}B.dl = {tex}\mu _0l{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. Draw a labelled diagram of a moving coil galvanometer. Describe briefly its principle and working.
  2. Answer the following questions.
    1. Why is it necessary to introduce a cylindrical soft iron core inside the coil of a galvanometer?
    2. Increasing the current sensitivity of a galvanometer may not necessarily increase its voltage sensitivity. Explain with giving reasons
question_text: Draw a labelled diagram of a moving coil galvanometer. Describe briefly its principle and working. Answer the following questions. Why is it necessary to introduce a cylindrical soft iron core inside the coil of a galvanometer? Increasing the current sensitivity of a galvanometer may not necessarily increase its voltage sensitivity. Explain with giving reasons
explanation: Construction : It consists of a circular coil of insulated copper wire wound on a circular non magnetic frame. The frame is mounted vertically on a horizontal base provided with leveling screws on the base. The coil can be rotated on a vertical axis passing through its centre. A compass box is mounted horizontally at the centre of a circular scale. The compass box is circular in shape. It consists of a tiny, powerful magnetic needle pivoted at the centre of the coil. The magnetic needle is free to rotate in the horizontal plane. The circular scale is divided into four quadrants. Each quadrant is graduated from 0 0 to 90 0 . A long thin aluminium pointer is attached to the needle at its centre and at right angle to it. To avoid errors due to parallax a plane mirror is mounted below the compass needle. Working : The current to be measured is now sent through the coil, and produces a magnetic field, perpendicular to the plane of the coil, and directly proportional to the current. The magnitude of the magnetic field produced by the coil is B; the magnitude of the horizontal component the earth's magnetic field is B H . The compass needle aligns itself along the vector sum of B and B H after rotating through an angle theta from its original orientation, such that, tan theta = B/B H . Since the magnetic field of the earth is constant, and B depends directly on the current, the current is thus proportional to the tangent of the angle through which the needle has turned. A soft iron core is used in a moving coil galvanometer. The soft iron core attracts the magnetic lines of force and hence the strength of the magnetic field increases if we use soft iron core. Thus the sensitivity of galvanometer increases. Also, the use of soft iron core makes the magnetic field radial (i.e the plane of the coil will be always parallel to the direction of magnetic field). Current sensitivity does not depend upon resistance(R), whereas voltage sensitivity does. Current sensitivity = {tex}\frac{\theta }{I} = \frac{{NBA}}{K}{/tex} Voltage sensitivity = {tex}\frac{\theta }{V} = \frac{\theta }{{IR}} = \frac{{NBA}}{{(KR)}}{/tex} Current sensitivity can be increased by increasing the number of turns of the coil. However, this increases the resistance of the coil also (R is proportional to length of conductor). Since voltage sensitivity decreases with increase in resistance of the coil, the effect of increase in number of turns is nullified in the case of voltage sensitivity. Hence, there is no increase in voltage sensitivity.
solution: Construction : It consists of a circular coil of insulated copper wire wound on a circular non magnetic frame. The frame is mounted vertically on a horizontal base provided with leveling screws on the base. The coil can be rotated on a vertical axis passing through its centre. A compass box is mounted horizontally at the centre of a circular scale. The compass box is circular in shape. It consists of a tiny, powerful magnetic needle pivoted at the centre of the coil. The magnetic needle is free to rotate in the horizontal plane. The circular scale is divided into four quadrants. Each quadrant is graduated from 0 0 to 90 0 . A long thin aluminium pointer is attached to the needle at its centre and at right angle to it. To avoid errors due to parallax a plane mirror is mounted below the compass needle. Working : The current to be measured is now sent through the coil, and produces a magnetic field, perpendicular to the plane of the coil, and directly proportional to the current. The magnitude of the magnetic field produced by the coil is B; the magnitude of the horizontal component the earth's magnetic field is B H . The compass needle aligns itself along the vector sum of B and B H after rotating through an angle theta from its original orientation, such that, tan theta = B/B H . Since the magnetic field of the earth is constant, and B depends directly on the current, the current is thus proportional to the tangent of the angle through which the needle has turned. A soft iron core is used in a moving coil galvanometer. The soft iron core attracts the magnetic lines of force and hence the strength of the magnetic field increases if we use soft iron core. Thus the sensitivity of galvanometer increases. Also, the use of soft iron core makes the magnetic field radial (i.e the plane of the coil will be always parallel to the direction of magnetic field). Current sensitivity does not depend upon resistance(R), whereas voltage sensitivity does. Current sensitivity = {tex}\frac{\theta }{I} = \frac{{NBA}}{K}{/tex} Voltage sensitivity = {tex}\frac{\theta }{V} = \frac{\theta }{{IR}} = \frac{{NBA}}{{(KR)}}{/tex} Current sensitivity can be increased by increasing the number of turns of the coil. However, this increases the resistance of the coil also (R is proportional to length of conductor). Since voltage sensitivity decreases with increase in resistance of the coil, the effect of increase in number of turns is nullified in the case of voltage sensitivity. Hence, there is no increase in voltage sensitivity.
solution_html:
1. Construction: It consists of a circular coil of insulated copper wire wound on a circular non magnetic frame. The frame is mounted vertically on a horizontal base provided with leveling screws on the base. The coil can be rotated on a vertical axis passing through its centre. A compass box is mounted horizontally at the centre of a circular scale. The compass box is circular in shape. It consists of a tiny, powerful magnetic needle pivoted at the centre of the coil. The magnetic needle is free to rotate in the horizontal plane. The circular scale is divided into four quadrants. Each quadrant is graduated from 00 to 900. A long thin aluminium pointer is attached to the needle at its centre and at right angle to it. To avoid errors due to parallax a plane mirror is mounted below the compass needle.

    Working: The current to be measured is now sent through the coil, and produces a magnetic field, perpendicular to the plane of the coil, and directly proportional to the current. The magnitude of the magnetic field produced by the coil is B; the magnitude of the horizontal component the earth's magnetic field is BH. The compass needle aligns itself along the vector sum of B and BH after rotating through an angle theta from its original orientation, such that, tan theta = B/BH. Since the magnetic field of the earth is constant, and B depends directly on the current, the current is thus proportional to the tangent of the angle through which the needle has turned.

  2.  
    1. A soft iron core is used in a moving coil galvanometer. The soft iron core attracts the magnetic lines of force and hence the strength of the magnetic field increases if we use soft iron core. Thus the sensitivity of galvanometer increases. Also, the use of soft iron core makes the magnetic field radial (i.e the plane of the coil will be always parallel to the direction of magnetic field).
    2. Current sensitivity does not depend upon resistance(R), whereas voltage sensitivity does.
      Current sensitivity = {tex}\frac{\theta }{I} = \frac{{NBA}}{K}{/tex}
      Voltage sensitivity = {tex}\frac{\theta }{V} = \frac{\theta }{{IR}} = \frac{{NBA}}{{(KR)}}{/tex}
      Current sensitivity can be increased by increasing the number of turns of the coil. However, this increases the resistance of the coil also (R is proportional to length of conductor). Since voltage sensitivity decreases with increase in resistance of the coil, the effect of increase in number of turns is nullified in the case of voltage sensitivity. Hence, there is no increase in voltage sensitivity.

subject: Physics
question_html: Using the concept of force between two infinitely long parallel current carrying conductors define one ampere of current.
question_text: Using the concept of force between two infinitely long parallel current carrying conductors define one ampere of current.
explanation: One ampere current is the current which flows through each of two parallel uniform linear conductors of length 1m each, which are placed in free space at a distance of 1m from each other and for which they attract or repel each other with a force of 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -7 N in that space.
solution: One ampere current is the current which flows through each of two parallel uniform linear conductors of length 1m each, which are placed in free space at a distance of 1m from each other and for which they attract or repel each other with a force of 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -7 N in that space.

question_html: A rectangular coil of sides l and b carrying a current I is subjected to a uniform magnetic field B acting perpendicular to its plane. Obtain the expression for the torque acting on it.
question_text: A rectangular coil of sides l and b carrying a current I is subjected to a uniform magnetic field B acting perpendicular to its plane. Obtain the expression for the torque acting on it.
explanation: A rectangular coil PQRS is placed in a uniform magnetic field as shown in the figure below. Consider the plane of the loop makes an angle with the field. Forces on arm PQ and RS are equal and opposite and they cancel each other as they are collinear. Force on SP is F 1 and Force on QR is F 2 and are equal and opposite, with magnitude, {tex}F_1\;=\;F_2=I\;l\;B{/tex} Torque is present due to forces F 1 and F 2 . Thus, magnitude torque due to these forces on the coil will be given as {tex}\begin{array}{l}\tau\;=\;F_1\frac b2\sin\theta\;+\;F_2\frac b2\sin\theta\;\\=\;I\;lb\;B\;\sin\theta\;=\;I\;A\;B\;sin\theta\end{array}{/tex} where, A= lb (area of coil), {tex}\theta{/tex} is the angle between the field and normal to the coil. In vector form, {tex}\tau = M B \sin \theta \hat { \ { n } } = M \times B{/tex} where, M = N I A, is the magnetic moment of the current loop. According to the question, {tex}\theta = 0 ^ { \circ }{/tex} (as plane of coil is perpendicular to the field) Thus, {tex}\tau = 0{/tex} i.e. Torque acting on the coil will be zero.
solution: A rectangular coil PQRS is placed in a uniform magnetic field as shown in the figure below. Consider the plane of the loop makes an angle with the field. Forces on arm PQ and RS are equal and opposite and they cancel each other as they are collinear. Force on SP is F 1 and Force on QR is F 2 and are equal and opposite, with magnitude, {tex}F_1\;=\;F_2=I\;l\;B{/tex} Torque is present due to forces F 1 and F 2 . Thus, magnitude torque due to these forces on the coil will be given as {tex}\begin{array}{l}\tau\;=\;F_1\frac b2\sin\theta\;+\;F_2\frac b2\sin\theta\;\\=\;I\;lb\;B\;\sin\theta\;=\;I\;A\;B\;sin\theta\end{array}{/tex} where, A= lb (area of coil), {tex}\theta{/tex} is the angle between the field and normal to the coil. In vector form, {tex}\tau = M B \sin \theta \hat { \ { n } } = M \times B{/tex} where, M = N I A, is the magnetic moment of the current loop. According to the question, {tex}\theta = 0 ^ { \circ }{/tex} (as plane of coil is perpendicular to the field) Thus, {tex}\tau = 0{/tex} i.e. Torque acting on the coil will be zero.
solution_html:
A rectangular coil PQRS is placed in a uniform magnetic field as shown in the figure below.

Consider the plane of the loop makes an angle with the field. Forces on arm PQ and RS are equal and opposite and they cancel each other as they are collinear.
Force on SP is F1 and Force on QR is F2 and are equal and opposite, with magnitude,
{tex}F_1\;=\;F_2=I\;l\;B{/tex}
Torque is present due to forces F1 and F2. Thus, magnitude torque due to these forces on the coil will be given as
{tex}\begin{array}{l}\tau\;=\;F_1\frac b2\sin\theta\;+\;F_2\frac b2\sin\theta\;\\=\;I\;lb\;B\;\sin\theta\;=\;I\;A\;B\;sin\theta\end{array}{/tex}
where, A= lb (area of coil), {tex}\theta{/tex} is the angle between the field and normal to the coil.
In vector form,
{tex}\tau = M B \sin \theta \hat { \ { n } } = M \times B{/tex}
where, M = N I A, is the magnetic moment of the current loop.
According to the question,
{tex}\theta = 0 ^ { \circ }{/tex}(as plane of coil is perpendicular to the field)
Thus, {tex}\tau = 0{/tex} 
i.e. Torque acting on the coil will be zero.

question_html: State Ampere’s circuital law, expressing it in the integral form.
question_text: State Ampere’s circuital law, expressing it in the integral form.
explanation: It states that the line integral of a magnetic field ( {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}}{/tex} ) around any closed path or circuit is equal to {tex}\mu{/tex} 0 (absolute permeability of free space) times the total current (I) threading the closed circuit. Mathematically, {tex}\oint \overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}=\mu_{0} \mathrm{I}{/tex}
solution: It states that the line integral of a magnetic field ( {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}}{/tex} ) around any closed path or circuit is equal to {tex}\mu{/tex} 0 (absolute permeability of free space) times the total current (I) threading the closed circuit. Mathematically, {tex}\oint \overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}=\mu_{0} \mathrm{I}{/tex}
solution_html:
It states that the line integral of a magnetic field ({tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}}{/tex}) around any closed path or circuit is equal to {tex}\mu{/tex}0 (absolute permeability of free space) times the total current (I) threading the closed circuit.
Mathematically, {tex}\oint \overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}=\mu_{0} \mathrm{I}{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html: Why is it necessary to introduce a cylindrical soft iron core inside the coil of a galvanometer?
question_text: Why is it necessary to introduce a cylindrical soft iron core inside the coil of a galvanometer?
explanation: When a cylindrical soft iron core is used, the magnetic field lines tend to crowd through the core. It is because, soft iron core is ferromagnetic in nature. As a result, the strength of the magnetic field due to the field magnet increases, which in turn increases the sensitivity of the galvanometer.
solution: When a cylindrical soft iron core is used, the magnetic field lines tend to crowd through the core. It is because, soft iron core is ferromagnetic in nature. As a result, the strength of the magnetic field due to the field magnet increases, which in turn increases the sensitivity of the galvanometer.
solution_html: When a cylindrical soft iron core is used, the magnetic field lines tend to crowd through the core. It is because, soft iron core is ferromagnetic in nature. As a result, the strength of the magnetic field due to the field magnet increases, which in turn increases the sensitivity of the galvanometer.

question_html: Deduce an expression for the frequency of revolution of a charged particle in a magnetic field and show that it is independent of the velocity or energy of the particle.
question_text: Deduce an expression for the frequency of revolution of a charged particle in a magnetic field and show that it is independent of the velocity or energy of the particle.
explanation: When a particle of mass m and charge q, moves with a velocity v, in a uniform magnetic held B, it experiences a force F where {tex}\vec{F}=q(\vec{v} \times \vec{B}){/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Centripetal force, {tex}\frac{m v^{2}}{r}{/tex} = qvB and sin90 0 =1 or, r = {tex}\frac{m v}{q B_{\perp}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Frequency, f = {tex}\frac{v}{2 \pi r}=\frac{q B_{\perp}}{2 \pi m}{/tex} Hence, frequency is independent of the velocity or energy of the particle.
solution: When a particle of mass m and charge q, moves with a velocity v, in a uniform magnetic held B, it experiences a force F where {tex}\vec{F}=q(\vec{v} \times \vec{B}){/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Centripetal force, {tex}\frac{m v^{2}}{r}{/tex} = qvB and sin90 0 =1 or, r = {tex}\frac{m v}{q B_{\perp}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Frequency, f = {tex}\frac{v}{2 \pi r}=\frac{q B_{\perp}}{2 \pi m}{/tex} Hence, frequency is independent of the velocity or energy of the particle.
solution_html:
When a particle of mass m and charge q, moves with a velocity v, in a uniform magnetic held B, it experiences a force F where
{tex}\vec{F}=q(\vec{v} \times \vec{B}){/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Centripetal force, {tex}\frac{m v^{2}}{r}{/tex} = qvB and sin900 =1
or, r = {tex}\frac{m v}{q B_{\perp}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Frequency, f = {tex}\frac{v}{2 \pi r}=\frac{q B_{\perp}}{2 \pi m}{/tex}
Hence, frequency is independent of the velocity or energy of the particle.

question_html:
1. State Ampere's circuital law expressing it in the integral form.
  2. Two long co-axial insulated solenoids S1 and S2 of equal length are wound one over the other as shown in the figure. A steady current I flows through the inner solenoid S1 to the other end B which is connected to the outer solenoid S2 through which the some current I flows in the opposite direction so, as to come out at end A. If n1 and n2 are the number of turns per unit length, find the magnitude and direction of the net magnetic field at a point
    1. inside on the axis and
    2. outside the combined system.
question_text: State Ampere's circuital law expressing it in the integral form. Two long co-axial insulated solenoids S 1 and S 2 of equal length are wound one over the other as shown in the figure. A steady current I flows through the inner solenoid S 1 to the other end B which is connected to the outer solenoid S 2 through which the some current I flows in the opposite direction so, as to come out at end A. If n 1 and n 2 are the number of turns per unit length, find the magnitude and direction of the net magnetic field at a point inside on the axis and outside the combined system.
explanation: Ampere’s circuital law: The line integral of the magnetic field around a closed path is {tex}\mu_0{/tex} times of total current enclosed by the path. {tex}\oint\overrightarrow B.\overrightarrow{dl}=\mu_0I {/tex} a. The magnetic field due to a current carrying solenoid: {tex}B=\mu_0nI{/tex} where n = number of turns per unit length i = current through the solenoid Now, the magnetic field due to solenoid S 1 will be in the upward direction and the magnetic field due to S 2 will be in the downward direction (by right-hand screw rule). {tex}B_{net}={B_S}_1-{B_S}_2{/tex} {tex}B_{net}=\mu_0n_1I-\mu_0n_2I=\mu_0I\left(n_1-n_2\right){/tex} The direction of field is in the upward direction. b. The magnetic field is zero outside a solenoid.
solution: Ampere’s circuital law: The line integral of the magnetic field around a closed path is {tex}\mu_0{/tex} times of total current enclosed by the path. {tex}\oint\overrightarrow B.\overrightarrow{dl}=\mu_0I {/tex} a. The magnetic field due to a current carrying solenoid: {tex}B=\mu_0nI{/tex} where n = number of turns per unit length i = current through the solenoid Now, the magnetic field due to solenoid S 1 will be in the upward direction and the magnetic field due to S 2 will be in the downward direction (by right-hand screw rule). {tex}B_{net}={B_S}_1-{B_S}_2{/tex} {tex}B_{net}=\mu_0n_1I-\mu_0n_2I=\mu_0I\left(n_1-n_2\right){/tex} The direction of field is in the upward direction. b. The magnetic field is zero outside a solenoid.
solution_html:
1. Ampere’s circuital law: The line integral of the magnetic field around a closed path is {tex}\mu_0{/tex}times of total current enclosed by the path.
    {tex}\oint\overrightarrow B.\overrightarrow{dl}=\mu_0I {/tex}
  2. a. The magnetic field due to a current carrying solenoid:

    {tex}B=\mu_0nI{/tex}
    where n = number of turns per unit length
    i = current through the solenoid
    Now, the magnetic field due to solenoid S1 will be in the upward direction and the magnetic field due to S2 will be in the downward direction (by right-hand screw rule).
    {tex}B_{net}={B_S}_1-{B_S}_2{/tex}
    {tex}B_{net}=\mu_0n_1I-\mu_0n_2I=\mu_0I\left(n_1-n_2\right){/tex}
    The direction of field is in the upward direction.

    b. The magnetic field is zero outside a solenoid.

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. Two long straight parallel conductors 'a' and 'b' carrying steady currents Ia and lb respectively are separated by a distance 'd'. Write the magnitude and direction, what is the nature and magnitude of the force between the two conductors?
  2. Show with the help of a diagram, how the force between the two conductors would change when the currents in them flow in the opposite directions.
question_text: Two long straight parallel conductors 'a' and 'b' carrying steady currents I a and l b respectively are separated by a distance 'd'. Write the magnitude and direction, what is the nature and magnitude of the force between the two conductors? Show with the help of a diagram, how the force between the two conductors would change when the currents in them flow in the opposite directions.
explanation: Let a and b be two long straight parallel conductors, I a and l b are the current flowing through them and separated by a distance d. Magnetic field induction at a point P on a conductor b due to current I a passing through conductor a is B 1 = {tex}\;\frac{\mu_oI_a}{2\pi d}{/tex} From right hand rule, the direction of this field is downwards. The conductor b carrying a current I b will experience a force F 2 due to field B 1 . The direction of this force is left, towards conductor a, according to vector rule. Now, Force per unit length of the wire {tex}F _ { 2 } = B _ { 1 } I _ { b } \times 1 = B _ { 1 } I _ { b }{/tex} F 2 {tex}\;=\;\frac{\mu_o\;I_a\;I_b}{2\pi d}{/tex} Conductor 'a' also experiences the same amount of force and is directed towards b. Thus, F 2 = -F 1 Hence the conductors a and b attract each other when the currents flowing in same direction. Now, let the direction of current in conductor b be reversed. The magnetic field B 1 at point P due to current I a flowing through conductor a will be downwards. Similarly, the magnetic field B 2 at point Q due to current I b passing through conductor b will also be downward as shown. According to vector rule, the force F 1 on conductor a will be towards the left. Also, the force F 2 on conductor b will be towards the right. Hence, the two conductors will repel each other as shown when the currents flow in opposite direction.
solution: Let a and b be two long straight parallel conductors, I a and l b are the current flowing through them and separated by a distance d. Magnetic field induction at a point P on a conductor b due to current I a passing through conductor a is B 1 = {tex}\;\frac{\mu_oI_a}{2\pi d}{/tex} From right hand rule, the direction of this field is downwards. The conductor b carrying a current I b will experience a force F 2 due to field B 1 . The direction of this force is left, towards conductor a, according to vector rule. Now, Force per unit length of the wire {tex}F _ { 2 } = B _ { 1 } I _ { b } \times 1 = B _ { 1 } I _ { b }{/tex} F 2 {tex}\;=\;\frac{\mu_o\;I_a\;I_b}{2\pi d}{/tex} Conductor 'a' also experiences the same amount of force and is directed towards b. Thus, F 2 = -F 1 Hence the conductors a and b attract each other when the currents flowing in same direction. Now, let the direction of current in conductor b be reversed. The magnetic field B 1 at point P due to current I a flowing through conductor a will be downwards. Similarly, the magnetic field B 2 at point Q due to current I b passing through conductor b will also be downward as shown. According to vector rule, the force F 1 on conductor a will be towards the left. Also, the force F 2 on conductor b will be towards the right. Hence, the two conductors will repel each other as shown when the currents flow in opposite direction.
solution_html:
1. Let a and b be two long straight parallel conductors, Ia and lb are the current flowing through them and separated by a distance d.

    Magnetic field induction at a point P on a conductor b due to current Ia passing through conductor a is
    B1 = {tex}\;\frac{\mu_oI_a}{2\pi d}{/tex}
    From right hand rule, the direction of this field is downwards.
    The conductor b carrying a current Ib will experience a force F2 due to field B1. The direction of this force is left, towards conductor a, according to vector rule.
    Now, Force per unit length of the wire
    {tex}F _ { 2 } = B _ { 1 } I _ { b } \times 1 = B _ { 1 } I _ { b }{/tex}
    F2 {tex}\;=\;\frac{\mu_o\;I_a\;I_b}{2\pi d}{/tex}
    Conductor 'a' also experiences the same amount of force and is directed towards b.
    Thus, F2 = -F1
    Hence the conductors a and b attract each other when the currents flowing in same direction.


  2. Now, let the direction of current in conductor b be reversed. The magnetic field B1 at point P due to current Ia flowing through conductor a will be downwards. Similarly, the magnetic field B2 at point Q due to current Ib passing through conductor b will also be downward as shown. According to vector rule, the force F1 on conductor a will be towards the left. Also, the force F2 on conductor b will be towards the right. Hence, the two conductors will repel each other as shown when the currents flow in opposite direction.

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. State using a suitable diagram, the working principle of a moving coil galvanometer. What is the function of a radial magnetic field and the soft iron core used in it?
  2. For converting a galvanometer into an ammeter, a shunt resistance of small value is used in parallel, whereas in the case of a voltmeter a resistance of large value is used in series. Explain, why?
question_text: State using a suitable diagram, the working principle of a moving coil galvanometer. What is the function of a radial magnetic field and the soft iron core used in it? For converting a galvanometer into an ammeter, a shunt resistance of small value is used in parallel, whereas in the case of a voltmeter a resistance of large value is used in series. Explain, why?
explanation: Priciple and working: When current is passed in the coil, it experiences torque due to magnetic force. When the magnetic field is radial, as in the case of cylindrical pole pieces and soft iron core, then in every position of coil the plane of the coil, is parallel to the magnetic field lines, so that θ = 90° and sin 90° = 1 Deflecting torque = NIAB If C is the torsional rigidity of the wire and {tex}\theta{/tex} is the twist of suspension strip, then restoring torque {tex}= C\theta{/tex} For equilibrium, deflecting torque = restoring torque so, {tex}\theta \propto I{/tex} So deflection of coil is directly proportional to current flowing in the coil and hence we can construct a linear scale. In other words, the radial magnetic field makes the scale linear. To produce radial magnetic field pole pieces of permanent magnet are made cylindrical and a soft iron core is placed between them. The soft iron core helps in making the field radial and reduce energy losses produced due to eddy currents. A voltmeter is used to measure potential difference across a resistance in an electrical circuit. It is connected in parallel across the resistance. If a voltmeter has very high resistance, it will not affect the resistance of circuit, hence reading will be true. That is why while using a moving coil galvanometer on a voltmeter, a high resistance in series is required. An ammeter is used to measure current in circuit, hence it is connected in series with the circuit. If an ammeter has very low resistance it will not affect the circuit - resistance and so reading will be true. That is why while using a moving coil galvanometer as an ammeter, a shunt (small resistance in parallel) is used.
solution: Priciple and working: When current is passed in the coil, it experiences torque due to magnetic force. When the magnetic field is radial, as in the case of cylindrical pole pieces and soft iron core, then in every position of coil the plane of the coil, is parallel to the magnetic field lines, so that θ = 90° and sin 90° = 1 Deflecting torque = NIAB If C is the torsional rigidity of the wire and {tex}\theta{/tex} is the twist of suspension strip, then restoring torque {tex}= C\theta{/tex} For equilibrium, deflecting torque = restoring torque so, {tex}\theta \propto I{/tex} So deflection of coil is directly proportional to current flowing in the coil and hence we can construct a linear scale. In other words, the radial magnetic field makes the scale linear. To produce radial magnetic field pole pieces of permanent magnet are made cylindrical and a soft iron core is placed between them. The soft iron core helps in making the field radial and reduce energy losses produced due to eddy currents. A voltmeter is used to measure potential difference across a resistance in an electrical circuit. It is connected in parallel across the resistance. If a voltmeter has very high resistance, it will not affect the resistance of circuit, hence reading will be true. That is why while using a moving coil galvanometer on a voltmeter, a high resistance in series is required. An ammeter is used to measure current in circuit, hence it is connected in series with the circuit. If an ammeter has very low resistance it will not affect the circuit - resistance and so reading will be true. That is why while using a moving coil galvanometer as an ammeter, a shunt (small resistance in parallel) is used.
solution_html:
1. Priciple and working: When current is passed in the coil, it experiences torque due to magnetic force.

    When the magnetic field is radial, as in the case of cylindrical pole pieces and soft iron core, then in every position of coil the plane of the coil, is parallel to the magnetic field lines, so that θ = 90° and sin 90° = 1  
    Deflecting torque = NIAB 
    If C is the torsional rigidity of the wire and {tex}\theta{/tex} is the twist of suspension strip, then
    restoring torque {tex}= C\theta{/tex}
    For equilibrium, deflecting torque = restoring torque  
    so, {tex}\theta \propto I{/tex}
    So deflection of coil is directly proportional to current flowing in the coil and hence we can construct a linear scale. In other words, the radial magnetic field makes the scale linear. To produce radial magnetic field pole pieces of permanent magnet are made cylindrical and a soft iron core is placed between them. The soft iron core helps in making the field radial and reduce energy losses produced due to eddy currents. 
  2. A voltmeter is used to measure potential difference across a resistance in an electrical circuit. It is connected in parallel across the resistance. If a voltmeter has very high resistance, it will not affect the resistance of circuit, hence reading will be true. That is why while using a moving coil galvanometer on a voltmeter, a high resistance in series is required.
    An ammeter is used to measure current in circuit, hence it is connected in series with the circuit. If an ammeter has very low resistance it will not affect the circuit - resistance and so reading will be true. That is why while using a moving coil galvanometer as an ammeter, a shunt (small resistance in parallel) is used.

question_html:
1. State Biot-Savart's law in vector form expressing the magnetic field due to an element dl carrying current I at a distance r from the element.
  2. Write the expression for the magnitude of the magnetic field at the centre of a circular loop of radius r carrying a steady current I. Draw the field lines due to the current loop.
question_text: State Biot-Savart's law in vector form expressing the magnetic field due to an element dl carrying current I at a distance r from the element. Write the expression for the magnitude of the magnetic field at the centre of a circular loop of radius r carrying a steady current I. Draw the field lines due to the current loop.
explanation: According to Biot-Savart's law, the magnetic held due to a current-carrying element {tex}\vec {dl}{/tex} carrying the current I at a point P with the position vector {tex}\vec r{/tex} is given by {tex}d \vec{B}=\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} I\left(\frac{\vec{d} l \times \vec{r}}{r^{3}}\right){/tex} The magnetic field at the center of loop, B = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I}{2 r}{/tex} Field lines:
solution: According to Biot-Savart's law, the magnetic held due to a current-carrying element {tex}\vec {dl}{/tex} carrying the current I at a point P with the position vector {tex}\vec r{/tex} is given by {tex}d \vec{B}=\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} I\left(\frac{\vec{d} l \times \vec{r}}{r^{3}}\right){/tex} The magnetic field at the center of loop, B = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I}{2 r}{/tex} Field lines:
solution_html:
1. According to Biot-Savart's law, the magnetic held due to a current-carrying element {tex}\vec {dl}{/tex} carrying  the current I at a point P with the position vector {tex}\vec r{/tex} is given by {tex}d \vec{B}=\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} I\left(\frac{\vec{d} l \times \vec{r}}{r^{3}}\right){/tex}
  2. The magnetic field at the center of loop, B = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I}{2 r}{/tex}

question_html: Two very small identical circular loops, (1) and (2), carrying equal currents I are placed vertically (with respect to the plane of the paper) with their geometrical axes perpendicular to each other as shown in the figure. Find the magnitude and direction of the net magnetic field produced at point O.
question_text: Two very small identical circular loops, (1) and (2), carrying equal currents I are placed vertically (with respect to the plane of the paper) with their geometrical axes perpendicular to each other as shown in the figure. Find the magnitude and direction of the net magnetic field produced at point O.
explanation: B = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I r^{2}}{2\left(r^{2}+x^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}}{/tex} where, / = current through the loop a = radius of the loop r = distance of O from the centre of the loop. The net field at O, {tex}B_{0}=\sqrt{2} B=\frac{\sqrt{2} \mu_{0} I r^{2}}{2\left(r^{2}+x^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}}{/tex} For small loop (r << x), B 0 = {tex}\frac{\sqrt{2} \mu_{0} I}{2 x^{3}} r^{2}{/tex} The direction of B 0 is {tex}45^{\circ}{/tex} with either of the field.
solution: B = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I r^{2}}{2\left(r^{2}+x^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}}{/tex} where, / = current through the loop a = radius of the loop r = distance of O from the centre of the loop. The net field at O, {tex}B_{0}=\sqrt{2} B=\frac{\sqrt{2} \mu_{0} I r^{2}}{2\left(r^{2}+x^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}}{/tex} For small loop (r << x), B 0 = {tex}\frac{\sqrt{2} \mu_{0} I}{2 x^{3}} r^{2}{/tex} The direction of B 0 is {tex}45^{\circ}{/tex} with either of the field.
solution_html:
B = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I r^{2}}{2\left(r^{2}+x^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}}{/tex}where, / = current through the loop
a = radius of the loop
r = distance of O from the centre of the loop.

The net field at O, {tex}B_{0}=\sqrt{2} B=\frac{\sqrt{2} \mu_{0} I r^{2}}{2\left(r^{2}+x^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}}{/tex}
For small loop (r << x), B0 = {tex}\frac{\sqrt{2} \mu_{0} I}{2 x^{3}} r^{2}{/tex}
The direction of B0 is {tex}45^{\circ}{/tex} with either of the field.

question_html: A rectangular coil of sides 'I' and ‘b' carrying a current I is subjected to a uniform magnetic field {tex}\vec{B}{/tex} acting perpendicular to its plane. Obtain the expression for the torque acting on it.
question_text: A rectangular coil of sides 'I' and ‘b' carrying a current I is subjected to a uniform magnetic field {tex}\vec{B}{/tex} acting perpendicular to its plane. Obtain the expression for the torque acting on it.
explanation: Magnetic moment associated with the current carrying coil is {tex}\vec{m}=L A \hat{n}=\Pi l b \hat{n}{/tex} where {tex}\hat{n}{/tex} is a unit vector perpendicular to the plane of the coil. Torque, {tex}\vec{\tau}=\vec{m} \times \vec{B}=\Pi b \hat{n} \times \vec{B}=m b \overrightarrow{0}=\overrightarrow{0}{/tex} This is because {tex}\hat{n}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{B}{/tex} are eithet parallel or antiparallel vectors.
solution: Magnetic moment associated with the current carrying coil is {tex}\vec{m}=L A \hat{n}=\Pi l b \hat{n}{/tex} where {tex}\hat{n}{/tex} is a unit vector perpendicular to the plane of the coil. Torque, {tex}\vec{\tau}=\vec{m} \times \vec{B}=\Pi b \hat{n} \times \vec{B}=m b \overrightarrow{0}=\overrightarrow{0}{/tex} This is because {tex}\hat{n}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{B}{/tex} are eithet parallel or antiparallel vectors.
solution_html:
Magnetic moment associated with the current carrying coil is {tex}\vec{m}=L A \hat{n}=\Pi l b \hat{n}{/tex}
where {tex}\hat{n}{/tex} is a unit vector perpendicular to the plane of the coil.
Torque,{tex}\vec{\tau}=\vec{m} \times \vec{B}=\Pi b \hat{n} \times \vec{B}=m b \overrightarrow{0}=\overrightarrow{0}{/tex}
This is because {tex}\hat{n}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{B}{/tex} are eithet parallel or antiparallel vectors.

question_html: Using Biot-Savart's law, derive the expression for the magnitude of the magnetic field at the centre of a circular loop of radius r carrying a steady current I. Draw the field lines due to the current loop.
question_text: Using Biot-Savart's law, derive the expression for the magnitude of the magnetic field at the centre of a circular loop of radius r carrying a steady current I. Draw the field lines due to the current loop.
explanation: direction of dB is perpendicular to the plane containing, dl and r and is given by right hand screw rule. As the angle between Idl and r is 90° the magnitude of the magnetic induction dB is given by, {tex}\begin{equation} \vec{d} B_x=\frac{\mu_0}{4 \pi} \frac{I \delta I \sin \alpha}{r^2} \end{equation}{/tex} The component of d dB along the axis, {tex}\begin{equation} \overrightarrow{d B c}=\frac{\mu_0 I d l}{4 \pi r^2} \sin \alpha \end{equation}{/tex} But {tex}\begin{equation} \sin \alpha=\frac{R}{r} \end{equation}{/tex} and {tex}\begin{equation} r=\left(R^2+x^2\right)^{1 / 2} \end{equation}{/tex} {tex}\begin{equation} \therefore \vec{d} B_x=\frac{\mu_0 I d l}{4 \pi r^2} \cdot \frac{R}{r}=\frac{\mu_0 I R}{4 \pi r^3} d l \end{equation}{/tex} {tex}\begin{equation} =\frac{\mu_0 I R}{4 \pi\left(R^2+x^2\right)^{3 / 2}} d l \end{equation}{/tex} Total field {tex}\begin{equation} \vec{B} \end{equation}{/tex} {tex}\begin{equation} =\oint \frac{\mu_0 I R}{4 \pi\left(R^2+x^2\right)^{3 / 2}} d l \end{equation}{/tex} {tex}\begin{equation} =\frac{\mu_0 I R}{4 \pi\left(R^2+x^2\right)^{3 / 2}} \oint d l \end{equation}{/tex} But, {tex}\begin{equation} \oint \mathrm{d} \mathrm{l} \oint \mathrm{dl} \end{equation}{/tex} = length of the loop = 2 {tex}\pi{/tex} R Therefore, {tex}\begin{equation} B=\frac{\mu_0 I R}{2 \pi\left(R^2+x^2\right)^{3 / 2}}(2 \pi R) \end{equation}{/tex} {tex}\begin{equation} \Rightarrow \vec{B}=B_x i=\frac{\mu_0 I R^2}{2\left(R^2+x^2\right)^{3 / 2}} \hat{i} \end{equation}{/tex} If the coil contain N turns then {tex}B=\frac{\mu_0 N I R^2}{2\left(R^2+x^2\right)^{3 / 2}}{/tex} tesla.
solution: direction of dB is perpendicular to the plane containing, dl and r and is given by right hand screw rule. As the angle between Idl and r is 90° the magnitude of the magnetic induction dB is given by, {tex}\begin{equation} \vec{d} B_x=\frac{\mu_0}{4 \pi} \frac{I \delta I \sin \alpha}{r^2} \end{equation}{/tex} The component of d dB along the axis, {tex}\begin{equation} \overrightarrow{d B c}=\frac{\mu_0 I d l}{4 \pi r^2} \sin \alpha \end{equation}{/tex} But {tex}\begin{equation} \sin \alpha=\frac{R}{r} \end{equation}{/tex} and {tex}\begin{equation} r=\left(R^2+x^2\right)^{1 / 2} \end{equation}{/tex} {tex}\begin{equation} \therefore \vec{d} B_x=\frac{\mu_0 I d l}{4 \pi r^2} \cdot \frac{R}{r}=\frac{\mu_0 I R}{4 \pi r^3} d l \end{equation}{/tex} {tex}\begin{equation} =\frac{\mu_0 I R}{4 \pi\left(R^2+x^2\right)^{3 / 2}} d l \end{equation}{/tex} Total field {tex}\begin{equation} \vec{B} \end{equation}{/tex} {tex}\begin{equation} =\oint \frac{\mu_0 I R}{4 \pi\left(R^2+x^2\right)^{3 / 2}} d l \end{equation}{/tex} {tex}\begin{equation} =\frac{\mu_0 I R}{4 \pi\left(R^2+x^2\right)^{3 / 2}} \oint d l \end{equation}{/tex} But, {tex}\begin{equation} \oint \mathrm{d} \mathrm{l} \oint \mathrm{dl} \end{equation}{/tex} = length of the loop = 2 {tex}\pi{/tex} R Therefore, {tex}\begin{equation} B=\frac{\mu_0 I R}{2 \pi\left(R^2+x^2\right)^{3 / 2}}(2 \pi R) \end{equation}{/tex} {tex}\begin{equation} \Rightarrow \vec{B}=B_x i=\frac{\mu_0 I R^2}{2\left(R^2+x^2\right)^{3 / 2}} \hat{i} \end{equation}{/tex} If the coil contain N turns then {tex}B=\frac{\mu_0 N I R^2}{2\left(R^2+x^2\right)^{3 / 2}}{/tex} tesla.
solution_html:
direction of dB is perpendicular to the plane containing, dl and r and is given by right hand screw rule. As the angle between Idl and r is 90° the magnitude of the magnetic induction  dB is given by,
{tex}\begin{equation} \vec{d} B_x=\frac{\mu_0}{4 \pi} \frac{I \delta I \sin \alpha}{r^2} \end{equation}{/tex}
The component of d dB along the axis,
{tex}\begin{equation} \overrightarrow{d B c}=\frac{\mu_0 I d l}{4 \pi r^2} \sin \alpha \end{equation}{/tex}
But {tex}\begin{equation} \sin \alpha=\frac{R}{r} \end{equation}{/tex} and {tex}\begin{equation} r=\left(R^2+x^2\right)^{1 / 2} \end{equation}{/tex}
{tex}\begin{equation} \therefore \vec{d} B_x=\frac{\mu_0 I d l}{4 \pi r^2} \cdot \frac{R}{r}=\frac{\mu_0 I R}{4 \pi r^3} d l \end{equation}{/tex}
{tex}\begin{equation} =\frac{\mu_0 I R}{4 \pi\left(R^2+x^2\right)^{3 / 2}} d l \end{equation}{/tex}
Total field {tex}\begin{equation} \vec{B} \end{equation}{/tex}
{tex}\begin{equation} =\oint \frac{\mu_0 I R}{4 \pi\left(R^2+x^2\right)^{3 / 2}} d l \end{equation}{/tex}
{tex}\begin{equation} =\frac{\mu_0 I R}{4 \pi\left(R^2+x^2\right)^{3 / 2}} \oint d l \end{equation}{/tex}
But, {tex}\begin{equation} \oint \mathrm{d} \mathrm{l} \oint \mathrm{dl} \end{equation}{/tex} = length of the loop =  2 {tex}\pi{/tex}R
Therefore, {tex}\begin{equation} B=\frac{\mu_0 I R}{2 \pi\left(R^2+x^2\right)^{3 / 2}}(2 \pi R) \end{equation}{/tex}
{tex}\begin{equation} \Rightarrow \vec{B}=B_x i=\frac{\mu_0 I R^2}{2\left(R^2+x^2\right)^{3 / 2}} \hat{i} \end{equation}{/tex}
If the coil contain N turns then
{tex}B=\frac{\mu_0 N I R^2}{2\left(R^2+x^2\right)^{3 / 2}}{/tex} tesla.

question_text: Three particles having charges in the ratio of 2 : 3 : 5 produces the same point on the photographic film in the Thomson experiment. Their masses are in the ratio of:

options.explanation: As the three charges produce same spot on the photographic film, so they have must same value of specific charge. Thus, the ratio of masses will be 2 : 3 : 5.

question_html: A long solenoid with closely wound turns has n turns, per unit of its length. A steady current I flows through this solenoid. Use Ampere's circuital law to obtain an expression, for the magnetic field, at a point on its axis and close to its mid point. Draw its field lines.
question_text: A long solenoid with closely wound turns has n turns, per unit of its length. A steady current I flows through this solenoid. Use Ampere's circuital law to obtain an expression, for the magnetic field, at a point on its axis and close to its mid point. Draw its field lines.
explanation: Let I be the current flowing in the solenoid, then by right hand rule, the magnetic field is parallel to the axis of the solenoid. Field outside the solenoid: Consider a closed path abcd Applying Ampere’s law to this path {tex}\oint \mathrm{B\cdot dl}{/tex} = {tex}\mu \times 0{/tex} (since net current enclosed by path is zero) As dI {tex}\neq{/tex} 0 {tex}\therefore{/tex} B = 0 This means that the magnetic field outside the solenoid is zero. Field inside the solenoid: Consider a closed path pqrs The line integral of magnetic field along path pqrs is {tex}\oint_{\mathrm{pqrs}} \vec{B} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{dl}}=\int_{\mathrm{pq}}{/tex} {tex}\vec{B} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{dl}}+\int_{\mathrm{qr}} \vec{B} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{dl}}{/tex} + {tex}\int_{\mathrm{rs}} \vec{B} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{dl}}+ \int_{\mathrm{sp}} \vec{B} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{dl}} {/tex} ...(i) For path pq, B and d I are along the same direction, {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\int_{\mathrm{pq}} \vec{B} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{dl}}{/tex} = {tex}\int{/tex} B dl = Bl (pq = l say) For path qr and sp, B and dI are mutually perpendicular. {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\int_{\mathrm{qr}} \vec{B} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{dl}}{/tex} = {tex}\int_{\mathrm{sp}} \vec{B} \cdot d \vec{l}{/tex} = {tex}\int B \mathrm{dl}{/tex} cos 90 o = 0 For path rs, B = 0 (since field is zero outside a solenoid) {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\int_{\mathrm{Is}} \vec{B} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{dl}}{/tex} = 0 In view of these, equation (i) gives {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\oint_{\mathrm{pqrs}} \vec{B} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{dl}}=\int_{\mathrm{pq}} \vec{B} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{dl}}{/tex} = Bl ...(ii) By Ampere's law {tex}\oint \mathrm{B} . \mathrm{dl}{/tex} = {tex}\mu \times{/tex} net current enclosed by path {tex}\therefore{/tex} Bl = {tex}\mu_0{/tex} (nl l) {tex}\therefore{/tex} B = {tex}\mu_0{/tex} nl
solution: Let I be the current flowing in the solenoid, then by right hand rule, the magnetic field is parallel to the axis of the solenoid. Field outside the solenoid: Consider a closed path abcd Applying Ampere’s law to this path {tex}\oint \mathrm{B\cdot dl}{/tex} = {tex}\mu \times 0{/tex} (since net current enclosed by path is zero) As dI {tex}\neq{/tex} 0 {tex}\therefore{/tex} B = 0 This means that the magnetic field outside the solenoid is zero. Field inside the solenoid: Consider a closed path pqrs The line integral of magnetic field along path pqrs is {tex}\oint_{\mathrm{pqrs}} \vec{B} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{dl}}=\int_{\mathrm{pq}}{/tex} {tex}\vec{B} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{dl}}+\int_{\mathrm{qr}} \vec{B} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{dl}}{/tex} + {tex}\int_{\mathrm{rs}} \vec{B} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{dl}}+ \int_{\mathrm{sp}} \vec{B} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{dl}} {/tex} ...(i) For path pq, B and d I are along the same direction, {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\int_{\mathrm{pq}} \vec{B} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{dl}}{/tex} = {tex}\int{/tex} B dl = Bl (pq = l say) For path qr and sp, B and dI are mutually perpendicular. {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\int_{\mathrm{qr}} \vec{B} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{dl}}{/tex} = {tex}\int_{\mathrm{sp}} \vec{B} \cdot d \vec{l}{/tex} = {tex}\int B \mathrm{dl}{/tex} cos 90 o = 0 For path rs, B = 0 (since field is zero outside a solenoid) {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\int_{\mathrm{Is}} \vec{B} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{dl}}{/tex} = 0 In view of these, equation (i) gives {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\oint_{\mathrm{pqrs}} \vec{B} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{dl}}=\int_{\mathrm{pq}} \vec{B} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{dl}}{/tex} = Bl ...(ii) By Ampere's law {tex}\oint \mathrm{B} . \mathrm{dl}{/tex} = {tex}\mu \times{/tex} net current enclosed by path {tex}\therefore{/tex} Bl = {tex}\mu_0{/tex} (nl l) {tex}\therefore{/tex} B = {tex}\mu_0{/tex} nl
solution_html:
Let I be the current flowing in the solenoid, then by right hand rule, the magnetic field is parallel to the axis of the solenoid.

Field outside the solenoid: Consider a closed path abcd Applying Ampere’s law to this path {tex}\oint \mathrm{B\cdot dl}{/tex} = {tex}\mu \times 0{/tex} (since net current enclosed by path is zero)
As dI {tex}\neq{/tex} 0 {tex}\therefore{/tex} B = 0
This means that the magnetic field outside the solenoid is zero.
Field inside the solenoid: Consider a closed path pqrs The line integral of magnetic field along path pqrs is
{tex}\oint_{\mathrm{pqrs}} \vec{B} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{dl}}=\int_{\mathrm{pq}}{/tex}
{tex}\vec{B} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{dl}}+\int_{\mathrm{qr}} \vec{B} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{dl}}{/tex} + {tex}\int_{\mathrm{rs}} \vec{B} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{dl}}+ \int_{\mathrm{sp}} \vec{B} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{dl}} {/tex} ...(i)
For path pq, B and d I are along the same direction,
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\int_{\mathrm{pq}} \vec{B} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{dl}}{/tex} = {tex}\int{/tex} B dl = Bl
(pq = l say)
For path qr and sp, B and dI are mutually perpendicular.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\int_{\mathrm{qr}} \vec{B} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{dl}}{/tex} = {tex}\int_{\mathrm{sp}} \vec{B} \cdot d \vec{l}{/tex} = {tex}\int B \mathrm{dl}{/tex}
cos 90o = 0
For path rs, B = 0 (since field is zero outside a solenoid)
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\int_{\mathrm{Is}} \vec{B} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{dl}}{/tex} = 0
In view of these, equation (i) gives
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\oint_{\mathrm{pqrs}} \vec{B} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{dl}}=\int_{\mathrm{pq}} \vec{B} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{dl}}{/tex} = Bl ...(ii)
By Ampere's law {tex}\oint \mathrm{B} . \mathrm{dl}{/tex} = {tex}\mu \times{/tex} net current enclosed by path
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Bl = {tex}\mu_0{/tex} (nl l) {tex}\therefore{/tex} B = {tex}\mu_0{/tex} nl

question_html: The current flowing in the galvanometer G when the key K2 is kept open is I. On closing the key K2 the current in the galvanometer becomes {tex}\frac1n{/tex},  where n is an integer. Obtain an expression for resistance Rg of the galvanometer in terms of R, S, and n. To what form does this expression reduce when the value of R is very large as compared to S?
question_text: The current flowing in the galvanometer G when the key K 2 is kept open is I. On closing the key K 2 the current in the galvanometer becomes {tex}\frac1n{/tex} , where n is an integer. Obtain an expression for resistance R g of the galvanometer in terms of R, S, and n. To what form does this expression reduce when the value of R is very large as compared to S?
explanation: With key K 2 open, the current I in the galvanometer is given by {tex}I=\frac{E}{R+R_{G}}{/tex} When K 2 is closed, the equivalent resistance, say R', of the parallel combination of S and R G is given by {tex}R^{\prime}=\frac{S R_{G}}{S+R_{G}}{/tex} The total current, say I' drawn from the battery would now be {tex}I'=\frac{E}{R+R^{\prime}}{/tex} This current gets subdivided in the inverse ratio of S and R G . Hence the current I" through G, would now be given by {tex}I^{\prime \prime}=\frac{S}{S+R_{G}} I^\prime =\frac{S}{\left(S+R_{G}\right)} \frac{E}{(R+R')}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{S E}{\left(S+R_{G}\right)\left[R+\frac{S R_{G}}{S+R_{G}}\right]}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{S E}{R S+R R_{G}+S R_{G}}{/tex} But {tex}I^{\prime \prime}=\frac{I}{n}=\frac{1}{n}\left(\frac{E}{R+R_{G}}\right){/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad \frac{E}{n\left(R+R_{G}\right)}=\frac{S . E}{R S+R R_{G}+S R_{G}}{/tex} Or nRS + nSR G = RS + RR G + SR G Or (n-1) RS = RR G - (n-1)SR G Or (n-1) RS = R[R - (n-1)S] {tex}\therefore \quad R_{G}=\frac{(n-1) R S}{R-(n-1) S}{/tex} This is the required expression When R >> S, we have {tex}R_G=\frac{(n-1) R S}{R}{/tex} = (n-1)S , this is the required result.
solution: With key K 2 open, the current I in the galvanometer is given by {tex}I=\frac{E}{R+R_{G}}{/tex} When K 2 is closed, the equivalent resistance, say R', of the parallel combination of S and R G is given by {tex}R^{\prime}=\frac{S R_{G}}{S+R_{G}}{/tex} The total current, say I' drawn from the battery would now be {tex}I'=\frac{E}{R+R^{\prime}}{/tex} This current gets subdivided in the inverse ratio of S and R G . Hence the current I" through G, would now be given by {tex}I^{\prime \prime}=\frac{S}{S+R_{G}} I^\prime =\frac{S}{\left(S+R_{G}\right)} \frac{E}{(R+R')}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{S E}{\left(S+R_{G}\right)\left[R+\frac{S R_{G}}{S+R_{G}}\right]}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{S E}{R S+R R_{G}+S R_{G}}{/tex} But {tex}I^{\prime \prime}=\frac{I}{n}=\frac{1}{n}\left(\frac{E}{R+R_{G}}\right){/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad \frac{E}{n\left(R+R_{G}\right)}=\frac{S . E}{R S+R R_{G}+S R_{G}}{/tex} Or nRS + nSR G = RS + RR G + SR G Or (n-1) RS = RR G - (n-1)SR G Or (n-1) RS = R[R - (n-1)S] {tex}\therefore \quad R_{G}=\frac{(n-1) R S}{R-(n-1) S}{/tex} This is the required expression When R >> S, we have {tex}R_G=\frac{(n-1) R S}{R}{/tex} = (n-1)S , this is the required result.
solution_html:
With key K2 open, the current I in the galvanometer is given by {tex}I=\frac{E}{R+R_{G}}{/tex}
When K2 is closed, the equivalent resistance, say R', of the parallel combination of S and RG is given by
{tex}R^{\prime}=\frac{S R_{G}}{S+R_{G}}{/tex}
The total current, say I' drawn from the battery would now be
{tex}I'=\frac{E}{R+R^{\prime}}{/tex}
This current gets subdivided in the inverse ratio of S and RG. Hence the current I" through G, would now be given by
{tex}I^{\prime \prime}=\frac{S}{S+R_{G}} I^\prime =\frac{S}{\left(S+R_{G}\right)} \frac{E}{(R+R')}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{S E}{\left(S+R_{G}\right)\left[R+\frac{S R_{G}}{S+R_{G}}\right]}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{S E}{R S+R R_{G}+S R_{G}}{/tex}
But {tex}I^{\prime \prime}=\frac{I}{n}=\frac{1}{n}\left(\frac{E}{R+R_{G}}\right){/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad \frac{E}{n\left(R+R_{G}\right)}=\frac{S . E}{R S+R R_{G}+S R_{G}}{/tex}
Or nRS + nSRG = RS + RRG + SRG
Or (n-1) RS = RRG - (n-1)SRG
Or (n-1) RS = R[R - (n-1)S]
{tex}\therefore \quad R_{G}=\frac{(n-1) R S}{R-(n-1) S}{/tex}
This is the required expression
When R >> S, we have
{tex}R_G=\frac{(n-1) R S}{R}{/tex} = (n-1)S , this is the required result.

question_html: Two identical long conducting wires AOB and COD are placed at a right angle to each other, with one above the other such that O is their common point for the two. The wires carry I1 and I2 currents, respectively. Point P is lying at distance d from O along a direction perpendicular to the plane containing the wires. The magnetic field at the point P will be:
question_text: Two identical long conducting wires AOB and COD are placed at a right angle to each other, with one above the other such that O is their common point for the two. The wires carry I 1 and I 2 currents, respectively. Point P is lying at distance d from O along a direction perpendicular to the plane containing the wires. The magnetic field at the point P will be:

options.explanation: {tex}B_{1}=\frac{\mu_{0} I_{1}}{2 \pi d}{/tex} and {tex}B_{2}=\frac{\mu_{0} I_{2}}{2 \pi d}{/tex} {tex}B_{1} \perp B_{2}{/tex}

question_html:
1. State Ampere's circuital law, expressing it in the integral form.
  2. Two long coaxial insulated solenoids, Sj and S, of equal lengths are wound one over the other as shown in the figure. A steady current "I" flows through the inner solenoid S2 to the other end B, which is connected to the outer solenoid S1 through which the same current "I" flows in the opposite direction so as to come out at end A. If n, and are the number of turns per unit length, find the magnitude and direction of the net magnetic field at a point (i) inside on the axis and (ii) outside the combined system.
question_text: State Ampere's circuital law, expressing it in the integral form. Two long coaxial insulated solenoids, Sj and S, of equal lengths are wound one over the other as shown in the figure. A steady current "I" flows through the inner solenoid S 2 to the other end B, which is connected to the outer solenoid S 1 through which the same current "I" flows in the opposite direction so as to come out at end A. If n, and are the number of turns per unit length, find the magnitude and direction of the net magnetic field at a point (i) inside on the axis and (ii) outside the combined system.
explanation: {tex}\oint \vec{B} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}=\mu_{0} I{/tex} Magnetic field inside solenoid {tex}S_{1}, B_{1}=\mu_{0} n_{1} I{/tex} Magnetic field inside solenoid {tex}S_{2}, B_{2}=\mu_{0} n_{2} I{/tex} As the currents in the two solenoids are oppositely directed, so direction of B 2 is opposite to that of B]. The net magnetic field at any inside point along the axis, {tex}B=B_{1}-B_{2}=\mu_{0}\left(n_{1}-n_{2}\right) I{/tex} Outside the combined system, net magnetic field = 0.
solution: {tex}\oint \vec{B} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}=\mu_{0} I{/tex} Magnetic field inside solenoid {tex}S_{1}, B_{1}=\mu_{0} n_{1} I{/tex} Magnetic field inside solenoid {tex}S_{2}, B_{2}=\mu_{0} n_{2} I{/tex} As the currents in the two solenoids are oppositely directed, so direction of B 2 is opposite to that of B]. The net magnetic field at any inside point along the axis, {tex}B=B_{1}-B_{2}=\mu_{0}\left(n_{1}-n_{2}\right) I{/tex} Outside the combined system, net magnetic field = 0.
solution_html:
1. {tex}\oint \vec{B} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}=\mu_{0} I{/tex}
  2.  
    1. Magnetic field inside solenoid {tex}S_{1}, B_{1}=\mu_{0} n_{1} I{/tex}
      Magnetic field inside solenoid {tex}S_{2}, B_{2}=\mu_{0} n_{2} I{/tex}
      As the currents in the two solenoids are oppositely directed, so direction of B2 is opposite to that of B]. The net magnetic field at any inside point along the axis,
      {tex}B=B_{1}-B_{2}=\mu_{0}\left(n_{1}-n_{2}\right) I{/tex}
    2. Outside the combined system, net magnetic field = 0.

question_html: The wire shown in given figure carries a current of 10 A. Determine the magnitude of the magnetic field at the centre O. Given radius of the bent coil is 3 cm.
question_text: The wire shown in given figure carries a current of 10 A. Determine the magnitude of the magnetic field at the centre O. Given radius of the bent coil is 3 cm.
explanation: As {tex}\theta{/tex} (rad) = {tex}\frac{\text { Arc }}{\text { Radius }}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{3 \pi}{2}=\frac{l}{r}{/tex} or l = {tex}\frac{3 \pi r}{2}{/tex} According to Biot-Savart law, magnetic field at the centre O is B = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \frac{I l}{r^{2}}=\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \cdot \frac{I}{r^{2}} \cdot \frac{3 \pi r}{2}=\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \cdot \frac{3}{2} \cdot \frac{\pi I}{r}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{4 \pi \times 10^{-7}}{4 \pi} \frac{3}{2} \cdot \frac{22}{7} \times \frac{10}{3 \times 10^{-2}}{/tex} = 1.57 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 T
solution: As {tex}\theta{/tex} (rad) = {tex}\frac{\text { Arc }}{\text { Radius }}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{3 \pi}{2}=\frac{l}{r}{/tex} or l = {tex}\frac{3 \pi r}{2}{/tex} According to Biot-Savart law, magnetic field at the centre O is B = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \frac{I l}{r^{2}}=\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \cdot \frac{I}{r^{2}} \cdot \frac{3 \pi r}{2}=\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \cdot \frac{3}{2} \cdot \frac{\pi I}{r}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{4 \pi \times 10^{-7}}{4 \pi} \frac{3}{2} \cdot \frac{22}{7} \times \frac{10}{3 \times 10^{-2}}{/tex} = 1.57 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 T
solution_html:
As {tex}\theta{/tex} (rad) = {tex}\frac{\text { Arc }}{\text { Radius }}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{3 \pi}{2}=\frac{l}{r}{/tex} or l = {tex}\frac{3 \pi r}{2}{/tex}
According to Biot-Savart law, magnetic field at the centre O is
B = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \frac{I l}{r^{2}}=\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \cdot \frac{I}{r^{2}} \cdot \frac{3 \pi r}{2}=\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \cdot \frac{3}{2} \cdot \frac{\pi I}{r}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{4 \pi \times 10^{-7}}{4 \pi} \frac{3}{2} \cdot \frac{22}{7} \times \frac{10}{3 \times 10^{-2}}{/tex}
= 1.57 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 T

subject: Physics
question_html: Why is it that while using a moving coil galvanometer as a voltmeter a high resistance in series is required whereas in an ammeter a shunt is used?
question_text: Why is it that while using a moving coil galvanometer as a voltmeter a high resistance in series is required whereas in an ammeter a shunt is used?
explanation: In order that the insertion of an ammeter in series in a circuit does not affect the current in the circuit, an ammeter must have the least possible resistance. So a galvanometer is converted into ammeter by connecting a small resistance in parallel with it. A voltmeter is connected in parallel with the component across which the p.d. is measured. It must have very high resistance so that a very small current passes through it and the p.d. across that component is not affected. So a galvanometer is converted into a voltmeter by connecting a high resistance in series with it.
solution: In order that the insertion of an ammeter in series in a circuit does not affect the current in the circuit, an ammeter must have the least possible resistance. So a galvanometer is converted into ammeter by connecting a small resistance in parallel with it. A voltmeter is connected in parallel with the component across which the p.d. is measured. It must have very high resistance so that a very small current passes through it and the p.d. across that component is not affected. So a galvanometer is converted into a voltmeter by connecting a high resistance in series with it.
solution_html: In order that the insertion of an ammeter in series in a circuit does not affect the current in the circuit, an ammeter must have the least possible resistance. So a galvanometer is converted into ammeter by connecting a small resistance in parallel with it. A voltmeter is connected in parallel with the component across which the p.d. is measured. It must have very high resistance so that a very small current passes through it and the p.d. across that component is not affected. So a galvanometer is converted into a voltmeter by connecting a high resistance in series with it.

question_html: Is the steady electric current the only source of magnetic field? Justify your answer.
question_text: Is the steady electric current the only source of magnetic field? Justify your answer.
explanation: No, the steady electric current is not the only source of the magnetic field. The magnetic field can also be produced by other sources like alternating current, a moving charged particle, a permanent magnet, changing electric field, etc.
solution: No, the steady electric current is not the only source of the magnetic field. The magnetic field can also be produced by other sources like alternating current, a moving charged particle, a permanent magnet, changing electric field, etc.

question_text: A coil of N turns and radius R carries a current I. It is unwound and rewound to make a square coil of side a having same number of turns N. Keeping the current I same, find the ratio of the magnetic moments of the square coil and the circular coil.
explanation: As the same circular coil is unwound and rewound into square coil, length remains same. Thus, {tex}(2\mathrm{ {tex}\pi{/tex} r})\;\mathrm N\;=(4\mathrm a)\;\mathrm N{/tex} {tex}2 \pi r = 4 a \Rightarrow a = \frac { \pi r } { 2 }{/tex} ...... (i) Thus, the ratio of magnetic moment of square coil and circular coil is given as {tex}\begin{array}{l}\frac{M_s}{M_c}=\frac{N\;I\;A_s}{N\;I\;A_c}=\frac{a^2}{\mathrm{ {tex}\pi{/tex} r}^2}\\\;\;\;\;\;\;\;=\frac{(\mathrm{ {tex}\pi{/tex} r}/2)^2}{\mathrm{ {tex}\pi{/tex} r}^2}=\frac{\mathrm\pi}4\end{array}{/tex} [ {tex}\because{/tex} from Eq. (i)] {tex}\Rightarrow \quad M _ { s } : M _ { c } = \pi : 4{/tex}
solution: As the same circular coil is unwound and rewound into square coil, length remains same. Thus, {tex}(2\mathrm{ {tex}\pi{/tex} r})\;\mathrm N\;=(4\mathrm a)\;\mathrm N{/tex} {tex}2 \pi r = 4 a \Rightarrow a = \frac { \pi r } { 2 }{/tex} ...... (i) Thus, the ratio of magnetic moment of square coil and circular coil is given as {tex}\begin{array}{l}\frac{M_s}{M_c}=\frac{N\;I\;A_s}{N\;I\;A_c}=\frac{a^2}{\mathrm{ {tex}\pi{/tex} r}^2}\\\;\;\;\;\;\;\;=\frac{(\mathrm{ {tex}\pi{/tex} r}/2)^2}{\mathrm{ {tex}\pi{/tex} r}^2}=\frac{\mathrm\pi}4\end{array}{/tex} [ {tex}\because{/tex} from Eq. (i)] {tex}\Rightarrow \quad M _ { s } : M _ { c } = \pi : 4{/tex}
solution_html:
As the same circular coil is unwound and rewound into square coil, length remains same. Thus,
{tex}(2\mathrm{{tex}\pi{/tex}r})\;\mathrm N\;=(4\mathrm a)\;\mathrm N{/tex}
{tex}2 \pi r = 4 a \Rightarrow a = \frac { \pi r } { 2 }{/tex} ...... (i)
Thus, the ratio of magnetic moment of square coil and circular coil is given as
{tex}\begin{array}{l}\frac{M_s}{M_c}=\frac{N\;I\;A_s}{N\;I\;A_c}=\frac{a^2}{\mathrm{{tex}\pi{/tex}r}^2}\\\;\;\;\;\;\;\;=\frac{(\mathrm{{tex}\pi{/tex}r}/2)^2}{\mathrm{{tex}\pi{/tex}r}^2}=\frac{\mathrm\pi}4\end{array}{/tex} [{tex}\because{/tex} from Eq. (i)]
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad M _ { s } : M _ { c } = \pi : 4{/tex}

question_html: A closed-circuit is in the form of a regular hexagon of side a. If the circuit carries current I, what is magnetic induction at the centre of the hexagon?
question_text: A closed-circuit is in the form of a regular hexagon of side a. If the circuit carries current I, what is magnetic induction at the centre of the hexagon?
explanation: ON = a ' = {tex}\sqrt{a^{2}-\frac{a^{2}}{4}}=\frac{\sqrt{3} a}{2}{/tex} Magnetic field at O due to current in AB is B 1 = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I}{4 \pi a^{\prime}}[\sin \alpha+\sin \beta]{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I}{4 \pi \sqrt{3} a / 2}\left[\sin 30^{\circ}+\sin 30^{\circ}\right]=\frac{\mu_{0} I}{2 \pi \sqrt{3} a}{/tex} Total field at O = 6B 1 = {tex}\frac{\sqrt{3} \mu_{0} I}{\pi a}{/tex}
solution: ON = a ' = {tex}\sqrt{a^{2}-\frac{a^{2}}{4}}=\frac{\sqrt{3} a}{2}{/tex} Magnetic field at O due to current in AB is B 1 = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I}{4 \pi a^{\prime}}[\sin \alpha+\sin \beta]{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I}{4 \pi \sqrt{3} a / 2}\left[\sin 30^{\circ}+\sin 30^{\circ}\right]=\frac{\mu_{0} I}{2 \pi \sqrt{3} a}{/tex} Total field at O = 6B 1 = {tex}\frac{\sqrt{3} \mu_{0} I}{\pi a}{/tex}
solution_html:
ON = a' = {tex}\sqrt{a^{2}-\frac{a^{2}}{4}}=\frac{\sqrt{3} a}{2}{/tex}

Magnetic field at O due to current in AB is
B1 = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I}{4 \pi a^{\prime}}[\sin \alpha+\sin \beta]{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I}{4 \pi \sqrt{3} a / 2}\left[\sin 30^{\circ}+\sin 30^{\circ}\right]=\frac{\mu_{0} I}{2 \pi \sqrt{3} a}{/tex}
Total field at O = 6B1 = {tex}\frac{\sqrt{3} \mu_{0} I}{\pi a}{/tex}

question_html: When a charged particle moving with a velocity {tex}\vec{v}{/tex} is subjected to a magnetic field {tex}\vec{B}{/tex} the force acting on it is non-zero. Would the particle gain any energy?
question_text: When a charged particle moving with a velocity {tex}\vec{v}{/tex} is subjected to a magnetic field {tex}\vec{B}{/tex} the force acting on it is non-zero. Would the particle gain any energy?
explanation: The magnetic force acts perpendicular to the direction of motion of the charged particle. No work is done by the magnetic force on it. The particle does not gain any energy.
solution: The magnetic force acts perpendicular to the direction of motion of the charged particle. No work is done by the magnetic force on it. The particle does not gain any energy.

question_text: A long straight wire carries a current I along the positive y-direction. A particle of charge +Q is moving with a velocity {tex}\vec{v}{/tex} along the x-axis. In which direction will the particle experience a force?
explanation: The field {tex}\vec{B}{/tex} due to current I act along -ve z-direction. By Fleming's left-hand rule, the charge + Q will experience a force along +ve y-direction.

question_html: When a proton is released from rest in a room, it starts with an initial acceleration a0 towards west. When it is projected towards the north with a speed v0, it moves with an initial acceleration 3a0 toward the west. The electric and magnetic fields in the room are:
question_text: When a proton is released from rest in a room, it starts with an initial acceleration a 0 towards west. When it is projected towards the north with a speed v 0 , it moves with an initial acceleration 3a 0 toward the west. The electric and magnetic fields in the room are:

options.text: {tex}\frac{m a_{0}}{e} \text { west, } \frac{2 m a_{0}}{e v_{0}}{/tex} down
options.html: {tex}\frac{m a_{0}}{e} \text { west, } \frac{2 m a_{0}}{e v_{0}}{/tex} down
options.explanation: {tex}a_{0}=\frac{F_{0}}{m}=\frac{e E}{m} \Rightarrow E=\frac{m a_{0}}{e}{/tex} (west) {tex}\frac{F_{e}+F_{m}}{m}=\frac{e E+e v_{0} B}{m}=3 a_{0}{/tex} ev 0 B = 3ma 0 - eE = 3ma 0 - ma 0 = 2ma 0 {tex}\therefore B=\frac{2 m a_{0}}{e v_{0}}{/tex} The field B must act downwards in accordance with Fleming's left-hand rule.

options.text: {tex}\frac{m a_{0}}{e} \text { west, } \frac{2 m a_{0}}{e v_{0}}{/tex} up
options.html: {tex}\frac{m a_{0}}{e} \text { west, } \frac{2 m a_{0}}{e v_{0}}{/tex} up

options.text: {tex}\frac{m a_{0}}{e} \text { east, } \frac{3 m a_{0}}{e v_{0}}{/tex} up
options.html: {tex}\frac{m a_{0}}{e} \text { east, } \frac{3 m a_{0}}{e v_{0}}{/tex} up

options.text: {tex}\frac{m a_{0}}{e} \text { east, } \frac{3 m a_{0}}{e v_{0}}{/tex} down
options.html: {tex}\frac{m a_{0}}{e} \text { east, } \frac{3 m a_{0}}{e v_{0}}{/tex} down

correct_option_text: {tex}\frac{m a_{0}}{e} \text { west, } \frac{2 m a_{0}}{e v_{0}}{/tex} down
explanation: {tex}a_{0}=\frac{F_{0}}{m}=\frac{e E}{m} \Rightarrow E=\frac{m a_{0}}{e}{/tex} (west) {tex}\frac{F_{e}+F_{m}}{m}=\frac{e E+e v_{0} B}{m}=3 a_{0}{/tex} ev 0 B = 3ma 0 - eE = 3ma 0 - ma 0 = 2ma 0 {tex}\therefore B=\frac{2 m a_{0}}{e v_{0}}{/tex} The field B must act downwards in accordance with Fleming's left-hand rule.

Find the mass per unit length of the wire CD so that it remains suspended at its position when left free. Give the direction of the current flowing in CD with respect to that in AB.
question_text: A wire AB is carrying a steady current of 12 A and is lying on the table. Another wire CD carrying 5 A is held directly above AB at a height of 1 mm. Find the mass per unit length of the wire CD so that it remains suspended at its position when left free. Give the direction of the current flowing in CD with respect to that in AB.
explanation: Weight per unit length of upper wire = = Magnetic force per unit length {tex}\frac{W}{l}=\frac{F}{l} \Rightarrow \frac{m g}{l}=\frac{\mu_{0} l_{1} L_{2}}{2 \pi r}{/tex} Mass per unit length {tex}=\frac{m}{l}=\frac{\mu_{0} I_{1} I_{2}}{2 \pi r g}=\frac{4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times 12 \times 5}{2 \pi \times 10^{-3} \times 10}{/tex} =1.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 kgm -1

Mass per unit length
{tex}=\frac{m}{l}=\frac{\mu_{0} I_{1} I_{2}}{2 \pi r g}=\frac{4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times 12 \times 5}{2 \pi \times 10^{-3} \times 10}{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html:
A proton and an alpha particle enter at right angles into a uniform magnetic field of intensity B. Calculate the ratio of the radii of their paths, when they enter the field with the

  1. same momentum.
  2. same kinetic energy
question_text: A proton and an alpha particle enter at right angles into a uniform magnetic field of intensity B. Calculate the ratio of the radii of their paths, when they enter the field with the same momentum. same kinetic energy
explanation: The Centripetal force on a charged particle = Magnetic force on the particle or {tex}\frac{m v^{2}}{r}=q v B \sin 90^{\circ}{/tex} or {tex}r=\frac{m v}{q B}=\frac{p}{q B}{/tex} For the same momentum p and field B, {tex}r \propto \frac{1}{q} \quad \therefore \quad \frac{r_{p}}{r_{\alpha}}=\frac{q_{\alpha}}{q_{p}}=\frac{2 e}{e}=2: \mathbf{1}{/tex} Kinetic energy, {tex}K=\frac{1}{2} m v^{2}{/tex} or {tex}v=\sqrt{\frac{2 K}{m}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Radius, {tex}r=\frac{m v}{q B}=\frac{m}{q B} \sqrt{\frac{2 K}{m}}=\frac{\sqrt{2 m K}}{q B}{/tex} For the same kinetic energy K and field B, {tex}r \propto \frac{\sqrt{m}}{q} \quad \therefore \quad \frac{r_{p}}{r_{\alpha}}=\sqrt{\frac{m_{p}}{m_{\alpha}}} \cdot \frac{q_{\alpha}}{q_{p}}=\sqrt{\frac{m_{p}}{4 m_{p}}} \times \frac{2 e}{e}=1: 1{/tex}
solution: The Centripetal force on a charged particle = Magnetic force on the particle or {tex}\frac{m v^{2}}{r}=q v B \sin 90^{\circ}{/tex} or {tex}r=\frac{m v}{q B}=\frac{p}{q B}{/tex} For the same momentum p and field B, {tex}r \propto \frac{1}{q} \quad \therefore \quad \frac{r_{p}}{r_{\alpha}}=\frac{q_{\alpha}}{q_{p}}=\frac{2 e}{e}=2: \mathbf{1}{/tex} Kinetic energy, {tex}K=\frac{1}{2} m v^{2}{/tex} or {tex}v=\sqrt{\frac{2 K}{m}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Radius, {tex}r=\frac{m v}{q B}=\frac{m}{q B} \sqrt{\frac{2 K}{m}}=\frac{\sqrt{2 m K}}{q B}{/tex} For the same kinetic energy K and field B, {tex}r \propto \frac{\sqrt{m}}{q} \quad \therefore \quad \frac{r_{p}}{r_{\alpha}}=\sqrt{\frac{m_{p}}{m_{\alpha}}} \cdot \frac{q_{\alpha}}{q_{p}}=\sqrt{\frac{m_{p}}{4 m_{p}}} \times \frac{2 e}{e}=1: 1{/tex}
solution_html:
1. The Centripetal force on a charged particle = Magnetic force on the particle
    or {tex}\frac{m v^{2}}{r}=q v B \sin 90^{\circ}{/tex} or {tex}r=\frac{m v}{q B}=\frac{p}{q B}{/tex}
    For the same momentum p and field B,
    {tex}r \propto \frac{1}{q} \quad \therefore \quad \frac{r_{p}}{r_{\alpha}}=\frac{q_{\alpha}}{q_{p}}=\frac{2 e}{e}=2: \mathbf{1}{/tex}
  2. Kinetic energy, {tex}K=\frac{1}{2} m v^{2}{/tex} or {tex}v=\sqrt{\frac{2 K}{m}}{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} Radius,{tex}r=\frac{m v}{q B}=\frac{m}{q B} \sqrt{\frac{2 K}{m}}=\frac{\sqrt{2 m K}}{q B}{/tex}
    For the same kinetic energy K and field B,
    {tex}r \propto \frac{\sqrt{m}}{q} \quad \therefore \quad \frac{r_{p}}{r_{\alpha}}=\sqrt{\frac{m_{p}}{m_{\alpha}}} \cdot \frac{q_{\alpha}}{q_{p}}=\sqrt{\frac{m_{p}}{4 m_{p}}} \times \frac{2 e}{e}=1: 1{/tex}
