question_html:
The magnitude F of the force between two straight parallel current-carrying conductors kept at a distance d apart in the air is given by F = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0}}{2 \pi} \cdot \frac{I_{1} I_{2}}{d}{/tex} where I1 and I2 are the currents flowing through the two wires
Use this expression, and the sign convention that the: " Force of attraction is assigned a negative sign and force of repulsion assigned a positive sign". Draw graphs showing the dependence of F on:

  1. I1I2 when d kept constant
  2. when the product I1I2 is maintained at a constant positive value.
  3. when the product I1I2 is maintained at a constant negative value.
question_text: The magnitude F of the force between two straight parallel current-carrying conductors kept at a distance d apart in the air is given by F = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0}}{2 \pi} \cdot \frac{I_{1} I_{2}}{d}{/tex} where I 1 and I 2 are the currents flowing through the two wires Use this expression, and the sign convention that the: " Force of attraction is assigned a negative sign and force of repulsion assigned a positive sign". Draw graphs showing the dependence of F on: I 1 I 2 when d kept constant when the product I 1 I 2 is maintained at a constant positive value. when the product I 1 I 2 is maintained at a constant negative value.
explanation: The magnitude F of the force between two straight parallel current-carrying conductors kept at a distance d apart in the air We know that F is an attractive (- ve) force when the currents l 1 and I 2 are 'like' currents, i.e., when the product l 1 l 2 is positive or when they are flowing in the same direction. Similarly, F is a repulsive (+ve) force when the currents l 1 and I 2 are 'unlike' currents, i.e., when the product l 1 l 2 is negative or when they are flowing in the opposite direction. Now F {tex}\propto{/tex} (l 1 l 2 ), when d is kept constant and F {tex}\propto \frac{1}{d}{/tex} when l 1 l 2 is kept constant. The required graphs, therefore, have the forms shown below.
solution: The magnitude F of the force between two straight parallel current-carrying conductors kept at a distance d apart in the air We know that F is an attractive (- ve) force when the currents l 1 and I 2 are 'like' currents, i.e., when the product l 1 l 2 is positive or when they are flowing in the same direction. Similarly, F is a repulsive (+ve) force when the currents l 1 and I 2 are 'unlike' currents, i.e., when the product l 1 l 2 is negative or when they are flowing in the opposite direction. Now F {tex}\propto{/tex} (l 1 l 2 ), when d is kept constant and F {tex}\propto \frac{1}{d}{/tex} when l 1 l 2 is kept constant. The required graphs, therefore, have the forms shown below.
solution_html:
The magnitude F of the force between two straight parallel current-carrying conductors kept at a distance d apart in the air We know that F is an attractive (- ve) force when the currents l1 and I2 are 'like' currents, i.e., when the product l1l2 is positive or when they are flowing in the same direction.
Similarly, F is a repulsive (+ve) force when the currents l1 and I2 are 'unlike' currents, i.e., when the product l1l2 is negative or when they are flowing in the opposite direction.
Now F {tex}\propto{/tex} (l1l2), when d is kept constant and F {tex}\propto \frac{1}{d}{/tex}
when l1l2 is kept constant.

question_html: Two identical circular loops P and Q, each of radius r and carrying equal currents are kept in the parallel planes having a common axis passing through O. The direction of current in P is clockwise and in Q is anti-clockwise as seen from O which is equidistant from the loops P and Q. Find the magnitude of the net magnetic field at O.
question_text: Two identical circular loops P and Q, each of radius r and carrying equal currents are kept in the parallel planes having a common axis passing through O. The direction of current in P is clockwise and in Q is anti-clockwise as seen from O which is equidistant from the loops P and Q. Find the magnitude of the net magnetic field at O.
explanation: In general, at an axial distance x the field due to circular coil of radius r carrying current I is given by {tex}B=\frac{\displaystyle\mu_0Ir^2}{\displaystyle2(r^2\;+\;x^2)^{3/2}}{/tex} In the figure, from Right Hand Rule the direction of magnetic field in P and Q will be towards left, so net magnetic field at point O is the sum of B P and B Q and as the loops are equidistant from point O and carrying the same current I, so B P = B Q . Here, x = r Thus, B = B P + B Q = 2B P = {tex}2\left[\frac{\displaystyle\mu_0Ir^2}{\displaystyle2(r^2\;+\;r^2)^{3/2}}\right]=\frac{\mu_0\;I}{2^{3/2}\;r}{/tex}
solution: In general, at an axial distance x the field due to circular coil of radius r carrying current I is given by {tex}B=\frac{\displaystyle\mu_0Ir^2}{\displaystyle2(r^2\;+\;x^2)^{3/2}}{/tex} In the figure, from Right Hand Rule the direction of magnetic field in P and Q will be towards left, so net magnetic field at point O is the sum of B P and B Q and as the loops are equidistant from point O and carrying the same current I, so B P = B Q . Here, x = r Thus, B = B P + B Q = 2B P = {tex}2\left[\frac{\displaystyle\mu_0Ir^2}{\displaystyle2(r^2\;+\;r^2)^{3/2}}\right]=\frac{\mu_0\;I}{2^{3/2}\;r}{/tex}
solution_html:
In general, at an axial distance x the field due to circular coil of radius r carrying current I is given by
{tex}B=\frac{\displaystyle\mu_0Ir^2}{\displaystyle2(r^2\;+\;x^2)^{3/2}}{/tex}
In the figure, from Right Hand Rule the direction of magnetic field in P and Q will be towards left, so net magnetic field at point O is the sum of BP and BQ and as the loops are equidistant from point O and carrying the same current I, so BP = BQ. Here, x = r
Thus, B = BP + BQ = 2BP = {tex}2\left[\frac{\displaystyle\mu_0Ir^2}{\displaystyle2(r^2\;+\;r^2)^{3/2}}\right]=\frac{\mu_0\;I}{2^{3/2}\;r}{/tex}

question_html: A particle of charge q and mass m is moving with velocity v. It is subjected to a uniform magnetic field B directed perpendicular to its velocity. Show that it describes a circular path. Write the expression for its radius.
question_text: A particle of charge q and mass m is moving with velocity v. It is subjected to a uniform magnetic field B directed perpendicular to its velocity. Show that it describes a circular path. Write the expression for its radius.
explanation: Consider a charge q moving with velocity v projected perpendicular to the uniform magnetic field B. As v and B are perpendicular, so the perpendicular force, qvB, acts as a centripetal force and produces a circular motion perpendicular to the magnetic field as shown in the Figure. Thus, {tex}q v B = \frac { m v ^ { 2 } } { r }{/tex} {tex}r = \frac { m v } { q B }{/tex} where, r = radius of the circular path followed by charge projected perpendicular to a uniform magnetic field.
solution: Consider a charge q moving with velocity v projected perpendicular to the uniform magnetic field B. As v and B are perpendicular, so the perpendicular force, qvB, acts as a centripetal force and produces a circular motion perpendicular to the magnetic field as shown in the Figure. Thus, {tex}q v B = \frac { m v ^ { 2 } } { r }{/tex} {tex}r = \frac { m v } { q B }{/tex} where, r = radius of the circular path followed by charge projected perpendicular to a uniform magnetic field.
solution_html:
Consider a charge q moving with velocity v projected perpendicular to the uniform magnetic field B. As v and B are perpendicular, so the perpendicular force, qvB, acts as a centripetal force and produces a circular motion perpendicular to the magnetic field as shown in the Figure.

Thus,
{tex}q v B = \frac { m v ^ { 2 } } { r }{/tex}

{tex}r = \frac { m v } { q B }{/tex}
where, r = radius of the circular path followed by charge projected perpendicular to a uniform magnetic field.

question_html:
Write the expression for the force F, acting on a charged particle of charge q moving with a velocity v in the presence of both electric field E and magnetic field B.
Obtain the condition under which the particle moves undeflected through the fields.
question_text: Write the expression for the force F, acting on a charged particle of charge q moving with a velocity v in the presence of both electric field E and magnetic field B. Obtain the condition under which the particle moves undeflected through the fields.
explanation: Force experienced by the charged particle in the presence of both electric and magnetic field is the sum of electric force and magnetic force on it. Let a point charge q is moving with a velocity v in the presence of Electric Field E and Magnetic Field B. As, Electric force, F e = qE Magnetic force, {tex}F_m\;=\;q\;(v\times\;B){/tex} Thus the expression of Lorentz force is, {tex}F=\;F_e+\;F_m\;=\;qE\;+\;q\;(v\times B){/tex} = {tex}F\;=\;q\lbrack E\;+\;(v\times\;B)\rbrack{/tex} When a charged particle moves perpendicular to both electric and magnetic fields, a charged particle remains undeflected and F = 0. Thus, {tex}q\lbrack E\;+\;(v\times\;B)\rbrack\;=\;0{/tex} or {tex}\mathbf { E } + ( \mathbf { v } \times \mathbf { b } ) = 0{/tex} In scalar form, {tex}E\;=\;B\;v\;\sin\theta{/tex} = B v when {tex}\theta = 90 ^ { \circ }{/tex} Thus, {tex}v\;=\;\frac EB{/tex}
solution: Force experienced by the charged particle in the presence of both electric and magnetic field is the sum of electric force and magnetic force on it. Let a point charge q is moving with a velocity v in the presence of Electric Field E and Magnetic Field B. As, Electric force, F e = qE Magnetic force, {tex}F_m\;=\;q\;(v\times\;B){/tex} Thus the expression of Lorentz force is, {tex}F=\;F_e+\;F_m\;=\;qE\;+\;q\;(v\times B){/tex} = {tex}F\;=\;q\lbrack E\;+\;(v\times\;B)\rbrack{/tex} When a charged particle moves perpendicular to both electric and magnetic fields, a charged particle remains undeflected and F = 0. Thus, {tex}q\lbrack E\;+\;(v\times\;B)\rbrack\;=\;0{/tex} or {tex}\mathbf { E } + ( \mathbf { v } \times \mathbf { b } ) = 0{/tex} In scalar form, {tex}E\;=\;B\;v\;\sin\theta{/tex} = B v when {tex}\theta = 90 ^ { \circ }{/tex} Thus, {tex}v\;=\;\frac EB{/tex}
solution_html:
Force experienced by the charged particle in the presence of both electric and magnetic field is the sum of electric force and magnetic force on it.
Let a point charge q is moving with a velocity v in the presence of Electric Field E and Magnetic Field B.
As, Electric force, Fe = qE
Magnetic force, {tex}F_m\;=\;q\;(v\times\;B){/tex}
Thus the expression of Lorentz force is, {tex}F=\;F_e+\;F_m\;=\;qE\;+\;q\;(v\times B){/tex}= {tex}F\;=\;q\lbrack E\;+\;(v\times\;B)\rbrack{/tex}
When a charged particle moves perpendicular to both electric and magnetic fields, a charged particle remains undeflected and F = 0. Thus,
{tex}q\lbrack E\;+\;(v\times\;B)\rbrack\;=\;0{/tex}
or {tex}\mathbf { E } + ( \mathbf { v } \times \mathbf { b } ) = 0{/tex}
In scalar form, {tex}E\;=\;B\;v\;\sin\theta{/tex} = B v when {tex}\theta = 90 ^ { \circ }{/tex}
Thus, {tex}v\;=\;\frac EB{/tex}

question_html: A circular coil of N turns and diameter d carries a current I. It is unwound and rewound to make another coil of diameter 2d, current I remaining the same. Calculate the ratio of the magnetic moments of the new coil and the original coil.
question_text: A circular coil of N turns and diameter d carries a current I. It is unwound and rewound to make another coil of diameter 2d, current I remaining the same. Calculate the ratio of the magnetic moments of the new coil and the original coil.
explanation: In Ampere's law, the loop boundary is circle and magnetic field B is tangential to the circumference of the circle. Thus length, {tex}\mathrm L\;=\;2\mathrm{ {tex}\pi{/tex} r}\;=\;\mathrm{ {tex}\pi{/tex} d}{/tex} where r and d is the radius and diameter of the circle. As the same coil in unwound and rewound, total length remains same. Thus, Total Length of wire of coil 1 = Total Length of wire of coil 2 {tex}N _ { 1 } \times \pi d _ { 1 } = N _ { 2 } \times \pi d _ { 2 }{/tex} where N 1 and N 2 are the number of turns of coil 1 and 2 respectively. {tex}N _ { 1 } \times \pi d = N _ { 2 } \times \pi \times 2 d{/tex} {tex}N _ { 2 } = \frac { N _ { 1 } } { 2 }{/tex} [where, N 1 = N] {tex}\Rightarrow \quad N _ { 2 } : N _ { 1 } = 1 : 2 \Rightarrow N _ { 1 } : N _ { 2 } = 2 : 1{/tex} Magnetic moment, M = N I A {tex}\therefore \quad \frac { M _ { 1 } } { M _ { 2 } } = \frac { N _ { 1 } I A _ { 1 } } { N _ { 2 } I A _ { 2 } } = \frac { N _ { 1 } \pi d ^ { 2 } } { N _ { 2 } \pi ( 2 d ) ^ { 2 } }{/tex} {tex}\frac { M _ { 1 } } { M _ { 2 } } = \left( \frac { N _ { 1 } } { N _ { 2 } } \right) \times \frac { 1 } { 4 } = 2 \times \frac { 1 } { 4 } = \frac { 1 } { 2 }{/tex} {tex}\frac { M _ { 1 } } { M _ { 2 } } = \frac { 1 } { 2 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad M _ { 1 } : M _ { 2 } = 1 : 2{/tex}
solution: In Ampere's law, the loop boundary is circle and magnetic field B is tangential to the circumference of the circle. Thus length, {tex}\mathrm L\;=\;2\mathrm{ {tex}\pi{/tex} r}\;=\;\mathrm{ {tex}\pi{/tex} d}{/tex} where r and d is the radius and diameter of the circle. As the same coil in unwound and rewound, total length remains same. Thus, Total Length of wire of coil 1 = Total Length of wire of coil 2 {tex}N _ { 1 } \times \pi d _ { 1 } = N _ { 2 } \times \pi d _ { 2 }{/tex} where N 1 and N 2 are the number of turns of coil 1 and 2 respectively. {tex}N _ { 1 } \times \pi d = N _ { 2 } \times \pi \times 2 d{/tex} {tex}N _ { 2 } = \frac { N _ { 1 } } { 2 }{/tex} [where, N 1 = N] {tex}\Rightarrow \quad N _ { 2 } : N _ { 1 } = 1 : 2 \Rightarrow N _ { 1 } : N _ { 2 } = 2 : 1{/tex} Magnetic moment, M = N I A {tex}\therefore \quad \frac { M _ { 1 } } { M _ { 2 } } = \frac { N _ { 1 } I A _ { 1 } } { N _ { 2 } I A _ { 2 } } = \frac { N _ { 1 } \pi d ^ { 2 } } { N _ { 2 } \pi ( 2 d ) ^ { 2 } }{/tex} {tex}\frac { M _ { 1 } } { M _ { 2 } } = \left( \frac { N _ { 1 } } { N _ { 2 } } \right) \times \frac { 1 } { 4 } = 2 \times \frac { 1 } { 4 } = \frac { 1 } { 2 }{/tex} {tex}\frac { M _ { 1 } } { M _ { 2 } } = \frac { 1 } { 2 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad M _ { 1 } : M _ { 2 } = 1 : 2{/tex}
solution_html:
In Ampere's law, the loop boundary is circle and magnetic field B is tangential to the circumference of the circle. Thus length,
{tex}\mathrm L\;=\;2\mathrm{{tex}\pi{/tex}r}\;=\;\mathrm{{tex}\pi{/tex}d}{/tex}
where r and d is the radius and diameter of the circle.
As the same coil in unwound and rewound, total length remains same. Thus,
Total Length of wire of coil 1 = Total Length of wire of coil 2
{tex}N _ { 1 } \times \pi d _ { 1 } = N _ { 2 } \times \pi d _ { 2 }{/tex} where N1 and N2 are the number of turns of coil 1 and 2 respectively.
{tex}N _ { 1 } \times \pi d = N _ { 2 } \times \pi \times 2 d{/tex}
{tex}N _ { 2 } = \frac { N _ { 1 } } { 2 }{/tex} [where, N1 = N]
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad N _ { 2 } : N _ { 1 } = 1 : 2 \Rightarrow N _ { 1 } : N _ { 2 } = 2 : 1{/tex}
Magnetic moment, M = N I A
{tex}\therefore \quad \frac { M _ { 1 } } { M _ { 2 } } = \frac { N _ { 1 } I A _ { 1 } } { N _ { 2 } I A _ { 2 } } = \frac { N _ { 1 } \pi d ^ { 2 } } { N _ { 2 } \pi ( 2 d ) ^ { 2 } }{/tex}
{tex}\frac { M _ { 1 } } { M _ { 2 } } = \left( \frac { N _ { 1 } } { N _ { 2 } } \right) \times \frac { 1 } { 4 } = 2 \times \frac { 1 } { 4 } = \frac { 1 } { 2 }{/tex}
{tex}\frac { M _ { 1 } } { M _ { 2 } } = \frac { 1 } { 2 }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad M _ { 1 } : M _ { 2 } = 1 : 2{/tex}

question_html: A proton, a deutron and an alpha particle having the same kinetic energy are allowed to pass through a uniform magnetic field perpendicular to their direction of motion. Compare the radii o f their circular paths.
question_text: A proton, a deutron and an alpha particle having the same kinetic energy are allowed to pass through a uniform magnetic field perpendicular to their direction of motion. Compare the radii o f their circular paths.
explanation: If m p , m d and m a are the masses of proton, deutron and a-particle respectively, then m d = 2m p and m a = 4m p Also q d = q p and {tex}q_{\alpha}{/tex} = 2q p Kinetic energy, {tex}K=\frac{1}{2} m v^{2}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Velocity {tex}v=\sqrt{\frac{2 K}{m}}{/tex} Radius of the circular path of a charged particle in a magnetic field is given by {tex}r=\frac{m v}{q B}=\frac{m}{q B} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2 K}{m}}=\frac{\sqrt{2 m K}}{q B}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad r_{p}=\frac{\sqrt{2 m_{p} K}}{q_{p} B}, r_{d}=\frac{\sqrt{2 m_{d} K}}{q_{d} B}{/tex} and {tex}r_{\alpha}=\frac{\sqrt{2 m_{\alpha} K}}{q_{\alpha} B}{/tex} {tex}\frac{r_{p}}{r_{d}}=\frac{q_{d}}{q_{p}} \sqrt{\frac{m_{p}}{m_{d}}}=\frac{q_{p}}{q_{p}} \sqrt{\frac{m_{p}}{2 m_{p}}}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{r_{p}}{r_{\alpha}}=\frac{q_{\alpha}}{q_{p}} \sqrt{\frac{m_{p}}{m_{\alpha}}}=\frac{2 q_{p}}{q_{p}} \sqrt{\frac{m_{p}}{4 m_{p}}}=1{/tex} Hence {tex}r_{p}: r_{d}: r_{\alpha}=1: \sqrt{2}: \mathbf{1}{/tex}
solution: If m p , m d and m a are the masses of proton, deutron and a-particle respectively, then m d = 2m p and m a = 4m p Also q d = q p and {tex}q_{\alpha}{/tex} = 2q p Kinetic energy, {tex}K=\frac{1}{2} m v^{2}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Velocity {tex}v=\sqrt{\frac{2 K}{m}}{/tex} Radius of the circular path of a charged particle in a magnetic field is given by {tex}r=\frac{m v}{q B}=\frac{m}{q B} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2 K}{m}}=\frac{\sqrt{2 m K}}{q B}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad r_{p}=\frac{\sqrt{2 m_{p} K}}{q_{p} B}, r_{d}=\frac{\sqrt{2 m_{d} K}}{q_{d} B}{/tex} and {tex}r_{\alpha}=\frac{\sqrt{2 m_{\alpha} K}}{q_{\alpha} B}{/tex} {tex}\frac{r_{p}}{r_{d}}=\frac{q_{d}}{q_{p}} \sqrt{\frac{m_{p}}{m_{d}}}=\frac{q_{p}}{q_{p}} \sqrt{\frac{m_{p}}{2 m_{p}}}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{r_{p}}{r_{\alpha}}=\frac{q_{\alpha}}{q_{p}} \sqrt{\frac{m_{p}}{m_{\alpha}}}=\frac{2 q_{p}}{q_{p}} \sqrt{\frac{m_{p}}{4 m_{p}}}=1{/tex} Hence {tex}r_{p}: r_{d}: r_{\alpha}=1: \sqrt{2}: \mathbf{1}{/tex}
solution_html:
If mp , md and ma are the masses of proton, deutron and a-particle respectively, then
md = 2mp and ma = 4mp
Also qd = qp and {tex}q_{\alpha}{/tex} = 2qp
Kinetic energy,
{tex}K=\frac{1}{2} m v^{2}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Velocity {tex}v=\sqrt{\frac{2 K}{m}}{/tex}
Radius of the circular path of a charged particle in a magnetic field is given by
{tex}r=\frac{m v}{q B}=\frac{m}{q B} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2 K}{m}}=\frac{\sqrt{2 m K}}{q B}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad r_{p}=\frac{\sqrt{2 m_{p} K}}{q_{p} B}, r_{d}=\frac{\sqrt{2 m_{d} K}}{q_{d} B}{/tex} and {tex}r_{\alpha}=\frac{\sqrt{2 m_{\alpha} K}}{q_{\alpha} B}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{r_{p}}{r_{d}}=\frac{q_{d}}{q_{p}} \sqrt{\frac{m_{p}}{m_{d}}}=\frac{q_{p}}{q_{p}} \sqrt{\frac{m_{p}}{2 m_{p}}}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{r_{p}}{r_{\alpha}}=\frac{q_{\alpha}}{q_{p}} \sqrt{\frac{m_{p}}{m_{\alpha}}}=\frac{2 q_{p}}{q_{p}} \sqrt{\frac{m_{p}}{4 m_{p}}}=1{/tex}
Hence {tex}r_{p}: r_{d}: r_{\alpha}=1: \sqrt{2}: \mathbf{1}{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html:
Two identical circular wires P and Q, each of radius r and carrying currents I and 2I respectively are lying in parallel planes, such that they have a common axis as shown in the figure.

The direction of current in both the loops is clockwise as seen from O, which is equidistant from both the loops. Find the magnitude and net magnetic field at point O.
question_text: Two identical circular wires P and Q, each of radius r and carrying currents I and 2I respectively are lying in parallel planes, such that they have a common axis as shown in the figure. The direction of current in both the loops is clockwise as seen from O, which is equidistant from both the loops. Find the magnitude and net magnetic field at point O.
explanation: Magnetic field due circular wire P, B P = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \cdot \frac{2 \pi \mathrm{I}}{r}{/tex} (along vertically upwards) Magnetic field due to circular wire Q, B Q = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \cdot \frac{2 \pi(2 \mathrm{I})}{r}=\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \cdot \frac{4 \pi \mathrm{I}}{r}{/tex} (along horizontal towards left) Net magnetic field at the common centre of the two coils, B = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \cdot \frac{2 \pi \mathrm{I}}{r}{/tex} + {tex}\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \cdot \frac{4 \pi \mathrm{I}}{r}=\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \cdot \frac{6 \pi \mathrm{I}}{r}{/tex} The direction of the net magnetic field at the common centre of the two coils will be towards the left.
solution: Magnetic field due circular wire P, B P = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \cdot \frac{2 \pi \mathrm{I}}{r}{/tex} (along vertically upwards) Magnetic field due to circular wire Q, B Q = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \cdot \frac{2 \pi(2 \mathrm{I})}{r}=\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \cdot \frac{4 \pi \mathrm{I}}{r}{/tex} (along horizontal towards left) Net magnetic field at the common centre of the two coils, B = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \cdot \frac{2 \pi \mathrm{I}}{r}{/tex} + {tex}\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \cdot \frac{4 \pi \mathrm{I}}{r}=\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \cdot \frac{6 \pi \mathrm{I}}{r}{/tex} The direction of the net magnetic field at the common centre of the two coils will be towards the left.
solution_html:
Magnetic field due circular wire P,
BP = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \cdot \frac{2 \pi \mathrm{I}}{r}{/tex} (along vertically upwards)
Magnetic field due to circular wire Q,
BQ = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \cdot \frac{2 \pi(2 \mathrm{I})}{r}=\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \cdot \frac{4 \pi \mathrm{I}}{r}{/tex} (along horizontal towards left)
Net magnetic field at the common centre of the two coils,
B = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \cdot \frac{2 \pi \mathrm{I}}{r}{/tex} + {tex}\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \cdot \frac{4 \pi \mathrm{I}}{r}=\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \cdot \frac{6 \pi \mathrm{I}}{r}{/tex}
The direction of the net magnetic field at the common centre of the two coils will be towards the left.

subject: Physics
question_html: Two identical circular wires P and Q, each of radius r and carrying current I are kept in perpendicular planes, such that they have a common centre as shown in figure. Find the magnitude and direction of the net magnetic field at the common centre of the two coils.
question_text: Two identical circular wires P and Q, each of radius r and carrying current I are kept in perpendicular planes, such that they have a common centre as shown in figure. Find the magnitude and direction of the net magnetic field at the common centre of the two coils.
explanation: Magnetic field due circular wire P, B P = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \cdot \frac{2 \pi \mathrm{I}}{r}{/tex} (along vertically upwards) Magnetic field due to circular wire Q, B Q = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \cdot \frac{2 \pi \mathrm{I}}{r}{/tex} (along horizontal towards left) Net magnetic field at the common centre of the two coils, B = {tex}\sqrt{B_{P}^{2}+B_{Q}^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{2} \times \frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \cdot \frac{2 \pi \mathrm{I}}{r}{/tex} ( {tex}\because{/tex} B P = B Q ) As the fields produced by the two coils is equal (in magnitude), the net magnetic field will be inclined equally to both the coils.
solution: Magnetic field due circular wire P, B P = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \cdot \frac{2 \pi \mathrm{I}}{r}{/tex} (along vertically upwards) Magnetic field due to circular wire Q, B Q = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \cdot \frac{2 \pi \mathrm{I}}{r}{/tex} (along horizontal towards left) Net magnetic field at the common centre of the two coils, B = {tex}\sqrt{B_{P}^{2}+B_{Q}^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{2} \times \frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \cdot \frac{2 \pi \mathrm{I}}{r}{/tex} ( {tex}\because{/tex} B P = B Q ) As the fields produced by the two coils is equal (in magnitude), the net magnetic field will be inclined equally to both the coils.
solution_html:
Magnetic field due circular wire P,
BP = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \cdot \frac{2 \pi \mathrm{I}}{r}{/tex} (along vertically upwards)
Magnetic field due to circular wire Q,
BQ = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \cdot \frac{2 \pi \mathrm{I}}{r}{/tex} (along horizontal towards left)
Net magnetic field at the common centre of the two coils,
B = {tex}\sqrt{B_{P}^{2}+B_{Q}^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{2} \times \frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \cdot \frac{2 \pi \mathrm{I}}{r}{/tex} ({tex}\because{/tex} BP = BQ)
As the fields produced by the two coils is equal (in magnitude), the net magnetic field will be inclined equally to both the coils.

question_html:
A rectangular loop of wire of size 4 cm {tex}\times{/tex} 10 cm carries a steady current of 2 A. A straight long wire carrying 5 A current is kept near the loop
as shown. If the loop and the wire are coplanar, find:

  1. the torque acting on the loop and
  2. the magnitude and direction of the force on the loop due to the current-carrying wire.
question_text: A rectangular loop of wire of size 4 cm {tex}\times{/tex} 10 cm carries a steady current of 2 A. A straight long wire carrying 5 A current is kept near the loop as shown. If the loop and the wire are coplanar, find: the torque acting on the loop and the magnitude and direction of the force on the loop due to the current-carrying wire.
explanation: Torque on the loop {tex}\tau{/tex} = MBsin {tex}\theta{/tex} As M and B are parallel to each other so {tex}\theta{/tex} =0 Therefore, {tex}\tau{/tex} = 0 Force acting on the loop is given by |F| = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I_{1} I_{2}}{2 \pi} l\left(\frac{1}{r_{1}}-\frac{1}{r_{2}}\right){/tex} = 2 {tex}\times 10^{-7} \times 2 \times 2 \times 10^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{10^{-2}}-\frac{1}{5 \times 10^{-2}}\right) \mathrm{N}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{8 \times 10^{-8}}{10^{-2}}\left(1-\frac{1}{5}\right) \mathrm{N}{/tex} = 8 {tex}\times 10^{-6}\left(\frac{4}{5}\right) \mathrm{N}{/tex} = 6.4 {tex}\times 10^{-6} \mathrm{N}{/tex} Direction: Towards conductor/Attractive.
solution: Torque on the loop {tex}\tau{/tex} = MBsin {tex}\theta{/tex} As M and B are parallel to each other so {tex}\theta{/tex} =0 Therefore, {tex}\tau{/tex} = 0 Force acting on the loop is given by |F| = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I_{1} I_{2}}{2 \pi} l\left(\frac{1}{r_{1}}-\frac{1}{r_{2}}\right){/tex} = 2 {tex}\times 10^{-7} \times 2 \times 2 \times 10^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{10^{-2}}-\frac{1}{5 \times 10^{-2}}\right) \mathrm{N}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{8 \times 10^{-8}}{10^{-2}}\left(1-\frac{1}{5}\right) \mathrm{N}{/tex} = 8 {tex}\times 10^{-6}\left(\frac{4}{5}\right) \mathrm{N}{/tex} = 6.4 {tex}\times 10^{-6} \mathrm{N}{/tex} Direction: Towards conductor/Attractive.
solution_html:
1. Torque on the loop
    {tex}\tau{/tex} = MBsin {tex}\theta{/tex}
    As M and B are parallel to each other so {tex}\theta{/tex}=0
    Therefore, {tex}\tau{/tex} = 0
  2. Force acting on the loop is given by  |F| = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I_{1} I_{2}}{2 \pi} l\left(\frac{1}{r_{1}}-\frac{1}{r_{2}}\right){/tex}
    = 2 {tex}\times 10^{-7} \times 2 \times 2 \times 10^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{10^{-2}}-\frac{1}{5 \times 10^{-2}}\right) \mathrm{N}{/tex}
    = {tex}\frac{8 \times 10^{-8}}{10^{-2}}\left(1-\frac{1}{5}\right) \mathrm{N}{/tex}
    = 8 {tex}\times 10^{-6}\left(\frac{4}{5}\right) \mathrm{N}{/tex}
    = 6.4 {tex}\times 10^{-6} \mathrm{N}{/tex}
    Direction: Towards conductor/Attractive.

subject: Physics
question_html: Define current sensitivity and voltage sensitivity of a galvanometer. Increasing the current sensitivity may not necessarily increase the voltage sensitivity of a galvanometer. Justify.
question_text: Define current sensitivity and voltage sensitivity of a galvanometer. Increasing the current sensitivity may not necessarily increase the voltage sensitivity of a galvanometer. Justify.
explanation: Current sensitivity of a galvanometer is the deflection produced per unit flow of current while voltage sensitivity is the deflection produced per unit applied potential difference. Current sensitivity, {tex}I_{s}=\frac{\alpha}{I}=\frac{N B A}{k}{/tex} Voltage sensitivity, {tex}V_{\mathrm{s}}=\frac{\alpha}{V}=\frac{\alpha}{I R}=\frac{N B A}{k R}{/tex} If the current sensitivity is increased by increasing the number of turns N, the resistance R will also increase. So, the voltage sensitivity might not increase on increasing the current sensitivity.
solution: Current sensitivity of a galvanometer is the deflection produced per unit flow of current while voltage sensitivity is the deflection produced per unit applied potential difference. Current sensitivity, {tex}I_{s}=\frac{\alpha}{I}=\frac{N B A}{k}{/tex} Voltage sensitivity, {tex}V_{\mathrm{s}}=\frac{\alpha}{V}=\frac{\alpha}{I R}=\frac{N B A}{k R}{/tex} If the current sensitivity is increased by increasing the number of turns N, the resistance R will also increase. So, the voltage sensitivity might not increase on increasing the current sensitivity.
solution_html:
Current sensitivity of a galvanometer is the deflection produced per unit flow of current while voltage sensitivity is the deflection produced per unit applied potential difference.
Current sensitivity, {tex}I_{s}=\frac{\alpha}{I}=\frac{N B A}{k}{/tex}
Voltage sensitivity,{tex}V_{\mathrm{s}}=\frac{\alpha}{V}=\frac{\alpha}{I R}=\frac{N B A}{k R}{/tex}
If the current sensitivity is increased by increasing the number of turns N, the resistance R will also increase. So, the voltage sensitivity might not increase on increasing the current sensitivity.

question_html: Find the expression for magnetic dipole moment of a revolving electron. What is Bohr magneton?
question_text: Find the expression for magnetic dipole moment of a revolving electron. What is Bohr magneton?
explanation: Electron revolving in circular orbit is a source of current in that orbit due to which this orbit behave as current carrying loop. We know that, Current in this orbit is given as: {tex}\Rightarrow I = e / T = e v / 2 \pi r{/tex} where, time period {tex} \Rightarrow T = 2 \pi r / v{/tex} , r = radius of orbit and v = velocity of electron in that orbit The magnetic moment of revolving electron due to the current, {tex} M= I A = \frac { e v } { 2 \pi r } \times \pi r ^ { 2 } {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow M= \frac { e vr } { 2 }{/tex} If electron revolves in anti-clockwise sense, the current will be in clockwise sense. Hence, according to right-hand rule, the direction of magnetic moment will be perpendicular to the plane of orbit and directed inwards to the plane. So, {tex}\therefore M= e vr m / 2 m = e L/ 2 m{/tex} where, mvr = L= angular momentum orbital of electron and m is the mass of electron. {tex}\therefore \quad M= - e ( L/ 2 m ){/tex} Negative sign indicates µ and L are in mutually opposite directions. From Bohr's postulates, {tex} L=mvr=\frac{nh}{2\mathrm\pi}{/tex} , where n = 1, 2, 3, .... {tex}\Rightarrow \quad M = \frac { e } { 2 m } \cdot \frac { n h } { 2 \pi } = n \mu _ B{/tex} where, {tex} \mu_B=\frac{eh}{4\mathrm{ {tex}\pi{/tex} m}}{/tex} is known as Bohr magneton. Bohr magneton is defined as the magnetic moment of first orbit of H atom.
solution: Electron revolving in circular orbit is a source of current in that orbit due to which this orbit behave as current carrying loop. We know that, Current in this orbit is given as: {tex}\Rightarrow I = e / T = e v / 2 \pi r{/tex} where, time period {tex} \Rightarrow T = 2 \pi r / v{/tex} , r = radius of orbit and v = velocity of electron in that orbit The magnetic moment of revolving electron due to the current, {tex} M= I A = \frac { e v } { 2 \pi r } \times \pi r ^ { 2 } {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow M= \frac { e vr } { 2 }{/tex} If electron revolves in anti-clockwise sense, the current will be in clockwise sense. Hence, according to right-hand rule, the direction of magnetic moment will be perpendicular to the plane of orbit and directed inwards to the plane. So, {tex}\therefore M= e vr m / 2 m = e L/ 2 m{/tex} where, mvr = L= angular momentum orbital of electron and m is the mass of electron. {tex}\therefore \quad M= - e ( L/ 2 m ){/tex} Negative sign indicates µ and L are in mutually opposite directions. From Bohr's postulates, {tex} L=mvr=\frac{nh}{2\mathrm\pi}{/tex} , where n = 1, 2, 3, .... {tex}\Rightarrow \quad M = \frac { e } { 2 m } \cdot \frac { n h } { 2 \pi } = n \mu _ B{/tex} where, {tex} \mu_B=\frac{eh}{4\mathrm{ {tex}\pi{/tex} m}}{/tex} is known as Bohr magneton. Bohr magneton is defined as the magnetic moment of first orbit of H atom.
solution_html:
Electron revolving in circular orbit is a source of current in that orbit due to which this orbit behave as current carrying loop.
We know that, Current in this orbit is given as:
{tex}\Rightarrow I = e / T = e v / 2 \pi r{/tex}
where, time period
 {tex} \Rightarrow T = 2 \pi r / v{/tex},
r = radius of orbit and
v = velocity of electron in that orbit
The magnetic moment of revolving electron due to the current,
{tex} M= I A = \frac { e v } { 2 \pi r } \times \pi r ^ { 2 } {/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow M= \frac { e vr } { 2 }{/tex}
If electron revolves in anti-clockwise sense, the current will be in clockwise sense. Hence, according to right-hand rule, the direction of magnetic moment will be perpendicular to the plane of orbit and directed inwards to the plane.
So,
{tex}\therefore M= e vr m / 2 m = e L/ 2 m{/tex}
where,
mvr = L= angular momentum orbital of electron and m is the mass of electron.
{tex}\therefore \quad M= - e ( L/ 2 m ){/tex}
Negative sign indicates µ and L are in mutually opposite directions.
From Bohr's postulates,
{tex} L=mvr=\frac{nh}{2\mathrm\pi}{/tex},
where n = 1, 2, 3, ....
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad M = \frac { e } { 2 m } \cdot \frac { n h } { 2 \pi } = n \mu _ B{/tex}
where, {tex} \mu_B=\frac{eh}{4\mathrm{{tex}\pi{/tex}m}}{/tex} is known as Bohr magneton.
Bohr magneton is defined as the magnetic moment of first orbit of H atom.

subject: Physics
question_html: A proton moving in a straight line enters a strong magnetic field along the field direction. How will its path and velocity change?
question_text: A proton moving in a straight line enters a strong magnetic field along the field direction. How will its path and velocity change?
explanation: When a proton enters a strong magnetic field along the field direction ( {tex}\theta{/tex} = 0°), the force experienced by proton is, F = qvB sin {tex}\theta{/tex} = qv B sin 0 o = 0. Therefore the path and velocity of the proton will remain unchanged.
solution: When a proton enters a strong magnetic field along the field direction ( {tex}\theta{/tex} = 0°), the force experienced by proton is, F = qvB sin {tex}\theta{/tex} = qv B sin 0 o = 0. Therefore the path and velocity of the proton will remain unchanged.
solution_html: When a proton enters a strong magnetic field along the field direction ({tex}\theta{/tex} = 0°), the force experienced by proton is, F = qvB sin {tex}\theta{/tex} = qv B sin 0o = 0. Therefore the path and velocity of the proton will remain unchanged.

subject: Physics
question_html: A long solenoid of length L having N turns carries a current I. Deduce the expression for the magnetic field in the interior of the solenoid.
question_text: A long solenoid of length L having N turns carries a current I. Deduce the expression for the magnetic field in the interior of the solenoid.
explanation: Figure shows the longitudinal sectional view of current carrying long solenoid. Let B be the magnetic field at any point inside the solenoid along ab. The current comes out of the plane of paper at points marked. The rectangular Amperian loop abcd is considered to determine the magnetic field. Applying Ampere's Circuital Law over loop abcd, {tex}\oint \mathbf { B } \cdot d \mathbf { l } = \mathbf { \mu } _ { 0 } \times{/tex} (Total enclosed current) {tex}\int_a^b {B \cdot dl} + \int_b^c {B \cdot dl} + \int_c^d {B \cdot dl} + \int_d^a {B \cdot dl} = {\mu _0}\left( {\frac{N}{L}lI} \right){/tex} where, {tex}\frac { N } { L } ={/tex} n, number of turns per unit length and ab = cd = l = length of rectangular Amperian loop, and total number of turns = {tex}\frac NLl{/tex} As the field outside the solenoid is zero, {tex}\int_c^dB.\operatorname dl\;=\;0{/tex} , {tex}\int _ { a } ^ { b } B d l \cos 0 ^ { \circ } + \int _ { b } ^ { c } B d l \cos 90 ^ { \circ } + 0{/tex} {tex}+ \int _ { d } ^ { a } B d l \cos 90 ^ { \circ } = \mu _ { 0 } \left( \frac { N } { L } \right) l I{/tex} {tex}B \int _ { a } ^ { b } d l = \mu _ { 0 } \left( \frac { N } { L } \right) lI \Rightarrow B l = \mu _ { 0 } \left( \frac { N } { L } \right) lI{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad B = \mu _ { 0 } ( N / L ) I{/tex} or {tex}B = \mu _ { 0 } n I{/tex} This is a required expression for magnetic field inside the current carrying long solenoid.
solution: Figure shows the longitudinal sectional view of current carrying long solenoid. Let B be the magnetic field at any point inside the solenoid along ab. The current comes out of the plane of paper at points marked. The rectangular Amperian loop abcd is considered to determine the magnetic field. Applying Ampere's Circuital Law over loop abcd, {tex}\oint \mathbf { B } \cdot d \mathbf { l } = \mathbf { \mu } _ { 0 } \times{/tex} (Total enclosed current) {tex}\int_a^b {B \cdot dl} + \int_b^c {B \cdot dl} + \int_c^d {B \cdot dl} + \int_d^a {B \cdot dl} = {\mu _0}\left( {\frac{N}{L}lI} \right){/tex} where, {tex}\frac { N } { L } ={/tex} n, number of turns per unit length and ab = cd = l = length of rectangular Amperian loop, and total number of turns = {tex}\frac NLl{/tex} As the field outside the solenoid is zero, {tex}\int_c^dB.\operatorname dl\;=\;0{/tex} , {tex}\int _ { a } ^ { b } B d l \cos 0 ^ { \circ } + \int _ { b } ^ { c } B d l \cos 90 ^ { \circ } + 0{/tex} {tex}+ \int _ { d } ^ { a } B d l \cos 90 ^ { \circ } = \mu _ { 0 } \left( \frac { N } { L } \right) l I{/tex} {tex}B \int _ { a } ^ { b } d l = \mu _ { 0 } \left( \frac { N } { L } \right) lI \Rightarrow B l = \mu _ { 0 } \left( \frac { N } { L } \right) lI{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad B = \mu _ { 0 } ( N / L ) I{/tex} or {tex}B = \mu _ { 0 } n I{/tex} This is a required expression for magnetic field inside the current carrying long solenoid.
solution_html:
Figure shows the longitudinal sectional view of current carrying long solenoid.

Let B be the magnetic field at any point inside the solenoid along ab. The current comes out of the plane of paper at points marked.
The rectangular Amperian loop abcd is considered to determine the magnetic field. 
Applying Ampere's Circuital Law over loop abcd,
{tex}\oint \mathbf { B } \cdot d \mathbf { l } = \mathbf { \mu } _ { 0 } \times{/tex}(Total enclosed current)
{tex}\int_a^b {B \cdot dl} + \int_b^c {B \cdot dl} + \int_c^d {B \cdot dl} + \int_d^a {B \cdot dl} = {\mu _0}\left( {\frac{N}{L}lI} \right){/tex}
where, {tex}\frac { N } { L } ={/tex} n, number of turns per unit length and ab = cd = l = length of rectangular Amperian loop, and total number of turns = {tex}\frac NLl{/tex}
As the field outside the solenoid is zero, {tex}\int_c^dB.\operatorname dl\;=\;0{/tex},
{tex}\int _ { a } ^ { b } B d l \cos 0 ^ { \circ } + \int _ { b } ^ { c } B d l \cos 90 ^ { \circ } + 0{/tex}{tex}+ \int _ { d } ^ { a } B d l \cos 90 ^ { \circ } = \mu _ { 0 } \left( \frac { N } { L } \right) l I{/tex}
{tex}B \int _ { a } ^ { b } d l = \mu _ { 0 } \left( \frac { N } { L } \right) lI \Rightarrow B l = \mu _ { 0 } \left( \frac { N } { L } \right) lI{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad B = \mu _ { 0 } ( N / L ) I{/tex}
or {tex}B = \mu _ { 0 } n I{/tex}
This is a required expression for magnetic field inside the current carrying long solenoid.

question_html: A narrow beam of protons and deuterons, each having the same momentum enters a region of the uniform magnetic field directed perpendicular to their direction of momentum. What would be the ratio of the radii of the circular path described by them?
question_text: A narrow beam of protons and deuterons, each having the same momentum enters a region of the uniform magnetic field directed perpendicular to their direction of momentum. What would be the ratio of the radii of the circular path described by them?
explanation: The radius of circular path depends on the charge and magnetic field as, r = {tex}\frac{mv}{qB}{/tex} [ {tex}\because{/tex} qvB = {tex}\frac{mv^2}{r}{/tex} ] For constant momentum, {tex}r \propto (\frac{1}{q} ){/tex} Also, charge on proton = charge on deuteron {tex}\therefore\;\frac{r_p}{r_d}=\frac{q_d}{q_p}=\frac{q_p}{q_p}=1{/tex} Hence, r p : r d = 1 : 1
solution: The radius of circular path depends on the charge and magnetic field as, r = {tex}\frac{mv}{qB}{/tex} [ {tex}\because{/tex} qvB = {tex}\frac{mv^2}{r}{/tex} ] For constant momentum, {tex}r \propto (\frac{1}{q} ){/tex} Also, charge on proton = charge on deuteron {tex}\therefore\;\frac{r_p}{r_d}=\frac{q_d}{q_p}=\frac{q_p}{q_p}=1{/tex} Hence, r p : r d = 1 : 1
solution_html:
The radius of circular path depends on the charge and magnetic field as,
r = {tex}\frac{mv}{qB}{/tex} [{tex}\because{/tex} qvB = {tex}\frac{mv^2}{r}{/tex}]
For constant momentum, {tex}r \propto (\frac{1}{q} ){/tex}
Also, charge on proton = charge on deuteron
{tex}\therefore\;\frac{r_p}{r_d}=\frac{q_d}{q_p}=\frac{q_p}{q_p}=1{/tex}
Hence, rp : rd = 1 : 1

question_html: How is a moving coil galvanometer can be converted into a voltmeter? Explain it giving the necessary circuit diagram and the required mathematical relation used.
question_text: How is a moving coil galvanometer can be converted into a voltmeter? Explain it giving the necessary circuit diagram and the required mathematical relation used.
explanation: Galvanometer can be used as voltmeter by connecting it in parallel across the section to measure the voltage. A galvanometer can be converted into a voltmeter by connecting a very high resistance R in series with it. Thus very negligible current flows through it and there is no potential drop across it. G is the resistance of the galvanometer. R is so chosen that current I g gives full deflection in the galvanometer. Now, {tex}V = I _ { g } ( G + R ) \Rightarrow R + G = \frac { V } { I _ { g } } \Rightarrow R = \frac { V } { I _ { g } } - G{/tex} This equation gives the value of resistance R that is connected in series with the galvanometer to convert into a voltmeter of range 0 to V volts. The appropriate scale of voltmeter needs to be graduated to measure the potential difference.
solution: Galvanometer can be used as voltmeter by connecting it in parallel across the section to measure the voltage. A galvanometer can be converted into a voltmeter by connecting a very high resistance R in series with it. Thus very negligible current flows through it and there is no potential drop across it. G is the resistance of the galvanometer. R is so chosen that current I g gives full deflection in the galvanometer. Now, {tex}V = I _ { g } ( G + R ) \Rightarrow R + G = \frac { V } { I _ { g } } \Rightarrow R = \frac { V } { I _ { g } } - G{/tex} This equation gives the value of resistance R that is connected in series with the galvanometer to convert into a voltmeter of range 0 to V volts. The appropriate scale of voltmeter needs to be graduated to measure the potential difference.
solution_html:
Galvanometer can be used as voltmeter by connecting it in parallel across the section to measure the voltage. A galvanometer can be converted into a voltmeter by connecting a very high resistance R in series with it. Thus very negligible current flows through it and there is no potential drop across it.
G is the resistance of the galvanometer. R is so chosen that current Ig gives full deflection in the galvanometer.

Now,
{tex}V = I _ { g } ( G + R ) \Rightarrow R + G = \frac { V } { I _ { g } } \Rightarrow R = \frac { V } { I _ { g } } - G{/tex}
This equation gives the value of resistance R that is connected in series with the galvanometer to convert into a voltmeter of range 0 to V volts.
The appropriate scale of voltmeter needs to be graduated to measure the potential difference.

question_html: Two particles A and B of masses m and 2m have charges q and 2q respectively. They are moving with velocities {tex}\upsilon {/tex}1 and {tex}\upsilon {/tex}2 respectively in the same direction, enters the same magnetic field B acting normally to their direction of motion. If the two forces FA and FB acting on them are in the ratio of 1 : 2, find the ratio of their velocities.
question_text: Two particles A and B of masses m and 2m have charges q and 2q respectively. They are moving with velocities {tex}\upsilon {/tex} 1 and {tex}\upsilon {/tex} 2 respectively in the same direction, enters the same magnetic field B acting normally to their direction of motion. If the two forces F A and F B acting on them are in the ratio of 1 : 2, find the ratio of their velocities.
explanation: The ratio of forces acting on the two particles is, {tex}\begin{array}{l}\frac{F_A}{F_B}=\frac12\\\Rightarrow\frac{qv_1B_1\;\sin90^\circ}{(2q)v_2B_2\;\sin90^\circ}=\frac12\end{array}{/tex} As given, magnetic field is same, so B 1 = B 2 = B {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{v _ { 1 }}{ v _ { 2 }} = 1 \Rightarrow v _ { 1 } : v _ { 2 } = 1 : 1{/tex}
solution: The ratio of forces acting on the two particles is, {tex}\begin{array}{l}\frac{F_A}{F_B}=\frac12\\\Rightarrow\frac{qv_1B_1\;\sin90^\circ}{(2q)v_2B_2\;\sin90^\circ}=\frac12\end{array}{/tex} As given, magnetic field is same, so B 1 = B 2 = B {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{v _ { 1 }}{ v _ { 2 }} = 1 \Rightarrow v _ { 1 } : v _ { 2 } = 1 : 1{/tex}
solution_html:
The ratio of forces acting on the two particles is,
{tex}\begin{array}{l}\frac{F_A}{F_B}=\frac12\\\Rightarrow\frac{qv_1B_1\;\sin90^\circ}{(2q)v_2B_2\;\sin90^\circ}=\frac12\end{array}{/tex}
As given, magnetic field is same, so B1 = B2 = B
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{v _ { 1 }}{ v _ { 2 }} = 1 \Rightarrow v _ { 1 } : v _ { 2 } = 1 : 1{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html: A uniform magnetic field B is set up along the positive X-axis. A particle of charge q and mass m moving with a velocity v enters the field at the origin in XY-plane such that it has velocity components both along and perpendicular to the magnetic field B. Trace, giving reason, the trajectory followed by the particle. Find out the expression for the distance moved by the particle along the magnetic field in one rotation.
question_text: A uniform magnetic field B is set up along the positive X-axis. A particle of charge q and mass m moving with a velocity v enters the field at the origin in XY-plane such that it has velocity components both along and perpendicular to the magnetic field B. Trace, giving reason, the trajectory followed by the particle. Find out the expression for the distance moved by the particle along the magnetic field in one rotation.
explanation: The path of the charged particle will be helix as the charge moves linearly in the direction of the magnetic field with velocity {tex}v \cos \theta{/tex} and also describe the circular path due to velocity {tex}v \sin \theta{/tex} . Let r be the radius of the circular path of a particle. The centripetal force perpendicular to the path towards the centre of the circle is given by {tex}m\;v^2\;/\;r{/tex} If velocity v is perpendicular to magnetic field B, the magnetic force is perpendicular to both v and B and acts like centripetal force of magnitude q v B. Thus, {tex}m\;v^2\;/\;r{/tex} = q v B Now, v = {tex}\mathrm\omega{/tex} r where {tex}\mathrm\omega{/tex} is the angular frequency. So, {tex}\mathrm\omega{/tex} = q B / m Time taken to complete one revolution, {tex}T\;=\;2\mathrm\pi\;/\;\mathrm\omega{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad T = 2 \pi m / B q{/tex} If parallel component of velocity is also present, the particle will move along the field in a helical path. Distance moved by the particle along the magnetic field in one rotation (pitch of the helix path) is, = {tex}v_\parallel\;T{/tex} {tex}\left[ \because v _ { \| } = v _ { \text { parallel } } \right]{/tex} {tex}= v \cos \theta \times 2 \pi m / B q \Rightarrow P = 2 \pi m v \cos \theta / q B{/tex}
solution: The path of the charged particle will be helix as the charge moves linearly in the direction of the magnetic field with velocity {tex}v \cos \theta{/tex} and also describe the circular path due to velocity {tex}v \sin \theta{/tex} . Let r be the radius of the circular path of a particle. The centripetal force perpendicular to the path towards the centre of the circle is given by {tex}m\;v^2\;/\;r{/tex} If velocity v is perpendicular to magnetic field B, the magnetic force is perpendicular to both v and B and acts like centripetal force of magnitude q v B. Thus, {tex}m\;v^2\;/\;r{/tex} = q v B Now, v = {tex}\mathrm\omega{/tex} r where {tex}\mathrm\omega{/tex} is the angular frequency. So, {tex}\mathrm\omega{/tex} = q B / m Time taken to complete one revolution, {tex}T\;=\;2\mathrm\pi\;/\;\mathrm\omega{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad T = 2 \pi m / B q{/tex} If parallel component of velocity is also present, the particle will move along the field in a helical path. Distance moved by the particle along the magnetic field in one rotation (pitch of the helix path) is, = {tex}v_\parallel\;T{/tex} {tex}\left[ \because v _ { \| } = v _ { \text { parallel } } \right]{/tex} {tex}= v \cos \theta \times 2 \pi m / B q \Rightarrow P = 2 \pi m v \cos \theta / q B{/tex}
solution_html:
The path of the charged particle will be helix as the charge moves linearly in the direction of the magnetic field with velocity {tex}v \cos \theta{/tex} and also describe the circular path due to velocity {tex}v \sin \theta{/tex}.

Let r be the radius of the circular path of a particle. The centripetal force perpendicular to the path towards the centre of the circle is given by {tex}m\;v^2\;/\;r{/tex}
If velocity v is perpendicular to magnetic field B, the magnetic force is perpendicular to both v and B and acts like centripetal force of magnitude q v B.
Thus, {tex}m\;v^2\;/\;r{/tex} = q v B
Now, v = {tex}\mathrm\omega{/tex} r
where {tex}\mathrm\omega{/tex} is the angular frequency. So, {tex}\mathrm\omega{/tex} = q B / m
Time taken to complete one revolution,
{tex}T\;=\;2\mathrm\pi\;/\;\mathrm\omega{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad T = 2 \pi m / B q{/tex}
If parallel component of velocity is also present, the particle will move along the field in a helical path.
Distance moved by the particle along the magnetic field in one rotation (pitch of the helix path) is,
= {tex}v_\parallel\;T{/tex} {tex}\left[ \because v _ { \| } = v _ { \text { parallel } } \right]{/tex}
{tex}= v \cos \theta \times 2 \pi m / B q \Rightarrow P = 2 \pi m v \cos \theta / q B{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html: A straight wire of length L is bent into a semi-circular loop. Use Biot-Savart's law to deduce an expression for the magnetic field at its centre due to the current I passing through it.
question_text: A straight wire of length L is bent into a semi-circular loop. Use Biot-Savart's law to deduce an expression for the magnetic field at its centre due to the current I passing through it.
explanation: A straight wire of length L carrying a current I is bent into semi-circular loop of arc radius r. Consider the figure. Considering a small element dl on circular current loop. The magnitude dB of magnetic field due to small current element dl is given by Biot-Savart's law: {tex}dB=\frac{\mu_0}{4\mathrm\pi}\frac{I\left|dl\;\times\;r\right|}{r^3}{/tex} Now, dB due to the straight segments is zero as dl and r are parallel. The magnetic field at the centre C of the loop for segments of semi-circular arc is calculated below. Length of wire = Circumference of semi-equal circular wire {tex}\Rightarrow \quad L = \pi r \Rightarrow r = \frac { L } { \pi }{/tex} ....... (i) The magnetic field dB, {tex}d B = \frac { \mu _ { 0 } } { 4 \pi } \cdot \frac { I d l \sin 90 ^ { \circ } } { r ^ { 2 } }{/tex} {tex}\left[ \because \text { Idl } \perp \mathbf { r } , \ldots \theta = 90 ^ { \circ } \right]{/tex} {tex}d B = \frac { \mu _ { 0 } } { 4 \pi } \cdot \frac { I d l } { r ^ { 2 } }{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Net magnetic field at C due to semi-circular loop {tex}B = \int _ { \text { semicircle } } \frac { \mu _ { 0 } } { 4 \pi } \frac { I d l } { r ^ { 2 } }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow B = \frac { \mu _ { 0 } } { 4 \pi } \frac { I } { r ^ { 2 } } \int _ { \text { semicircle } } d l{/tex} {tex}B = \frac { \mu _ { 0 } } { 4 \pi } \cdot \frac { I } { r ^ { 2 } } L{/tex} Using (i), {tex}r = L / \pi{/tex} {tex}B = \frac { \mu _ { 0 } } { 4 \pi } \cdot \frac { IL } { ( L / \pi ) ^ { 2 } } = \frac { \mu _ { 0 } } { 4 \pi } \times \frac { I L } { L ^ { 2 } } \times \pi ^ { 2 } \Rightarrow B = \frac { \mu _ { 0 } I \pi } { 4 L }{/tex} This is the required expression of the magnetic field. From right hand rule, direction of B is normal to the plane of the paper going into it.
solution: A straight wire of length L carrying a current I is bent into semi-circular loop of arc radius r. Consider the figure. Considering a small element dl on circular current loop. The magnitude dB of magnetic field due to small current element dl is given by Biot-Savart's law: {tex}dB=\frac{\mu_0}{4\mathrm\pi}\frac{I\left|dl\;\times\;r\right|}{r^3}{/tex} Now, dB due to the straight segments is zero as dl and r are parallel. The magnetic field at the centre C of the loop for segments of semi-circular arc is calculated below. Length of wire = Circumference of semi-equal circular wire {tex}\Rightarrow \quad L = \pi r \Rightarrow r = \frac { L } { \pi }{/tex} ....... (i) The magnetic field dB, {tex}d B = \frac { \mu _ { 0 } } { 4 \pi } \cdot \frac { I d l \sin 90 ^ { \circ } } { r ^ { 2 } }{/tex} {tex}\left[ \because \text { Idl } \perp \mathbf { r } , \ldots \theta = 90 ^ { \circ } \right]{/tex} {tex}d B = \frac { \mu _ { 0 } } { 4 \pi } \cdot \frac { I d l } { r ^ { 2 } }{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Net magnetic field at C due to semi-circular loop {tex}B = \int _ { \text { semicircle } } \frac { \mu _ { 0 } } { 4 \pi } \frac { I d l } { r ^ { 2 } }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow B = \frac { \mu _ { 0 } } { 4 \pi } \frac { I } { r ^ { 2 } } \int _ { \text { semicircle } } d l{/tex} {tex}B = \frac { \mu _ { 0 } } { 4 \pi } \cdot \frac { I } { r ^ { 2 } } L{/tex} Using (i), {tex}r = L / \pi{/tex} {tex}B = \frac { \mu _ { 0 } } { 4 \pi } \cdot \frac { IL } { ( L / \pi ) ^ { 2 } } = \frac { \mu _ { 0 } } { 4 \pi } \times \frac { I L } { L ^ { 2 } } \times \pi ^ { 2 } \Rightarrow B = \frac { \mu _ { 0 } I \pi } { 4 L }{/tex} This is the required expression of the magnetic field. From right hand rule, direction of B is normal to the plane of the paper going into it.
solution_html:
A straight wire of length L carrying a current I is bent into semi-circular loop of arc radius r. Consider the figure.

Considering a small element dl on circular current loop. The magnitude dB of magnetic field due to small current element dl is given by Biot-Savart's law:

{tex}dB=\frac{\mu_0}{4\mathrm\pi}\frac{I\left|dl\;\times\;r\right|}{r^3}{/tex}

Now, dB due to the straight segments is zero as dl and r are parallel.

The magnetic field at the centre C of the loop for segments of semi-circular arc is calculated below.
Length of wire = Circumference of semi-equal circular wire
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad L = \pi r \Rightarrow r = \frac { L } { \pi }{/tex}....... (i)
The magnetic field dB,
{tex}d B = \frac { \mu _ { 0 } } { 4 \pi } \cdot \frac { I d l \sin 90 ^ { \circ } } { r ^ { 2 } }{/tex} {tex}\left[ \because \text { Idl } \perp \mathbf { r } , \ldots \theta = 90 ^ { \circ } \right]{/tex}
{tex}d B = \frac { \mu _ { 0 } } { 4 \pi } \cdot \frac { I d l } { r ^ { 2 } }{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Net magnetic field at C due to semi-circular loop
{tex}B = \int _ { \text { semicircle } } \frac { \mu _ { 0 } } { 4 \pi } \frac { I d l } { r ^ { 2 } }{/tex}{tex}\Rightarrow B = \frac { \mu _ { 0 } } { 4 \pi } \frac { I } { r ^ { 2 } } \int _ { \text { semicircle } } d l{/tex}
{tex}B = \frac { \mu _ { 0 } } { 4 \pi } \cdot \frac { I } { r ^ { 2 } } L{/tex}
Using (i), {tex}r = L / \pi{/tex}
{tex}B = \frac { \mu _ { 0 } } { 4 \pi } \cdot \frac { IL } { ( L / \pi ) ^ { 2 } } = \frac { \mu _ { 0 } } { 4 \pi } \times \frac { I L } { L ^ { 2 } } \times \pi ^ { 2 } \Rightarrow B = \frac { \mu _ { 0 } I \pi } { 4 L }{/tex}
This is the required expression of the magnetic field. From right hand rule, direction of B is normal to the plane of the paper going into it.

subject: Physics
question_html: A steady current I1 flows through a long straight wire. Another wire carrying steady current I2 in the same direction is kept close and parallel to the first wire. Show with the help of a diagram, how the magnetic field due to the current I1 exert a magnetic force on the second wire. Deduce the expression for this force.
question_text: A steady current I 1 flows through a long straight wire. Another wire carrying steady current I 2 in the same direction is kept close and parallel to the first wire. Show with the help of a diagram, how the magnetic field due to the current I 1 exert a magnetic force on the second wire. Deduce the expression for this force.
explanation: When a conductor carries current, magnetic field exists. This external magnetic field will exert a force on the current carrying conductor. Thus, when two current carrying conductors are placed near to each other, they exert magnetic forces on each other. Consider the figure. Let two infinitely long straight current carrying conductors separated by distance 'r' carry currents I 1 and I 2 in the same direction. Magnetic field B 1 due to a on b from Ampere circuital law is, {tex}B _ { 1 } = \frac { \mu _ { 0 } } { 4 \pi } \frac { 2 I _ { 1 } } { r } = \frac { \mu _ { 0 } I _ { 1 } } { 2 \pi r }{/tex} ....... (i) The direction of magnetic field is perpendicular to the plane of paper and directed inwards. Now, the conductor 'b' experience a force, F 2 due to the field B 1 . Thus, Magnetic Force on length L of second wire is given by {tex}F _ { 2 } = I _ { 2 } B _ { 1 } L \sin 90 ^ { \circ } \Rightarrow F _ { 2 } = I _ { 2 } \left( \frac { \mu _ { 0 } } { 4 \pi } \cdot \frac { 2 I _ { 1 } } { r } \right) L{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { F _ { 2 } } { L } = \frac { \mu _ { 0 } } { 4 \pi } \frac { 2 I _ { 1 } I _ { 2 } } { r } = \frac { \mu _ { 0 } I _ { 1 } I _ { 2 } } { 2 \pi r }{/tex} ..... (ii) By Fleming's left hand rule, the direction of force F 2 is towards the conductor 'a' Similarly, F 1 is calculated which is equal in magnitude to F 2 and directed towards 'b'. Thus, F 1 = - F 2 Thus the two straight parallel current carrying conductors having the same direction of flow of currents attract each other.
solution: When a conductor carries current, magnetic field exists. This external magnetic field will exert a force on the current carrying conductor. Thus, when two current carrying conductors are placed near to each other, they exert magnetic forces on each other. Consider the figure. Let two infinitely long straight current carrying conductors separated by distance 'r' carry currents I 1 and I 2 in the same direction. Magnetic field B 1 due to a on b from Ampere circuital law is, {tex}B _ { 1 } = \frac { \mu _ { 0 } } { 4 \pi } \frac { 2 I _ { 1 } } { r } = \frac { \mu _ { 0 } I _ { 1 } } { 2 \pi r }{/tex} ....... (i) The direction of magnetic field is perpendicular to the plane of paper and directed inwards. Now, the conductor 'b' experience a force, F 2 due to the field B 1 . Thus, Magnetic Force on length L of second wire is given by {tex}F _ { 2 } = I _ { 2 } B _ { 1 } L \sin 90 ^ { \circ } \Rightarrow F _ { 2 } = I _ { 2 } \left( \frac { \mu _ { 0 } } { 4 \pi } \cdot \frac { 2 I _ { 1 } } { r } \right) L{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { F _ { 2 } } { L } = \frac { \mu _ { 0 } } { 4 \pi } \frac { 2 I _ { 1 } I _ { 2 } } { r } = \frac { \mu _ { 0 } I _ { 1 } I _ { 2 } } { 2 \pi r }{/tex} ..... (ii) By Fleming's left hand rule, the direction of force F 2 is towards the conductor 'a' Similarly, F 1 is calculated which is equal in magnitude to F 2 and directed towards 'b'. Thus, F 1 = - F 2 Thus the two straight parallel current carrying conductors having the same direction of flow of currents attract each other.
solution_html:
When a conductor carries current, magnetic field exists. This external magnetic field will exert a force on the current carrying conductor. Thus, when two current carrying conductors are placed near to each other, they exert magnetic forces on each other.

Consider the figure. Let two infinitely long straight current carrying conductors separated by distance 'r' carry currents I1 and I2 in the same direction.


Magnetic field B1 due to a on b from Ampere circuital law is,
{tex}B _ { 1 } = \frac { \mu _ { 0 } } { 4 \pi } \frac { 2 I _ { 1 } } { r } = \frac { \mu _ { 0 } I _ { 1 } } { 2 \pi r }{/tex} ....... (i)
The direction of magnetic field is perpendicular to the plane of paper and directed inwards.

Now, the conductor 'b' experience a force, F2 due to the field B1. Thus, Magnetic Force on length L of second wire is given by
{tex}F _ { 2 } = I _ { 2 } B _ { 1 } L \sin 90 ^ { \circ } \Rightarrow F _ { 2 } = I _ { 2 } \left( \frac { \mu _ { 0 } } { 4 \pi } \cdot \frac { 2 I _ { 1 } } { r } \right) L{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { F _ { 2 } } { L } = \frac { \mu _ { 0 } } { 4 \pi } \frac { 2 I _ { 1 } I _ { 2 } } { r } = \frac { \mu _ { 0 } I _ { 1 } I _ { 2 } } { 2 \pi r }{/tex} ..... (ii)
By Fleming's left hand rule, the direction of force F2 is towards the conductor 'a'
Similarly, F1 is calculated which is equal in magnitude to F2 and directed towards 'b'. Thus,
F1 = - F2
Thus the two straight parallel current carrying conductors having the same direction of flow of currents attract each other.

subject: Physics
question_html: State Biot-Savart's law and give the mathematical expression for it. Use this law to derive the expression for the magnetic field due to a circular coil carrying current at a point along its axis. How does a circular loop carrying current behave as a magnet?
question_text: State Biot-Savart's law and give the mathematical expression for it. Use this law to derive the expression for the magnetic field due to a circular coil carrying current at a point along its axis. How does a circular loop carrying current behave as a magnet?
explanation: Biot Savart's Law: It states that the magnetic field strength (dB) produced due to a current element (of current I and length dl) at a point having position vector r relative to current element is {tex}\overrightarrow{dB}\;=\frac{\mu_0}{4\pi}\frac{\overrightarrow{Idl}\;\times\overrightarrow r}{r^3}{/tex} Where {tex}\mu_0{/tex} is permeability of free space. The direction of magnetic field vector dB is perpendicular to the plane containing vector Idl and vector r. Magnetic field at the axis of a circular loop: Consider a circular loop of radius R carrying current I, with its plane perpendicular to the plane of paper. Let P be a point of observation on the axis of this circular loop at a distance x from its centre O as shown in figure. Consider a small element of length dl of the coil at point A. The magnitude of the magnetic induction vector dB at point P due to this element is given by {tex}dB\;=\frac{\mu_0I\;dl\sin90}{4\pi r^2}=\frac{\mu_0I\;dl}{4\pi r^2}{/tex} ........ (1) (angle between I vector dl and vector r is 90°) The direction of vector dB is perpendicular to the plane containing vector dl and vector r and is given by right hand screw rule. In figure, vector dB has been represented by vector Q. The vector Q can be resolved into two components, namely PM and PN. {tex}dB_x\;=\frac{\mu_0I\;dl}{4\pi r^2}\sin\alpha{/tex} {tex}dB_y\;=\frac{\mu_0I\;dl}{4\pi r^2}\cos\alpha{/tex} Now consider another small current element of length dI' at A'. The magnetic induction vector dB due to this element has been represented by vector Q ' and is given as {tex}dB'\;=\frac{\mu_0I\;dl}{4\pi r^2}\sin90=\frac{\mu_0I\;dl}{4\pi r^2}{/tex} ....... (2) So dB = dB ' which can also be resolved into two components; PM and PN' along the axis and perpendicular to the axis respectively. Thus if we consider the magnetic induction produced by the whole of the circular coil, then by symmetry the components of magnetic induction perpendicular to the axis will be cancelled out, while those parallel to the axis will be added up. Thus the resultant magnetic induction vector B at axial point P is along the axis and may be evaluated as follows: {tex}B=\int dB_x\;=\int\frac{\mu_0I\;dl}{4\pi r^2}\sin\alpha=\int\frac{\mu_0Idl\;}{4\pi r^2}\frac Rr{/tex} {tex}B=\frac{\mu_0IR\;}{4\pi r^3}\int dl\;=\;\frac{\mu_0IR\;}{4\pi r^3}2\mathrm{ {tex}\pi{/tex} R}{/tex} ...... (3) Putting {tex}\mathrm r=\sqrt{\mathrm R^2+\mathrm x^2}{/tex} we get, {tex}B=\frac{\mu_0IR^2}{2\left(R^2+x^2\right)^{3/2}}{/tex} This is the total magnetic field due to current carrying coil on the axis of coil.
solution: Biot Savart's Law: It states that the magnetic field strength (dB) produced due to a current element (of current I and length dl) at a point having position vector r relative to current element is {tex}\overrightarrow{dB}\;=\frac{\mu_0}{4\pi}\frac{\overrightarrow{Idl}\;\times\overrightarrow r}{r^3}{/tex} Where {tex}\mu_0{/tex} is permeability of free space. The direction of magnetic field vector dB is perpendicular to the plane containing vector Idl and vector r. Magnetic field at the axis of a circular loop: Consider a circular loop of radius R carrying current I, with its plane perpendicular to the plane of paper. Let P be a point of observation on the axis of this circular loop at a distance x from its centre O as shown in figure. Consider a small element of length dl of the coil at point A. The magnitude of the magnetic induction vector dB at point P due to this element is given by {tex}dB\;=\frac{\mu_0I\;dl\sin90}{4\pi r^2}=\frac{\mu_0I\;dl}{4\pi r^2}{/tex} ........ (1) (angle between I vector dl and vector r is 90°) The direction of vector dB is perpendicular to the plane containing vector dl and vector r and is given by right hand screw rule. In figure, vector dB has been represented by vector Q. The vector Q can be resolved into two components, namely PM and PN. {tex}dB_x\;=\frac{\mu_0I\;dl}{4\pi r^2}\sin\alpha{/tex} {tex}dB_y\;=\frac{\mu_0I\;dl}{4\pi r^2}\cos\alpha{/tex} Now consider another small current element of length dI' at A'. The magnetic induction vector dB due to this element has been represented by vector Q ' and is given as {tex}dB'\;=\frac{\mu_0I\;dl}{4\pi r^2}\sin90=\frac{\mu_0I\;dl}{4\pi r^2}{/tex} ....... (2) So dB = dB ' which can also be resolved into two components; PM and PN' along the axis and perpendicular to the axis respectively. Thus if we consider the magnetic induction produced by the whole of the circular coil, then by symmetry the components of magnetic induction perpendicular to the axis will be cancelled out, while those parallel to the axis will be added up. Thus the resultant magnetic induction vector B at axial point P is along the axis and may be evaluated as follows: {tex}B=\int dB_x\;=\int\frac{\mu_0I\;dl}{4\pi r^2}\sin\alpha=\int\frac{\mu_0Idl\;}{4\pi r^2}\frac Rr{/tex} {tex}B=\frac{\mu_0IR\;}{4\pi r^3}\int dl\;=\;\frac{\mu_0IR\;}{4\pi r^3}2\mathrm{ {tex}\pi{/tex} R}{/tex} ...... (3) Putting {tex}\mathrm r=\sqrt{\mathrm R^2+\mathrm x^2}{/tex} we get, {tex}B=\frac{\mu_0IR^2}{2\left(R^2+x^2\right)^{3/2}}{/tex} This is the total magnetic field due to current carrying coil on the axis of coil.
solution_html:
Biot Savart's Law: It states that the magnetic field strength (dB) produced due to a current element (of current I and length dl) at a point having position vector r relative to current element is 
{tex}\overrightarrow{dB}\;=\frac{\mu_0}{4\pi}\frac{\overrightarrow{Idl}\;\times\overrightarrow r}{r^3}{/tex}
Where {tex}\mu_0{/tex} is permeability of free space.
The direction of magnetic field vector dB is perpendicular to the plane containing vector Idl and vector r. 
Magnetic field at the axis of a circular loop: Consider a circular loop of radius R carrying current I, with its plane perpendicular to the plane of paper. Let P be a point of observation on the axis of this circular loop at a distance x from its centre O as shown in figure. Consider a small element of length dl of the coil at point A. The magnitude of the magnetic induction vector dB at point P due to this element is given by

{tex}dB\;=\frac{\mu_0I\;dl\sin90}{4\pi r^2}=\frac{\mu_0I\;dl}{4\pi r^2}{/tex} ........ (1)
(angle between I vector dl and vector r is 90°)
The direction of vector dB is perpendicular to the plane containing vector dl and vector r and is given by right hand screw rule. In figure, vector dB has been represented by vector Q. The vector Q can be
resolved into two components, namely PM and PN.
{tex}dB_x\;=\frac{\mu_0I\;dl}{4\pi r^2}\sin\alpha{/tex}
{tex}dB_y\;=\frac{\mu_0I\;dl}{4\pi r^2}\cos\alpha{/tex}
Now consider another small current element of length dI' at  A'. The magnetic induction vector dB due to this element has been represented by vector Q' and is given as
{tex}dB'\;=\frac{\mu_0I\;dl}{4\pi r^2}\sin90=\frac{\mu_0I\;dl}{4\pi r^2}{/tex} ....... (2)
So dB = dB'
which can also be resolved into two components; PM and PN' along the axis and perpendicular to the axis respectively. Thus if we consider the magnetic induction produced by the whole of the circular coil, then by symmetry the components of magnetic induction perpendicular to the axis will be cancelled out, while those parallel to the axis will be added up. Thus the resultant magnetic induction vector B at axial point P is along the axis and may be evaluated as follows:
{tex}B=\int dB_x\;=\int\frac{\mu_0I\;dl}{4\pi r^2}\sin\alpha=\int\frac{\mu_0Idl\;}{4\pi r^2}\frac Rr{/tex}
{tex}B=\frac{\mu_0IR\;}{4\pi r^3}\int dl\;=\;\frac{\mu_0IR\;}{4\pi r^3}2\mathrm{{tex}\pi{/tex}R}{/tex} ...... (3)
Putting {tex}\mathrm r=\sqrt{\mathrm R^2+\mathrm x^2}{/tex} we get,
{tex}B=\frac{\mu_0IR^2}{2\left(R^2+x^2\right)^{3/2}}{/tex}
This is the total magnetic field due to current carrying coil on the axis of coil.

question_html: Two long straight parallel conductors carrying steady currents I1 and I2 are separated by a distance d. Explain briefly, with the help of a suitable diagram, how the magnetic field due to one conductor acts on the other. Hence deduce the expression for the force acting between the two conductors. Mention the nature of this force.
question_text: Two long straight parallel conductors carrying steady currents I 1 and I 2 are separated by a distance d. Explain briefly, with the help of a suitable diagram, how the magnetic field due to one conductor acts on the other. Hence deduce the expression for the force acting between the two conductors. Mention the nature of this force.
explanation: B a =magnetic field due to wire 'a' B b = magnetic field due to wire 'b' The magnetic field, due to wire 1, at any point on the wire 2, is directed normal to the direction of current flow in wire 2. Magnetic field around wire 2 due to a current l 1 in wire {tex}\mathrm{B}_{a}=\frac{\mu_{0} \mathrm{I}_{1}}{2 \pi r}{/tex} Force upon conductor carrying current due to magnetic field {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{F}}=I(\vec{l} \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}}){/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Force, F 21 on a length l of wire 2 = {tex}\mathrm{I}_{2} l \frac{\mu_{0} \mathrm{I}_{1}}{2 \pi r}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I_{1} I_{2}}{2 \pi r}.l{/tex} Similarly, F 12 = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I_{1} I_{2}}{2 \pi r}.l{/tex} The nature of the force is repulsive for currents in opposite direction and attractive when currents flow in the same direction.
solution: B a =magnetic field due to wire 'a' B b = magnetic field due to wire 'b' The magnetic field, due to wire 1, at any point on the wire 2, is directed normal to the direction of current flow in wire 2. Magnetic field around wire 2 due to a current l 1 in wire {tex}\mathrm{B}_{a}=\frac{\mu_{0} \mathrm{I}_{1}}{2 \pi r}{/tex} Force upon conductor carrying current due to magnetic field {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{F}}=I(\vec{l} \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}}){/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Force, F 21 on a length l of wire 2 = {tex}\mathrm{I}_{2} l \frac{\mu_{0} \mathrm{I}_{1}}{2 \pi r}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I_{1} I_{2}}{2 \pi r}.l{/tex} Similarly, F 12 = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I_{1} I_{2}}{2 \pi r}.l{/tex} The nature of the force is repulsive for currents in opposite direction and attractive when currents flow in the same direction.
solution_html:
Ba =magnetic field due to wire 'a'

Bb = magnetic field due to wire 'b'
The magnetic field, due to wire 1, at any point on the wire 2, is directed normal to the direction of current flow in wire 2.
Magnetic field around wire 2 due to a current l1 in wire
{tex}\mathrm{B}_{a}=\frac{\mu_{0} \mathrm{I}_{1}}{2 \pi r}{/tex}
Force upon conductor carrying current due to magnetic field
{tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{F}}=I(\vec{l} \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}}){/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Force, F21 on a length l of wire 2 = {tex}\mathrm{I}_{2} l \frac{\mu_{0} \mathrm{I}_{1}}{2 \pi r}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I_{1} I_{2}}{2 \pi r}.l{/tex}
Similarly, F12 = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I_{1} I_{2}}{2 \pi r}.l{/tex}
The nature of the force is repulsive for currents in opposite direction and attractive when currents flow in the same direction.

subject: Physics
question_html: How does a current-carrying coil behave like a bar magnet?
question_text: How does a current-carrying coil behave like a bar magnet?
explanation: A current-carrying loop behaves as a bar magnet because It possesses a magnetic dipole moment (m = IA), and It experiences a torque in an external magnetic field. This torque tends to align the axis of the loop along the direction of the field.
solution: A current-carrying loop behaves as a bar magnet because It possesses a magnetic dipole moment (m = IA), and It experiences a torque in an external magnetic field. This torque tends to align the axis of the loop along the direction of the field.
solution_html:
A current-carrying loop behaves as a bar magnet because

  1. It possesses a magnetic dipole moment (m = IA), and
  2. It experiences a torque in an external magnetic field. This torque tends to align the axis of the loop along the direction of the field.

question_html: Two particles A and B of masses m and 2m have charges q and 2q respectively. Both these particles moving with velocities v1 and v2 respectively in the same direction enter the same magnetic field B acting normally to their direction of motion. If the two forces FA and FB acting on them are in the ratio 1 : 2, find the ratio of their velocities.
question_text: Two particles A and B of masses m and 2m have charges q and 2q respectively. Both these particles moving with velocities v 1 and v 2 respectively in the same direction enter the same magnetic field B acting normally to their direction of motion. If the two forces F A and F B acting on them are in the ratio 1 : 2, find the ratio of their velocities.
explanation: {tex}\frac{F_{A}}{F_{B}}=\frac{q v_{1} B \sin 90^{\circ}}{2 q v_{2} B \sin 90^{\circ}}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{v_{1}}{v_{2}}=1: 1{/tex}
solution: {tex}\frac{F_{A}}{F_{B}}=\frac{q v_{1} B \sin 90^{\circ}}{2 q v_{2} B \sin 90^{\circ}}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{v_{1}}{v_{2}}=1: 1{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}\frac{F_{A}}{F_{B}}=\frac{q v_{1} B \sin 90^{\circ}}{2 q v_{2} B \sin 90^{\circ}}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \frac{v_{1}}{v_{2}}=1: 1{/tex}

options.explanation: Current, {tex}I=\frac{q}{T}=q f{/tex} {tex}B=\frac{\mu_{0} I}{2 R}=\frac{\mu_{0} q f}{2 R}{/tex}

explanation: Current, {tex}I=\frac{q}{T}=q f{/tex} {tex}B=\frac{\mu_{0} I}{2 R}=\frac{\mu_{0} q f}{2 R}{/tex}

question_html: What uniform magnetic field applied perpendicular to a beam of electrons moving at 1.3 {tex}\times{/tex} 106 m/s, is required to make the electrons travel in a circular arc of radius 0.35 m?
question_text: What uniform magnetic field applied perpendicular to a beam of electrons moving at 1.3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 6 m/s, is required to make the electrons travel in a circular arc of radius 0.35 m?

options.explanation: From {tex}\mathbf{r}=\frac{\mathbf{m v}}{\mathbf{q B}}{/tex} we find {tex}\mathbf{B}=\frac{\mathbf{m}_{\mathrm{e}} \mathbf{v}}{\mathrm{er}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\left(9.11 \times 10^{-31} \mathrm{~kg}\right)\left(1.30 \times 10^{6} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}\right)}{\left(1.60 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{C}\right)(0.350 \mathrm{~m})}{/tex} = 2.11 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -5 T.

explanation: From {tex}\mathbf{r}=\frac{\mathbf{m v}}{\mathbf{q B}}{/tex} we find {tex}\mathbf{B}=\frac{\mathbf{m}_{\mathrm{e}} \mathbf{v}}{\mathrm{er}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\left(9.11 \times 10^{-31} \mathrm{~kg}\right)\left(1.30 \times 10^{6} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}\right)}{\left(1.60 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{C}\right)(0.350 \mathrm{~m})}{/tex} = 2.11 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -5 T.

question_html: A current-carrying closed loop in the form of a right-angle isosceles triangle ABC is placed in a uniform magnetic field acting along with AB. If the magnetic force on the arm BC is {tex}\vec{F}{/tex}, the force on the arm AC is:
question_text: A current-carrying closed loop in the form of a right-angle isosceles triangle ABC is placed in a uniform magnetic field acting along with AB. If the magnetic force on the arm BC is {tex}\vec{F}{/tex} , the force on the arm AC is:

options.explanation: The component of AC perpendicular to the magnetic field is just equal in magnitude and opposite in direction to BC. So the force on AC is {tex}-\vec{F}{/tex} .
