subject: Physics
question_html:
1. Using Ampere's circuital law, obtain the expression for the magnetic field due to a long solenoid at a point inside the solenoid on its axis.
  2. In what respect is a toroid different from a solenoid? Draw and compare the pattern of the magnetic field lines in two cases.
  3. How is the magnetic field inside a given solenoid made strong?
question_text: Using Ampere's circuital law, obtain the expression for the magnetic field due to a long solenoid at a point inside the solenoid on its axis. In what respect is a toroid different from a solenoid? Draw and compare the pattern of the magnetic field lines in two cases. How is the magnetic field inside a given solenoid made strong?
explanation: Consider a symmetrical long solenoid having a number of turns per unit length equal to n. Let I be the current flowing in the solenoid, then by the right-hand rule, the magnetic field is parallel to the axis of the solenoid. Field inside the solenoid: Consider a closed path abcd. Now, using Ampere's circuital law to this path, we have {tex}\oint \overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}=\mu \times 0{/tex} Therefore, B = 0 This implies, the magnetic field outside the solenoid is 0. Field inside the solenoid: Consider a closed path pqrs. The line integral of the magnetic field is given by, {tex}\oint_{\text {pqrs }} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}{/tex} = {tex}\int_{p q} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}+\int_{q r} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}+\int_{r s} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}+\int_{s p} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}{/tex} ....(i) For path pq, {tex}\vec{B}{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{dl}}{/tex} are along the same direction, {tex}\therefore \quad \int_{q r} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}=\int_{S p} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}} \cdot d \vec{l}=\int B d l \cos 90^{\circ}{/tex} = 0 For path rs, B = 0 because outside the solenoid field is zero. {tex}\therefore \quad \int_{r s} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}=0{/tex} Now, using Ampere's law, Using these equations, equation (i) gives, {tex}\oint_{p q r s} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}=\int_{p q} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}=B l{/tex} Now, using Ampere's law, {tex}\oint \vec{B} \cdot \vec{d} I=\mu_{0} I{/tex} This implies, {tex}B l=\mu_{0}(n l I){/tex} {tex}\therefore B=\mu_{0} n I{/tex} Magnetic lines do not exist outside the body of a toroid. Toroid is closed and the solenoid is open on both sides. The magnetic field is uniform inside a toroid whereas, for a solenoid, it is different at two ends and centre. The magnetic field is made strong by, passing large current and using a laminated coil of soft iron.
solution: Consider a symmetrical long solenoid having a number of turns per unit length equal to n. Let I be the current flowing in the solenoid, then by the right-hand rule, the magnetic field is parallel to the axis of the solenoid. Field inside the solenoid: Consider a closed path abcd. Now, using Ampere's circuital law to this path, we have {tex}\oint \overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}=\mu \times 0{/tex} Therefore, B = 0 This implies, the magnetic field outside the solenoid is 0. Field inside the solenoid: Consider a closed path pqrs. The line integral of the magnetic field is given by, {tex}\oint_{\text {pqrs }} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}{/tex} = {tex}\int_{p q} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}+\int_{q r} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}+\int_{r s} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}+\int_{s p} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}{/tex} ....(i) For path pq, {tex}\vec{B}{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{dl}}{/tex} are along the same direction, {tex}\therefore \quad \int_{q r} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}=\int_{S p} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}} \cdot d \vec{l}=\int B d l \cos 90^{\circ}{/tex} = 0 For path rs, B = 0 because outside the solenoid field is zero. {tex}\therefore \quad \int_{r s} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}=0{/tex} Now, using Ampere's law, Using these equations, equation (i) gives, {tex}\oint_{p q r s} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}=\int_{p q} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}=B l{/tex} Now, using Ampere's law, {tex}\oint \vec{B} \cdot \vec{d} I=\mu_{0} I{/tex} This implies, {tex}B l=\mu_{0}(n l I){/tex} {tex}\therefore B=\mu_{0} n I{/tex} Magnetic lines do not exist outside the body of a toroid. Toroid is closed and the solenoid is open on both sides. The magnetic field is uniform inside a toroid whereas, for a solenoid, it is different at two ends and centre. The magnetic field is made strong by, passing large current and using a laminated coil of soft iron.
solution_html:
1. Consider a symmetrical long solenoid having a number of turns per unit length equal to n.
    Let I be the current flowing in the solenoid, then by the right-hand rule, the magnetic field is parallel to the axis of the solenoid.

    Field inside the solenoid:
    Consider a closed path abcd.
    Now, using Ampere's circuital law to this path, we have
    {tex}\oint \overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}=\mu \times 0{/tex}
    Therefore, B = 0
    This implies, the magnetic field outside the solenoid is 0.
    Field inside the solenoid:
    Consider a closed path pqrs.
    The line integral of the magnetic field is given by,
    {tex}\oint_{\text {pqrs }} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}{/tex} = {tex}\int_{p q} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}+\int_{q r} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}+\int_{r s} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}+\int_{s p} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}{/tex} ....(i)
    For path pq, {tex}\vec{B}{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{dl}}{/tex} are along the same direction,
    {tex}\therefore \quad \int_{q r} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}=\int_{S p} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}} \cdot d \vec{l}=\int B d l \cos 90^{\circ}{/tex} = 0
    For path rs, B = 0 because outside the solenoid field is zero.
    {tex}\therefore \quad \int_{r s} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}=0{/tex}
    Now, using Ampere's law,
    Using these equations, equation (i) gives,
    {tex}\oint_{p q r s} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}=\int_{p q} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}=B l{/tex}
    Now, using Ampere's law,
    {tex}\oint \vec{B} \cdot \vec{d} I=\mu_{0} I{/tex}
    This implies,
    {tex}B l=\mu_{0}(n l I){/tex}
    {tex}\therefore B=\mu_{0} n I{/tex}
  2. Magnetic lines do not exist outside the body of a toroid. Toroid is closed and the solenoid is open on both sides. The magnetic field is uniform inside a toroid whereas, for a solenoid, it is different at two ends and centre.
  3. The magnetic field is made strong by,
    1. passing large current and
    2. using a laminated coil of soft iron.

subject: Physics
question_html: A wire of length L is bent into a semi-circular loop. Use Biot-Savart law to deduce an expression for the magneitc field at the centre due to current 7 passing through it.
question_text: A wire of length L is bent into a semi-circular loop. Use Biot-Savart law to deduce an expression for the magneitc field at the centre due to current 7 passing through it.
explanation: As shown in fig. consider a wire of length L bent into a semicircular loop of radius {tex}\begin{equation} R\left(R=\frac{L}{\pi}\right) \end{equation}{/tex} . Let a current I flows through it. To calculate magnetic field at centre O of the loop, Let us consider a small current Idl. Then as per Biot-Savart.s law {tex}\begin{equation} d B=\frac{\mu_0}{4 \pi} \frac{I d l \sin \theta}{R^2}=\frac{\mu_0}{4 \pi} \frac{I d l \sin 90^{\circ}}{R^2}=\frac{\mu_0 I d l}{4 \pi R^2} \end{equation}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Total magnetic field at point O due to entire wire {tex}\begin{equation} B=\frac{\mu_0 I}{4 \pi R^2} \sum d l=\frac{\mu_0 I}{4 \pi R^2} \cdot \pi R=\frac{\mu_0 I}{4 R}=\frac{\mu_0 \pi I}{4 L} \end{equation}{/tex}
solution: As shown in fig. consider a wire of length L bent into a semicircular loop of radius {tex}\begin{equation} R\left(R=\frac{L}{\pi}\right) \end{equation}{/tex} . Let a current I flows through it. To calculate magnetic field at centre O of the loop, Let us consider a small current Idl. Then as per Biot-Savart.s law {tex}\begin{equation} d B=\frac{\mu_0}{4 \pi} \frac{I d l \sin \theta}{R^2}=\frac{\mu_0}{4 \pi} \frac{I d l \sin 90^{\circ}}{R^2}=\frac{\mu_0 I d l}{4 \pi R^2} \end{equation}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Total magnetic field at point O due to entire wire {tex}\begin{equation} B=\frac{\mu_0 I}{4 \pi R^2} \sum d l=\frac{\mu_0 I}{4 \pi R^2} \cdot \pi R=\frac{\mu_0 I}{4 R}=\frac{\mu_0 \pi I}{4 L} \end{equation}{/tex}
solution_html:
As shown in fig. consider a wire of length L bent into a semicircular loop of radius {tex}\begin{equation} R\left(R=\frac{L}{\pi}\right) \end{equation}{/tex}.
Let a current I flows through it. To calculate magnetic field at centre O of the loop, Let us consider a small current Idl. Then as per Biot-Savart.s law
{tex}\begin{equation} d B=\frac{\mu_0}{4 \pi} \frac{I d l \sin \theta}{R^2}=\frac{\mu_0}{4 \pi} \frac{I d l \sin 90^{\circ}}{R^2}=\frac{\mu_0 I d l}{4 \pi R^2} \end{equation}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Total magnetic field at point O due to entire wire
{tex}\begin{equation} B=\frac{\mu_0 I}{4 \pi R^2} \sum d l=\frac{\mu_0 I}{4 \pi R^2} \cdot \pi R=\frac{\mu_0 I}{4 R}=\frac{\mu_0 \pi I}{4 L} \end{equation}{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html: Figure shows a long straight wire of a circular cross-section (radius a) carrying steady current I. The current I is uniformly distributed across this cross-section. Calculate the magnetic field in the region r < a and r > a.
question_text: Figure shows a long straight wire of a circular cross-section (radius a) carrying steady current I. The current I is uniformly distributed across this cross-section. Calculate the magnetic field in the region r < a and r > a.
explanation: Figure shows a long straight wire of a circular cross-section (radius a) carrying steady current I. The current I is uniformly distributed across this cross-section. Consider the case r > a. The Amperian loop, labelled 2, is a circle concentric with the cross-section. For this loop, L = 2 {tex}\pi{/tex} r I e = Current enclosed by the loop = I The result is the familiar expression for a long straight wire B (2 {tex}\pi{/tex} r) = {tex}\mu {/tex} 0 I {tex}B=\frac{\mu_{0} I}{2 \pi r}{/tex} {tex}B \propto \frac{1}{r} \quad(r>a){/tex} Consider the case r < a. The Amperian loop is a circle labelled 1. For this loop, taking the radius of the circle to be r, L = 2 {tex}\pi{/tex} r Now the current enclosed Ie is not I, but is less than this value. Since the current distribution is uniform, the current enclosed is, {tex}I_{e}=I\left(\frac{\pi r^{2}}{\pi a^{2}}\right)=\frac{I r^{2}}{a^{2}}{/tex} Using Ampere’s law, {tex}B(2 \pi r)=\mu_{0} \frac{I r^{2}}{a^{2}}{/tex} {tex}B=\left(\frac{\mu_{0} I}{2 a^{2}}\right) r{/tex} {tex}B \propto r \quad(r<a){/tex} The figure shows a plot of the magnitude of B with distance r from the centre of the wire. The direction of the field is tangential to the respective circular loop (1 or 2) and given by the right-hand rule described earlier in this section. This example possesses the required symmetry so that Ampere’s law can be applied readily.
solution: Figure shows a long straight wire of a circular cross-section (radius a) carrying steady current I. The current I is uniformly distributed across this cross-section. Consider the case r > a. The Amperian loop, labelled 2, is a circle concentric with the cross-section. For this loop, L = 2 {tex}\pi{/tex} r I e = Current enclosed by the loop = I The result is the familiar expression for a long straight wire B (2 {tex}\pi{/tex} r) = {tex}\mu {/tex} 0 I {tex}B=\frac{\mu_{0} I}{2 \pi r}{/tex} {tex}B \propto \frac{1}{r} \quad(r>a){/tex} Consider the case r < a. The Amperian loop is a circle labelled 1. For this loop, taking the radius of the circle to be r, L = 2 {tex}\pi{/tex} r Now the current enclosed Ie is not I, but is less than this value. Since the current distribution is uniform, the current enclosed is, {tex}I_{e}=I\left(\frac{\pi r^{2}}{\pi a^{2}}\right)=\frac{I r^{2}}{a^{2}}{/tex} Using Ampere’s law, {tex}B(2 \pi r)=\mu_{0} \frac{I r^{2}}{a^{2}}{/tex} {tex}B=\left(\frac{\mu_{0} I}{2 a^{2}}\right) r{/tex} {tex}B \propto r \quad(r<a){/tex} The figure shows a plot of the magnitude of B with distance r from the centre of the wire. The direction of the field is tangential to the respective circular loop (1 or 2) and given by the right-hand rule described earlier in this section. This example possesses the required symmetry so that Ampere’s law can be applied readily.
solution_html:
1. Figure shows a long straight wire of a circular cross-section (radius a) carrying steady current I. The current I is uniformly distributed across this cross-section. Consider the case r > a. The Amperian loop, labelled 2, is a circle concentric with the cross-section. For this loop,
    L = 2{tex}\pi{/tex}r
    Ie = Current enclosed by the loop = I
    The result is the familiar expression for a long straight wire
    B (2{tex}\pi{/tex} r) = {tex}\mu {/tex}0I
    {tex}B=\frac{\mu_{0} I}{2 \pi r}{/tex}
    {tex}B \propto \frac{1}{r} \quad(r>a){/tex}
  2. Consider the case r < a. The Amperian loop is a circle labelled 1. For this loop, taking the radius of the circle to be r, L = 2{tex}\pi{/tex}r Now the current enclosed Ie
    is not I, but is less than this value. Since the current distribution is uniform, the current enclosed is, {tex}I_{e}=I\left(\frac{\pi r^{2}}{\pi a^{2}}\right)=\frac{I r^{2}}{a^{2}}{/tex}
    Using Ampere’s law, {tex}B(2 \pi r)=\mu_{0} \frac{I r^{2}}{a^{2}}{/tex}
    {tex}B=\left(\frac{\mu_{0} I}{2 a^{2}}\right) r{/tex}
    {tex}B \propto r \quad(r<a){/tex}

    The figure shows a plot of the magnitude of B with distance r from the centre of the wire. The direction of the field is tangential to the respective circular loop (1 or 2) and given by the right-hand rule described earlier in this section. This example possesses the required symmetry so that Ampere’s law can be applied readily.

question_html:
1. Derive an expression for the force between two long parallel current carrying conductors.
  2. Use this expression to define SI unit of current.
  3. A long straight wire AB carries a current I. A proton P travels with a speed v, parallel to the wire, at a distance d from it in a direction opposite to the current as shown in Fig. What is the force experienced by the proton and what is its direction?
question_text: Derive an expression for the force between two long parallel current carrying conductors. Use this expression to define SI unit of current. A long straight wire AB carries a current I. A proton P travels with a speed v, parallel to the wire, at a distance d from it in a direction opposite to the current as shown in Fig. What is the force experienced by the proton and what is its direction?
explanation: Let the conductors PQ and RS carry currents I 1 I 2 and in same direction and placed at separation r.(fig.). Consider a current–element ‘ab’ of length {tex}\Delta{/tex} L of wire RS. The magnetic field produced by current-carrying conductor PQ at the location of other wire RS {tex}B_1=\frac{\mu_0 I_1}{2 \pi r}{/tex} ...(i) According to Maxwell’s right hand rule the direction of B 1 will be perpendicular to the plane of paper and directed downward. Due to this magnetic field, each element of other wire experiences a force. The direction of current element is perpendicular to the magnetic field; therefore the magnetic force on element ab of length {tex}\Delta{/tex} L {tex}\Delta{/tex} F = B 1 I 2 {tex}\Delta{/tex} L sin 90 o = {tex}\frac{\mu_0 I_1}{2 \pi r} I_2{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex} L {tex}\therefore{/tex} The total force on conductor of length L will be F = {tex}\frac{\mu_0 I_1 I_2}{2 \pi r} \Sigma{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex} L = {tex}\frac{\mu_0 I_1 I_2}{2 \pi r}{/tex} L {tex}\therefore{/tex} Force acting on per unit length of conductor f = {tex}\frac{F}{L}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mu_0 I_1 I_2}{2 \pi r}{/tex} N/m ...(ii) Definition S.I. unit of Current (Ampere): Thus 1 ampere is the current which when flowing in each of parallel conductors placed at separation 1 m in vacuum exert a force of 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -7 on 1m length of either wire. Magnetic field due to current carrying wire is perpendicular to plane of paper - downward. i.e., {tex}\vec{B}{/tex} = {tex}-\frac{\mu_0 I}{2 \pi d} \hat{k}{/tex} Force {tex}\vec{F}{/tex} = q {tex}\vec{v} \times \vec{B}{/tex} = e(-v {tex}\hat{j}{/tex} ) {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\left(-\frac{\mu_0 I}{2 \pi d} \hat{k}\right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mu_0 e v I}{2 \pi d}{/tex} {tex}\hat{i}{/tex} That is the magnetic force has magnitude {tex}\frac{(\mu _0evI)}{2\pi d}{/tex} and is directed along positive x-axis i.e., in the plane of paper perpendicular to direction of vector v and to the right.
solution: Let the conductors PQ and RS carry currents I 1 I 2 and in same direction and placed at separation r.(fig.). Consider a current–element ‘ab’ of length {tex}\Delta{/tex} L of wire RS. The magnetic field produced by current-carrying conductor PQ at the location of other wire RS {tex}B_1=\frac{\mu_0 I_1}{2 \pi r}{/tex} ...(i) According to Maxwell’s right hand rule the direction of B 1 will be perpendicular to the plane of paper and directed downward. Due to this magnetic field, each element of other wire experiences a force. The direction of current element is perpendicular to the magnetic field; therefore the magnetic force on element ab of length {tex}\Delta{/tex} L {tex}\Delta{/tex} F = B 1 I 2 {tex}\Delta{/tex} L sin 90 o = {tex}\frac{\mu_0 I_1}{2 \pi r} I_2{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex} L {tex}\therefore{/tex} The total force on conductor of length L will be F = {tex}\frac{\mu_0 I_1 I_2}{2 \pi r} \Sigma{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex} L = {tex}\frac{\mu_0 I_1 I_2}{2 \pi r}{/tex} L {tex}\therefore{/tex} Force acting on per unit length of conductor f = {tex}\frac{F}{L}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mu_0 I_1 I_2}{2 \pi r}{/tex} N/m ...(ii) Definition S.I. unit of Current (Ampere): Thus 1 ampere is the current which when flowing in each of parallel conductors placed at separation 1 m in vacuum exert a force of 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -7 on 1m length of either wire. Magnetic field due to current carrying wire is perpendicular to plane of paper - downward. i.e., {tex}\vec{B}{/tex} = {tex}-\frac{\mu_0 I}{2 \pi d} \hat{k}{/tex} Force {tex}\vec{F}{/tex} = q {tex}\vec{v} \times \vec{B}{/tex} = e(-v {tex}\hat{j}{/tex} ) {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\left(-\frac{\mu_0 I}{2 \pi d} \hat{k}\right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mu_0 e v I}{2 \pi d}{/tex} {tex}\hat{i}{/tex} That is the magnetic force has magnitude {tex}\frac{(\mu _0evI)}{2\pi d}{/tex} and is directed along positive x-axis i.e., in the plane of paper perpendicular to direction of vector v and to the right.
solution_html:
1. Let the conductors PQ and RS carry currents I1 I2 and in same direction and placed at separation r.(fig.). Consider a current–element ‘ab’ of length {tex}\Delta{/tex}L of wire RS. The magnetic field produced by current-carrying conductor PQ at the location of other wire RS {tex}B_1=\frac{\mu_0 I_1}{2 \pi r}{/tex} ...(i)
    According to Maxwell’s right hand rule the direction of B1 will be perpendicular to the plane of paper and directed downward. Due to this magnetic field, each element of other wire experiences a force. The direction of current element is perpendicular to the magnetic field; therefore the magnetic force on element ab of length {tex}\Delta{/tex}L

    {tex}\Delta{/tex}F = B1I2 {tex}\Delta{/tex}L sin 90o = {tex}\frac{\mu_0 I_1}{2 \pi r} I_2{/tex}{tex}\Delta{/tex}L
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} The total force on conductor of length L will be
    F = {tex}\frac{\mu_0 I_1 I_2}{2 \pi r} \Sigma{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex}L = {tex}\frac{\mu_0 I_1 I_2}{2 \pi r}{/tex}L
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} Force acting on per unit length of conductor
    f = {tex}\frac{F}{L}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mu_0 I_1 I_2}{2 \pi r}{/tex} N/m ...(ii)
  2. Definition S.I. unit of Current (Ampere): Thus 1 ampere is the current which when flowing in each of parallel conductors placed at separation 1 m in vacuum exert a force of 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-7 on 1m length of either wire.
  3. Magnetic field due to current carrying wire is perpendicular to plane of paper - downward. i.e., {tex}\vec{B}{/tex} = {tex}-\frac{\mu_0 I}{2 \pi d} \hat{k}{/tex}
    Force {tex}\vec{F}{/tex}= q {tex}\vec{v} \times \vec{B}{/tex}= e(-v{tex}\hat{j}{/tex}) {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\left(-\frac{\mu_0 I}{2 \pi d} \hat{k}\right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mu_0 e v I}{2 \pi d}{/tex}{tex}\hat{i}{/tex}
    That is the magnetic force has magnitude {tex}\frac{(\mu _0evI)}{2\pi d}{/tex} and is directed along positive x-axis i.e., in the plane of paper perpendicular to direction of vector v and to the right.

subject: Physics
question_html: A beam of {tex}\alpha{/tex}-particles projected along +X-axis experiences a force due to a magnetic field along the + Y-axis. What is the direction of the magnetic field?
question_text: A beam of {tex}\alpha{/tex} -particles projected along +X-axis experiences a force due to a magnetic field along the + Y-axis. What is the direction of the magnetic field?
explanation: Velocity of {tex}\alpha - {/tex} particles along +x axis, {tex}\mathbf { v } = v \hat { \mathbf { i } }{/tex} Force experienced is along +y axis, {tex}F=F\widehat j{/tex} Magnetic Lorentz force on {tex}\alpha {/tex} -particles {tex}\begin{array}{l}\overrightarrow F=q\;(\overrightarrow v\times\overrightarrow B)\\Therefore,\\F\widehat j=q\;(v\widehat i\times\overrightarrow B)\end{array}{/tex} Thus, B vector must be in {tex}-\widehat k{/tex} direction for F to be in {tex}\widehat j{/tex} direction. Hence the direction of the magnetic field must be along negative Z-axis.
solution: Velocity of {tex}\alpha - {/tex} particles along +x axis, {tex}\mathbf { v } = v \hat { \mathbf { i } }{/tex} Force experienced is along +y axis, {tex}F=F\widehat j{/tex} Magnetic Lorentz force on {tex}\alpha {/tex} -particles {tex}\begin{array}{l}\overrightarrow F=q\;(\overrightarrow v\times\overrightarrow B)\\Therefore,\\F\widehat j=q\;(v\widehat i\times\overrightarrow B)\end{array}{/tex} Thus, B vector must be in {tex}-\widehat k{/tex} direction for F to be in {tex}\widehat j{/tex} direction. Hence the direction of the magnetic field must be along negative Z-axis.
solution_html:
Velocity of {tex}\alpha - {/tex}particles along +x axis, 
{tex}\mathbf { v } = v \hat { \mathbf { i } }{/tex}
Force experienced is along +y axis, {tex}F=F\widehat j{/tex}
Magnetic Lorentz force on {tex}\alpha {/tex}-particles
{tex}\begin{array}{l}\overrightarrow F=q\;(\overrightarrow v\times\overrightarrow B)\\Therefore,\\F\widehat j=q\;(v\widehat i\times\overrightarrow B)\end{array}{/tex}
Thus, B vector must be in {tex}-\widehat k{/tex} direction for F to be in {tex}\widehat j{/tex} direction. Hence the direction of the magnetic field must be along negative Z-axis.

subject: Physics
question_html: State the underlying principle of working of a moving coil galvanometer. Write two reasons why a galvanometer cannot be used as such to measure the current in a given circuit. Name any two factors on which the current sensitivity of a galvanometer depends.
question_text: State the underlying principle of working of a moving coil galvanometer. Write two reasons why a galvanometer cannot be used as such to measure the current in a given circuit. Name any two factors on which the current sensitivity of a galvanometer depends.
explanation: Principle : The current carrying coil placed in normal magnetic field experiences a torque when current passes through it, which is given by {tex}\tau = N I (\vec A × \vec B){/tex} where, N =number of turns of the coil, I = current in the coil, A = area of coil and B = magnetic field applied The galvanometer cannot be used to measure the current because all the currents to be measured has to be passed through coil which would get damaged due to heating effect of electric current or its coil has considerable resistance because of length and it may affect original current. Current sensitivity is defined as the angular deflection of a moving coil galvanometer when unit current pass through it and expressed as {tex}\frac {\theta}{I} = \frac {NAB}{K}{/tex} . It can be increased by increasing the magnetic field intensity, B and decreasing the value of torsional constant, K.
solution: Principle : The current carrying coil placed in normal magnetic field experiences a torque when current passes through it, which is given by {tex}\tau = N I (\vec A × \vec B){/tex} where, N =number of turns of the coil, I = current in the coil, A = area of coil and B = magnetic field applied The galvanometer cannot be used to measure the current because all the currents to be measured has to be passed through coil which would get damaged due to heating effect of electric current or its coil has considerable resistance because of length and it may affect original current. Current sensitivity is defined as the angular deflection of a moving coil galvanometer when unit current pass through it and expressed as {tex}\frac {\theta}{I} = \frac {NAB}{K}{/tex} . It can be increased by increasing the magnetic field intensity, B and decreasing the value of torsional constant, K.
solution_html:
Principle : The current carrying coil placed in normal magnetic field experiences a torque when current passes through it, which is given by
{tex}\tau = N I (\vec A × \vec B){/tex}
where, N =number of turns of the coil, I = current in the coil, A = area of coil and B = magnetic field applied
The galvanometer cannot be used to measure the current because

  1. all the currents to be measured has to be passed through coil which would get damaged due to heating effect of electric current or
  2. its coil has considerable resistance because of length and it may affect original current.

Current sensitivity is defined as the angular deflection of a moving coil galvanometer when unit current pass through it and expressed as {tex}\frac {\theta}{I} = \frac {NAB}{K}{/tex}. It can be increased by

  1. increasing the magnetic field intensity, B and
  2. decreasing the value of torsional constant, K.

question_html: A square coil of side 10cm has 20 turns and carries a current of 12A. The coil is suspended vertically and normal to the plane of the coil, makes an angle {tex}\theta {/tex} with the direction of a uniform horizontal magnetic field of 0.80 T. If the torque experienced by the coil equals 0.96 Nm, find the value of {tex}\theta {/tex}.
question_text: A square coil of side 10cm has 20 turns and carries a current of 12A. The coil is suspended vertically and normal to the plane of the coil, makes an angle {tex}\theta {/tex} with the direction of a uniform horizontal magnetic field of 0.80 T. If the torque experienced by the coil equals 0.96 Nm, find the value of {tex}\theta {/tex} .
explanation: Here, Area (A) of coil {tex}= 10 \times 10 = 100 \mathrm { cm } ^ { 2 } = 10 ^ { - 2 } \mathrm { m } ^ { 2 }{/tex} Number of turns, N = 20 turns Coil Current, I = 12 A Magnetic Field Strength, B = 0.8 T Torque, {tex}\tau\;=\;0.96\;Nm{/tex} Torque {tex}(\tau ){/tex} experienced by current carrying coil in the magnetic field is given by, {tex}\tau = N I A B \sin \theta{/tex} {tex}0.96 = 20 \times 12 \times 10 ^ { - 2 } \times 0.8 \times \sin \theta{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \sin \theta = \frac { 0.96 } { 1.92 } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \Rightarrow \theta = \frac { \pi } { 6 } \mathrm { rad }{/tex} Thus, plane of the coil makes an angle of {tex}\theta\;=\;30^o{/tex} with the direction of the uniform magnetic field.
solution: Here, Area (A) of coil {tex}= 10 \times 10 = 100 \mathrm { cm } ^ { 2 } = 10 ^ { - 2 } \mathrm { m } ^ { 2 }{/tex} Number of turns, N = 20 turns Coil Current, I = 12 A Magnetic Field Strength, B = 0.8 T Torque, {tex}\tau\;=\;0.96\;Nm{/tex} Torque {tex}(\tau ){/tex} experienced by current carrying coil in the magnetic field is given by, {tex}\tau = N I A B \sin \theta{/tex} {tex}0.96 = 20 \times 12 \times 10 ^ { - 2 } \times 0.8 \times \sin \theta{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \sin \theta = \frac { 0.96 } { 1.92 } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \Rightarrow \theta = \frac { \pi } { 6 } \mathrm { rad }{/tex} Thus, plane of the coil makes an angle of {tex}\theta\;=\;30^o{/tex} with the direction of the uniform magnetic field.
solution_html:
Here, Area (A) of coil {tex}= 10 \times 10 = 100 \mathrm { cm } ^ { 2 } = 10 ^ { - 2 } \mathrm { m } ^ { 2 }{/tex}
Number of turns, N = 20 turns
Coil Current, I = 12 A
Magnetic Field Strength, B = 0.8 T
Torque, {tex}\tau\;=\;0.96\;Nm{/tex}
Torque {tex}(\tau ){/tex} experienced by current carrying coil in the magnetic field is given by,
{tex}\tau = N I A B \sin \theta{/tex}
{tex}0.96 = 20 \times 12 \times 10 ^ { - 2 } \times 0.8 \times \sin \theta{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \sin \theta = \frac { 0.96 } { 1.92 } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \Rightarrow \theta = \frac { \pi } { 6 } \mathrm { rad }{/tex}
Thus, plane of the coil makes an angle of {tex}\theta\;=\;30^o{/tex} with the direction of the uniform magnetic field.

question_html: State Ampere's circuital law. Show through an example, how this law enables an easy evaluation of the magnetic field when there is a symmetry in the system.
question_text: State Ampere's circuital law. Show through an example, how this law enables an easy evaluation of the magnetic field when there is a symmetry in the system.
explanation: Ampere's circuital law: It states that the line integral of magnetic field B along any closed loop is equal to µ 0 times the total current passing through the loop. i.e. {tex}\oint \mathbf { B } \cdot d \mathbf { l } = \boldsymbol { \mu } _ { 0 } I{/tex} When there is a symmetry in the system such as for an infinitely long conductor wire carrying a steady current I, the magnetic field can be calculated easily using Ampere Circuital Law. Consider the Figure. Here, B (magnetic field) is tangential to circumference of the circular loop. The magnetic field at point P at a distance R from the centre of the circular loop is calculated as under. {tex}\begin{array}{l}\oint B.\;dl\;=\mu_o\;I\\B.\;2\mathrm{ {tex}\pi{/tex} R}\;=\;{\mathrm\mu}_{\mathrm o}\;\mathrm I\end{array}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad B = \mu _ { 0 } I / 2 \pi R{/tex} [From Ampere's Circuital law] The direction of magnetic field will be determined by Right Hand Thumb Rule.
solution: Ampere's circuital law: It states that the line integral of magnetic field B along any closed loop is equal to µ 0 times the total current passing through the loop. i.e. {tex}\oint \mathbf { B } \cdot d \mathbf { l } = \boldsymbol { \mu } _ { 0 } I{/tex} When there is a symmetry in the system such as for an infinitely long conductor wire carrying a steady current I, the magnetic field can be calculated easily using Ampere Circuital Law. Consider the Figure. Here, B (magnetic field) is tangential to circumference of the circular loop. The magnetic field at point P at a distance R from the centre of the circular loop is calculated as under. {tex}\begin{array}{l}\oint B.\;dl\;=\mu_o\;I\\B.\;2\mathrm{ {tex}\pi{/tex} R}\;=\;{\mathrm\mu}_{\mathrm o}\;\mathrm I\end{array}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad B = \mu _ { 0 } I / 2 \pi R{/tex} [From Ampere's Circuital law] The direction of magnetic field will be determined by Right Hand Thumb Rule.
solution_html:
Ampere's circuital law: It states that the line integral of magnetic field B along any closed loop is equal to µ0 times the total current passing through the loop.
i.e. {tex}\oint \mathbf { B } \cdot d \mathbf { l } = \boldsymbol { \mu } _ { 0 } I{/tex}


When there is a symmetry in the system such as for an infinitely long conductor wire carrying a steady current I, the magnetic field can be calculated easily using Ampere Circuital Law. Consider the Figure.

Here, B (magnetic field) is tangential to circumference of the circular loop. The magnetic field at point P at a distance R from the centre of the circular loop is calculated as under. 

 {tex}\begin{array}{l}\oint B.\;dl\;=\mu_o\;I\\B.\;2\mathrm{{tex}\pi{/tex}R}\;=\;{\mathrm\mu}_{\mathrm o}\;\mathrm I\end{array}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad B = \mu _ { 0 } I / 2 \pi R{/tex} [From Ampere's Circuital law]

The direction of magnetic field will be determined by Right Hand Thumb Rule.

question_html: A deuteron and a proton moving with the same speed enter the same magnetic field region at right angles to the direction of the field. Show the trajectories followed by the two particles in the magnetic field. Find the ratio of the radii of the circular paths which the two particles may describe.
question_text: A deuteron and a proton moving with the same speed enter the same magnetic field region at right angles to the direction of the field. Show the trajectories followed by the two particles in the magnetic field. Find the ratio of the radii of the circular paths which the two particles may describe.
explanation: The trajectories of the two particles, Proton and Deuteron in the magnetic field are shown in the Figure. Now, as {tex}r=\frac{mv}{qB}\Rightarrow r\propto m{/tex} So, ratio of radii of deuteron path and proton path is {tex}\frac{r_d}{r_p}=\frac{m_d}{m_p}{/tex} {tex}\because \quad m _ { d } = 2 m _ { p }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad r _ { d } = 2 r _ { p }{/tex} or {tex}r _ { d } : r _ { p } = 2 : 1{/tex} Also note that smaller the radius, greater the curvature and vice-versa. This is why, proton's path has got greater curvature.
solution: The trajectories of the two particles, Proton and Deuteron in the magnetic field are shown in the Figure. Now, as {tex}r=\frac{mv}{qB}\Rightarrow r\propto m{/tex} So, ratio of radii of deuteron path and proton path is {tex}\frac{r_d}{r_p}=\frac{m_d}{m_p}{/tex} {tex}\because \quad m _ { d } = 2 m _ { p }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad r _ { d } = 2 r _ { p }{/tex} or {tex}r _ { d } : r _ { p } = 2 : 1{/tex} Also note that smaller the radius, greater the curvature and vice-versa. This is why, proton's path has got greater curvature.
solution_html:
The trajectories of the two particles, Proton and Deuteron in the magnetic field are shown in the Figure.

Now, as {tex}r=\frac{mv}{qB}\Rightarrow r\propto m{/tex}
So, ratio of radii of deuteron path and proton path is
{tex}\frac{r_d}{r_p}=\frac{m_d}{m_p}{/tex}
{tex}\because \quad m _ { d } = 2 m _ { p }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad r _ { d } = 2 r _ { p }{/tex}
or {tex}r _ { d } : r _ { p } = 2 : 1{/tex}
Also note that smaller the radius, greater the curvature and vice-versa. This is why, proton's path has got greater curvature.

question_html: Use the expression, {tex}F = q(v \times B){/tex} to define the SI unit of magnetic field.
question_text: Use the expression, {tex}F = q(v \times B){/tex} to define the SI unit of magnetic field.
explanation: Lorentz magnetic force, {tex}\ { F } = q ( \ { v } \times \ { B } ) \Rightarrow F = q v B \sin \theta{/tex} where, {tex}\theta {/tex} is the angle between v and B. {tex}\Rightarrow \quad B = F / q v \sin \theta{/tex} If q = 1 C, v = 1 ms -1 , {tex}\theta = 90 ^ { \circ }{/tex} Thus the magnetic field is, {tex}B\;=\;\frac{1N}{(1C)(1ms^{-1})\;\sin\;90^o}=1\;Ns/Cm\;=1T{/tex} So, SI unit of magnetic field is Tesla (T). Thus, the magnitude of magnetic field at a point is said to be one Tesla, if a charge of one Coulomb moving at a right angle to a magnetic field B with a velocity of 1ms -1 experiences a force of one Newton.
solution: Lorentz magnetic force, {tex}\ { F } = q ( \ { v } \times \ { B } ) \Rightarrow F = q v B \sin \theta{/tex} where, {tex}\theta {/tex} is the angle between v and B. {tex}\Rightarrow \quad B = F / q v \sin \theta{/tex} If q = 1 C, v = 1 ms -1 , {tex}\theta = 90 ^ { \circ }{/tex} Thus the magnetic field is, {tex}B\;=\;\frac{1N}{(1C)(1ms^{-1})\;\sin\;90^o}=1\;Ns/Cm\;=1T{/tex} So, SI unit of magnetic field is Tesla (T). Thus, the magnitude of magnetic field at a point is said to be one Tesla, if a charge of one Coulomb moving at a right angle to a magnetic field B with a velocity of 1ms -1 experiences a force of one Newton.
solution_html:
Lorentz magnetic force, {tex}\ { F } = q ( \ { v } \times \ { B } ) \Rightarrow F = q v B \sin \theta{/tex}
where, {tex}\theta {/tex} is the angle between v and B.
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad B = F / q v \sin \theta{/tex}
If q = 1 C, v = 1 ms-1, {tex}\theta = 90 ^ { \circ }{/tex}
Thus the magnetic field is,
{tex}B\;=\;\frac{1N}{(1C)(1ms^{-1})\;\sin\;90^o}=1\;Ns/Cm\;=1T{/tex}
So, SI unit of magnetic field is Tesla (T).
Thus, the magnitude of magnetic field at a point is said to be one Tesla, if a charge of one Coulomb moving at a right angle to a magnetic field B with a velocity of 1ms-1 experiences a force of one Newton.

question_html: A moving coil galvanometer of resistance G gives its full scale deflection when a current Ig flows through its coil. It can be converted into an ammeter of range (0 to 1), (I > Ig) when a shunt of resistance S is connected. Find the expression for the shunt required in terms of Ig and G.
question_text: A moving coil galvanometer of resistance G gives its full scale deflection when a current I g flows through its coil. It can be converted into an ammeter of range (0 to 1), (I > I g ) when a shunt of resistance S is connected. Find the expression for the shunt required in terms of I g and G.
explanation: A moving coil galvanometer of range I g and resistance G can be converted into an ammeter by connecting a very low shunt resistance (S) in parallel with the galvanometer. Consider the figure. Now, the potential difference across the combination is same. Therefore, PD across Galvanometer = PD across shunt resistance, S I g G = I s S where I s is the current in shunt resistance, S But I s + I g = I {tex}\Rightarrow \quad I _ { s } = I - I _ { g }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad I _ {s } S = \left( I - I _ { g } \right) S{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad I _ { g } G = \left( I - I _ { g } \right) S{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow\quad S=\frac{I_g}{I-I_g}G{/tex} This is the required expression.
solution: A moving coil galvanometer of range I g and resistance G can be converted into an ammeter by connecting a very low shunt resistance (S) in parallel with the galvanometer. Consider the figure. Now, the potential difference across the combination is same. Therefore, PD across Galvanometer = PD across shunt resistance, S I g G = I s S where I s is the current in shunt resistance, S But I s + I g = I {tex}\Rightarrow \quad I _ { s } = I - I _ { g }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad I _ {s } S = \left( I - I _ { g } \right) S{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad I _ { g } G = \left( I - I _ { g } \right) S{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow\quad S=\frac{I_g}{I-I_g}G{/tex} This is the required expression.
solution_html:
A moving coil galvanometer of range Ig and resistance G can be converted into an ammeter by connecting a very low shunt resistance (S) in parallel with the galvanometer. Consider the figure.

Now, the potential difference across the combination is same.
Therefore, PD across Galvanometer = PD across shunt resistance, S
Ig G = Is S
where Is is the current in shunt resistance, S
But Is + Ig = I
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad I _ { s } = I - I _ { g }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad I _ {s } S = \left( I - I _ { g } \right) S{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad I _ { g } G = \left( I - I _ { g } \right) S{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow\quad S=\frac{I_g}{I-I_g}G{/tex}
This is the required expression.

question_html:
Write the expression for the magnetic moment (m) due to a planar square loop of side '{tex}l{/tex}' carrying a steady current {tex}I{/tex} in a vector form. In the given figure, this loop is placed in a horizontal plane near a long straight conductor carrying a steady current I1 at a distance '{tex}l{/tex}' as shown.
Give reasons to explain that the loop will experience a net force but no torque. Write the expression for this force acting on the loop.
question_text: Write the expression for the magnetic moment (m) due to a planar square loop of side ' {tex}l{/tex} ' carrying a steady current {tex}I{/tex} in a vector form. In the given figure, this loop is placed in a horizontal plane near a long straight conductor carrying a steady current I 1 at a distance ' {tex}l{/tex} ' as shown. Give reasons to explain that the loop will experience a net force but no torque. Write the expression for this force acting on the loop.
explanation: The magnetic moment due to the planar square current-carrying loop of side l is, {tex}m=I\;A=I\;l^2{/tex} where A = area of the loop (square) {tex}\therefore \quad \mathbf { A } = l ^ { 2 } \hat { \mathbf { n } }{/tex} Here, {tex}\hat { \ { n } }{/tex} is a unit vector normal to the direction of area vector The forces acting on the arms QR and SP of the given loop are equal and opposite and collinear. Hence, they are balanced by one another and the net force on these sides is zero. Force on arm PQ, {tex}F_1=B_1\;I\;l=\frac{\mu_o\;I_1}{2\mathrm{ {tex}\pi{/tex} l}}I\;l=\frac{\mu_o\;I\;I_1}{2\mathrm\pi}{/tex} Since the direction of the current in the arm PQ and the wire is the same, so F 1 is of the attractive nature. Force on arm RS, {tex}F _ { 2 } = B _ { 2 } I l = \frac { \mu _ { 0 } I _ { 1 } I l } { 2 \pi ( 2 l ) } = \frac { \mu _ { 0 } I _ { 1 } I } { 4 \pi }{/tex} Since, the direction of the current in the arm RS and the wire is opposite, so F 2 is repulsive in nature. The net force on loop PQRS, {tex}\Rightarrow \quad F _ { \text { net } } = \ { F } _ { 1 } - \ { F } _ { 2 } = \frac { \mu _ { 0 } I _ { 1 } I } { 2 \pi } - \frac { \mu _ { 0 } I _ { 1 } I } { 4 \pi }{/tex} or {tex}F _ { \mathrm { net } } = \frac { \mu _ { 0 } I _ { 1 } I } { 4 \pi }{/tex} Torque is given by, {tex}\tau=m\times\;B{/tex} Net Torque is zero as angle between m and B is zero. Also as F 1 and F 2 are collinear, hence they do not produce torque on the loop PQRS.
solution: The magnetic moment due to the planar square current-carrying loop of side l is, {tex}m=I\;A=I\;l^2{/tex} where A = area of the loop (square) {tex}\therefore \quad \mathbf { A } = l ^ { 2 } \hat { \mathbf { n } }{/tex} Here, {tex}\hat { \ { n } }{/tex} is a unit vector normal to the direction of area vector The forces acting on the arms QR and SP of the given loop are equal and opposite and collinear. Hence, they are balanced by one another and the net force on these sides is zero. Force on arm PQ, {tex}F_1=B_1\;I\;l=\frac{\mu_o\;I_1}{2\mathrm{ {tex}\pi{/tex} l}}I\;l=\frac{\mu_o\;I\;I_1}{2\mathrm\pi}{/tex} Since the direction of the current in the arm PQ and the wire is the same, so F 1 is of the attractive nature. Force on arm RS, {tex}F _ { 2 } = B _ { 2 } I l = \frac { \mu _ { 0 } I _ { 1 } I l } { 2 \pi ( 2 l ) } = \frac { \mu _ { 0 } I _ { 1 } I } { 4 \pi }{/tex} Since, the direction of the current in the arm RS and the wire is opposite, so F 2 is repulsive in nature. The net force on loop PQRS, {tex}\Rightarrow \quad F _ { \text { net } } = \ { F } _ { 1 } - \ { F } _ { 2 } = \frac { \mu _ { 0 } I _ { 1 } I } { 2 \pi } - \frac { \mu _ { 0 } I _ { 1 } I } { 4 \pi }{/tex} or {tex}F _ { \mathrm { net } } = \frac { \mu _ { 0 } I _ { 1 } I } { 4 \pi }{/tex} Torque is given by, {tex}\tau=m\times\;B{/tex} Net Torque is zero as angle between m and B is zero. Also as F 1 and F 2 are collinear, hence they do not produce torque on the loop PQRS.
solution_html:
The magnetic moment due to the planar square current-carrying loop of side l is,
{tex}m=I\;A=I\;l^2{/tex}
where A = area of the loop (square)
{tex}\therefore \quad \mathbf { A } = l ^ { 2 } \hat { \mathbf { n } }{/tex}
Here, {tex}\hat { \ { n } }{/tex} is a unit vector normal to the direction of area vector
The forces acting on the arms QR and SP of the given loop are equal and opposite and collinear. Hence, they are balanced by one another and the net force on these sides is zero.

Force on arm PQ, {tex}F_1=B_1\;I\;l=\frac{\mu_o\;I_1}{2\mathrm{{tex}\pi{/tex}l}}I\;l=\frac{\mu_o\;I\;I_1}{2\mathrm\pi}{/tex}
Since the direction of the current in the arm PQ and the wire is the same, so F1 is of the attractive nature.
Force on arm RS,
{tex}F _ { 2 } = B _ { 2 } I l = \frac { \mu _ { 0 } I _ { 1 } I l } { 2 \pi ( 2 l ) } = \frac { \mu _ { 0 } I _ { 1 } I } { 4 \pi }{/tex}
Since, the direction of the current in the arm RS and the wire is opposite, so F2 is repulsive in nature.
The net force on loop PQRS,
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad F _ { \text { net } } = \ { F } _ { 1 } - \ { F } _ { 2 } = \frac { \mu _ { 0 } I _ { 1 } I } { 2 \pi } - \frac { \mu _ { 0 } I _ { 1 } I } { 4 \pi }{/tex}
or {tex}F _ { \mathrm { net } } = \frac { \mu _ { 0 } I _ { 1 } I } { 4 \pi }{/tex}
Torque is given by, {tex}\tau=m\times\;B{/tex}
Net Torque is zero as angle between m and B is zero. Also as F1 and F2 are collinear, hence they do not produce torque on the loop PQRS.

question_html:
1. State Ampere's circuital law. Use it to derive an expression for magnetic field inside along the axis of an air-cored solenoid.
  2. Sketch the magnetic field lines for a finite solenoid. How are these field lines different from the electric field lines from an electric dipole?
question_text: State Ampere's circuital law. Use it to derive an expression for magnetic field inside along the axis of an air-cored solenoid. Sketch the magnetic field lines for a finite solenoid. How are these field lines different from the electric field lines from an electric dipole?
explanation: Ampere circuital law: It states that the line integral of magnetic field induction along a closed path is equal to {tex}\mu_0{/tex} times the current enclosed by the path. Magnetic Field Due to a Current Carrying Long Solenoid: A solenoid is a long wire wound in the form of a close-packed helix, carrying current. When an electric current is passed through the solenoid, a magnetic field is produced within the solenoid. If the solenoid is long and the successive insulated copper turns have no gaps, then the magnetic field within the solenoid is uniform with practically no magnetic field outside it. Take a closed path 'pqrs' in the solenoid as shown in the figure: Using Ampere's circuital law we have {tex}\oint \vec{B} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}=\mu_{0} I{/tex} {tex}f \vec{B} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}=\int_{p q} \vec{B} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}+\int_{qr} \vec{B} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}+\int_{r s} \vec{B} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}+\int_{s p} \vec{B} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}{/tex} {tex}\oint \vec{B} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}=\int_{p q} B d l \cos 0+\int_{q r} B d l \cos 90+\int_{s p} B d l \cos 90{/tex} {tex}\oint \vec{B} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}=\int_{p q} B d l=B l{/tex} Also by Ampere circuital law we have, {tex}\oint\overrightarrow B.\overrightarrow{dl\;}=\mu_0NI{/tex} From above equation, {tex}B l=\mu_{0} N I{/tex} {tex}B=\mu_{0} \frac{N}{l} I{/tex} If there are ‘n’ number of turns per metre length of the solenoid and I amperes is the current flowing. Let n = {tex}\frac{N}{l} {/tex} where N is the total number turns of coil. So, {tex}B=\mu_0nI{/tex} Magnetic field lines due to a finite length solenoid are as shown in the figure. Electric field lines due to dipole start from positive charge and end at negative charge while magnetic field lines inside the solenoid are uniform inside the solenoid and are from south pole towards the negative pole.
solution: Ampere circuital law: It states that the line integral of magnetic field induction along a closed path is equal to {tex}\mu_0{/tex} times the current enclosed by the path. Magnetic Field Due to a Current Carrying Long Solenoid: A solenoid is a long wire wound in the form of a close-packed helix, carrying current. When an electric current is passed through the solenoid, a magnetic field is produced within the solenoid. If the solenoid is long and the successive insulated copper turns have no gaps, then the magnetic field within the solenoid is uniform with practically no magnetic field outside it. Take a closed path 'pqrs' in the solenoid as shown in the figure: Using Ampere's circuital law we have {tex}\oint \vec{B} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}=\mu_{0} I{/tex} {tex}f \vec{B} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}=\int_{p q} \vec{B} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}+\int_{qr} \vec{B} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}+\int_{r s} \vec{B} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}+\int_{s p} \vec{B} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}{/tex} {tex}\oint \vec{B} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}=\int_{p q} B d l \cos 0+\int_{q r} B d l \cos 90+\int_{s p} B d l \cos 90{/tex} {tex}\oint \vec{B} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}=\int_{p q} B d l=B l{/tex} Also by Ampere circuital law we have, {tex}\oint\overrightarrow B.\overrightarrow{dl\;}=\mu_0NI{/tex} From above equation, {tex}B l=\mu_{0} N I{/tex} {tex}B=\mu_{0} \frac{N}{l} I{/tex} If there are ‘n’ number of turns per metre length of the solenoid and I amperes is the current flowing. Let n = {tex}\frac{N}{l} {/tex} where N is the total number turns of coil. So, {tex}B=\mu_0nI{/tex} Magnetic field lines due to a finite length solenoid are as shown in the figure. Electric field lines due to dipole start from positive charge and end at negative charge while magnetic field lines inside the solenoid are uniform inside the solenoid and are from south pole towards the negative pole.
solution_html:
1. Ampere circuital law: It states that the line integral of magnetic field induction along a closed path is equal to {tex}\mu_0{/tex} times the current enclosed by the path.
    Magnetic Field Due to a Current Carrying Long Solenoid: A solenoid is a long wire wound in the form of a close-packed helix, carrying current. When an electric current is passed through the solenoid, a magnetic field is produced within the solenoid. If the solenoid is long and the successive insulated copper turns have no gaps, then the magnetic field within the solenoid is uniform with practically no magnetic field outside it.
    Take a closed path 'pqrs' in the solenoid as shown in the figure:

    Using Ampere's circuital law we have
    {tex}\oint \vec{B} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}=\mu_{0} I{/tex}
    {tex}f \vec{B} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}=\int_{p q} \vec{B} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}+\int_{qr} \vec{B} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}+\int_{r s} \vec{B} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}+\int_{s p} \vec{B} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}{/tex}
    {tex}\oint \vec{B} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}=\int_{p q} B d l \cos 0+\int_{q r} B d l \cos 90+\int_{s p} B d l \cos 90{/tex}
    {tex}\oint \vec{B} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}=\int_{p q} B d l=B l{/tex}
    Also by Ampere circuital law we have,
    {tex}\oint\overrightarrow B.\overrightarrow{dl\;}=\mu_0NI{/tex}
    From above equation,
    {tex}B l=\mu_{0} N I{/tex}
    {tex}B=\mu_{0} \frac{N}{l} I{/tex}
    If there are ‘n’ number of turns per metre length of the solenoid and I amperes is the current flowing.
    Let n = {tex}\frac{N}{l} {/tex} where N is the total number turns of coil.
    So, {tex}B=\mu_0nI{/tex}
  2. Magnetic field lines due to a finite length solenoid are as shown in the figure.

    Electric field lines due to dipole start from positive charge and end at negative charge while magnetic field lines inside the solenoid are uniform inside the solenoid and are from south pole towards the negative pole.

question_html: Out of an ammeter and a voltmeter, which of the two has higher resistance and why?
question_text: Out of an ammeter and a voltmeter, which of the two has higher resistance and why?
explanation: In an ammeter, a small resistance S is connected in parallel to the coil of the galvanometer. Therefore, resistance of the ammeter, R A = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{GS}}{\mathrm{G}+\mathrm{S}}{/tex} Since is very small as compared to G, R A {tex}\approx{/tex} S (very small) On the other hand, in a voltmeter, a suitable high resistance R in series with its coil. Therefore, resistance of the voltmeter, R V = G + R {tex}\approx{/tex} R (very large) Hence, a voltmeter has higher resistance than an ammeter.
solution: In an ammeter, a small resistance S is connected in parallel to the coil of the galvanometer. Therefore, resistance of the ammeter, R A = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{GS}}{\mathrm{G}+\mathrm{S}}{/tex} Since is very small as compared to G, R A {tex}\approx{/tex} S (very small) On the other hand, in a voltmeter, a suitable high resistance R in series with its coil. Therefore, resistance of the voltmeter, R V = G + R {tex}\approx{/tex} R (very large) Hence, a voltmeter has higher resistance than an ammeter.
solution_html:
In an ammeter, a small resistance S is connected in parallel to the coil of the galvanometer.
Therefore, resistance of the ammeter,
RA = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{GS}}{\mathrm{G}+\mathrm{S}}{/tex}
Since is very small as compared to G,
RA {tex}\approx{/tex} S (very small)
On the other hand, in a voltmeter, a suitable high resistance R in series with its coil.
Therefore, resistance of the voltmeter,
RV = G + R {tex}\approx{/tex} R (very large)
Hence, a voltmeter has higher resistance than an ammeter.

question_html: A charged particle moving in a uniform magnetic field penetrates a layer of lead and thereby loses one-half of its kinetic energy. How does the radius of curvature of its path change?
question_text: A charged particle moving in a uniform magnetic field penetrates a layer of lead and thereby loses one-half of its kinetic energy. How does the radius of curvature of its path change?
explanation: The radius of curvature, {tex}r=\frac{m v}{q B}=\frac{m}{q B} \sqrt{\frac{2 K}{m}}=\frac{\sqrt{2 m K}}{q B}{/tex} i.e., {tex}r \propto \sqrt{K}{/tex}
solution: The radius of curvature, {tex}r=\frac{m v}{q B}=\frac{m}{q B} \sqrt{\frac{2 K}{m}}=\frac{\sqrt{2 m K}}{q B}{/tex} i.e., {tex}r \propto \sqrt{K}{/tex}
solution_html: The radius of curvature, {tex}r=\frac{m v}{q B}=\frac{m}{q B} \sqrt{\frac{2 K}{m}}=\frac{\sqrt{2 m K}}{q B}{/tex} i.e., {tex}r \propto \sqrt{K}{/tex}

question_html:
1. State Ampere's circuital law. Show through an example, how this law enables an easy evaluation of this magnetic field when there is a symmetry in the system?
  2. What does a toroid consist of? Show that for an ideal toroid of closely wound turns, the magnetic field
    1. inside the toroid is constant.
    2. in the open space inside, and exterior to the toroid is zero.
question_text: State Ampere's circuital law. Show through an example, how this law enables an easy evaluation of this magnetic field when there is a symmetry in the system? What does a toroid consist of? Show that for an ideal toroid of closely wound turns, the magnetic field inside the toroid is constant. in the open space inside, and exterior to the toroid is zero.
explanation: Ampere's circuital law: Ampere’s law states that the line integral of magnetic field around closed path is equal to {tex}\mu_0{/tex} times the total current passing through the closed surface. {tex}\oint\overrightarrow B.\overrightarrow{dl\;}=\mu_0I{/tex} This law helps to evaluate the magnetic field due to a wire carrying current when the current is uniformly distributed across the cross section. Toroid is a hollow circular ring on which a large number of turns of a wire are closely wound. Figure shows a sectional view of the toroid. The direction of the magnetic field inside is clockwise as per the right-hand thumb rule for circular loops. Two circular Amperian loops 1 and 2 are shown by dashed lines. By symmetry, the magnetic field should be tangential to each of them and constant in magnitude for a given loop. Let the magnetic field inside the toroid be B . We shall now consider the magnetic field at S. Once again we employ Ampere’s law in the form of, {tex}\oint\overrightarrow B.\overrightarrow{dl}=\mu_0NI{/tex} {tex}B\oint dl=\mu_0NI{/tex} {tex}\begin{array}{l}BL=\mu_0NI\\\end{array} {/tex} ........ (1) Where, L is the length of the loop for which B is tangential, I be the current enclosed by the loop and N be the number of turns. We find, {tex}\begin{array}{l}L=2\mathrm{ {tex}\pi{/tex} r}\\\end{array}{/tex} ...... (2) From (1) and (2) we have {tex}\begin{array}{l}\mathrm B=\frac{{\mathrm\mu}_0\mathrm{NI}}{2\mathrm{ {tex}\pi{/tex} r}}={\mathrm\mu}_0\mathrm{nI}\\\end{array}{/tex} here n is number of turns per unit length of toroid. Open space inside the toroid encloses no current thus, I = 0. Hence, B = 0
solution: Ampere's circuital law: Ampere’s law states that the line integral of magnetic field around closed path is equal to {tex}\mu_0{/tex} times the total current passing through the closed surface. {tex}\oint\overrightarrow B.\overrightarrow{dl\;}=\mu_0I{/tex} This law helps to evaluate the magnetic field due to a wire carrying current when the current is uniformly distributed across the cross section. Toroid is a hollow circular ring on which a large number of turns of a wire are closely wound. Figure shows a sectional view of the toroid. The direction of the magnetic field inside is clockwise as per the right-hand thumb rule for circular loops. Two circular Amperian loops 1 and 2 are shown by dashed lines. By symmetry, the magnetic field should be tangential to each of them and constant in magnitude for a given loop. Let the magnetic field inside the toroid be B . We shall now consider the magnetic field at S. Once again we employ Ampere’s law in the form of, {tex}\oint\overrightarrow B.\overrightarrow{dl}=\mu_0NI{/tex} {tex}B\oint dl=\mu_0NI{/tex} {tex}\begin{array}{l}BL=\mu_0NI\\\end{array} {/tex} ........ (1) Where, L is the length of the loop for which B is tangential, I be the current enclosed by the loop and N be the number of turns. We find, {tex}\begin{array}{l}L=2\mathrm{ {tex}\pi{/tex} r}\\\end{array}{/tex} ...... (2) From (1) and (2) we have {tex}\begin{array}{l}\mathrm B=\frac{{\mathrm\mu}_0\mathrm{NI}}{2\mathrm{ {tex}\pi{/tex} r}}={\mathrm\mu}_0\mathrm{nI}\\\end{array}{/tex} here n is number of turns per unit length of toroid. Open space inside the toroid encloses no current thus, I = 0. Hence, B = 0
solution_html:
1. Ampere's circuital law: Ampere’s law states that the line integral of magnetic field around closed path is equal to {tex}\mu_0{/tex} times the total current passing through the closed surface.
    {tex}\oint\overrightarrow B.\overrightarrow{dl\;}=\mu_0I{/tex}
    This law helps to evaluate the magnetic field due to a wire carrying current when the current is uniformly distributed across the cross section.
  2. Toroid is a hollow circular ring on which a large number of turns of a wire are closely wound.
    Figure shows a sectional view of the toroid. The direction of the magnetic field inside is clockwise as per the right-hand thumb rule for circular loops. Two circular Amperian loops 1 and 2 are shown by dashed lines.

    By symmetry, the magnetic field should be tangential to each of them and constant in magnitude for a given loop.
    Let the magnetic field inside the toroid be B. We shall now consider the magnetic field at S. Once again we employ Ampere’s law in the form of,
    {tex}\oint\overrightarrow B.\overrightarrow{dl}=\mu_0NI{/tex}
    {tex}B\oint dl=\mu_0NI{/tex}
    {tex}\begin{array}{l}BL=\mu_0NI\\\end{array} {/tex} ........ (1)
    Where, L is the length of the loop for which B is tangential, I be the current enclosed by the loop and N be the number of turns.
    We find, {tex}\begin{array}{l}L=2\mathrm{{tex}\pi{/tex}r}\\\end{array}{/tex}...... (2)
    From (1) and (2) we have
    {tex}\begin{array}{l}\mathrm B=\frac{{\mathrm\mu}_0\mathrm{NI}}{2\mathrm{{tex}\pi{/tex}r}}={\mathrm\mu}_0\mathrm{nI}\\\end{array}{/tex}
    here n is number of turns per unit length of toroid.
    Open space inside the toroid encloses no current thus, I = 0.
    Hence, B = 0

question_html:
An electron and a proton moving with the same speed enter the same magnetic field region at right angles to the direction of the field.
Show the trajectory followed by the two particles in the magnetic field. Find the ratio of the radii of the circular paths which the particles may describe.
question_text: An electron and a proton moving with the same speed enter the same magnetic field region at right angles to the direction of the field. Show the trajectory followed by the two particles in the magnetic field. Find the ratio of the radii of the circular paths which the particles may describe.
explanation: When a charged particle enters in the magnetic field at right angle, then the particle follows a circular path. The trajectory of the two particles in the magnetic field is shown below. Radius of the circular path, {tex}r = \frac { m v } { q B }{/tex} For same speed v, magnitude of charge and magnetic field {tex}r \propto m \Rightarrow \frac { r _ { e } } { r _ { p } } = \frac { m _ { e } } { m _ { p } }{/tex} where, m e and m p are masses of electron and proton, respectively {tex}\because {/tex} {tex}m _ { e } < m _ { p }{/tex} (Proton is much heavier than electron) {tex}\Rightarrow \quad r _ { e } < r _ { p }{/tex} The curvature of path of the electron is much less than curvature of path of proton. {tex}\begin{array}{l}\frac{r_e}{r_p}=\frac{m_e}{m_p}=\frac{9.1\times10^{-31}}{1.66\times10^{-27}}=\;5.48\times10^{-4}\\\end{array}{/tex}
solution: When a charged particle enters in the magnetic field at right angle, then the particle follows a circular path. The trajectory of the two particles in the magnetic field is shown below. Radius of the circular path, {tex}r = \frac { m v } { q B }{/tex} For same speed v, magnitude of charge and magnetic field {tex}r \propto m \Rightarrow \frac { r _ { e } } { r _ { p } } = \frac { m _ { e } } { m _ { p } }{/tex} where, m e and m p are masses of electron and proton, respectively {tex}\because {/tex} {tex}m _ { e } < m _ { p }{/tex} (Proton is much heavier than electron) {tex}\Rightarrow \quad r _ { e } < r _ { p }{/tex} The curvature of path of the electron is much less than curvature of path of proton. {tex}\begin{array}{l}\frac{r_e}{r_p}=\frac{m_e}{m_p}=\frac{9.1\times10^{-31}}{1.66\times10^{-27}}=\;5.48\times10^{-4}\\\end{array}{/tex}
solution_html:
When a charged particle enters in the magnetic field at right angle, then the particle follows a circular path. The trajectory of the two particles in the magnetic field is shown below.

Radius of the circular path, {tex}r = \frac { m v } { q B }{/tex}
For same speed v, magnitude of charge and magnetic field 
{tex}r \propto m \Rightarrow \frac { r _ { e } } { r _ { p } } = \frac { m _ { e } } { m _ { p } }{/tex}
where, me and mp are masses of electron and proton, respectively
{tex}\because {/tex} {tex}m _ { e } < m _ { p }{/tex} (Proton is much heavier than electron)
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad r _ { e } < r _ { p }{/tex}
The curvature of path of the electron is much less than curvature of path of proton.
{tex}\begin{array}{l}\frac{r_e}{r_p}=\frac{m_e}{m_p}=\frac{9.1\times10^{-31}}{1.66\times10^{-27}}=\;5.48\times10^{-4}\\\end{array}{/tex}

question_text: A beam of electrons projected along + x-axis, experiences a force due to a magnetic field along the + y-axis. What is the direction of the magnetic field?
explanation: By Fleming's left hand rule, the magnetic field is directed along +z -axis.

question_html: A long straight wire of a circular cross-section of radius V carries a steady current ‘I'. The current is uniformly distributed across the cross-section. Apply Ampere's circuital law to calculate the magnetic field at a point ' r ' in the region for (i) r < a and (ii) r > a.
question_text: A long straight wire of a circular cross-section of radius V carries a steady current ‘I'. The current is uniformly distributed across the cross-section. Apply Ampere's circuital law to calculate the magnetic field at a point ' r ' in the region for (i) r < a and (ii) r > a.
explanation: As per Ampere's circuital law: {tex}\oint \vec{B} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}=\mu_0 I{/tex} For r < a Ie = current enclosed by Amperian circuit loop of radius r. {tex}=I \cdot \frac{\pi r^2}{\pi a^2}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{I r^2}{a^2}{/tex} {tex}\therefore B \oint \overrightarrow{d l}=\mu_0 \cdot I_e{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\mu_0 I r^2}{a^2}{/tex} Or {tex}B=\frac{\mu_0 I r^2}{a^2} \cdot\left(\frac{1}{2 \pi r}\right){/tex} {tex}=\left(\frac{\mu_0}{2 \pi} \frac{I}{a^2}\right){/tex} For r > a {tex}\oint \vec{B} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}=\mu_0 I{/tex} {tex}\therefore B .2 \pi r=\mu_0 I{/tex} Or {tex}B=\frac{\mu_0 I}{2 \pi r}{/tex}
solution: As per Ampere's circuital law: {tex}\oint \vec{B} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}=\mu_0 I{/tex} For r < a Ie = current enclosed by Amperian circuit loop of radius r. {tex}=I \cdot \frac{\pi r^2}{\pi a^2}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{I r^2}{a^2}{/tex} {tex}\therefore B \oint \overrightarrow{d l}=\mu_0 \cdot I_e{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\mu_0 I r^2}{a^2}{/tex} Or {tex}B=\frac{\mu_0 I r^2}{a^2} \cdot\left(\frac{1}{2 \pi r}\right){/tex} {tex}=\left(\frac{\mu_0}{2 \pi} \frac{I}{a^2}\right){/tex} For r > a {tex}\oint \vec{B} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}=\mu_0 I{/tex} {tex}\therefore B .2 \pi r=\mu_0 I{/tex} Or {tex}B=\frac{\mu_0 I}{2 \pi r}{/tex}
solution_html:
As per Ampere's circuital law:
{tex}\oint \vec{B} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}=\mu_0 I{/tex}

  1. For r < a
    Ie = current enclosed by Amperian circuit loop of radius r.
    {tex}=I \cdot \frac{\pi r^2}{\pi a^2}{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{I r^2}{a^2}{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore B \oint \overrightarrow{d l}=\mu_0 \cdot I_e{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{\mu_0 I r^2}{a^2}{/tex}
    Or {tex}B=\frac{\mu_0 I r^2}{a^2} \cdot\left(\frac{1}{2 \pi r}\right){/tex}
    {tex}=\left(\frac{\mu_0}{2 \pi} \frac{I}{a^2}\right){/tex}
  2. For r > a
    {tex}\oint \vec{B} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}=\mu_0 I{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore B .2 \pi r=\mu_0 I{/tex}
    Or {tex}B=\frac{\mu_0 I}{2 \pi r}{/tex}

question_html:
1. Using Biot-Savart's law, deduce an expression for the magnetic field on the axis of a circular current carrying loop.
  2. Draw the magnetic field lines due to a current carrying loop.
  3. A straight wire carrying a current of 12 A is bent into a semi-circular arc of radius 2.0 cm as shown in the figure. What is the magnetic field B at O due to
question_text: Using Biot-Savart's law, deduce an expression for the magnetic field on the axis of a circular current carrying loop. Draw the magnetic field lines due to a current carrying loop. A straight wire carrying a current of 12 A is bent into a semi-circular arc of radius 2.0 cm as shown in the figure. What is the magnetic field B at O due to
explanation: Consider two small current elements on the circular loop which are opposite to each other. then the magnetic field at a point p which is at a distance X from the centre of the coil is due to the x component of the fields of A and B and y components cancel out. dB X = {tex}\frac{\mu_0 I d l}{4 \pi} \times \frac{1}{\left(\sqrt{R^2+X^2}\right)^2} \times \frac{R}{\sqrt{R^2+X^2}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mu_0 I d l}{4 \pi} \frac{R}{\left(X^2+R^2\right)^{\frac{3}{2}}}{/tex} B = {tex}\int{/tex} dBX = {tex}\frac{\mu_0 I R}{4 \pi\left(x^2+R^2\right)^{\frac{3}{2}}} \int d l{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mu_0 I \times 2 \pi R^2}{4 \pi\left(x^2+R^2\right)^{\frac{3}{2}}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mu_0 I R^2}{2\left(x^2+R^2\right)^{\frac{3}{2}}}{/tex} The magnetic field induction at O due to semicircular arc is B = {tex}\frac {\mu_02\pi I}{4\pi r}{/tex} = {tex}\frac {\mu_0 l}{2r}{/tex} = {tex}\frac {4 \pi \times 10^{-7}\times 12}{2\times 2 \times 10^{-2 }}{/tex} = 1.9 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -4 T
solution: Consider two small current elements on the circular loop which are opposite to each other. then the magnetic field at a point p which is at a distance X from the centre of the coil is due to the x component of the fields of A and B and y components cancel out. dB X = {tex}\frac{\mu_0 I d l}{4 \pi} \times \frac{1}{\left(\sqrt{R^2+X^2}\right)^2} \times \frac{R}{\sqrt{R^2+X^2}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mu_0 I d l}{4 \pi} \frac{R}{\left(X^2+R^2\right)^{\frac{3}{2}}}{/tex} B = {tex}\int{/tex} dBX = {tex}\frac{\mu_0 I R}{4 \pi\left(x^2+R^2\right)^{\frac{3}{2}}} \int d l{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mu_0 I \times 2 \pi R^2}{4 \pi\left(x^2+R^2\right)^{\frac{3}{2}}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mu_0 I R^2}{2\left(x^2+R^2\right)^{\frac{3}{2}}}{/tex} The magnetic field induction at O due to semicircular arc is B = {tex}\frac {\mu_02\pi I}{4\pi r}{/tex} = {tex}\frac {\mu_0 l}{2r}{/tex} = {tex}\frac {4 \pi \times 10^{-7}\times 12}{2\times 2 \times 10^{-2 }}{/tex} = 1.9 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -4 T
solution_html:
1. Consider two small current elements on the circular loop which are opposite to each other.
    then the magnetic field at a point p which is at a distance X from the centre of the coil is due to the x component of the fields of A and B and y components cancel out.
    dBX = {tex}\frac{\mu_0 I d l}{4 \pi} \times \frac{1}{\left(\sqrt{R^2+X^2}\right)^2} \times \frac{R}{\sqrt{R^2+X^2}}{/tex}
    = {tex}\frac{\mu_0 I d l}{4 \pi} \frac{R}{\left(X^2+R^2\right)^{\frac{3}{2}}}{/tex}
    B = {tex}\int{/tex}dBX = {tex}\frac{\mu_0 I R}{4 \pi\left(x^2+R^2\right)^{\frac{3}{2}}} \int d l{/tex}
    = {tex}\frac{\mu_0 I \times 2 \pi R^2}{4 \pi\left(x^2+R^2\right)^{\frac{3}{2}}}{/tex}
    = {tex}\frac{\mu_0 I R^2}{2\left(x^2+R^2\right)^{\frac{3}{2}}}{/tex}
  3. The magnetic field induction at O due to semicircular arc is
    B = {tex}\frac {\mu_02\pi I}{4\pi r}{/tex} = {tex}\frac {\mu_0 l}{2r}{/tex} = {tex}\frac {4 \pi \times 10^{-7}\times 12}{2\times 2 \times 10^{-2 }}{/tex}
    = 1.9 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 T

question_html:
1. State Ampere's circuital law.
  2. Use it to derive an expression for magnetic field inside along the axis of an air cored solenoid.
  3. Sketch the magnetic field lines for a finite solenoid. How are these field lines different from the electric field lines from an electric dipole?
question_text: State Ampere's circuital law. Use it to derive an expression for magnetic field inside along the axis of an air cored solenoid. Sketch the magnetic field lines for a finite solenoid. How are these field lines different from the electric field lines from an electric dipole?
explanation: Ampere's circuital law states that the line integral of magnetic field induction along a closed path is equal to μ0 -times the current enclosed by the path i.e., {tex}\oint \vec{B} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}=\mu_0 I{/tex} Consider a closed path pqrs. The line integral of magnetic field vector B along path pqrs is For paths qr and sp, {tex}\vec{B}{/tex} and d {tex}\vec{l}{/tex} are mutually perpendicular. {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\int_{q r} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}{/tex} = {tex}\int_{s p}{/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}} \cdot d \vec{l}{/tex} = {tex}\int{/tex} Bdl cos 90 o = 0 For path rs, B = 0 (since field is zero outside a solenoid) {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\int_{r s}{/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}{/tex} = 0 In view of these, equation (i) gives {tex}\oint_{p q r s}{/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}=\int_{p q} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}=B l{/tex} ...(ii) By Ampere's law {tex}\oint \vec{B} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}=\mu_0{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} net current enclosed by path {tex}\therefore{/tex} Bl = {tex}\mu_0{/tex} (nl I) {tex}\therefore{/tex} B = {tex}\mu_0{/tex} nI Difference: Field lines of a solenoid form continuous current loops, while in the case of an electric dipole the field lines begin from a positive charge and end on a negative charge or escape to infinity.
solution: Ampere's circuital law states that the line integral of magnetic field induction along a closed path is equal to μ0 -times the current enclosed by the path i.e., {tex}\oint \vec{B} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}=\mu_0 I{/tex} Consider a closed path pqrs. The line integral of magnetic field vector B along path pqrs is For paths qr and sp, {tex}\vec{B}{/tex} and d {tex}\vec{l}{/tex} are mutually perpendicular. {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\int_{q r} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}{/tex} = {tex}\int_{s p}{/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}} \cdot d \vec{l}{/tex} = {tex}\int{/tex} Bdl cos 90 o = 0 For path rs, B = 0 (since field is zero outside a solenoid) {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\int_{r s}{/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}{/tex} = 0 In view of these, equation (i) gives {tex}\oint_{p q r s}{/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}=\int_{p q} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}=B l{/tex} ...(ii) By Ampere's law {tex}\oint \vec{B} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}=\mu_0{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} net current enclosed by path {tex}\therefore{/tex} Bl = {tex}\mu_0{/tex} (nl I) {tex}\therefore{/tex} B = {tex}\mu_0{/tex} nI Difference: Field lines of a solenoid form continuous current loops, while in the case of an electric dipole the field lines begin from a positive charge and end on a negative charge or escape to infinity.
solution_html:
1. Ampere's circuital law states that the line integral of magnetic field induction along a closed path is equal to μ0 -times the current enclosed by the path i.e., {tex}\oint \vec{B} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}=\mu_0 I{/tex}

  2. Consider a closed path pqrs. The line integral of magnetic field vector B along path pqrs is

    For paths qr and sp, {tex}\vec{B}{/tex} and d{tex}\vec{l}{/tex} are mutually perpendicular.
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\int_{q r} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}{/tex} = {tex}\int_{s p}{/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}} \cdot d \vec{l}{/tex} = {tex}\int{/tex} Bdl cos 90o = 0
    For path rs, B = 0 (since field is zero outside a solenoid)
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\int_{r s}{/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}{/tex} = 0
    In view of these, equation (i) gives
    {tex}\oint_{p q r s}{/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}=\int_{p q} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}=B l{/tex} ...(ii)
    By Ampere's law {tex}\oint \vec{B} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}=\mu_0{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} net current enclosed by path
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} Bl = {tex}\mu_0{/tex}(nl I) {tex}\therefore{/tex} B = {tex}\mu_0{/tex} nI



  3. Difference: Field lines of a solenoid form continuous current loops, while in the case of an electric dipole the field lines begin from a positive charge and end on a negative charge or escape to infinity.
