question_html: Deduce the expression for the torque experienced by a rectangular loop carrying a steady current I and placed in a uniform magnetic field B. Indicate the direction of the torque acting on the loop.
question_text: Deduce the expression for the torque experienced by a rectangular loop carrying a steady current I and placed in a uniform magnetic field B. Indicate the direction of the torque acting on the loop.
explanation: Let a current carrying rectangular loop PQRS is carrying a steady current I placed in a uniform magnetic field B keeping the axis of the coil perpendicular to the field as shown in figure. Let at any instant the area vector A makes an angle {tex}\theta {/tex} with the direction of magnetic field intensity B. Let length and breadth of the coil are I and b respectively. Now, magnetic force on PS arm of the coil is given by {tex}F _ { 1 } = BIl \sin 90 ^ { \circ }{/tex} [ {tex}\because {/tex} PS || axis of coil, {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\theta = 90 ^ { \circ }{/tex} ] F 1 = BIl ...... (i) By Fleming's left hand rule, the direction of the above force is perpendicular to SP and B is along upward direction. Similarly, force on QR arm of the coil F 2 = BIl sin 90° ..... (ii) The direction of force is perpendicular to QR and B is along downward direction {tex}\because{/tex} F 1 and F 2 are equal in magnitude and opposite in direction, parallel to each other acting on the loop forms a couple which try to rotate the coil. Now, force on RS part of the coil {tex}F _ { 3 } = I B b \sin \left( 90 ^ { \circ } + \theta \right) \Rightarrow F _ { 3 } = I B b \cos \theta{/tex} and also force on PQ part of the coil {tex}F _ { 4 } = I B b \sin \left( 90 ^ { \circ } - \theta \right) = I B b \cos \theta{/tex} But by Fleming's left hand rule, F 3 and F 4 are equal in magnitude and opposite in direction along the same line of action. Therefore, they balance each other. Now, torque due to F 1 and F 2 is given by {tex}\tau {/tex} = force {tex} \times {/tex} perpendicular distance between Iines of action of F 1 and F 2 . {tex}\tau = F \times b \sin \theta{/tex} But, {tex}F _ { 1 } = F _ { 2 } = F = I B l, \therefore \tau = ( I B l ) \times ( b \sin \theta ){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \tau = I B ( l b ) \sin \theta{/tex} {tex}\therefore \tau = I B A \sin \theta{/tex} where, A = lb = area of coil. For N turns of the coil, we have {tex}\tau = N I A B \sin \theta{/tex} , this is the required expression of the torque in the current carrying rectangular loop.
solution: Let a current carrying rectangular loop PQRS is carrying a steady current I placed in a uniform magnetic field B keeping the axis of the coil perpendicular to the field as shown in figure. Let at any instant the area vector A makes an angle {tex}\theta {/tex} with the direction of magnetic field intensity B. Let length and breadth of the coil are I and b respectively. Now, magnetic force on PS arm of the coil is given by {tex}F _ { 1 } = BIl \sin 90 ^ { \circ }{/tex} [ {tex}\because {/tex} PS || axis of coil, {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\theta = 90 ^ { \circ }{/tex} ] F 1 = BIl ...... (i) By Fleming's left hand rule, the direction of the above force is perpendicular to SP and B is along upward direction. Similarly, force on QR arm of the coil F 2 = BIl sin 90° ..... (ii) The direction of force is perpendicular to QR and B is along downward direction {tex}\because{/tex} F 1 and F 2 are equal in magnitude and opposite in direction, parallel to each other acting on the loop forms a couple which try to rotate the coil. Now, force on RS part of the coil {tex}F _ { 3 } = I B b \sin \left( 90 ^ { \circ } + \theta \right) \Rightarrow F _ { 3 } = I B b \cos \theta{/tex} and also force on PQ part of the coil {tex}F _ { 4 } = I B b \sin \left( 90 ^ { \circ } - \theta \right) = I B b \cos \theta{/tex} But by Fleming's left hand rule, F 3 and F 4 are equal in magnitude and opposite in direction along the same line of action. Therefore, they balance each other. Now, torque due to F 1 and F 2 is given by {tex}\tau {/tex} = force {tex} \times {/tex} perpendicular distance between Iines of action of F 1 and F 2 . {tex}\tau = F \times b \sin \theta{/tex} But, {tex}F _ { 1 } = F _ { 2 } = F = I B l, \therefore \tau = ( I B l ) \times ( b \sin \theta ){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \tau = I B ( l b ) \sin \theta{/tex} {tex}\therefore \tau = I B A \sin \theta{/tex} where, A = lb = area of coil. For N turns of the coil, we have {tex}\tau = N I A B \sin \theta{/tex} , this is the required expression of the torque in the current carrying rectangular loop.
solution_html:
Let a current carrying rectangular loop PQRS is carrying a steady current I placed in a uniform magnetic field B keeping the axis of the coil perpendicular to the field as shown in figure. Let at any instant the area vector A makes an angle {tex}\theta {/tex} with the direction of magnetic field intensity B.


Let length and breadth of the coil are I and b respectively.
Now, magnetic force on PS arm of the coil is given by {tex}F _ { 1 } = BIl \sin 90 ^ { \circ }{/tex} [{tex}\because {/tex} PS || axis of coil,  {tex}\therefore{/tex}  {tex}\theta = 90 ^ { \circ }{/tex}]
F1 = BIl ...... (i)
By Fleming's left hand rule, the direction of the above force is perpendicular to SP and B is along upward direction. Similarly, force on QR arm of the coil
F2 = BIl sin 90° ..... (ii)
The direction of force is perpendicular to QR and B is along downward direction
{tex}\because{/tex} F1 and F2 are equal in magnitude and opposite in direction, parallel to each other acting on the loop forms a couple which try to rotate the coil.
Now, force on RS part of the coil
{tex}F _ { 3 } = I B b \sin \left( 90 ^ { \circ } + \theta \right) \Rightarrow F _ { 3 } = I B b \cos \theta{/tex}
and also force on PQ part of the coil
{tex}F _ { 4 } = I B b \sin \left( 90 ^ { \circ } - \theta \right) = I B b \cos \theta{/tex}
But by Fleming's left hand rule, F3 and F4 are equal in magnitude and opposite in direction along the same line of action. Therefore, they balance each other.
Now, torque due to F1 and F2 is given by
{tex}\tau {/tex} = force {tex} \times {/tex} perpendicular distance between Iines of action of F1 and F2.
{tex}\tau = F \times b \sin \theta{/tex}
But, {tex}F _ { 1 } = F _ { 2 } = F = I B l, \therefore \tau = ( I B l ) \times ( b \sin \theta ){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \tau = I B ( l b ) \sin \theta{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \tau = I B A \sin \theta{/tex}
where, A = lb = area of coil.
For N turns of the coil, we have
{tex}\tau = N I A B \sin \theta{/tex}, this is the required expression of the torque in the current carrying rectangular loop.

question_html: An element M = {tex}\triangle\;l =\triangle x\;\widehat i{/tex} is placed at the origin (as shown in figure) and carries a current I = 2 A. Find out the magnetic field at a point P on the Y-axis at a distance of 1.0 m due to the element {tex}\Delta x = 1 \ cm{/tex}. Also, give the direction of the field produced.
question_text: An element M = {tex}\triangle\;l =\triangle x\;\widehat i{/tex} is placed at the origin (as shown in figure) and carries a current I = 2 A. Find out the magnetic field at a point P on the Y-axis at a distance of 1.0 m due to the element {tex}\Delta x = 1 \ cm{/tex} . Also, give the direction of the field produced.
explanation: According to Biot-Savart's law, {tex}\left|dB\right|=\frac{\mu_o}{4\mathrm\pi}\frac{I\;dl\;\sin\theta}{r^2}{/tex} dl = {tex}\triangle x{/tex} = 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -2 m, I = 2 A, r = 1m = y, {tex}\mu_o\;/\;4\mathrm\pi{/tex} = 10 -7 Tm/A and {tex}\theta{/tex} = 90 o So, {tex}\left|dB\right|{/tex} = 10 -7 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -2 sin 90 o = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -9 T Its direction is along +Z-axis as: dl {tex}\times{/tex} r = {tex}\triangle x{/tex} {tex}\widehat i{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} y {tex}\widehat j{/tex} = y {tex}\triangle x{/tex} ( {tex}\widehat i{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\widehat j{/tex} ) = y {tex}\triangle x{/tex} {tex}\widehat k{/tex}
solution: According to Biot-Savart's law, {tex}\left|dB\right|=\frac{\mu_o}{4\mathrm\pi}\frac{I\;dl\;\sin\theta}{r^2}{/tex} dl = {tex}\triangle x{/tex} = 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -2 m, I = 2 A, r = 1m = y, {tex}\mu_o\;/\;4\mathrm\pi{/tex} = 10 -7 Tm/A and {tex}\theta{/tex} = 90 o So, {tex}\left|dB\right|{/tex} = 10 -7 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -2 sin 90 o = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -9 T Its direction is along +Z-axis as: dl {tex}\times{/tex} r = {tex}\triangle x{/tex} {tex}\widehat i{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} y {tex}\widehat j{/tex} = y {tex}\triangle x{/tex} ( {tex}\widehat i{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\widehat j{/tex} ) = y {tex}\triangle x{/tex} {tex}\widehat k{/tex}
solution_html:
According to Biot-Savart's law,
{tex}\left|dB\right|=\frac{\mu_o}{4\mathrm\pi}\frac{I\;dl\;\sin\theta}{r^2}{/tex}
dl = {tex}\triangle x{/tex} = 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-2m, I = 2 A, r = 1m = y, {tex}\mu_o\;/\;4\mathrm\pi{/tex} = 10-7 Tm/A and {tex}\theta{/tex} = 90o
So,
{tex}\left|dB\right|{/tex} = 10-7 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-2 sin 90o = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-9 T
Its direction is along +Z-axis as:
dl {tex}\times{/tex} r = {tex}\triangle x{/tex} {tex}\widehat i{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} y {tex}\widehat j{/tex} = y {tex}\triangle x{/tex} ({tex}\widehat i{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\widehat j{/tex}) = y {tex}\triangle x{/tex} {tex}\widehat k{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html: An electron does not suffer any deflection while passing through a region of a uniform magnetic field. What is the direction of the magnetic field?
question_text: An electron does not suffer any deflection while passing through a region of a uniform magnetic field. What is the direction of the magnetic field?
explanation: Force on a charge, F = qvB sin {tex}\theta{/tex} As an electron does not suffer any deflection in the magnetic field, force is zero. It means {tex}\theta{/tex} = 0 o or 180 o Thus, the direction of the magnetic field should be parallel or antiparallel to the motion of the electron causing zero magnetic force on electron so that it suffers no deflection.
solution: Force on a charge, F = qvB sin {tex}\theta{/tex} As an electron does not suffer any deflection in the magnetic field, force is zero. It means {tex}\theta{/tex} = 0 o or 180 o Thus, the direction of the magnetic field should be parallel or antiparallel to the motion of the electron causing zero magnetic force on electron so that it suffers no deflection.
solution_html:
Force on a charge, F = qvB sin{tex}\theta{/tex}
As an electron does not suffer any deflection in the magnetic field, force is zero. It means {tex}\theta{/tex} = 0o or 180o
Thus, the direction of the magnetic field should be parallel or antiparallel to the motion of the electron causing zero magnetic force on electron so that it suffers no deflection.

subject: Physics
question_html: A point charge is moving with a constant velocity perpendicular to a uniform magnetic field as shown in the figure. What should be magnitude and direction of the electric field so that the particle moves undeviated along the same path?
question_text: A point charge is moving with a constant velocity perpendicular to a uniform magnetic field as shown in the figure. What should be magnitude and direction of the electric field so that the particle moves undeviated along the same path?
explanation: For the undeflected motion of the particle, total force on the particle is zero. So, Force due to electric field + Force due to the magnetic field = 0 {tex}\begin{array}{l}q\overrightarrow E+q(\overrightarrow v\times\overrightarrow B)=0\\\Rightarrow\overrightarrow E=-(\overrightarrow v\times\overrightarrow B)\end{array}{/tex} From the figure, {tex}\overrightarrow v=-v\;\widehat i\;and\;\overrightarrow B=-B\;\widehat k{/tex} As the particle is moving along X-direction and the magnetic field is perpendicular to the plane of the paper directed inwards, i.e. Z-direction Thus, {tex}\overrightarrow E=-(-v\;\widehat i\;\times-B\;\widehat k)=-vB(-\widehat j)=vB\;\widehat j{/tex} Therefore, Magnitude of electric field is vB and direction of electric field will be perpendicular to both v and B, i.e. along positive Y-axis.
solution: For the undeflected motion of the particle, total force on the particle is zero. So, Force due to electric field + Force due to the magnetic field = 0 {tex}\begin{array}{l}q\overrightarrow E+q(\overrightarrow v\times\overrightarrow B)=0\\\Rightarrow\overrightarrow E=-(\overrightarrow v\times\overrightarrow B)\end{array}{/tex} From the figure, {tex}\overrightarrow v=-v\;\widehat i\;and\;\overrightarrow B=-B\;\widehat k{/tex} As the particle is moving along X-direction and the magnetic field is perpendicular to the plane of the paper directed inwards, i.e. Z-direction Thus, {tex}\overrightarrow E=-(-v\;\widehat i\;\times-B\;\widehat k)=-vB(-\widehat j)=vB\;\widehat j{/tex} Therefore, Magnitude of electric field is vB and direction of electric field will be perpendicular to both v and B, i.e. along positive Y-axis.
solution_html:
For the undeflected motion of the particle, total force on the particle is zero. So,
Force due to electric field + Force due to the magnetic field = 0
{tex}\begin{array}{l}q\overrightarrow E+q(\overrightarrow v\times\overrightarrow B)=0\\\Rightarrow\overrightarrow E=-(\overrightarrow v\times\overrightarrow B)\end{array}{/tex}
From the figure, {tex}\overrightarrow v=-v\;\widehat i\;and\;\overrightarrow B=-B\;\widehat k{/tex}
As the particle is moving along X-direction and the magnetic field is perpendicular to the plane of the paper directed inwards, i.e. Z-direction
Thus, {tex}\overrightarrow E=-(-v\;\widehat i\;\times-B\;\widehat k)=-vB(-\widehat j)=vB\;\widehat j{/tex}
Therefore, Magnitude of electric field is vB and direction of electric field will be perpendicular to both v and B, i.e. along positive Y-axis.

question_html: A wire of length L is bent round in the form of a coil having N turns of same radius. If a steady current I flows through it in clockwise direction, then find the magnitude and direction of the magnetic field produced at its centre.
question_text: A wire of length L is bent round in the form of a coil having N turns of same radius. If a steady current I flows through it in clockwise direction, then find the magnitude and direction of the magnetic field produced at its centre.
explanation: When a straight wire of length L is bent in the form of a circular coil of N turns, then the length of the wire becomes equal to the circumference of the circular coil multiplied by the number of turns. Let the radius of coil be r as shown in the figure. {tex}\therefore N \times {/tex} circumference of the coil = Length of the wire {tex}\therefore \quad ( 2 \pi r ) \times N = L \Rightarrow r = L / 2 \pi N{/tex} .....(i) Magnitude of magnetic field at the centre due to N turns of a coil is given by {tex}B = \frac { \mu _ { 0 } ( N I ) } { 2 r } \Rightarrow B = \frac { \mu _ { 0 } ( N I ) } { 2 ( L / 2 \pi N ) }{/tex} [From Eq. (i)] {tex}B = \mu _ { 0 } \pi N ^ { 2 } I / L{/tex} According to right hand thumb rule, the direction of magnetic field is perpendicular to the plane of loop and entering into it represented as cross.
solution: When a straight wire of length L is bent in the form of a circular coil of N turns, then the length of the wire becomes equal to the circumference of the circular coil multiplied by the number of turns. Let the radius of coil be r as shown in the figure. {tex}\therefore N \times {/tex} circumference of the coil = Length of the wire {tex}\therefore \quad ( 2 \pi r ) \times N = L \Rightarrow r = L / 2 \pi N{/tex} .....(i) Magnitude of magnetic field at the centre due to N turns of a coil is given by {tex}B = \frac { \mu _ { 0 } ( N I ) } { 2 r } \Rightarrow B = \frac { \mu _ { 0 } ( N I ) } { 2 ( L / 2 \pi N ) }{/tex} [From Eq. (i)] {tex}B = \mu _ { 0 } \pi N ^ { 2 } I / L{/tex} According to right hand thumb rule, the direction of magnetic field is perpendicular to the plane of loop and entering into it represented as cross.
solution_html:
When a straight wire of length L is bent in the form of a circular coil of N turns, then the length of the wire becomes equal to the circumference of the circular coil multiplied by the number of turns.
Let the radius of coil be r as shown in the figure.

{tex}\therefore N \times {/tex} circumference of the coil = Length of the wire
{tex}\therefore \quad ( 2 \pi r ) \times N = L \Rightarrow r = L / 2 \pi N{/tex}.....(i)
Magnitude of magnetic field at the centre due to N turns of a coil is given by 
{tex}B = \frac { \mu _ { 0 } ( N I ) } { 2 r } \Rightarrow B = \frac { \mu _ { 0 } ( N I ) } { 2 ( L / 2 \pi N ) }{/tex} [From Eq. (i)]
{tex}B = \mu _ { 0 } \pi N ^ { 2 } I / L{/tex}
According to right hand thumb rule, the direction of magnetic field is perpendicular to the plane of loop and entering into it represented as cross.

question_html: In a certain arrangement, a proton does not get deflected, while passing through a magnetic field region. Under what condition is it possible?
question_text: In a certain arrangement, a proton does not get deflected, while passing through a magnetic field region. Under what condition is it possible?
explanation: Now, {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{F}}=e(\vec{v} \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}}){/tex} Obviously, {tex}\vec F{/tex} will be zero, if {tex}\vec{v} \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}}=0{/tex} i.e. {tex}\vec{v}\ \| \overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}}{/tex} Therefore, if a proton does not get deflected while passing through a magnetic field, it must be moving along the direction of the magnetic field.
solution: Now, {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{F}}=e(\vec{v} \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}}){/tex} Obviously, {tex}\vec F{/tex} will be zero, if {tex}\vec{v} \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}}=0{/tex} i.e. {tex}\vec{v}\ \| \overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}}{/tex} Therefore, if a proton does not get deflected while passing through a magnetic field, it must be moving along the direction of the magnetic field.
solution_html:
Now, {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{F}}=e(\vec{v} \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}}){/tex}
Obviously, {tex}\vec F{/tex} will be zero, if {tex}\vec{v} \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}}=0{/tex} i.e. {tex}\vec{v}\ \| \overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}}{/tex} 
Therefore, if a proton does not get deflected while passing through a magnetic field, it must be moving along the direction of the magnetic field.

question_html:
State Biot-Savart's law.
A current I flows in a conductor placed perpendicular to the plane of the paper. Indicate the direction of the magnetic field due to a small element dl at a point P situated at a distance r from the element as shown in the figure.
question_text: State Biot-Savart's law. A current I flows in a conductor placed perpendicular to the plane of the paper. Indicate the direction of the magnetic field due to a small element dl at a point P situated at a distance r from the element as shown in the figure.
explanation: Biot-Savart's law states that the magnitude of the magnetic field (dB) is directly proportional to the current I, length of current element dl of the conductor. directly proportional to {tex}\sin \theta{/tex} , i.e. {tex}d B \propto \sin \theta{/tex} where, {tex}\theta {/tex} is the angle between dl and r. inversely proportional to the square of the distance r, {tex}d B \propto \frac { 1 } { r ^ { 2 } }{/tex} Combining all the inequalities, {tex}d B \propto \frac { I d l \sin \theta } { r ^ { 2 } } = \frac { \mu _ { 0 } } { 4 \pi } \cdot \frac { I d l \sin \theta } { r ^ { 2 } }{/tex} where, {tex}\frac{\mu_o}{4\pi}=10^{-7}\;Tm/A{/tex} for free space where {tex}\mu_o{/tex} is permeability of free space. In vector form, {tex}d \mathbf { \vec B } = \frac { \mu _ { 0 } } { 4 \pi } \cdot \frac { I d \mathbf { \vec l } \times { \mathbf { \vec r } } } { \mathbf { r } ^ { 3 } }{/tex} The direction of the magnetic field dB is perpendicular to the plane containing dl and r. So, according to the given figure, {tex}\overrightarrow B\;=\;\frac{\mu_o}{4\pi}\frac{(I\;dl\;\widehat k)\;\times\;(r\;\widehat{j)}}{r^3}=\frac{\mu_o}{4\pi}\frac{I\;dl}{r^2}(-\widehat i){/tex} Thus the direction of the magnetic field is along the negative X direction.
solution: Biot-Savart's law states that the magnitude of the magnetic field (dB) is directly proportional to the current I, length of current element dl of the conductor. directly proportional to {tex}\sin \theta{/tex} , i.e. {tex}d B \propto \sin \theta{/tex} where, {tex}\theta {/tex} is the angle between dl and r. inversely proportional to the square of the distance r, {tex}d B \propto \frac { 1 } { r ^ { 2 } }{/tex} Combining all the inequalities, {tex}d B \propto \frac { I d l \sin \theta } { r ^ { 2 } } = \frac { \mu _ { 0 } } { 4 \pi } \cdot \frac { I d l \sin \theta } { r ^ { 2 } }{/tex} where, {tex}\frac{\mu_o}{4\pi}=10^{-7}\;Tm/A{/tex} for free space where {tex}\mu_o{/tex} is permeability of free space. In vector form, {tex}d \mathbf { \vec B } = \frac { \mu _ { 0 } } { 4 \pi } \cdot \frac { I d \mathbf { \vec l } \times { \mathbf { \vec r } } } { \mathbf { r } ^ { 3 } }{/tex} The direction of the magnetic field dB is perpendicular to the plane containing dl and r. So, according to the given figure, {tex}\overrightarrow B\;=\;\frac{\mu_o}{4\pi}\frac{(I\;dl\;\widehat k)\;\times\;(r\;\widehat{j)}}{r^3}=\frac{\mu_o}{4\pi}\frac{I\;dl}{r^2}(-\widehat i){/tex} Thus the direction of the magnetic field is along the negative X direction.
solution_html:
Biot-Savart's law states that the magnitude of the magnetic field (dB) is

  1. directly proportional to the current I, length of current element dl of the conductor.
  2. directly proportional to {tex}\sin \theta{/tex} , i.e. {tex}d B \propto \sin \theta{/tex} where, {tex}\theta {/tex} is the angle between dl and r.
  3. inversely proportional to the square of the distance r,
    {tex}d B \propto \frac { 1 } { r ^ { 2 } }{/tex}
    Combining all the inequalities,
    {tex}d B \propto \frac { I d l \sin \theta } { r ^ { 2 } } = \frac { \mu _ { 0 } } { 4 \pi } \cdot \frac { I d l \sin \theta } { r ^ { 2 } }{/tex}
    where, {tex}\frac{\mu_o}{4\pi}=10^{-7}\;Tm/A{/tex} for free space where {tex}\mu_o{/tex} is permeability of free space.
    In vector form, {tex}d \mathbf { \vec B } = \frac { \mu _ { 0 } } { 4 \pi } \cdot \frac { I d \mathbf { \vec l } \times { \mathbf { \vec r } } } { \mathbf { r } ^ { 3 } }{/tex}

The direction of the magnetic field dB is perpendicular to the plane containing dl and r. So, according to the given figure,

{tex}\overrightarrow B\;=\;\frac{\mu_o}{4\pi}\frac{(I\;dl\;\widehat k)\;\times\;(r\;\widehat{j)}}{r^3}=\frac{\mu_o}{4\pi}\frac{I\;dl}{r^2}(-\widehat i){/tex}
Thus the direction of the magnetic field is along the negative X direction.

question_html:
An element {tex}\Delta{/tex}{tex}\vec l{/tex} = {tex}\Delta{/tex}x {tex}\widehat i{/tex} is placed at the origin and carries a current I = 2 A as shown in the figure.

Find out the magnetic field at a point P on Y-axis at a distance of 1 m due to the element {tex}\Delta{/tex}x = 1 cm. Give also the direction of the field produced.
question_text: An element {tex}\Delta{/tex} {tex}\vec l{/tex} = {tex}\Delta{/tex} x {tex}\widehat i{/tex} is placed at the origin and carries a current I = 2 A as shown in the figure. Find out the magnetic field at a point P on Y-axis at a distance of 1 m due to the element {tex}\Delta{/tex} x = 1 cm. Give also the direction of the field produced.
explanation: According to Biot Savart's law, the magnetic field (both in magnitude and direction) at point P due to a small current element is given by {tex}\overrightarrow {dB}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \cdot \frac{\mathrm{I} \overrightarrow{d l} \times \hat{r}}{r^{2}}{/tex} Here, {tex}\overrightarrow{d l}{/tex} = {tex}\Delta x \hat{i}{/tex} and {tex}\hat{r}=\hat{j}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \overrightarrow{d B}=\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \cdot \frac{I \Delta x \hat{i} \times \hat{j}}{r^{2}}=\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \cdot \frac{I \Delta x \hat{k}}{r^{2}}{/tex} Thus, the magnetic field at point P is directed along a positive direction of the Y-axis. Further, {tex}\Delta{/tex} x = 1 cm = 0.01 m and r = 1m The magnitude of the magnetic field is given by dB = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \cdot \frac{\mathrm{I} \Delta x}{r^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{10^{-7} \times 2 \times 0 \cdot 01}{1^{2}}{/tex} = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -9 T
solution: According to Biot Savart's law, the magnetic field (both in magnitude and direction) at point P due to a small current element is given by {tex}\overrightarrow {dB}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \cdot \frac{\mathrm{I} \overrightarrow{d l} \times \hat{r}}{r^{2}}{/tex} Here, {tex}\overrightarrow{d l}{/tex} = {tex}\Delta x \hat{i}{/tex} and {tex}\hat{r}=\hat{j}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \overrightarrow{d B}=\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \cdot \frac{I \Delta x \hat{i} \times \hat{j}}{r^{2}}=\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \cdot \frac{I \Delta x \hat{k}}{r^{2}}{/tex} Thus, the magnetic field at point P is directed along a positive direction of the Y-axis. Further, {tex}\Delta{/tex} x = 1 cm = 0.01 m and r = 1m The magnitude of the magnetic field is given by dB = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \cdot \frac{\mathrm{I} \Delta x}{r^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{10^{-7} \times 2 \times 0 \cdot 01}{1^{2}}{/tex} = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -9 T
solution_html:
According to Biot Savart's law, the magnetic field (both in magnitude and direction) at point P due to a small current element is given by
{tex}\overrightarrow {dB}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \cdot \frac{\mathrm{I} \overrightarrow{d l} \times \hat{r}}{r^{2}}{/tex}
Here, {tex}\overrightarrow{d l}{/tex} = {tex}\Delta x \hat{i}{/tex} and {tex}\hat{r}=\hat{j}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \overrightarrow{d B}=\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \cdot \frac{I \Delta x \hat{i} \times \hat{j}}{r^{2}}=\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \cdot \frac{I \Delta x \hat{k}}{r^{2}}{/tex}
Thus, the magnetic field at point P is directed along a positive direction of the Y-axis.
Further, {tex}\Delta{/tex}x = 1 cm = 0.01 m and r = 1m
The magnitude of the magnetic field is given by
dB = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \cdot \frac{\mathrm{I} \Delta x}{r^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{10^{-7} \times 2 \times 0 \cdot 01}{1^{2}}{/tex} = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-9 T

question_html:
As shown in Figure., a charge q moving along the X-axis with a velocity v is subjected to a uniform magnetic field B acting along the Z-axis as it crosses the origin O.

  1. Trace its trajectory
  2. Does the charge gain kinetic energy, as it enters the magnetic field? Justify your answer.
question_text: As shown in Figure., a charge q moving along the X-axis with a velocity v is subjected to a uniform magnetic field B acting along the Z-axis as it crosses the origin O. Trace its trajectory Does the charge gain kinetic energy, as it enters the magnetic field? Justify your answer.
explanation: As the charged particle crosses the origin O with velocity {tex}\vec v{/tex} (along negative X-axis), it comes under the effect of magnetic field {tex}\vec B{/tex} (acting along positive Z-axis). A force equal to {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{F}}=q(\vec{v} \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}}){/tex} acts on the charged particle positive Y-axis. As a result, the particle moves along a circular path in XY-plane as shown in Fig (ii). The force on the charged particle due to magnetic field is always perpendicular to its path and therefore, work done by a magnetic field on the moving charge is zero. Hence, the charged particle will not gain kinetic energy.
solution: As the charged particle crosses the origin O with velocity {tex}\vec v{/tex} (along negative X-axis), it comes under the effect of magnetic field {tex}\vec B{/tex} (acting along positive Z-axis). A force equal to {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{F}}=q(\vec{v} \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}}){/tex} acts on the charged particle positive Y-axis. As a result, the particle moves along a circular path in XY-plane as shown in Fig (ii). The force on the charged particle due to magnetic field is always perpendicular to its path and therefore, work done by a magnetic field on the moving charge is zero. Hence, the charged particle will not gain kinetic energy.
solution_html:
1. As the charged particle crosses the origin O with velocity {tex}\vec v{/tex} (along negative X-axis), it comes under the effect of magnetic field {tex}\vec B{/tex} (acting along positive Z-axis). A force equal to {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{F}}=q(\vec{v} \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}}){/tex} acts on the charged particle positive Y-axis. As a result, the particle moves along a circular path in XY-plane as shown in Fig (ii).
  2. The force on the charged particle due to magnetic field is always perpendicular to its path and therefore, work done by a magnetic field on the moving charge is zero. Hence, the charged particle will not gain kinetic energy.

question_html: A wire of length L is bent round in the form of a coil having N turns of same radius. If a steady current I flows through it in a clockwise direction, find the magnitude and direction of the magnetic field produced at its centre.
question_text: A wire of length L is bent round in the form of a coil having N turns of same radius. If a steady current I flows through it in a clockwise direction, find the magnitude and direction of the magnetic field produced at its centre.
explanation: If r is the radius of the coil, then {tex}N \times 2 \pi r=L{/tex} or {tex}r=\frac{L}{2 \pi N}{/tex} Magnetic field produced at the centre of the coil due to current I, {tex}B=\frac{\mu_{0} N I}{2 r}=\frac{\mu_{0} N I}{2(L / 2 \pi N)}=\frac{\mu_{0} \pi N^{2} I}{L}{/tex} As the current flows clockwise throughout coil, the direction of the magnetic field will be out of the plane of the coil.
solution: If r is the radius of the coil, then {tex}N \times 2 \pi r=L{/tex} or {tex}r=\frac{L}{2 \pi N}{/tex} Magnetic field produced at the centre of the coil due to current I, {tex}B=\frac{\mu_{0} N I}{2 r}=\frac{\mu_{0} N I}{2(L / 2 \pi N)}=\frac{\mu_{0} \pi N^{2} I}{L}{/tex} As the current flows clockwise throughout coil, the direction of the magnetic field will be out of the plane of the coil.
solution_html:
If r is the radius of the coil, then
{tex}N \times 2 \pi r=L{/tex} or {tex}r=\frac{L}{2 \pi N}{/tex}
Magnetic field produced at the centre of the coil due to current I, {tex}B=\frac{\mu_{0} N I}{2 r}=\frac{\mu_{0} N I}{2(L / 2 \pi N)}=\frac{\mu_{0} \pi N^{2} I}{L}{/tex}
As the current flows clockwise throughout coil, the direction of the magnetic field will be out of the plane of the coil.

Which practical machine makes use of this fact? What is the frequency of the alternating electric field, used in this machine?
explanation: The radius of the circular path traced by charged particle in a perpendicular uniform magnetic field is r = {tex}\frac{mv}{qB}{/tex} Frequency of revolution, v c = {tex}\frac{1}{T}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{v}{2\pi r}{/tex} = v {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\frac{qB}{2\pi}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} mv = {tex}\frac{qB}{2\pi m}{/tex} which is independent of speed v. A cyclotron makes use of this fact in which alternating electric field of frequency v c is applied.
solution: The radius of the circular path traced by charged particle in a perpendicular uniform magnetic field is r = {tex}\frac{mv}{qB}{/tex} Frequency of revolution, v c = {tex}\frac{1}{T}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{v}{2\pi r}{/tex} = v {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\frac{qB}{2\pi}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} mv = {tex}\frac{qB}{2\pi m}{/tex} which is independent of speed v. A cyclotron makes use of this fact in which alternating electric field of frequency v c is applied.
solution_html:
The radius of the circular path traced by charged particle in a perpendicular uniform magnetic field is r = {tex}\frac{mv}{qB}{/tex}
Frequency of revolution,
vc = {tex}\frac{1}{T}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{v}{2\pi r}{/tex} = v {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\frac{qB}{2\pi}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} mv = {tex}\frac{qB}{2\pi m}{/tex} which is independent of speed v.
A cyclotron makes use of this fact in which alternating electric field of frequency vc is applied.

question_html: A moving coil galvanometer of resistance G gives a full scale deflection for a current I Use he suitable circuit diagram to convert it into an 4 mmeter of range 0 to I(I > Ig) Deduce the expression for the shunt required for this conversion. Hence write the expression for the resistance of the ammeter thus obtained.
question_text: A moving coil galvanometer of resistance G gives a full scale deflection for a current I Use he suitable circuit diagram to convert it into an 4 mmeter of range 0 to I(I > I g ) Deduce the expression for the shunt required for this conversion. Hence write the expression for the resistance of the ammeter thus obtained.
explanation: Galvanometer is converted into ammeter of range 0 to I 0 . IgG = (I 0 - I g ) R A R A = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{g}} \mathrm{G}}{\left(\mathrm{I}_0-\mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{g}}\right)}{/tex} ...(i) As in an ammeter resistance of galvanometer R G and shunt resistance r s are joined in parallel , the net resistance of the ammeter (r A ) is given by r A = R G ⋅r s (R G + r s ), which is even less than the shunt resistance r s .
solution: Galvanometer is converted into ammeter of range 0 to I 0 . IgG = (I 0 - I g ) R A R A = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{g}} \mathrm{G}}{\left(\mathrm{I}_0-\mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{g}}\right)}{/tex} ...(i) As in an ammeter resistance of galvanometer R G and shunt resistance r s are joined in parallel , the net resistance of the ammeter (r A ) is given by r A = R G ⋅r s (R G + r s ), which is even less than the shunt resistance r s .
solution_html:
Galvanometer is converted into ammeter of range 0 to I0.

IgG = (I0 - Ig) RA
RA = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{g}} \mathrm{G}}{\left(\mathrm{I}_0-\mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{g}}\right)}{/tex} ...(i)
As in an ammeter resistance of galvanometer RG and shunt resistance rs are joined in parallel , the net resistance of the ammeter (rA) is given by
rA = RG⋅rs(RG + rs), which is even less than the shunt resistance rs.

subject: Physics
question_html: Two identical charged particles moving with same speed enter a region of uniform magnetic field. If one of these enters normal to the field direction and the other enters along a direction at 30o with the field, what would be the ratio of their angular frequencies?
question_text: Two identical charged particles moving with same speed enter a region of uniform magnetic field. If one of these enters normal to the field direction and the other enters along a direction at 30 o with the field, what would be the ratio of their angular frequencies?
explanation: The angular frequency of a charged particle is given by {tex}\omega=\frac{\mathrm{B} q}{m}{/tex} Thus, {tex}\omega{/tex} is independent of the angle, at which the charge enters the field. Therefore, the angular frequencies of the two particles will be equal i.e. their ratio will be 1.
solution: The angular frequency of a charged particle is given by {tex}\omega=\frac{\mathrm{B} q}{m}{/tex} Thus, {tex}\omega{/tex} is independent of the angle, at which the charge enters the field. Therefore, the angular frequencies of the two particles will be equal i.e. their ratio will be 1.
solution_html:
The angular frequency of a charged particle is given by
{tex}\omega=\frac{\mathrm{B} q}{m}{/tex}
Thus, {tex}\omega{/tex} is independent of the angle, at which the charge enters the field. Therefore, the angular frequencies of the two particles will be equal i.e. their ratio will be 1.

question_html: The coils in certain galvanometers have a fixed core made of non-magnetic metallic materials. Why does this oscillating coil come to rest so quickly in such a core?
question_text: The coils in certain galvanometers have a fixed core made of non-magnetic metallic materials. Why does this oscillating coil come to rest so quickly in such a core?
explanation: The restoring torque due to eddy current in core tries to restore the coil back to its original position and thus brings it quickly to rest.
solution: The restoring torque due to eddy current in core tries to restore the coil back to its original position and thus brings it quickly to rest.
solution_html: The restoring torque due to eddy current in core tries to restore the coil back to its original position and thus brings it quickly to rest.

question_html: A solenoid, of length 1.5 m, has a radius of 1.5 cm and has a total of 1500 turns wound on it. It carries a current of 3 A. Calculate the magnitude of the axial magnetic field inside the solenoid. If an electron were to move with a speed of 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 104 ms-1 along the axis of this current-carrying solenoid, what would be the force experienced by this electron?
question_text: A solenoid, of length 1.5 m, has a radius of 1.5 cm and has a total of 1500 turns wound on it. It carries a current of 3 A. Calculate the magnitude of the axial magnetic field inside the solenoid. If an electron were to move with a speed of 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 4 ms -1 along the axis of this current-carrying solenoid, what would be the force experienced by this electron?
explanation: B = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} N I}{l}=\frac{4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times 1500 \times 3}{1.5 \times 10^{-2}} \mathrm{~T}{/tex} = 0.38 T Force, F = evB sin0 o = 0
solution: B = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} N I}{l}=\frac{4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times 1500 \times 3}{1.5 \times 10^{-2}} \mathrm{~T}{/tex} = 0.38 T Force, F = evB sin0 o = 0
solution_html:
B = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} N I}{l}=\frac{4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times 1500 \times 3}{1.5 \times 10^{-2}} \mathrm{~T}{/tex} = 0.38 T
Force, F = evB sin0o = 0

question_html:
If a particle of charge q is moving with velocity v along the z-axis and the magnetic field B is acting along the x-axis, use the expression {tex}\vec{F}=q(\vec{v} \times \vec{B}){/tex} to find the direction of the force F acting on it. A beam of proton passes undeflected with a horizontal velocity v, through a region of electric and magnetic fields, mutually perpendicular to each other and normal to the direction of the beam. If the magnitudes of the electric and magnetic fields are 100 kV/m and 50 mT respectively, calculate:

  1. velocity v of the beam,
  2. force with which it strikes a target on a screen if the proton beam current is equal to 0.80 mA.
question_text: If a particle of charge q is moving with velocity v along the z-axis and the magnetic field B is acting along the x-axis, use the expression {tex}\vec{F}=q(\vec{v} \times \vec{B}){/tex} to find the direction of the force F acting on it. A beam of proton passes undeflected with a horizontal velocity v, through a region of electric and magnetic fields, mutually perpendicular to each other and normal to the direction of the beam. If the magnitudes of the electric and magnetic fields are 100 kV/m and 50 mT respectively, calculate: velocity v of the beam, force with which it strikes a target on a screen if the proton beam current is equal to 0.80 mA.
explanation: {tex}\vec{F}=q(\vec{v} \times \vec{B})=q(v \hat{j} \times B \hat{k}){/tex} = {tex}q v B \hat{j} \times \hat{k}=q v B \hat{i}{/tex} Thus the force F acts on the charge q along the +ve x-direction. For undeflected proton beam, qvB = qE v = {tex}\frac{E}{B}=\frac{100 {kVm}^{-1}}{50 {mT}}=\frac{100 \times 10^{3} {Vm}^{-1}}{50 \times 10^{-3} {~T}}{/tex} = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 6 ms -1 Current carried by proton beam, I = 0.8 mA = 8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -4 A Number of protons striking the screen per second, n = {tex}\frac{I}{e}=\frac{8 \times 10^{-4}}{1.6 \times 10^{-19}}{/tex} = 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 15 s -1 m p = 1.675 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -27 kg Force with which a proton beam strikes a target on the screen, F = {tex}\frac{d p}{d t}{/tex} = m p nv = 1.675 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -27 {tex}\times{/tex} 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 15 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 6 N = 1.675 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -5 N
solution: {tex}\vec{F}=q(\vec{v} \times \vec{B})=q(v \hat{j} \times B \hat{k}){/tex} = {tex}q v B \hat{j} \times \hat{k}=q v B \hat{i}{/tex} Thus the force F acts on the charge q along the +ve x-direction. For undeflected proton beam, qvB = qE v = {tex}\frac{E}{B}=\frac{100 {kVm}^{-1}}{50 {mT}}=\frac{100 \times 10^{3} {Vm}^{-1}}{50 \times 10^{-3} {~T}}{/tex} = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 6 ms -1 Current carried by proton beam, I = 0.8 mA = 8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -4 A Number of protons striking the screen per second, n = {tex}\frac{I}{e}=\frac{8 \times 10^{-4}}{1.6 \times 10^{-19}}{/tex} = 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 15 s -1 m p = 1.675 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -27 kg Force with which a proton beam strikes a target on the screen, F = {tex}\frac{d p}{d t}{/tex} = m p nv = 1.675 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -27 {tex}\times{/tex} 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 15 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 6 N = 1.675 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -5 N
solution_html:
{tex}\vec{F}=q(\vec{v} \times \vec{B})=q(v \hat{j} \times B \hat{k}){/tex}
= {tex}q v B \hat{j} \times \hat{k}=q v B \hat{i}{/tex}
Thus the force F acts on the charge q along the +ve x-direction.

  1. For undeflected proton beam,
    qvB = qE
    v = {tex}\frac{E}{B}=\frac{100 {kVm}^{-1}}{50 {mT}}=\frac{100 \times 10^{3} {Vm}^{-1}}{50 \times 10^{-3} {~T}}{/tex}
    = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 106 ms-1
  2. Current carried by proton beam,
    I = 0.8 mA = 8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 A
    Number of protons striking the screen per second,
    n = {tex}\frac{I}{e}=\frac{8 \times 10^{-4}}{1.6 \times 10^{-19}}{/tex} = 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 1015s-1
    mp = 1.675 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-27 kg
    Force with which a proton beam strikes a target on the screen,
    F = {tex}\frac{d p}{d t}{/tex} = mp nv
    = 1.675 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-27 {tex}\times{/tex} 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 1015 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 106 N
    = 1.675 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 N

question_html: Two coaxial circular loops L1 and L2 of radii 3 cm and 4 cm are placed as shown. What should be the magnitude and direction of the current in the loop L2 so that the net magnetic field at the point O be zero?
question_text: Two coaxial circular loops L 1 and L 2 of radii 3 cm and 4 cm are placed as shown. What should be the magnitude and direction of the current in the loop L 2 so that the net magnetic field at the point O be zero?
explanation: For the net magnetic field at the point O to be zero, the direction of current in loop L 2 should be opposite to that in loop L 1 . Magnitude of magnetic field due to current I 1 in L 1 = Magnitude of magnetic field due to current I 2 in L 2 or {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I_{1}(0.03)^{2}}{2\left[(0.03)^{2}+(0.04)^{2}\right]^{3 / 2}}=\frac{\mu_{0} I_{2}(0.04)^{2}}{2\left[(0.04)^{2}+(0.03)^{2}\right]^{3 / 2}}{/tex} or I 2 = {tex}\frac{(0.03)^{2}}{(0.04)^{2}}{/tex} I 1 = {tex}\frac{9}{16} \times 1 {A}{/tex} = 0.56 A
solution: For the net magnetic field at the point O to be zero, the direction of current in loop L 2 should be opposite to that in loop L 1 . Magnitude of magnetic field due to current I 1 in L 1 = Magnitude of magnetic field due to current I 2 in L 2 or {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I_{1}(0.03)^{2}}{2\left[(0.03)^{2}+(0.04)^{2}\right]^{3 / 2}}=\frac{\mu_{0} I_{2}(0.04)^{2}}{2\left[(0.04)^{2}+(0.03)^{2}\right]^{3 / 2}}{/tex} or I 2 = {tex}\frac{(0.03)^{2}}{(0.04)^{2}}{/tex} I 1 = {tex}\frac{9}{16} \times 1 {A}{/tex} = 0.56 A
solution_html:
For the net magnetic field at the point O to be zero, the direction of current in loop L2 should be opposite to that in loop L1.
Magnitude of magnetic field due to current I1 in L1 = Magnitude of magnetic field due to current I2 in L2
or {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I_{1}(0.03)^{2}}{2\left[(0.03)^{2}+(0.04)^{2}\right]^{3 / 2}}=\frac{\mu_{0} I_{2}(0.04)^{2}}{2\left[(0.04)^{2}+(0.03)^{2}\right]^{3 / 2}}{/tex}
or I2 = {tex}\frac{(0.03)^{2}}{(0.04)^{2}}{/tex} I1 = {tex}\frac{9}{16} \times 1 {A}{/tex} = 0.56 A

question_html: Write two factors by which the voltage sensitivity of a moving coil galvanometer can be increased.
question_text: Write two factors by which the voltage sensitivity of a moving coil galvanometer can be increased.
explanation: The voltage sensitivity of a moving coil galvanometer can be increased by increasing the number of turns of the galvanometer coil. decreasing the torsion constant of the suspension fibre.
solution: The voltage sensitivity of a moving coil galvanometer can be increased by increasing the number of turns of the galvanometer coil. decreasing the torsion constant of the suspension fibre.
solution_html:
The voltage sensitivity of a moving coil galvanometer can be increased by

  1. increasing the number of turns of the galvanometer coil.
  2. decreasing the torsion constant of the suspension fibre.

Reason (R): The torque tends to rotate the coil about its own axis.

options.text: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A.

subject: Physics
question_html: State Biot-Savart law expressing it in vector form. Use it to obtain the magnetic field, at an axial point, distant r from the centre of a circular coil of radius a carrying a current I. Hence, compare the magnitudes of the magnetic field of this coil at the centre and at an axial point for which {tex}r=\sqrt{3} a{/tex}
question_text: State Biot-Savart law expressing it in vector form. Use it to obtain the magnetic field, at an axial point, distant r from the centre of a circular coil of radius a carrying a current I. Hence, compare the magnitudes of the magnetic field of this coil at the centre and at an axial point for which {tex}r=\sqrt{3} a{/tex}
explanation: Biot- Savart law: The magnetic field due to a current element of length dl carrying a current I at a point P, which is at a distance r from it is directly proportional to the current I and element length {tex}\overrightarrow{d l}{/tex} . Inversely proportional to the square of the distance between the current element and the point P. The direction of the magnetic field is perpendicular to the plane containing {tex}\overrightarrow{d l}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{r}{/tex} Vector form: {tex}\overrightarrow{d B}=\frac{\mu_0}{4 \pi} I \frac{\overrightarrow{d l} \times \vec{r}}{r^3}{/tex} Due to the current element dl considered at A the magnetic field at P is- dB = {tex}\frac{\mu_0 I}{4 \pi} \frac{|\overrightarrow{d l} \times \vec{r}|}{r^3}{/tex} Since, dl {tex}\times{/tex} r = dlr dB = {tex}\frac{\mu_0 I}{4 \pi} \frac{d l}{r^2}{/tex} dB = {tex}\frac{\mu_0 I}{4 \pi} \frac{d l}{\left(X^2+R^2\right)}{/tex} Consider a current element opposite to A, that is at B, then we can see that the y components of the magnetic, field due to this current element cancel and X component is only present. So we can say that the net magnetic field is along the X-direction. dB X = dB cos {tex}\theta{/tex} Net magnetic field at P is B = {tex}\int{/tex} dB X = {tex}\int{/tex} dB cos {tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}\int{/tex} {tex}\frac{\mu_0}{4 \pi} \frac{I d l}{\left(X^2+R^2\right)}{/tex} cos {tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}\int{/tex} {tex}\frac{\mu_0}{4 \pi} \frac{I d l}{\left(X^2+R^2\right)} {/tex} {tex}\frac{R}{\left(X^2+R^2\right)^{\frac{1}{2}}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mu_0 I R}{4 \pi\left(X^2+R^2\right)^{\frac{3}{2}}}{/tex} {tex}\int{/tex} dl = {tex}\frac{\mu_0 I R 2 \pi R}{4 \pi\left(X^2+R^2\right)^{\frac{3}{2}}}{/tex} B = {tex}\frac{\mu_0 I R^2}{2\left(X^2+R^2\right)^{\frac{3}{2}}}{/tex}
solution: Biot- Savart law: The magnetic field due to a current element of length dl carrying a current I at a point P, which is at a distance r from it is directly proportional to the current I and element length {tex}\overrightarrow{d l}{/tex} . Inversely proportional to the square of the distance between the current element and the point P. The direction of the magnetic field is perpendicular to the plane containing {tex}\overrightarrow{d l}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{r}{/tex} Vector form: {tex}\overrightarrow{d B}=\frac{\mu_0}{4 \pi} I \frac{\overrightarrow{d l} \times \vec{r}}{r^3}{/tex} Due to the current element dl considered at A the magnetic field at P is- dB = {tex}\frac{\mu_0 I}{4 \pi} \frac{|\overrightarrow{d l} \times \vec{r}|}{r^3}{/tex} Since, dl {tex}\times{/tex} r = dlr dB = {tex}\frac{\mu_0 I}{4 \pi} \frac{d l}{r^2}{/tex} dB = {tex}\frac{\mu_0 I}{4 \pi} \frac{d l}{\left(X^2+R^2\right)}{/tex} Consider a current element opposite to A, that is at B, then we can see that the y components of the magnetic, field due to this current element cancel and X component is only present. So we can say that the net magnetic field is along the X-direction. dB X = dB cos {tex}\theta{/tex} Net magnetic field at P is B = {tex}\int{/tex} dB X = {tex}\int{/tex} dB cos {tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}\int{/tex} {tex}\frac{\mu_0}{4 \pi} \frac{I d l}{\left(X^2+R^2\right)}{/tex} cos {tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}\int{/tex} {tex}\frac{\mu_0}{4 \pi} \frac{I d l}{\left(X^2+R^2\right)} {/tex} {tex}\frac{R}{\left(X^2+R^2\right)^{\frac{1}{2}}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mu_0 I R}{4 \pi\left(X^2+R^2\right)^{\frac{3}{2}}}{/tex} {tex}\int{/tex} dl = {tex}\frac{\mu_0 I R 2 \pi R}{4 \pi\left(X^2+R^2\right)^{\frac{3}{2}}}{/tex} B = {tex}\frac{\mu_0 I R^2}{2\left(X^2+R^2\right)^{\frac{3}{2}}}{/tex}
solution_html:
Biot- Savart law:
The magnetic field due to a current element of length dl carrying a current I at a point P, which is at a distance r from it is

  1. directly proportional to the current I and element length {tex}\overrightarrow{d l}{/tex}.
  2. Inversely proportional to the square of the distance between the current element and the point P.

The direction of the magnetic field is perpendicular to the plane containing {tex}\overrightarrow{d l}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{r}{/tex}
Vector form:
{tex}\overrightarrow{d B}=\frac{\mu_0}{4 \pi} I \frac{\overrightarrow{d l} \times \vec{r}}{r^3}{/tex}

Due to the current element dl considered at A the magnetic field at P is-
dB = {tex}\frac{\mu_0 I}{4 \pi} \frac{|\overrightarrow{d l} \times \vec{r}|}{r^3}{/tex}
Since, dl {tex}\times{/tex} r = dlr
dB = {tex}\frac{\mu_0 I}{4 \pi} \frac{d l}{r^2}{/tex}
dB = {tex}\frac{\mu_0 I}{4 \pi} \frac{d l}{\left(X^2+R^2\right)}{/tex}
Consider a current element opposite to A, that is at B, then we can see that the y components of the magnetic, field due to this current element cancel and X component is only present.
So we can say that the net magnetic field is along the X-direction.
dBX = dB cos{tex}\theta{/tex}
Net magnetic field at P is
B = {tex}\int{/tex}dBX = {tex}\int{/tex}dB cos{tex}\theta{/tex}
= {tex}\int{/tex}{tex}\frac{\mu_0}{4 \pi} \frac{I d l}{\left(X^2+R^2\right)}{/tex}cos{tex}\theta{/tex}
= {tex}\int{/tex}{tex}\frac{\mu_0}{4 \pi} \frac{I d l}{\left(X^2+R^2\right)} {/tex}{tex}\frac{R}{\left(X^2+R^2\right)^{\frac{1}{2}}}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{\mu_0 I R}{4 \pi\left(X^2+R^2\right)^{\frac{3}{2}}}{/tex} {tex}\int{/tex}dl = {tex}\frac{\mu_0 I R 2 \pi R}{4 \pi\left(X^2+R^2\right)^{\frac{3}{2}}}{/tex}
B = {tex}\frac{\mu_0 I R^2}{2\left(X^2+R^2\right)^{\frac{3}{2}}}{/tex}

question_html: An {tex}\alpha{/tex}-particle and a proton are moving in the plane of the paper in a region where there is a uniform magnetic field ({tex}\vec{B}{/tex}) directed normal to the plane of the paper. If the two particles have equal linear momenta, what will be the ratio of the radii of their trajectories in the field?
question_text: An {tex}\alpha{/tex} -particle and a proton are moving in the plane of the paper in a region where there is a uniform magnetic field ( {tex}\vec{B}{/tex} ) directed normal to the plane of the paper. If the two particles have equal linear momenta, what will be the ratio of the radii of their trajectories in the field?
explanation: Radius, {tex}r=\frac{m v}{q B}=\frac{p}{q B}{/tex} For same p and B, {tex}\frac{r_{\alpha}}{r_{p}}=\frac{q_{p}}{q_{\alpha}}=\frac{e}{2 e}=\frac{1}{2}=\mathbf{1}: 2{/tex}
solution: Radius, {tex}r=\frac{m v}{q B}=\frac{p}{q B}{/tex} For same p and B, {tex}\frac{r_{\alpha}}{r_{p}}=\frac{q_{p}}{q_{\alpha}}=\frac{e}{2 e}=\frac{1}{2}=\mathbf{1}: 2{/tex}
solution_html:
Radius, {tex}r=\frac{m v}{q B}=\frac{p}{q B}{/tex}
For same p and B, {tex}\frac{r_{\alpha}}{r_{p}}=\frac{q_{p}}{q_{\alpha}}=\frac{e}{2 e}=\frac{1}{2}=\mathbf{1}: 2{/tex}

question_text: A student records the following data for the magnitudes (B) of the magnetic field at axial points at different distances x from the centre of a circular coil of radius a carrying a current I. Verify (for any two) that these observations are in good agreement with the expected theoretical variation of B with x. x {tex} \to {/tex} x = 0 x - a x = 2 a x = 3a y {tex} \to {/tex} B 0 {tex}0.25 \sqrt{2} B_{0}{/tex} {tex}0.039 \sqrt{5} B_{0}{/tex} {tex}0.010 \sqrt{10} B_{0}{/tex}
explanation: The magnetic field at an axial point at distance x from the centre of the circular current loop is given by {tex}B=\frac{\mu_{0} I a^{2}}{2\left(a^{2}+x^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}}=\frac{\mu_{0} I}{2 a\left(1+x^{2} / a^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}}{/tex} At x = 0 {tex}B=\frac{\mu_{0} I}{2 a}=B_{0}{/tex} At x = a {tex}B=\frac{\mu_{0} I}{2 a\left(1+\frac{a^{2}}{a^{2}}\right)^{3 / 2}}=\frac{\mu_{0} I}{2 a(2)^{3 / 2}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{B_{0}}{2 \sqrt{2}}=\frac{\sqrt{2}}{4} B_{0}=0.25 \sqrt{2} B_{0}{/tex} At x =2a, {tex}B=\frac{\mu_{0} I}{2 a\left(1+\frac{4 a^{2}}{a^{2}}\right)^{3 / 2}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{B_{0}}{5^{3 / 2}}=\frac{\sqrt{5}}{25} B_{0}=0.04 \sqrt{5} B_{0}{/tex} At x =3 a, {tex}B=\frac{\mu_{0} I}{3 a\left(1+\frac{9 a^{2}}{a^{2}}\right)^{3 / 2}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{B_{0}}{10^{3 / 2}}=\frac{\sqrt{10}}{100} B_{0}=0.01 \sqrt{10} B_{0}{/tex}
solution: The magnetic field at an axial point at distance x from the centre of the circular current loop is given by {tex}B=\frac{\mu_{0} I a^{2}}{2\left(a^{2}+x^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}}=\frac{\mu_{0} I}{2 a\left(1+x^{2} / a^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}}{/tex} At x = 0 {tex}B=\frac{\mu_{0} I}{2 a}=B_{0}{/tex} At x = a {tex}B=\frac{\mu_{0} I}{2 a\left(1+\frac{a^{2}}{a^{2}}\right)^{3 / 2}}=\frac{\mu_{0} I}{2 a(2)^{3 / 2}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{B_{0}}{2 \sqrt{2}}=\frac{\sqrt{2}}{4} B_{0}=0.25 \sqrt{2} B_{0}{/tex} At x =2a, {tex}B=\frac{\mu_{0} I}{2 a\left(1+\frac{4 a^{2}}{a^{2}}\right)^{3 / 2}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{B_{0}}{5^{3 / 2}}=\frac{\sqrt{5}}{25} B_{0}=0.04 \sqrt{5} B_{0}{/tex} At x =3 a, {tex}B=\frac{\mu_{0} I}{3 a\left(1+\frac{9 a^{2}}{a^{2}}\right)^{3 / 2}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{B_{0}}{10^{3 / 2}}=\frac{\sqrt{10}}{100} B_{0}=0.01 \sqrt{10} B_{0}{/tex}
solution_html:
The magnetic field at an axial point at distance x from the centre of the circular current loop is given by {tex}B=\frac{\mu_{0} I a^{2}}{2\left(a^{2}+x^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}}=\frac{\mu_{0} I}{2 a\left(1+x^{2} / a^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}}{/tex}

  1. At x = 0 {tex}B=\frac{\mu_{0} I}{2 a}=B_{0}{/tex}
  2. At x = a
    {tex}B=\frac{\mu_{0} I}{2 a\left(1+\frac{a^{2}}{a^{2}}\right)^{3 / 2}}=\frac{\mu_{0} I}{2 a(2)^{3 / 2}}{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{B_{0}}{2 \sqrt{2}}=\frac{\sqrt{2}}{4} B_{0}=0.25 \sqrt{2} B_{0}{/tex}
  3. At x =2a, {tex}B=\frac{\mu_{0} I}{2 a\left(1+\frac{4 a^{2}}{a^{2}}\right)^{3 / 2}}{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{B_{0}}{5^{3 / 2}}=\frac{\sqrt{5}}{25} B_{0}=0.04 \sqrt{5} B_{0}{/tex}
  4. At x =3 a, {tex}B=\frac{\mu_{0} I}{3 a\left(1+\frac{9 a^{2}}{a^{2}}\right)^{3 / 2}}{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{B_{0}}{10^{3 / 2}}=\frac{\sqrt{10}}{100} B_{0}=0.01 \sqrt{10} B_{0}{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html:
Write the relation for the force {tex}\vec F{/tex} acting on a charge carrier q moving with velocity {tex}\vec v{/tex} through a magnetic field {tex}\vec B{/tex} in vector notation. Using this relation, deduce the conditions under which, this force is

  1. maximum and
  2. minimum
question_text: Write the relation for the force {tex}\vec F{/tex} acting on a charge carrier q moving with velocity {tex}\vec v{/tex} through a magnetic field {tex}\vec B{/tex} in vector notation. Using this relation, deduce the conditions under which, this force is maximum and minimum
explanation: The relation is {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{F}}=q(\vec{v} \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}}){/tex} The force is maximum, when the charge moves perpendicular to the direction of magnetic field i.e. {tex}\vec{v} \perp \overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}}{/tex} The force is minimum, when the charge moves along the direction of magnetic field i.e. {tex}\vec v \ || \vec B{/tex}
solution: The relation is {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{F}}=q(\vec{v} \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}}){/tex} The force is maximum, when the charge moves perpendicular to the direction of magnetic field i.e. {tex}\vec{v} \perp \overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}}{/tex} The force is minimum, when the charge moves along the direction of magnetic field i.e. {tex}\vec v \ || \vec B{/tex}
solution_html:
The relation is {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{F}}=q(\vec{v} \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}}){/tex}

  1. The force is maximum, when the charge moves perpendicular to the direction of magnetic field i.e. {tex}\vec{v} \perp \overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}}{/tex}
  2. The force is minimum, when the charge moves along the direction of magnetic field i.e. {tex}\vec v \ || \vec B{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html: A charged particle enters a region of uniform magnetic field with its initial velocity directed (t) parallel to the field, (ii) perpendicular to the field. Show that there is no change in the kinetic energy of the particle in both cases.
question_text: A charged particle enters a region of uniform magnetic field with its initial velocity directed (t) parallel to the field, (ii) perpendicular to the field. Show that there is no change in the kinetic energy of the particle in both cases.
explanation: The parallel magnetic field does not exert any force on the moving charged particle. Hence there is no change in the velocity or the kinetic energy of the charged particle. The magnetic force acts on the charged particle perpendicular to its direction of motion. Hence no work is done and there is no change in the kinetic energy of the particle.
solution: The parallel magnetic field does not exert any force on the moving charged particle. Hence there is no change in the velocity or the kinetic energy of the charged particle. The magnetic force acts on the charged particle perpendicular to its direction of motion. Hence no work is done and there is no change in the kinetic energy of the particle.
solution_html:
1. The parallel magnetic field does not exert any force on the moving charged particle. Hence there is no change in the velocity or the kinetic energy of the charged particle.
  2. The magnetic force acts on the charged particle perpendicular to its direction of motion. Hence no work is done and there is no change in the kinetic energy of the particle.

question_html:
1. Two straight long parallel conductors carry currents I1 and I2 in the same direction. Deduce the expression for the force per unit length between them. Depict the pattern of magnetic field lines around them.
  2. A rectangular current carrying loop EFGH is kept in a uniform magnetic field as shown in Fig.
    1. What is the direction of the magnetic moment of the current loop?
    2. When is the torque acting on the loop (A) maximum, (B) zero?
question_text: Two straight long parallel conductors carry currents I 1 and I 2 in the same direction. Deduce the expression for the force per unit length between them. Depict the pattern of magnetic field lines around them. A rectangular current carrying loop EFGH is kept in a uniform magnetic field as shown in Fig. What is the direction of the magnetic moment of the current loop? When is the torque acting on the loop (A) maximum, (B) zero?
explanation: Consider a current–element ‘ab’ of length {tex}\Delta{/tex} L of wire RS. The magnetic field produced by current-carrying conductor PQ at the location of other wire RS B 1 = {tex}\frac{\mu_0 I_1}{2 \pi r}{/tex} ...(i) According to Maxwell’s right hand rule or right hand palm rule no. 1, the direction of B 1 will be perpendicular to the plane of paper and directed downward. Due to this magnetic field, each element of other wire experiences a force. The direction of current element is perpendicular to the magnetic field; therefore the magnetic force on element ab of length {tex}\Delta{/tex} L The total force on conductor of length L will be F = {tex}\frac{\mu_0 I_1 I_2}{2 \pi r} \Sigma{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex} L = {tex}\frac{\mu_0 I_1 I_2}{2 \pi r}{/tex} L Force acting on per unit length of conductor f = {tex}\frac{F}{L}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mu_0 I_1 I_2}{2 \pi r}{/tex} N/m ...(ii) According to Fleming’s left hand rule, the direction of magnetic force will be towards PQ i.e. the force will be attractive. The magnetic field lies due to two current carrying parallel wires are shown in figure The force between parallel wires = {tex}\frac{\mu_0 I_1 I_2}{2 \pi r}{/tex} N/m Torque acting on the current loop is (A) maximum when vector m is perpendicular to vector B. (B) Zero when vector M is parallel to vector B.
solution: Consider a current–element ‘ab’ of length {tex}\Delta{/tex} L of wire RS. The magnetic field produced by current-carrying conductor PQ at the location of other wire RS B 1 = {tex}\frac{\mu_0 I_1}{2 \pi r}{/tex} ...(i) According to Maxwell’s right hand rule or right hand palm rule no. 1, the direction of B 1 will be perpendicular to the plane of paper and directed downward. Due to this magnetic field, each element of other wire experiences a force. The direction of current element is perpendicular to the magnetic field; therefore the magnetic force on element ab of length {tex}\Delta{/tex} L The total force on conductor of length L will be F = {tex}\frac{\mu_0 I_1 I_2}{2 \pi r} \Sigma{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex} L = {tex}\frac{\mu_0 I_1 I_2}{2 \pi r}{/tex} L Force acting on per unit length of conductor f = {tex}\frac{F}{L}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mu_0 I_1 I_2}{2 \pi r}{/tex} N/m ...(ii) According to Fleming’s left hand rule, the direction of magnetic force will be towards PQ i.e. the force will be attractive. The magnetic field lies due to two current carrying parallel wires are shown in figure The force between parallel wires = {tex}\frac{\mu_0 I_1 I_2}{2 \pi r}{/tex} N/m Torque acting on the current loop is (A) maximum when vector m is perpendicular to vector B. (B) Zero when vector M is parallel to vector B.
solution_html:
1. Consider a current–element ‘ab’ of length {tex}\Delta{/tex}L of wire RS. The magnetic field produced by current-carrying conductor PQ at the location of other wire RS
    B1 = {tex}\frac{\mu_0 I_1}{2 \pi r}{/tex} ...(i)
    According to Maxwell’s right hand rule or right hand palm rule no. 1, the direction of B1 will be perpendicular to the plane of paper and directed downward. Due to this magnetic field, each element of other wire experiences a force. The direction of current element is perpendicular to the magnetic field; therefore the magnetic force on element ab of length {tex}\Delta{/tex}L

    The total force on conductor of length L will be
    F = {tex}\frac{\mu_0 I_1 I_2}{2 \pi r} \Sigma{/tex}{tex}\Delta{/tex}L = {tex}\frac{\mu_0 I_1 I_2}{2 \pi r}{/tex}L
    Force acting on per unit length of conductor
    f = {tex}\frac{F}{L}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mu_0 I_1 I_2}{2 \pi r}{/tex}N/m ...(ii)
    According to Fleming’s left hand rule, the direction of magnetic force will be towards PQ i.e. the force will be attractive.
  2. The magnetic field lies due to two current carrying parallel wires are shown in figure
    The force between parallel wires
    1. = {tex}\frac{\mu_0 I_1 I_2}{2 \pi r}{/tex} N/m
    2. Torque acting on the current loop is
      (A) maximum when vector m is perpendicular to vector B.
      (B) Zero when vector M is parallel to vector B.

subject: Physics
question_html: Under what condition is the force acting on a charge moving through a uniform magnetic field minimum?
question_text: Under what condition is the force acting on a charge moving through a uniform magnetic field minimum?
explanation: When {tex}\theta=0^{\circ} \text { or } 180^{\circ}{/tex} , {tex}F_{m}=q v B \sin \theta=q v B(0)=0{/tex} So when a charge moves parallel or antiparallel to the direction of the magnetic field, it experiences minimum (zero) force.
solution: When {tex}\theta=0^{\circ} \text { or } 180^{\circ}{/tex} , {tex}F_{m}=q v B \sin \theta=q v B(0)=0{/tex} So when a charge moves parallel or antiparallel to the direction of the magnetic field, it experiences minimum (zero) force.
solution_html:
When {tex}\theta=0^{\circ} \text { or } 180^{\circ}{/tex},
{tex}F_{m}=q v B \sin \theta=q v B(0)=0{/tex}
So when a charge moves parallel or antiparallel to the direction of the magnetic field, it experiences minimum (zero) force.

subject: Physics
question_html: An electron beam projected along + X-axis, experiences a force due to a magnetic field along the + Y-axis. What is the direction of the magnetic field?
question_text: An electron beam projected along + X-axis, experiences a force due to a magnetic field along the + Y-axis. What is the direction of the magnetic field?
explanation: According to Fleming's left hand rule, the magnetic field acts in the +Z-direction.
solution: According to Fleming's left hand rule, the magnetic field acts in the +Z-direction.
solution_html: According to Fleming's left hand rule, the magnetic field acts in the +Z-direction.

question_html: A coil in the shape of an equilateral triangle of side l is suspended between the pole pieces of a permanent magnet, such that {tex}\vec{B}{/tex} is in plane of the coil. If due to a current I in the triangle, a torque {tex}\vec{\tau}{/tex} acts on it, the side l of the triangle is:
question_text: A coil in the shape of an equilateral triangle of side l is suspended between the pole pieces of a permanent magnet, such that {tex}\vec{B}{/tex} is in plane of the coil. If due to a current I in the triangle, a torque {tex}\vec{\tau}{/tex} acts on it, the side l of the triangle is:

options.explanation: Normal to the plane of the coil will be perpendicular to the field {tex}\vec{B}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \tau{/tex} = IBA sin 90 o = IBA Area of an equilateral triangle, {tex}A=\frac{1}{2} \times{/tex} Base {tex}\times{/tex} Height = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \times l \times l \sin 60^{\circ}=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{4} l^{2}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \tau=I B \times \frac{\sqrt{3}}{4} l^{2} \text { or } l=2\left(\frac{\tau}{\sqrt{3} B I}\right)^{1 / 2}{/tex}

explanation: Normal to the plane of the coil will be perpendicular to the field {tex}\vec{B}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \tau{/tex} = IBA sin 90 o = IBA Area of an equilateral triangle, {tex}A=\frac{1}{2} \times{/tex} Base {tex}\times{/tex} Height = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \times l \times l \sin 60^{\circ}=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{4} l^{2}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \tau=I B \times \frac{\sqrt{3}}{4} l^{2} \text { or } l=2\left(\frac{\tau}{\sqrt{3} B I}\right)^{1 / 2}{/tex}

question_text: A very long straight wire carries a current I. At the instant when a charge + Q at point P has the velocity {tex}\vec{v}{/tex} as shown, the force on the charge is:

options.explanation: The field {tex}\vec{B}{/tex} due to current I act normally into the plane of paper i.e., along -ve Z-direction {tex}\vec{F}=Q(\vec{v} \times \vec{B})=Q[v \hat{i} \times(-B \hat{k})]{/tex} {tex}=Q v B \hat{k} \times \hat{i}=Q v B \hat{j}{/tex} , thus the force on the charge is along OY direction.

subject: Physics
question_html: A rectangular loop of sides 25 cm and 10 cm carrying a current of 15 A is placed with its longer side parallel to a long straight conductor 2.0 cm apart carrying a current of 25A. What is the net force on the loop?
question_text: A rectangular loop of sides 25 cm and 10 cm carrying a current of 15 A is placed with its longer side parallel to a long straight conductor 2.0 cm apart carrying a current of 25A. What is the net force on the loop?
explanation: Consider the rectangular loop ABCD placed near a long straight conductor XY, as shown in Figure. The arm AB will get attracted, while CD will get repelled. Forces on arms BC and AD, being equal, opposite and collinear, will cancel each other. Current through the rectangular loop, f = 15 A Current through the long wire XY, I 2 =25 A Force on AB, {tex}F_{1}=\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \cdot \frac{2 I_{1} I_{2}}{r_{1}} \times{/tex} length of conductor AB {tex}=\frac{10^{-7} \times 2 \times 15 \times 25}{2.0 \times 10^{-2}} \times 25 \times 10^{-2}{/tex} = 9.375 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -4 N (Attractive) Force on CD, {tex}F_{2}=\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \cdot \frac{2 I_{1} I_{2}}{r_{2}} \times{/tex} length of conductor CD {tex}=\frac{10^{-7} \times 2 \times 15 \times 25}{12.0 \times 10^{-2}} \times 25 \times 10^{-2}{/tex} {tex}=1.5625 \times 10^{-4}{/tex} (Repulsive) {tex}\therefore{/tex} Net force on the loop {tex}F=F_{1}-F_{2}=9.375 \times 10^{-4}-1.5625 \times 10^{-4}{/tex} {tex}=7.8125 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~N} \simeq 7.8 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~N}{/tex} (Attractive) Thus the force on the loop will act towards the long conductor (attractive) if the current in its closer side is in the same direction as the current in the long conductor, otherwise, it will be repulsive.
solution: Consider the rectangular loop ABCD placed near a long straight conductor XY, as shown in Figure. The arm AB will get attracted, while CD will get repelled. Forces on arms BC and AD, being equal, opposite and collinear, will cancel each other. Current through the rectangular loop, f = 15 A Current through the long wire XY, I 2 =25 A Force on AB, {tex}F_{1}=\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \cdot \frac{2 I_{1} I_{2}}{r_{1}} \times{/tex} length of conductor AB {tex}=\frac{10^{-7} \times 2 \times 15 \times 25}{2.0 \times 10^{-2}} \times 25 \times 10^{-2}{/tex} = 9.375 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -4 N (Attractive) Force on CD, {tex}F_{2}=\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \cdot \frac{2 I_{1} I_{2}}{r_{2}} \times{/tex} length of conductor CD {tex}=\frac{10^{-7} \times 2 \times 15 \times 25}{12.0 \times 10^{-2}} \times 25 \times 10^{-2}{/tex} {tex}=1.5625 \times 10^{-4}{/tex} (Repulsive) {tex}\therefore{/tex} Net force on the loop {tex}F=F_{1}-F_{2}=9.375 \times 10^{-4}-1.5625 \times 10^{-4}{/tex} {tex}=7.8125 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~N} \simeq 7.8 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~N}{/tex} (Attractive) Thus the force on the loop will act towards the long conductor (attractive) if the current in its closer side is in the same direction as the current in the long conductor, otherwise, it will be repulsive.
solution_html:
Consider the rectangular loop ABCD placed near a long straight conductor XY, as shown in Figure. The arm AB will get attracted, while CD will get repelled. Forces on arms BC and AD, being equal, opposite and collinear, will cancel each other.

Current through the rectangular loop, f = 15 A
Current through the long wire XY, I2 =25 A
Force on AB,

{tex}F_{1}=\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \cdot \frac{2 I_{1} I_{2}}{r_{1}} \times{/tex} length of conductor AB
{tex}=\frac{10^{-7} \times 2 \times 15 \times 25}{2.0 \times 10^{-2}} \times 25 \times 10^{-2}{/tex}
= 9.375 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4N (Attractive)
Force on CD,
{tex}F_{2}=\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \cdot \frac{2 I_{1} I_{2}}{r_{2}} \times{/tex} length of conductor CD
{tex}=\frac{10^{-7} \times 2 \times 15 \times 25}{12.0 \times 10^{-2}} \times 25 \times 10^{-2}{/tex}
{tex}=1.5625 \times 10^{-4}{/tex} (Repulsive)
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Net force on the loop
{tex}F=F_{1}-F_{2}=9.375 \times 10^{-4}-1.5625 \times 10^{-4}{/tex}
{tex}=7.8125 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~N} \simeq 7.8 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~N}{/tex} (Attractive)
Thus the force on the loop will act towards the long conductor (attractive) if the current in its closer side is in the same direction as the current in the long conductor, otherwise, it will be repulsive.

subject: Physics
question_html: An electron is moving with a velocity v, along the axis of a long straight solenoid, carrying a current I. What will be the force acting on the electron due to the magnetic field of the solenoid?
question_text: An electron is moving with a velocity v, along the axis of a long straight solenoid, carrying a current I. What will be the force acting on the electron due to the magnetic field of the solenoid?
explanation: The electron will continue to move with uniform velocity along the axis of the solenoid. Let the electron (e) is projected with a uniform velocity (v) in a uniform magnetic field B. The magnitude of force on it is {tex}|\bar{F}|=-e|\bar{v} \times \bar{B}|=-e v B \sin \theta{/tex} As {tex}\theta=0^{\circ},|\bar{F}|=-e v B \sin 0^{\circ}{/tex} Hence the electron will continue to move with uniform velocity along the axis of the solenoid.
solution: The electron will continue to move with uniform velocity along the axis of the solenoid. Let the electron (e) is projected with a uniform velocity (v) in a uniform magnetic field B. The magnitude of force on it is {tex}|\bar{F}|=-e|\bar{v} \times \bar{B}|=-e v B \sin \theta{/tex} As {tex}\theta=0^{\circ},|\bar{F}|=-e v B \sin 0^{\circ}{/tex} Hence the electron will continue to move with uniform velocity along the axis of the solenoid.
solution_html:
The electron will continue to move with uniform velocity along the axis of the solenoid.
Let the electron (e) is projected with a uniform velocity (v) in a uniform magnetic field B. The magnitude of force on it is
{tex}|\bar{F}|=-e|\bar{v} \times \bar{B}|=-e v B \sin \theta{/tex}
As {tex}\theta=0^{\circ},|\bar{F}|=-e v B \sin 0^{\circ}{/tex}
Hence the electron will continue to move with uniform velocity along the axis of the solenoid.

question_text: The magnetic field at a distance r from a long wire carrying current I is 0.4 tesla. The magnetic field at a distance 2r is:

options.explanation: {tex}\frac{B_{2}}{B_{1}}=\frac{r_{1}}{r_{2}}=\frac{r}{2 r}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\therefore B_{2}=\frac{1}{2} B_{1}=\frac{1}{2} \times {/tex} 0.4 = 0.2 T

explanation: {tex}\frac{B_{2}}{B_{1}}=\frac{r_{1}}{r_{2}}=\frac{r}{2 r}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\therefore B_{2}=\frac{1}{2} B_{1}=\frac{1}{2} \times {/tex} 0.4 = 0.2 T

subject: Physics
question_html: Write an expression for the force experienced by a charged particle moving in a uniform magnetic field B.
question_text: Write an expression for the force experienced by a charged particle moving in a uniform magnetic field B.
explanation: It is given by {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{F}}=q(\vec{v} \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}}){/tex} The magnitude of force is given by {tex}\mathrm{F}_{m}=\mathrm{B} \ q v \sin \theta{/tex}
solution: It is given by {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{F}}=q(\vec{v} \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}}){/tex} The magnitude of force is given by {tex}\mathrm{F}_{m}=\mathrm{B} \ q v \sin \theta{/tex}
solution_html:
It is given by
{tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{F}}=q(\vec{v} \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}}){/tex}
The magnitude of force is given by
{tex}\mathrm{F}_{m}=\mathrm{B} \ q v \sin \theta{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html: A straight wire, of length {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} metre, is bent into a circular shape. If the wire were to carry a current of 5 A, calculate the magnetic field, due to it, before bending, at a point distant 0.01 times the radius of the circle formed from it. Also calculate the magnetic field, at the centre of the circular loop formed, for the same value of current.
question_text: A straight wire, of length {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} metre, is bent into a circular shape. If the wire were to carry a current of 5 A, calculate the magnetic field, due to it, before bending, at a point distant 0.01 times the radius of the circle formed from it. Also calculate the magnetic field, at the centre of the circular loop formed, for the same value of current.
explanation: Here 2 {tex}\pi{/tex} r = {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} metre {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} r = {tex}\frac{1}{4}{/tex} = 0.25 m Magnetic field due to straight wire, B = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I}{2 \pi r^{\prime}}=\frac{\mu_{0} I}{2 \pi \times 0.01 r}=\frac{4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times 5}{2 \pi \times 0.01 \times 0.25}{/tex} = 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -4 T Magnetic field at the centre of the circular loop, B = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I}{2 r}=\frac{4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times 5}{2 \times 0.25}{/tex} = 1.256 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -5 T
solution: Here 2 {tex}\pi{/tex} r = {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} metre {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} r = {tex}\frac{1}{4}{/tex} = 0.25 m Magnetic field due to straight wire, B = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I}{2 \pi r^{\prime}}=\frac{\mu_{0} I}{2 \pi \times 0.01 r}=\frac{4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times 5}{2 \pi \times 0.01 \times 0.25}{/tex} = 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -4 T Magnetic field at the centre of the circular loop, B = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I}{2 r}=\frac{4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times 5}{2 \times 0.25}{/tex} = 1.256 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -5 T
solution_html:
Here 2{tex}\pi{/tex}r = {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} metre
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} r = {tex}\frac{1}{4}{/tex} = 0.25 m
Magnetic field due to straight wire,
B = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I}{2 \pi r^{\prime}}=\frac{\mu_{0} I}{2 \pi \times 0.01 r}=\frac{4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times 5}{2 \pi \times 0.01 \times 0.25}{/tex}
= 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 T
Magnetic field at the centre of the circular loop,
B = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I}{2 r}=\frac{4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times 5}{2 \times 0.25}{/tex} = 1.256 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 T

subject: Physics
question_html: A proton and an {tex}\alpha{/tex}-particle moving with the same velocity enter into a uniform magnetic field, acting normal to the plane of their motion. The ratio of the radii of the circular paths described by the proton and {tex}\alpha{/tex}-particle is:
question_text: A proton and an {tex}\alpha{/tex} -particle moving with the same velocity enter into a uniform magnetic field, acting normal to the plane of their motion. The ratio of the radii of the circular paths described by the proton and {tex}\alpha{/tex} -particle is:

options.explanation: Radius, {tex}r=\frac{m v}{q B}{/tex} For same v and B, {tex}r \propto \frac{m}{q}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \ \frac{r_{p}}{r_{\alpha}}=\frac{m_{p}}{m_{\alpha}} \cdot \frac{q_{\alpha}}{q_{p}}=\frac{m_{p}}{4 m_{\mu}} \cdot \frac{2 e}{e}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} = 1 : 2

explanation: Radius, {tex}r=\frac{m v}{q B}{/tex} For same v and B, {tex}r \propto \frac{m}{q}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \ \frac{r_{p}}{r_{\alpha}}=\frac{m_{p}}{m_{\alpha}} \cdot \frac{q_{\alpha}}{q_{p}}=\frac{m_{p}}{4 m_{\mu}} \cdot \frac{2 e}{e}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} = 1 : 2
