question_html: A vertical straight conductor carries a current vertically upwards. A point P lies to the east of it at a small distance and another point Q lies to the west in the same direction. The magnetic field at P is:
question_text: A vertical straight conductor carries a current vertically upwards. A point P lies to the east of it at a small distance and another point Q lies to the west in the same direction. The magnetic field at P is:

options.explanation: As per Biot Savart Law, Magnetic field at a point is inversely proportional to square of distance from the current carrying conductor. Therefore magnitude of magnetic field is same at both points P and Q, irrespective of their position from the conductor.

options.text: greater or less than at Q, depending upon the strength of the current
options.html: greater or less than at Q, depending upon the strength of the current

explanation: As per Biot Savart Law, Magnetic field at a point is inversely proportional to square of distance from the current carrying conductor. Therefore magnitude of magnetic field is same at both points P and Q, irrespective of their position from the conductor.

subject: Physics
question_html: A semicircular arc of radius 20 cm carries a current of 10 A. Calculate the magnitude of the magnetic field at the centre of the arc.
question_text: A semicircular arc of radius 20 cm carries a current of 10 A. Calculate the magnitude of the magnetic field at the centre of the arc.
explanation: The magnetic field due to a semi-circular arc of radius ′r′ carrying current (I) at centre is given by {tex}\Delta {B}=\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \frac{{I} \triangle {l} \sin 90^{\circ}}{{r}^{2}}=\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \frac{{I} \Delta {l}}{{r}^{2}}{/tex} The net magnetic field due to the whole length of arc l will be B = {tex}=\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \frac{{I}}{{r}^{2}} \sum \Delta {l}{/tex} For semi-circular arc {tex}\sum \Delta {l}=\pi {r}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} B = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \frac{{I}}{{r}^{2}}=\frac{\mu_{0} {I}}{4 {r}}{/tex} Given I = 10 A, r = 20m = 0.20 m {tex}\therefore{/tex} B = {tex}\frac{4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times 10}{4 \times 0.20}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{4 \times 3.14 \times 10^{-7} \times 10}{4 \times 0.20}{/tex} = 1.57 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -5 T
solution: The magnetic field due to a semi-circular arc of radius ′r′ carrying current (I) at centre is given by {tex}\Delta {B}=\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \frac{{I} \triangle {l} \sin 90^{\circ}}{{r}^{2}}=\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \frac{{I} \Delta {l}}{{r}^{2}}{/tex} The net magnetic field due to the whole length of arc l will be B = {tex}=\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \frac{{I}}{{r}^{2}} \sum \Delta {l}{/tex} For semi-circular arc {tex}\sum \Delta {l}=\pi {r}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} B = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \frac{{I}}{{r}^{2}}=\frac{\mu_{0} {I}}{4 {r}}{/tex} Given I = 10 A, r = 20m = 0.20 m {tex}\therefore{/tex} B = {tex}\frac{4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times 10}{4 \times 0.20}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{4 \times 3.14 \times 10^{-7} \times 10}{4 \times 0.20}{/tex} = 1.57 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -5 T
solution_html:
The magnetic field due to a semi-circular arc of radius ′r′ carrying current (I) at centre is given by
{tex}\Delta {B}=\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \frac{{I} \triangle {l} \sin 90^{\circ}}{{r}^{2}}=\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \frac{{I} \Delta {l}}{{r}^{2}}{/tex}
The net magnetic field due to the whole length of arc l will be
B = {tex}=\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \frac{{I}}{{r}^{2}} \sum \Delta {l}{/tex}
For semi-circular arc {tex}\sum \Delta {l}=\pi {r}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} B = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \frac{{I}}{{r}^{2}}=\frac{\mu_{0} {I}}{4 {r}}{/tex}
Given I = 10 A, r = 20m = 0.20 m
{tex}\therefore{/tex} B = {tex}\frac{4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times 10}{4 \times 0.20}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{4 \times 3.14 \times 10^{-7} \times 10}{4 \times 0.20}{/tex} = 1.57 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 T

question_html: An electron is moving at 106 ms-1 in a direction parallel to a current of 5 A, flowing through an infinitely long straight wire, separated by a perpendicular distance of 10 cm in air. Calculate the magnitude of the force experienced by the electron.
question_text: An electron is moving at 10 6 ms -1 in a direction parallel to a current of 5 A, flowing through an infinitely long straight wire, separated by a perpendicular distance of 10 cm in air. Calculate the magnitude of the force experienced by the electron.
explanation: The magnetic field of the straight wire carrying a current of 2 A, at a distance of 10 cm or 0.1 m from it is B = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I}{2 \pi r}=\frac{4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times 5}{2 \pi \times 0.1}{/tex} = 10 -5 T This field acts perpendicular to the direction of the electron. So magnetic force on the electron is F = qvB sin 90 o = 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -19 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -5 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 = 1 .6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -18 N
solution: The magnetic field of the straight wire carrying a current of 2 A, at a distance of 10 cm or 0.1 m from it is B = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I}{2 \pi r}=\frac{4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times 5}{2 \pi \times 0.1}{/tex} = 10 -5 T This field acts perpendicular to the direction of the electron. So magnetic force on the electron is F = qvB sin 90 o = 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -19 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -5 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 = 1 .6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -18 N
solution_html:
The magnetic field of the straight wire carrying a current of 2 A, at a distance of 10 cm or 0.1 m from it is
B = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I}{2 \pi r}=\frac{4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times 5}{2 \pi \times 0.1}{/tex} = 10-5 T
This field acts perpendicular to the direction of the electron. So magnetic force on the electron is
F = qvB sin 90o
= 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-19 {tex}\times{/tex} 106 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 {tex}\times{/tex} 1
= 1 .6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-18 N

question_html: The radius of the first orbit of a hydrogen atom is 0.5 {tex}\mathop {\text{A}}\limits^{\text{o}}{/tex}. The electron moves in an orbit with a uniform speed of 2.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 106 ms-1. Calculate (i) the equivalent current (ii) the equivalent dipole moment and (iii) the magnetic field produced at the centre of the nucleus due to the motion of this electron? Use {tex}\mu_{0} = 4 \pi \times 10^{-7} {NA}^{-2}{/tex} and electronic charge = 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-19 C.
question_text: The radius of the first orbit of a hydrogen atom is 0.5 {tex}\mathop {\text{A}}\limits^{\text{o}}{/tex} . The electron moves in an orbit with a uniform speed of 2.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 6 ms -1 . Calculate (i) the equivalent current (ii) the equivalent dipole moment and (iii) the magnetic field produced at the centre of the nucleus due to the motion of this electron? Use {tex}\mu_{0} = 4 \pi \times 10^{-7} {NA}^{-2}{/tex} and electronic charge = 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -19 C.
explanation: Here r = 0.5 {tex}\mathop {\text{A}}\limits^{\text{o}}{/tex} = 0.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -10 m v = 2.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 6 ms -1 Period of revolution of electron, T = {tex}\frac{2 \pi r}{v}=\frac{2 \times 22 \times 0.5 \times 10^{-10}}{7 \times 2.2 \times 10^{6}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{7} \times 10^{-15} \mathrm{~s}{/tex} Equivalent current, I = {tex}\frac{\text { Charge }}{\text { Time }}=\frac{e}{T}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 7}{10^{-15}}{/tex} = 1.12 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 A Equivalent dipole moment, m = IA = l {tex}\times{/tex} nr 2 = 1.12 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 {tex}\times{/tex} 3.14 {tex}\times{/tex} (0.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -10 ) 2 Am 2 = 8.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -24 Am 2 Magnetic field produced at the centre of the nucleus, B = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I}{2 r}=\frac{4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times 1.12 \times 10^{-3}}{2 \times 0.5 \times 10^{-10}}{/tex} = 14.07 T
solution: Here r = 0.5 {tex}\mathop {\text{A}}\limits^{\text{o}}{/tex} = 0.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -10 m v = 2.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 6 ms -1 Period of revolution of electron, T = {tex}\frac{2 \pi r}{v}=\frac{2 \times 22 \times 0.5 \times 10^{-10}}{7 \times 2.2 \times 10^{6}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{7} \times 10^{-15} \mathrm{~s}{/tex} Equivalent current, I = {tex}\frac{\text { Charge }}{\text { Time }}=\frac{e}{T}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 7}{10^{-15}}{/tex} = 1.12 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 A Equivalent dipole moment, m = IA = l {tex}\times{/tex} nr 2 = 1.12 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 {tex}\times{/tex} 3.14 {tex}\times{/tex} (0.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -10 ) 2 Am 2 = 8.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -24 Am 2 Magnetic field produced at the centre of the nucleus, B = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I}{2 r}=\frac{4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times 1.12 \times 10^{-3}}{2 \times 0.5 \times 10^{-10}}{/tex} = 14.07 T
solution_html:
1. Here r = 0.5 {tex}\mathop {\text{A}}\limits^{\text{o}}{/tex} = 0.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-10 m
    v = 2.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 106 ms-1
    Period of revolution of electron,
    T = {tex}\frac{2 \pi r}{v}=\frac{2 \times 22 \times 0.5 \times 10^{-10}}{7 \times 2.2 \times 10^{6}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{7} \times 10^{-15} \mathrm{~s}{/tex}
    Equivalent current,
    I = {tex}\frac{\text { Charge }}{\text { Time }}=\frac{e}{T}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 7}{10^{-15}}{/tex} = 1.12 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 A
  2. Equivalent dipole moment,
    m = IA = l {tex}\times{/tex} nr2
    = 1.12 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 {tex}\times{/tex} 3.14 {tex}\times{/tex} (0.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-10)2 Am2
    = 8.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-24 Am2
  3. Magnetic field produced at the centre of the nucleus,
    B = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I}{2 r}=\frac{4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times 1.12 \times 10^{-3}}{2 \times 0.5 \times 10^{-10}}{/tex} = 14.07 T

question_text: A solenoid is 2.0 m long and 3.0 cm in diameter. It has 5 layers of winding of 1000 turns each and carries a current of 5.0 A. What is the magnetic field at the centre? Use the standard value of {tex}\mu_0{/tex} .

question_html: The figure given below shows a right-angled isosceles {tex}\triangle{/tex}PQR having its base equal to a. A current of I ampere is passing downwards along a thin straight wire cutting the plane of paper normally as shown at Q. Likewise a similar wire carries an equal current passing normally upwards at R. Find the magnitude and direction of the magnetic induction B at P. Assume the wires to be infinitely long.
question_text: The figure given below shows a right-angled isosceles {tex}\triangle{/tex} PQR having its base equal to a. A current of I ampere is passing downwards along a thin straight wire cutting the plane of paper normally as shown at Q. Likewise a similar wire carries an equal current passing normally upwards at R. Find the magnitude and direction of the magnetic induction B at P. Assume the wires to be infinitely long.
explanation: Let PQ = QR = r. In right {tex}\triangle{/tex} PQR a 2 = r 2 + r 2 = 2r 2 or r = {tex}\frac{a}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex} Magnetic induction at point P due to the conductor passing through Q B 1 = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I}{2 \pi r}=\frac{\sqrt{2} \mu_{0} I}{2 \pi a}=\frac{\mu_{0} I}{\sqrt{2} \pi a}{/tex} , acting along PR Magnetic induction at point P due to the conductor passing through R, B 2 = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I}{\sqrt{2} \pi a}{/tex} , acting along PQ As the two fields at point P are acting along with perpendicular directions, the resultant magnetic induction at point P is B = {tex}\sqrt{B_{1}^{2}+B_{2}^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{\left(\frac{\mu_{0} I}{\sqrt{2} \pi a}\right)^{2}+\left(\frac{\mu_{0} I}{\sqrt{2} \pi a}\right)^{2}}=\sqrt{2} \cdot \frac{\mu_{0} I}{\sqrt{2} \pi a}{/tex} or B = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I}{\pi a}{/tex} This field acts towards the midpoint of QR.
solution: Let PQ = QR = r. In right {tex}\triangle{/tex} PQR a 2 = r 2 + r 2 = 2r 2 or r = {tex}\frac{a}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex} Magnetic induction at point P due to the conductor passing through Q B 1 = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I}{2 \pi r}=\frac{\sqrt{2} \mu_{0} I}{2 \pi a}=\frac{\mu_{0} I}{\sqrt{2} \pi a}{/tex} , acting along PR Magnetic induction at point P due to the conductor passing through R, B 2 = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I}{\sqrt{2} \pi a}{/tex} , acting along PQ As the two fields at point P are acting along with perpendicular directions, the resultant magnetic induction at point P is B = {tex}\sqrt{B_{1}^{2}+B_{2}^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{\left(\frac{\mu_{0} I}{\sqrt{2} \pi a}\right)^{2}+\left(\frac{\mu_{0} I}{\sqrt{2} \pi a}\right)^{2}}=\sqrt{2} \cdot \frac{\mu_{0} I}{\sqrt{2} \pi a}{/tex} or B = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I}{\pi a}{/tex} This field acts towards the midpoint of QR.
solution_html:
Let PQ = QR = r. In right {tex}\triangle{/tex}PQR
a2 = r2 + r2 = 2r2
or
r = {tex}\frac{a}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex}
Magnetic induction at point P due to the conductor passing through Q
B1 = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I}{2 \pi r}=\frac{\sqrt{2} \mu_{0} I}{2 \pi a}=\frac{\mu_{0} I}{\sqrt{2} \pi a}{/tex}, acting along PR
Magnetic induction at point P due to the conductor passing through R,
B2 = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I}{\sqrt{2} \pi a}{/tex}, acting along PQ
As the two fields at point P are acting along with perpendicular directions, the resultant magnetic induction at point P is
B = {tex}\sqrt{B_{1}^{2}+B_{2}^{2}}{/tex}
= {tex}\sqrt{\left(\frac{\mu_{0} I}{\sqrt{2} \pi a}\right)^{2}+\left(\frac{\mu_{0} I}{\sqrt{2} \pi a}\right)^{2}}=\sqrt{2} \cdot \frac{\mu_{0} I}{\sqrt{2} \pi a}{/tex}
or
B = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I}{\pi a}{/tex}
This field acts towards the midpoint of QR.

question_text: A 0.5 m long solenoid has 500 turns and has a flux density of 2.52 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 T at its centre. Find the current in the solenoid. Given {tex}\mu_{0}{/tex} = 4 {tex}\pi{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -7 Hm -1
explanation: Number of turns per unit length, n = {tex}\frac{N}{l}=\frac{500}{0.5 {m}}{/tex} = 1000 turns/m As B = {tex}\mu_{0} n I{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} I = {tex}\frac{B}{\mu_{0} n}=\frac{2.52 \times 10^{-3}}{4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times 1000}{/tex} = 2.0 A
solution: Number of turns per unit length, n = {tex}\frac{N}{l}=\frac{500}{0.5 {m}}{/tex} = 1000 turns/m As B = {tex}\mu_{0} n I{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} I = {tex}\frac{B}{\mu_{0} n}=\frac{2.52 \times 10^{-3}}{4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times 1000}{/tex} = 2.0 A
solution_html:
Number of turns per unit length,
n = {tex}\frac{N}{l}=\frac{500}{0.5 {m}}{/tex} = 1000 turns/m
As B = {tex}\mu_{0} n I{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} I = {tex}\frac{B}{\mu_{0} n}=\frac{2.52 \times 10^{-3}}{4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times 1000}{/tex} = 2.0 A

subject: Physics
question_html: An electron after being accelerated through a potential difference of 100 V enters a uniform magnetic field of 0.004 T perpendicular to its direction of motion. Calculate the radius of the path described by the electron.
question_text: An electron after being accelerated through a potential difference of 100 V enters a uniform magnetic field of 0.004 T perpendicular to its direction of motion. Calculate the radius of the path described by the electron.
explanation: Potential difference, V = 100V Magnetic field, B = 0.004T Force, F = {tex}{q} {V}=\frac{{mv}^{2}}{2}{/tex} (K.E.) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} V = {tex}\sqrt{\frac{2 {q} v}{{m}}}{/tex} for radius of path, {tex}\frac{{mv}^{2}}{{R}}{/tex} = qvB {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} R = {tex}\frac{{mv}}{{qB}}=\frac{{m}}{{qB}} \times \sqrt{\frac{2 {q} {v}}{{m}}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} R = {tex}\frac{1}{0.004} \times \sqrt{\frac{9.1 \times 10^{-31} \times 2 \times 100}{1.6 \times 10^{-19}}}{/tex} = 843.17 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -5 m = 8.43 mm
solution: Potential difference, V = 100V Magnetic field, B = 0.004T Force, F = {tex}{q} {V}=\frac{{mv}^{2}}{2}{/tex} (K.E.) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} V = {tex}\sqrt{\frac{2 {q} v}{{m}}}{/tex} for radius of path, {tex}\frac{{mv}^{2}}{{R}}{/tex} = qvB {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} R = {tex}\frac{{mv}}{{qB}}=\frac{{m}}{{qB}} \times \sqrt{\frac{2 {q} {v}}{{m}}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} R = {tex}\frac{1}{0.004} \times \sqrt{\frac{9.1 \times 10^{-31} \times 2 \times 100}{1.6 \times 10^{-19}}}{/tex} = 843.17 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -5 m = 8.43 mm
solution_html:
Potential difference, V = 100V
Magnetic field, B = 0.004T
Force, F = {tex}{q} {V}=\frac{{mv}^{2}}{2}{/tex} (K.E.)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} V = {tex}\sqrt{\frac{2 {q} v}{{m}}}{/tex}
for radius of path, {tex}\frac{{mv}^{2}}{{R}}{/tex} = qvB
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} R = {tex}\frac{{mv}}{{qB}}=\frac{{m}}{{qB}} \times \sqrt{\frac{2 {q} {v}}{{m}}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} R = {tex}\frac{1}{0.004} \times \sqrt{\frac{9.1 \times 10^{-31} \times 2 \times 100}{1.6 \times 10^{-19}}}{/tex}
= 843.17 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5m
= 8.43 mm

subject: Physics
question_html: A current of 1.0 A is flowing in the sides of an equilateral triangle of side 4.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-2 m. Find the magnetic field at the centroid of the triangle.
question_text: A current of 1.0 A is flowing in the sides of an equilateral triangle of side 4.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -2 m. Find the magnetic field at the centroid of the triangle.
explanation: The situation is shown in figure below. The magnetic field at the centre O due to the current through side PQ is given by: B 1 = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I}{4 \pi a}\left[\sin \theta_{1}+\sin \theta_{2}\right]{/tex} where a is the distance of PQ from O and {tex}\theta_1{/tex} , {tex}\theta_2{/tex} are the angles as shown. The magnetic field due to each of the three sides is the same in magnitude and direction, therefore, total magnetic field at O is B = 3B 1 = {tex}\frac{3 \mu_{0} I}{4 \pi a}\left[\sin \theta_{1}+\sin \theta_{2}\right]{/tex} Here I = 1.0 A, {tex}\theta_1{/tex} = {tex}\theta_2{/tex} = 60 o , {tex}\mu_0{/tex} = 4 {tex}\pi{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -7 Tm A -1 {tex}\frac{P S}{O S}=\tan \theta_{1}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{l / 2}{a}=\tan 60^{\circ}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} a = {tex}\frac{l}{2 \tan 60^{\circ}}=\frac{4.5 \times 10^{-2}}{2 \sqrt{3}} {m}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} B = {tex}\frac{3 \times 4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times 1.0 \times 2 \sqrt{3}}{4 \pi \times 4.5 \times 10^{-2}}\left[\sin 60^{\circ}+\sin 60^{\circ}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\frac{6 \sqrt{3} \times 10^{-5}}{4.5}\left[\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}+\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right]{/tex} = 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -5 T directed normally outwards.
solution: The situation is shown in figure below. The magnetic field at the centre O due to the current through side PQ is given by: B 1 = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I}{4 \pi a}\left[\sin \theta_{1}+\sin \theta_{2}\right]{/tex} where a is the distance of PQ from O and {tex}\theta_1{/tex} , {tex}\theta_2{/tex} are the angles as shown. The magnetic field due to each of the three sides is the same in magnitude and direction, therefore, total magnetic field at O is B = 3B 1 = {tex}\frac{3 \mu_{0} I}{4 \pi a}\left[\sin \theta_{1}+\sin \theta_{2}\right]{/tex} Here I = 1.0 A, {tex}\theta_1{/tex} = {tex}\theta_2{/tex} = 60 o , {tex}\mu_0{/tex} = 4 {tex}\pi{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -7 Tm A -1 {tex}\frac{P S}{O S}=\tan \theta_{1}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{l / 2}{a}=\tan 60^{\circ}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} a = {tex}\frac{l}{2 \tan 60^{\circ}}=\frac{4.5 \times 10^{-2}}{2 \sqrt{3}} {m}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} B = {tex}\frac{3 \times 4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times 1.0 \times 2 \sqrt{3}}{4 \pi \times 4.5 \times 10^{-2}}\left[\sin 60^{\circ}+\sin 60^{\circ}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\frac{6 \sqrt{3} \times 10^{-5}}{4.5}\left[\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}+\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right]{/tex} = 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -5 T directed normally outwards.
solution_html:
The situation is shown in figure below. The magnetic field at the centre O due to the current through side PQ is given by:
B1 = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I}{4 \pi a}\left[\sin \theta_{1}+\sin \theta_{2}\right]{/tex}
where a is the distance of PQ from O and {tex}\theta_1{/tex}, {tex}\theta_2{/tex} are the angles as shown. The magnetic field due to each of the three sides is the same in magnitude and direction, therefore, total magnetic field at O is
B = 3B1
= {tex}\frac{3 \mu_{0} I}{4 \pi a}\left[\sin \theta_{1}+\sin \theta_{2}\right]{/tex}

Here I = 1.0 A, {tex}\theta_1{/tex} = {tex}\theta_2{/tex} = 60o, {tex}\mu_0{/tex} = 4{tex}\pi{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 10-7 Tm A-1
{tex}\frac{P S}{O S}=\tan \theta_{1}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{l / 2}{a}=\tan 60^{\circ}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} a = {tex}\frac{l}{2 \tan 60^{\circ}}=\frac{4.5 \times 10^{-2}}{2 \sqrt{3}} {m}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} B = {tex}\frac{3 \times 4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times 1.0 \times 2 \sqrt{3}}{4 \pi \times 4.5 \times 10^{-2}}\left[\sin 60^{\circ}+\sin 60^{\circ}\right]{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{6 \sqrt{3} \times 10^{-5}}{4.5}\left[\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}+\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right]{/tex} = 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 T
directed normally outwards.

subject: Physics
question_html: A straight wire carrying a current of 12 A is bent into a semicircular arc of radius 2.0 cm as shown in the given figure (a). What is the direction and magnitude of B at the centre of the arc? Would your answer change if the wire were bent into a semicircular arc of the same radius but in the opposite way as shown in given figure (b).
question_text: A straight wire carrying a current of 12 A is bent into a semicircular arc of radius 2.0 cm as shown in the given figure (a). What is the direction and magnitude of B at the centre of the arc? Would your answer change if the wire were bent into a semicircular arc of the same radius but in the opposite way as shown in given figure (b).
explanation: Magnetic field at the centre of the arc is B = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I}{4 r}{/tex} Here I = 12 A, r = 2.0 cm = 0.02 m, {tex}\mu_{0}{/tex} = 4 {tex}\pi{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -7 T mA -1 {tex}\therefore{/tex} B = {tex}\frac{4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times 12}{4 \times 0.02}{/tex} = 1.9 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -4 T According to right-hand rule, the direction of the field is normally into the plane of paper. The magnetic field will be of the same magnitude, B = 1.9 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -4 T The direction of the field is normally out of the plane of paper.
solution: Magnetic field at the centre of the arc is B = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I}{4 r}{/tex} Here I = 12 A, r = 2.0 cm = 0.02 m, {tex}\mu_{0}{/tex} = 4 {tex}\pi{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -7 T mA -1 {tex}\therefore{/tex} B = {tex}\frac{4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times 12}{4 \times 0.02}{/tex} = 1.9 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -4 T According to right-hand rule, the direction of the field is normally into the plane of paper. The magnetic field will be of the same magnitude, B = 1.9 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -4 T The direction of the field is normally out of the plane of paper.
solution_html:
1. Magnetic field at the centre of the arc is
    B = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} I}{4 r}{/tex}
    Here I = 12 A, r = 2.0 cm = 0.02 m,
    {tex}\mu_{0}{/tex} = 4{tex}\pi{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 10-7 T mA-1
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} B = {tex}\frac{4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times 12}{4 \times 0.02}{/tex} = 1.9 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 T
    According to right-hand rule, the direction of the field is normally into the plane of paper.
  2. The magnetic field will be of the same magnitude,
    B = 1.9 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 T
    The direction of the field is normally out of the plane of paper.
